title: Predicting magnetism with first-principles AI
abstract: Computational discovery of magnetic materials remains challenging because magnetism arises from the competition between kinetic energy and Coulomb interaction that is often beyond the reach of standard electronic-structure methods. Here we tackle this challenge by directly solving the many-electron Schrödinger equation with neural-network variational Monte Carlo, which provides a highly expressive variational wavefunction for strongly correlated systems. Applying this technique to transition metal dichalcogenide moiré semicondutors, we predict itinerant ferromagnetism in WSe$_2$/WS$_2$ and an antiferromagnetic insulator in twisted $Γ$-valley homobilayer, using the same neural network without any physics input beyond the microscopic Hamiltonian. Crucially, both types of magnetic states are obtained from a single calculation within the $S_z=0$ sector, removing the need to compute and compare multiple $S_z$ sectors. This significantly reduces computational cost and paves the way for faster and more reliable magnetic material design.

conceptDetails.name: neural wavefunction
conceptDetails.description: A novel neural-network parameterization of the fermionic many-body wavefunction with spinful generalized orbitals that can represent non-magnetic, ferromagnetic, and antiferromagnetic states on equal footing.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs highly expressive variational many-electron wavefunctions as sums of determinants of generalized orbitals to represent ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic ground states.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The fermionic network employs permutation-equivariant architecture to correctly handle antisymmetry of the many-electron wavefunction.

conceptDetails.name: itinerant magnetism
conceptDetails.description: The paper predicts and distinguishes itinerant ferromagnetism driven by kinetic-interaction competition from antiferromagnetic insulating order arising from superexchange, both from first principles.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: SU(2) spin rotation symmetry is explicitly leveraged to identify magnetism via spin quantum numbers without biasing the calculation toward a particular magnetic sector.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A transformer neural network is used as a universal approximator of permutation-equivariant sequence-to-sequence functions to construct the generalized orbitals.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper predicts distinct quantum phase transitions between itinerant ferromagnetic metal and antiferromagnetic insulator phases as a function of filling in moiré semiconductors.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The continuum microscopic Hamiltonian—encoding Coulomb interactions and kinetic energy in a moiré potential—is the sole physics input to the neural-network solver.

conceptDetails.name: moiré heterostructures
conceptDetails.description: Transition metal dichalcogenide moiré semiconductors (WSe2/WS2 and twisted Γ-valley homobilayers) serve as the physical platform where magnetism is predicted.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: The NN-VMC approach is proposed as a first-principles route to discovering and designing magnetic materials, including rare-earth-free magnets.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The method simulates strongly correlated quantum many-body systems in two-dimensional moiré heterostructures without empirical parameters.

title: The Ensemble Inverse Problem: Applications and Methods
abstract: We introduce a new multivariate statistical problem that we refer to as the Ensemble Inverse Problem (EIP). The aim of EIP is to invert for an ensemble that is distributed according to the pushforward of a prior under a forward process. In high energy physics (HEP), this is related to a widely known problem called unfolding, which aims to reconstruct the true physics distribution of quantities, such as momentum and angle, from measurements that are distorted by detector effects. In recent applications, the EIP also arises in full waveform inversion (FWI) and inverse imaging with unknown priors. We propose non-iterative inference-time methods that construct posterior samplers based on a new class of conditional generative models, which we call ensemble inverse generative models. For the posterior modeling, these models additionally use the ensemble information contained in the observation set on top of single measurements. Unlike existing methods, our proposed methods avoid explicit and iterative use of the forward model at inference time via training across several sets of truth-observation pairs that are consistent with the same forward model, but originate from a wide range of priors. We demonstrate that this training procedure implicitly encodes the likelihood model. The use of ensemble information helps posterior inference and enables generalization to unseen priors. We benchmark the proposed method on several synthetic and real datasets in inverse imaging, HEP, and FWI. The codes are available at https://github.com/ZhengyanHuan/The-Ensemble-Inverse-Problem--Applications-and-Methods.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a new formulation called the Ensemble Inverse Problem (EIP), which generalizes classical inverse problems by inverting for an ensemble distributed according to a prior's pushforward under a forward process.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The proposed solution constructs posterior samplers using a new class of conditional generative models called ensemble inverse generative models.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble inverse learning
conceptDetails.description: Novel method introduced in this paper: using the full observation ensemble Y (not just single measurements) to condition posterior inference, enabling generalization to unseen priors.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: EIP-II specifically targets posterior estimation—generating samples from p(x|y,Y) that approximate the true posterior p(x|y)—using ensemble information to improve inference.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: HEP unfolding is the primary physics application motivating EIP, where detector distortions must be removed to recover true particle physics distributions.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The method implicitly encodes the likelihood model through training on truth-observation pairs, avoiding explicit forward model simulation at inference time—a key feature of simulation-based inference.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework is grounded in Bayesian inference, framing the problem as recovering posteriors p(x|y) and priors p(x) from observations corrupted by a forward process.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are used as a backbone architecture for the conditional generative models that perform posterior sampling in the EIP framework.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: High-energy physics collider experiments provide a key real-world benchmark, where the EIP framework is applied to reconstruct particle distributions distorted by detector effects.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion-based approaches are benchmarked and incorporated into the ensemble inverse generative model framework for posterior sampling.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Learning the prior density p(x) and posterior density p(x|y) from data is a core objective of both EIP-I and EIP-II.

conceptDetails.name: full waveform inversion
conceptDetails.description: Seismic FWI is a major application domain benchmarked in the paper, where subsurface velocity maps must be recovered from seismic observations with unknown priors.

title: Universality of Neural Network Field Theory
abstract: We prove that any quantum field theory, or more generally any probability distribution over tempered distributions in $\mathbb{R}^d$, admits a neural network description with a countable infinity of parameters. As an example, we realize the $2d$ Liouville theory as a neural network and numerically compute the three-point function of vertex operators, finding agreement with the DOZZ formula.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves a universality theorem showing every QFT—formalized as a probability distribution over tempered Schwartz distributions—admits a neural network description with countably infinite parameters.

conceptDetails.name: neural network field theory
conceptDetails.description: The NN-FT correspondence—representing QFTs via parameterized neural network architectures with a parameter density—is the central framework proven universal in this work.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: 2d Liouville theory, a CFT, is used as the primary example to validate the NN-FT framework by numerically reproducing the exact DOZZ three-point function.

conceptDetails.name: borel isomorphism
conceptDetails.description: The key mathematical tool enabling the universality proof: the Borel isomorphism theorem guarantees a measurable bijection between any standard Borel space and [0,1], yielding a single-parameter NN representation.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The NN-FT correspondence is framed in terms of stochastic processes: field configurations are random variables, and the paper generalizes this to distributions over Schwartz spaces.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: QFTs are treated as probability distributions (parameter densities) over field configuration spaces, and the neural network encodes these distributions via a parameter density P(θ).

conceptDetails.name: generalized quantum system
conceptDetails.description: A novel abstraction introduced in the paper for random variables valued in topologically suitable spaces, unifying QM, QFT, and other physical systems under a single NN universality theorem.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The canonical NN representation is constructed using orthonormal eigenfunction expansions of the two-point function, providing a countable basis for field configurations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Numerical computation of the three-point function of Liouville vertex operators is performed by sampling from the NN parameter density and estimating the correlator.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The neural network field theory acts as a generative model: drawing parameters θ from the parameter density P(θ) generates valid field configurations consistent with the target QFT.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The NN-FT construction serves as a computable surrogate for analytically or numerically intractable QFTs, enabling simulation of theories like Liouville via finite-parameter approximations.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Worldsheet string theory is cited as a prior application of NN-FT, motivating the push for a general universality result.

title: PFT: Phonon Fine-tuning for Machine Learned Interatomic Potentials
abstract: Many materials properties depend on higher-order derivatives of the potential energy surface, yet machine learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) trained with a standard loss on energy, force, and stress errors can exhibit error in curvature, degrading the prediction of vibrational properties. We introduce phonon fine-tuning (PFT), which directly supervises second-order force constants of materials by matching MLIP energy Hessians to DFT-computed force constants from finite displacement phonon calculations. To scale to large supercells, PFT stochastically samples Hessian columns and computes the loss with a single Hessian-vector product. We also use a simple co-training scheme to incorporate upstream data to mitigate catastrophic forgetting. On the MDR Phonon benchmark, PFT improves Nequix MP by 55% on average across phonon thermodynamic properties and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among models trained on Materials Project trajectories. PFT also generalizes to improve properties beyond second-derivatives, improving thermal conductivity predictions that rely on third-order derivatives of the potential energy.

conceptDetails.name: interatomic potentials
conceptDetails.description: Machine learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) are the core subject of this paper, which introduces a fine-tuning procedure to improve their accuracy on phonon properties.

conceptDetails.name: phonon properties
conceptDetails.description: Phonon thermodynamic properties (frequency, vibrational entropy, free energy, heat capacity) are the primary target of improvement in PFT.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: PFT introduces a fine-tuning procedure that directly supervises second-order force constants to improve pre-trained MLIPs without full retraining.

conceptDetails.name: hessian supervision
conceptDetails.description: A novel training technique that directly matches MLIP energy Hessians to DFT-computed force constants, enabling explicit curvature supervision during fine-tuning.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: PFT introduces a novel Hessian-matching loss that directly supervises curvature of the potential energy surface, replacing the standard energy-force-stress loss for fine-tuning.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: PFT applies transfer learning by fine-tuning a pre-trained foundation model on phonon-specific data while preserving general performance via co-training.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: PFT addresses scalability by stochastically sampling Hessian columns and using Hessian-vector products to reduce training cost from quadratic to linear in system size.

conceptDetails.name: thermal conductivity
conceptDetails.description: PFT generalizes beyond second-order derivatives to improve thermal conductivity predictions that depend on third-order derivatives of the potential energy surface.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: MLIPs act as fast surrogates for density functional theory (DFT) computations in large-scale materials screening and simulation workflows.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: A co-training scheme interleaves upstream pre-training data with phonon fine-tuning data to mitigate catastrophic forgetting across multiple tasks.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper fine-tunes Nequip, an equivariant neural network architecture, as its base MLIP model.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: Crystal symmetries and periodic supercell geometry are exploited to reduce the number of required finite displacements and enable efficient phonon force constant computation.

title: String Theory from Infinite Width Neural Networks
abstract: We realize bosonic string theory with ensembles of infinite width neural networks. The string tension is tuned by the variance of the output weights. The construction provides a new computation of the foundational Virasoro-Shapiro and Veneziano amplitudes as neural network correlators.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central goal is to realize bosonic string theory—including its Polyakov action, string tension, and amplitudes—using ensembles of infinite-width neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: neural network field theory
conceptDetails.description: A novel framework (NN-FT) in which quantum field theories, and here string theory, are realized by specifying neural network architectures and probability distributions over their parameters.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The construction relies on realizing the 2d free boson CFT and the bc-ghost system as neural network field theories, which together define the gauge-fixed bosonic string.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The Veneziano and Virasoro-Shapiro amplitudes are recomputed as neural network correlators, demonstrating crossing symmetry, Regge behavior, and UV softness.

conceptDetails.name: neural network gaussian process
conceptDetails.description: The NNGP correspondence—that infinite-width networks converge to Gaussian processes—underpins the free-field limit, enabling exact analytic computation of all two-point correlators.

conceptDetails.name: infinite width limit
conceptDetails.description: Taking the number of neurons N→∞ suppresses non-Gaussian connected correlators via the Central Limit Theorem, yielding the free boson and ghost theories required by the bosonic string.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The neural network field theory (NN-FT) framework maps ensembles of neural networks onto free and interacting quantum field theories, with this paper extending it to string/gravity.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian processes and Grassmann-valued Gaussian distributions over network parameters are the core probabilistic tools used to derive field-theory correlators.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Worldsheet rotation/translation invariance and spacetime Lorentz symmetry are encoded directly into the parameter distributions of the neural network architectures.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: String amplitudes are obtained by averaging over ensembles of neural networks, with the ensemble statistics reproducing the required field-theory path integral.

title: A glimpse into the Ultrametric spectrum
abstract: The non-relativistic string spectrum is built from integer-spaced energy quanta in such a way that the high-temperature asymptotics, via the Hardy-Ramanujan formula for integer partitions, reduces to standard two-dimensional thermodynamics. Here we explore deformed realizations of this behavior motivated by $p$-adic string theory and Lorentzian versions thereof with a non-trivial spectrum. We study the microstate scaling that results on associating quantum harmonic oscillators to the normal modes of tree-graphs rather than string graphs and observe that Hardy-Ramanujan scaling is not realized. But by computing the eigenvalues of the derivative operator on the $p$-adic circle and by determining the eigenspectrum of the Neumann-to-Dirichlet operator, we uncover a spectrum of exponentially growing energies but with exponentially growing degeneracies balanced in such a way that Hardy-Ramanujan scaling is realized, but modulated with log-periodic fluctuations.

conceptDetails.name: ultrametric spectrum
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study: the energy eigenspectrum arising from quantizing harmonic oscillators on p-adic circle normal modes, exhibiting exponentially growing energies and degeneracies.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops an ultrametric analog of string theory by generalizing the worldsheet geometry from string-graphs to tree-graphs and studying the resulting spectrum and thermodynamics.

conceptDetails.name: p-adic string theory
conceptDetails.description: The Freund-Olson formulation of string theory over p-adic numbers provides the foundational framework the paper seeks to extend by constructing a non-trivial tower of states.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Eigenvalue computation of the derivative (Vladimirov) operator on the p-adic circle and the Neumann-to-Dirichlet operator are the central technical tools used to uncover the ultrametric spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly derives eigenspectra of tree-graph Laplacians and the Neumann-to-Dirichlet operator to characterize normal mode frequencies and their degeneracies.

conceptDetails.name: log-periodic fluctuations
conceptDetails.description: A key novel finding: Hardy-Ramanujan entropy scaling is recovered in the ultrametric setting but modulated by log-periodic oscillations arising from the discrete self-similar structure of the p-adic spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The ultrametric string is formulated by associating quantum harmonic oscillators to normal modes, embedding the analysis in the broader QFT framework of second quantization and partition functions.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: P-adic conformal field theories are referenced as existing structures on hierarchical systems that motivate the ultrametric string construction.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Freeman Dyson's proof of a second-order phase transition in hierarchical systems is cited as a key result motivating the study of p-adic and ultrametric physics.

title: MARVEL: A Multi Agent-based Research Validator and Enabler using Large Language Models
abstract: We present MARVEL (https://ligogpt.mit.edu/marvel), a locally deployable, open-source framework for domain-aware question answering and assisted scientific research. It is designed to address the increasing demands of a digital assistant for scientific groups that can read highly technical data, cite precisely, and operate within authenticated networks. MARVEL combines a fast path for straightforward queries with a more deliberate DeepSearch mode that integrates retrieval-augmented generation and Monte Carlo Tree Search. It explores complementary subqueries, allocates more compute to promising branches, and maintains a global evidence ledger that preserves sources during drafting. We applied this framework in the context of gravitational-wave research related to the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory. Answers are grounded in a curated semantic index of research literature, doctoral theses, LIGO documents, and long-running detector electronic logbooks, with targeted web searches when appropriate. Because direct benchmarking against commercial LLMs cannot be performed on private data, we evaluated MARVEL on two publicly available surrogate datasets that capture comparable semantic and technical characteristics. On these benchmarks, MARVEL matches a GPT-4o mini baseline on literature-centric queries and substantially outperforms it on detector-operations content, where domain retrieval and guided reasoning are decisive. By making the complete framework and evaluation datasets openly available, we aim to provide a reproducible foundation for developing domain-specific scientific assistants.

conceptDetails.name: retrieval-augmented generation
conceptDetails.description: The core information retrieval mechanism in MARVEL that grounds LLM answers in a curated semantic index of papers, theses, LIGO documents, and logbooks.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo Tree Search drives MARVEL's DeepSearch mode, allocating compute to promising subquery branches and maintaining a global evidence ledger.

conceptDetails.name: multi-agent orchestration
conceptDetails.description: MARVEL's multi-agent architecture coordinates specialized agents for query decomposition, retrieval, re-ranking, and report synthesis.

conceptDetails.name: domain-specific qa
conceptDetails.description: MARVEL is designed specifically for domain-aware question answering over private and highly technical scientific corpora, outperforming general LLMs on detector-operations content.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Semantic embeddings power the curated index enabling similarity-based retrieval across heterogeneous scientific document types.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: MARVEL constitutes an end-to-end scientific workflow automating literature retrieval, evidence aggregation, and citation-grounded drafting for research groups.

conceptDetails.name: test-time scaling
conceptDetails.description: MARVEL's DeepSearch mode adaptively allocates more LLM inference compute to high-potential query branches at inference time, a form of test-time compute scaling.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain; MARVEL is deployed for LIGO gravitational-wave research, indexing detector logbooks, technical documents, and GW literature.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Citation-grounded answers and a transparent evidence ledger make MARVEL's reasoning auditable and reproducible for scientific users.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: The adaptive subquery exploration strategy selectively expands promising reasoning branches, analogous to active learning's principle of targeted information acquisition.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detector-operations queries about noise mitigation, calibration, and data-quality represent a key use case where MARVEL substantially outperforms baseline LLMs.

title: Green's function on the Tate curve
abstract: Motivated by the question of defining a $p$-adic string worldsheet action in genus one, we define a Laplacian operator on the Tate curve, and study its Green's function. We show that the Green's function exists. We provide an explicit formula for the Green's function, which turns out to be a non-Archimedean counterpart of the Archimedean Green's function on a flat torus. In particular, it turns out that this Green's function recovers the Néron local height function for the Tate curve in the $p\to\infty$ limit, when the $j$-invariant has odd valuation. So this non-Archimedean height function now acquires a physics meaning in terms of the large $p$ limit of a non-Archimedean conformal field theory two point function on the Tate curve, as well as a direct analytic interpretation as a Green's function, on the same footing as in the Archimedean place.

conceptDetails.name: p-adic analysis
conceptDetails.description: The entire framework operates over p-adic and non-Archimedean local fields, with the Vladimirov derivative, p-adic norms, and Haar measures as foundational analytic tools.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes a p-adic string worldsheet action in genus one on the Tate curve, extending p-adic open and closed string theory beyond genus zero.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: A non-Archimedean conformal field theory of free scalar bosons underpins the proposed worldsheet action, with the Green's function serving as the CFT two-point function.

conceptDetails.name: néron height function
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves that the non-Archimedean Green's function recovers the Néron local height function for the Tate curve in the p→∞ limit, providing a new physical interpretation of this arithmetic-geometric object.

conceptDetails.name: bruhat-tits tree
conceptDetails.description: The Bruhat-Tits tree T_p serves as the bulk geometry dual to the p-adic string boundary, and its truncations provide the finite-dimensional filtration used to compute the Green's function.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The Tate curve is realized as a quotient under a discrete subgroup of PGL(2,Q_p), and representation-theoretic aspects of GL(2,F) symmetry are central to the action's invariance.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs a field-theoretic action with a Laplacian operator on a p-adic elliptic curve, situating the work within QFT on non-Archimedean spaces.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The Laplacian operator on the Tate curve is analyzed spectrally via finite-dimensional approximations, with eigenvalue decomposition used to establish existence and an explicit formula for the Green's function.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The Veneziano amplitude and p-adic tachyon scattering amplitudes motivate the genus-one extension and provide the genus-zero foundation for the construction.

title: Machine Learning Invariants of Tensors
abstract: We propose a data-driven approach to identifying the functionally independent invariants that can be constructed from a tensor with a given symmetry structure. Our algorithm proceeds by first enumerating graphs, or tensor networks, that represent inequivalent contractions of a product of tensors, computing instances of these scalars using randomly generated data, and then seeking linear relations between invariants using numerical linear algebra. Such relations yield syzygies, or functional dependencies relating different invariants. We apply this approach in an extended case study of the independent invariants that can be constructed from an antisymmetric $3$-form $H_{μνρ}$ in six dimensions, finding five independent invariants. This result confirms that the most general Lagrangian for such a $3$-form, which depends on $H_{μνρ}$ but not its derivatives, is an arbitrary function of five variables, and we give explicit formulas relating other invariants to the five independent scalars in this generating set.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The central mathematical framework: the paper studies invariants under Lie group actions (Lorentz group, SO(n)), with the full algorithm built around identifying group-theoretic singlets.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm systematically finds scalars that are invariant under group transformations, directly targeting symmetry-preserving quantities for physics applications.

conceptDetails.name: invariant enumeration
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a data-driven computational method to systematically enumerate and certify all functionally independent invariants for tensors with prescribed symmetry structures.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Graphs representing inequivalent index contractions of tensor products are enumerated as the first step of the algorithm, serving as the combinatorial backbone of invariant generation.

conceptDetails.name: syzygy detection
conceptDetails.description: A novel contribution: the algorithm identifies syzygies — polynomial functional dependencies between invariants — by finding vanishing linear combinations across random numerical samples.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Numerical linear algebra (SVD and rank computation over random samples) is used to detect linear dependencies among candidate invariants and identify syzygies.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics application is determining the most general Lagrangian for an antisymmetric 3-form field in six dimensions — a canonical effective field theory construction problem.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies invariant classification to Lorentz-scalar Lagrangians, including non-linear electrodynamics and α′ corrections to supergravity effective actions.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm automates the discovery and classification of functionally independent invariants and their polynomial relations, replacing case-by-case mathematical analysis.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Randomly generated tensor components drawn from Gaussian distributions provide numerical instances used to test functional independence of candidate invariants.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Classification of Riemann tensor invariants in ten dimensions for α′ corrections to type IIA/IIB supergravity is cited as a primary motivating application.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Entanglement classification under local unitary and SLOCC transformations is discussed as a direct application of the tensor invariant framework in quantum information.

title: Entanglement cohomology for GHZ and W states
abstract: Entanglement cohomology assigns a graded cohomology ring to a multipartite pure state, providing homological invariants that are stable under local unitaries and characterize inequivalent patterns of entanglement. In this work we derive exact expressions for the dimensions of these cohomology groups in two canonical entanglement classes, generalized GHZ and W states on an arbitrary number of parties and local Hilbert space dimensions, thus proving conjectures of arXiv:1901.02011. Using the additional structure of the Hodge star and wedge product operations, we propose two new classes of local unitary invariants: the spectrum of the natural Laplacian acting on entanglement $k$-forms, and the intersection numbers obtained from wedge products of representatives for cohomology classes. We present numerical experiments which investigate these invariants in particular states, suggesting that they may provide useful quantities for describing multipartite entanglement.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes multipartite pure quantum states (GHZ and W states) as the primary objects of study for computing entanglement cohomology invariants.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Multipartite entanglement is the central phenomenon being characterized, with cohomological invariants distinguishing inequivalent entanglement classes.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement cohomology
conceptDetails.description: The central framework of the paper: a graded cohomology ring assigned to multipartite pure states whose Betti numbers and Laplacian spectra classify entanglement classes.

conceptDetails.name: hodge theory
conceptDetails.description: The Hodge star, wedge product, adjoint operator, and Laplacian from Hodge theory on manifolds are adapted to the entanglement cohomology setting to define new invariants.

conceptDetails.name: intersection numbers
conceptDetails.description: A novel class of local unitary invariants proposed in this paper, computed from wedge products of cohomology class representatives for multipartite states.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The spectrum of the entanglement Laplacian acting on entanglement k-forms is proposed as a new class of local unitary invariants and investigated numerically.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Computing the spectrum of the entanglement Laplacian requires eigenvalue decomposition to extract invariants distinguishing multipartite quantum states.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Local unitary invariance under the group of local unitary transformations is the symmetry constraint that the cohomological invariants must respect.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical structures employed (differential forms, Hodge theory, Laplacians) are drawn from mathematical physics formalisms closely related to QFT.

title: Constraining primordial non-Gaussianity from DESI DR1 quasars and Planck PR4 CMB Lensing
abstract: We present the first measurement of local-type primordial non-Gaussianity from the cross-correlation between $1.2$ million spectroscopically confirmed quasars from the first data release (DR1) of the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) and the Planck PR4 CMB lensing reconstructions. The analysis is performed in three tomographic redshift bins covering $0.8 < z < 3.5$, covering a sky fraction of $\sim 20\%$. We adopt a catalog-based pseudo-$C_\ell$ estimator and apply linear imaging weights validated on noiseless mocks. Compared to previous analyses using photometric quasar samples, our results benefit from the high purity of the DESI spectroscopic sample, the reduced noise of PR4 lensing, and the absence of excess large-scale power in the spectroscopic quasar auto-correlation. Fitting simultaneously for the non-Gaussianity parameter $f_{\mathrm{NL}}$ and the linear bias amplitude in each redshift bin, we obtain $f_{\mathrm{NL}} = 2^{+28}_{-34}$ for a response parameter $p=1.6$, and $f_{\mathrm{NL}} = 6^{+20}_{-24}$ for $p=1.0$. These results improve the constraints on $f_{\mathrm{NL}}$ by $\sim 35\%$ compared to the previous analysis based on the Legacy Imaging Survey DR9. Our results demonstrate the statistical power of DESI quasars for probing inflationary physics, and highlight the promise of future DESI data releases.

conceptDetails.name: primordial non-gaussianity
conceptDetails.description: The central measurement target: local-type fNL constraining inflationary physics via scale-dependent quasar bias.

conceptDetails.name: scale-dependent bias
conceptDetails.description: Local PNG imprints a k^-2 scale-dependent enhancement in quasar clustering used as the primary observable signal.

conceptDetails.name: cmb lensing cross-correlation
conceptDetails.description: Cross-correlation of quasar number counts with CMB lensing convergence provides a systematic-robust probe of large-scale structure.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: Planck PR4 CMB lensing maps are cross-correlated with DESI quasars to probe primordial non-Gaussianity at large scales.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Catalog-based pseudo-Cl estimator is used to measure angular power spectra from quasar and CMB lensing maps.

conceptDetails.name: tomographic redshift binning
conceptDetails.description: Analysis is performed in three tomographic redshift bins (0.8 < z < 3.5) to exploit redshift evolution of the fNL signal.

conceptDetails.name: optimal weighting
conceptDetails.description: Optimal redshift weights derived to maximize signal-to-noise on fNL from the quasar-CMB lensing cross-power spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Simultaneous fitting of fNL and linear bias parameters in each redshift bin uses Bayesian posterior estimation.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Noiseless mocks are created to validate imaging systematic weights and test the pseudo-Cl estimator pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo normalization corrections are applied to Planck lensing maps and mocks are generated to validate systematic weights.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Scale-dependent bias from local PNG is modeled within an effective field theory framework relating fNL to galaxy clustering.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: DESI DR1 quasars serve as large-scale structure tracers probing cosmological parameters including dark energy constraints.

title: A Search for Binary Black Hole Mergers in LIGO O1-O3 Data with Convolutional Neural Networks
abstract: Since the first detection of gravitational waves in 2015 by LIGO from the binary black hole merger GW150914, gravitational-wave astronomy has developed significantly, with over 200 compact binary merger events cataloged. The use of neural networks has the potential to significantly speed up the detection, classification, and especially parameter estimation for gravitational wave events, compared to current techniques, quite important for electromagnetic follow-up of events. In this work, we present a machine learning pipeline using neural networks to detect gravitational wave events. We generate training data using real LIGO data to train and refine neural networks that can detect binary black hole (BBH) mergers, and apply these models to search through LIGO's first three observing runs. We detect 57 out of the 75 total cataloged BBH events with two detectors of data in O1, O2, and O3, with 57 false positives that can mostly be ruled out with parameter inference and human inspection. Finally, we extensively test this pipeline on time-shifted data to characterize its False Alarm Rate (FAR). These results are an important step in developing machine learning-based GW searches, enabling low-latency detection and multi-messenger astronomy.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for binary black hole merger gravitational wave signals in LIGO O1-O3 data, directly applying CNNs to this astrophysical detection problem.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNNs are the primary neural architecture used to classify time-series and time-frequency LIGO data as containing BBH merger signals or noise.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The core scientific task is detecting gravitational wave signals embedded in detector noise across the full LIGO observing runs O1-O3.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The ML pipeline performs binary classification between BBH merger signals and background noise templates.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The paper rigorously characterizes the False Alarm Rate using time-shifted data, providing a statistical framework for evaluating detection significance.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Training data is constructed by injecting simulated BBH waveforms into real LIGO noise, with deliberate over-sampling of low-mass binaries to improve coverage.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: CNNs extract discriminative features from both whitened time-series and time-frequency (spectrogram) representations of LIGO strain data.

conceptDetails.name: multi-messenger astronomy
conceptDetails.description: Low-latency CNN detection enables rapid electromagnetic follow-up of GW events, a key motivation for replacing slower matched-filter approaches.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Parameter estimation via Bayesian inference is used as a post-detection step to rule out false positives identified by the CNN pipeline.

title: Bridging Simulations and EFT: A Hybrid Model of the Lyman-Alpha Forest Field
abstract: The Lyman-alpha (Lya) forest is a unique probe of cosmology and the intergalactic medium at high redshift and small scales. The statistical power of the ongoing Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) demands precise theoretical tools to model the Lya forest. We present a hybrid effective field theory (HEFT) forward model in redshift space that leverages the accuracy of non-linear particle displacements computed using the N-body simulation suite AbacusSummit with the predictive power of an analytical, perturbative bias forward model in the framework of the effective field theory (EFT). The residual noise between the model and the simulated Lya field has a nearly white (scale-and orientation-independent) power spectrum on quasi-linear scales, substantially simplifying its modeling compared to a purely perturbative description. As a consequence of the improved control over the 3D Lya forest stochasticity, we find agreement between the modeled and the true power spectra at the 5 per cent level down to scales of k <= 1 h/Mpc. This procedure offers a promising path toward constructing efficient and accurate emulators to predict large-scale clustering summary statistics for full-shape cosmological analyses of Lya forest data from both DESI and its successor, DESI-II.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The HEFT forward model is built on EFT of large-scale structure, providing the perturbative bias expansion framework for the Lyman-alpha forest field.

conceptDetails.name: lyman-alpha forest

conceptDetails.name: bias expansion

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes

title: Electronic crystals and quasicrystals in semiconductor quantum wells: an AI-powered discovery
abstract: The homogeneous electron gas is a cornerstone of quantum condensed matter physics, providing the foundation for developing density functional theory and understanding electronic phases in semiconductors. However, theoretical understanding of strongly-correlated electrons in realistic semiconductor systems remains limited. In this work, we develop a neural network based variational approach to study quantum wells in three dimensional geometry for a variety of electron densities and well thicknesses. Starting from first principles, our unbiased AI-powered method reveals metallic and crystalline phases with both monolayer and bilayer charge distributions. In the emergent bilayer, we discover a new quantum phase of matter: the electronic quasicrystal.

conceptDetails.name: electronic quasicrystal
conceptDetails.description: A newly discovered quantum phase of matter with quasicrystalline charge order stabilized by quantum fluctuations in the bilayer regime of semiconductor quantum wells, representing the paper's central finding.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo is the core computational framework for evaluating the Hamiltonian expectation value and optimizing the neural network wavefunction via energy minimization.

conceptDetails.name: neural network variational monte carlo
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops and benchmarks NN-VMC for three-dimensional quantum well geometry, combining deep neural network wavefunctions with VMC optimization to access the full phase diagram.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms

conceptDetails.name: quantum states

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The NN-VMC method simulates strongly correlated electrons in three-dimensional semiconductor quantum wells from first principles without bias toward any particular ground state.

conceptDetails.description: The neural network wavefunction enforces fermionic antisymmetry through a Slater-determinant-like structure and respects the spatial symmetries of the slab geometry.

title: Self-lensing flares from black hole binaries V: systematic searches in LSST
abstract: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory has now seen first light, and over a 10 year duration, LSST is projected to catalogue tens of millions of quasars, many of which are expected to be associated with sub-parsec supermassive black hole binaries (SMBHBs). Out of these SMBHBs, up to thousands of relatively massive binary-quasars are expected to exhibit gravitational self-lensing flares (SLFs) that last for at least 20-30 days. We assess the effectiveness of the Lomb-Scargle (LS) periodogram and matched filters (MFs) as methods for systematic searches for these binaries, using toy-models of hydrodynamical, Doppler, and self-lensing variability from equal-mass, eccentric SMBHBs. We inject SLFs into random realizations of damped random walk (DRW) lightcurves, representing stochastic quasar variability, and compute the LS periodogram with and without the SLF. We find that periodograms of SLF+DRW light-curves do not have maximum peak heights that could not arise from DRW-only periodograms. On the other hand, the matched filter signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) can distinguish SLFs from noise even with LSST-like cadences and DRW noise. Furthermore, we develop a three-step procedure with matched filters, which can also recover injected binary parameters from these light-curves. We expect this method to be computationally efficient enough to be applicable to millions of quasar light-curves in LSST.

conceptDetails.name: self-lensing flares

conceptDetails.name: supermassive black hole binaries

title: A Fully Photometric Approach to Type Ia Supernova Cosmology in the LSST Era: Host Galaxy Redshifts and Supernova Classification
authors: The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration
abstract: The upcoming Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) is expected to discover nearly a million Type Ia supernovae (SNeIa), offering an unprecedented opportunity to constrain dark energy. The vast majority of these events will lack spectroscopic classification and redshifts, necessitating a fully photometric approach to maximize cosmology constraining power. We present detailed simulations based on the Extended LSST Astronomical Time Series Classification Challenge (ELAsTiCC), and a cosmological analysis using photometrically classified SNeIa with host galaxy photometric redshifts. This dataset features realistic multi-band light curves, non-SNIa contamination, host mis-associations, and transient-host correlations across the high-redshift Deep Drilling Fields (DDF) (~ 50 deg^2). We also include a spectroscopically confirmed low-redshift sample based on the Wide Fast Deep (WFD) fields. We employ a joint SN+host photometric redshift fit, a neural network based photometric classifier (SCONE), and BEAMS with Bias Corrections (BBC) methodology to construct a bias-corrected Hubble diagram. We produce statistical + systematic covariance matrices, and perform cosmology fitting with a prior using Cosmic Microwave Background constraints. We fit and present results for the wCDM dark energy model, and the more general Chevallier-Polarski-Linder (CPL) w0wa model. With a simulated sample of ~6000 events, we achieve a Figure of Merit (FoM) value of about 150, which is significantly larger than the DESVYR FoM of 54. Averaging analysis results over 25 independent samples, we find small but significant biases indicating a need for further analysis testing and development.

title: SN 2024afav: A Superluminous Supernova with Multiple Light Curve Bumps and Spectroscopic Signatures of Circumstellar Interaction
abstract: We present a comprehensive optical and near-infrared spectroscopic study of SN 2024afav - a hydrogen-poor superluminous supernova (SLSN-I) that peaks at $\approx$ -20.7 mag and exhibits an unusual multi-bumped light curve. Our spectroscopic observations, spanning phases of -14 to +160 d, reveal several unusual features: (i) a narrow (1,800 km s$^{-1}$) and blueshifted (11,000 km s$^{-1}$) absorption from H$α$ starting at +20 d; (ii) persistent optical and NIR He I lines at all available phases, showing double absorption structure in NIR spectra at +23 d, with a high velocity component at a similar velocity to H$α$; (iii) early appearance of nebular [O III] emission starting at $\approx$ +50 d; and (iv) strong [O II] + [Ca II] 7300 Å emission complex starting at $\approx$ +110 d. These unusual features, and their onset at the time of the light curve bumps, provide compelling evidence of circumstellar interaction between the SN ejecta and a nearby hydrogen-rich shell, as well as the presence of helium in both the outer layers of the progenitor star and in the circumstellar medium. A comparison of SN 2024afav to other SLSNe-I showing bumpy light curves and similar spectral properties (PTF10hgi, SN 2017egm, SN 2019hge), points to a rare sub-group of SLSNe-I in which CSM interaction provides an important modulation to the energy input.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides spectroscopic evidence for ejecta-CSM interaction via narrow Hα absorption, He I lines, and nebular emission features driving light curve bumps.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification

conceptDetails.name: light curve bumps
conceptDetails.description: Multiple post-peak light curve bumps in SN 2024afav and comparison events (PTF10hgi, SN 2017egm, SN 2019hge) are the primary observational phenomenon motivating the CSM interaction interpretation.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Optical and near-infrared spectroscopic analysis spanning phases -14 to +160 days is used to track the temporal evolution of absorption and emission features.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution

conceptDetails.name: nebular spectroscopy

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detection of narrow spectral features (Hα, He I, [O III], [O II]+[Ca II]) in optical and NIR spectra is the primary observational method used to infer CSM interaction.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar spin-down
conceptDetails.description: The magnetar spin-down model is evaluated as the primary powering mechanism for SLSNe-I, with CSM interaction proposed as an additional energy modulation source for bumpy light curves.

title: Lattice field theory for superconducting circuits
abstract: Large superconducting quantum circuits have a number of important applications in quantum computing. Accurately predicting the performance of these devices from first principles is challenging, as it requires solving the many-body Schrödinger equation. This work introduces a new, general ab-initio method for analyzing large quantum circuits based on lattice field theory, a tool commonly applied in nuclear and particle physics. This method is competitive with state-of-the-art techniques such as tensor networks, but avoids introducing systematic errors due to truncation of the infinite-dimensional Hilbert space associated with superconducting phases. The approach is applied to fluxonium, a specific many-component superconducting qubit with favorable qualities for quantum computation. A systematic study of the influence of impedance on fluxonium is conducted that parallels previous experimental studies, and ground capacitance effects are explored. The qubit frequency and charge noise dephasing rate are extracted from statistical analyses of charge noise, where thousands of instantiations of charge disorder in the Josephson junction array of a fixed fluxonium qubit are explicitly averaged over at the microscopic level. This is difficult to achieve with any other existing method.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly adapts lattice quantum field theory methods from nuclear/particle physics to analyze superconducting circuits, treating each circuit node as a lattice site with a U(1) field variable.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Circuit QED is formulated as a lattice field theory with path integrals and Euclidean-time correlation functions to extract the many-body spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling over the compact U(1) field space enables ab-initio computation of qubit properties without Hilbert space truncation.

conceptDetails.name: fluxonium qubit
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept: the specific many-junction superconducting qubit that serves as the primary test case for demonstrating the lattice field theory method.

conceptDetails.name: path integral formulation
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept: quantum dynamics expressed via Euclidean path integrals enabling Monte Carlo sampling over superconducting phase variables on the circuit lattice.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The method targets practical quantum computing applications, specifically characterizing superconducting qubits like fluxonium for use in scalable quantum processors.

conceptDetails.name: charge noise dephasing
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept: the qubit decoherence mechanism arising from charge disorder in Josephson junction arrays, central to the paper's phenomenological studies.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Tensor networks are the primary competing state-of-the-art method against which the lattice approach is benchmarked, with the lattice method offering advantages in Hilbert space completeness.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The lattice method enables first-principles simulation of thousands of fluxonium qubit instantiations to statistically analyze charge noise effects.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Coherent quantum phase slips — a many-body tunneling effect — are analyzed as the mechanism driving charge dispersion and dephasing in high-impedance fluxonium.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The superinductance model is discussed as an effective field theory approximation to the full many-body circuit theory, with the lattice method going beyond its limitations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Statistical analyses of charge disorder use Bayesian-style averaging over thousands of microscopic disorder instantiations to extract qubit frequency and dephasing rates.

title: The DREAMS Project: Disentangling the Impact of Halo-to-Halo Variance and Baryonic Feedback on Milky Way Dark Matter Speed Distributions
abstract: Direct detection experiments require information about the local dark matter speed distribution to produce constraints on dark matter candidates, or infer their properties in the event of a discovery. In this paper, we analyze how the uncertainty in the dark matter speed distribution near the Sun is affected by baryonic feedback, halo-to-halo variance, and halo mass. To do so, we harness the statistical power of the new DREAMS Cold Dark Matter simulation suite, which is comprised of 1024 zoom-in Milky Way-mass halos with varied initial conditions as well as cosmological and astrophysical parameters. Applying a normalizing flows emulator to these simulations, we find that the uncertainty in the local DM speed distribution is dominated by halo-to-halo variance and, to a lesser extent, uncertainty in host halo mass. Uncertainties in supernova and black hole feedback (from the IllustrisTNG model in this case) are negligible in comparison. Using the DREAMS suite, we present a state-of-the-art prediction for the DM speed distribution in the Milky Way. Although the Standard Halo Model is contained within the uncertainty of this prediction, individual galaxies may have distributions that differ from it. Lastly, we apply our DREAMS results to the XENON1T experiment and demonstrate that the astrophysical uncertainties are comparable to the experimental ones, solidifying previous results in the literature obtained with a smaller sample of simulated Milky Way-mass halos.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation

conceptDetails.name: halo-to-halo variance

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A primary goal is quantifying how astrophysical uncertainties—from halo-to-halo variance, halo mass, and baryonic feedback—affect the local DM speed distribution.

conceptDetails.name: emulation

conceptDetails.name: baryonic feedback

title: Minuet: A Diffusion Autoencoder for Compact Semantic Compression of Multi-Band Galaxy Images
abstract: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory is slated to observe nearly 20 billion galaxies during its decade-long Legacy Survey of Space and Time. The rich imaging data it collects will be an invaluable resource for probing galaxy evolution across cosmic time, characterizing the host galaxies of transient phenomena, and identifying novel populations of anomalous systems. While machine learning models have shown promise for extracting galaxy features from multi-band astronomical imaging, the large dimensionality of the learned latent space presents a challenge for mechanistic interpretability studies. In this work, we present Minuet, a low-dimensional diffusion autoencoder for multi-band galaxy imaging. Minuet is trained to reconstruct 72x72-pixel $grz$ image cutouts of 6M galaxies within $z<1$ from the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey using only five latent dimensions. By using a diffusion model conditioned on the transformer-based autoencoder's output for image reconstruction, we achieve semantically-meaningful latent representations of galaxy images while still allowing for high-fidelity, probabilistic reconstructions. We train a series of binary classifiers on Minuet's latent features to quantify their connection to morphological labels from Galaxy Zoo, and a conditional flow to produce posterior distributions of SED-derived redshifts, stellar masses, and star-formation rates. We further show the value of Minuet for nearest neighbor searches in the learned latent space. Minuet provides strong evidence for the low intrinsic dimensionality of galaxy imaging, and introduces a class of astrophysical models that produce highly compact representations for diverse science goals.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The core architecture is a diffusion autoencoder that compresses multi-band galaxy images into only five latent dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Minuet compresses 72x72x3 pixel galaxy images into 5-dimensional latent vectors, demonstrating the low intrinsic dimensionality of galaxy imaging.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The model learns semantically meaningful latent representations of galaxy morphology and physical properties without supervised training.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Minuet is trained with a self-supervised reconstruction objective, extracting generalizable features without labeled training data.

conceptDetails.name: transformers

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: A conditional normalizing flow is trained on Minuet's latent features to produce posterior distributions of redshift, stellar mass, and star-formation rates.

conceptDetails.description: Minuet's five latent dimensions are shown to correlate with distinct physical properties such as morphology, stellar mass, and star-formation rate.

title: The DREAMS Project: Disentangling the Impact of Halo-to-Halo Variance and Baryonic Feedback on Milky Way Dark Matter Density Profiles
abstract: Astrophysical searches for dark matter in the Milky Way require a reliable model for its density distribution, which in turn depends on the influence of baryonic feedback on the Galaxy. In this work, we utilize a new suite of Milky Way-mass halos from the DREAMS Project, simulated with Cold Dark Matter (CDM),to quantify the influence of baryon feedback and intrinsic halo-to-halo variance on dark matter density profiles. Our suite of 1024 halos varies over supernova and black hole feedback parameters from the IllustrisTNG model, as well as variations in two cosmological parameters. We find that Milky Way-mass dark matter density profiles in the IllustrisTNG model are largely insensitive to astrophysics and cosmology variations, with the dominant source of scatter instead arising from halo-to-halo variance. However, most of the (comparatively minor) feedback-driven variations come from the changes to supernova prescriptions. By comparing to dark matter-only simulations, we find that the strongest supernova wind energies are so effective at preventing galaxy formation that the halos are nearly entirely collisionless dark matter. Finally, regardless of physics variation, all the DREAMS halos are roughly consistent with a halo contracting adiabatically from the presence of baryons, unlike models that have bursty stellar feedback. This work represents a step toward assessing the robustness of Milky Way dark matter profiles, with direct implications for dark matter searches where systematic uncertainty in the density profile remains a major challenge.

conceptDetails.name: halo-to-halo variance

conceptDetails.description: The Navarro-Frenk-White density profile parameterization serves as the baseline model against which simulated dark matter density profiles and their scatter are characterized.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The work validates the robustness of IllustrisTNG-based dark matter density profiles by comparing hydrodynamical runs against dark matter-only simulations across wide parameter ranges.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The 1024-halo simulation suite effectively samples a multi-dimensional parameter space of feedback and cosmological parameters, enabling statistical characterization of profile scatter.

title: The DREAMS Project: Disentangling the Impact of Halo-to-Halo Variance and Baryonic Feedback on Milky Way Satellite Galaxies
abstract: We analyze the properties of satellite galaxies around 1,024 Milky Way-mass hosts from the DREAMS Project, simulated within a $Λ$CDM cosmology. Utilizing the TNG galaxy-formation model, the DREAMS simulations incorporate both baryonic physics and cosmological uncertainties for a large sample of galaxies with diverse environments and formation histories. We investigate the relative impact of the physical uncertainty from the galaxy-formation model on predicted satellite properties using four metrics: the satellite stellar mass function, radial distribution, inner slope of dark matter density profile, and stellar half-light radius. We compare these predictions to observations from the SAGA Survey and the DREAMS N-body simulations and find that uncertainties from baryonic physics modeling are subdominant to the scatter arising from halo-to-halo variance. Where baryonic modeling does affect satellites, the supernova wind energy has the largest effect on the satellite properties that we investigate. Specifically, increased supernova wind energy suppresses the stellar mass of satellites and results in more extended stellar half-light radii. The adopted wind speed has only a minor impact, and other astrophysical and cosmological parameters show no measurable effect. Our findings highlight the robustness of satellite properties against uncertainties in baryonic physics modeling.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The DREAMS Project runs 1,024 Milky Way-mass host simulations within ΛCDM cosmology using the TNG galaxy-formation model to study satellite galaxy properties.

conceptDetails.name: halo-to-halo variance
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to this paper: the scatter in satellite properties arising from diverse host dark matter halo formation histories, shown to dominate over baryonic modeling uncertainties.

conceptDetails.name: baryonic feedback
conceptDetails.description: The impact of supernova-driven winds and other baryonic physics on satellite galaxy stellar masses, sizes, and dark matter profiles is a primary focus of the analysis.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically quantifies physical uncertainties from baryonic physics modeling versus halo-to-halo variance across four satellite property metrics.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the inner slope of dark matter density profiles of satellite galaxies and how baryonic feedback and halo variance affect dark matter structure.

conceptDetails.name: satellite stellar mass function
conceptDetails.description: One of four key observational metrics used to compare DREAMS simulation predictions against SAGA Survey data and quantify baryonic versus variance effects.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Stellar feedback processes, particularly supernova wind energy and wind speed, are investigated for their effects on satellite stellar mass functions and half-light radii.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: DREAMS uses large simulation suites to infer which physical parameters (supernova wind energy, wind speed, cosmological parameters) drive satellite galaxy property variations.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The DREAMS suite serves as a large-scale emulator for exploring parameter space of galaxy formation models to predict observable satellite properties.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Statistical comparison of predicted satellite properties against SAGA Survey observations employs Bayesian-style uncertainty decomposition between model and variance contributions.

title: Refining Heuristic Predictors of Fractional Chern Insulators using Machine Learning
abstract: We develop an interpretable, data-driven framework to quantify how single-particle band geometry governs the stability of fractional Chern insulators (FCIs). Using large-scale exact diagonalization, we evaluate an FCI metric that yields a continuous spectral measure of FCI stability across parameter space. We then train Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) -- a recently developed interpretable neural architecture -- to regress this metric from two band-geometric descriptors: the trace violation $T$ and the Berry curvature fluctuations $σ_B$. Applied to spinless fermions at filling $ν=1/3$ in models on the checkerboard and kagome lattices, our approach yields compact analytical formulas that predict FCI stability with over $>80 \%$ accuracy in both regression and classification tasks, and remain reliable even in data-scarce regimes. The learned relations reveal model-dependent trends, clarifying the limits of Landau-level-mimicking heuristics. Our framework provides a general method for extracting simple, phenomenological "laws" that connect many-body phase stability to chosen physical descriptors, enabling rapid hypothesis formation and targeted design of quantum phases.

conceptDetails.name: fractional chern insulators
conceptDetails.description: The central physical phenomenon: lattice analogs of fractional quantum Hall states arising from partially filled topological Chern bands.

conceptDetails.name: kolmogorov-arnold networks

conceptDetails.name: band geometry

conceptDetails.name: regression

conceptDetails.name: exact diagonalization
conceptDetails.description: The many-body computational method used to generate large-scale training data by directly computing low-energy spectra of the FCI Hamiltonian.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes the stability boundary of topologically ordered FCI phases as a function of band-geometric parameters.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classification of parameter-space points as FCI or non-FCI phases, achieving >80% accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: FCI ground states on torus geometries are computed via exact diagonalization and characterized through quasi-degeneracy and flux insertion.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: KAN models serve as efficient surrogates for expensive exact diagonalization, enabling rapid prediction of FCI stability across parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: The entanglement spectrum and particle-cut entanglement are used as signatures to identify FCI ground states in exact diagonalization.

title: Test-time scaling of diffusions with flow maps
abstract: A common recipe to improve diffusion models at test-time so that samples score highly against a user-specified reward is to introduce the gradient of the reward into the dynamics of the diffusion itself. This procedure is often ill posed, as user-specified rewards are usually only well defined on the data distribution at the end of generation. While common workarounds to this problem are to use a denoiser to estimate what a sample would have been at the end of generation, we propose a simple solution to this problem by working directly with a flow map. By exploiting a relationship between the flow map and velocity field governing the instantaneous transport, we construct an algorithm, Flow Map Trajectory Tilting (FMTT), which provably performs better ascent on the reward than standard test-time methods involving the gradient of the reward. The approach can be used to either perform exact sampling via importance weighting or principled search that identifies local maximizers of the reward-tilted distribution. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach against other look-ahead techniques, and show how the flow map enables engagement with complicated reward functions that make possible new forms of image editing, e.g. by interfacing with vision language models.

conceptDetails.name: flow map trajectory tilting
conceptDetails.description: Novel algorithm introduced in this paper that uses the flow map's look-ahead capability for provably better reward-guided generation.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models

conceptDetails.name: test-time scaling
conceptDetails.description: The central problem addressed: improving diffusion model outputs at inference time without retraining.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: The flow-based transport framework underpinning the flow map methodology and velocity field formulation.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The objective of FMTT is to sample from a reward-tilted distribution, performing principled ascent on user-specified reward functions.

conceptDetails.name: reward-tilted distribution
conceptDetails.description: The target distribution proportional to the base generative distribution reweighted by the exponentiated reward, which FMTT samples from.

title: Fermions and Supersymmetry in Neural Network Field Theories
abstract: We introduce fermionic neural network field theories via Grassmann-valued neural networks. Free theories are obtained by a generalization of the Central Limit Theorem to Grassmann variables. This enables the realization of the free Dirac spinor at infinite width and a four fermion interaction at finite width. Yukawa couplings are introduced by breaking the statistical independence of the output weights for the fermionic and bosonic fields. A large class of interacting supersymmetric quantum mechanics and field theory models are introduced by super-affine transformations on the input that realize a superspace formalism.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper establishes the neural network field theory correspondence for fermionic theories, realizing free Dirac spinors and interacting field theories from Grassmann-valued neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: grassmann neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Novel neural networks with Grassmann-valued weights or post-activations that encode fermionic anticommuting statistics, the core new contribution of this paper.

conceptDetails.name: neural network field theory
conceptDetails.description: The framework connecting neural network architectures and parameter distributions to quantum field theories, extended here from scalar to fermionic and supersymmetric theories.

conceptDetails.name: supersymmetric field theory
conceptDetails.description: A large class of interacting supersymmetric quantum mechanics and field theory models are realized via super-affine transformations on neural network inputs implementing superspace formalism.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian processes emerge as infinite-width limits of neural networks, and the paper generalizes the Central Limit Theorem to Grassmann random variables to establish Gaussian limits for fermionic theories.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Section 5.4 analyzes supersymmetry breaking in neural network field theories, studying conditions under which SUSY is spontaneously broken.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Superalgebra and superspace formalism via super-affine transformations are used to construct the supersymmetric neural network theories introduced in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Wilsonian RG and exact renormalization group flow schemes are discussed as part of the broader NN-FT correspondence framework this work extends.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling of randomly initialized neural networks is cited as a method for computing correlators and studying interacting theories via the parameter space partition function.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: Conformal neural network field theories constructed via the embedding formalism are referenced as part of the scalar NN-FT results that this paper's fermionic extension builds upon.

title: Large Language Model Driven Analysis of General Coordinates Network (GCN) Circulars
abstract: The General Coordinates Network (GCN) is NASA's time-domain and multi-messenger alert system. GCN distributes two data products - automated ``Notices,'' and human-generated ``Circulars,'' that report the observations of high-energy and multi-messenger astronomical transients. The flexible and non-structured format of GCN Circulars, comprising of more than 40500 Circulars accumulated over three decades, makes it challenging to manually extract observational information, such as redshift or observed wavebands. In this work, we employ large language models (LLMs) to facilitate the automated parsing of transient reports. We develop a neural topic modeling pipeline with open-source tools for the automatic clustering and summarization of astrophysical topics in the Circulars database. Using neural topic modeling and contrastive fine-tuning, we classify Circulars based on their observation wavebands and messengers. Additionally, we separate gravitational wave (GW) event clusters and their electromagnetic (EM) counterparts from the Circulars database. Finally, using the open-source Mistral model, we implement a system to automatically extract gamma-ray burst (GRB) redshift information from the Circulars archive, without the need for any training. Evaluation against the manually curated Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory GRB table shows that our simple system, with the help of prompt-tuning, output parsing, and retrieval augmented generation (RAG), can achieve an accuracy of 97.2 % for redshift-containing Circulars. Our neural search enhanced RAG pipeline accurately retrieved 96.8 % of redshift circulars from the manually curated database. Our study demonstrates the potential of LLMs, to automate and enhance astronomical text mining, and provides a foundation work for future advances in transient alert analysis.

conceptDetails.name: astronomical text mining
