conceptDetails.name: retrieval augmented generation
conceptDetails.description: A neural search enhanced RAG pipeline is used to retrieve and extract GRB redshift information from Circulars with 96.8% accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Neural topic modeling clusters GCN Circulars into astrophysical topic groups for automatic summarization and classification.

conceptDetails.name: neural topic modeling
conceptDetails.description: A novel neural topic modeling pipeline clusters and summarizes astrophysical topics across 40,500+ GCN Circulars without manual labeling.

conceptDetails.name: classification

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: Contrastive fine-tuning is applied to improve waveband and messenger classification of GCN Circulars.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Neural sentence embeddings form the backbone of the topic modeling and semantic search pipeline over the Circulars corpus.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Open-source LLMs are fine-tuned with contrastive objectives and prompt-tuning to adapt them to astrophysical text extraction tasks.

conceptDetails.description: The paper builds an end-to-end automated pipeline for parsing, clustering, classifying, and extracting information from the GCN archive.

title: Hydrogen-Poor Superluminous Supernovae in the Nebular Phase: Spectral Diversity Due to Ejecta Ionization as a Probe of the Power Source
abstract: We present a large sample of 39 nebular-phase optical spectra of 25 hydrogen-poor superluminous supernovae (SLSNe-I) and jointly analyze them with previously published spectra of 12 events. We measure the properties of key emission features, namely those at 6300, 7300, and 7774 angstroms (associated with [O I], [Ca II]/[O II], and O I, respectively), and find that SLSNe exhibit much wider spectral diversity than normal SNe Ic, primarily in the line ratio $L_{7300}/L_{6300}$, which is highly sensitive to ejecta ionization. Some events exhibit weak [O I] and a clear [O II] contribution to the 7300 angstrom feature, enhancing the ratio, along with [O III] lines at 4363 and 5007 angstroms. Other SLSNe show weak or no lines of ionized oxygen. Moreover, we find that the population exhibits decreasing $L_{7300}/L_{6300}$ over time, while a few outliers instead display sustained high or increasing ratios for extended periods. The ratio $L_{7300}/L_{6300}$ is also correlated with the rise and decline times of the light curves, with slower events exhibiting higher ionization, the first robust connection between early light curve and late-time spectral properties, likely due to the magnetar's impact: slower-evolving SLSNe are generally powered by engines with longer spin-down timescales, which deposit more energy at later phases. Among the events with decreasing $L_{7300}/L_{6300}$, SLSNe with high ionization are on average powered by magnetars with higher thermalized spin-down power, a correlation that is most significant for events with $M_{\rm ej}\lesssim12$ M$_{\odot}$. The ionization in the outliers with increasing $L_{7300}/L_{6300}$ may be due to late CSM interaction. $L_{7300}/L_{6300}$ and its evolution are therefore key diagnostics of SLSN engines and progenitor mass loss.

conceptDetails.name: ejecta ionization
conceptDetails.description: The ionization state of supernova ejecta as traced by oxygen emission line ratios, serving as a probe of the central engine's energy deposition.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses specifically on hydrogen-poor superluminous supernovae (SLSNe-I), analyzing their spectral diversity and distinguishing them from normal SNe Ic.

conceptDetails.name: nebular spectroscopy
conceptDetails.description: Late-phase optical spectroscopy when ejecta are optically thin, revealing inner ejecta properties and constraining the central engine through emission line diagnostics.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Emission line ratios (L7300/L6300) are used as key spectral diagnostics to probe ejecta ionization state and distinguish power sources.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution

title: Learning to Validate Generative Models: a Goodness-of-Fit Approach
abstract: Generative models are increasingly central to scientific workflows, yet their systematic use and interpretation require a proper understanding of their limitations through rigorous validation. Classic approaches struggle with scalability, statistical power, or interpretability when applied to high-dimensional data, making it difficult to certify the reliability of these models in realistic, high-dimensional scientific settings. Here, we propose the use of the New Physics Learning Machine (NPLM), a learning-based approach to goodness-of-fit testing inspired by the Neyman--Pearson construction, to test generative networks trained on high-dimensional scientific data. We demonstrate the performance of NPLM for validation in two benchmark cases: generative models trained on mixtures of Gaussian models with increasing dimensionality, and a public end-to-end model, known as FlowSim, developed to generate high-energy physics collision events. We demonstrate that the NPLM can serve as a powerful validation method while also providing a means to diagnose sub-optimally modeled regions of the data.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: The central methodology of the paper, using NPLM to perform statistical goodness-of-fit tests to validate generative models against reference distributions.

conceptDetails.name: model validation

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: NPLM is grounded in the Neyman-Pearson likelihood-ratio construction, estimating density ratios between reference and data samples to build the test statistic.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing

conceptDetails.name: generative models

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Used both as the generative model architecture under validation (FlowSim, mixture-of-Gaussians NFs) and as a benchmark test case throughout the experiments.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The specific NPLM implementation studied in this paper is kernel-based, used to estimate likelihood ratios without explicit density estimation.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: NPLM was originally developed for new physics discovery (anomaly detection in collider data) and is here repurposed for generative model diagnostics.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation

conceptDetails.name: jet physics

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling

conceptDetails.name: interpretability

title: Distance by de-correlation: Computing distance with heterogeneous grid cells
abstract: Encoding the distance between locations in space is essential for accurate navigation. Grid cells, a functional class of neurons in medial entorhinal cortex, are believed to support this computation. However, existing theories of how populations of grid cells code distance rely on complex coding schemes, with assumptions that may not be met by anatomical constraints. Inspired by recent work finding grid cells to have small, but robust heterogeneity in their grid properties, we hypothesize that distance coding can be achieved by a simple de-correlation of population activity. We develop a mathematical theory for describing this de-correlation in one-dimension, showing that its predictions are consistent with simulations of noisy grid cells. Our simulations highlight a non-intuitive prediction of such a distance by de-correlation framework. Namely, that some further distances are better encoded than some nearer distances. We find evidence of this "sweet spot" in previously published rodent behavioral experiments and demonstrate that a decoder which estimates distance from the de-correlation of populations of simulated noisy grid cells leads to a similar pattern of errors. Finally, by simulating noisy grid cells in two-dimensions, we find that there exists a trade-off between the range of distances that can be encoded by de-correlation of population activity and the distinguishability of different distances, which is controlled by the amount of variability in grid properties. We show that the previously observed average amount of grid property variability strikes a balance between the two, enabling the encoding of distances up to several meters. Our work provides new insight on how grid cells can underlie the coding of distance, without the assumptions previously needed, and why grid cells may have small amounts of heterogeneity in their grid properties.

conceptDetails.name: grid cell coding
conceptDetails.description: The primary subject of the paper: how populations of grid cells in the medial entorhinal cortex encode spatial distance through population activity de-correlation.

conceptDetails.name: population vector correlation
conceptDetails.description: The central computational primitive — the correlation between population activity vectors at different locations — used to implement the distance-by-de-correlation coding scheme.

conceptDetails.name: neural heterogeneity
conceptDetails.description: Small but robust variability in grid spacing and orientation across cells within a single module, which the paper shows is sufficient to enable monotonic distance encoding.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Noisy grid cell activity is modeled as a stochastic process; the mathematical theory quantifies how noise interacts with heterogeneity to affect distance coding fidelity.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how neural populations geometrically represent distance, connecting population vector geometry to the fidelity of distance encoding.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Fourier/autocorrelation analysis is used to develop the analytical theory of how population vector correlation decays as a function of distance and grid property variability.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Decoding distance from the de-correlation of simulated grid cell populations is an inverse problem, and the paper evaluates decoder error patterns against rodent behavioral data.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Simulations of many noisy grid cells in 1D and 2D are used to validate the analytical theory and explore the trade-off between encoding range and distance distinguishability.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning

title: Spectral Diversity in Type Ibn Supernovae and the Large Host Offset of SN2024acyl
abstract: In this paper, we first present observations of SN~2024acyl, a normal Type Ibn supernova with a large projected offset ($\sim$35~kpc) from its host galaxy. The low star-formation rate measured at the explosion site raises the possibility that the progenitor of SN~2024acyl may not have been a massive star. We then examine, more broadly, the spectral diversity of Type Ibn supernovae around 20--35 days after peak brightness and identify two distinct groups: Group I, which shows bluer rest-frame optical color and narrower He~I emission lines; and Group II, which shows redder rest-frame optical color and broader He~I lines. Group~I also tends to show higher peak luminosities. The diversity we identify appears to be closely connected to the diversity observed around peak and to persist into late phases ($>80$ days after peak). Given its redder color and broader He~I lines, we classify SN~2024acyl as belonging to Group II. Based on the current dataset, we find no clear connection between this spectral diversity and either the host environments of Type Ibn SNe or their pre-explosion activity. The observed diversity in Type Ibn SNe likely reflects differences in circumstellar material properties and/or explosion energetics. These differences could result from a range of progenitor properties, such as different helium star mass, orbital period and companion type if they are in binary systems, and may indicate fundamentally diverse progenitors. Whether a continuous distribution exists between the two groups remains to be determined and will require further data to explore.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper classifies SN 2024acyl as a Type Ibn supernova and further subdivides the Type Ibn class into two spectral groups based on color and He I line width.

conceptDetails.name: spectral diversity

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar material interaction
conceptDetails.description: Type Ibn SNe are powered by ejecta interaction with He-rich circumstellar material, and differences in CSM properties are invoked to explain the observed spectral diversity.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The spectral diversity and host offset of Type Ibn SNe are interpreted in terms of progenitor properties including helium star mass, binary systems, and pre-explosion mass loss.

conceptDetails.name: host galaxy environment
conceptDetails.description: The star-formation rate and host offset of SN 2024acyl are analyzed to constrain the progenitor population and rule out standard massive-star channels.

conceptDetails.name: light curve analysis
conceptDetails.description: Multi-band photometric light curves are used to characterize peak luminosity, color evolution, and temporal behavior of Type Ibn SNe across both spectral groups.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies two distinct spectral groups among Type Ibn SNe based on photometric and spectroscopic properties, effectively performing an observational clustering of the population.

conceptDetails.name: binary stellar progenitors
conceptDetails.description: Binary He-star systems are proposed as a key progenitor channel for Type Ibn SNe, with orbital parameters influencing pre-explosion mass loss and observed spectral diversity.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection

conceptDetails.name: signal detection

title: Sparse, self-organizing ensembles of local kernels detect rare statistical anomalies
abstract: Modern artificial intelligence has revolutionized our ability to extract rich and versatile data representations across scientific disciplines. Yet, the statistical properties of these representations remain poorly controlled, causing misspecified anomaly detection (AD) methods to falter. Weak or rare signals can remain hidden within the apparent regularity of normal data, creating a gap in our ability to detect and interpret anomalies. We examine this gap and identify a set of structural desiderata for detection methods operating under minimal prior information: sparsity, to enforce parsimony; locality, to preserve geometric sensitivity; and competition, to promote efficient allocation of model capacity. These principles define a class of self-organizing local kernels that adaptively partition the representation space around regions of statistical imbalance. As an instantiation of these principles, we introduce SparKer, a sparse ensemble of Gaussian kernels trained within a semi-supervised Neyman--Pearson framework to locally model the likelihood ratio between a sample that may contain anomalies and a nominal, anomaly-free reference. We provide theoretical insights into the mechanisms that drive detection and self-organization in the proposed model, and demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach on realistic high-dimensional problems of scientific discovery, open-world novelty detection, intrusion detection, and generative-model validation. Our applications span both the natural- and computer-science domains. We demonstrate that ensembles containing only a handful of kernels can identify statistically significant anomalous locations within representation spaces of thousands of dimensions, underscoring both the interpretability, efficiency and scalability of the proposed approach.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central problem addressed by this paper, specifically targeting rare, in-distribution anomalies that manifest as subtle density distortions in representation space.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: SparKer is built on an ensemble of self-organizing Gaussian kernels that adaptively partition representation space around anomalous regions.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: SparKer models the log-density ratio between inspected data and an anomaly-free reference within a Neyman-Pearson framework to score anomalies.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity is a core design principle of SparKer, enforcing parsimony so that a handful of kernels can detect anomalies in thousands of dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: self-organizing kernels
conceptDetails.description: A novel contribution: kernels that autonomously migrate to regions of statistical imbalance during training via a competition mechanism, requiring no predefined placement.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: SparKer uses a sparse ensemble of local Gaussian kernels whose competition mechanism enables efficient allocation of model capacity.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: SparKer is trained in a semi-supervised Neyman-Pearson framework using a labeled anomaly-free reference alongside potentially anomalous unlabeled data.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The Neyman-Pearson statistical framework underpins SparKer's detection criterion, enabling principled significance testing for identified anomalies.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Sparse kernel components provide decomposable anomaly scores that isolate distinct anomalous regions, enabling geometry-aware interpretation of detected signals.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning

conceptDetails.name: novelty detection
conceptDetails.description: Open-world novelty detection is one of the primary application domains demonstrated for SparKer across computer science benchmarks.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: SparKer is applied to validate generative models by detecting statistical discrepancies between generated and real data distributions.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Scientific discovery in high-energy physics is a key application, where SparKer detects rare anomalous signals in particle physics data representations.

title: The Compressed 3D Lyman-Alpha Forest Bispectrum
abstract: Cosmological studies of the Lyman-Alpha (Lya) forest typically constrain parameters using two-point statistics. However, higher-order statistics, such as the three-point function (or its Fourier counterpart, the bispectrum) offer additional information and help break the degeneracy between the mean flux and power spectrum amplitude, albeit at a significant computational cost. To address this, we extend an existing highly informative compression of the bispectrum, the skew spectra, to the Lya forest. We derive the tree-level bispectrum of Lya forest fluctuations in the framework of effective field theory (EFT) directly in redshift space and validate our methodology on synthetic Lya forest data. We measure the anisotropic cross-spectra between the transmitted flux fraction and all quadratic operators arising in the bispectrum, yielding a set of 26 skew spectra. Using idealized 3D Gaussian smoothing (R=10 Mpc/h), we find good agreement (1-2 sigma level based on the statistical errors of the mocks) with the theoretical tree-level bispectrum prediction for monopole and quadrupole up to k <= 0.17 h/Mpc. To enable the cosmological analysis of Lya forest data from the currently observing Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI), where we cannot do 3D smoothing, we use a line-of-sight smoothing and introduce a new statistic, the shifted skew spectra. These probe non-squeezed bispectrum triangles and avoid locally applying quadratic operators to the field by displacing one copy of the field in the radial direction. Using a fixed displacement of 40 Mpc/h (and line-of-sight smoothing of 10 Mpc/h) yields a similar agreement with the theory prediction. For the special case of correlating the squared (and displaced) field with the original one, we analytically forward model the window function making this approach readily applicable to DESI data.

conceptDetails.name: skew spectra
conceptDetails.description: Cross-power spectra of quadratic operators applied to the transmitted flux fraction with the original field, yielding 26 compressed bispectrum statistics that depend on a single wavenumber.

conceptDetails.name: lyman-alpha forest
conceptDetails.description: The intergalactic medium absorption signal in quasar spectra used as a high-redshift large-scale structure tracer; the primary observable analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives the tree-level bispectrum of Lyman-alpha forest fluctuations directly in redshift space using the EFT of large-scale structure framework.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Skew spectra — cross-power spectra between the transmitted flux fraction and quadratic operators — are the central statistical tool for compressing bispectrum information.

conceptDetails.name: shifted skew spectra
conceptDetails.description: A new statistic introduced to probe non-squeezed bispectrum triangles via radial displacement of one field copy, designed for line-of-sight-only smoothing in DESI data.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The methodology is validated on AbacusSummit N-body simulation mocks and synthetic second-order perturbation theory Lya forest fields.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Skew spectra serve as computationally efficient proxy statistics for the full bispectrum, reducing cost while retaining most of its cosmological information.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: The developed statistics target data from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI), with window function treatment enabling direct application to DESI observations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The skew spectra formalism is motivated by its role in breaking parameter degeneracies and tightening cosmological posterior constraints beyond two-point statistics.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: N-body simulations (AbacusSummit) provide the mock Lya forest data used to validate the theoretical bispectrum predictions.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Theoretical tree-level bispectrum predictions are benchmarked against two mock datasets to validate the skew spectra pipeline before application to real survey data.

title: AutoSciDACT: Automated Scientific Discovery through Contrastive Embedding and Hypothesis Testing
abstract: Novelty detection in large scientific datasets faces two key challenges: the noisy and high-dimensional nature of experimental data, and the necessity of making statistically robust statements about any observed outliers. While there is a wealth of literature on anomaly detection via dimensionality reduction, most methods do not produce outputs compatible with quantifiable claims of scientific discovery. In this work we directly address these challenges, presenting the first step towards a unified pipeline for novelty detection adapted for the rigorous statistical demands of science. We introduce AutoSciDACT (Automated Scientific Discovery with Anomalous Contrastive Testing), a general-purpose pipeline for detecting novelty in scientific data. AutoSciDACT begins by creating expressive low-dimensional data representations using a contrastive pre-training, leveraging the abundance of high-quality simulated data in many scientific domains alongside expertise that can guide principled data augmentation strategies. These compact embeddings then enable an extremely sensitive machine learning-based two-sample test using the New Physics Learning Machine (NPLM) framework, which identifies and statistically quantifies deviations in observed data relative to a reference distribution (null hypothesis). We perform experiments across a range of astronomical, physical, biological, image, and synthetic datasets, demonstrating strong sensitivity to small injections of anomalous data across all domains.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: Core pre-training method used to learn expressive low-dimensional embeddings from high-dimensional scientific data, leveraging simulations and data augmentation to define positive/negative pairs.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central task of AutoSciDACT: identifying statistically significant deviations in observed data relative to a reference (background) distribution across multiple scientific domains.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The NPLM framework performs rigorous statistical hypothesis testing to quantify the significance of observed anomalies beyond simple outlier flagging.

conceptDetails.name: novelty detection
conceptDetails.description: The explicit scientific goal of the pipeline — detecting genuinely new phenomena in large, noisy datasets with statistically robust claims of discovery.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Contrastive pre-training reduces potentially thousands of input dimensions to a compact embedding space that enables sensitive downstream statistical testing.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: AutoSciDACT automates the scientific method's steps of data reduction, hypothesis formulation, and statistical testing into a domain-agnostic, end-to-end pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The low-dimensional representations produced by contrastive pre-training that serve as input to the NPLM two-sample test in the discovery phase.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: NPLM provides a machine learning-based goodness-of-fit test that outperforms classical GOF tests in high-dimensional settings and allows incorporation of systematic uncertainties.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: AutoSciDACT targets distribution-level OOD deviations rather than individual OOD instances, framing novelty as a distributional shift relative to a null hypothesis.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: NPLM is built on a likelihood ratio framework to identify and score regions of data space that deviate most from the reference distribution.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Domain-expert-guided augmentation strategies define positive pairs for contrastive pre-training, encoding scientific knowledge into the learned representations.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Self-supervised contrastive methods (SimCLR, MoCo, Barlow Twins) form the basis for the pre-training stage, enabling representation learning without labeled data.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The NPLM framework originates from high-energy physics searches for beyond-Standard-Model signals, and AutoSciDACT generalizes this approach across scientific domains.

title: On Uncertainty Calibration for Equivariant Functions
abstract: Data-sparse settings such as robotic manipulation, molecular physics, and galaxy morphology classification are some of the hardest domains for deep learning. For these problems, equivariant networks can help improve modeling across undersampled parts of the input space, and uncertainty estimation can guard against overconfidence. However, until now, the relationships between equivariance and model confidence, and more generally equivariance and model calibration, has yet to be studied. Since traditional classification and regression error terms show up in the definitions of calibration error, it is natural to suspect that previous work can be used to help understand the relationship between equivariance and calibration error. In this work, we present a theory relating equivariance to uncertainty estimation. By proving lower and upper bounds on uncertainty calibration errors (ECE and ENCE) under various equivariance conditions, we elucidate the generalization limits of equivariant models and illustrate how symmetry mismatch can result in miscalibration in both classification and regression. We complement our theoretical framework with numerical experiments that clarify the relationship between equivariance and uncertainty using a variety of real and simulated datasets, and we comment on trends with symmetry mismatch, group size, and aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central focus: deriving lower and upper bounds on Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Expected Normalized Calibration Error (ENCE) for equivariant models in both classification and regression settings.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The primary model class studied; networks that encode group symmetries into their architecture, whose calibration properties are theoretically characterized throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops theory and experiments relating equivariance to model confidence, epistemic uncertainty, and aleatoric uncertainty estimation.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry mismatch
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the condition where the symmetry group encoded in the model does not match the true symmetry of the data-generating process, shown to increase calibration error.

conceptDetails.name: calibration error bounds
conceptDetails.description: Novel theoretical contribution: proved lower and upper bounds on ECE and ENCE as functions of equivariance conditions and group size.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical backbone of the paper; symmetry groups, group actions, quotient spaces, and group representations are used to derive calibration error bounds.

conceptDetails.name: aleatoric bleed
conceptDetails.description: Novel metric introduced by the authors to quantify a model's ability to distinguish aleatoric from epistemic uncertainty; a lower bound is derived for equivariant models.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The property of equivariant models that constrains their output space via group orbits, directly bounding calibration error through preimage structure.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The broader research field encompassing equivariant and invariant architectures for structured data, within which this paper's theoretical contributions sit.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: One of the two main settings analyzed; the paper generalizes ENCE to multivariate predictions and derives its bounds under equivariance conditions.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The second main setting; ECE bounds are proved for equivariant classifiers, with tighter bounds in the invariance limit.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Evidential regression and Bayesian methods are used in experiments to study how equivariance affects the separation of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses model reliability under symmetry mismatch, relevant to safe deployment of equivariant models in safety-critical applications.

title: Diffusion Autoencoders with Perceivers for Long, Irregular and Multimodal Astronomical Sequences
abstract: Self-supervised learning has become a central strategy for representation learning, but the majority of architectures used for encoding data have only been validated on regularly-sampled inputs such as images, audios. and videos. In many scientific domains, data instead arrive as long, irregular, and multimodal sequences. To extract semantic information from these data, we introduce the Diffusion Autoencoder with Perceivers (daep). daep tokenizes heterogeneous measurements, compresses them with a Perceiver encoder, and reconstructs them with a Perceiver-IO diffusion decoder, enabling scalable learning in diverse data settings. To benchmark the daep architecture, we adapt the masked autoencoder to a Perceiver encoder/decoder design, and establish a strong baseline (maep) in the same architectural family as daep. Across diverse spectroscopic and photometric astronomical datasets, daep achieves lower reconstruction errors, produces more discriminative latent spaces, and better preserves fine-scale structure than both VAE and maep baselines. These results establish daep as an effective framework for scientific domains where data arrives as irregular, heterogeneous sequences.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The core training paradigm used in daep, enabling representation learning from unlabeled astronomical data without manual annotations.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The generative backbone of daep's decoder, used to reconstruct irregular sequences via a conditional denoising process guided by the latent encoding.

conceptDetails.name: perceiver architectures
conceptDetails.description: The key neural architecture enabling daep to encode and decode variable-length, irregularly-sampled multimodal sequences through cross-attention with learned latent arrays.

conceptDetails.name: irregular time series encoding
conceptDetails.description: Novel capability of daep to natively handle non-uniformly sampled multimodal sequences through tokenization and Perceiver cross-attention.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The overall architectural framework of daep, combining an encoder for compression and a diffusion decoder for reconstruction of astronomical sequences.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The primary goal of daep: learning discriminative, semantically structured latent spaces from heterogeneous astronomical data.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: The mechanism underlying the Perceiver encoder and Perceiver-IO decoder, enabling flexible cross-attention between input tokens and a fixed-size latent bottleneck.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical foundation of daep's diffusion decoder, which learns a noise prediction (score) function conditioned on the latent encoding.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: daep learns a compact latent bottleneck that compresses long, high-dimensional astronomical sequences into structured low-dimensional representations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key design goal of daep, which scales linearly with input length via the Perceiver architecture to handle large-scale astronomical survey data.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A key baseline model compared against daep to demonstrate improvements in reconstruction quality and latent space structure.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: One of the downstream astronomical tasks used to evaluate daep's learned latent representations on spectroscopic and photometric data.

title: FINDER: Feature Inference on Noisy Datasets using Eigenspace Residuals
abstract: ''Noisy'' datasets (regimes with low signal to noise ratios, small sample sizes, faulty data collection, etc) remain a key research frontier for classification methods with both theoretical and practical implications. We introduce FINDER, a rigorous framework for analyzing generic classification problems, with tailored algorithms for noisy datasets. FINDER incorporates fundamental stochastic analysis ideas into the feature learning and inference stages to optimally account for the randomness inherent to all empirical datasets. We construct ''stochastic features'' by first viewing empirical datasets as realizations from an underlying random field (without assumptions on its exact distribution) and then mapping them to appropriate Hilbert spaces. The Kosambi-Karhunen-Loéve expansion (KLE) breaks these stochastic features into computable irreducible components, which allow classification over noisy datasets via an eigen-decomposition: data from different classes resides in distinct regions, identified by analyzing the spectrum of the associated operators. We validate FINDER on several challenging, data-deficient scientific domains, producing state of the art breakthroughs in: (i) Alzheimer's Disease stage classification, (ii) Remote sensing detection of deforestation. We end with a discussion on when FINDER is expected to outperform existing methods, its failure modes, and other limitations.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: FINDER is fundamentally a classification framework, providing rigorous binary and multi-class classification for noisy, data-deficient datasets.

conceptDetails.name: kosambi-karhunen-loève expansion
conceptDetails.description: A generalized KLE theorem is the mathematical backbone of FINDER, enabling Fourier-like decomposition of stochastic features into computable irreducible components.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The core of FINDER uses eigen-decomposition of covariance operators to separate data classes into distinct spectral regions.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: FINDER decomposes stochastic features via eigenvalue decomposition of covariance operators to identify class-discriminative components.

conceptDetails.name: noisy dataset learning
conceptDetails.description: FINDER is specifically tailored for data-deficient regimes with low SNR, small sample sizes, and faulty collection — a novel algorithmic contribution.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Datasets are modeled as realizations of an underlying random field, embedding stochastic analysis throughout the feature learning pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The construction of 'stochastic features' by composing random field realizations with Hilbert space mappings is the central contribution of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The Kosambi-Karhunen-Loève expansion reduces high-dimensional stochastic features to computable irreducible components, analogous to optimal PCA.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: FINDER is explicitly designed to be robust to low signal-to-noise ratios, small sample sizes, and faulty data collection.

conceptDetails.name: functional data analysis
conceptDetails.description: FINDER operates in the FDA paradigm, treating data as functions in Hilbert spaces rather than fixed-dimensional vectors, enabling analysis when F >> N.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: FINDER maps datasets to Hilbert spaces via kernel-like feature maps (stochastic features) to achieve class separability.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: By treating datasets as random field realizations without distributional assumptions, FINDER inherently accounts for data uncertainty in feature construction.

title: An integrated neural wavefunction solver for spinful Fermi systems
abstract: We present an approach to solving the ground state of Fermi systems that contain spin or other discrete degrees of freedom in addition to continuous coordinates. The approach combines a Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling for energy estimation that we adapted to cover the extended configuration space with a transformer-based wavefunction to represent fermionic states. This sampling is necessary when the Hamiltonian contains explicit spin dependence and, for spin-independent Hamiltonians, we find that the inclusion of spin updates leads to faster convergence to an antiferromagnetic ground state. A transformer with both continuous position and discrete spin as inputs achieves universal approximation to spinful generalized orbitals. We validate the method on a range of two-dimensional material problems: a two-dimensional electron gas with Rashba spin-orbit coupling, a noncollinear spin texture, and a quantum antiferromagnet in a honeycomb moiré potential.

conceptDetails.name: neural variational monte carlo
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends NN-VMC to spinful Fermi systems by combining a transformer wavefunction with a spinful MCMC sampler for ground-state optimization.

conceptDetails.name: spinful fermionic ansatz
conceptDetails.description: A novel wavefunction parameterization that jointly handles continuous spatial and discrete spin degrees of freedom to universally approximate generalized Slater-determinant orbitals.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A transformer neural network with attention mechanisms processes both continuous particle positions and discrete spin inputs to represent fermionic wavefunctions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling is extended to cover combined continuous-coordinate and discrete-spin configuration spaces for energy estimation in variational quantum calculations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The neural wavefunction ansatz represents many-body fermionic ground states with explicit spin degrees of freedom, achieving universal approximation of generalized orbitals.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Attention layers couple the position and spin processing streams within the transformer architecture to capture spin-coordinate correlations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The method is validated on two-dimensional quantum matter problems including a Rashba spin-orbit coupled electron gas, noncollinear spin textures, and a honeycomb moiré antiferromagnet.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The variational energy is minimized with respect to Hamiltonians containing explicit spin-dependent terms such as Rashba spin-orbit coupling and exchange interactions.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The wavefunction ansatz enforces fermionic antisymmetry via determinants (Pauli principle) while the MCMC procedure can be constrained to fixed magnetization sectors.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The solver captures antiferromagnetic ordering and noncollinear spin textures emerging from electron-electron interactions in two-dimensional systems.

conceptDetails.name: moiré materials
conceptDetails.description: The method is applied to electrons in a honeycomb moiré potential, demonstrating its capability to resolve antiferromagnetic ground states in twisted two-dimensional materials.

title: Tropical super Gromov-Witten invariants
abstract: We show that super Gromov-Witten invariants can be defined and computed by methods of tropical geometry. When the target is a point, the super invariants are descendant invariants on the moduli space of curves, which can be computed tropically. When the target is a convex, toric variety $X$, we describe a procedure to compute the tropical Euler class of the SUSY normal bundle $\overline{N}_{n, β}$ on $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{0,n}(X, β)$, assuming it is locally tropicalizable in the sense of [CG], [CGM]. Then, we define the tropical, genus-0, $n$-marked, super Gromov-Witten invariant of $X$, and compute an example. This gives a tropical interpretation of super Gromov-Witten invariants of convex, toric varieties.

conceptDetails.name: super gromov-witten invariants
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study — enumerative invariants counting J-holomorphic maps from genus-0 supercurves into convex toric varieties, extended here to a tropical setting.

conceptDetails.name: tropical geometry
conceptDetails.description: The core method of the paper — tropical geometry is used to define and compute super Gromov-Witten invariants combinatorially via tropical curves and cone complexes.

conceptDetails.name: susy normal bundle
conceptDetails.description: The supersymmetric normal bundle N_{n,β} of the embedding of the Kontsevich space into the super moduli space is the central geometric object whose Euler class drives the invariant computation.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant euler class
conceptDetails.description: A tropical version of the inverse equivariant Euler class of the SUSY normal bundle is defined and computed as the key technical tool for the invariants.

conceptDetails.name: moduli space of curves
conceptDetails.description: The paper works extensively with moduli spaces of stable maps and tropical curves to define and compute invariants tropically.

conceptDetails.name: toric varieties
conceptDetails.description: The target spaces for the super Gromov-Witten invariants are convex toric varieties, whose combinatorial (fan) structure enables tropicalization.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops super Gromov-Witten theory, which extends classical enumerative geometry using supersymmetric (SUSY) structures rooted in quantum field theoretic constructions.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Gromov-Witten invariants are foundational to string theory via the A-model topological string, and super Gromov-Witten invariants extend this to supercurves and supergeometry.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper exploits a torus C*-action (equivariant symmetry) and localization with respect to this action to compute super Gromov-Witten invariants.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Toric group actions and equivariant cohomology underlie the localization techniques used throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Tropical geometry replaces continuous algebraic geometry with combinatorial/piecewise-linear structures analogous to discrete stochastic frameworks for counting invariants.

title: Optimizing Kilonova Searches: A Case Study of the Type IIb SN 2025ulz in the Localization Volume of the Low-Significance Gravitational Wave Event S250818k
abstract: Kilonovae, the ultraviolet/optical/infrared counterparts to binary neutron star mergers, are an exceptionally rare class of transients. Optical follow-up campaigns are plagued by contaminating transients, which may mimic kilonovae, but do not receive sufficient observations to measure the full photometric evolution. In this work, we present an analysis of the multi-wavelength dataset of supernova (SN) 2025ulz, a proposed kilonova candidate following the low-significance detection of gravitational waves originating from the potential binary neutron star merger S250818k. Despite an early rapid decline in brightness, our multi-wavelength observations of SN 2025ulz reveal that it is a type IIb supernova. As part of this analysis, we demonstrate the capabilities of a novel quantitative scoring algorithm to determine the likelihood that a transient candidate is a kilonova, based primarily on its 3D location and light curve evolution. We also apply our scoring algorithm to other transient candidates in the localization volume of S250818k and find that, at all times after the discovery of SN 2025ulz, there are $\geq 4$ candidates with a score comparable to SN 2025ulz, indicating that the kilonova search may have benefited from the additional follow-up of other candidates. During future kilonova searches, this type of scoring algorithm will be useful to rule out contaminating transients in real time, optimizing the use of valuable telescope resources.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on electromagnetic follow-up of the low-significance gravitational wave event S250818k from a potential binary neutron star merger.

conceptDetails.name: kilonova scoring
conceptDetails.description: A novel quantitative algorithm that scores kilonova candidacy based on 3D spatial location within the GW localization volume and photometric light curve behavior.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: SN 2025ulz is classified as a Type IIb supernova through multi-wavelength observations, distinguishing it from a kilonova.

conceptDetails.name: transient contamination
conceptDetails.description: Core-collapse supernovae and other transients that mimic kilonova light curves are identified as the primary obstacle to efficient GW electromagnetic follow-up.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a scoring algorithm to detect genuine kilonova signals among contaminating transients in GW localization volumes.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A quantitative scoring algorithm classifies transient candidates by their likelihood of being a kilonova based on 3D location and light curve evolution.

conceptDetails.name: multi-messenger follow-up
conceptDetails.description: Coordinated multi-wavelength (UV/optical/IR/radio) observations triggered by GW detection to identify kilonova counterparts.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Identifying and ruling out contaminating transients (e.g., Type IIb supernovae) that mimic kilonovae in real time during follow-up campaigns.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: The scoring framework is used to optimize telescope resource allocation by prioritizing the most promising kilonova candidates.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Statistical significance of the GW event S250818k is assessed, and the scoring algorithm quantifies the probability that each transient is a kilonova.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The kilonova scoring algorithm is validated by applying it retrospectively to all transient candidates in the localization volume.

title: Hierarchical Simulation-Based Inference of Supernova Power Sources and their Physical Properties
abstract: Time domain surveys such as the Vera C. Rubin Observatory are projected to annually discover millions of astronomical transients. This and complementary programs demand fast, automated methods to constrain the physical properties of the most interesting objects for spectroscopic follow up. Traditional approaches to likelihood-based inference are computationally expensive and ignore the multi-component energy sources powering astrophysical phenomena. In this work, we present a hierarchical simulation-based inference model for multi-band light curves that 1) identifies the energy sources powering an event of interest, 2) infers the physical properties of each subclass, and 3) separates physical anomalies in the learned embedding space. Our architecture consists of a transformer-based light curve summarizer coupled to a flow-matching regression module and a categorical classifier for the physical components. We train and test our model on $\sim$150k synthetic light curves generated with $\texttt{MOSFiT}$. Our network achieves a 90% classification accuracy at identifying energy sources, yields well-calibrated posteriors for all active components, and detects rare anomalies such as tidal disruption events (TDEs) through the learned latent space. This work demonstrates a scalable joint framework for population studies of known transients and the discovery of novel populations in the era of Rubin.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The core framework of the paper, enabling likelihood-free posterior estimation over supernova energy sources and physical parameters from light curves.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper learns calibrated posterior distributions over both discrete energy source combinations and continuous physical parameters.

conceptDetails.name: multi-component energy modeling
conceptDetails.description: Novel hierarchical framework jointly infers which combination of physical energy sources (Ni decay, CSM, magnetar) power a given transient.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: A flow-matching regression module is used to model the continuous parameter posterior conditioned on the inferred energy source class.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper classifies supernovae by their active energy sources (radioactive decay, CSM interaction, magnetar spin-down) with 90% accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A transformer-based multi-band light curve summarizer encodes irregular photometric time series into a shared latent representation.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The model detects rare astrophysical events such as tidal disruption events by identifying outliers in the learned latent embedding space.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A categorical classifier predicts the discrete combination of energy sources powering each supernova event.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The paper validates that the inferred posteriors for physical parameters are well-calibrated across all active energy source components.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: A shared summarizer learns optimal summary statistics from light curves that support both classification and parameter inference tasks.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: Tidal disruption events and other anomalies are identified as out-of-distribution samples in the learned embedding space.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The latent embedding space learned by the summarizer enables separation of distinct physical populations and anomaly detection.

title: Constraining Power of Wavelet vs. Power Spectrum Statistics for CMB Lensing and Weak Lensing with Learned Binning
abstract: We present forecasts for constraints on the matter density ($Ω_m$) and the amplitude of matter density fluctuations at 8h$^{-1}$Mpc ($σ_8$) from CMB lensing convergence maps and galaxy weak lensing convergence maps. For CMB lensing convergence auto statistics, we compare the angular power spectra ($C_\ell$'s) to the wavelet scattering transform (WST) coefficients. For CMB lensing convergence $\times$ galaxy weak lensing convergence statistics, we compare the cross angular power spectra to wavelet phase harmonics (WPH). This work also serves as the first application of WST and WPH to these probes. For CMB lensing convergence, we find that WST and $C_\ell$'s yield similar constraints in forecasts for all surveys considered in this work. When CMB lensing convergence is crossed with galaxy weak lensing convergence projected from $\textit{Euclid}$ Data Release 2 (DR2), we find that WPH outperforms cross-$C_\ell$'s by factors between $2.2$ and $3.4$ for individual parameter constraints. To compare these different summary statistics, we develop a novel learned binning approach. This method compresses summary statistics while maintaining interpretability. We find this leads to improved constraints compared to more naive binning schemes for our wavelet-based statistics, but not for $C_\ell$'s. By learning the binning and measuring constraints on distinct data sets, our method is robust to overfitting by construction.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: CMB lensing convergence maps are the primary observational probe used to constrain cosmological parameters Ω_m and σ_8.

conceptDetails.name: wavelet scattering transform
conceptDetails.description: A non-Gaussian summary statistic applied for the first time to CMB lensing convergence auto-statistics, capturing multi-scale structure beyond two-point functions.

conceptDetails.name: wavelet phase harmonics
conceptDetails.description: A wavelet-based summary statistic applied for the first time to CMB × galaxy weak lensing cross-statistics, outperforming cross-power spectra by factors of 2.2–3.4.

conceptDetails.name: learned binning
conceptDetails.description: A novel method that learns optimal compression of summary statistics by maximizing the Fisher information determinant on held-out data, robust to overfitting by construction.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The ULAGAM suite of N-body simulations provides the simulated convergence maps and Fisher forecast derivatives used throughout the analysis.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The learned binning approach compresses high-dimensional summary statistics into compact, informative representations while preserving Fisher information.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Angular power spectra (C_ℓ's) serve as the baseline two-point summary statistic against which wavelet-based methods are benchmarked.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The Fisher forecast framework and forward-modeled simulation pipeline are used to compare the constraining power of different summary statistics.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Wavelet scattering transform and wavelet phase harmonics extract multi-scale non-Gaussian features from lensing convergence maps.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Fisher matrix formalism provides the Bayesian framework for forecasting parameter constraints from different summary statistics.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The matter density Ω_m and clustering amplitude σ_8 constrained in this work characterize the large-scale dark matter distribution.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian likelihood assumptions underlie the Fisher forecast pipeline used to evaluate information content of each summary statistic.

title: Studying the gravitational-wave population without looking that FAR out
abstract: From catalogs of gravitational-wave transients, the population-level properties of their sources and the formation channels of merging compact binaries can be constrained. However, astrophysical conclusions can be biased by misspecification or misestimation of the population likelihood. Despite detection thresholds on the false-alarm rate (FAR) or signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), the current catalog is likely contaminated by noise transients. Further, computing the population likelihood becomes less accurate as the catalog grows. Current methods to address these challenges often scale poorly with the number of events and potentially become infeasible for future catalogs. Here, we evaluate a simple remedy: increasing the significance threshold for including events in population analyses. To determine the efficacy of this approach, we analyze simulated catalogs of up to 1600 gravitational-wave signals from black-hole mergers using full Bayesian parameter estimation with current detector sensitivities. We show that the growth in statistical uncertainty about the black-hole population, as we analyze fewer events but with higher SNR, depends on the source parameters of interest. When the SNR threshold is raised from 11 to 15 -- reducing our catalog size by two--thirds -- we find that statistical uncertainties on the mass distribution only grow by a few 10% and constraints on the spin distribution are essentially unchanged; meanwhile, uncertainties on the high-redshift cosmic merger rate more than double. Simultaneously, numerical uncertainty in the estimate of the population likelihood more than halves, allowing us to ensure unbiased inference without additional computational expense. Our results demonstrate that focusing on higher-significance events is an effective way to facilitate robust astrophysical inference with growing gravitational-wave catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes catalogs of gravitational-wave transients from black-hole mergers to study population-level properties and formation channels of compact binaries.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Full Bayesian parameter estimation and hierarchical Bayesian analysis are the core statistical framework used to infer population properties from GW catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: A central challenge addressed is the accurate estimation of the population likelihood, which degrades as catalog size grows.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper quantifies how statistical and numerical uncertainties on population parameters evolve as the SNR detection threshold is raised.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Single-event posterior samples are computed and used as inputs to the population likelihood estimation throughout the analysis.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper evaluates the effect of raising detection significance thresholds (SNR/FAR) to filter noise transients from astrophysical catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: selection bias correction
conceptDetails.description: Accounting for detector selection effects is central to unbiased hierarchical population inference in this work.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo integration is used to estimate the population likelihood and selection effects, and its numerical accuracy is a key focus of the work.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A primary motivation is improving the scalability of population analyses as gravitational-wave catalogs grow to thousands or hundreds of thousands of events.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Simulated catalogs of BBH mergers with realistic parameter estimation are used to evaluate inference quality under different detection thresholds.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Fitting density estimators to single-event posterior samples is discussed as one of the existing approaches to efficiently compute the population likelihood.

title: Topological Invariance and Breakdown in Learning
abstract: We prove that for a broad class of permutation-equivariant learning rules (including SGD, Adam, and others), the training process induces a bi-Lipschitz mapping between neurons and strongly constrains the topology of the neuron distribution during training. This result reveals a qualitative difference between small and large learning rates $η$. With a learning rate below a topological critical point $η^*$, the training is constrained to preserve all topological structure of the neurons. In contrast, above $η^*$, the learning process allows for topological simplification, making the neuron manifold progressively coarser and thereby reducing the model's expressivity. Viewed in combination with the recent discovery of the edge of stability phenomenon, the learning dynamics of neuron networks under gradient descent can be divided into two phases: first they undergo smooth optimization under topological constraints, and then enter a second phase where they learn through drastic topological simplifications. A key feature of our theory is that it is independent of specific architectures or loss functions, enabling the universal application of topological methods to the study of deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: topological invariance
conceptDetails.description: Below the critical learning rate, training induces a bi-Lipschitz (homeomorphic) mapping that preserves all topological structure of the neuron set.

conceptDetails.name: topological critical point
conceptDetails.description: The paper establishes a precise threshold η* in learning rate space that separates topology-preserving from topology-breaking training regimes.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Permutation equivariance of learning algorithms (SGD, Adam) is the core structural property that constrains neuron topology during training.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on permutation-equivariant learning rules as its foundational class, showing these induce bi-Lipschitz mappings between neurons.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: A topological phase transition occurs at a critical learning rate η*, separating topology-preserving from topology-simplifying training regimes.

conceptDetails.name: edge of stability
conceptDetails.description: The empirically discovered edge-of-stability phenomenon is reinterpreted as the onset of topological breakdown, unifying it with the paper's phase-transition framework.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The neuron distribution forms a manifold whose topology is either preserved (homeomorphically) or coarsened depending on the learning rate.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Permutation group symmetry of neural network components is the mathematical foundation for the universal topological constraints derived.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The theory provides a principled geometric/topological lens for understanding why large vs. small learning rates lead to qualitatively different model behaviors.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: SGD and its variants are analyzed as discrete-time stochastic dynamics whose step sizes govern topological preservation.

title: Reducing Simulation Dependence in Neutrino Telescopes with Masked Point Transformers
abstract: Machine learning techniques in neutrino physics have traditionally relied on simulated data, which provides access to ground-truth labels. However, the accuracy of these simulations and the discrepancies between simulated and real data remain significant concerns, particularly for large-scale neutrino telescopes that operate in complex natural media. In recent years, self-supervised learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for reducing dependence on labeled datasets. Here, we present the first self-supervised training pipeline for neutrino telescopes, leveraging point cloud transformers and masked autoencoders. By shifting the majority of training to real data, this approach minimizes reliance on simulations, thereby mitigating associated systematic uncertainties. This represents a fundamental departure from previous machine learning applications in neutrino telescopes, paving the way for substantial improvements in event reconstruction and classification.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The core paradigm of the paper: pre-training NEPTUNE on unlabeled real detector data using masked reconstruction to reduce reliance on Monte Carlo simulation.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The target application domain—IceCube-scale neutrino telescopes—motivates the entire pipeline and defines the data structure and downstream tasks.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The NEPTUNE architecture uses a transformer encoder to process tokenized point-cloud representations of neutrino telescope events.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Masked autoencoders provide the pre-training objective, reconstructing masked spatio-temporal coordinates of neutrino event tokens.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The primary motivation: SSL-trained models maintain performance under unmodeled detector effects (injected noise hits) where supervised models degrade significantly.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Directional reconstruction of muon neutrinos and tau-neutrino double-cascade classification serve as the benchmark downstream tasks for evaluating the SSL approach.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: Novel challenge addressed: bridging the domain gap between imperfect Monte Carlo simulation and real detector data in neutrino telescopes.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulation provides ground-truth labels but introduces systematic discrepancies from real data; the paper's goal is to minimize dependence on it.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The pre-train-then-fine-tune paradigm transfers representations learned from real unlabeled data to labeled simulation for downstream task heads.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Block-expansion fine-tuning inserts identity-initialized transformer blocks atop frozen pre-trained layers to prevent catastrophic forgetting during supervised adaptation.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The SSL pipeline learns rich internal representations of neutrino events from unlabeled real data, enabling generalizable downstream performance.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: PointNet-inspired point-cloud tokenization with farthest-point sampling and k-nearest-neighbor pooling handles the irregular spatial geometry of neutrino telescope hits.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations generate the labeled training set and serve as the 'simulation' dataset in controlled mismatch experiments.

title: Matching the Optimal Denoiser in Point Cloud Diffusion with (Improved) Rotational Alignment
abstract: Diffusion models are a popular class of generative models trained to reverse a noising process starting from a target data distribution. Training a diffusion model consists of learning how to denoise noisy samples at different noise levels. When training diffusion models for point clouds such as molecules and proteins, there is often no canonical orientation that can be assigned. To capture this symmetry, the true data samples are often augmented by transforming them with random rotations sampled uniformly over $SO(3)$. Then, the denoised predictions are often rotationally aligned via the Kabsch-Umeyama algorithm to the ground truth samples before computing the loss. However, the effect of this alignment step has not been well studied. Here, we show that the optimal denoiser can be expressed in terms of a matrix Fisher distribution over $SO(3)$. Alignment corresponds to sampling the mode of this distribution, and turns out to be the zeroth order approximation for small noise levels, explaining its effectiveness. We build on this perspective to derive better approximators to the optimal denoiser in the limit of small noise. Our experiments highlight that alignment is often a `good enough' approximation for the noise levels that matter most for training diffusion models.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the training objective of diffusion models for point clouds, specifically how the denoising loss and alignment step affect learning the optimal denoiser.

conceptDetails.name: rotational alignment
conceptDetails.description: The Kabsch-Umeyama alignment algorithm used before computing the denoising loss is the central algorithmic operation analyzed and improved in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: SO(3) rotational invariance/equivariance is the central symmetry constraint that motivates the paper's analysis of rotational augmentation and alignment.

conceptDetails.name: matrix fisher distribution
conceptDetails.description: The optimal denoiser is shown to involve an expectation over a matrix Fisher distribution on SO(3), providing the theoretical framework for understanding alignment.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses SO(3) group theory and Haar measure to formalize rotational augmentation and derive the optimal denoiser over the rotation group.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper shows that the alignment step in the denoising loss corresponds to a zeroth-order approximation of the optimal denoiser, and proposes improved estimators.

conceptDetails.name: laplace approximation
conceptDetails.description: Laplace's method is applied to approximate the expectation over the matrix Fisher distribution, yielding higher-order correction terms to the alignment estimator at no added computational cost.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper contrasts SO(3)-equivariant denoisers with rotational augmentation as two approaches for enforcing rotational symmetry in point cloud diffusion.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: Point cloud processing in 3D space with rotation group structure is the geometric setting that motivates all theoretical contributions.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Random rotational augmentation over SO(3) is analyzed as the mechanism that produces an SO(3)-invariant training distribution for point cloud diffusion.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper's contributions apply broadly to generative modeling of 3D point clouds such as molecular conformations and protein structures.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The denoising score matching framework underlies the diffusion model training objective analyzed in this paper.

title: To Augment or Not to Augment? Diagnosing Distributional Symmetry Breaking
abstract: Symmetry-aware methods for machine learning, such as data augmentation and equivariant architectures, encourage correct model behavior on all transformations (e.g. rotations or permutations) of the original dataset. These methods can improve generalization and sample efficiency, under the assumption that the transformed datapoints are highly probable, or "important", under the test distribution. In this work, we develop a method for critically evaluating this assumption. In particular, we propose a metric to quantify the amount of anisotropy, or symmetry-breaking, in a dataset, via a two-sample neural classifier test that distinguishes between the original dataset and its randomly augmented equivalent. We validate our metric on synthetic datasets, and then use it to uncover surprisingly high degrees of alignment in several benchmark point cloud datasets. We show theoretically that distributional symmetry-breaking can actually prevent invariant methods from performing optimally even when the underlying labels are truly invariant, as we show for invariant ridge regression in the infinite feature limit. Empirically, we find that the implication for symmetry-aware methods is dataset-dependent: equivariant methods still impart benefits on some anisotropic datasets, but not others. Overall, these findings suggest that understanding equivariance -- both when it works, and why -- may require rethinking symmetry biases in the data.

conceptDetails.name: distributional symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Novel metric and theoretical framework introduced to quantify and analyze when data distributions are non-uniform over symmetry orbits.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper critically evaluates when equivariant architectures provide benefits, showing their performance is dataset-dependent under distributional symmetry breaking.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes when data augmentation helps or hurts, providing theoretical and empirical evidence that augmentation can be harmful under distributional asymmetry.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies the assumption of distributional symmetry and develops a metric to quantify when this assumption holds or breaks down.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Group transformations and orbits are the mathematical foundation for analyzing symmetry breaking and equivariance in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: anisotropy metric
conceptDetails.description: A concrete scalar measure derived from classifier accuracy that quantifies the degree of distributional asymmetry in a dataset.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The work directly addresses core questions in geometric deep learning about when and why symmetry-aware methods succeed.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: A two-sample classifier test is proposed as the core metric to statistically distinguish original from augmented data distributions.

conceptDetails.name: canonicalization
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies full canonicalization as the extreme case of distributional symmetry breaking and finds it prevalent in benchmark point cloud datasets.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Invariant ridge regression in the infinite feature limit is analyzed theoretically to show when augmentation hurts even with truly invariant labels.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The neural tangent kernel framework is used to connect ridge regression theory to neural network behavior.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how symmetry biases in data affect learned representations and model inductive biases.

title: A universal compression theory for lottery ticket hypothesis and neural scaling laws
abstract: When training large-scale models, the performance typically scales with the number of parameters and the dataset size according to a slow power law. A fundamental theoretical and practical question is whether comparable performance can be achieved with significantly smaller models and substantially less data. In this work, we provide a positive and constructive answer.
