We prove that a generic permutation-invariant function of $d$ objects can be asymptotically compressed into a function of $\operatorname{polylog} d$ objects with vanishing error, which is proved to be the optimal compression rate. This theorem yields two key implications: (Ia) a large neural network can be compressed to polylogarithmic width while preserving its learning dynamics; (Ib) a large dataset can be compressed to polylogarithmic size while leaving the loss landscape of the corresponding model unchanged. Implication (Ia) directly establishes a proof of the dynamical lottery ticket hypothesis, which states that any ordinary network can be strongly compressed such that the learning dynamics and result remain unchanged. (Ib) shows that a neural scaling law of the form $L\sim d^{-α}$ can be boosted to an arbitrarily fast power law decay, and ultimately to $\exp(-α' \sqrt[m]{d})$.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Permutation symmetry of neural networks and datasets is the mathematical foundation enabling the universal compression theorem proved in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: lottery ticket hypothesis
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides a formal proof of the dynamical lottery ticket hypothesis, showing any network can be strongly compressed while preserving its learning dynamics and final result.

conceptDetails.name: polylogarithmic compression
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept introduced in this paper: the optimal compression rate of d objects to O(polylog d) objects for smooth permutation-invariant functions, proved to be tight.

conceptDetails.name: neural scaling laws
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves that standard power-law scaling L ~ d^{-α} can be boosted to sub-exponential decay via polylogarithmic dataset compression, directly improving data efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The work addresses the scalability problem of large models by proving that comparable performance can be achieved with polylogarithmically smaller models and datasets.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The compression results yield sparse subnetworks of polylogarithmic width that exactly replicate the training dynamics of the original dense network.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The core theorem compresses d-object permutation-invariant functions to polylog(d) objects with vanishing error, representing an extreme form of dimensionality reduction.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The permutation group structure of neural network weights and datasets is the algebraic underpinning of the universal compression framework.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Preserving the loss landscape under dataset compression is a central technical guarantee required to ensure the compressed dataset yields the same trained model.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Self-attention and attention heads are identified as key architectural components possessing permutation symmetry, making them directly amenable to the compression theory.

title: Implicit Augmentation from Distributional Symmetry in Turbulence Super-Resolution
abstract: The immense computational cost of simulating turbulence has motivated the use of machine learning approaches for super-resolving turbulent flows. A central challenge is ensuring that learned models respect physical symmetries, such as rotational equivariance. We show that standard convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can partially acquire this symmetry without explicit augmentation or specialized architectures, as turbulence itself provides implicit rotational augmentation in both time and space. Using 3D channel-flow subdomains with differing anisotropy, we find that models trained on more isotropic mid-plane data achieve lower equivariance error than those trained on boundary layer data, and that greater temporal or spatial sampling further reduces this error. We show a distinct scale-dependence of equivariance error that occurs regardless of dataset anisotropy that is consistent with Kolmogorov's local isotropy hypothesis. These results clarify when rotational symmetry must be explicitly incorporated into learning algorithms and when it can be obtained directly from turbulence, enabling more efficient and symmetry-aware super-resolution.

conceptDetails.name: distributional symmetry
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept central to the paper: the probability law of turbulence velocity fields is invariant under group actions, meaning the data distribution itself encodes rotational symmetry that CNNs can absorb.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates when CNNs acquire rotational equivariance implicitly from turbulence data versus when it must be explicitly imposed through architectural design.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Rotational symmetry (isotropy) is the central physical symmetry studied, with the paper quantifying how well trained models preserve it under octahedral group rotations.

conceptDetails.name: implicit equivariance learning
conceptDetails.description: Novel phenomenon studied: CNNs trained on statistically isotropic turbulence data acquire approximate rotational equivariance without explicit augmentation or equivariant architectures.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The task is turbulence super-resolution — reconstructing high-resolution velocity fields from coarse inputs using CNNs trained on direct numerical simulation data.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central thesis is that turbulence provides implicit rotational augmentation via distributional symmetry, reducing the need for explicit augmentation strategies.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Standard CNNs are the model architecture used throughout, chosen specifically to test whether equivariance can emerge implicitly without specialized equivariant architectures.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Equivariance error is evaluated over the discrete octahedral group O, providing a formal mathematical framework for measuring rotational symmetry compliance.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The work situates itself within geometric deep learning by examining how group-theoretic symmetries can be learned from data distributions rather than imposed architecturally.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Energy spectra are computed to characterize anisotropy across boundary-layer and mid-plane subdomains, linking inertial-range scaling to equivariance error behavior.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The SR model serves as a surrogate for expensive direct numerical simulation, enabling high-resolution turbulence fields at reduced computational cost.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: MAE loss is chosen over MSE to better preserve perceptual quality and reduce oversmoothing in the super-resolved velocity fields.

title: Deep Learning for Clouds and Cloud Shadow Segmentation in Methane Satellite and Airborne Imaging Spectroscopy
abstract: Effective cloud and cloud shadow detection is a critical prerequisite for accurate retrieval of concentrations of atmospheric methane (CH4) or other trace gases in hyperspectral remote sensing. This challenge is especially pertinent for MethaneSAT, a satellite mission launched in March 2024, to fill a significant data gap in terms of resolution, precision and swath between coarse-resolution global mappers and fine-scale point-source imagers of methane, and for its airborne companion mission, MethaneAIR. MethaneSAT delivers hyperspectral data at an intermediate spatial resolution (approx. 100 x 400, m), whereas MethaneAIR provides even finer resolution (approx. 25 m), enabling the development of highly detailed maps of concentrations that enable quantification of both the sources and rates of emissions. In this study, we use machine learning methods to address the cloud and cloud shadow detection problem for sensors with these high spatial resolutions. Cloud and cloud shadows in remote sensing data need to be effectively screened out as they bias methane retrievals in remote sensing imagery and impact the quantification of emissions. We deploy and evaluate conventional techniques-including Iterative Logistic Regression (ILR) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)-with advanced deep learning architectures, namely U-Net and a Spectral Channel Attention Network (SCAN) method. Our results show that conventional methods struggle with spatial coherence and boundary definition, affecting the detection of clouds and cloud shadows. Deep learning models substantially improve detection quality: U-Net performs best in preserving spatial structure, while SCAN excels at capturing fine boundary details... Our data and code is publicly available at: https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/IKLZOJ

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: U-Net and SCAN architectures leverage convolutional operations to capture spatial structure in hyperspectral imagery for cloud and shadow segmentation.

conceptDetails.name: hyperspectral segmentation
conceptDetails.description: Novel application of deep learning to segment clouds and shadows in hyperspectral methane imaging spectroscopy data across multiple sensor platforms.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: The Spectral Channel Attention Network (SCAN) uses spectral attention to selectively weight hyperspectral channels, improving detection of satellite-specific cloud features.

conceptDetails.name: methane remote sensing
conceptDetails.description: The applied domain driving the need for cloud screening: accurate CH4 retrieval from MethaneSAT and MethaneAIR hyperspectral missions.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: A CNN-based ensemble combines U-Net and SCAN predictions, achieving best F1 scores on both MethaneAIR and MethaneSAT datasets.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The core task is pixel-level classification of clouds and cloud shadows in hyperspectral remote sensing imagery.

conceptDetails.name: spectral channel attention
conceptDetails.description: A novel architecture component that applies learned attention weights across spectral channels to capture instrument-specific cloud signatures in SWIR data.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Deep learning models extract high-dimensional spectral and spatial features from hyperspectral SWIR bands to distinguish clouds from surface features.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Models trained on MethaneAIR data are evaluated for generalization to MethaneSAT, testing cross-sensor transfer of learned representations.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Label generation leverages a combination of manual annotation and algorithm-derived labels to address the scarcity of ground truth for cloud segmentation.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The work addresses detection quality at varying spatial resolutions (25 m MethaneAIR vs. 100x400 m MethaneSAT), requiring models robust to resolution differences.

title: The Pareto Frontier of Resilient Jet Tagging
abstract: Classifying hadronic jets using their constituents' kinematic information is a critical task in modern high-energy collider physics. Often, classifiers are designed by targeting the best performance using metrics such as accuracy, AUC, or rejection rates. However, the use of a single metric can lead to the use of architectures that are more model-dependent than competitive alternatives, leading to potential uncertainty and bias in analysis. We explore such trade-offs and demonstrate the consequences of using networks with high performance metrics but low resilience.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces 'resilience' as a measure of model robustness to Monte Carlo generator differences (PYTHIA vs. HERWIG), arguing it must be considered alongside raw performance.

conceptDetails.name: pareto frontier
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: constructing the Pareto frontier of AUC vs. resilience to identify optimal trade-offs between classifier performance and model-dependence.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central application domain, focusing on classifying hadronic jets from quark/gluon and top-quark processes at the LHC using their substructure.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Jet tagging is formulated as a binary classification task, evaluated via AUC and ROC curves across multiple neural architectures.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental context of the LHC where jet tagging is performed, using Monte Carlo simulated event samples from PYTHIA and HERWIG generators.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Model-dependence on the training simulation is treated as a systematic uncertainty, quantified as AUC percent-difference across MC generators.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: PYTHIA 8 and HERWIG 7 Monte Carlo event generators are used to produce training and alternative test samples for quantifying resilience.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The Neyman-Pearson Lemma is used to extract per-event q/g likelihood ratios from classifiers for downstream maximum likelihood estimation of flavor fractions.

conceptDetails.name: knowledge distillation
conceptDetails.description: A complex teacher PFN model is used to train simpler student models via KL-divergence minimization, tested as a method to overcome the Pareto frontier without success.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The Particle Transformer (ParT) is one of the state-of-the-art architectures benchmarked, achieving highest AUC but lowest resilience on the Pareto frontier.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Expert features (angularities, multiplicities) are used as physics-motivated hand-crafted inputs, forming a baseline that proves highly resilient.

title: When IIb Ceases To Be: Bridging the Gap Between IIb and Short-plateau Supernovae
abstract: Hydrogen-rich supernovae (SNe) span a range of hydrogen envelope masses at core collapse, producing diverse light curves from extended plateaus in Type II SNe to double-peaked Type IIb SNe. Recent hydrodynamic modeling predicts a continuous sequence of light-curve morphologies as hydrogen is removed, with short plateau SNe (plateau durations ~50--70 days) emerging as a transitional class. However, the observational boundary between IIb and short-plateau remains poorly defined, and thus far unobserved. We report on extensive photometric and spectroscopic follow-up of SN 2023wdd and SN 2022acrv, candidate transitional events on the low-mass end of the short-plateau class. Both exhibit weak, double-peaked light curves which we interpret as exceptionally short plateaus (10--20 days), and hybrid spectral features: persistent H$α$ absorption with He I contamination, but without the helium dominance characteristic of IIb SNe. Using analytic shock-cooling models and numerical light curve fitting, we estimate hydrogen-rich envelope masses of ~0.6--0.8 $M_\odot$ -- significantly larger than canonical IIb values ($\lesssim0.1\,M_\odot$) but consistent with the ${\sim}0.9\,M_\odot$ threshold predicted for short-plateau behavior. Although the progenitor radii inferred from analytic and numerical methods differ by factors of 2--5, envelope mass estimates are consistent across approaches. Comparisons to well-studied IIb (SN 2016gkg, SN 2022hnt), short-plateau (SN 2023ufx, SN 2006ai, SN 2016egz, SN 2006Y), and II SNe (SN 2023ixf, SN 2013ej) suggest a monotonic relationship between hydrogen envelope mass and plateau length consistent with analytic and numerical expectations. These findings provide additional evidence for a continuous distribution of envelope stripping in hydrogen-rich core-collapse progenitors and place SN 2023wdd and SN 2022acrv along the IIb/short-plateau boundary.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the observational boundary between Type IIb and short-plateau supernovae, directly challenging existing classification schemes for hydrogen-rich core-collapse events.

conceptDetails.name: envelope stripping continuum
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides observational evidence for a continuous distribution of hydrogen envelope masses in core-collapse supernovae rather than discrete classification bins.

conceptDetails.name: light curve modeling
conceptDetails.description: Numerical and analytic light curve fitting is central to deriving envelope masses and distinguishing transitional supernovae from canonical IIb and IIP events.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The study connects diverse supernova light curve morphologies to the degree of hydrogen envelope stripping in massive star progenitors, probing the continuum of pre-collapse stellar evolution.

conceptDetails.name: shock-cooling emission
conceptDetails.description: Analytic shock-cooling models are applied to the early-time light curve peaks to constrain progenitor radius and hydrogen envelope mass.

conceptDetails.name: spectral line analysis
conceptDetails.description: Spectroscopic follow-up reveals hybrid H-alpha and He I features used to place the two supernovae along the IIb/short-plateau boundary.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Hydrodynamic and numerical light curve models are used to infer physical parameters (envelope mass, progenitor radius) from observed photometric data.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Analytic shock-cooling models and numerical light curve fitting are used to estimate posterior envelope masses and progenitor radii from photometric observations.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Analytic and numerical fitting methods are applied to photometric and spectroscopic data to derive quantitative physical parameters for the transitional supernovae.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: SN 2023wdd and SN 2022acrv are identified as outlier transitional events that do not conform to standard IIb or IIP classification criteria.

title: Towards non-commutative crepant resolutions of affine toric Gorenstein varieties
abstract: In this paper we prove a common generalisation of results by Špenko-Van den Bergh and Iyama-Wemyss that can be used to generate non-commutative crepant resolutions (NCCRs) of some affine toric Gorenstein varieties. We use and generalise results by Novaković to study NCCRs for affine toric Gorenstein varieties associated to cones over polytopes with interior points. As a special case, we consider the case where the polytope is reflexive with $\le \dim P+2$ vertices, using results of Borisov and Hua to show the existence of NCCRs.

conceptDetails.name: non-commutative resolutions
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study: algebras of the form End_R(M) that serve as non-commutative analogues of geometric crepant resolutions for Gorenstein singularities.

conceptDetails.name: toric geometry
conceptDetails.description: The geometric framework throughout: affine toric Gorenstein varieties are defined by cones over polytopes, and their resolution theory is studied via toric DM stacks and fans.

conceptDetails.name: derived categories
conceptDetails.description: The categorical language used to formulate equivalences between geometric and non-commutative resolutions, following the Bondal-Orlov and Kawamata conjectures.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Torus actions on toric varieties and equivariant vector bundles underpin the construction of tilting complexes and NCCR generators throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Equivariance with respect to torus actions is preserved throughout the construction of tilting complexes on toric DM stacks, ensuring the NCCR inherits the correct symmetry structure.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Reflexive polytopes and Fano varieties studied in the final section are central to mirror symmetry in string theory, providing physical motivation for the mathematical results.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The homological and categorical methods employed, including derived equivalences and D-brane interpretations of tilting bundles, connect to QFT frameworks.

title: Gradient-based search of quantum phases: discovering unconventional fractional Chern insulators
abstract: The discovery and understanding of new quantum phases has time and again transformed both fundamental physics and technology, yet progress often relies on slow, intuition-based theoretical considerations or experimental serendipity. Here, we introduce a general gradient-based framework for targeted phase discovery. We define a differentiable function, dubbed "target-phase loss function", which encodes spectral fingerprints of a quantum state, thereby recasting phase search as a tractable optimization problem in Hamiltonian space. The method is broadly applicable to phases characterized by ground-state degeneracy and can be extended to a wide range of symmetry-broken and topological orders. As a demonstration, we apply it to spinless fermions on the kagome lattice and discover two distinctive fractional Chern insulators (FCIs), verified through detailed exact diagonalization: (i) at filling $ν= 1/3$, a "non-ideal" Abelian FCI whose band geometry lies far beyond the Landau-level mimicry paradigm and all recent generalizations; and (ii) at $ν= 1/2$, a non-Abelian FCI stabilized purely by finite-range two-body interactions. These results provide the first explicit realization of such types of FCIs and establish a versatile paradigm for systematic quantum-phase discovery.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a differentiable 'target-phase loss function' encoding spectral fingerprints of a quantum phase, recasting phase discovery as a tractable optimization problem in Hamiltonian space.

conceptDetails.name: fractional chern insulators
conceptDetails.description: The paper discovers two novel fractional Chern insulator states on the kagome lattice — a non-ideal Abelian FCI at ν=1/3 and a non-Abelian Moore–Read FCI at ν=1/2 — as the primary physical results.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The optimization is performed directly over the parameter space of many-body Hamiltonians, with gradient descent navigating hopping amplitudes, interaction strengths, and lattice geometry.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The loss function is constructed from spectral fingerprints — the low-energy many-body spectrum organized by symmetry sectors — as the primary signal for phase identification.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The method targets specific quantum ground states and their degeneracy structure, including Abelian and non-Abelian fractional Chern insulator states.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The framework provides a systematic, gradient-driven protocol for discovering novel quantum phases, replacing intuition-based or serendipitous approaches.

conceptDetails.name: topological order
conceptDetails.description: Both discovered FCIs exhibit topological order characterized by ground-state degeneracy, fractionalized excitations, and long-range entanglement, motivating the spectral fingerprint approach.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Conservation of quantum numbers (charge, momentum, spin) organizes the many-body spectrum into symmetry sectors that define both the loss function and the signature of the target phase.

conceptDetails.name: exact diagonalization
conceptDetails.description: Full diagonalization of finite-size many-body Hamiltonians is used both as the differentiable computational engine within the optimization loop and as the independent verification method for discovered phases.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Exact diagonalization of symmetry-block Hamiltonians produces the spectral data used to evaluate and differentiate through the loss function.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: Exact diagonalization serves as the numerical quantum simulation engine that is combined with the gradient-based optimizer to verify discovered phases.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper maps phase diagrams in high-dimensional Hamiltonian parameter space, identifying boundaries between the discovered FCIs and competing phases.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The loss function formulation explicitly handles symmetry-broken phases (e.g., charge and spin density waves) alongside topological orders as a broad class of ground-state-degenerate systems.

title: Soliton Surfaces and the Geometry of Integrable Deformations of the $\mathbb{CP}^{N-1}$ Model
abstract: The $\mathbb{CP}^{N-1}$ model is an analytically tractable $2d$ quantum field theory which shares several properties with $4d$ Yang-Mills theory. By virtue of its classical integrability, this model also admits a family of integrable higher-spin auxiliary field deformations, including the $T \overline{T}$ deformation as a special case. We study the $\mathbb{CP}^{N-1}$ model and its deformations from a geometrical perspective, constructing their soliton surfaces and recasting physical properties of these theories as statements about surface geometry. We examine how the $T \overline{T}$ flow affects the unit constraint in the $\mathbb{CP}^{N-1}$ model and prove that any solution of this theory with vanishing energy-momentum tensor remains a solution under analytic stress tensor deformations -- an argument that extends to generic dimensions and instanton-like solutions in stress tensor flows including the non-analytic, $2d$, root-$T \overline{T}$ case and classes of higher-spin, Smirnov-Zamolodchikov-type, deformations. Finally, we give two geometric interpretations for general $T \overline{T}$-like deformations of symmetric space sigma models, showing that such flows can be viewed as coupling the undeformed theory to a unit-determinant field-dependent metric, or using a particular choice of moving frame on the soliton surface.

conceptDetails.name: tt-bar deformation
conceptDetails.description: The irrelevant deformation by the determinant of the stress-energy tensor is the central deformation studied, with its geometric and analytic properties analyzed in detail.

conceptDetails.name: soliton surfaces
conceptDetails.description: Immersed surfaces in Lie algebras constructed from the Lax pair of integrable models, used to recast physical properties of the CP^{N-1} model geometrically.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The CP^{N-1} model is analyzed as a 2d quantum field theory sharing properties with 4d Yang-Mills, forming the foundational framework of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: classical integrability
conceptDetails.description: The CP^{N-1} model's classical integrability via a Lax connection and zero-curvature condition underpins the entire geometric and deformation analysis.

conceptDetails.name: sigma model geometry
conceptDetails.description: The CP^{N-1} model is formulated as a symmetric space sigma model, and TT-bar deformations are interpreted as coupling to field-dependent metrics or moving frames.

conceptDetails.name: instanton solutions
conceptDetails.description: Topologically non-trivial field configurations in the CP^{N-1} model are proven to remain solutions under analytic stress tensor deformations, a key theorem of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives and analyzes deformed Lagrangians for the CP^{N-1} model under TT-bar and higher-spin deformations using auxiliary field formalisms.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The CP^{N-1} model is realized as a symmetric space coset sigma model, with its geometry and integrability grounded in Lie group and Lie algebra structure.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The TT-bar deformation modifies the symmetry structure of the CP^{N-1} model, and the paper analyzes how deformations affect constraints and solutions.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The CP^{N-1} model is classically conformally invariant, and the paper studies deformations that break or preserve this symmetry.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The TT-bar and higher-spin auxiliary field deformations are studied as systematic deformations of the base sigma model, analogous to EFT flows.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The integrable structure and conserved charges of the CP^{N-1} model relate to factorized scattering, relevant to the TT-bar deformation context.

title: Beyond I-Con: Exploring New Dimension of Distance Measures in Representation Learning
abstract: The Information Contrastive (I-Con) framework revealed that over 23 representation learning methods implicitly minimize KL divergence between data and learned distributions that encode similarities between data points. However, a KL-based loss may be misaligned with the true objective, and properties of KL divergence such as asymmetry and unboundedness may create optimization challenges. We present Beyond I-Con, a framework that enables systematic discovery of novel loss functions by exploring alternative statistical divergences. Key findings: (1) on unsupervised clustering of DINO-ViT embeddings, we achieve state-of-the-art results by modifying the PMI algorithm to use total variation (TV) distance; (2) supervised contrastive learning with Euclidean distance as the feature space metric is improved by replacing the standard loss function with Jenson-Shannon divergence (JSD); (3) on dimensionality reduction, we achieve superior qualitative results and better performance on downstream tasks than SNE by replacing KL with a bounded $f$-divergence. Our results highlight the importance of considering divergence choices in representation learning optimization.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central focus is on improving representation learning by systematically exploring alternative statistical divergences beyond KL divergence in the I-Con framework.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is a systematic framework for discovering novel loss functions by exploring f-divergences as alternatives to KL divergence in representation learning objectives.

conceptDetails.name: f-divergence generalization
conceptDetails.description: A novel framework contribution replacing KL divergence with any f-divergence (TV, JSD, Hellinger) in the I-Con objective, enabling systematic discovery of superior loss functions.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper improves supervised contrastive learning (SupCon) on CIFAR-10 by replacing KL divergence with Jensen-Shannon divergence, achieving state-of-the-art classification accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies alternative f-divergences to SNE for dimensionality reduction, resolving the crowding problem caused by KL divergence and achieving better cluster separation.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: The paper achieves state-of-the-art ImageNet-1K unsupervised clustering by modifying the PMI algorithm to use total variation distance instead of KL divergence.

conceptDetails.name: optimization landscape
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes how KL divergence's unboundedness and asymmetry cause gradient instability and crowding, motivating bounded f-divergences for more stable optimization.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses DINO-ViT embeddings for unsupervised clustering experiments and evaluates the quality of learned embeddings across multiple downstream tasks.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper builds on self-supervised DINO-ViT embeddings and the I-Con framework, which unifies self-supervised and unsupervised representation learning methods.

title: Attention is all you need to solve chiral superconductivity
abstract: Recent advances on neural quantum states have shown that correlations between quantum particles can be efficiently captured by {\it attention} -- a foundation of modern neural architectures that enables neural networks to learn the relation between objects. In this work, we show that a general-purpose self-attention Fermi neural network is able to find chiral $p_x \pm i p_y$ superconductivity in an attractive Fermi gas by energy minimization, {\it without prior knowledge or bias towards pairing}. The superconducting state is identified from the optimized wavefunction by measuring various physical observables: the pair binding energy, the total angular momentum of the ground state, and off-diagonal long-range order in the two-body reduced density matrix. Our work paves the way for AI-driven discovery of unconventional and topological superconductivity in strongly correlated quantum materials.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Self-attention is the core architectural component of the Fermi neural network wavefunction, enabling the network to capture electron correlations without prior physical bias.

conceptDetails.name: chiral superconductivity
conceptDetails.description: The topological px±ipy superconducting phase with broken time-reversal symmetry is the central physical phenomenon discovered and characterized by the neural network approach.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A transformer neural network generates the many-body orbitals that form the variational wavefunction ansatz for the quantum many-body problem.

conceptDetails.name: neural quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Deep neural networks parameterize many-body wavefunctions as a new class of variational ansatz capable of capturing complex quantum correlations in fermionic systems.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Neural quantum states serve as the variational ansatz representing the many-body wavefunction of interacting fermions in two dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo is the optimization framework used to minimize energy and find the superconducting ground state wavefunction.

conceptDetails.name: topological quantum matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper establishes topological nontriviality of the chiral superconductor through the odd-even effect in pair-binding energy, characterizing the system's topological properties.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The self-attention layers are designed to be permutation-equivariant, enforcing fermionic antisymmetry through generalized Slater determinants.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The chiral px±ipy superconducting state spontaneously breaks time-reversal symmetry, which the neural network discovers without any built-in bias toward pairing.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The neural network variational approach provides an accurate simulation of strongly correlated quantum many-body physics beyond perturbative methods like BCS mean-field theory.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes the quantum phase transition from a normal Fermi gas to a topological chiral superconductor as a function of interaction strength.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The general-purpose network autonomously identifies chiral superconductivity from first principles without problem-specific architectural modifications or pretraining.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: The work demonstrates a pathway for AI-driven discovery of unconventional and topological superconductivity in strongly correlated quantum materials.

title: The Optimiser Hidden in Plain Sight: Training with the Loss Landscape's Induced Metric
abstract: We present a class of novel optimisers for training neural networks that makes use of the Riemannian metric naturally induced when the loss landscape is embedded in higher-dimensional space. This is the same metric that underlies common visualisations of loss landscapes. By taking this geometric perspective literally and using the induced metric, we develop a new optimiser and compare it to existing methods, namely: SGD, Adam, AdamW, and Muon, across a range of tasks and architectures. Empirically, we conclude that this new class of optimisers is highly effective in low dimensional examples, and provides slight improvement over state-of-the-art methods for training neural networks. These new optimisers have theoretically desirable properties. In particular, the effective learning rate is automatically decreased in regions of high curvature acting as a smoothed out form of gradient clipping. Similarly, one variant of these optimisers can also be viewed as inducing an effective scheduled learning rate and decoupled weight decay is the natural choice from our geometric perspective. The basic method can be used to modify any existing preconditioning method. The new optimiser has a computational complexity comparable to that of Adam.

conceptDetails.name: induced riemannian metric
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution: a novel optimizer derived from the Riemannian metric naturally induced when the loss landscape is embedded in higher-dimensional space.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies differential geometry — specifically Riemannian manifold theory — as the mathematical foundation for deriving new optimization algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: loss landscape geometry
conceptDetails.description: The geometric structure of the loss landscape, particularly its curvature under the induced metric, is used to adaptively control optimizer step sizes.

conceptDetails.name: curvature-adaptive steps
conceptDetails.description: A novel property of the induced-metric optimizers whereby effective learning rates are automatically reduced in high-curvature regions, acting as implicit gradient clipping.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper designs a new class of optimizers by embedding the loss function geometrically and deriving gradient flow in the induced metric space.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key claim is that the induced-metric optimizers achieve O(N) computational complexity — comparable to Adam — making them practical for large-scale neural network training.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Transformer models trained on the TinyShakespeare language modeling task are used as one benchmark to evaluate the new optimizers.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: ResNet-18 trained on CIFAR-10 serves as a benchmark for evaluating the induced-metric optimizers on image classification.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Hamiltonian dynamics are cited as a precedent for physics-inspired optimization methods, situating the paper within a broader tradition of differential-geometry-based approaches.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: MLP regression on high-order polynomials is used as a benchmark demonstrating optimizer performance on function approximation tasks.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: MNIST and CIFAR-10 classification experiments benchmark the new optimizers against SGD, Adam, AdamW, and Muon.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: Lagrangian dynamics are referenced alongside Hamiltonian methods as prior physics-motivated optimization frameworks that inspired this geometric approach.

title: Any-Order Flexible Length Masked Diffusion
abstract: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models over discrete domains. MDMs generate sequences in an any-order, parallel fashion, enabling fast inference and strong performance on non-causal tasks. However, a crucial limitation is that they do not support token insertions and are thus limited to fixed-length generations. To this end, we introduce Flexible Masked Diffusion Models (FlexMDMs), a discrete diffusion paradigm that simultaneously can model sequences of flexible length while provably retaining MDMs' flexibility of any-order inference. Grounded in an extension of the stochastic interpolant framework, FlexMDMs generate sequences by inserting mask tokens and unmasking them. Empirically, we show that FlexMDMs match MDMs in perplexity while modeling length statistics with much higher fidelity. On a synthetic maze planning task, they achieve $\approx 60 \%$ higher success rate than MDM baselines. Finally, we show pretrained MDMs can easily be retrofitted into FlexMDMs: on 16 H100s, it takes only three days to fine-tune LLaDA-8B into a FlexMDM, achieving superior performance on math (GSM8K, $58\% \to 67\%$) and code infilling performance ($52\% \to 65\%$).

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) form the foundational paradigm that FlexMDMs extend, using discrete masking and unmasking processes for sequence generation.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) provide the mathematical backbone for defining the FlexMDM generative process and its theoretical guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: masked diffusion
conceptDetails.description: The any-order discrete generation paradigm central to this paper, extended by FlexMDMs to support variable-length token insertion and unmasking.

conceptDetails.name: variable-length generation
conceptDetails.description: The core novel contribution: enabling generative models over discrete sequences to produce outputs of flexible rather than fixed length.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: Discrete flow matching provides theoretical grounding for FlexMDMs, connecting MDMs to interpolant-based frameworks for sequence generation.

conceptDetails.name: joint interpolant
conceptDetails.description: A novel theoretical extension of stochastic interpolants introduced to jointly model token identity and sequence length in a single continuous-time framework.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: FlexMDM is a new discrete generative model architecture for sequences, evaluated on language modeling, planning, math, and code infilling.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Pretrained MDMs such as LLaDA-8B are retrofitted into FlexMDMs via fine-tuning, demonstrating efficient adaptation to variable-length generation.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The retrofitting procedure leverages MDM pretraining as initialization, enabling transfer of learned representations to the FlexMDM objective.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Perplexity is used as the primary evaluation metric to compare FlexMDMs against MDM baselines on language modeling quality.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: LLaDA-8B, a transformer-based masked diffusion language model, serves as the base model for large-scale FlexMDM fine-tuning experiments.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Fine-tuning at 8B+ parameter scale on 16 H100s in three days demonstrates the practical scalability of the FlexMDM approach.

title: GW-YOLO: Multi-transient segmentation in LIGO using computer vision
abstract: Time series data and their time-frequency representation from gravitational-wave interferometers present multiple opportunities for the use of artificial intelligence methods associated with signal and image processing. Closely connected with this is the real-time aspect associated with gravitational-wave interferometers and the astrophysical observations they perform; the discovery potential of these instruments can be significantly enhanced when data processing can be achieved in O(1s) timescales. In this work, we introduce a novel signal and noise identification tool based on the YOLO (You Only Look Once) object detection framework. For its application into gravitational waves, we will refer to it as GW-YOLO. This tool can provide scene identification capabilities and essential information regarding whether an observed transient is any combination of noise and signal. Additionally, it supplies detailed time-frequency coordinates of the detected objects in the form of pixel masks, an essential property that can be used to understand and characterize astrophysical sources, as well as instrumental noise. The simultaneous identification of noise and signal, combined with precise pixel-level localization, represents a significant advancement in gravitational-wave data analysis. Our approach yields a 50\% detection efficiency for binary black hole signals at a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 15 when such signals overlap with transient noise artifacts. When noise artifacts overlap with binary neutron star signals, our algorithm attains 50\% detection efficiency at an SNR of 30. This presents the first quantitative assessment of the ability to detect astrophysical events overlapping with realistic, instrument noise present in gravitational-wave interferometers.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core domain — GW-YOLO is designed to detect and localize gravitational-wave signals from binary black hole and binary neutron star mergers in LIGO interferometer data.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: GW-YOLO is built on the YOLO (You Only Look Once) convolutional neural network architecture, adapted for real-time detection and segmentation of transients in time-frequency images.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary goal is detecting astrophysical GW signals overlapping with instrumental noise transients, providing quantitative detection efficiency benchmarks at varying SNR levels.

conceptDetails.name: instance segmentation
conceptDetails.description: Novel application of YOLO's instance segmentation capability — outputting pixel masks per detected transient — enabling precise time-frequency localization of individual signals and glitches.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: GW-YOLO simultaneously identifies and localizes non-astrophysical noise transients (glitches) alongside real signals, treating glitch detection as an anomaly segmentation task.

conceptDetails.name: glitch mitigation
conceptDetails.description: A core motivation and application of GW-YOLO is automating the identification and bounding of instrumental glitches to facilitate downstream noise subtraction pipelines like BayesWave.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Time-frequency representations (Q-transforms and spectrograms) of interferometer strain data serve as the image inputs to GW-YOLO, making spectral decomposition a core preprocessing step.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The model classifies each detected object in the time-frequency image as one of several categories (BBH, BNS, or specific glitch morphologies) alongside its segmentation mask.

conceptDetails.name: real-time gw inference
conceptDetails.description: GW-YOLO achieves O(1s) inference latency, enabling real-time astrophysical alerts and noise characterization during observing runs — a key novelty over offline analysis methods.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The YOLO backbone extracts hierarchical morphological features from Q-transform images to distinguish astrophysical signals from noise transients by their time-frequency shape.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: GW-YOLO adapts a pretrained YOLO architecture to the gravitational-wave domain through fine-tuning on synthetic and real LIGO transient data.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering pixel-level time-frequency coordinates of astrophysical sources from noisy detector images constitutes an inverse problem that GW-YOLO addresses through instance segmentation.

title: Training a Foundation Model for Materials on a Budget
abstract: Foundation models for materials modeling are advancing quickly, but their training remains expensive, often placing state-of-the-art methods out of reach for many research groups. We introduce Nequix, a compact E(3)-equivariant potential that pairs a simplified NequIP design with modern training practices, including equivariant root-mean-square layer normalization and the Muon optimizer, to retain accuracy while substantially reducing compute requirements. Nequix has 700K parameters and was trained in 100 A100 GPU-hours. On the Matbench-Discovery and MDR Phonon benchmarks, Nequix ranks third overall while requiring a 20 times lower training cost than most other methods, and it delivers two orders of magnitude faster inference speed than the current top-ranked model. We release model weights and fully reproducible codebase at https://github.com/atomicarchitects/nequix.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Nequix is built on E(3)-equivariant architecture (NequIP), ensuring the model respects rotational and translational symmetries of atomic systems.

conceptDetails.name: interatomic potentials
conceptDetails.description: Nequix is a machine-learned interatomic potential (MLIP) trained to predict energies, forces, and stresses for atomistic materials simulations.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: Nequix is evaluated on Matbench-Discovery, a benchmark for high-throughput materials screening and stability prediction.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central contribution is achieving competitive accuracy at dramatically reduced training cost (100 GPU-hours, 20x cheaper than comparable models).

conceptDetails.name: budget-conscious training
conceptDetails.description: Novel training recipe combining the Muon optimizer and dynamic batching to achieve fast convergence with minimal compute resources.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Nequix uses a message-passing graph neural network over atomic radius graphs to aggregate neighborhood information for energy prediction.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The architecture leverages geometric deep learning principles, encoding physical symmetries (E(3) group) into the network structure.

conceptDetails.name: molecular dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The model enables fast molecular dynamics simulations by providing efficient energy and force predictions for atomic systems.

conceptDetails.name: phonon properties
conceptDetails.description: Nequix is benchmarked on the MDR Phonon benchmark, evaluating its ability to predict phonon mode contributions to thermal conductivity.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The equivariant RMSNorm layer normalization is designed to preserve E(3) symmetry throughout the network while improving training stability.

conceptDetails.name: dynamic batching
conceptDetails.description: Technique to fill GPU batches up to node/edge caps rather than fixed graph counts, improving GPU utilization for variable-size atomic graphs.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Nequix releases pretrained model weights trained on MPtrj for downstream fine-tuning on specific materials tasks.

title: Efficient Constraint-Aware Flow Matching via Randomized Exploration
abstract: We consider the problem of generating samples via Flow Matching (FM) with an additional requirement that the generated samples must satisfy given constraints. We consider two scenarios, viz.: (a) when a differentiable distance function to the constraint set is given, and (b) when the constraint set is only available via queries to a membership oracle. For case (a), we propose a simple adaptation of the FM objective with an additional term that penalizes the distance between the constraint set and the generated samples. For case (b), we propose to employ randomization and learn a mean flow that is numerically shown to have a high likelihood of satisfying the constraints. This approach deviates significantly from existing works that require simple convex constraints, knowledge of a barrier function, or a reflection mechanism to constrain the probability flow. Furthermore, in the proposed setting we show that a two-stage approach, where both stages approximate the same original flow but with only the second stage probing the constraints via randomization, is more computationally efficient. Through several synthetic cases of constrained generation, we numerically show that the proposed approaches achieve significant gains in terms of constraint satisfaction while matching the target distributions. As a showcase for a practical oracle-based constraint, we show how our approach can be used for training an adversarial example generator, using queries to a hard-label black-box classifier. We conclude with several future research directions. Our code is available at https://github.com/ZhengyanHuan/FM-RE.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: The core generative modeling framework extended in this paper to support constrained sample generation via modified objectives and randomized exploration.

conceptDetails.name: constraint-aware generation
conceptDetails.description: Novel formulation of the generative modeling problem requiring generated samples to lie within a specified constraint set, the central contribution of this paper.

conceptDetails.name: randomized exploration
conceptDetails.description: The proposed strategy for learning a mean flow that satisfies oracle-based constraints by stochastically probing the constraint boundary during training.

conceptDetails.name: membership oracle
conceptDetails.description: A black-box constraint specification that returns a binary indicator of constraint membership, enabling more general constrained generation than prior convex or barrier-function approaches.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The broader class of implicit generative models (score-based, diffusion, bridge matching) that provide context for the constraint-aware flow matching contribution.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes a modified FM objective with a distance-penalty term (case a) and a randomized mean-flow objective (case b) for constraint satisfaction.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical foundation of the probability flow ODEs and stochastic interpolants that underpin the flow matching framework.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Used to approximate the intractable flow matching objective and to perform randomized exploration of the constraint set.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: Used as a coupling strategy for the stochastic interpolant in flow matching, referenced as a key prior improvement to the FM framework.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Motivates the adversarial example generation application, where the constraint oracle is a hard-label black-box classifier.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: The randomized exploration strategy for probing the membership oracle shares conceptual overlap with active querying of unknown constraint sets.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: A related generative modeling paradigm cited as background context alongside flow matching and diffusion models.

title: The Neutrino Kaleidoscope: Searches for Non-Standard Neutrino Oscillations at Neutrino Telescopes with a TeV Muon Accelerator Source
abstract: Muon accelerators, a potential technology for enabling $\mathcal{O}$(10 TeV) parton center of mass energy collisions, would also source an intense, collimated beam of neutrinos at TeV energies. The energy and size of this beam would be excellently matched as a source for existing and planned neutrino telescopes: gigaton-sized detectors of astrophysical neutrinos at and above TeV energies. In this paper, we introduce the technical considerations and scientific reach of pairing a muon accelerator source of neutrinos with a neutrino telescope detector, a combination we dub the ''Neutrino Kaleidoscope''. In particular, such a pairing would enable searches for non-standard oscillations of the beam neutrinos as they traverse the earth between source and detector. These non-standard neutrino oscillations could be sourced by Lorentz invariance violation, which a neutrino kaleidoscope could probe up to the quantum gravity-motivated Planck scale. Such a search would also have a reach on sterile neutrinos orders of magnitude beyond existing terrestrial limits. Finally, we touch on some of the non-oscillation potential of a neutrino kaleidoscope.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: Neutrino telescopes (IceCube, KM3NeT, P-ONE) serve as the far detectors for the proposed neutrino kaleidoscope, detecting TeV-energy beam neutrinos traversing the Earth.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is designing searches for non-standard neutrino oscillations as signatures of beyond-Standard-Model physics.

conceptDetails.name: sterile neutrinos
conceptDetails.description: Searches for active-to-sterile neutrino oscillations at eV-scale mass splittings are a primary physics target, with sensitivity orders of magnitude beyond existing limits.

conceptDetails.name: lorentz invariance violation
conceptDetails.description: Non-standard oscillations sourced by Lorentz invariance violation are probed up to the Planck scale using the long Earth-traversing baseline.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: A TeV muon accelerator/collider serves as the neutrino source, and its beam parameters (emittance, divergence, muon decay rates) are central to the experimental design.

conceptDetails.name: matter resonance oscillations
conceptDetails.description: MSW-like matter resonance effects for sterile neutrino oscillations in the Earth's mantle and core are a key feature enabling sensitivity at TeV energies.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Lorentz invariance violation is parameterized via effective field theory operators suppressed by the Planck scale, providing the theoretical framework for new physics sensitivity.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Sensitivity estimates are derived using statistical hypothesis testing to distinguish standard from non-standard oscillation scenarios.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: Standard neutrino oscillations within the Standard Model provide the baseline against which non-standard effects (sterile neutrinos, Lorentz violation) are searched.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations of neutrino fluxes and detector responses are used to estimate event rates and oscillation sensitivities.

title: The DNA of nuclear models: How AI predicts nuclear masses
abstract: Obtaining high-precision predictions of nuclear masses, or equivalently nuclear binding energies, $E_b$, remains an important goal in nuclear-physics research. Recently, many AI-based tools have shown promising results on this task, some achieving precision that surpasses the best physics models. However, the utility of these AI models remains in question given that predictions are only useful where measurements do not exist, which inherently requires extrapolation away from the training (and testing) samples. Since AI models are largely black boxes, the reliability of such an extrapolation is difficult to assess. We present an AI model that not only achieves cutting-edge precision for $E_b$, but does so in an interpretable manner. For example, we find that (and explain why) the most important dimensions of its internal representation form a double helix, where the analog of the hydrogen bonds in DNA here link the number of protons and neutrons found in the most stable nucleus of each isotopic chain. Furthermore, we show that the AI prediction of $E_b$ can be factorized and ordered hierarchically, with the most important terms corresponding to well-known symbolic models (such as the famous liquid drop). Remarkably, the improvement of the AI model over symbolic ones can almost entirely be attributed to an observation made by Jaffe in 1969 based on the structure of most known nuclear ground states. The end result is a fully interpretable data-driven model of nuclear masses based on physics deduced by AI.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central finding is that the AI's internal representations form a double helix structure, with the most important dimensions encoding proton and neutron numbers of the most stable nucleus in each isotopic chain.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The primary contribution is a fully interpretable AI model whose predictions are hierarchically factorized into physically meaningful terms, revealing what the AI learned about nuclear physics.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear binding energy
conceptDetails.description: Nuclear binding energy is the primary prediction target, with the AI achieving 0.13 MeV RMS precision—roughly twice the precision of the best symbolic physics models.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The AI model is trained with a multi-task learning approach to simultaneously predict nuclear binding energies, separation energies, and charge radii from a shared internal representation.

conceptDetails.name: liquid drop model
conceptDetails.description: The classical semi-empirical liquid drop formula is recovered as the dominant term in the hierarchical factorization of the AI's predictions, validating the AI's learned physics.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: High-dimensional neural network representations are analyzed to reveal that the most important low-dimensional subspace encodes nuclear structure in a double helix geometry.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear shell structure
conceptDetails.description: Shell structure effects encoded in Jaffe's 1969 observation about nuclear ground state configurations are identified by the AI as the primary source of improvement over symbolic models.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The AI prediction of binding energy is hierarchically factorized and ordered, with dominant terms corresponding to the liquid drop model and subsequent terms adding physically interpretable corrections.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Predicting nuclear binding energies from proton and neutron numbers is cast as a regression task, evaluated by RMS error against experimental measurements.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The interpretable AI model serves as a data-driven surrogate for nuclear mass predictions, replacing intractable first-principles calculations for larger nuclei.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses the reliability of AI extrapolation to unmeasured highly unstable nuclei, framing interpretability as a prerequisite for trustworthy out-of-sample predictions.

title: Detecting Model Misspecification in Cosmology with Scale-Dependent Normalizing Flows
abstract: Current and upcoming cosmological surveys will produce unprecedented amounts of high-dimensional data, which require complex high-fidelity forward simulations to accurately model both physical processes and systematic effects which describe the data generation process. However, validating whether our theoretical models accurately describe the observed datasets remains a fundamental challenge. An additional complexity to this task comes from choosing appropriate representations of the data which retain all the relevant cosmological information, while reducing the dimensionality of the original dataset. In this work we present a novel framework combining scale-dependent neural summary statistics with normalizing flows to detect model misspecification in cosmological simulations through Bayesian evidence estimation. By conditioning our neural network models for data compression and evidence estimation on the smoothing scale, we systematically identify where theoretical models break down in a data-driven manner. We demonstrate a first application to our approach using matter and gas density fields from three CAMELS simulation suites with different subgrid physics implementations.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Used as the core density estimation method to model the distribution of cosmological fields and compute Bayesian evidence for model misspecification detection.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The central task of the paper: detecting when cosmological observations deviate from the theoretical model used in training simulations.

conceptDetails.name: scale-dependent inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel method of conditioning neural networks on the smoothing scale to identify at which spatial scales theoretical models break down.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: CAMELS simulation suites with different subgrid physics implementations are used as the test bed for the misspecification detection framework.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The framework provides a systematic, data-driven method for validating whether cosmological theoretical models accurately describe observed datasets.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The broader framework in which neural networks are trained on cosmological simulations to perform inference without explicit likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian evidence estimation is used to quantify how well a theoretical model explains observed data at different scales.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Scale-dependent normalizing flows estimate the probability density of cosmological fields to identify low-likelihood out-of-distribution regions.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The method flags anomalous data points by identifying regions of low probability under the trained density model, enabling model-independent anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Neural summary statistics are learned to compress high-dimensional cosmological fields while retaining cosmologically relevant information.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Exact marginal likelihoods from normalizing flows are used to compute Bayesian evidence ratios as a function of spatial scale.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Tensions in cosmological parameters including dark energy evolution from DESI BAO measurements motivate the need for model misspecification detection.

title: Identification and photometric classification of extragalactic transients in the Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Data Preview 1
abstract: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory will soon survey the southern sky, delivering a depth and sky coverage that is unprecedented in time domain astronomy. As part of commissioning, Data Preview 1 (DP1) has been released. It comprises a LSSTComCam observing campaign between November and December 2024 with multi-band imaging of seven fields, covering roughly 0.4 square degrees each, providing a first glimpse into the data products that will become available once the Legacy Survey of Space and Time begins. In this work, we search three fields for extragalactic transients. We identify eight new likely supernovae, and three known ones from a sample of 369,644 difference image analysis objects. Photometric classification using Superphot+ assigns sub-classes with >95% confidence to only one SN Ia and one SN II in this sample. Our findings are in agreement with supernova detection rate predictions of $15\pm4$ supernovae from simulations using simsurvey. The supernova detection rate in the data is possibly affected by the lack of suitable templates. Nevertheless, this work demonstrates the quality of the data products delivered in DP1 and indicates that the Rubin Observatory's Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) is well placed to fulfill its discovery potential in time domain astronomy.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Photometric classification of supernovae into sub-types (SN Ia, SN II) using the Superphot+ algorithm applied to multi-band light curves from Rubin DP1 data.

conceptDetails.name: difference image analysis
conceptDetails.description: Core observational method subtracting reference templates from science images to isolate flux-variable sources across 0.4 sq. deg. fields.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Machine learning classification of 369,644 difference image analysis objects to identify likely supernovae with >95% confidence thresholds.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detection of extragalactic transient signals (supernovae, AGN, TDEs) from multi-band difference imaging of Rubin Observatory commissioning fields.

conceptDetails.name: photometric light curve fitting
conceptDetails.description: Parametric fitting of multi-band photometric time series to extract supernova features for classification and redshift estimation.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Confidence calibration of photometric classifier outputs, with Superphot+ assigning sub-type labels only when confidence exceeds 95%.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Quantification of classification confidence scores and supernova detection rate uncertainties (15±4 SNe predicted vs. observed).

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Identification of genuine astrophysical transients from a large catalog of difference image artifacts, variable stars, and extragalactic sources.

conceptDetails.name: transient survey pipeline
conceptDetails.description: Automated detection-to-classification pipeline architecture designed to process up to 10 million nightly alerts from the Rubin Observatory.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations via simsurvey to predict expected supernova detection rates for the DP1 observing campaign.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Comparison of observed transient yields against forward-simulated survey predictions to validate data quality and pipeline completeness.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: End-to-end LSST alert generation, difference image analysis pipelines, and broker workflows evaluated on commissioning data.

title: Analytic Regression of Feynman Integrals from High-Precision Numerical Sampling
abstract: In mathematics or theoretical physics one is often interested in obtaining an exact analytic description of some data which can be produced, in principle, to arbitrary accuracy. For example, one might like to know the exact analytical form of a definite integral. Such problems are not well-suited to numerical symbolic regression, since typical numerical methods lead only to approximations. However, if one has some sense of the function space in which the analytic result should lie, it is possible to deduce the exact answer by judiciously sampling the data at a sufficient number of points with sufficient precision. We demonstrate how this can be done for the computation of Feynman integrals. We show that by combining high-precision numerical integration with analytic knowledge of the function space one can often deduce the exact answer using lattice reduction. A number of examples are given as well as an exploration of the trade-offs between number of datapoints, number of functional predicates, precision of the data, and compute. This method provides a bottom-up approach that neatly complements the top-down Landau-bootstrap approach of trying to constrain the exact answer using the analytic structure alone. Although we focus on the application to Feynman integrals, the techniques presented here are more general and could apply to a wide range of problems where an exact answer is needed and the function space is sufficiently well understood.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops methods to analytically reconstruct Feynman integrals, which are fundamental building blocks of scattering amplitude calculations in quantum field theory.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Analytic regression is the core method of the paper — fitting exact rational coefficients to decompose Feynman integrals in a known function space basis.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within the framework of QFT, computing multi-loop Feynman integrals that arise in perturbative quantum field theory calculations.

conceptDetails.name: high-precision numerical integration
conceptDetails.description: The method requires numerical evaluations of Feynman integrals to tens or hundreds of digits of precision, enabled by tools like AMFlow.

conceptDetails.name: lattice reduction
conceptDetails.description: LLL lattice reduction is the central algorithmic technique used to recover exact rational coefficients from high-precision floating-point numerical data.

conceptDetails.name: function space basis
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept capturing the use of analytically motivated basis functions (polylogarithms, iterated integrals) as the hypothesis space for analytic regression.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing the exact analytic form of a Feynman integral from numerical samples is an inverse problem — inferring the function from its evaluations.

conceptDetails.name: landau bootstrap
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly complements the top-down Landau-bootstrap approach (constraining integrals from analytic structure) with this bottom-up numerical sampling strategy.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper contrasts analytic regression (exact, within a known function space) against standard symbolic regression (approximate, noisy data), positioning itself as a new paradigm.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: High-precision numerical integration of Feynman integrals relies on numerical sampling techniques including Monte Carlo-based approaches for multi-loop integrals.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The method exploits sparsity in the coefficient vector (many ci are zero) to reduce the number of required sample points and basis functions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Multi-loop Feynman integral computations are central to EFT matching calculations, and the examples include diagrams relevant to higher-order EFT corrections.

title: Mixture-of-Expert Variational Autoencoders for Cross-Modality Embedding of Type Ia Supernova Data
abstract: Time-domain astrophysics relies on heterogeneous and multi-modal data. Specialized models are often constructed to extract information from a single modality, but this approach ignores the wealth of cross-modality information that may be relevant for the tasks to which the model is applied. In this work, we propose a multi-modal, mixture-of-expert variational autoencoder to learn a joint embedding for supernova light curves and spectra. Our method, which is inspired by the Perceiver architecture, natively accommodates variable-length inputs and the irregular temporal sampling inherent to supernova light curves. We train our model on radiative transfer simulations and validate its performance on cross-modality reconstruction of supernova spectra and physical parameters from the simulation. Our model achieves superior performance in cross-modality generation to nearest-neighbor searches in a contrastively-trained latent space, showing its promise for constructing informative latent representations of multi-modal astronomical datasets.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The MMVAE (Mixture-of-Expert VAE) is the core architecture for learning joint latent representations of supernova light curves and spectra.

conceptDetails.name: mixture of experts
conceptDetails.description: The mixture-of-experts formulation combines per-modality encoders into a shared latent distribution, enabling both private and shared feature learning across photometry and spectra.

conceptDetails.name: cross-modality generation
conceptDetails.description: The primary novel task: inferring time-evolving supernova spectra solely from photometric light curve observations, a many-to-many mapping problem.

conceptDetails.name: perceiver architectures
