conceptDetails.description: The proposed 'transceiver' encoder-decoder is directly inspired by Perceiver-IO, using cross-attention between a fixed-size latent query and variable-length inputs to handle irregular astronomical time series.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central goal is learning informative joint latent representations of multi-modal supernova data suitable for downstream astrophysical tasks.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Cross-attention is the mechanism by which the transceiver compresses variable-length light curve and spectral embeddings into a fixed-size latent space.

conceptDetails.name: irregular time series encoding
conceptDetails.description: Sinusoidal temporal encodings and masking enable the model to natively handle the non-uniform cadence and variable lengths of real astronomical light curve observations.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The MMVAE framework explicitly models both private (modality-specific) and shared latent variables to disentangle information across light curves and spectra.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Sinusoidal positional encodings, filter class embeddings, and linear projections are combined to produce dense embeddings for irregular photometric and spectroscopic sequences.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: A contrastively-trained latent space is used as the primary baseline against which the MMVAE's cross-modality generation is evaluated.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained on radiative transfer simulations of Type Ia supernovae, using simulated light curves and spectra as paired training data.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Type Ia supernovae are the astrophysical target, and the model's latent representations are intended to support characterization and identification of unusual SNe Ia in survey data.

title: reLAISS: A Python Package for Flexible Similarity Searches of Supernovae and Their Host Galaxies
abstract: Discovery rates of supernovae are expected to surpass one million events annually with the Vera C. Rubin Observatory. With unprecedented sample sizes of both common and rare transient types, photometric classification alone will be insufficient for finding one-in-a-million events and prioritizing the 1% of events for spectroscopic follow-up observations. Here, we present reLAISS, a modified framework for similarity searches of supernovae using extracted features of ZTF light curves and Pan-STARRS host galaxy photometry and built on the original LAISS framework. Unlike its predecessor, reLAISS couples interpretable light curve morphology features with extinction-corrected host-galaxy colors to probe both explosion physics and associated stellar populations simultaneously. The library allows users to customize the number of neighbors retrieved, the weight of host and light curve features, and the use of Monte Carlo simulations to ensure relevant matches when features are poorly constrained. We release reLAISS as a pip-installable package with an accompanying reference set of 20,000 features, and a set of tutorials that demonstrate the code's expanded functionality. All source code can be found at https://github.com/evan-reynolds/re-laiss .

conceptDetails.name: transient similarity search
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is a flexible nearest-neighbor retrieval framework that matches supernovae by combined light curve and host galaxy feature spaces.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: reLAISS performs similarity-based classification and retrieval of supernovae using extracted photometric and host galaxy features.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The package computes interpretable light curve morphology features (peak magnitude, rise/decline times, curvature) and host galaxy colors as the basis for similarity searches.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: reLAISS assigns calibrated anomaly scores using a Generalized Pareto distribution fit to nearest-neighbor distances, replacing a previous random forest approach.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations are used to re-query and re-rank neighbors when extracted features have high uncertainties, ensuring robust matches.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The anomaly scoring pipeline identifies rare or anomalous transients that deviate from the reference distribution of 20,000 archival events.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Host galaxy photometric colors from Pan-STARRS are incorporated as features to constrain the stellar populations associated with each supernova.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The Prost library uses Bayesian posterior estimation to associate transients with their most probable host galaxy from Pan-STARRS.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: reLAISS is released as a pip-installable Python package with tutorials, enabling scalable automated workflows for transient follow-up prioritization.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Measured feature uncertainties are propagated through Monte Carlo re-querying to quantify their impact on neighbor retrieval.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Locality-sensitive hashing via ANNOY builds an indexed feature space enabling approximate nearest-neighbor searches across high-dimensional feature vectors.

title: CosmoFlow: Scale-Aware Representation Learning for Cosmology with Flow Matching
abstract: Generative machine learning models have been demonstrated to be able to learn low dimensional representations of data that preserve information required for downstream tasks. In this work, we demonstrate that flow matching based generative models can learn compact, semantically rich latent representations of field level cold dark matter (CDM) simulation data without supervision. Our model, CosmoFlow, learns representations 32x smaller than the raw field data, usable for field level reconstruction, synthetic data generation, and parameter inference. Our model also learns interpretable representations, in which different latent channels correspond to features at different cosmological scales.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: The core generative modeling paradigm used in CosmoFlow, learning a time-dependent velocity field to map noise samples to cosmological field data.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The central objective of CosmoFlow: learning compact, semantically rich latent representations of cold dark matter simulation data without supervision.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The primary data domain — cold dark matter (CDM) field simulations from datasets like CAMELS and AbacusSummit that CosmoFlow learns to encode and reconstruct.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: CosmoFlow compresses 256x256 field data to representations 32x smaller while preserving information needed for downstream cosmological tasks.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: CosmoFlow is a generative framework enabling reconstruction, synthetic data generation, and parameter inference from learned latent representations.

conceptDetails.name: scale-aware latent space
conceptDetails.description: Novel contribution where CosmoFlow's latent channels are organized by cosmological scale, enabling targeted interpolation of high- or low-frequency structure.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The physical phenomenon being modeled — cold dark matter density fields form the input data for CosmoFlow's representation learning.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: Different latent channels in CosmoFlow encode features at distinct cosmological scales (high vs. low frequency), demonstrating scale-aware disentanglement.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: CosmoFlow learns disentangled latent channels corresponding to features at different cosmological scales, enabling interpretable representations.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: CosmoFlow's latent representations are used for cosmological parameter inference with accuracy equivalent to estimation on raw field data.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Used as a baseline comparison; VAEs produce blurry reconstructions that lose high-frequency cosmological details, motivating the flow matching approach.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The compressed latent space enables fast synthetic data generation as a surrogate for expensive full cosmological simulations.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A ResNet encoder and UNet-based velocity field predictor form the core neural architecture of CosmoFlow.

title: The $\mathcal{D}$-Geometric Hilbert Scheme -- Part I: Involutivity and Stability
abstract: We construct a moduli space of formally integrable and involutive ideal sheaves arising from systems of partial differential equations (PDEs) in the algebro-geometric setting, by introducing the $\mathcal{D}$-Hilbert and $\mathcal{D}$-Quot functors in the sense of Grothendieck and establishing their representability. Central to this construction is the notion of Spencer (semi-)stability, which presents an extension of classical stability conditions from gauge theory and complex geometry, and which provides the boundedness needed for our moduli problem. As an application, we show that for flat connections on compact Kähler manifolds, Spencer poly-stability of the associated PDE ideal is equivalent to the existence of a Hermitian-Yang-Mills metric. This result provides a refinement of the classical Donaldson-Uhlenbeck-Yau correspondence, and identifies Spencer cohomology and stability as a unifying framework for geometric PDEs.

conceptDetails.name: spencer cohomology
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central unifying framework: a cohomological invariant of PDE systems that generalizes de Rham, Dolbeault, and foliated cohomologies and defines the new stability condition.

conceptDetails.name: moduli of pde systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary construction: a moduli space parameterizing formally integrable and involutive ideal sheaves arising from systems of PDEs in the algebro-geometric setting.

conceptDetails.name: spencer stability
conceptDetails.description: A novel stability condition for D_X-ideal sheaves extending Gieseker and slope stability from algebraic geometry, shown to be equivalent to Hermitian-Yang-Mills existence for flat connections.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Algebraic pseudogroups and Geometric Invariant Theory (GIT) are used to construct quotients of families of differential ideals and establish representability of the D-Hilbert functor.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper refines the Donaldson-Uhlenbeck-Yau correspondence, a foundational result in gauge theory connecting flat connections on Kähler manifolds to Hermitian-Yang-Mills metrics.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The D_X-algebra structure encodes the action of total derivative operators on PDE systems, preserving the differential symmetry underlying formal integrability.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Characteristic modules and symbol sequences of differential operators play a role analogous to spectral data, controlling involutivity and boundedness of the moduli problem.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: Conservation laws appear as fundamental invariants of PDE systems within Vinogradov's differential complex framework, motivating the cohomological approach to PDEs.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Determining when a PDE system admits formally integrable solutions is framed as an existence problem resolved through Spencer stability and the Hermitian-Yang-Mills criterion.

title: Proof of a perfect platonic representation hypothesis
abstract: In this note, we elaborate on and explain in detail the proof given by Ziyin et al. (2025) of the ``perfect" Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) for the embedded deep linear network model (EDLN). We show that if trained with the stochastic gradient descent (SGD), two EDLNs with different widths and depths and trained on different data will become Perfectly Platonic, meaning that every possible pair of layers will learn the same representation up to a rotation. Because most of the global minima of the loss function are not Platonic, that SGD only finds the perfectly Platonic solution is rather extraordinary. The proof also suggests at least six ways the PRH can be broken. We also show that in the EDLN model, the emergence of the Platonic representations is due to the same reason as the emergence of progressive sharpening. This implies that these two seemingly unrelated phenomena in deep learning can, surprisingly, have a common cause. Overall, the theory and proof highlight the importance of understanding emergent "entropic forces" due to the irreversibility of SGD training and their role in representation learning. The goal of this note is to be instructive while avoiding jargon and lengthy technical details.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves that SGD-trained embedded deep linear networks converge to universally aligned representations across different architectures and datasets, providing a mathematical foundation for the Platonic Representation Hypothesis.

conceptDetails.name: platonic representation hypothesis
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper: a formal proof that embedded deep linear networks trained with SGD satisfy the 'perfect' PRH, with every layer pair learning identical representations up to rotation.

conceptDetails.name: implicit regularization
conceptDetails.description: SGD's discrete-time, stochastic nature acts as an implicit regularizer that selects Platonic solutions even though most global minima of the explicit loss are non-Platonic.

conceptDetails.name: entropic forces
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept drawn from nonequilibrium physics: the irreversibility of SGD training generates effective entropic forces that drive networks toward universal, maximally disordered representations.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: SGD is analyzed as a stochastic, irreversible dynamical process whose discretization and gradient noise drive networks toward Platonic solutions via entropic forces.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: An 'entropic loss' is derived as a first-order approximation of discrete-time SGD dynamics, capturing implicit regularization effects and enabling exact analysis of the Platonic solution.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The analysis shows how SGD implicitly breaks the rotational symmetry among equivalent global minima, selecting the unique Platonic solution through entropic regularization.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Spectral analysis of weight matrices is central to characterizing global minima and proving that SGD selects the rotationally-aligned Platonic solution among all possible global minima.

conceptDetails.name: progressive sharpening
conceptDetails.description: The paper reveals that the emergence of Platonic representations and the progressive sharpening phenomenon share a common cause in the entropic loss, unifying two previously unrelated deep learning phenomena.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The proof demonstrates that the Platonic alignment holds regardless of network width and depth, establishing architecture-independent universality in representation learning.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies the precise mechanistic cause of representational universality—entropic forces from SGD irreversibility—offering a interpretable account of an empirically observed deep learning phenomenon.

title: Modeling the Cosmological Lyman-$α$ Forest at the Field Level
abstract: The distribution of absorption lines in the spectra of distant quasars, called the Lyman-$α$ (Ly-$α$) forest, is a unique probe of cosmology and the intergalactic medium at high redshifts and small scales. The statistical power of ongoing redshift surveys demands precise theoretical tools to model the Ly-$α$ forest. We address this challenge by developing an analytic, perturbative forward model to predict the Ly-$α$ forest at the field level for a given set of cosmological initial conditions. Our model shows a remarkable performance when compared with the Sherwood hydrodynamic simulations: it reproduces the flux distribution, the Ly-$α$ - dark matter halo cross-correlations, and the count-in-cell statistics at the percent level down to scales of a few Mpc. Our work provides crucial tools that bridge analytic modeling on large scales with simulations on small-scales, enabling field-level inference from Ly-$α$ forest data and simulation-based priors for cosmological analyses. This is especially timely for realizing the full scientific potential of the Ly-$α$ forest measurements by the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument.

conceptDetails.name: lyman-alpha forest
conceptDetails.description: The primary observable studied: absorption lines in quasar spectra tracing neutral hydrogen in the intergalactic medium, used as a cosmological probe.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core theoretical framework, providing a systematic first-principles description of the Ly-α field on large scales based on symmetries and dimensional analysis.

conceptDetails.name: field-level inference
conceptDetails.description: The inference methodology enabled by this work, performing cosmological parameter estimation at the level of individual Fourier modes rather than compressed summary statistics.

conceptDetails.name: perturbative bias expansion
conceptDetails.description: Novel application: the paper extends the galaxy bias EFT framework to the Ly-α forest including line-of-sight-dependent operators dictated by IGM symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Sherwood hydrodynamic simulation is used as the ground-truth benchmark to validate the analytic forward model at the percent level.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The forward model enables simulation-based priors for cosmological analyses, bridging analytic large-scale modeling with small-scale hydrodynamic simulations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Field-level posterior estimation over cosmological parameters is the primary scientific application enabled by the developed forward model.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: A key scientific target: the paper directly supports DESI Ly-α forest measurements that constrain dynamical dark energy.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The Ly-α forest traces the dark matter density field through bias expansion, and the paper validates Ly-α–dark matter halo cross-correlations.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Power spectrum analysis and Fourier-mode-by-mode comparison are the primary validation tools used to assess model accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The forward model provides a field-level likelihood for Ly-α flux fluctuations, essential for cosmological parameter inference.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Statistical sampling techniques underlying the field-level inference pipeline and uncertainty quantification for cosmological parameters.

title: Exploration Behavior of Untrained Policies
abstract: Exploration remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), particularly in environments with sparse or adversarial reward structures. In this work, we study how the architecture of deep neural policies implicitly shapes exploration before training. We theoretically and empirically demonstrate strategies for generating ballistic or diffusive trajectories from untrained policies in a toy model. Using the theory of infinite-width networks and a continuous-time limit, we show that untrained policies return correlated actions and result in non-trivial state-visitation distributions. We discuss the distributions of the corresponding trajectories for a standard architecture, revealing insights into inductive biases for tackling exploration. Our results establish a theoretical and experimental framework for using policy initialization as a design tool to understand exploration behavior in early training.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies exploration behavior in RL agents, specifically how untrained policy networks shape the initial data distribution before any reward-driven training begins.

conceptDetails.name: policy initialization
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: demonstrating that the choice of network architecture and initialization distribution is itself a design tool that controls early-training exploration behavior.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs Fokker-Planck analysis and continuous-time limits to characterize the stochastic trajectory distributions arising from random policy ensembles.

conceptDetails.name: infinite-width limit
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical framework that enables convergence of the neural policy to a Gaussian Process, allowing closed-form characterization of action covariance across states.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The Gaussian Process kernel K(s,s') governs action covariance structure in the infinite-width limit, enabling closed-form analysis of exploration geometry.

conceptDetails.name: ballistic exploration
conceptDetails.description: A novel trajectory regime identified in the paper where Lipschitz-smooth untrained networks induce near-linear, drift-dominated paths rather than diffuse random walks.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The infinite-width limit (Neal's theorem) frames the untrained policy as a Gaussian Process prior, grounding the trajectory analysis in a Bayesian perspective on network initialization.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: A random policy ensemble — resampling network parameters at each timestep — is used to generate diffusive rather than ballistic trajectories.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper aims to reveal inductive biases of standard MLP architectures by characterizing what exploration behaviors they implicitly encode before any training.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Empirical simulations of random policy trajectories are used to validate the theoretical predictions about ballistic and diffusive behavior.

title: Holography with Null Boundaries
abstract: One of the key issues in holography is going beyond $\mathrm{AdS}$ and defining quantum gravity in spacetimes with a null boundary. Recent examples of this type involve linear dilaton asymptotics and are related to the $T \overline{T}$ deformation. We present a holographic correspondence derived from string theory, which is an example of a kind of celestial holography. The holographic definition is a spacetime non-commutative open string theory supported on D1-D5 branes together with fundamental strings. The gravity solutions interpolate between $\mathrm{AdS}_3$ metrics and six-dimensional metrics. Radiation can escape to null infinity, which makes both the encoding of quantum information in the boundary and the dynamics of black holes quite different from $\mathrm{AdS}$ spacetimes.

conceptDetails.name: holography
conceptDetails.description: The central contribution is a new holographic correspondence for spacetimes with null boundaries, going beyond AdS/CFT to celestial-type holography.

conceptDetails.name: null boundary holography
conceptDetails.description: A novel holographic framework for spacetimes where radiation escapes to null infinity, distinct from standard AdS holography with timelike boundaries.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives a holographic correspondence directly from type IIB string theory, using D1-D5 brane systems with fundamental strings.

conceptDetails.name: non-commutative open string theory
conceptDetails.description: The holographic dual of the null-boundary spacetime is a spacetime non-commutative open string theory (NCOS) supported on D1-D5 branes with fundamental strings.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The interior AdS3 region is described by the holographic CFT of the D1-D5 system, and the paper discusses TT-deformed CFTs as related boundary theories.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The holographic dual is a non-commutative open string theory (NCOS), analyzed as a quantum field theory deformed by irrelevant operators via a Moyal star product.

conceptDetails.name: tt-bar deformation
conceptDetails.description: The TT deformation of 2D QFT is deeply connected to the ALD spacetimes and motivates the study of non-commutative string theories as holographic duals.

conceptDetails.name: black hole thermodynamics
conceptDetails.description: The paper computes mass and entropy for non-extremal black hole solutions in the null-boundary spacetimes, finding qualitative differences from AdS black holes.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses encoding of quantum information in null boundaries, contrasting with AdS spacetimes where information encoding is well-understood.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Black hole dynamics and quantum information encoding at null boundaries differ from AdS, raising questions about entanglement structure in these spacetimes.

title: Qubit thermodynamics: Entropy production from nonadiabatic driving
abstract: Adiabaticity is a cornerstone of many promising approaches to quantum control, computing, and simulation. In practice, however, there is always a trade-off. Although the deleterious effects of noise can be diminished by running a control schedule more quickly, this benefit comes at the expense of nonadiabaticity. To put these two unwanted effects on the same theoretical footing, we analyze the nonadiabatic error in qubit control as a form of entropy production, examining the mechanism by which fine-grained information is effectively lost despite the dynamics being fundamentally unitary. A crucial issue here is the question of how to define equilibrium under a time-dependent Hamiltonian. Using the Landau--Zener protocol as a test case, we show that entropy increases nearly monotonically when equilibrium is defined with respect to the effective Hamiltonian in the optimal superadiabatic frame. We then consider single-passage Landau--Zener--Stückelberg--Majorana interferometry, in which the initial state of the qubit is arbitrary. Violations of the second law of thermodynamics are possible but require exquisite control to achieve deliberately.

conceptDetails.name: nonadiabatic driving
conceptDetails.description: The core phenomenon under study: finite-rate qubit driving that induces transitions between energy levels and generates entropy, treated as a thermodynamic process.

conceptDetails.name: superadiabatic frame
conceptDetails.description: The optimal rotating reference frame in which entropy production is nearly monotonic, identified as the natural thermodynamic basis for qubit control analysis.

conceptDetails.name: coarse-grained entropy
conceptDetails.description: A frame-dependent von Neumann entropy derived by time-averaging the qubit Bloch vector, capturing effective information loss in closed unitary dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The density matrix and Bloch vector representation of qubit states are the central objects for formulating entropy production and thermodynamic equilibrium.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The time-dependent Hamiltonian drives qubit evolution, and the frame-dependence of equilibrium under a time-varying Hamiltonian is the paper's central theoretical challenge.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: Adiabatic quantum computing is the primary application context motivating the thermodynamic analysis of nonadiabatic errors in qubit control.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The Landau-Zener level-crossing protocol, analogous to a quantum phase transition, serves as the principal test case for analyzing frame-dependent entropy production.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Concepts from stochastic thermodynamics—irreversible work, entropy monotonicity, and second-law violations—frame the analysis of coarse-grained unitary dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: Quantum simulation is listed alongside quantum computing as a motivating application domain for adiabatic control protocols and nonadiabaticity tradeoffs.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Formal parallels to particle production in expanding universes and time-dependent electric fields are invoked to situate the frame-dependence results in a broader physics context.

title: Geography of Landau-Ginzburg models and threefold syzygies
abstract: We study the behavior of toric Landau-Ginzburg models under extremal contraction and minimal model program. We also establish a relation between the moduli space of toric Landau-Ginzburg models and the geography of central models. We conjecture that there is a correspondence between extremal contractions and minimal model program on Fano varieties, and degenerations of their associated toric Landau-Ginzburg models written explicitly. We prove the conjectures for smooth toric varieties, as well as general smooth Fano varieties in dimensions 2 and 3. As an application, we compute the elementary syzygies for smooth Fano threefolds.

conceptDetails.name: landau-ginzburg mirror symmetry
conceptDetails.description: The paper establishes explicit correspondences between Fano varieties and their toric Landau-Ginzburg mirror models under the minimal model program.

conceptDetails.name: mori minimal model program
conceptDetails.description: The minimal model program (MMP) framework provides the birational geometry context in which the behavior of Landau-Ginzburg models under extremal contraction is analyzed.

conceptDetails.name: sarkisov program
conceptDetails.description: The Sarkisov program, which factorizes birational maps between Mori fibre spaces into elementary links, is the primary algebraic geometry framework motivating the syzygy computations.

conceptDetails.name: fano variety geometry
conceptDetails.description: Smooth Fano varieties in dimensions 2 and 3 are the primary geometric objects for which conjectures about extremal contractions and LG model degenerations are proved.

conceptDetails.name: threefold syzygies
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces and computes elementary syzygies (higher relations among Sarkisov links) for smooth Fano threefolds, classified by Picard number.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Landau-Ginzburg models are a class of quantum field theories whose geometric structure and mirror symmetry properties are central to this paper's analysis.

conceptDetails.name: toric geometry
conceptDetails.description: Toric varieties and toric Landau-Ginzburg models provide the explicit combinatorial setting in which the main conjectures are proved in the toric case.

conceptDetails.name: moduli space
conceptDetails.description: The paper establishes a relation between the moduli space of toric Landau-Ginzburg models and the geography of central models.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Mirror symmetry, a duality arising from string theory, underpins the correspondence between Fano varieties and their Landau-Ginzburg mirror models studied here.

conceptDetails.name: laurent polynomial mutations
conceptDetails.description: Mutations of Laurent polynomials encode the wall-crossing transitions between different Landau-Ginzburg models and are used to construct the geography of LG models.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Extremal contractions and degenerations of Landau-Ginzburg models represent geometric symmetry-breaking transitions studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Computing elementary syzygies for smooth Fano threefolds involves inverting the correspondence between central models and Sarkisov links, an inverse problem in birational geometry.

title: The Price of Freedom: Exploring Expressivity and Runtime Tradeoffs in Equivariant Tensor Products
abstract: $E(3)$-equivariant neural networks have demonstrated success across a wide range of 3D modelling tasks. A fundamental operation in these networks is the tensor product, which interacts two geometric features in an equivariant manner to create new features. Due to the high computational complexity of the tensor product, significant effort has been invested to optimize the runtime of this operation. For example, Luo et al. (2024) recently proposed the Gaunt tensor product (GTP) which promises a significant speedup. In this work, we provide a careful, systematic analysis of a number of tensor product operations. In particular, we emphasize that different tensor products are not performing the same operation. The reported speedups typically come at the cost of expressivity. We introduce measures of expressivity and interactability to characterize these differences. In addition, we realized the original implementation of GTP can be greatly simplified by directly using a spherical grid at no cost in asymptotic runtime. This spherical grid approach is faster on our benchmarks and in actual training of the MACE interatomic potential by 30%. Finally, we provide the first systematic microbenchmarks of the various tensor product operations. We find that the theoretical runtime guarantees can differ wildly from empirical performance, demonstrating the need for careful application-specific benchmarking. Code is available at https://github.com/atomicarchitects/PriceofFreedom.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically analyzes tensor product operations that are the core computational primitive in E(3)-equivariant neural networks for 3D modeling.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: SO(3)/E(3) group theory and irreducible representations (irreps) form the mathematical foundation for understanding how tensor products must transform under symmetry operations.

conceptDetails.name: tensor product expressivity
conceptDetails.description: Novel measure introduced to quantify how much of the full Clebsch-Gordan tensor product's interaction space is captured by a given tensor product operation.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper contributes to geometric deep learning by characterizing the expressivity and runtime tradeoffs of different equivariant bilinear operations on geometric features.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: All tensor product operations analyzed are evaluated on their ability to preserve E(3) equivariance while improving computational efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Reducing the O(L^5) complexity of the Clebsch-Gordan tensor product to O(L^3) is the central scalability challenge motivating all compared approaches.

conceptDetails.name: spherical grid projection
conceptDetails.description: Novel simplified implementation of the Gaunt tensor product using direct S^2 projection, achieving the same O(L^3) asymptotics with better empirical performance.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spherical harmonics and the spherical grid (S^2) projection are used as a spectral-domain approach to simplify the Gaunt tensor product implementation with 30% speedup.

conceptDetails.name: interatomic potentials
conceptDetails.description: The MACE interatomic potential model is used as a practical benchmark to validate the 30% training speedup from the spherical grid tensor product approach.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Irreducible representations (irreps) of O(3) serve as the feature representation space, and the paper analyzes how different tensor products interact and mix these representations.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: 3D graph convolutions using spherical harmonic projections of edge vectors are a key use case for the optimized tensor product operations studied.

conceptDetails.name: molecular dynamics
conceptDetails.description: Molecular force field and catalyst discovery applications motivate the need for scalable equivariant tensor products in the paper.

title: A Detection of Helium in the Bright Superluminous Supernova SN 2024rmj
abstract: We present extensive ultraviolet (UV), optical, and near-infrared (NIR) photometric and spectroscopic observations of the nearby hydrogen-poor superluminous supernova (SLSN-I) SN2024rmj at z = 0.1189. SN 2024rmj reached a peak absolute magnitude of Mg $\approx$ -21.9, placing it at the luminous end of the SLSN-I distribution. The light curve exhibits a pronounced pre-peak bump ($\approx$ 60 d before the main peak) and a post-peak bump ($\approx$ 55 d after the main peak). The bulk of the light curve is otherwise well fit by a magnetar spin-down model, with typical values (spin: $\approx$ 2.1 ms; magnetic field: $\approx$ 6 $\times$ 10$^{13}$ G; ejecta mass: $\approx$ 12 M$_\odot$). The optical spectra exhibit characteristic SLSN-I features and evolution, but with a relatively high velocity of $\approx$ 8,000 km s$^{-1}$ post-peak. Most significantly, we find a clear detection of helium in the NIR spectra at He I $λ$1.083 $μ$m and $λ$2.058 $μ$m, blueshifted by $\approx$ 15,000 km s$^{-1}$ (13 d before peak) and $\approx$ 13,000 km s$^{-1}$ (40 d after peak), indicating that helium is confined to the outermost ejecta; based on these NIR detections, we also identify likely contribution from He I $λ$5876 Å in the optical spectra on a similar range of timescales. This represents the most definitive detection of helium in a bright SLSN-I to date, and indicates that progenitors with a thin helium layer can still explode as SLSNe.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detection of He I absorption lines at 1.083 µm and 2.058 µm in NIR spectra constitutes the paper's primary observational contribution and confirms helium in a bright SLSN-I.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: SN 2024rmj is classified and characterized as a hydrogen-poor superluminous supernova (SLSN-I) based on spectral features, peak absolute magnitude, and light curve morphology.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectroscopic analysis across UV, optical, and NIR wavelengths identifies elemental absorption features, measures ejecta velocities, and tracks spectral evolution over ~200 days.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar spin-down
conceptDetails.description: A central engine model in which rotational energy from a rapidly spinning, highly magnetized neutron star powers the extreme luminosity of SN 2024rmj.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The helium detection constrains the progenitor star's envelope structure, indicating a stripped massive star that retained a thin helium shell prior to explosion.

conceptDetails.name: ejecta velocity mapping
conceptDetails.description: Blueshift measurements of helium lines (~15,000–13,000 km/s) localize helium to the outermost ejecta layers, constraining the compositional structure of the explosion.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The multi-band light curve is fit using a magnetar spin-down model to derive physical parameters including spin period (~2.1 ms), magnetic field (~6×10¹³ G), and ejecta mass (~12 M☉).

conceptDetails.name: light curve bumps
conceptDetails.description: Pronounced pre-peak (~60 d) and post-peak (~55 d) flux excesses deviate from the standard magnetar model and require additional physical explanations such as CSM interaction or shell ejection.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Systematic uncertainties in spectral line identification, velocity measurements, and model-fit parameters are assessed to validate the helium detection and derived physical quantities.

title: Generative Perception of Shape and Material from Differential Motion
abstract: Perceiving the shape and material of an object from a single image is inherently ambiguous, especially when lighting is unknown and unconstrained. Despite this, humans can often disentangle shape and material, and when they are uncertain, they often move their head slightly or rotate the object to help resolve the ambiguities. Inspired by this behavior, we introduce a novel conditional denoising-diffusion model that generates samples of shape-and-material maps from a short video of an object undergoing differential motions. Our parameter-efficient architecture allows training directly in pixel-space, and it generates many disentangled attributes of an object simultaneously. Trained on a modest number of synthetic object-motion videos with supervision on shape and material, the model exhibits compelling emergent behavior: For static observations, it produces diverse, multimodal predictions of plausible shape-and-material maps that capture the inherent ambiguities; and when objects move, the distributions converge to more accurate explanations. The model also produces high-quality shape-and-material estimates for less ambiguous, real-world objects. By moving beyond single-view to continuous motion observations, and by using generative perception to capture visual ambiguities, our work suggests ways to improve visual reasoning in physically-embodied systems.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core architecture is a conditional denoising-diffusion model (U-ViT3D-Mixer) that generates samples of shape and material maps from video observations.

conceptDetails.name: generative perception
conceptDetails.description: A novel framework proposed in the paper where vision systems generate diverse samples capturing visual ambiguities rather than predicting a single best estimate.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The generative perception framework produces diverse samples of plausible shape-material explanations rather than collapsing to a single deterministic estimate.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The model jointly disentangles surface normals, diffuse texture, and reflectance from a shared backbone, allowing each attribute to inform the others.

conceptDetails.name: differential motion cues
conceptDetails.description: The use of small viewpoint or object motions across a few video frames as a computational signal to resolve inherent shape-material ambiguities in static images.

conceptDetails.name: shape-material ambiguity
conceptDetails.description: The fundamental ill-posedness arising from the intertwined roles of geometry and reflectance in image formation, which the model explicitly represents rather than suppresses.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The framework explicitly models inherent visual ambiguities (e.g., convex/concave ambiguity) by producing multimodal posterior distributions over shape-material explanations.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The diffusion model samples from the posterior distribution over shape and material given video observations, with distributions converging as more motion frames are provided.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: The U-ViT3D-Mixer architecture uses spatiotemporal attention at coarse scales and parameter-efficient neighborhood attention at fine scales to reason about shape and material jointly.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The architecture is built on a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone extended to 3D spatiotemporal reasoning over video frames.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering intrinsic object properties (shape, material) from image observations is framed as an inverse rendering problem without requiring explicit illumination estimation.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The denoising diffusion process is directly related to score-based generative modeling, learning to reverse a noise process conditioned on video observations.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The model learns joint pixel-space representations of shape and material attributes from synthetic training data that generalize to real-world images.

title: A Wide Field Map of Ultra-Compact Dwarfs in the Coma Cluster
abstract: A dataset of 23,351 globular clusters (GCs) and ultra-compact dwarfs (UCDs) in the Coma cluster of galaxies was built using Hubble Space Telescope Advanced Camera for Surveys data. Based on the standard magnitude cut of $M_V \leq -11$, a total of 523 UCD candidates are found within this dataset of Compact Stellar Systems (CSS). From a color-magnitude diagram (CMD) analysis built using this catalog, we find a clear mass-magnitude relation extending marginally into the UCD parameter space. The luminosity function defined by this dataset, shows an excess of sources at bright magnitudes, suggesting a bimodal formation scenario for UCDs. We estimate the number of UCDs with a different origin than GC to be $N_{UCD} \geq 32 \pm 1$. We derive the total number of CSS within the core (1 Mpc) of Coma to be $N_{CSS} \approx 69,400 \pm 1400$. The radial distribution of UCDs in Coma shows that, like GCs, UCDs agglomerate around three giant ellipticals: NGC 4874, NGC 4889, and IC 4051. We find UCDs are more centrally concentrated around these three ellipticals than GCs. IC 4051 has a satellite population of UCDs similar to NGC 4874 and NGC 4889. We estimate only ~14% of UCDs, inhabit the intracluster space (ICUCD) between galaxies in the region, in comparison to ~24% for GCs (ICGC). We find red (metal-rich) UCDs are more likely located closer to a host galaxy, with blue (metal-poor) UCDs showing a greater dispersion and lower average density in the region.

conceptDetails.name: compact stellar systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents the largest wide-field catalog of compact stellar systems (GCs and UCDs) in the Coma cluster, comprising 23,351 sources across 26 HST ACS pointings.

conceptDetails.name: color-magnitude diagram
conceptDetails.description: CMD analysis using HST F475W and F814W photometry reveals bimodal color sequences and a mass-magnitude relation for compact stellar systems in Coma.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper classifies and catalogs compact stellar systems (GCs, UCDs, compact ellipticals) in the Coma cluster using HST photometric data and morphological analysis.

conceptDetails.name: tidal stripping
conceptDetails.description: Tidal stripping of nucleated dwarf galaxies is investigated as a primary formation pathway for UCDs, supported by the luminosity function bimodality and SMBH evidence.

conceptDetails.name: intracluster population
conceptDetails.description: The fraction of UCDs and GCs inhabiting intracluster space between galaxies is quantified and compared, revealing that UCDs are more concentrated around host galaxies than GCs.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Statistical clustering analysis is used to study the spatial distribution and concentration of UCDs around giant elliptical galaxies in the Coma cluster.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper examines formation pathways for UCDs including tidal stripping of nucleated dwarf galaxies and high-mass GC formation scenarios, probing the evolutionary history of compact stellar systems.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Bimodal formation scenarios for UCDs are tested using luminosity function analysis and estimates of UCDs with non-GC origins.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The luminosity function excess at bright magnitudes is used to identify UCDs with non-GC origins, effectively detecting anomalous sources within the compact stellar system population.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: UCDs are discussed as tracers of dark matter distribution within the Coma cluster, with their spatial distribution informing the cluster's dark matter structure.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: A mass-magnitude relation is derived from color-magnitude diagram analysis, extending into the UCD parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The radial distribution and intracluster fraction of UCDs are analyzed in the context of galaxy cluster formation and accretion history.

title: A Tale of Two Symmetries: Exploring the Loss Landscape of Equivariant Models
abstract: Equivariant neural networks have proven to be effective for tasks with known underlying symmetries. However, optimizing equivariant networks can be tricky and best training practices are less established than for standard networks. In particular, recent works have found small training benefits from relaxing equivariance constraints. This raises the question: do equivariance constraints introduce fundamental obstacles to optimization? Or do they simply require different hyperparameter tuning? In this work, we investigate this question through a theoretical analysis of the loss landscape geometry. We focus on networks built using permutation representations, which we can view as a subset of unconstrained MLPs. Importantly, we show that the parameter symmetries of the unconstrained model has nontrivial effects on the loss landscape of the equivariant subspace and under certain conditions can provably prevent learning of the global minima. Further, we empirically demonstrate in such cases, relaxing to an unconstrained MLP can sometimes solve the issue. Interestingly, the weights eventually found via relaxation corresponds to a different choice of group representation in the hidden layer. From this, we draw 3 key takeaways. (1) By viewing the unconstrained version of an architecture, we can uncover hidden parameter symmetries which were broken by choice of constraint enforcement (2) Hidden symmetries give important insights on loss landscapes and can induce critical points and even minima (3) Hidden symmetry induced minima can sometimes be escaped by constraint relaxation and we observe the network jumps to a different choice of constraint enforcement. Effective equivariance relaxation may require rethinking the fixed choice of group representation in the hidden layers.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper, analyzing how equivariance constraints on neural networks affect the loss landscape and optimization.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Provides the mathematical foundation for analyzing group representations, permutation representations, and the symmetries acting on model parameter spaces.

conceptDetails.name: loss landscape geometry
conceptDetails.description: Novel theoretical contribution characterizing how hidden parameter symmetries of unconstrained MLPs shape the geometry of the equivariant subspace loss landscape.

conceptDetails.name: parameter symmetry
conceptDetails.description: Hidden symmetries in unconstrained model parameter spaces that persist into constrained subspaces and provably induce spurious minima and critical points.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how enforcing symmetry constraints (equivariance) can paradoxically hide and break other symmetries, creating optimization obstacles.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Constraint enforcement breaks parameter symmetries of the unconstrained model, creating hidden fixed-point subspaces that induce spurious critical points and minima.

conceptDetails.name: spurious minima
conceptDetails.description: Key phenomenon discovered: equivariance constraints can create local minima that prevent convergence to the global optimum, escapable via constraint relaxation.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides a theoretical analysis of loss landscape geometry for equivariant subspaces, proving conditions under which global minima cannot be reached.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The broader field context for equivariant architectures applied to molecular, physics, and structured data tasks discussed as motivation.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper seeks to explain why equivariant models are hard to optimize and what structural properties of the loss landscape cause training failures.

title: SPLASH: A Rapid Host-Based Supernova Classifier for Wide-Field Time-Domain Surveys
abstract: The upcoming Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) conducted by the Vera C. Rubin Observatory will detect millions of supernovae (SNe) and generate millions of nightly alerts, far outpacing available spectroscopic resources. Rapid, scalable photometric classification methods are therefore essential for identifying young SNe for follow-up and enabling large-scale population studies. We present SPLASH, a host-based classification pipeline that infers supernova classes using only host galaxy photometry. SPLASH first associates SNe with their hosts (yielding a redshift estimate), then infers host galaxy stellar mass and star formation rate using deep learning, and finally classifies SNe using a random forest trained on these inferred properties, along with host-SN angular separation and redshift. SPLASH achieves a binary (Type Ia vs. core-collapse) classification accuracy of $76\%$ and an F1-score of $69\%$, comparable to other state-of-the-art methods. By selecting only the most confident predictions, SPLASH can return highly pure subsets of all major SN types, making it well-suited for targeted follow-up. Its efficient design allows classification of $\sim 500$ SNe per second, making it ideal for next-generation surveys. Moreover, its intermediate inference step enables selection of transients by host environment, providing a tool not only for classification but also for probing the demographics of stellar death.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The core task of SPLASH: classifying supernovae into types (Ia vs. core-collapse and subtypes) using host galaxy properties.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Random forest classification trained on inferred host galaxy properties to assign SN types with confidence scores.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Random forest, an ensemble of decision trees, is the primary classifier used in SPLASH's final classification stage.

conceptDetails.name: host-sn association
conceptDetails.description: Novel pipeline component that matches supernova alerts to host galaxies using angular separation and directional likelihood to obtain redshift estimates.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: SPLASH infers host galaxy stellar mass and star formation rate from multi-band photometry as an intermediate step enabling SN classification.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Deep learning extracts physical properties (stellar mass, SFR) from host galaxy photometry to serve as features for the downstream classifier.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional neural networks are used to infer host galaxy physical properties from multi-band imaging photometry.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: SPLASH is designed to classify ~500 SNe per second, enabling deployment at LSST scale with millions of nightly alerts.

conceptDetails.name: photometric sed fitting
conceptDetails.description: Spectral energy distribution fitting is used to generate training labels for host galaxy stellar mass and star formation rate inference.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The physical basis for SPLASH is the correlation between host galaxy stellar populations and the types of supernovae they produce.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: SPLASH leverages pre-trained deep learning models and SED-fit derived labels from existing catalogs to infer host galaxy properties.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: SPLASH produces confidence scores that enable selection of high-purity subsets, requiring well-calibrated probability estimates.

title: Frequentist Uncertainties on Neural Density Ratios with wifi Ensembles
abstract: We introduce wifi ensembles as a novel framework to obtain asymptotic frequentist uncertainties on density ratios, with a particular focus on neural ratio estimation in the context of high-energy physics. When the density ratio of interest is a likelihood ratio conditioned on parameters, wifi ensembles can be used to perform simulation-based inference on those parameters. After training the basis functions f_i(x), uncertainties on the weights w_i can be straightforwardly propagated to the estimated parameters without requiring extraneous bootstraps. To demonstrate this approach, we present an application in quantum chromodynamics at the Large Hadron Collider, using wifi ensembles to estimate the likelihood ratio between generated quark and gluon jets. We use this learned likelihood ratio to estimate the quark fraction in a synthetic mixed quark/gluon sample, showing that the resultant uncertainties empirically satisfy the desired coverage properties.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The core quantity estimated by wifi ensembles; used for quark/gluon discrimination and simulation-based parameter inference at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The central contribution of the paper: deriving asymptotic frequentist uncertainties on neural density ratios with rigorous coverage guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: wifi ensembles
conceptDetails.description: Novel framework introduced by this paper: a weighted ensemble of neural basis functions enabling analytic frequentist uncertainty propagation on density ratios.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: wifi ensembles combine weighted neural network basis functions to produce a flexible, uncertainty-aware density ratio estimator.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: wifi ensembles are applied within the SBI paradigm to infer physical parameters from high-dimensional collider data without tractable likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Density ratio estimation (DRE) is the foundational task; the paper provides a principled framework for quantifying uncertainty on the estimated ratio.

conceptDetails.name: frequentist coverage
conceptDetails.description: The statistical property that confidence intervals contain the true parameter with the nominal probability; the key guarantee wifi ensembles are designed to satisfy.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Coverage properties of the confidence intervals on wifi ensemble weights and downstream parameters are empirically validated to ensure correct calibration.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain is the Large Hadron Collider, where wifi ensembles are used for quark/gluon jet likelihood ratio estimation.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Quark/gluon jet discrimination is the main physics case study, using wifi ensembles to estimate the quark fraction in a mixed sample.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The Neyman construction and pseudo-likelihood ratio tests underpin the frequentist inference framework used throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classification (quark vs. gluon jets) motivates DRE via the Neyman-Pearson lemma, contextualizing wifi ensembles within jet tagging.

title: Outcome-Based Online Reinforcement Learning: Algorithms and Fundamental Limits
abstract: Reinforcement learning with outcome-based feedback faces a fundamental challenge: when rewards are only observed at trajectory endpoints, how do we assign credit to the right actions? This paper provides the first comprehensive analysis of this problem in online RL with general function approximation. We develop a provably sample-efficient algorithm achieving $\widetilde{O}({C_{\rm cov} H^3}/{ε^2})$ sample complexity, where $C_{\rm cov}$ is the coverability coefficient of the underlying MDP. By leveraging general function approximation, our approach works effectively in large or infinite state spaces where tabular methods fail, requiring only that value functions and reward functions can be represented by appropriate function classes. Our results also characterize when outcome-based feedback is statistically separated from per-step rewards, revealing an unavoidable exponential separation for certain MDPs. For deterministic MDPs, we show how to eliminate the completeness assumption, dramatically simplifying the algorithm. We further extend our approach to preference-based feedback settings, proving that equivalent statistical efficiency can be achieved even under more limited information. Together, these results constitute a theoretical foundation for understanding the statistical properties of outcome-based reinforcement learning.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The central framework of the paper, analyzing online RL with outcome-based (trajectory-level) feedback and general function approximation.

conceptDetails.name: outcome-based feedback
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the feedback paradigm where rewards are only observed at trajectory endpoints, motivating the paper's theoretical analysis.

conceptDetails.name: credit assignment
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the problem of attributing which actions in a trajectory caused the observed terminal reward, central to outcome-based RL.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how to optimize policies when reward signals are only available at trajectory endpoints rather than per-step.

conceptDetails.name: coverability coefficient
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: a complexity measure of the underlying MDP that determines sample complexity bounds in the paper's main algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Markov Decision Processes with stochastic transitions and reward structures form the mathematical foundation of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: Online exploration strategies are analyzed, where the agent actively gathers data to learn efficiently in unknown environments.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops algorithms that scale to large or infinite state spaces via general function approximation, beyond tabular methods.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Preference-based feedback under the Bradley-Terry-Luce model is analyzed, connecting to probabilistic inference over trajectory comparisons.

title: How to build a consistency model: Learning flow maps via self-distillation
abstract: Flow-based generative models achieve state-of-the-art sample quality, but require the expensive solution of a differential equation at inference time. Flow map models, commonly known as consistency models, encompass many recent efforts to improve inference-time efficiency by learning the solution operator of this differential equation. Yet despite their promise, these models lack a unified description that clearly explains how to learn them efficiently in practice. Here, building on the methodology proposed in Boffi et. al. (2024), we present a systematic algorithmic framework for directly learning the flow map associated with a flow or diffusion model. By exploiting a relationship between the velocity field underlying a continuous-time flow and the instantaneous rate of change of the flow map, we show how to convert any distillation scheme into a direct training algorithm via self-distillation, eliminating the need for pre-trained teachers. We introduce three algorithmic families based on different mathematical characterizations of the flow map: Eulerian, Lagrangian, and Progressive methods, which we show encompass and extend all known distillation and direct training schemes for consistency models. We find that the novel class of Lagrangian methods, which avoid both spatial derivatives and bootstrapping from small steps by design, achieve significantly more stable training and higher performance than more standard Eulerian and Progressive schemes. Our methodology unifies existing training schemes under a single common framework and reveals new design principles for accelerated generative modeling. Associated code is available at https://github.com/nmboffi/flow-maps.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: The core training paradigm extended in this paper; the authors build their self-distillation framework on top of off-the-shelf flow matching procedures to learn flow maps directly.

conceptDetails.name: flow map
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study — a function mapping points along probability flow trajectories, enabling single-step or few-step sample generation instead of full ODE integration.

conceptDetails.name: consistency models
conceptDetails.description: The model class that this paper systematically unifies; consistency models learn flow maps to achieve 10-100x inference speedup over standard diffusion/flow models.

conceptDetails.name: self-distillation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's key algorithmic contribution: a method to convert any distillation scheme into a direct training algorithm by simultaneously training and distilling the flow map's implicit velocity, eliminating the need for pre-trained teachers.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The broad class of models studied; the paper advances accelerated inference for state-of-the-art generative models across vision benchmarks (CIFAR-10, CelebA-64, AFHQ-64).

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: One of the two base model families (alongside flow models) whose solution operators are being learned; the paper provides unified training schemes applicable to both.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: One of the three novel algorithmic families introduced (Lagrangian Self-Distillation), shown to outperform Eulerian and Progressive methods by avoiding spatial derivatives and self-consistent bootstrapping.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A primary contribution: three families of training objectives (ESD, LSD, PSD) are derived and shown to have the correct unique minimizer with 2-Wasserstein error guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical foundation of the framework; stochastic interpolants define the probability flow whose solution operator (flow map) is being learned.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: Foundational prior work (score functions/teachers) that the paper's self-distillation framework replaces, enabling training without a pre-trained teacher model.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: Provides theoretical grounding for the paper's guarantees; 2-Wasserstein distance bounds on the learned one-step model's error are proven for ESD and LSD methods.

title: Fast Low Energy Reconstruction using Convolutional Neural Networks
abstract: IceCube is a Cherenkov detector instrumenting over a cubic kilometer of glacial ice deep under the surface of the South Pole. The DeepCore sub-detector lowers the detection energy threshold to a few GeV, enabling the precise measurements of neutrino oscillation parameters with atmospheric neutrinos. The reconstruction of neutrino interactions inside the detector is essential in studying neutrino oscillations. It is particularly challenging to reconstruct sub-100 GeV events with the IceCube detectors due to the relatively sparse detection units and detection medium. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are broadly used in physics experiments for both classification and regression purposes. This paper discusses the CNNs developed and employed for the latest IceCube-DeepCore oscillation measurements. These CNNs estimate various properties of the detected neutrinos, such as their energy, direction of arrival, interaction vertex position, flavor-related signature, and are also used for background classification.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNNs are the core architecture used to reconstruct neutrino event properties such as energy, direction, vertex position, and flavor from IceCube-DeepCore detector data.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on reconstructing sub-100 GeV neutrino interaction properties from sparse Cherenkov light signals in the IceCube detector.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: IceCube-DeepCore detects atmospheric neutrinos at GeV energies to enable oscillation parameter measurements, forming the experimental context of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino oscillation reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Novel application context: the CNN outputs directly serve the IceCube-DeepCore atmospheric neutrino oscillation analysis pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: CNNs are used for background classification to distinguish neutrino signal events from atmospheric muon backgrounds.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: CNNs perform regression to estimate continuous neutrino properties including energy and direction of arrival.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The CNNs extract relevant features from the sparse 3D spatial-temporal photon detection patterns recorded by IceCube DOM arrays.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Simulated neutrino events are used to train the CNNs, requiring accurate modeling of Cherenkov light propagation through glacial ice.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Estimating reconstruction uncertainties is important for the oscillation parameter measurement that these CNNs feed into.

title: On the creation of narrow AI: hierarchy and nonlocality of neural network skills
abstract: We study the problem of creating strong, yet narrow, AI systems. While recent AI progress has been driven by the training of large general-purpose foundation models, the creation of smaller models specialized for narrow domains could be valuable for both efficiency and safety. In this work, we explore two challenges involved in creating such systems, having to do with basic properties of how neural networks learn and structure their representations. The first challenge regards when it is possible to train narrow models from scratch. Through experiments on a synthetic task, we find that it is sometimes necessary to train networks on a wide distribution of data to learn certain narrow skills within that distribution. This effect arises when skills depend on each other hierarchically, and training on a broad distribution introduces a curriculum which substantially accelerates learning. The second challenge regards how to transfer particular skills from large general models into small specialized models. We find that model skills are often not perfectly localized to a particular set of prunable components. However, we find that methods based on pruning can still outperform distillation. We investigate the use of a regularization objective to align desired skills with prunable components while unlearning unnecessary skills.

conceptDetails.name: curriculum learning
conceptDetails.description: Training on a broad data distribution implicitly introduces a curriculum that substantially accelerates learning of hierarchically dependent narrow skills.

conceptDetails.name: model pruning
conceptDetails.description: Ablation-score and attribution-score based pruning of neuron groups is the central method investigated for extracting narrow specialized models from general ones.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how neural networks structure and distribute skill representations across model components, which is central to both the nonlocality findings and pruning challenges.

conceptDetails.name: skill nonlocality
conceptDetails.description: The empirical finding that neural network skills are distributed across model components rather than localized to prunable units, posing a fundamental challenge for model specialization.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates and attempts to enforce alignment between specific skills and prunable model components, essentially seeking to disentangle skill representations for surgical pruning.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: A core challenge addressed is how to transfer specific skills from large general-purpose foundation models into smaller specialized models.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The compositional multitask sparse parity (CMSP) synthetic benchmark involves training networks on multiple tasks simultaneously to study hierarchical skill dependencies.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Group lasso regularization is used to induce sparsity at the level of prunable parameter groups, enabling cleaner skill localization and removal.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes where and how skills are implemented within network components, drawing on mechanistic interpretability to understand skill nonlocality.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A group-sparsity regularization objective (group lasso) is designed and investigated to align desired skills with prunable components during training.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Efficiency motivation for narrow AI systems is a primary driver, as smaller specialized models reduce unnecessary computation and improve deployability.

title: On the normality of commuting scheme for general linear Lie algebra
abstract: The commuting scheme $\mathfrak{C}^{d}_{\mathfrak{g}}$ for reductive Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ over an algebraically closed field $\mathbb{K}$ is the subscheme of $\mathfrak{g}^{d}$ defined by quadratic equations, whose $\mathbb{K}$-valued points are $d$-tuples of commuting elements in $\mathfrak{g}$ over $\mathbb{K}$. There is a long-standing conjecture that the commuting scheme $\mathfrak{C}^{d}_{\mathfrak{g}}$ is reduced. Moreover, a higher dimensional analog of Chevalley restriction conjecture was conjectured by Chen-Ngô. We show that the commuting scheme of $\mathfrak{C}^{2}_{\mathfrak{g}l_{n}}$ is Cohen-Macaulay and normal. As a corollary, we prove a 2-dimensional Chevalley restriction theorem for general linear group in positive characteristic.

conceptDetails.name: commuting scheme
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study: the scheme-theoretic fiber of the commutator map, whose reducedness, Cohen-Macaulayness, and normality are established for gl_n in the d=2 case.

conceptDetails.name: cohen-macaulay normality
conceptDetails.description: The main algebraic properties proved for the coordinate ring R(n) of the commuting scheme, established by an inductive construction through intermediate rings.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on reductive Lie algebras and their associated Weyl groups acting on commuting tuples, with the Chevalley restriction theorem as a core result about G-invariant morphisms.

conceptDetails.name: chevalley restriction theorem
conceptDetails.description: The 2-dimensional generalization of the classical theorem is proved as a corollary for GL_n in positive characteristic, confirming the Chen-Ngô conjecture in this case.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The Chen-Ngô conjecture and Chevalley restriction theorem concern G-equivariant morphisms from torus quotients to categorical quotients, encoding symmetry constraints of reductive group actions.

conceptDetails.name: hitchin fibration
conceptDetails.description: The Hitchin morphism from moduli stacks of principal G-Higgs bundles motivates the higher-dimensional Chevalley restriction conjecture and gives geometric context to the commuting scheme.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
