conceptDetails.description: The universal spectral data morphism sd connecting the commuting scheme to the torus quotient is a key structural map motivating the normality result.

title: Neural Thermodynamics: Entropic Forces in Deep and Universal Representation Learning
abstract: With the rapid discovery of emergent phenomena in deep learning and large language models, understanding their cause has become an urgent need. Here, we propose a rigorous entropic-force theory for understanding the learning dynamics of neural networks trained with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and its variants. Building on the theory of parameter symmetries and an entropic loss landscape, we show that representation learning is crucially governed by emergent entropic forces arising from stochasticity and discrete-time updates. These forces systematically break continuous parameter symmetries and preserve discrete ones, leading to a series of gradient balance phenomena that resemble the equipartition property of thermal systems. These phenomena, in turn, (a) explain the universal alignment of neural representations between AI models and lead to a proof of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, and (b) reconcile the seemingly contradictory observations of sharpness- and flatness-seeking behavior of deep learning optimization. Our theory and experiments demonstrate that a combination of entropic forces and symmetry breaking is key to understanding emergent phenomena in deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: entropic forces
conceptDetails.description: The central novel contribution: macroscopic forces emerging from SGD stochasticity and discrete-time updates that govern representation learning dynamics, analogous to thermodynamic entropic forces.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Entropic forces systematically break continuous parameter symmetries while preserving discrete ones, which is the key mechanism explaining universal phenomena in deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper explains how universal neural representations emerge and align across AI models as a consequence of entropic forces and symmetry breaking during training.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives a rigorous entropic loss function that captures the implicit effects of SGD stochasticity and discrete-time updates beyond the standard empirical risk.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: SGD's stochastic minibatch sampling is the physical source of the entropic forces; the paper analyzes discrete-time stochastic gradient dynamics as a statistical-mechanical system.

conceptDetails.name: equipartition theorem
conceptDetails.description: A novel deep-learning analog of the thermodynamic equipartition theorem is derived, predicting gradient balance and alignment phenomena as universal consequences of entropic forces.

conceptDetails.name: platonic representation hypothesis
conceptDetails.description: A recently proposed hypothesis about universal alignment of neural representations across models, which this paper provides a formal proof of via the entropic-force framework.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly leverages Lagrangian formalism from theoretical physics to analyze gradient flow and SGD training dynamics through an effective energy landscape.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: Continuous parameter symmetries in the loss give rise to conservation laws that make SGD initialization-dependent; entropic forces break these, producing universal outcomes.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Discrete parameter symmetries are selectively preserved by entropic forces while continuous ones are broken, a key structural result distinguishing the two symmetry classes.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses phase-transition-like emergent behaviors in deep learning, unifying progressive sharpening and flattening observations under the entropic-force framework.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Universal alignment of internal embeddings across independently trained AI models is explained and proven as a direct consequence of entropic symmetry-breaking dynamics.

title: Neural Thermodynamic Laws for Large Language Model Training
abstract: Beyond neural scaling laws, little is known about the laws underlying large language models (LLMs). We introduce Neural Thermodynamic Laws (NTL) -- a new framework that offers fresh insights into LLM training dynamics. On the theoretical side, we demonstrate that key thermodynamic quantities (e.g., temperature, entropy, heat capacity, thermal conduction) and classical thermodynamic principles (e.g., the three laws of thermodynamics and the equipartition theorem) naturally emerge under river-valley loss landscape assumptions. On the practical side, this scientific perspective yields intuitive guidelines for designing learning rate schedules.

conceptDetails.name: neural thermodynamic laws
conceptDetails.description: The core framework introduced by the paper, mapping classical thermodynamic quantities (temperature, entropy, heat capacity) and principles (three laws, equipartition theorem) to LLM training dynamics under river-valley loss landscape assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: river-valley loss landscape
conceptDetails.description: The foundational loss landscape model characterizing LLM training, where flat 'river' directions (slow dynamics) lie at the bottom of sharp 'valley' directions (fast dynamics), enabling the thermodynamic analogy.

conceptDetails.name: learning rate schedules
conceptDetails.description: The primary practical application of the framework, providing thermodynamically-motivated guidelines for designing warmup-stable-decay (WSD) schedules by clarifying the competing effects of learning rate on slow vs. fast dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: SGD gradient noise drives fast dynamics to a steady-state thermal distribution, directly analogous to Brownian motion reaching thermal equilibrium, underpinning the temperature and entropy definitions.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives optimal learning rate schedule design from first principles via the thermodynamic framework, connecting loss landscape geometry to practical training choices.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A central goal of the work is to provide mechanistic, rather than purely empirical, understanding of why LLM training dynamics behave as observed, including the function of each phase in WSD schedules.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: The first law of thermodynamics (ΔU = W + Q) is formally mapped to the fast-slow loss decomposition (ℓ = ℓs + ℓf), grounding the analogy in a conservation principle.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The framework is validated against real LLM training runs, demonstrating that insights from the analytically solvable toy model generalize to large-scale language model training.

title: Symbolic Learning of Topological Bands in Photonic Crystals
abstract: Topological photonic crystals (PhCs) that support disorder-resistant modes, protected degeneracies, and robust transport have recently been explored for applications in waveguiding, optical isolation, light trapping, and lasing. However, designing PhCs with prescribed topological properties remains challenging because of the highly nonlinear mapping from the continuous real-space design of PhCs to the discrete output space of band topology. Here, we introduce a machine learning approach to address this problem, employing Kolmogorov--Arnold networks (KANs) to predict and inversely design the band symmetries of two-dimensional PhCs with two-fold rotational (C2) symmetry. We show that a single-hidden-layer KAN, trained on a dataset of C2-symmetric unit cells, achieves high accuracy in classifying the topological classes of the lowest lying bands. We use the symbolic regression capabilities of KANs to extract algebraic formulas that express the topological classes directly in terms of the Fourier components of the dielectric function. These formulas not only retain the full predictive power of the network but also provide novel insights and enable deterministic inverse design. Using this approach, we generate photonic crystals with target topological bands, achieving high accuracy even for high-contrast, experimentally realizable structures beyond the training domain.

conceptDetails.name: kolmogorov-arnold networks
conceptDetails.description: The primary ML architecture used to classify topological bands and extract symbolic formulas mapping PhC geometry to band topology.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper leverages KANs' symbolic regression capability to extract human-readable algebraic formulas, making the learned model fully interpretable.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: Novel method by which KANs compress the geometry-to-topology mapping into explicit analytic expressions in terms of Fourier components of the dielectric function.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses the extracted symbolic formulas for deterministic inverse design of photonic crystals with prescribed topological band classes.

conceptDetails.name: topological photonic crystals
conceptDetails.description: The physical system under study—2D photonic crystals hosting disorder-resistant modes, Chern bands, and Dirac points arising from band topology.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The KAN classifies the topological class (atomic limit vs. topological) of the lowest photonic bands from real-space geometry inputs.

conceptDetails.name: band topology
conceptDetails.description: The discrete topological classification of photonic bands (Wannierizable vs. topological) that the model learns to predict and design for.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: C2 rotational symmetry of the photonic crystal unit cell is the structural constraint that defines the topological classification scheme used throughout.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Irreducible representations and symmetry eigenvalues of the C2 group at high-symmetry Brillouin zone points are used to diagnose topological band classes.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Fourier decomposition of the dielectric function provides the input feature space; the model reveals that only the lowest Fourier components govern band topology.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: The real-space geometry of photonic crystal unit cells, parameterized by Wyckoff positions and dielectric contrast, forms the design space.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Topological phase boundaries between atomic-limit and topological band classes are mapped and leveraged for inverse design.

title: Symbolic Regression with Multimodal Large Language Models and Kolmogorov Arnold Networks
abstract: We present a novel approach to symbolic regression using vision-capable large language models (LLMs) and the ideas behind Google DeepMind's Funsearch. The LLM is given a plot of a univariate function and tasked with proposing an ansatz for that function. The free parameters of the ansatz are fitted using standard numerical optimisers, and a collection of such ansätze make up the population of a genetic algorithm. Unlike other symbolic regression techniques, our method does not require the specification of a set of functions to be used in regression, but with appropriate prompt engineering, we can arbitrarily condition the generative step. By using Kolmogorov Arnold Networks (KANs), we demonstrate that ``univariate is all you need'' for symbolic regression, and extend this method to multivariate functions by learning the univariate function on each edge of a trained KAN. The combined expression is then simplified by further processing with a language model.

conceptDetails.name: multimodal llm symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: A novel method (LLM-LEx) that feeds function plots directly to vision-capable LLMs to propose symbolic ansätze, combining visual reasoning with expression generation.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression—finding closed-form mathematical expressions that best fit a dataset—is the central task the paper addresses.

conceptDetails.name: kolmogorov-arnold networks
conceptDetails.description: KANs are used to decompose multivariate symbolic regression into a series of univariate problems on each network edge, enabling the LLM-based method to scale beyond single-variable functions.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames symbolic regression as automated scientific discovery, aiming to recover interpretable analytic expressions from data without manual specification of functional forms.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The method prioritizes producing interpretable symbolic expressions rather than black-box fits, making interpretability a core motivation and evaluation criterion.

conceptDetails.name: prompt engineering
conceptDetails.description: Prompt engineering is used to condition the LLM's generative step, enabling domain-specific constraints such as requiring special functions or particular functional forms.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Multiple LLM-generated ansätze form a population that is evolved via a genetic algorithm, constituting an ensemble-based search strategy.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Trained KANs serve as surrogate representations of the target multivariate function, whose edge functions are then symbolically regressed.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering an analytic expression from observed data is an ill-posed inverse problem, and the paper addresses this via LLM-guided search and genetic optimization.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: A genetic algorithm inspired by FunSearch is used to evolve ansatz populations, with fitness-based selection acting as an implicit reward signal driving discovery.

title: AI-Driven Robotics for Optics
abstract: Optics is foundational to research in many areas of science and engineering, including nanophotonics, quantum information, materials science, biomedical imaging, and metrology. However, the design, assembly, and alignment of optical experiments remain predominantly manual, limiting throughput and reproducibility. Automating such experiments is challenging due to the strict, non-negotiable precision requirements and the diversity of optical configurations found in typical laboratories. Here, we introduce a platform that integrates generative artificial intelligence, computer vision, and robotics to automate free-space optical experiments. The platform translates user-defined goals into valid optical configurations, assembles them using a robotic arm, and performs micrometer-scale fine alignment using a robot-deployable tool. It then executes a range of automated measurements, including beam characterization, polarization mapping, and spectroscopy, with consistency surpassing that of human operators. This work demonstrates the first flexible, AI-driven automation platform for optics, offering a path towards remote operation, cloud labs, and high-throughput discovery in the optical sciences.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The platform integrates design, robotic assembly, fine alignment, and automated measurement into a complete end-to-end scientific workflow for free-space optics.

conceptDetails.name: robotic optical assembly
conceptDetails.description: A robotic arm physically assembles free-space optical setups by placing components at LLM-specified positions, a novel capability demonstrated for the first time in this work.

conceptDetails.name: llm optical agent
conceptDetails.description: A fine-tuned LLM acts as an 'Optics agent' that interprets natural language experimental goals and generates geometrically and physically valid optical layout configurations.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: LLaMA3.1-8B-Instruct is fine-tuned using QuanTA (Quantum-informed Tensor Adaptation) to generate physically valid optical setup configurations from natural language descriptions.

conceptDetails.name: micrometer alignment tool
conceptDetails.description: A purpose-built robot-deployable tool achieves sub-micron fine alignment of optical components, meeting the strict precision requirements of free-space optical experiments.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: The platform automates the full pipeline of optical experiment design, translating user-defined goals into valid spatial configurations of optical components.

conceptDetails.name: computer vision
conceptDetails.description: A vision system on the robotic arm performs optical component identification, positional error correction, and guides pick-and-place operations with sub-millimeter precision.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The system enables high-throughput, autonomous exploration of optical configurations in the laboratory, accelerating scientific discovery in optics.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Geometric similarity transformations (translations, scaling, rotations, reflections) are used to synthetically expand a small set of reference optical setups into a large labeled training dataset.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA employs quantum-inspired tensor networks for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, enabling high-rank weight adaptations with fewer trainable parameters.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Fine alignment and real-space optimization involve iterative feedback loops that solve inverse problems to minimize optical misalignment from sensor measurements.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: The closed-loop validation and refinement cycle, where failed configurations are returned to the LLM for correction, embodies an active learning paradigm.

title: High-redshift Millennium and Astrid galaxies in effective field theory at the field level
abstract: Effective Field Theory (EFT) modeling is expected to be a useful tool in the era of future higher-redshift galaxy surveys such as DESI-II and Spec-S5 due to its robust description of various large-scale structure tracers. However, large values of EFT bias parameters of higher-redshift galaxies could jeopardize the convergence of the perturbative expansion. In this paper we measure the bias parameters and other EFT coefficients from samples of two types of star-forming galaxies in the state-of-the-art MilleniumTNG and Astrid hydrodynamical simulations. Our measurements are based on the field-level EFT forward model that allows for precision EFT parameter measurements by virtue of cosmic variance cancellation. Specifically, we consider approximately representative samples of Lyman-break galaxies (LBGs) and Lyman-alpha emitters (LAEs) that are consistent with the observed (angular) clustering and number density of these galaxies at $z=3$. Reproducing the linear biases and number densities observed from existing LAE and LBG data, we find quadratic bias parameters that are roughly consistent with those predicted from the halo model coupled with a simple halo occupation distribution model. We also find non-perturbative velocity contributions (Fingers of God) of a similar size for LBGs to the familiar case of Luminous Red Galaxies. However, these contributions are quite small for LAEs despite their large satellite fraction values of up to $\sim 30\%$. Our results indicate that the effective momentum reach $k_{\rm{Max}}$ at $z=3$ for LAEs (LBGs) will be in the range $0.3-0.6 ~h\rm{Mpc}^{-1}$ ($0.2-0.8~h\rm{Mpc}^{-1}$), suggesting that EFT will perform well for high redshift galaxy clustering. This work provides the first step toward obtaining realistic simulation-based priors on EFT parameters for LAEs and LBGs.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The core framework used to model galaxy bias and large-scale structure at perturbative level for high-redshift galaxy samples.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy bias
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the relationship between galaxy number density and underlying dark matter field, parameterized at linear and quadratic order for LBGs and LAEs at z=3.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: MillenniumTNG and Astrid hydrodynamical simulations provide the galaxy samples used to calibrate EFT bias parameters.

conceptDetails.name: halo occupation distribution
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: statistical model describing how galaxies populate dark matter halos, used to interpret bias parameters and satellite fractions of LBGs and LAEs.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Used in the field-level forward model to extract posterior estimates of EFT bias parameters with cosmic variance cancellation.

conceptDetails.name: perturbative expansion
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the convergence of the EFT perturbative series is evaluated by measuring the effective momentum reach k_max at z=3 for high-redshift galaxy tracers.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The field-level EFT forward model acts as a simulation-based approach to precision parameter inference from galaxy fields.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Dark matter halos and their occupation distributions underpin the bias model connecting galaxies to the underlying density field.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: EFT bias parameter measurements from simulations serve as simulation-based priors for future survey analyses.

title: Variational diffusion transformers for conditional sampling of supernovae spectra
abstract: Type Ia Supernovae (SNe Ia) have become the most precise distance indicators in astrophysics due to their incredible observational homogeneity. Increasing discovery rates, however, have revealed multiple sub-populations with spectroscopic properties that are both diverse and difficult to interpret using existing physical models. These peculiar events are hard to identify from sparsely sampled observations and can introduce systematics in cosmological analyses if not flagged early; they are also of broader importance for building a cohesive understanding of thermonuclear explosions. In this work, we introduce DiTSNe-Ia, a variational diffusion-based generative model conditioned on light curve observations and trained to reproduce the observed spectral diversity of SNe Ia. In experiments with realistic light curves and spectra from radiative transfer simulations, DiTSNe-Ia achieves significantly more accurate reconstructions than the widely used SALT3 templates across a broad range of observation phases (from 10 days before peak light to 30 days after it). DiTSNe-Ia yields a mean squared error of 0.108 across all phases-five times lower than SALT3's 0.508-and an after-peak error of just 0.0191, an order of magnitude smaller than SALT3's 0.305. Additionally, our model produces well-calibrated credible intervals with near-nominal coverage, particularly at post-peak phases. DiTSNe-Ia is a powerful tool for rapidly inferring the spectral properties of SNe Ia and other transient astrophysical phenomena for which a physical description does not yet exist.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: DiTSNe-Ia is built on a variational diffusion framework that learns to denoise and generate spectra conditioned on light curve observations.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture is used as the backbone, enabling flexible modeling of irregularly-sampled light curve and spectral sequences.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The DiT architecture serves as the score model, predicting added noise at each diffusion timestep for spectral generation.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model casts spectra inference as sampling from a posterior of SNe Ia spectra conditioned on light curve and time.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Cross-attention is used to condition spectral generation on light curve observations, phase, and diffusion time representations.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The model produces well-calibrated credible intervals over predicted spectra with near-nominal coverage, enabling principled uncertainty estimates.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The core task is reconstructing high-resolution spectra from sparse, multi-band photometric light curve observations.

conceptDetails.name: light curve conditioning
conceptDetails.description: Novel multi-modal conditioning scheme that embeds photometric light curves including filter transmission as categorical variables into the diffusion process.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: DiTSNe-Ia is evaluated for calibration quality, showing that its credible intervals achieve near-nominal coverage particularly at post-peak phases.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Rapid spectral inference from light curves enables early identification of peculiar SNe Ia sub-populations to flag systematics in cosmological analyses.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: DiTSNe-Ia serves as a data-driven surrogate for physical spectral templates like SALT3, achieving far lower reconstruction error across all phases.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained and evaluated on spectra and light curves from radiative transfer simulations in the absence of large observed datasets.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Sinusoidal positional embeddings encode irregularly-sampled wavelength and time coordinates for both spectra and light curves.

title: Learning simple heuristic rules for classifying materials based on chemical composition
abstract: In the past decade, there has been a significant interest in the use of machine learning approaches in materials science research. Conventional deep learning approaches that rely on complex, nonlinear models have become increasingly important in computational materials science due to their high predictive accuracy. In contrast to these approaches, we have shown in a recent work that a remarkably simple learned heuristic rule -- based on the concept of topogivity -- can classify whether a material is topological using only its chemical composition. In this paper, we go beyond the topology classification scenario by also studying the use of machine learning to develop simple heuristic rules for classifying whether a material is a metal based on chemical composition. Moreover, we present a framework for incorporating chemistry-informed inductive bias based on the structure of the periodic table. For both the topology classification and the metallicity classification tasks, we empirically characterize the performance of simple heuristic rules fit with and without chemistry-informed inductive bias across a wide range of training set sizes. We find evidence that incorporating chemistry-informed inductive bias can reduce the amount of training data required to reach a given level of test accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops binary classifiers for two materials tasks: predicting topological character and metallicity from chemical composition alone.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The central goal is learning simple heuristic rules (one parameter per element) that provide chemical intuition rather than black-box accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: heuristic rule learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core methodology: using ML to fit simple, table-lookup rules that replicate chemical intuition for material property prediction.

conceptDetails.name: topogivity
conceptDetails.description: A novel element-wise scalar parameter introduced in prior work and extended here, quantifying an element's tendency to form topological compounds.

conceptDetails.name: chemistry-informed inductive bias
conceptDetails.description: A framework that restricts model parameters using periodic table group structure, reducing required training data to reach a target accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The full model assigns exactly one scalar parameter per element, yielding a minimal, human-readable rule for material classification.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: The topogivity-based heuristic rule is applied to discover new topological materials not detectable by standard symmetry-based methods.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper designs chemical composition representations that encode periodic table structure as inductive bias, directly shaping what the model can learn.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Element fractions from the unit cell chemical composition serve as the sole input features for all classifiers.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Weight tying based on periodic table group structure enforces chemistry-motivated symmetry constraints in the restricted model.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The same modeling framework is systematically evaluated on both topology classification and metallicity classification tasks.

title: Heterosynaptic Circuits Are Universal Gradient Machines
abstract: We propose a design principle for the learning circuits of the biological brain. The principle states that almost any dendritic weights updated via heterosynaptic plasticity can implement a generalized and efficient class of gradient-based meta-learning. The theory suggests that a broad class of biologically plausible learning algorithms, together with the standard machine learning optimizers, can be grounded in heterosynaptic circuit motifs. This principle suggests that the phenomenology of (anti-) Hebbian (HBP) and heterosynaptic plasticity (HSP) may emerge from the same underlying dynamics, thus providing a unifying explanation. It also suggests an alternative perspective of neuroplasticity, where HSP is promoted to the primary learning and memory mechanism, and HBP is an emergent byproduct. We present simulations that show that (a) HSP can explain the metaplasticity of neurons, (b) HSP can explain the flexibility of the biology circuits, and (c) gradient learning can arise quickly from simple evolutionary dynamics that do not compute any explicit gradient. While our primary focus is on biology, the principle also implies a new approach to designing AI training algorithms and physically learnable AI hardware. Conceptually, our result demonstrates that contrary to the common belief, gradient computation may be extremely easy and common in nature.

conceptDetails.name: heterosynaptic plasticity
conceptDetails.description: The primary biological mechanism studied, where synapses not directly involved in a signal pathway are modulated by other neurons, shown here to universally implement gradient learning.

conceptDetails.name: meta-learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames heterosynaptic circuits as implementing gradient-based meta-learning, showing that biologically local update rules can simulate efficient learning-to-learn algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: biologically plausible learning
conceptDetails.description: The overarching design constraint requiring locality, flexibility, and robustness in learning rules, which the proposed HSDC mechanism satisfies while matching gradient descent performance.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Gradient descent optimization is the target behavior that heterosynaptic circuits are proven to simulate, with the consistency score serving as an emergent effective learning rate.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The meta-learning framework requires generalizing across tasks, and the paper shows HSP circuits implement this via dendritic weight updates that adapt rapidly to new environments.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Evolutionary dynamics without explicit gradient computation are modeled as stochastic processes, demonstrating that gradient learning can emerge quickly from random variation and selection.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The fast adaptation property of the proposed heterosynaptic circuits mirrors transfer learning, enabling rapid reuse of learned structure across different tasks and network topologies.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A key contribution is providing a mechanistic explanation of how gradient computation arises naturally in biological neural circuits, giving interpretable grounding to observed plasticity phenomena.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The theory explicitly addresses robustness across highly structured and seemingly random network topologies, demonstrating flexibility as a core design requirement satisfied by HSP.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Heterosynaptic circuits are formalized as superpositions of two computation graphs on the same node set, with the amorphous circuit variant resembling the generality of graph neural network architectures.

title: Scaling Laws For Scalable Oversight
abstract: Scalable oversight, the process by which weaker AI systems supervise stronger ones, has been proposed as a key strategy to control future superintelligent systems. However, it is still unclear how scalable oversight itself scales. To address this gap, we propose a framework that quantifies the probability of successful oversight as a function of the capabilities of the overseer and the system being overseen. Specifically, our framework models oversight as a game between capability-mismatched players; the players have oversight-specific Elo scores that are a piecewise-linear function of their general intelligence, with two plateaus corresponding to task incompetence and task saturation. We validate our framework with a modified version of the game Nim and then apply it to four oversight games: Mafia, Debate, Backdoor Code and Wargames. For each game, we find scaling laws that approximate how domain performance depends on general AI system capability. We then build on our findings in a theoretical study of Nested Scalable Oversight (NSO), a process in which trusted models oversee untrusted stronger models, which then become the trusted models in the next step. We identify conditions under which NSO succeeds and derive numerically (and in some cases analytically) the optimal number of oversight levels to maximize the probability of oversight success. We also apply our theory to our four oversight games, where we find that NSO success rates at a general Elo gap of 400 are 13.5% for Mafia, 51.7% for Debate, 10.0% for Backdoor Code, and 9.4% for Wargames; these rates decline further when overseeing stronger systems.

conceptDetails.name: scalable oversight
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper: quantifying how weaker AI systems can reliably supervise stronger ones as capabilities scale.

conceptDetails.name: nested scalable oversight
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: a recursive process where trusted models oversee untrusted stronger models, which then become trusted overseers in subsequent steps.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives scaling laws relating general AI capability (Elo) to domain-specific oversight performance across multiple games.

conceptDetails.name: double relu model
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: a piecewise-linear function mapping general intelligence Elo to domain-specific oversight Elo, capturing incompetence, payoff, and saturation phases.

conceptDetails.name: oversight elo
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: game-specific Elo scores assigned to AI models in their roles as Guards or Houdinis to quantify oversight capability independently of general intelligence.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The framework evaluates how robust oversight remains as the capability gap between overseer and overseen system increases.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The Double ReLU model is empirically validated on Counting-to-21 and four oversight games to confirm its predictive accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Nested Scalable Oversight builds on reward modeling and iterated amplification paradigms that optimize oversight reward signals through recursive supervision.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The framework quantifies the probability of oversight failure as a function of capability mismatch, providing numerical safety thresholds.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Backdoor Code oversight game tests whether a Guard can detect hidden malicious behavior in AI-generated code, a key interpretability challenge.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper situates its work within the broader RL alignment literature including Recursive Reward Modeling and Iterated Amplification.

title: Higher-Spin Currents and Flows in Auxiliary Field Sigma Models
abstract: We study local, higher-spin conserved currents in integrable $2d$ sigma models that have been deformed via coupling to auxiliary fields. These currents generate integrability-preserving flows introduced by Smirnov and Zamolodchikov. For auxiliary field (AF) deformations of a free boson, we prove that local spin-$n$ currents exist for all $n$ and give recursion relations that characterize Smirnov-Zamolodchikov (SZ) flows driven by these currents. We then show how to construct spin-$2n$ currents in a unified class of auxiliary field sigma models with common structure -- including AF theories based on the principal chiral model (PCM), its non-Abelian T-dual, (bi-)Yang-Baxter deformations of the PCM, and symmetric space models -- for interaction functions of one variable, and describe SZ flows driven by any function of the stress tensor in these cases. Finally, we give perturbative solutions for spin-$3$ SZ flows in any member of our unified class of AF models with underlying $\mathfrak{su}(3)$ algebra. Part of our analysis shows that the class of AF deformations can be extended by allowing the interaction function to depend on a larger set of variables than has previously been considered.

conceptDetails.name: auxiliary field deformations
conceptDetails.description: The core technical framework of the paper: sigma models deformed by coupling to auxiliary fields, generating a unified class of integrable theories including Yang-Baxter and T-dual models.

conceptDetails.name: higher-spin currents
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves existence of local spin-n conserved currents for all n in AF deformed free boson models and constructs spin-2n currents for a unified class of AF sigma models.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: Local higher-spin conserved currents are the central objects of study, with explicit proofs of their existence and recursion relations for their construction.

conceptDetails.name: integrability flows
conceptDetails.description: Smirnov-Zamolodchikov flows driven by higher-spin currents are characterized through recursion relations and perturbative solutions, constituting a central result of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies integrable 2d quantum field theories, analyzing their conserved currents and deformation flows within the QFT framework.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs integrability-preserving deformation flows that maintain the infinite tower of conserved charges characteristic of integrable theories.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Lie group structure (su(3), principal chiral model) underpins the sigma model targets, and representation theory is used to classify conserved currents.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: Smirnov-Zamolodchikov flows connect the deformed theories to CFT fixed points, and the stress tensor plays a central role in generating these flows.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Auxiliary field deformations generate new effective Lagrangians whose perturbative structure is analyzed order by order in the deformation parameter.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The local higher-spin charges constrain S-matrix factorization and absence of particle production in the integrable theories studied.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Smirnov-Zamolodchikov flows interpolate between theories under renormalization group-like deformations driven by composite operators.

title: I-Con: A Unifying Framework for Representation Learning
abstract: As the field of representation learning grows, there has been a proliferation of different loss functions to solve different classes of problems. We introduce a single information-theoretic equation that generalizes a large collection of modern loss functions in machine learning. In particular, we introduce a framework that shows that several broad classes of machine learning methods are precisely minimizing an integrated KL divergence between two conditional distributions: the supervisory and learned representations. This viewpoint exposes a hidden information geometry underlying clustering, spectral methods, dimensionality reduction, contrastive learning, and supervised learning. This framework enables the development of new loss functions by combining successful techniques from across the literature. We not only present a wide array of proofs, connecting over 23 different approaches, but we also leverage these theoretical results to create state-of-the-art unsupervised image classifiers that achieve a +8% improvement over the prior state-of-the-art on unsupervised classification on ImageNet-1K. We also demonstrate that I-Con can be used to derive principled debiasing methods which improve contrastive representation learners.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a unified framework (I-Con) that subsumes over 23 representation learning methods under a single information-theoretic objective.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: I-Con generalizes contrastive learning methods including SimCLR, MoCo, CLIP, and CMC as special cases of integrated KL divergence minimization.

conceptDetails.name: kl divergence unification
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core insight is that all unified methods minimize an integrated KL divergence between supervisory and learned conditional distributions over neighbors.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: I-Con enables principled construction of new loss functions by mixing supervisory and learned distribution families from across the representation learning literature.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The framework unifies self-supervised approaches by showing they minimize divergence between supervisory and learned conditional distributions.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: K-Means, spectral clustering, and deep clustering methods are derived as special cases of I-Con by choosing cluster-uniform learned distributions.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Methods like t-SNE, SNE, PCA, and UMAP are unified under I-Con by appropriate choice of conditional probability distributions.

conceptDetails.name: information geometry
conceptDetails.description: The paper exposes a hidden information geometry shared across clustering, dimensionality reduction, contrastive, and supervised learning via KL divergence minimization.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral clustering and graph-based methods such as Normalized Cuts are shown to emerge as special cases of the I-Con framework.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: I-Con produces learned embeddings by aligning a parameterized conditional distribution over neighbors with a supervisory target distribution.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian and Student-T kernel-defined neighborhood distributions are used as the core mechanism for specifying supervisory and learned conditionals in I-Con.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: I-Con bridges supervised, unsupervised, and self-supervised learning within a single objective, enabling cross-paradigm transfer of techniques.

title: High-performance training and inference for deep equivariant interatomic potentials
abstract: Machine learning interatomic potentials, particularly those based on deep equivariant neural networks, have demonstrated state-of-the-art accuracy and computational efficiency in atomistic modeling tasks like molecular dynamics and high-throughput screening. The size of datasets and demands of downstream workflows are growing rapidly, making robust and scalable software essential. This work presents a major overhaul of the NequIP framework focusing on multi-node parallelism, computational performance, and extensibility. The redesigned framework supports distributed training on large datasets and removes barriers preventing full utilization of the PyTorch 2.0 compiler at train time. We demonstrate this acceleration in a case study by training Allegro models on the SPICE 2 dataset of organic molecular systems. For inference, we introduce the first end-to-end infrastructure that uses the PyTorch Ahead-of-Time Inductor compiler for machine learning interatomic potentials. Additionally, we implement a custom kernel for the Allegro model's most expensive operation, the tensor product. Together, these advancements speed up molecular dynamics calculations on system sizes of practical relevance by up to a factor of 18.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The core architecture of NequIP and Allegro models, incorporating rotational, mirror, and other physical symmetries directly into the network structure for improved data efficiency and accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: interatomic potentials
conceptDetails.description: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) are the primary subject, with the paper presenting a major framework overhaul to accelerate their training and inference.

conceptDetails.name: molecular dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The primary downstream application, with the paper achieving up to 18x speedup for molecular dynamics calculations on system sizes of practical relevance.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central focus of the redesigned NequIP framework, enabling distributed multi-node training on large datasets like SPICE 2 and MLIP integration with high-performance simulation codes.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Equivariant architectures preserve rotational and mirror symmetries, ensuring conservative force fields and energy conservation in molecular dynamics simulations.

conceptDetails.name: ahead-of-time compilation
conceptDetails.description: A novel inference infrastructure using PyTorch's Ahead-of-Time Inductor (AOTI) compiler, introduced here as the first end-to-end AOTI deployment for machine learning interatomic potentials.

conceptDetails.name: tensor product kernel
conceptDetails.description: A custom CUDA kernel optimized for the tensor product operation, the most computationally expensive step in the Allegro model, delivering significant inference speedups.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The underlying network structure of NequIP and Allegro, operating on atomic neighborhood graphs to compute interatomic interactions.

conceptDetails.name: distributed data parallel
conceptDetails.description: A custom Distributed Data Parallel training paradigm that defers gradient communication until after the full backward pass, enabling TorchInductor to compile the entire backward pass without interruption.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: A key downstream application domain, with the generalized MLIPs trained in this work supporting high-throughput screening and property prediction across diverse material systems.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: MLIPs function as surrogate models for expensive ab initio calculations, enabling large-scale atomistic simulations at DFT-level accuracy.

title: Darkness in the Crust: Searching for the truly "Dark" Subhalos with Paleo-detectors
abstract: Low-mass dark matter (DM) subhalos are pivotal in understanding the small-scale structure of the universe, thereby offering a sensitive method to discriminate between different cosmological models. In this study, we estimate the local number density of cold DM subhalos in the solar neighborhood, and demonstrate that their sparse distribution makes their detection via direct detection experiments highly improbable. However, it is plausible to expect that an $\mathcal{O}(1)$ number of subhalos could be detected by Paleo-detectors, a proposed new technique to look for DM by reading out damage tracks left by past DM interactions in minerals, due to their extended exposure times. Hence, we explore how Paleo-detectors can serve as effective probes for the properties of low-mass subhalos, $\mathcal{O}(10^{-5}-10^8) M_{\odot}$. We find that Paleo-detectors might be able to constrain certain regions of the subhalo mass-concentration relation (for subhalo masses of $10-10^4 M_\odot$ if DM has a mass of $\sim5$GeV). This is a new and complementary type of study that seeks to combine information from the particle nature of DM to that of small scale structures.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates low-mass dark matter subhalos and their detectability via paleo-detectors, combining the particle nature of DM with small-scale structure properties.

conceptDetails.name: paleo-detectors
conceptDetails.description: A proposed detection technique that reads damage tracks left by past DM interactions in ancient minerals, providing exposure times of O(10^9) years central to this study.

conceptDetails.name: subhalo mass function
conceptDetails.description: The statistical distribution of subhalo abundances across mass scales, which paleo-detectors are shown to probe down to O(10^-5) solar masses.

conceptDetails.name: mass-concentration relation
conceptDetails.description: The relationship c(M) describing how halo density profiles scale with total mass, which this paper demonstrates paleo-detectors can constrain for subhalo masses of 10–10^4 solar masses.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: ΛCDM-based simulations are used to estimate the local number density of cold dark matter subhalos in the solar neighborhood and predict encounter rates.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper quantifies the probability of detecting a subhalo encounter event in both conventional direct detection and paleo-detectors, establishing detection thresholds.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Stochastic sampling techniques used to simulate subhalo encounter rates and propagate uncertainties in the subhalo population models.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Used to derive posterior constraints on the subhalo mass-concentration relation from paleo-detector (non-)detections.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring subhalo structural properties and the mass-concentration relation from paleo-detector track records constitutes an inverse problem framework.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: Mentioned as a secondary scientific application of paleo-detectors relevant to astrophysical events such as core-collapse supernovae.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: Referenced as a key observable that ΛCDM successfully explains, motivating the study of small-scale substructure as a complementary cosmological test.

title: Machine Learning Decoding of Circuit-Level Noise for Bivariate Bicycle Codes
abstract: Fault-tolerant quantum computers will depend crucially on the performance of the classical decoding algorithm which takes in the results of measurements and outputs corrections to the errors inferred to have occurred. Machine learning models have shown great promise as decoders for the surface code; however, this promise has not yet been substantiated for the more challenging task of decoding quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) codes. In this paper, we present a recurrent, transformer-based neural network designed to decode circuit-level noise on Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes, introduced recently by Bravyi et al (Nature 627, 778-782, 2024). For the $[[72,12,6]]$ BB code, at a physical error rate of $p=0.1\%$, our model achieves a logical error rate almost $5$ times lower than belief propagation with ordered statistics decoding (BP-OSD). Moreover, while BP-OSD has a wide distribution of runtimes with significant outliers, our model has a consistent runtime and is an order-of-magnitude faster than the worst-case times from a benchmark BP-OSD implementation. On the $[[144,12,12]]$ BB code, our model obtains worse logical error rates but maintains the speed advantage. These results demonstrate that machine learning decoders can out-perform conventional decoders on QLDPC codes, in regimes of current interest.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets fault-tolerant quantum computation, specifically addressing the classical decoding bottleneck that limits real-time operation of quantum computers.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A recurrent, transformer-based neural network is the core architecture proposed for decoding circuit-level noise on BB codes.

conceptDetails.name: qldpc decoding
conceptDetails.description: Quantum low-density parity-check codes, specifically Bivariate Bicycle codes [[72,12,6]] and [[144,12,12]], are the primary decoding targets studied.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Code-aware self-attention, adapted from classical coding theory, is introduced as a key contribution to accelerate ML decoder training and improve robustness.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: A recurrent architecture is employed to reduce the effective input size per iteration, improving trainability for large QLDPC codes under circuit-level noise.

conceptDetails.name: syndrome-based decoding
conceptDetails.description: The decoder takes syndrome measurement results as input and predicts error corrections, including under faulty measurement conditions (circuit-level noise).

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model is designed to approximate a maximum likelihood decoder via autoregressive prediction of conditional error probabilities, avoiding exponentially large output spaces.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central motivation is achieving consistent, real-time-compatible runtimes that outperform the high-variance, worst-case-cubic complexity of BP-OSD.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Error decoding is framed as predicting which logical error class occurred from syndrome measurements, a key classification task.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The paper works with logical qubit states encoded in stabilizer codes, where errors on physical qubits corrupt the logical quantum state being protected.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Training strategies that leverage pre-training and structured data augmentation are discussed as approaches to handle the exponentially growing syndrome space.

title: Learning Topological Invariance
abstract: Two geometric spaces are in the same topological class if they are related by certain geometric deformations. We propose machine learning methods that automate learning of topological invariance and apply it in the context of knot theory, where two knots are equivalent if they are related by ambient space isotopy. Specifically, given only the knot and no information about its topological invariants, we employ contrastive and generative machine learning techniques to map different representatives of the same knot class to the same point in an embedding vector space. An auto-regressive decoder Transformer network can then generate new representatives from the same knot class. We also describe a student-teacher setup that we use to interpret which known knot invariants are learned by the neural networks to compute the embeddings, and observe a strong correlation with the Goeritz matrix in all setups that we tested. We also develop an approach to resolving the Jones Unknot Conjecture by exploring the vicinity of the embedding space of the Jones polynomial near the locus where the unknots cluster, which we use to generate braid words with simple Jones polynomials.

conceptDetails.name: topological invariance learning
conceptDetails.description: The central methodological contribution: training neural networks to automatically learn equivalence classes under continuous deformations without labeled invariants.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: Semi-hard triplet loss and a novel centroid loss are used to map different braid word representatives of the same knot class to the same point in embedding space.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks learn an embedding vector space where topologically equivalent knots cluster together, enabling both classification and generation.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The core task is learning representations that capture topological invariance without any a priori knowledge of topological invariants.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: An encoder-decoder Transformer is trained to map braid word inputs to canonical representatives of the same knot class, enabling generative invariance learning.

conceptDetails.name: knot invariants
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies which known knot invariants (Goeritz matrix, Jones polynomial, hyperbolic volume) are implicitly discovered by the neural networks during training.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Auto-regressive Transformer decoder generates new braid word representatives from the same knot equivalence class as an input knot.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A student-teacher setup is developed where simpler student networks are trained on known invariants to identify which invariants (Goeritz matrix) the teacher network implicitly learned.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Training uses only knot equivalence class membership as supervision signal, without labeled topological invariants, making it inherently self-supervised.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Architectures are designed to be invariant under braid group symmetries, ensuring the embeddings respect the underlying topological equivalences.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Knots are encoded as braid words in the non-Abelian braid group using Artin generators, making group theory central to the data representation.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A novel centroid loss is developed specifically to enforce topological invariance by pulling all representatives of a class toward their class centroid.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A CNN architecture is employed in contrastive learning to achieve invariance under cyclic braid group symmetries via the convolutional structure.

title: Rapid inference and comparison of gravitational-wave population models with neural variational posteriors
abstract: The LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA catalog has been analyzed with an abundance of different population models due to theoretical uncertainty in the formation of gravitational-wave sources. To expedite model exploration, we introduce an efficient and accurate variational Bayesian approach that learns the population posterior with a normalizing flow and serves as a drop-in replacement for existing samplers. With hardware acceleration, inference takes just seconds for the current set of black-hole mergers and readily scales to larger catalogs. The trained posteriors provide an arbitrary number of independent samples with exact probability densities, unlike established stochastic sampling algorithms, while requiring up to three orders of magnitude fewer likelihood evaluations and as few as $\mathcal{O}(10^3)$. Provided the posterior support is covered, discrepancies can be addressed with smoothed importance sampling, which quantifies a goodness-of-fit metric for the variational approximation while also estimating the evidence for Bayesian model selection. Neural variational inference thus enables interactive development, analysis, and comparison of population models, making it a useful tool for astrophysical interpretation of current and future gravitational-wave observations.

conceptDetails.name: variational population inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel method combining variational Bayes with normalizing flows specifically for hierarchical gravitational-wave population analysis, the central contribution of this paper.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Used as the flexible parametric family for the variational posterior approximation, enabling exact probability density evaluation and efficient sampling.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The foundational framework for population-level inference over gravitational-wave source parameters using posterior distributions and model evidence.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The observational domain: LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA binary black-hole merger catalogs are the data being analyzed with population models.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The core task — learning accurate posterior distributions over population hyperparameters via variational optimization rather than stochastic sampling.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Importance sampling and smoothed importance sampling are used to correct variational approximation errors and estimate Bayesian evidence.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian model selection
conceptDetails.description: The framework estimates the Bayesian evidence via neural importance sampling, enabling Bayes factor computation for comparing competing population models.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The normalizing flow learns an explicit probability density over population parameters, enabling exact likelihood evaluation for downstream inference.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: Pareto smoothed importance sampling provides a diagnostic (k-hat statistic) that quantifies how well the variational posterior approximates the true posterior.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The variational approach drastically reduces the number of likelihood evaluations (orders of magnitude fewer than MCMC/nested sampling) needed for population inference.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key contribution is that the method scales to large catalogs with fixed computational cost independent of catalog size, using GPU acceleration.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The method parallels simulation-based inference in converting intractable likelihoods into tractable optimization problems using neural approximators.

title: Bayesian Component Separation for DESI LAE Automated Spectroscopic Redshifts and Photometric Targeting
abstract: Lyman Alpha Emitters (LAEs) are valuable high-redshift cosmological probes traditionally identified using specialized narrow-band photometric surveys. In ground-based spectroscopy, it can be difficult to distinguish the sharp LAE peak from residual sky emission lines using automated methods, leading to misclassified redshifts. We present a Bayesian spectral component separation technique to automatically determine spectroscopic redshifts for LAEs while marginalizing over sky residuals. We use visually inspected spectra of LAEs obtained using the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) to create a data-driven prior and can determine redshift by jointly inferring sky residual, LAE, and residual components for each individual spectrum. We demonstrate this method on 910 spectroscopically observed $z = 2-4$ DESI LAE candidate spectra and determine their redshifts with $>$90% accuracy when validated against visually inspected redshifts. Using the $Δχ^2$ value from our pipeline as a proxy for detection confidence, we then explore potential survey design choices and implications for targeting LAEs with medium-band photometry. This method allows for scalability and accuracy in determining redshifts from DESI spectra, and the results provide recommendations for LAE targeting in anticipation of future high-redshift spectroscopic surveys.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core methodology: a Bayesian spectral component separation technique that jointly infers sky residual, LAE emission, and noise components to determine spectroscopic redshifts.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method estimates the posterior over redshift by marginalizing over sky residuals and noise, enabling robust redshift recovery for individual spectra.

conceptDetails.name: sky residual marginalization
conceptDetails.description: Novel technique of building a data-driven basis for sky residual contamination and analytically marginalizing over it during redshift inference.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detecting the sharp Lyman-alpha emission peak against contaminating sky residual emission lines is the fundamental observational challenge addressed by the pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral component separation decomposes each observed spectrum into LAE, sky residual, and noise components using data-driven basis vectors.

conceptDetails.name: data-driven spectral prior
conceptDetails.description: Visually inspected LAE spectra are used to construct an empirical prior on the Lyman-alpha line profile, replacing parametric templates.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The Δχ² statistic between the best-fit LAE model and a null hypothesis is used as a proxy for detection confidence and drives photometric targeting recommendations.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Determining spectroscopic redshift from noisy spectra contaminated by sky lines is framed as an inverse inference problem with a data-driven prior.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline classifies 910 DESI LAE candidate spectra, assigning spectroscopic redshifts and distinguishing true LAEs from misclassified objects.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key design goal is automated, scalable redshift determination for current DESI observations and future high-redshift spectroscopic surveys without manual visual inspection.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: Survey design choices for medium-band photometric targeting of LAEs are explored using the pipeline's detection confidence metric as a guide.

title: On Soft Clustering For Correlation Estimators
abstract: Properly estimating correlations between objects at different spatial scales necessitates $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ distance calculations. For this reason, most widely adopted packages for estimating correlations use clustering algorithms to approximate local trends. However, methods for quantifying the error introduced by this clustering have been understudied. In response, we present an algorithm for estimating correlations that is probabilistic in the way that it clusters objects, enabling us to quantify the uncertainty caused by clustering simply through model inference. These soft clustering assignments enable correlation estimators that are theoretically differentiable with respect to their input catalogs. Thus, we also build a theoretical framework for differentiable correlation functions and describe their utility in comparison to existing surrogate models. Notably, we find that repeated normalization and distance function calls slow gradient calculations and that sparse Jacobians destabilize precision, pointing towards either approximate or surrogate methods as a necessary solution to exact gradients from correlation functions. To that end, we close with a discussion of surrogate models as proxies for correlation functions. We provide an example that demonstrates the efficacy of surrogate models to enable gradient-based optimization of astrophysical model parameters, successfully minimizing a correlation function output. Our numerical experiments cover science cases across cosmology, from point spread function (PSF) modeling efforts to gravitational simulations to galaxy intrinsic alignment (IA).

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Soft probabilistic clustering is the core algorithmic contribution, enabling uncertainty quantification in correlation estimators by assigning objects to clusters with probabilities rather than hard assignments.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary motivation is quantifying epistemic uncertainty introduced by clustering approximations in correlation function estimation.

conceptDetails.name: soft clustering assignments
conceptDetails.description: Novel probabilistic cluster membership assignments (as opposed to hard assignments) that make correlation estimators differentiable and uncertainty-aware.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Surrogate models are proposed and demonstrated as proxies for correlation functions to enable gradient-based optimization when exact differentiability is computationally intractable.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable correlation functions
conceptDetails.description: A novel theoretical framework for making correlation function estimators differentiable with respect to input catalogs, enabling gradient-based science workflows.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Model inference is used to quantify clustering uncertainty through probabilistic assignments, and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo is used for posterior sampling of astrophysical parameters.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo is employed for fast posterior sampling of intrinsic alignment parameters using differentiable surrogate models.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Gradient-based optimization of astrophysical model parameters through differentiable correlation functions represents an inverse problem framework.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Gravitational simulations are used as a numerical experiment to demonstrate differentiability of the correlation estimator through the clustering approximation.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The surrogate model experiment demonstrates posterior estimation of intrinsic alignment parameters by minimizing a correlation function output.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Galaxy intrinsic alignment and weak gravitational lensing, key science drivers of dark energy surveys, are among the primary astrophysical applications explored.

conceptDetails.name: rho statistics
conceptDetails.description: PSF model quality diagnostics based on correlations in size and shape residuals, used as a concrete science case for the soft clustering framework.

title: Quantum Mechanics and Neural Networks
abstract: We demonstrate that any Euclidean-time quantum mechanical theory may be represented as a neural network, ensured by the Kosambi-Karhunen-Loève theorem, mean-square path continuity, and finite two-point functions. The additional constraint of reflection positivity, which is related to unitarity, may be achieved by a number of mechanisms, such as imposing neural network parameter space splitting or the Markov property. Non-differentiability of the networks is related to the appearance of non-trivial commutators. Neural networks acting on Markov processes are no longer Markov, but still reflection positive, which facilitates the definition of deep neural network quantum systems. We illustrate these principles in several examples using numerical implementations, recovering classic quantum mechanical results such as Heisenberg uncertainty, non-trivial commutators, and the spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: reflection positivity
conceptDetails.description: The central Euclidean constraint ensuring unitarity of the Lorentzian continuation; the paper develops two mechanisms (parameter splitting and the Markov property) to achieve it in neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Euclidean-time quantum mechanical theories are recast as stochastic processes, with path properties like mean-square continuity and the Markov property playing central roles.

conceptDetails.name: neural network field theory
conceptDetails.description: The NN-FT framework — defining field-theoretic correlation functions via an ensemble of neural network functions with a parameter distribution — is the foundational language of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The Kosambi-Karhunen-Loève spectral decomposition is the key theorem guaranteeing that any qualifying stochastic process admits a neural network representation.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The Osterwalder-Schrader axioms and constructive QFT framework motivate the conditions (reflection positivity, unitarity) imposed on neural network quantum systems.

conceptDetails.name: deep quantum systems
conceptDetails.description: A novel construction where composing deep neural networks on Markov or reflection-positive processes yields new classes of valid Euclidean quantum systems.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The paper recovers canonical quantum mechanical results — Heisenberg uncertainty, commutators, and energy spectra — from the neural network representation of quantum states.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks are structured to satisfy physical axioms (reflection positivity, unitarity, translation invariance) rather than trained on data, bridging NN architecture with quantum mechanics constraints.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Eigenfunction expansions of two-point correlation functions underpin the KKL theorem and the construction of neural network architectures from quantum mechanical data.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process and deep NN quantum systems are numerically simulated to verify recovery of quantum harmonic oscillator physics.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Numerical implementations use stochastic sampling to compute correlation functions, commutators, and spectra for the neural network quantum systems.

title: IAEmu: Learning Galaxy Intrinsic Alignment Correlations
abstract: The intrinsic alignments (IA) of galaxies, a key contaminant in weak lensing analyses, arise from correlations in galaxy shapes driven by tidal interactions and galaxy formation processes. Accurate IA modeling is essential for robust cosmological inference, but current approaches rely on perturbative methods that break down on nonlinear scales or on expensive simulations. We introduce IAEmu, a neural network-based emulator that predicts the galaxy position-position ($ξ$), position-orientation ($ω$), and orientation-orientation ($η$) correlation functions and their uncertainties using mock catalogs based on the halo occupation distribution (HOD) framework. Compared to simulations, IAEmu achieves ~3% average error for $ξ$ and ~5% for $ω$, while capturing the stochasticity of $η$ without overfitting. The emulator provides both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, helping identify regions where predictions may be less reliable. We also demonstrate generalization to non-HOD alignment signals by fitting to IllustrisTNG hydrodynamical simulation data. As a fully differentiable neural network, IAEmu enables $\sim$10,000$\times$ speed-ups in mapping HOD parameters to correlation functions on GPUs, compared to CPU-based simulations. This acceleration facilitates inverse modeling via gradient-based sampling, making IAEmu a powerful surrogate model for galaxy bias and IA studies with direct applications to Stage IV weak lensing surveys.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
