conceptDetails.description: IAEmu serves as a surrogate model that replaces expensive HOD simulations for predicting galaxy IA correlation functions.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is an emulator that maps HOD parameters directly to correlation functions without running full simulations.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy intrinsic alignment
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on modeling galaxy shape correlations (IA) arising from tidal interactions, a systematic contaminant in weak lensing surveys.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: IAEmu provides both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties to assess prediction reliability across parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: halo occupation distribution
conceptDetails.description: HOD framework is used to populate N-body simulation halos with galaxies and generate training data for IAEmu.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The emulator is trained on HOD-based mock catalogs derived from N-body simulations and validated against IllustrisTNG hydrodynamical simulations.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: IAEmu's differentiability enables gradient-based sampling for efficient inverse modeling to infer HOD parameters from observed correlations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Gradient-based sampling facilitated by the differentiable emulator supports Bayesian posterior estimation of galaxy bias and IA parameters.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The neural network performs regression from HOD parameter inputs to correlation function outputs across multiple statistics.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: Epistemic uncertainty estimates help identify parameter regions where IAEmu predictions are less reliable, including OOD generalization to non-HOD signals.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Weak lensing and galaxy IA modeling are key probes of dark matter distribution and large-scale structure.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: IAEmu demonstrates generalization from HOD-based training data to non-HOD IllustrisTNG hydrodynamical simulation signals.

title: Do Two AI Scientists Agree?
abstract: When two AI models are trained on the same scientific task, do they learn the same theory or two different theories? Throughout history of science, we have witnessed the rise and fall of theories driven by experimental validation or falsification: many theories may co-exist when experimental data is lacking, but the space of survived theories become more constrained with more experimental data becoming available. We show the same story is true for AI scientists. With increasingly more systems provided in training data, AI scientists tend to converge in the theories they learned, although sometimes they form distinct groups corresponding to different theories. To mechanistically interpret what theories AI scientists learn and quantify their agreement, we propose MASS, Hamiltonian-Lagrangian neural networks as AI Scientists, trained on standard problems in physics, aggregating training results across many seeds simulating the different configurations of AI scientists. Our findings suggests for AI scientists switch from learning a Hamiltonian theory in simple setups to a Lagrangian formulation when more complex systems are introduced. We also observe strong seed dependence of the training dynamics and final learned weights, controlling the rise and fall of relevant theories. We finally demonstrate that not only can our neural networks aid interpretability, it can also be applied to higher dimensional problems.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper trains neural networks to learn the Hamiltonian scalar function and derives equations of motion via Hamilton's canonical equations, studying when AI scientists prefer this formulation.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper trains neural networks to learn the Lagrangian scalar function via Euler-Lagrange equations, and finds Lagrangian formulation generalizes better to complex physical systems.

conceptDetails.name: ai scientist agreement
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept introduced by this paper: the degree to which independently trained AI models converge on the same physical theory when trained on the same data.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: MASS is used to mechanistically interpret which physical theories AI scientists learn by analyzing significant network activations and distilling learned representations.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: MASS encodes physical structure by learning a scalar potential and deriving dynamics through the principle of stationary action, constraining the hypothesis space.

conceptDetails.name: theory space convergence
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the mechanism by which the set of viable theories learned by AI scientists narrows as training data includes more complex physical systems.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies AI scientists as autonomous agents capable of discovering physical laws from data with minimal human intervention.

conceptDetails.name: seed dependence
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the strong dependence of both training dynamics and final learned theory on random initialization, controlling which physical formulation an AI scientist adopts.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper examines internal network activations and learned weight representations to understand which physical theory an AI scientist has internalized.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: The Hamiltonian and Lagrangian frameworks encode conservation of energy and symmetry, and the paper tracks which conserved-quantity formulation the network learns.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Results are aggregated across many random seeds simulating independent AI scientists, and statistical patterns across the ensemble reveal theory convergence or divergence.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: MASS serves as a surrogate for physical dynamical systems, learning scalar functions that stand in for exact Hamiltonian or Lagrangian theories.

title: The Type I Superluminous Supernova Catalogue II: Spectroscopic Evolution in the Photospheric Phase, Velocity Measurements, and Constraints on Diversity
abstract: Hydrogen-poor superluminous supernovae (SLSNe) are among the most energetic explosions in the universe, reaching luminosities up to 100 times greater than those of normal supernovae. Detailed spectral analysis hold the potential to reveal their progenitors and underlying energy sources. This paper presents the largest compilation of SLSN photospheric spectra to date, encompassing data from ePESSTO+, the FLEET search and all published spectra up to December 2022. The dataset includes a total of 974 spectra of 234 SLSNe. By constructing average phase binned spectra, we find SLSNe initially exhibit high temperatures (10000 to 11000 K), with blue continua and weak lines. A rapid transformation follows, as temperatures drop to 5000 to 6000 K by 40 days post peak, leading to stronger P-Cygni features. These averages also suggest a fraction of SLSNe may contain some He at explosion. Variance within the dataset is slightly reduced when defining the phase of spectra relative to explosion, rather than peak, and normalising to the population's median e-folding time. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) supports this, requiring fewer components to explain the same level of variation when binning data by scaled days from explosion, suggesting a more homogeneous grouping. Using PCA and K-Means clustering, we identify outlying objects with unusual spectroscopic evolution and evidence for energy input from interaction, but find not support for groupings of two or more statistically significant subpopulations. We find Fe II λ5169 lines velocities closely track the radius implied from blackbody fits, indicating formation near the photosphere. We also confirm a correlation between velocity and velocity gradient, which can be explained if all SLSNe are in homologous expansion but with different scale velocities. This behaviour aligns with expectations for an internal powering mechanism.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes and characterizes Type I superluminous supernovae (SLSNe-I) as a distinct class, examining their spectroscopic properties to constrain diversity within the population.

conceptDetails.name: spectral time-series analysis
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs phase-binned average spectra from 974 spectra of 234 SLSNe to track the photospheric evolution from explosion through the photospheric phase.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is applied to the spectral dataset to identify the dominant modes of variation and reduce the high-dimensional spectral data to interpretable components.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar central engine
conceptDetails.description: The magnetar model—a rapidly rotating, highly magnetized neutron star powering the supernova—is the primary physical mechanism evaluated against the spectroscopic observations.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: K-means clustering is used in conjunction with PCA to search for statistically significant subpopulations within the SLSN spectral dataset.

conceptDetails.name: velocity gradient analysis
conceptDetails.description: Fe II λ5169 line velocities and their temporal gradients are measured across the sample, revealing a correlation consistent with homologous expansion with varying scale velocities.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the progenitor systems of SLSNe and their powering mechanisms (magnetar central engine, CSM interaction, pair-instability), which are directly tied to the life cycles of massive stars.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: PCA and clustering are used to identify outlying SLSNe with unusual spectroscopic evolution, including objects with evidence for CSM interaction.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: CSM interaction as an alternative or supplemental energy source for SLSNe is examined, with outlier objects showing spectroscopic evidence for this mechanism identified.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The paper tests for the existence of two or more statistically significant spectroscopic subpopulations within the SLSN sample, finding no support for distinct groupings.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Statistical methods including variance analysis and hypothesis testing are used to assess whether the SLSN population contains distinct subpopulations or is better described as a continuum.

title: Extracting the distribution amplitude of pseudoscalar mesons using the HOPE method
abstract: The pseudoscalar meson light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) are essential non-perturbative inputs for a range of high-energy exclusive processes in quantum chromodynamics. In this proceedings, progress towards a determination of the low Mellin moments of the pion and kaon LCDAs by the HOPE Collaboration is reported.

conceptDetails.name: hope method
conceptDetails.description: A novel approach using heavy-quark operator product expansion applied to a two-current hadronic matrix element to extract meson light-cone distribution amplitudes from lattice QCD.

conceptDetails.name: light-cone distribution amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics objects being computed — non-perturbative functions encoding the momentum fraction distributions of quarks in pseudoscalar mesons.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary computational framework used to calculate hadronic matrix elements and extract Mellin moments of meson LCDAs from first principles.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The heavy-quark operator product expansion (HOPE) serves as an effective field theory framework relating hadronic matrix elements to meson distribution amplitudes.

conceptDetails.name: mellin moments
conceptDetails.description: The low Mellin moments of pion and kaon LCDAs are the target quantities extracted via the HOPE formula and Gegenbauer moment relations.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Renormalization scale dependence of the LCDA and Gegenbauer moments is central to the matching between lattice and continuum QCD results.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical foundation for defining light-cone distribution amplitudes via matrix elements of quark bilinear operators with Wilson lines in QCD.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Lattice QCD calculations rely on Monte Carlo sampling of gauge field configurations to compute correlation functions numerically.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Fitting the HOPE formula to numerical hadronic matrix element data to extract Gegenbauer and Mellin moments of the meson LCDA.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The generalized eigenvalue problem (GEVP) is solved to construct optimized interpolating operators that maximize ground-state overlap in lattice correlation functions.

title: Generative Modeling for Mathematical Discovery
abstract: We present a new implementation of the LLM-driven genetic algorithm {\it funsearch}, whose aim is to generate examples of interest to mathematicians and which has already had some success in problems in extremal combinatorics. Our implementation is designed to be useful in practice for working mathematicians; it does not require expertise in machine learning or access to high-performance computing resources. Applying {\it funsearch} to a new problem involves modifying a small segment of Python code and selecting a large language model (LLM) from one of many third-party providers. We benchmarked our implementation on three different problems, obtaining metrics that may inform applications of {\it funsearch} to new problems. Our results demonstrate that {\it funsearch} successfully learns in a variety of combinatorial and number-theoretic settings, and in some contexts learns principles that generalize beyond the problem originally trained on.

conceptDetails.name: llm-driven genetic algorithm
conceptDetails.description: A novel hybrid method combining large language models as mutation/crossover operators within a genetic algorithm framework to evolve mathematical programs.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper implements funsearch, an LLM-driven system that automatically discovers mathematical examples and solutions to combinatorial and number-theoretic problems.

conceptDetails.name: program synthesis
conceptDetails.description: Funsearch evolves Python programs (priority functions) that solve mathematical problems, representing a form of automated program synthesis guided by LLMs.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: LLMs are used as generative models to produce new candidate Python programs (priority functions) from prompts containing previous high-scoring programs.

conceptDetails.name: combinatorial optimization
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain includes cap set problems and other extremal combinatorics challenges where the method searches for optimal combinatorial structures.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Funsearch is specifically designed for inverse problems where verifying a solution is easy but discovering one is hard, such as extremal combinatorics and number theory.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The implementation is explicitly designed to integrate into working mathematicians' workflows without requiring ML expertise or HPC resources.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: The system adaptively selects which candidate programs to include in prompts based on their scores, focusing computational resources on promising regions of program space.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Funsearch uses a score-based selection mechanism analogous to reinforcement learning, where candidate programs are evaluated and higher-scoring programs are preferentially selected for breeding.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes what principles funsearch learns and whether they generalize, examining the interpretable structure of discovered priority functions.

title: No-go theorem for environment-assisted invariance in non-unitary dynamics
abstract: We elucidate the requirements for quantum operations that achieve environment-assisted invariance (envariance), a symmetry of entanglement. While envariance has traditionally been studied within the framework of local unitary operations, we extend the analysis to consider non-unitary local operations. First, we investigate the conditions imposed on operators acting on pure bipartite entanglement to attain envariance. We show that the local operations must take a direct-sum form in their Kraus operator representations, establishing decoherence-free subspaces. Furthermore, we prove that this also holds for the multipartite scenario. As an immediate consequence, we demonstrate that environment-assisted shortcuts to adiabaticity cannot be achieved through non-unitary operations. In addition, we show that the static condition of the eternal black hole in AdS/CFT is violated when the CFTs are coupled to the external baths.

conceptDetails.name: envariance
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper: environment-assisted invariance is extended beyond unitary operations, and a no-go theorem is proven for general non-unitary CPTP maps.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes conditions on pure bipartite and multipartite quantum states required to achieve envariance under local quantum operations.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Envariance is defined as a symmetry of entanglement, and the paper characterizes which entangled states and operations can preserve this symmetry under non-unitary dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: decoherence-free subspaces
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves that any local operation achieving envariance must establish decoherence-free subspaces via a direct-sum structure in its Kraus operator representation.

conceptDetails.name: open quantum systems
conceptDetails.description: The entire framework extends envariance analysis from closed (unitary) to open quantum systems described by completely positive trace-preserving maps.

conceptDetails.name: shortcuts to adiabaticity
conceptDetails.description: As a corollary, the paper demonstrates that environment-assisted shortcuts to adiabaticity cannot be implemented through non-unitary operations.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves that non-unitary operations break envariance symmetry unless they take a very specific direct-sum Kraus form, establishing fundamental symmetry constraints.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies its no-go theorem to the AdS/CFT correspondence, showing the static condition of the eternal black hole is violated when CFTs are coupled to external baths.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The results have direct implications for quantum error correction and quantum control protocols in open quantum systems.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The AdS/CFT and spacetime emergence implications connect the work to broader quantum field theory and holography frameworks.

title: Denoising Hamiltonian Network for Physical Reasoning
abstract: Machine learning frameworks for physical problems must capture and enforce physical constraints that preserve the structure of dynamical systems. Many existing approaches achieve this by integrating physical operators into neural networks. While these methods offer theoretical guarantees, they face two key limitations: (i) they primarily model local relations between adjacent time steps, overlooking longer-range or higher-level physical interactions, and (ii) they focus on forward simulation while neglecting broader physical reasoning tasks. We propose the Denoising Hamiltonian Network (DHN), a novel framework that generalizes Hamiltonian mechanics operators into more flexible neural operators. DHN captures non-local temporal relationships and mitigates numerical integration errors through a denoising mechanism. DHN also supports multi-system modeling with a global conditioning mechanism. We demonstrate its effectiveness and flexibility across three diverse physical reasoning tasks with distinct inputs and outputs.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core framework generalizes Hamiltonian mechanics operators into flexible neural operators that enforce symplectic structure and energy conservation.

conceptDetails.name: neural operators
conceptDetails.description: DHN extends Hamiltonian neural operators into a sequence-based paradigm using block-wise transformations to capture non-local temporal relationships.

conceptDetails.name: denoising physical trajectories
conceptDetails.description: A novel contribution: the paper introduces a denoising objective applied to physical trajectory refinement to correct numerical integration errors and enable flexible task conditioning.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: DHN's denoising mechanism is inspired by denoising diffusion models, iteratively refining predictions toward physically valid trajectories to mitigate numerical integration errors.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: Global conservation laws (e.g., energy conservation) are leveraged to directly relate temporally distant states, forming a key motivation for DHN's non-local reasoning.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: DHN embeds physical constraints directly into its architecture, relating it to the broader class of physics-informed methods that enforce physical laws during learning.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: DHN treats groups of system states as tokens and applies transformer-style attention to capture non-local temporal dependencies across trajectories.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: DHN supports inferring physical parameters from partial observations, a key inverse problem task demonstrated across the paper's three reasoning scenarios.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: DHN preserves symplectic structure inherent to Hamiltonian mechanics, maintaining the geometric symmetries of dynamical systems throughout learning.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: DHN is designed to support multiple physical reasoning tasks—prediction, completion, parameter estimation, and super-resolution—under a unified framework.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: Progressive super-resolution of sparse trajectories is one of three core physical reasoning tasks DHN is evaluated on.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The global conditioning mechanism encodes system-specific properties into a shared latent code, maintaining disentangled representations of underlying dynamics across heterogeneous systems.

title: Decoding the Galactic Twirl: The Downfall of Milky Way-mass Galaxies Rotation Curves in the FIRE Simulations
abstract: Recent measurements of the Milky Way rotation curve found a sharp decline at around $15$-$20$ kpc from the center of the Galaxy, suggesting that the Galactic dark matter halo is much less massive than predicted by other dynamical tracers. To address this tension, we study the validity of the assumptions made in calculating the Milky Way's rotation curve. To do so, we apply Jeans' equation, the current standard approach of measuring rotation curves, to three cosmological zoom-in simulations of Milky Way-like galaxies from the FIRE-2 Latte suite. Using synthetic Gaia surveys, we replicate the sample selection process and calculation employed in measuring the Milky Way rotation curve. We examine four failure modes of this calculation and find that the measured curves deviate from the true curve by $5$-$20\%$ rather than below $5\%$, as estimated by previous works. Interestingly, there is a large galaxy-to-galaxy variance, and different systematics dominate different galaxies. We rederive the Milky Way's dark matter density profile with the rotation curve while incorporating systematics from the simulations. The posterior distribution of the density profiles is consistent with a fiducial NFW profile when assuming a gNFW profile for dark matter. We find that the virial mass, $7.32^{+1.98}_{-1.53}\times10^{11}~M_{\odot}$, consistent with other probes of the Milky Way's mass. However, we recommend that the field moves away from relying solely on the rotation curve when studying the dark matter profile, and adopts methods that incorporate additional probes and/or do not heavily depend on assumptions described in this study.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on inferring the Milky Way's dark matter halo mass and density profile from rotation curve measurements and simulations.

conceptDetails.name: jeans equation
conceptDetails.description: The collisionless Boltzmann-Jeans equation is the standard rotation-curve method whose assumptions and failure modes are the central subject of scrutiny in this work.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: FIRE-2 Latte suite cosmological zoom-in simulations of three Milky Way-mass galaxies are the primary tool for testing Jeans equation systematics.

conceptDetails.name: rotation curve systematics
conceptDetails.description: Four categories of systematic bias—selection function, asymmetric drift correction errors, axis asymmetry, and dynamical disequilibrium—are identified and quantified.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically quantifies four failure modes and their contributions to systematic uncertainties (5–20%) in rotation curve measurements.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The Jeans equation procedure is validated end-to-end against ground-truth simulation data to assess the reliability of its standard assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Used to rederive the Milky Way dark matter density profile by incorporating simulation-derived systematics into the posterior estimation.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Posterior distributions of gNFW dark matter density profiles and virial mass are derived, found consistent with a fiducial NFW profile.

conceptDetails.name: nfw profile fitting
conceptDetails.description: Generalized and fiducial NFW dark matter density profiles are fit to the rotation curve data to constrain the Milky Way virial mass and halo structure.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring the Milky Way's dark matter density profile and virial mass from observed stellar kinematics and the derived rotation curve is an ill-posed inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling is used in the Bayesian posterior analysis to propagate simulation-derived systematics into dark matter profile constraints.

title: Diffusion Models for Cayley Graphs
abstract: We review the problem of finding paths in Cayley graphs of groups and group actions, using the Rubik's cube as an example, and we list several more examples of significant mathematical interest. We then show how to formulate these problems in the framework of diffusion models. The exploration of the graph is carried out by the forward process, while finding the target nodes is done by the inverse backward process. This systematizes the discussion and suggests many generalizations. To improve exploration, we propose a ``reversed score'' ansatz which substantially improves over previous comparable algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The paper reformulates Cayley graph navigation problems using the forward/backward diffusion model framework, where forward diffusion explores the graph and backward diffusion finds target nodes.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Cayley graphs of groups and group actions are the core mathematical objects studied, with the Rubik's cube group as the primary example.

conceptDetails.name: cayley graph navigation
conceptDetails.description: Novel formalization of the path-finding problem on Cayley graphs of groups and group actions as a unified framework for combinatorial search.

conceptDetails.name: reversed score ansatz
conceptDetails.description: A novel proposal that reverses the score function direction to substantially improve exploration efficiency over prior comparable algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a 'reversed score' ansatz derived from score-based generative modeling principles to improve graph exploration and path-finding.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Random walks on Cayley graphs form both the data generation process (forward diffusion) and the exploration strategy for the solver.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Prior solvers (DeepCube, DeepCubeA) and the paper's approach build on RL frameworks for learning policies to navigate group state spaces.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper leverages the geometric structure of Cayley graphs and group symmetries to formulate learnable navigation algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Group symmetry of Cayley graphs is exploited to constrain the policy and score functions, improving generalization.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Finding the reverse path to the identity in a Cayley graph is framed as an inverse problem solved by the backward diffusion process.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: Quantum circuit design is listed as a concrete application domain, where gate sequences correspond to paths in a Cayley graph of a unitary group.

title: L$^2$M: Mutual Information Scaling Law for Long-Context Language Modeling
abstract: We present a universal theoretical framework for understanding long-context language modeling based on a bipartite mutual information scaling law that we rigorously verify in natural language. We demonstrate that bipartite mutual information captures multi-token interactions distinct from and scaling independently of conventional two-point mutual information, and show that this provides a more complete characterization of the dependencies needed for accurately modeling long sequences. Leveraging this scaling law, we formulate the Long-context Language Modeling (L$^2$M) condition, which lower bounds the necessary scaling of a model's history state -- the latent variables responsible for storing past information -- for effective long-context modeling. We validate the framework and its predictions on transformer and state-space models. Our work provides a principled foundation to understand long-context modeling and to design more efficient architectures with stronger long-context capabilities, with potential applications beyond natural language.

conceptDetails.name: bipartite mutual information scaling
conceptDetails.description: A novel empirically verified power-law scaling relationship for multi-token mutual information across text segments that forms the theoretical foundation of the L2M framework.

conceptDetails.name: long-context language modeling
conceptDetails.description: The central problem addressed: modeling statistical dependencies across long token sequences, formalized via the L2M condition derived from information-theoretic scaling laws.

conceptDetails.name: history state capacity
conceptDetails.description: The latent variables storing past information in autoregressive models, whose dimensionality must grow at a minimum rate determined by the bipartite mutual information scaling law.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Transformer architectures are validated against the L2M framework, with their key-value cache serving as the history state whose dimensionality must scale with context length.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The L2M condition provides principled guidance for scaling model history state dimensionality to match the power-law growth of bipartite mutual information in long sequences.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: State-space models and recurrent neural networks are analyzed as alternative architectures whose fixed-size recurrent states impose capacity constraints on long-context modeling.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Attention mechanisms are discussed as the source of quadratic computational cost in transformers that motivates the search for more efficient long-context architectures.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Autoregressive language models are analyzed as stochastic processes where the statistical dependencies between tokens govern the necessary history state size.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The framework provides theoretical interpretability into why certain architectures succeed or fail at long-context modeling by connecting architectural choices to information-theoretic requirements.

title: Trial by FIRE: Probing the dark matter density profile of dwarf galaxies with GraphNPE
abstract: The Dark Matter (DM) distribution in dwarf galaxies provides crucial insights into both structure formation and the particle nature of DM. GraphNPE (Graph Neural Posterior Estimator), first introduced in Nguyen et al. (2023), is a novel simulation-based inference framework that combines graph neural networks and normalizing flows to infer the DM density profile from line-of-sight stellar velocities. Here, we apply GraphNPE to satellite dwarf galaxies in the FIRE-2 Latte simulation suite of Milky Way-mass halos, testing it against both Cold and Self-Interacting DM scenarios. Our method demonstrates superior precision compared to conventional Jeans-based approaches, recovering DM density profiles to within the 95% confidence level even in systems with as few as 30 tracers. Moreover, we present the first evaluation of mass modeling methods in constraining two key parameters from realistic simulations: the peak circular velocity, $V_\mathrm{max}$, and the peak virial mass, $M_\mathrm{200m}^\mathrm{peak}$. Using only line-of-sight velocities, GraphNPE can reliably recover both $V_\mathrm{max}$ and $M_\mathrm{200m}^\mathrm{peak}$ within our quoted uncertainties, including those experiencing tidal effects ($\gtrsim$ 63% of systems are recovered with our 68% confidence intervals and $\gtrsim$ 92% within our 95% confidence intervals). The method achieves 10-20% accuracy in $V_\mathrm{max}$ recovery, while $M_\mathrm{200m}^\mathrm{peak}$ is recovered to 0.1-0.4 dex accuracy. This work establishes GraphNPE as a robust tool for inferring DM density profiles in dwarf galaxies, offering promising avenues for constraining DM models. The framework's potential extends beyond this study, as it can be adapted to non-spherical and disequilibrium models, showcasing the broader utility of simulation-based inference and graph-based learning in astrophysics.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: GraphNPE is a simulation-based inference framework that learns the posterior of dark matter density profile parameters from simulated stellar kinematics data.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The central scientific target is the dark matter density profile of dwarf galaxies, tested against Cold DM and Self-Interacting DM scenarios.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Graph neural networks are the core architecture of GraphNPE, encoding stellar position-velocity data as graphs to capture spatial relationships among tracer stars.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are used within GraphNPE to model the posterior distribution over dark matter density profile parameters given observed stellar velocities.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on inferring the full posterior of dark matter halo parameters (density profile, Vmax, M200m) from line-of-sight velocity observations.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The FIRE-2 Latte suite of hydrodynamic cosmological simulations provides the training and test data for GraphNPE.

conceptDetails.name: stellar kinematics inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel application of machine learning to infer dark matter halo properties directly from line-of-sight stellar velocity observations in dwarf galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The method provides Bayesian posterior constraints on dark matter halo parameters, enabling uncertainty-quantified inference from sparse stellar kinematic data.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper rigorously evaluates coverage of 68% and 95% confidence intervals for recovered dark matter parameters across simulated dwarf galaxy populations.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the dark matter density profile from projected stellar velocities is an ill-posed inverse problem that GraphNPE addresses with learned posteriors.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: GraphNPE employs geometric deep learning through graph neural networks to handle the permutation-invariant, unordered set of stellar tracers.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows within GraphNPE perform conditional density estimation to approximate the posterior over dark matter profile parameters.

title: A Deep, High-Angular Resolution 3D Dust Map of the Southern Galactic Plane
abstract: We present a deep, high-angular resolution 3D dust map of the southern Galactic plane over $239^\circ < \ell < 6^\circ$ and $|b| < 10^\circ$ built on photometry from the DECaPS2 survey, in combination with photometry from VVV, 2MASS, and unWISE and parallaxes from Gaia DR3 where available. To construct the map, we first infer the distance, extinction, and stellar types of over 700 million stars using the brutus stellar inference framework with a set of theoretical MIST stellar models. Our resultant 3D dust map has an angular resolution of $1'$, roughly an order of magnitude finer than existing 3D dust maps and comparable to the angular resolution of the Herschel 2D dust emission maps. We detect complexes at the range of distances associated with the Sagittarius-Carina and Scutum-Centaurus arms in the fourth quadrant, as well as more distant structures out to a maximum reliable distance of $d \approx$ 10 kpc from the Sun. The map is sensitive up to a maximum extinction of roughly $A_V \approx 12$ mag. We publicly release both the stellar catalog and the 3D dust map, the latter of which can easily be queried via the Python package dustmaps. When combined with the existing Bayestar19 3D dust map of the northern sky, the DECaPS 3D dust map fills in the missing piece of the Galactic plane, enabling extinction corrections over the entire disk $|b| < 10^\circ$. Our map serves as a pathfinder for the future of 3D dust mapping in the era of LSST and Roman, targeting regimes accessible with deep optical and near-infrared photometry but often inaccessible with Gaia.

conceptDetails.name: 3d dust mapping
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution of the paper — constructing a three-dimensional map of interstellar dust distribution by inferring distance and extinction for hundreds of millions of stars simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The brutus stellar inference framework uses Bayesian methods to jointly infer distance, extinction, and stellar type for over 700 million stars from multi-band photometry and parallax data.

conceptDetails.name: interstellar extinction
conceptDetails.description: The primary physical quantity being mapped — the wavelength-dependent absorption and scattering of starlight by dust grains, quantified as AV along lines of sight at varying distances.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: For each star, the pipeline estimates a posterior distribution over stellar parameters (distance, extinction, stellar type), which are then aggregated to reconstruct the 3D dust density field.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: MIST (MESA Isochrones and Stellar Tracks) theoretical stellar models provide the prior on intrinsic stellar colors and luminosities used in the Bayesian inference of stellar types and distances.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the 3D dust density distribution from projected stellar photometry observations is an ill-posed inverse problem requiring regularization and probabilistic modeling.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The 3D dust map is constructed by aggregating stellar extinction posteriors into a continuous volumetric density estimate of interstellar dust across the Galactic plane.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes reliability limits in distance and extinction across the map, including maximum reliable distance (~10 kpc) and maximum extinction sensitivity (~AV=12 mag).

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The map achieves 1 arcminute angular resolution, roughly an order of magnitude finer than existing 3D dust maps, enabled by the depth and density of the DECaPS2 photometric survey.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline processes over 700 million stellar sources, requiring efficient implementations of Bayesian stellar inference at unprecedented scale.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling is used within the brutus framework to draw samples from stellar parameter posteriors, enabling propagation of uncertainties into the dust map.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: Interstellar dust is a critical polarized foreground contaminant for CMB observations, providing scientific motivation for accurate 3D dust mapping across the Galactic plane.

title: Evidence for an Instability-Induced Binary Merger in the Double-Peaked, Helium-Rich Type IIn Supernova 2023zkd
abstract: We present ultraviolet to infrared observations of the extraordinary Type IIn supernova 2023zkd (SN 2023zkd). Photometrically, it exhibits persistent and luminous precursor emission spanning $\sim$4 years preceding discovery ($M_r\approx-15$ mag, 1,500~days in the observer frame), followed by a secondary stage of gradual brightening in its final year. Post-discovery, it exhibits two photometric peaks of comparable brightness ($M_r\lesssim-18.7$ mag and $M_r\approx-18.4$ mag, respectively) separated by 240 days. Spectroscopically, SN 2023zkd exhibits highly asymmetric and multi-component Balmer and He I profiles that we attribute to ejecta interaction with fast-moving ($1,\!000-2,\!000\;\mathrm{km}\;\mathrm{s}^{-1}$) He-rich polar material and slow-moving ($\sim$$400\;\mathrm{km}\;\mathrm{s}^{-1}$) equatorially-distributed H-rich material. He II features also appear during the second light curve peak and evolve rapidly. Shock-driven models fit to the multi-band photometry suggest that the event is powered by interaction with $\sim$$5-6\;M_{\odot}$ of CSM, with $2-3\;M_{\odot}$ associated with each light curve peak, expelled during mass-loss episodes $\sim$$3-4$ and $\sim$$1-2$ years prior to explosion. The observed precursor emission, combined with the extreme mass-loss rates required to power each light curve peak, favors either super-Eddington accretion onto a black hole or multiple long-lived eruptions from a massive star to luminosities that have not been previously observed. We consider multiple progenitor scenarios for SN 2023zkd, and find that the brightening optical precursor and inferred explosion properties are most consistent with a massive ($M_{\mathrm{ZAMS}}\geq30\;M_{\odot}$) and partially-stripped He star undergoing an instability-induced merger with a black hole companion.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar material interaction
conceptDetails.description: Interaction between supernova ejecta and ~5–6 M☉ of asymmetric CSM is the dominant power source for both photometric peaks and the defining physical mechanism of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Progenitor analysis centers on the evolution of a massive (MZAMS ≥ 30 M☉) partially-stripped He star in a binary system and the instability-driven merger with a black hole companion.

conceptDetails.name: binary merger progenitor
conceptDetails.description: An instability-induced merger between a massive partially-stripped He star and a black hole companion is proposed as the most consistent explanation for the observed brightening precursor and explosion properties.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper classifies SN 2023zkd as a Type IIn supernova with anomalous double-peaked light curves and He-rich spectral features, placing it within the observational taxonomy of core-collapse supernovae.

conceptDetails.name: precursor emission
conceptDetails.description: Persistent luminous optical emission spanning ~4 years before the main explosion is used as a key diagnostic to distinguish between super-Eddington accretion, stellar eruptions, and binary merger scenarios.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper infers progenitor mass, mass-loss history, and explosion timing by inverting multi-band photometric and spectroscopic observations through shock-driven models.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detection and characterization of multi-year precursor emission (~4 years, Mr ≈ −15 mag) preceding discovery is central to constraining the progenitor's mass-loss history.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Shock-driven analytical models are fit via regression to multi-band light curves to extract physical parameters such as CSM mass and ejecta velocity.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian parameter estimation is used to constrain CSM mass (2–3 M☉ per peak) and mass-loss episode timing from multi-band photometry fits.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: SN 2023zkd is identified as an extraordinary outlier among Type IIn events due to its extreme mass-loss rates, double peaks, and unprecedented precursor luminosity, motivating novel progenitor scenarios.

title: Seeing the Outer Edge of the Infant Type Ia Supernova 2024epr in the Optical and Near Infrared
abstract: We present optical-to-near-infrared (NIR) photometry and spectroscopy of the Type Ia supernova (SN Ia) 2024epr, including NIR spectra observed within two days of first light. The early-time optical spectra show strong, high-velocity Ca and Si features near rarely-observed velocities at $\sim$0.1$c$, and the NIR spectra show a C I "knee." Despite early-time, high-velocity features, SN 2024epr evolves into a normal SN Ia, albeit with stronger peak-light Ca absorption than other SNe Ia with the same light curve shape. Although we infer a normal decline rate, $Δm_{15}(B)=1.09\pm0.12$ mag, from the light-curve rise, SN 2024epr is a Branch "cool" object and has red early-time colors ($g-r\approx0.15$ mag at $-10$ days). The high velocities point to a density enhancement in the outer layers of the explosion, predicted by some models, but thick-shell He-detonation models do not match the smoothly rising light curve or apparent lack of He in our early-time NIR spectra. No current models (e.g., delayed detonation or thin He shell double detonation) appear to reproduce all observed properties, particularly the unusual early-time colors. Such constraints are only possible for SN 2024epr from the earliest optical and NIR observations, highlighting their importance for constraining SN Ia models. Finally, we identify several literature SNe Ia with intermediate mass elements at $\sim$30\,000 km s$^{-1}$ within days after the explosion that evolve into otherwise normal SNe Ia at peak light, suggesting the early-time spectra of SNe Ia may hide a broad diversity of observational characteristics.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper classifies SN 2024epr as a Branch 'cool', normal-decline SN Ia despite anomalous early-time high-velocity features, probing the diversity within Type Ia classifications.

conceptDetails.name: high-velocity ejecta
conceptDetails.description: SN 2024epr exhibits Ca and Si features at ~0.1c in early spectra, pointing to a density enhancement in the outermost ejecta layers that existing models cannot fully reproduce.

conceptDetails.name: early-phase spectroscopy
conceptDetails.description: NIR and optical spectra obtained within two days of first light provide uniquely tight constraints on outer ejecta composition and velocity structure unavailable at peak light.

conceptDetails.name: thermonuclear detonation
conceptDetails.description: Competing explosion mechanisms (delayed detonation, helium-shell double detonation) are the central physical framework evaluated to explain the observed properties.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Early-time optical and NIR observations are used to systematically test and rule out explosion models (delayed detonation, thin/thick He-shell double detonation) against observed light curves and spectra.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: White dwarf progenitor scenarios (single-degenerate, double-degenerate, core-degenerate) and their connection to the Chandrasekhar mass are evaluated against the observations.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Spectral features such as the C I 'knee' and high-velocity Ca/Si absorption are identified and measured in early-phase NIR and optical spectra.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Physical properties of the explosion (outer density structure, ejecta composition, progenitor mass) are inferred from photometric and spectroscopic observables.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Light-curve fitting is used to infer the decline rate parameter Δm15(B) and constrain the explosion epoch from the photometric rise.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Posterior estimation is applied to derive decline rates and first-light epochs with uncertainties from multi-band light-curve data.

title: Anomaly preserving contrastive neural embeddings for end-to-end model-independent searches at the LHC
abstract: Anomaly detection - identifying deviations from Standard Model predictions - is a key challenge at the Large Hadron Collider due to the size and complexity of its datasets. This is typically addressed by transforming high-dimensional detector data into lower-dimensional, physically meaningful features. We tackle feature extraction for anomaly detection by learning powerful low-dimensional representations via contrastive neural embeddings. This approach preserves potential anomalies indicative of new physics and enables rare signal extraction using novel machine learning-based statistical methods for signal-independent hypothesis testing. We compare supervised and self-supervised contrastive learning methods, for both MLP- and Transformer-based neural embeddings, trained on the kinematic observables of physics objects in LHC collision events. The learned embeddings serve as input representations for signal-agnostic statistical detection methods in inclusive final states. We achieve significant improvement in discovery power for both rare new physics signals and rare Standard Model processes across diverse final states, demonstrating its applicability for efficiently searching for diverse signals simultaneously. We study the impact of architectural choices, contrastive loss formulations, supervision levels, and embedding dimensionality on anomaly detection performance. We show that the optimal representation for background classification does not always maximize sensitivity to new physics signals, revealing an inherent trade-off between background structure preservation and anomaly enhancement. We demonstrate that combining compression with domain knowledge for label encoding produces the most effective data representation for statistical discovery of anomalies.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The core methodology used to learn low-dimensional embeddings that group normal LHC collision events while pushing anomalous events apart in latent space.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The primary task: identifying deviations from Standard Model predictions in LHC collision data without prior knowledge of the signal model.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how to learn optimal low-dimensional representations of high-dimensional detector data for downstream anomaly detection tasks.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The learned low-dimensional vector representations of LHC collision events that serve as input to statistical detection methods.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The physics motivation: searching for signals beyond the Standard Model in an inclusive, model-independent manner at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental domain: proton-proton collision events at the Large Hadron Collider providing the raw data for analysis.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: One of the two contrastive training paradigms evaluated, enabling embedding training without labeled anomaly examples.

conceptDetails.name: signal-agnostic search
conceptDetails.description: A novel search strategy that achieves sensitivity to diverse new physics signals simultaneously without assuming a specific signal model.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Compressing high-dimensional detector observables into compact representations while preserving anomaly-relevant information.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Signal-independent statistical methods applied to the learned embeddings for model-independent discovery of rare signals.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The overall learning paradigm for anomaly detection where supervision is partial or label-agnostic with respect to new physics signals.

title: Isolating Unisolated Upsilons with Anomaly Detection in CMS Open Data
abstract: We present the first study of anti-isolated Upsilon decays to two muons ($Υ\to μ^+ μ^-$) in proton-proton collisions at the Large Hadron Collider. Using a machine learning (ML)-based anomaly detection strategy, we "rediscover" the $Υ$ in 13 TeV CMS Open Data from 2016, despite overwhelming anti-isolated backgrounds. We elevate the signal significance to $6.4 σ$ using these methods, starting from $1.6 σ$ using the dimuon mass spectrum alone. Moreover, we demonstrate improved sensitivity from using an ML-based estimate of the multi-feature likelihood compared to traditional "cut-and-count" methods. Our work demonstrates that it is possible and practical to find real signals in experimental collider data using ML-based anomaly detection, and we distill a readily-accessible benchmark dataset from the CMS Open Data to facilitate future anomaly detection developments.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The core ML strategy used to identify anti-isolated Upsilon signals in CMS data without requiring signal simulation, elevating significance from 1.6σ to 6.4σ.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes 13 TeV proton-proton collision data from the LHC CMS detector to study Upsilon meson production.

conceptDetails.name: anti-isolated quarkonia
conceptDetails.description: Novel study of Upsilon mesons whose decay products are embedded within QCD jet activity, providing a new probe of bottomonium fragmentation.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: CATHODE uses density estimation to model the background distribution in sideband regions and construct a likelihood for the signal region.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The multi-feature likelihood ratio is used to reweight data and improve signal sensitivity beyond simple cut-and-count methods.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are employed within CATHODE to learn the conditional density of auxiliary features in the sideband regions.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Statistical hypothesis testing is used to evaluate signal significance and compare the observed data against the background-only null hypothesis.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Anti-isolated Upsilons are produced via QCD fragmentation within jets, making jet physics central to the physical motivation of the search.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates practical signal detection of a known resonance in a previously unstudied phase-space region as a benchmark for anomaly detection methods.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A classifier is trained to distinguish signal-region data from background-interpolated data as part of the CATHODE anomaly scoring procedure.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The methodology is motivated by and directly applicable to model-agnostic new physics searches at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The approach avoids reliance on signal simulation, instead inferring the background model directly from sideband data.

title: On the Learnability of Knot Invariants: Representation, Predictability, and Neural Similarity
abstract: We analyze different aspects of neural network predictions of knot invariants. First, we investigate the impact of different knot representations on the prediction of invariants and find that braid representations work in general the best. Second, we study which knot invariants are easy to learn, with invariants derived from hyperbolic geometry and knot diagrams being very easy to learn, while invariants derived from topological or homological data are harder. Predicting the Arf invariant could not be learned for any representation. Third, we propose a cosine similarity score based on gradient saliency vectors, and a joint misclassification score to uncover similarities in neural networks trained to predict related topological invariants.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a systematic comparison of five knot representations (braid words, DT codes, PD codes, Gauss codes, 3D coordinates) to determine which input format best enables neural networks to predict knot invariants.

conceptDetails.name: knot invariant learnability
conceptDetails.description: A novel systematic characterization of which topological and geometric invariants (hyperbolic geometry-derived vs. homological vs. polynomial-derived) are easy or hard for neural networks to predict, revealing a learnability hierarchy.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks are trained as classifiers to predict discrete scalar knot invariants, with accuracy as the primary metric for evaluating learnability across representations.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Gradient saliency vectors and cosine similarity scores are proposed as new tools to interpret neural network behavior and uncover structural similarities between networks trained on related topological invariants.

conceptDetails.name: gradient saliency similarity
conceptDetails.description: A novel cosine similarity metric computed from gradient saliency vectors that measures structural similarity between neural networks trained on related invariants, revealing hidden relationships among topological quantities.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Braid group theory underlies the best-performing knot representation, with braid words providing an algebraic and compact encoding of knots that proves most amenable to neural network learning.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes which features within each knot representation carry the most predictive information for different invariants, using gradient saliency to identify salient input dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Different knot representations are studied as numerical embeddings serving as neural network inputs, with their structural properties directly influencing prediction performance.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: Networks trained to predict different but related knot invariants are compared via joint misclassification scores to reveal which invariants share similar computational structure.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning for Kronecker Coefficients
abstract: We analyze the saliency of neural networks and employ interpretable machine learning models to predict whether the Kronecker coefficients of the symmetric group are zero or not. Our models use triples of partitions as input features, as well as b-loadings derived from the principal component of an embedding that captures the differences between partitions. Across all approaches, we achieve an accuracy of approximately 83% and derive explicit formulas for a decision function in terms of b-loadings. Additionally, we develop transformer-based models for prediction, achieving the highest reported accuracy of over 99%.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The central theme of the paper: using saliency analysis, KANs, small networks, and symbolic regression to explain why ML models achieve high accuracy on Kronecker coefficient prediction.

conceptDetails.name: kronecker coefficients
conceptDetails.description: The central mathematical objects: decomposition multiplicities of tensor products of symmetric group representations, whose vanishing is NP-hard to determine in general.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The core ML task is binary classification — predicting whether a Kronecker coefficient is zero or nonzero given a triple of partitions.

conceptDetails.name: kolmogorov-arnold networks
conceptDetails.description: KANs are used both as interpretable classifiers and as symbolic regressors to extract explicit decision formulas from the learned functions.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Transformer-based models achieve the highest reported accuracy (>99%) on Kronecker coefficient prediction, surpassing all interpretable baselines.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies Kronecker coefficients, which are decomposition multiplicities of tensor products of irreducible representations of the symmetric group S_n.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: b-loadings derived from the principal eigenvector of the difference matrix are used as compact, informative input features for all interpretable models.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: PySR and KANs are used as symbolic regressors to extract closed-form algebraic formulas for the decision boundary in terms of b-loadings.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: PCA applied to a partition-difference embedding produces the b-loading scalar, reducing high-dimensional partition triples to a single predictive feature.

conceptDetails.name: saliency analysis
conceptDetails.description: Gradient-based saliency analysis on a trained classifier reveals that the first and last entries of partition vectors contribute most to predictions.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The b-loading construction relies on computing the principal eigenvector of the symmetric difference matrix Z_n via the Perron-Frobenius theorem.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Partitions of n are embedded as n-dimensional vectors and a pairwise L1-distance matrix encodes their structural relationships.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Kernel-based approaches from prior work on b-loadings provide context and theoretical grounding for the sufficient conditions derived in this paper.

title: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap for Athletic Loco-Manipulation
abstract: Achieving athletic loco-manipulation on robots requires moving beyond traditional tracking rewards - which simply guide the robot along a reference trajectory - to task rewards that drive truly dynamic, goal-oriented behaviors. Commands such as "throw the ball as far as you can" or "lift the weight as quickly as possible" compel the robot to exhibit the agility and power inherent in athletic performance. However, training solely with task rewards introduces two major challenges: these rewards are prone to exploitation (reward hacking), and the exploration process can lack sufficient direction. To address these issues, we propose a two-stage training pipeline. First, we introduce the Unsupervised Actuator Net (UAN), which leverages real-world data to bridge the sim-to-real gap for complex actuation mechanisms without requiring access to torque sensing. UAN mitigates reward hacking by ensuring that the learned behaviors remain robust and transferable. Second, we use a pre-training and fine-tuning strategy that leverages reference trajectories as initial hints to guide exploration. With these innovations, our robot athlete learns to lift, throw, and drag with remarkable fidelity from simulation to reality.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: PPO-based reinforcement learning is the core training paradigm used to learn whole-body athletic behaviors from task rewards in simulation.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: The overarching challenge of deploying simulation-trained policies on real robots, addressed through learned actuator correction and domain randomization.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: A pre-training and fine-tuning strategy is a core contribution, where a whole-body controller is pre-trained on general motion skills then fine-tuned with task-specific rewards using reference trajectories as hints.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Sim-to-real transfer is the central challenge, with UAN and domain randomization enabling policies trained in simulation to transfer reliably to physical hardware.

conceptDetails.name: actuator dynamics modeling
conceptDetails.description: The UAN learns corrective residual models for non-ideal harmonic drive actuators exhibiting nonlinear friction, hysteresis, and lag that defeat standard domain randomization.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper contrasts task rewards (maximize throw distance) against dense tracking rewards, analyzing how task reward specification drives athletic behavior but introduces exploitation risks.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The Unsupervised Actuator Net acts as a learned surrogate for complex actuator dynamics, predicting corrective torques to close the sim-to-real gap without torque sensors.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: UAN mitigates reward hacking by ensuring learned behaviors remain robust and transferable despite imperfect simulation models.

conceptDetails.name: whole-body loco-manipulation
conceptDetails.description: The target task domain combining legged locomotion with arm manipulation for dynamic athletic tasks such as throwing, lifting, and dragging.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The tradeoff between dense tracking rewards and sparse task rewards is analyzed as a fundamental design choice affecting exploration quality and reward hacking susceptibility.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: UAN is trained unsupervised by minimizing discrepancies between simulated and real-world joint encoder measurements, requiring no labeled torque data.

title: LEAPS: A discrete neural sampler via locally equivariant networks
abstract: We propose "LEAPS", an algorithm to sample from discrete distributions known up to normalization by learning a rate matrix of a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC). LEAPS can be seen as a continuous-time formulation of annealed importance sampling and sequential Monte Carlo methods, extended so that the variance of the importance weights is offset by the inclusion of the CTMC. To derive these importance weights, we introduce a set of Radon-Nikodym derivatives of CTMCs over their path measures. Because the computation of these weights is intractable with standard neural network parameterizations of rate matrices, we devise a new compact representation for rate matrices via what we call "locally equivariant" functions. To parameterize them, we introduce a family of locally equivariant multilayer perceptrons, attention layers, and convolutional networks, and provide an approach to make deep networks that preserve the local equivariance. This property allows us to propose a scalable training algorithm for the rate matrix such that the variance of the importance weights associated to the CTMC are minimal. We demonstrate the efficacy of LEAPS on problems in statistical physics.

conceptDetails.name: locally equivariant networks
conceptDetails.description: A novel neural network architectural property introduced in LEAPS that enables compact, scalable parameterization of rate matrices for CTMCs by constraining how network outputs vary with inputs.

conceptDetails.name: discrete measure transport
conceptDetails.description: The core algorithmic framework of LEAPS, which learns to transport probability mass between discrete distributions over time using CTMCs as the dynamical vehicle.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Continuous-time Markov chains serve as the foundational mathematical object in LEAPS, governing the evolution of discrete distributions via learned rate matrices.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: LEAPS extends and unifies annealed importance sampling and sequential Monte Carlo methods using CTMC dynamics to reduce importance weight variance.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: LEAPS introduces locally equivariant MLPs, attention layers, and convolutional networks as a generalization of equivariant architectures suited to rate matrix parameterization.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: LEAPS trains the rate matrix by optimizing a PINN-style loss derived from the Kolmogorov forward equation, linking the learned dynamics to the target distribution.

conceptDetails.name: radon-nikodym path measures
conceptDetails.description: A key theoretical contribution deriving Radon-Nikodym derivatives for reverse-time CTMCs over path measures, enabling tractable computation of importance weights.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: LEAPS is conceptually related to continuous-time flow matching but extends the framework to discrete state spaces via CTMC measure transport.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: LEAPS draws on discrete diffusion model literature for its CTMC-based generative process and connects to score-based discrete generative models.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces locally equivariant attention layers as part of the new architecture family for parameterizing rate matrices in LEAPS.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The Ising model, a canonical statistical physics system exhibiting phase transitions, is used to experimentally validate LEAPS in high dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Sampling from distributions known only up to normalization—the core problem LEAPS addresses—is the fundamental challenge in Bayesian posterior estimation.

title: Do We Need to Verify Step by Step? Rethinking Process Supervision from a Theoretical Perspective
abstract:
As large language models have evolved, it has become crucial to distinguish between process supervision and outcome supervision -- two key reinforcement learning approaches to complex reasoning tasks. While process supervision offers intuitive advantages for long-term credit assignment, the precise relationship between these paradigms has remained an open question. Conventional wisdom suggests that outcome supervision is fundamentally more challenging due to the trajectory-level coverage problem, leading to significant investment in collecting fine-grained process supervision data.
  In this paper, we take steps towards resolving this debate. Our main theorem shows that, under standard data coverage assumptions, reinforcement learning through outcome supervision is no more statistically difficult than through process supervision, up to polynomial factors in horizon. At the core of this result lies the novel Change of Trajectory Measure Lemma -- a technical tool that bridges return-based trajectory measure and step-level distribution shift. Furthermore, for settings with access to a verifier or a rollout capability, we prove that any policy's advantage function can serve as an optimal process reward model, providing a direct connection between outcome and process supervision. These findings suggest that the empirically observed performance gap -- if any -- between outcome and process supervision likely stems from algorithmic limitations rather than inherent statistical difficulties, potentially transforming how we approach data collection and algorithm design for reinforcement learning.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The core framework studied, comparing outcome vs process supervision paradigms for training LLMs on complex reasoning tasks.

conceptDetails.name: outcome vs process supervision
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the central theoretical comparison showing outcome supervision is statistically equivalent to process supervision up to polynomial horizon factors.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Central to the paper's analysis of how reward signals (outcome vs process) shape statistical learning efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: process reward modeling
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paper analyzes when and why step-level reward models (PRMs) are or are not necessary for optimal RL performance.

conceptDetails.name: credit assignment
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the long-horizon credit assignment problem that motivates process supervision and is addressed by the Change of Trajectory Measure Lemma.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Markov Decision Processes and trajectory-level measure theory underpin the theoretical analysis.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: The practical context in which these supervision paradigms are applied to large language models.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses scalability of data collection by showing outcome supervision can match process supervision without exponentially more data.

conceptDetails.name: test-time scaling
conceptDetails.description: Related paradigm discussed in the context of verifier access and rollout capabilities for deriving process rewards from outcome data.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Process supervision's interpretability advantage is a key motivation discussed; the paper questions whether this advantage justifies its cost.

title: Machine learning the vanishing order of rational L-functions
abstract: In this paper, we study the vanishing order of rational $L$-functions from a data scientific perspective. Each $L$-function is represented in our data by finitely many Dirichlet coefficients, the normalisation of which depends on the context. We observe murmuration-like patterns in averages across our dataset, find that PCA clusters rational $L$-functions by their vanishing order, and record that LDA and neural networks may accurately predict this quantity.

conceptDetails.name: l-function vanishing order
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study: the order of vanishing of rational L-functions at their central point, conjecturally related to arithmetic rank via BSD conjecture.

conceptDetails.name: classification
