conceptDetails.description: Neural networks and LDA are used to classify L-functions by their vanishing order at the central point.

conceptDetails.name: arithmetic l-functions
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies rational L-functions arising from elliptic curves, modular forms, Dirichlet characters, and genus 2 curves as its primary mathematical objects.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: PCA is applied to Dirichlet coefficients to cluster rational L-functions by their vanishing order.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: PCA reveals that rational L-functions cluster by vanishing order in the principal component space.

conceptDetails.name: murmuration patterns
conceptDetails.description: Murmuration-like oscillatory patterns observed in averages of Dirichlet coefficients across datasets of rational L-functions.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional neural networks are applied to datasets of rational L-functions to predict vanishing orders.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Dirichlet coefficients serve as the primary features extracted from L-functions for machine learning analysis.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper explores transfer learning from one sub-dataset of L-functions to another to improve prediction.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) is used as a spectral/linear method to separate L-functions by vanishing order class.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The study aims to understand what arithmetic structure machine learning models are detecting when predicting vanishing order.

title: Mathematical Data Science
abstract: Can machine learning help discover new mathematical structures? In this article we discuss an approach to doing this which one can call "mathematical data science". In this paradigm, one studies mathematical objects collectively rather than individually, by creating datasets and doing machine learning experiments and interpretations. After an overview, we present two case studies: murmurations in number theory and loadings of partitions related to Kronecker coefficients in representation theory and combinatorics.

conceptDetails.name: mathematical data science
conceptDetails.description: The novel paradigm introduced in this paper: studying mathematical objects collectively by creating synthetic datasets and applying ML to discover new structures and theorems.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a systematic paradigm for using ML to discover new mathematical structures, conjectures, and theorems from datasets.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Interpreting 'black box' ML models is explicitly identified as a key challenge in mathematical data science, essential for turning statistical patterns into rigorous conjectures.

conceptDetails.name: murmurations
conceptDetails.description: A newly discovered oscillatory pattern in L-functions of elliptic curves identified through ML experimentation, presented as a primary case study of the MDS paradigm.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The MDS paradigm is presented as a structured four-step workflow: dataset generation, ML application, interpretation, and conjecture formulation.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Mathematical invariants of objects serve as features in the ML framework, and the choice of which invariants to include is identified as a critical design decision.

conceptDetails.name: kronecker coefficients
conceptDetails.description: Coefficients in the decomposition of tensor products of symmetric group representations studied via ML-generated partition datasets as the second case study.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Linear regression analysis is cited as the original tool behind the Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture and remains a core ML technique applied to mathematical datasets.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Supervised classification is applied to mathematical datasets to uncover structural patterns, including in the murmuration case study involving L-functions.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The process of converting ML-discovered statistical patterns into precisely formulated mathematical conjectures is framed as a form of hypothesis generation and testing.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Self-supervised learning is described as a standard ML paradigm used to model the probability distribution of mathematical object populations from finite samples.

title: Debiasing Guidance for Discrete Diffusion with Sequential Monte Carlo
abstract: Discrete diffusion models are a class of generative models that produce samples from an approximated data distribution within a discrete state space. Often, there is a need to target specific regions of the data distribution. Current guidance methods aim to sample from a distribution with mass proportional to $p_0(x_0) p(ζ|x_0)^α$ but fail to achieve this in practice. We introduce a Sequential Monte Carlo algorithm that generates unbiasedly from this target distribution, utilising the learnt unconditional and guided process. We validate our approach on low-dimensional distributions, controlled images and text generations. For text generation, our method provides strong control while maintaining low perplexity compared to guidance-based approaches.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses discrete diffusion models as the core generative framework, extending them with SMC-based debiasing for controlled generation.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Sequential Monte Carlo is the central algorithmic contribution, providing asymptotically unbiased sampling from tempered target distributions.

conceptDetails.name: discrete diffusion guidance
conceptDetails.description: A novel problem formulation identifying the theoretical failure modes of guidance in discrete diffusion and deriving exact corrective transition rates.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Continuous-Time Markov Chains (CTMCs) form the mathematical backbone of the discrete diffusion framework analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: importance weighting
conceptDetails.description: Unnormalised importance weights are derived and used within the SMC framework to correct for the bias introduced by standard guidance.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets sampling from the tempered posterior p0(x0)p(ζ|x0)^α, correcting biases in existing guidance-based posterior approximations.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The work advances generative modeling in discrete state spaces, with applications to text and image generation.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The tempered conditional distribution framework is inherently Bayesian, with α controlling the balance between prior and likelihood.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: Score entropy objectives for learning the reverse rate matrix are used as the unconditional backbone that the SMC algorithm exploits.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Likelihood-based objectives for training the discrete diffusion reverse process are part of the background methods the paper builds upon.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Fine-tuning via RL is discussed as a competing approach to sampling from tempered distributions that the proposed method avoids.

title: Parameter Symmetry Potentially Unifies Deep Learning Theory
abstract: The dynamics of learning in modern large AI systems is hierarchical, often characterized by abrupt, qualitative shifts akin to phase transitions observed in physical systems. While these phenomena hold promise for uncovering the mechanisms behind neural networks and language models, existing theories remain fragmented, addressing specific cases. In this position paper, we advocate for the crucial role of the research direction of parameter symmetries in unifying these fragmented theories. This position is founded on a centralizing hypothesis for this direction: parameter symmetry breaking and restoration are the unifying mechanisms underlying the hierarchical learning behavior of AI models. We synthesize prior observations and theories to argue that this direction of research could lead to a unified understanding of three distinct hierarchies in neural networks: learning dynamics, model complexity, and representation formation. By connecting these hierarchies, our position paper elevates symmetry -- a cornerstone of theoretical physics -- to become a potential fundamental principle in modern AI.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central mechanism: parameter symmetry breaking and restoration are proposed as the unifying explanation for hierarchical learning behavior in neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: parameter symmetry restoration
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the process by which model parameters transition from broken-symmetry states back to symmetric states, proposed as a key mechanism in learning dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Learning dynamics in AI systems exhibit abrupt, qualitative shifts analogous to physical phase transitions, which the paper connects to symmetry group transitions.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Formal group-theoretic definitions of parameter symmetry (G-parameter symmetry, symmetric states) underpin the entire theoretical framework.

conceptDetails.name: hierarchical learning dynamics
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the temporal, functional, and spatial hierarchies that emerge during neural network training, unified here under parameter symmetry transitions.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The hierarchy of neural representations—how layers encode increasingly abstract information—is explained as arising from layered parameter symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Symmetries embedded in loss functions are identified as key sources of parameter symmetry that drive learning hierarchies.

conceptDetails.name: neural collapse
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: a structured representation phenomenon in final network layers where class embeddings collapse to symmetric configurations, cited as evidence of symmetry restoration.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses deliberately engineering symmetries into model architectures, connecting to equivariant design principles.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Universal emergent phenomena across large AI systems motivate the need for a unified theory applicable at scale.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Understanding the mechanisms behind neural networks and language models is a driving motivation for the symmetry-unification program.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Simplicity biases and compressive information bottleneck phenomena—linked to functional complexity hierarchies—relate to sparse, low-complexity model solutions.

title: Harmonic Loss Trains Interpretable AI Models
abstract: In this paper, we introduce harmonic loss as an alternative supervisory signal for training neural networks and large language models (LLMs). Harmonic loss differs from standard cross-entropy loss by (a) replacing the usual SoftMax normalization with a scale-invariant HarMax function and (b) computing logits via Euclidean distance rather than a dot product. Harmonic loss enables improved interpretability and faster convergence, owing to its scale invariance and finite convergence point by design, which can be interpreted as a class center. We first validate the performance of harmonic models across algorithmic, vision, and language datasets. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that models trained with harmonic loss perform better than standard models by: (a) enhancing interpretability, (b) requiring less data for generalization, and (c) reducing grokking. Moreover, we compare a GPT-2 model trained with harmonic loss to the standard GPT-2, illustrating that the harmonic model develops more interpretable representations. Looking forward, we believe harmonic loss may become a valuable tool in domains with limited data availability or in high-stakes applications where interpretability and reliability are paramount, paving the way for more robust and efficient neural network models.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is harmonic loss, a novel supervisory signal replacing cross-entropy by using Euclidean distance logits and a scale-invariant HarMax normalization.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Harmonic loss is shown to produce more interpretable internal representations in neural networks and LLMs, including clearer class centers and weight structures.

conceptDetails.name: harmonic loss
conceptDetails.description: A novel loss function using Euclidean distance logits and the HarMax normalization, with scale invariance and a finite convergence point by design.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Models trained with harmonic loss develop qualitatively different and more structured representations compared to cross-entropy-trained models, including more interpretable GPT-2 embeddings.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The harmonic layer's finite convergence point acts as a class center in embedding space, giving geometric meaning to learned token and class representations.

conceptDetails.name: grokking
conceptDetails.description: Harmonic loss substantially reduces the delayed generalization phenomenon where test loss convergence lags far behind training loss convergence.

conceptDetails.name: scale invariance
conceptDetails.description: A key mathematical property of harmonic loss: the learned probabilities are invariant to the overall scale of weight vectors, enabling bounded convergence.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: GPT-2 is used as the primary large-scale testbed to demonstrate that harmonic loss generalizes to autoregressive language models.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Harmonic loss is validated on multiple classification benchmarks including MNIST and algorithmic datasets to measure generalization and data efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Experiments extend from toy 2D cases through vision tasks to LLM-scale GPT-2, demonstrating harmonic loss benefits hold at scale.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Improved data efficiency under harmonic loss is explored in limited-data regimes, relevant to semi-supervised and low-resource learning scenarios.

title: A Poisson Process AutoDecoder for X-ray Sources
abstract: X-ray observing facilities, such as the Chandra X-ray Observatory and the eROSITA, have detected millions of astronomical sources associated with high-energy phenomena. The arrival of photons as a function of time follows a Poisson process and can vary by orders-of-magnitude, presenting obstacles for common tasks such as source classification, physical property derivation, and anomaly detection. Previous work has either failed to directly capture the Poisson nature of the data or only focuses on Poisson rate function reconstruction. In this work, we present Poisson Process AutoDecoder (PPAD). PPAD is a neural field decoder that maps fixed-length latent features to continuous Poisson rate functions across energy band and time via unsupervised learning. PPAD reconstructs the rate function and yields a representation at the same time. We demonstrate the efficacy of PPAD via reconstruction, regression, classification and anomaly detection experiments using the Chandra Source Catalog.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: PPAD uses an autodecoder architecture (decoder-only variant) that maps fixed-length latent vectors to continuous Poisson rate functions without an encoder network.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is learning fixed-length latent representations of variable-length X-ray event files in an unsupervised, end-to-end manner.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Photon arrival times follow a Poisson process, and PPAD is explicitly designed to model and respect this intrinsic stochastic nature of X-ray data.

conceptDetails.name: neural field decoding
conceptDetails.description: A novel application of implicit neural representations (neural fields) to model continuous Poisson rate functions across energy band and time for X-ray light curves.

conceptDetails.name: poisson rate reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: PPAD uniquely reconstructs continuous, resolution-free Poisson rate functions directly from raw photon event files, going beyond stepwise approximations of prior methods like Gregory-Loredo.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: A Poisson likelihood-based loss function is central to PPAD's training, enabling the model to properly handle low-count regimes where Gaussian approximations fail.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: PPAD is evaluated on spectral anomaly detection tasks using the Chandra Source Catalog, leveraging reconstruction error in latent space.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: PPAD produces fixed-length latent embeddings of X-ray sources that are directly usable for downstream tasks such as clustering, classification, and anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: PPAD learns representations without labels, making it applicable to the large majority of X-ray sources that lack ground-truth classifications.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Source classification is demonstrated as a downstream application by training a classifier on top of the learned PPAD latent representations.

conceptDetails.name: neural operators
conceptDetails.description: PPAD employs a one-dimensional neural field (closely related to neural operators) that maps continuous time and energy inputs to Poisson rate values at arbitrary resolution.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Physical property derivation (regression) is demonstrated using PPAD embeddings to predict astrophysical parameters of X-ray sources.

title: Language Models Use Trigonometry to Do Addition
abstract: Mathematical reasoning is an increasingly important indicator of large language model (LLM) capabilities, yet we lack understanding of how LLMs process even simple mathematical tasks. To address this, we reverse engineer how three mid-sized LLMs compute addition. We first discover that numbers are represented in these LLMs as a generalized helix, which is strongly causally implicated for the tasks of addition and subtraction, and is also causally relevant for integer division, multiplication, and modular arithmetic. We then propose that LLMs compute addition by manipulating this generalized helix using the "Clock" algorithm: to solve $a+b$, the helices for $a$ and $b$ are manipulated to produce the $a+b$ answer helix which is then read out to model logits. We model influential MLP outputs, attention head outputs, and even individual neuron preactivations with these helices and verify our understanding with causal interventions. By demonstrating that LLMs represent numbers on a helix and manipulate this helix to perform addition, we present the first representation-level explanation of an LLM's mathematical capability.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Mechanistic interpretability is the central methodology, reverse-engineering how LLMs compute addition at the level of circuits and features to produce the first representation-level explanation of LLM mathematical capability.

conceptDetails.name: mechanistic interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Novel subfield methodology combining circuit analysis and feature understanding; this paper bridges both to give a complete algorithmic explanation of LLM addition.

conceptDetails.name: helix number representation
conceptDetails.description: The key discovery that LLMs encode integers as points on a generalized helix with multiple Fourier periods, causally implicated for addition, subtraction, and other arithmetic.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how LLMs internally represent numbers, discovering that integers are encoded as points on a generalized helix in the residual stream.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Number embeddings in the residual stream are analyzed via PCA and Fourier decomposition, revealing periodic helical structure across all three models studied.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: Numbers lie on a generalized helix manifold in LLM representation space, extending prior work showing that some features occupy non-linear manifolds rather than linear directions.

conceptDetails.name: causal intervention
conceptDetails.description: Activation patching and causal interventions are used throughout to verify that the helix representation is causally responsible for correct arithmetic outputs, not merely correlated.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: GPT-J, Pythia-6.9B, and Llama3.1-8B are the autoregressive transformer architectures reverse-engineered to understand how addition is mechanistically implemented.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Individual attention heads are analyzed to identify which contribute to constructing the a+b helix on the last token from the a and b input helices.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Interpretable helix features are extracted from MLP outputs, attention heads, and individual neuron preactivations to decompose the addition computation.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Fourier analysis identifies dominant periodic frequencies in number representations, revealing the Fourier components that constitute the helix structure.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are discussed as related work for decomposing model activations into interpretable features, contextualizing the helix finding within the broader MI literature.

title: Progress in Normalizing Flows for 4d Gauge Theories
abstract: Normalizing flows have arisen as a tool to accelerate Monte Carlo sampling for lattice field theories. This work reviews recent progress in applying normalizing flows to 4-dimensional nonabelian gauge theories, focusing on two advancements: an architectural improvement referred to as learned active loops, and the application of correlated ensemble methods to QCD with $N_f=2$ dynamical fermions.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The primary method reviewed, used to accelerate Monte Carlo sampling for 4d nonabelian lattice gauge theories.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The target physical system, specifically QCD with N_f=2 dynamical fermions studied using flow-based methods.

conceptDetails.name: learned active loops
conceptDetails.description: A novel architectural improvement replacing fixed loop geometry with a learned linear combination of loops per coupling layer, expanding the transformation space.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The broader framework in which normalizing flows are applied, encompassing SU(N) gauge field configurations on a discrete spacetime lattice.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The foundational sampling technique that normalizing flows are designed to accelerate and improve for lattice field theory calculations.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The flow architectures must preserve gauge symmetry (SU(N) equivariance), a key constraint in the design of learned active loops and staple networks.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Correlated ensemble methods are applied to compute the gluon momentum fraction of the pion, providing computational advantage over independent sampling.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge equivariance is enforced throughout the architecture via conjugation-equivariant projections onto SU(N) to maintain physical consistency.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Used in spectral flow architectures to isolate gauge-invariant information from active loops for transformation.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows function as generative models that learn to sample from lattice gauge theory path integral distributions.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Flows must accurately estimate the target lattice action density to enable unbiased importance sampling via reweighting.

conceptDetails.name: topological freezing
conceptDetails.description: A critical slowing-down phenomenon in lattice QCD at fine lattice spacings that flow-based methods aim to circumvent.

title: A Fast Periodicity Detection Algorithm Sensitive to Arbitrary Waveforms
abstract: A reexamination of period finding algorithms is prompted by new large area astronomical sky surveys that can identify billions of individual sources having a thousand or more observations per source. This large increase in data necessitates fast and efficient period detection algorithms. In this paper, we provide an initial description of an algorithm that is being used for detection of periodic behavior in a sample of 1.5 billion objects using light curves generated from Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) data (Bellm et al. 2019; Masci et al. 2018). We call this algorithm "Fast Periodicity Weighting" (FPW), derived using a Gaussian Process (GP) formalism. A major advantage of the FPW algorithm for ZTF analysis is that it is agnostic to the details of the phase-folded waveform. Periodic sources in ZTF show a wide variety of waveforms, some quite complex, including eclipsing objects, sinusoidally varying objects also exhibiting eclipses, objects with cyclotron emission at various phases, and accreting objects with complex waveforms. We describe the FPW algorithm and its application to ZTF, and provide efficient code for both CPU and GPU.

conceptDetails.name: phase-folded periodogram
conceptDetails.description: A novel waveform-agnostic periodogram algorithm (FPW) that operates by phase-binning observations at trial periods without assuming sinusoidal or boxcar waveform shapes.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is detecting periodic signals in massive astronomical time-series datasets with arbitrary waveform shapes.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The FPW algorithm is formally derived using a Gaussian Process (Bayesian) framework, providing a principled statistical foundation for the periodicity statistic.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm is engineered for scalability to 1.5 billion objects, with efficient CPU and GPU implementations and vectorized computation.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian Processes underpin the mathematical derivation of the FPW statistic, modeling the periodic signal as a stochastic process over phase bins.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: FPW produces a periodogram (power spectrum vs. frequency) analogous to classical spectral methods like Lomb-Scargle, enabling frequency-domain period detection.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The FPW statistic is formulated as a chi-squared difference between a periodic-source hypothesis and a constant-source null hypothesis.

conceptDetails.name: gpu acceleration
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides GPU-optimized code to enable real-time period search across the full ZTF dataset of 1.5 billion light curves.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm is applied to detect periodic variable stars including eclipsing binaries, cyclotron emitters, and accreting objects relevant to stellar physics.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Identifying periodic variable stars among billions of sources is fundamentally a signal-from-background detection task related to anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The phase-binning approach implicitly uses a sparse representation of the periodic signal, assigning observations to discrete phase bins rather than fitting dense basis functions.

title: Low-Rank Adapting Models for Sparse Autoencoders
abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose language model representations into a sparse set of linear latent vectors. Recent works have improved SAEs using language model gradients, but these techniques require many expensive backward passes during training and still cause a significant increase in cross entropy loss when SAE reconstructions are inserted into the model. In this work, we improve on these limitations by taking a fundamentally different approach: we use low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to finetune the \textit{language model itself} around a previously trained SAE. We analyze our method across SAE sparsity, SAE width, language model size, LoRA rank, and model layer on the Gemma Scope family of SAEs. In these settings, our method reduces the cross entropy loss gap by 30\% to 55\% when SAEs are inserted during the forward pass. We also find that compared to end-to-end (e2e) SAEs, our approach achieves the same downstream cross entropy loss 3$\times$ to 20$\times$ faster on \gemma and 2$\times$ to 10$\times$ faster on \llama. We further show that our technique improves downstream metrics and can adapt multiple SAEs at once without harming general language model capabilities. Our results demonstrate that improving model interpretability is not limited to post-hoc SAE training; Pareto improvements can also be achieved by directly optimizing the model itself.

conceptDetails.name: sparse autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The primary tool studied in this paper, used to decompose language model hidden states into sparse, interpretable linear latent vectors.

conceptDetails.name: low-rank adaptation
conceptDetails.description: The novel technique introduced to fine-tune language models around pre-trained SAEs, reducing cross entropy loss gap by 30–55% with minimal compute.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The central scientific goal: improving the interpretability-performance tradeoff by optimizing the model itself rather than only the SAE.

conceptDetails.name: mechanistic interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The broader research field motivating this work, which seeks to reverse-engineer language model computations into human-understandable algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: LoRA-based fine-tuning of pre-trained language models is the core methodological contribution, adapting models post-SAE-training.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: SAEs learn to decompose language model activations into interpretable feature directions, directly engaging with how representations are structured.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity is the architectural constraint central to SAEs, enforcing that only a small number of latent features activate for any given input.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: SAEs aim to produce monosemantic, disentangled features from polysemantic neuron activations, and this work improves that disentanglement.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders are the autoencoder variant at the center of this work, using encoder-decoder structure with a sparsity constraint.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: SAEs extract interpretable feature directions from language model activations; the paper improves the fidelity of this extraction via model adaptation.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper optimizes KL divergence between original model logits and SAE-augmented logits, carefully designing the training objective to preserve capabilities.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key contribution is achieving 2×–20× speedup over end-to-end SAEs and demonstrating the method scales to 27B parameter models.

title: QCD Theory meets Information Theory
abstract: We present a novel technique to incorporate precision calculations from quantum chromodynamics into fully differential particle-level Monte-Carlo simulations. By minimizing an information-theoretic quantity subject to constraints, our reweighted Monte Carlo incorporates systematic uncertainties absent in individual Monte Carlo predictions, achieving consistency with the theory input in precision and its estimated systematic uncertainties. Our method can be applied to arbitrary observables known from precision calculations, including multiple observables simultaneously. It generates strictly positive weights, thus offering a clear path to statistically powerful and theoretically precise computations for current and future collider experiments. As a proof of concept, we apply our technique to event-shape observables at electron-positron colliders, leveraging existing precision calculations of thrust. Our analysis highlights the importance of logarithmic moments of event shapes, which have not been previously studied in the collider physics literature.

conceptDetails.name: information-theoretic reweighting
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution: minimizing KL divergence subject to moment constraints to derive strictly positive event weights that embed precision QCD calculations into Monte Carlo samples.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo event generators are the primary tool being improved; the method reweights existing MC samples to achieve theoretical precision without negative-weight events.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The technique is designed for particle collider experiments, demonstrated on electron-positron collider event-shape observables, with scope for current and future hadron colliders.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Propagating systematic uncertainties from precision theory calculations directly into the reweighted Monte Carlo is a primary design goal of the method.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: QCD — a quantum field theory — provides the precision fixed-order and resummed calculations that serve as the theory target incorporated via the reweighting.

conceptDetails.name: logarithmic moments
conceptDetails.description: A novel basis of functions (ln^n v) identified by the authors as the natural way to encode Sudakov resummation structure; their importance in collider physics had not been previously studied.

conceptDetails.name: qcd resummation
conceptDetails.description: Resummed perturbative QCD calculations of event shapes (e.g., thrust at NLL/NNLL accuracy) provide the precision theory input whose moments are matched by the reweighting procedure.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Event-shape observables such as thrust — closely related to jet structure in e+e- collisions — serve as the proof-of-concept application for the method.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The method is grounded in Jaynes' maximum entropy principle and Boltzmann's statistical mechanics, making it conceptually a Bayesian update of the MC prior toward the theory target.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method constructs an optimal probability distribution over full phase space by matching a small number of moments of a target theoretical distribution.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The Lagrange multipliers enforcing moment constraints are solved by minimizing a least-squares loss via the ADAM optimizer, making numerical optimization central to the implementation.

title: Central densities of dark matter halos in FIRE-2 simulations of low-mass galaxies with cold dark matter and self-interacting dark matter
abstract: We investigate the central density structure of dark matter halos in cold dark matter (CDM) and self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) models using simulations that are part of the Feedback In Realistic Environments (FIRE) project. For simulated halos of dwarf galaxy scale ($M_{\rm halo}(z=0)\approx 10^{10}\,M_\odot$), we study the central structure in both dissipationless simulations and simulations with full FIRE-2 galaxy formation physics. As has been demonstrated extensively in recent years, both baryonic feedback and self-interactions can convert central cusps into cores, with the former process doing so in a manner that depends sensitively on stellar mass at fixed $M_{\rm halo}$. Whether the two processes (baryonic feedback and self-interactions) are distinguishable, however, remains an open question. Here we demonstrate that, compared to feedback-induced cores, SIDM-induced cores transition more quickly from the central region of constant density to the falling density at larger radial scales. This result holds true even when including identical galaxy formation modeling in SIDM simulations as is used in CDM simulations, since self-interactions dominate over galaxy formation physics in establishing the central structure of SIDM halos in this mass regime. The change in density profile slope as a function of radius therefore holds the potential to discriminate between self-interactions and galaxy formation physics as the driver of core formation in dwarf galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly investigates the central density structure of dark matter halos in CDM and SIDM models for dwarf galaxy-scale systems.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: FIRE-2 hydrodynamic cosmological simulations are the primary tool used to study dark matter halo structure across CDM and SIDM models.

conceptDetails.name: self-interacting dark matter
conceptDetails.description: SIDM is the alternative dark matter model under investigation, where elastic particle self-interactions thermalize halo cores and create density cores distinct from feedback-induced ones.

conceptDetails.name: cusp-core transformation
conceptDetails.description: The conversion of steep central density cusps into flat constant-density cores via baryonic feedback or dark matter self-interactions is the central physical phenomenon studied.

conceptDetails.name: baryonic feedback
conceptDetails.description: Stellar feedback-driven gas outflows cause gravitational potential fluctuations that heat dark matter orbits and drive cusp-to-core transformations in dwarf galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: density profile slope
conceptDetails.description: The radial variation of the logarithmic slope of the dark matter density profile is identified as the key observable that can discriminate SIDM-induced from feedback-induced cores.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses forward-modeled simulation outputs to distinguish between competing physical models (CDM vs SIDM) by comparing predicted density profiles to observations.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Comparing CDM and SIDM simulation predictions against observational rotation curve diversity serves as a model validation exercise for both dark matter and baryonic physics prescriptions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: N-body and hydrodynamic simulation techniques underpin the FIRE-2 cosmological runs used to generate the dark matter halo ensembles analyzed in this work.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: The ΛCDM (dark energy plus cold dark matter) standard cosmological model provides the theoretical framework and baseline against which SIDM modifications are compared.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Power-law and profile fitting to the simulated dark matter density distributions is used to quantify and compare halo central structure across models.

title: Theoretical Predictions for the Inner Dark Matter Distribution in the Milky Way Informed by Simulations
abstract:
We build a theoretical range for the Milky Way's (MW) inner dark matter (DM) distribution informed by the FIRE-2, Auriga, VINTERGATAN-GM, and TNG50 simulation suites assuming the canonical cold dark matter (CDM) model. The DM density profiles in Auriga, VINTERGATAN-GM, and TNG50 can be approximately modeled using the adiabatic contraction prescription of Gnedin et al. 2004, while FIRE-2 has stronger baryonic feedback, leading to a departure from the adiabatic contraction model. The simulated halos that are adiabatically contracted are close to spherical (axis ratio $q \in [0.75-0.9]$ at $5^\circ$), whereas halos that experience strong baryonic feedback are oblate ($q \in [0.5-0.7]$). Using the adiabatic contraction and strong baryonic feedback models, along with the observed stellar distribution of the MW, the inner logarithmic density slope for CDM in the MW is predicted to range from $ -0.5$ to $-1.3$. The $J$-factor, which determines the DM-annihilation flux, averaged over a solid angle of $5^\circ$ ($10^\circ$) is predicted to span the range $0.8$-$30$ ($0.6$-$10$) $\times 10^{23} \rm{GeV}^2/\rm{cm}^5$. The $D$-factor, which determines the flux due to DM decay, is predicted to be in the range $0.6$-$2$ ($0.5-1$) $\times10^{23} \rm{GeV}/\rm{cm}^2$.
  GitHub: The results for this work can be found at https://github.com/abdelazizhussein/MW-Inner-DM-Profile.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central focus: predicting the inner dark matter density profile of the Milky Way using simulation-calibrated models.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: FIRE-2, Auriga, VINTERGATAN-GM, and TNG50 hydrodynamic simulation suites are used to bracket the range of plausible MW inner DM profiles.

conceptDetails.name: adiabatic contraction
conceptDetails.description: A key analytic prescription describing the response of a DM halo to baryonic mass infall, used to model DM profiles in Auriga, VINTERGATAN-GM, and TNG50.

conceptDetails.name: baryonic feedback
conceptDetails.description: Stellar and AGN feedback processes that drive gravitational potential fluctuations, causing DM halo expansion in FIRE-2 simulations, departing from adiabatic contraction.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Simulation outputs are used to calibrate adiabatic contraction and feedback models, which are then applied to observed MW stellar data to infer the DM distribution.

conceptDetails.name: j-factor and d-factor
conceptDetails.description: Line-of-sight integrals of the DM density (squared) that determine the expected flux from DM annihilation and decay, predicted to span a wide range depending on feedback model.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The Gnedin et al. 2004 adiabatic contraction prescription serves as an analytic surrogate for the full hydrodynamic simulation response of DM halos to baryons.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: DM density profiles are modeled and compared across simulation suites to estimate inner logarithmic density slopes.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian methods are used to constrain the MW's stellar and DM profile parameters from observational data.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling is used to propagate uncertainties in stellar and halo parameters into predictions for the DM density and J/D-factors.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper inverts observed stellar kinematics and distributions to infer the underlying dark matter distribution in the Galactic Center.

title: A theoretical approach to density-split clustering
abstract: We present an analytical model for density-split correlation functions, that probe galaxy clustering in different density environments. Specifically, we focus on the cross-correlation between density-split regions and the tracer density field. We show that these correlation functions can be expressed in terms of the two-point probability density function (PDF) of the density field. We derive analytical predictions using three levels of approximation for the two-point PDF: a bivariate Gaussian distribution, a bivariate shifted log-normal distribution, and a prediction based on the Large Deviation Theory (LDT) framework. For count-in-cell densities, obtained through spherical top-hat smoothing, one can leverage spherical collapse dynamics and LDT to predict the density two-point PDF in the large-separation regime relative to the smoothing radius. We validate our model against dark matter N-body simulations in real space, incorporating Poisson shot noise and galaxy bias. Our results show that the LDT prediction outperforms the log-normal approximation, and agrees with simulations on large scales within the cosmic variance of a typical DESI DR1 sample, despite relying on only one degree of freedom.

conceptDetails.name: large deviation theory
conceptDetails.description: LDT is the primary theoretical framework used to predict the one-point and two-point PDF of the density field beyond the log-normal approximation.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Count-in-cell density estimation via spherical top-hat smoothing is a core component of the density-split framework.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper validates its analytical model against AbacusSummit N-body dark matter simulations in real space.

conceptDetails.name: spherical collapse dynamics
conceptDetails.description: Spherical collapse dynamics are leveraged within the LDT framework to predict the rate function governing the density PDF.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The two-point PDF of the density field is central to the model, with multiple approximations derived for the joint likelihood of density contrasts.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The model is validated against dark matter N-body simulations, and dark matter clustering is the primary physical system modeled.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy bias
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends the analytical model to biased tracers of matter, incorporating galaxy bias to connect dark matter statistics to observed galaxy clustering.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Density-split clustering is presented as a probe for constraining cosmological parameters related to dark energy through large-scale structure surveys like DESI.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops an analytical model to replace simulation-based emulators for density-split correlation functions, reducing dependence on large simulations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Density-split clustering is used for cosmological parameter inference, connecting the analytical model to posterior estimation on survey data.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: N-body simulations rely on Monte Carlo sampling techniques to model dark matter particle distributions for validation.

title: SIDDA: SInkhorn Dynamic Domain Adaptation for Image Classification with Equivariant Neural Networks
abstract: Modern neural networks (NNs) often do not generalize well in the presence of a "covariate shift"; that is, in situations where the training and test data distributions differ, but the conditional distribution of classification labels remains unchanged. In such cases, NN generalization can be reduced to a problem of learning more domain-invariant features. Domain adaptation (DA) methods include a range of techniques aimed at achieving this; however, these methods have struggled with the need for extensive hyperparameter tuning, which then incurs significant computational costs. In this work, we introduce SIDDA, an out-of-the-box DA training algorithm built upon the Sinkhorn divergence, that can achieve effective domain alignment with minimal hyperparameter tuning and computational overhead. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method on multiple simulated and real datasets of varying complexity, including simple shapes, handwritten digits, and real astronomical observations. SIDDA is compatible with a variety of NN architectures, and it works particularly well in improving classification accuracy and model calibration when paired with equivariant neural networks (ENNs). We find that SIDDA enhances the generalization capabilities of NNs, achieving up to a $\approx40\%$ improvement in classification accuracy on unlabeled target data. We also study the efficacy of DA on ENNs with respect to the varying group orders of the dihedral group $D_N$, and find that the model performance improves as the degree of equivariance increases. Finally, we find that SIDDA enhances model calibration on both source and target data--achieving over an order of magnitude improvement in the ECE and Brier score. SIDDA's versatility, combined with its automated approach to domain alignment, has the potential to advance multi-dataset studies by enabling the development of highly generalizable models.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: SIDDA pairs with equivariant neural networks (ENNs) that encode symmetry information, and the paper systematically studies how increasing dihedral group order improves domain adaptation performance.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The Sinkhorn divergence used in SIDDA is derived from optimal transport theory, providing the principled distance metric for aligning source and target distributions.

conceptDetails.name: covariate shift adaptation
conceptDetails.description: SIDDA is specifically designed to address covariate shift where input distributions differ between training and test data while label conditionals remain unchanged.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: SIDDA enables models trained on labeled source data to transfer effectively to unlabeled target domains with distribution shift.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: SIDDA achieves over an order of magnitude improvement in expected calibration error and Brier score, making model calibration a primary evaluation metric.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Image classification across multiple datasets (shapes, digits, astronomical observations, remote sensing) is the core task on which SIDDA is benchmarked.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: SIDDA aligns latent representations of source and target data to learn domain-invariant features that generalize across distribution shifts.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNNs serve as baseline architectures compatible with SIDDA, evaluated alongside equivariant networks across all benchmark datasets.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies equivariance under the dihedral group DN, varying group order to analyze its effect on domain adaptation performance.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: SIDDA operates in a semi-supervised setting where source domain data is labeled but target domain data is unlabeled.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Real astronomical datasets including galaxy morphology data from different telescopes are used as a primary application domain for SIDDA.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The Sinkhorn divergence loss is dynamically incorporated into the training objective to achieve domain alignment with minimal hyperparameter tuning.

title: Physics of Skill Learning
abstract: We aim to understand physics of skill learning, i.e., how skills are learned in neural networks during training. We start by observing the Domino effect, i.e., skills are learned sequentially, and notably, some skills kick off learning right after others complete learning, similar to the sequential fall of domino cards. To understand the Domino effect and relevant behaviors of skill learning, we take physicists' approach of abstraction and simplification. We propose three models with varying complexities -- the Geometry model, the Resource model, and the Domino model, trading between reality and simplicity. The Domino effect can be reproduced in the Geometry model, whose resource interpretation inspires the Resource model, which can be further simplified to the Domino model. These models present different levels of abstraction and simplification; each is useful to study some aspects of skill learning. The Geometry model provides interesting insights into neural scaling laws and optimizers; the Resource model sheds light on the learning dynamics of compositional tasks; the Domino model reveals the benefits of modularity. These models are not only conceptually interesting -- e.g., we show how Chinchilla scaling laws can emerge from the Geometry model, but also are useful in practice by inspiring algorithmic development -- e.g., we show how simple algorithmic changes, motivated by these toy models, can speed up the training of deep learning models.

conceptDetails.name: skill learning dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon studied: how individual skills emerge sequentially during neural network training, characterized by the Domino effect where skill acquisition triggers subsequent skill acquisition.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper models how networks learn multiple skills simultaneously, analyzing how tasks compete for limited model resources and how task imbalance or compositional dependency shapes learning order.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core goal is developing physics-like intuitive understanding of neural network training by constructing simplified analytical models of skill learning dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: neural scaling laws
conceptDetails.description: Chinchilla scaling laws are derived as emergent consequences of the Geometry model, connecting the paper's theoretical framework to empirical scaling behavior of large language models.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The Geometry model provides theoretical insights into how neural scaling laws arise from the geometry of the loss landscape across model and data size.

conceptDetails.name: grokking
conceptDetails.description: Sudden jumps in skill acquisition—a form of grokking—are identified as one of the diverse learning curve phenomenologies that the three proposed models aim to explain.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Sequential, abrupt transitions in skill learning (the Domino effect) are treated as phase-transition-like phenomena, and the models characterize the conditions under which they occur.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The Geometry model represents skills as directions in the loss landscape, and the geometry of that landscape drives the proposed resource-constrained dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse parity tasks serve as the primary experimental testbed for observing and validating the Domino effect in controlled settings.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Learning dynamics for each skill are modeled as continuous-time processes governed by resource competition, analogous to population dynamics in physics.

title: Inferring Transition Dynamics from Value Functions
abstract: In reinforcement learning, the value function is typically trained to solve the Bellman equation, which connects the current value to future values. This temporal dependency hints that the value function may contain implicit information about the environment's transition dynamics. By rearranging the Bellman equation, we show that a converged value function encodes a model of the underlying dynamics of the environment. We build on this insight to propose a simple method for inferring dynamics models directly from the value function, potentially mitigating the need for explicit model learning. Furthermore, we explore the challenges of next-state identifiability, discussing conditions under which the inferred dynamics model is well-defined. Our work provides a theoretical foundation for leveraging value functions in dynamics modeling and opens a new avenue for bridging model-free and model-based reinforcement learning.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core framework, specifically analyzing how the Bellman equation in RL encodes implicit information about environment transition dynamics within the value function.

conceptDetails.name: dynamics model inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel core contribution: a method to directly extract a model of environment transition dynamics from a converged value function by rearranging the Bellman equation.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring the environment's transition dynamics from a pre-trained value function is formulated as an inverse problem, rearranging the Bellman equation to recover the dynamics model.

conceptDetails.name: next-state identifiability
conceptDetails.description: Novel theoretical concept introduced to characterize conditions under which the inferred dynamics model uniquely recovers the true successor state mapping from a value function.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper leverages reward functions and discount factors (assumed known) as inputs to the dynamics inference procedure, connecting reward structure to recoverable dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: A key motivation and application: inferred dynamics models can be reused across tasks that share environment structure but differ in reward specification, enabling new-task adaptation.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Pre-trained value functions are repurposed beyond their original task to extract dynamics models, enabling policy adaptation without additional environment interactions.

conceptDetails.name: reverse lipschitz continuity
conceptDetails.description: Novel analytical tool used to establish identifiability conditions and bound dynamics inference errors — a lower-bound on value function gradient magnitude ensuring unique state recovery.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The inferred dynamics model serves as a surrogate for the true environment transition function, enabling model-based planning without direct simulator access.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides error analysis for dynamics inference when the value function is approximate, bounding estimation errors using reverse Lipschitz conditions.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A central insight is that the value function implicitly encodes the environment's dynamics, providing a new interpretive lens on what information is stored in learned value functions.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses stochastic transition dynamics as a challenge for next-state identifiability, contrasting with the deterministic case where a unique successor state exists.

title: Average-Reward Soft Actor-Critic
abstract: The average-reward formulation of reinforcement learning (RL) has drawn increased interest in recent years for its ability to solve temporally-extended problems without relying on discounting. Meanwhile, in the discounted setting, algorithms with entropy regularization have been developed, leading to improvements over deterministic methods. Despite the distinct benefits of these approaches, deep RL algorithms for the entropy-regularized average-reward objective have not been developed. While policy-gradient based approaches have recently been presented for the average-reward literature, the corresponding actor-critic framework remains less explored. In this paper, we introduce an average-reward soft actor-critic algorithm to address these gaps in the field. We validate our method by comparing with existing average-reward algorithms on standard RL benchmarks, achieving superior performance for the average-reward criterion.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core domain, specifically extending RL to the average-reward formulation with entropy regularization.

conceptDetails.name: average-reward mdp
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept describing the Markov Decision Process formulation where the agent maximizes expected reward rate rather than discounted cumulative reward.

conceptDetails.name: entropy regularization
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to the paper: the MaxEnt/KL-divergence penalty added to the average-reward objective to yield naturally stochastic optimal policies.

conceptDetails.name: actor-critic framework
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept referring to the combined policy (actor) and value function (critic) deep RL architecture extended to the average-reward entropy-regularized setting.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses optimizing the average-reward criterion rather than the discounted sum, targeting long-term average performance.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives novel actor and critic loss functions incorporating entropy regularization within the average-reward Bellman framework.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Average-reward MDPs rely on ergodic Markov chain theory, and the paper analyzes stationary distributions and communicating chains.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Stochastic policies derived from entropy regularization are shown to be more robust under environmental perturbations and transfer scenarios.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The proposed algorithm requires minimal changes to existing SAC codebases, emphasizing practical scalability to deep function approximation settings.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The entropy-regularized RL objective has a probabilistic inference interpretation via KL-divergence relative to a prior policy.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Entropy regularization is motivated in part by robustness to model uncertainty, sim-to-real gaps, and perturbations in deployment.

title: An Updated Detection Pipeline for Precursor Emission in Type II Supernova 2020tlf
abstract: We present a new photometric pipeline for the detection of pre-supernova (pre-SN) emission in the Young Supernova Experiment (YSE) sky survey. The method described is applied to SN 2020tlf, a type II SN (SN II) with precursor emission in the last ~100 days before first light. We re-analyze the YSE griz-band light curves of SN 2020tlf and provide revised pre-explosion photometry that includes a robust list of confident detection and limiting magnitudes. Compared to the results of Jacobson-Galan et al. 2022a, this new analysis yields fewer total r/i/z-band pre-SN detections at phases > -100 days. Furthermore, we discourage the use of the blackbody modeling of the pre-explosion spectral energy distribution, the pre-SN bolometric light curve and the blackbody model parameters presented in Jacobson-Galan et al. 2022a. Nevertheless, binned photometry of SN 2020tlf confirms a consistent progenitor luminosity of ~10$^{40}$ erg s$^{-1}$ before explosion.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is a refined pipeline for detecting statistically significant pre-supernova photometric signals above host-galaxy background noise using 3-sigma thresholds and 80% recovery fractions.

conceptDetails.name: pre-supernova precursor emission
conceptDetails.description: Novel phenomenon studied: elevated luminosity (~10^40 erg/s) from a massive star's circumstellar environment in the ~100 days before core-collapse explosion.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents an end-to-end photometric pipeline (built on Photpipe) for pre-SN emission detection in sky survey data, constituting a reusable scientific workflow.

conceptDetails.name: artificial source injection
conceptDetails.description: A key validation technique where discrete flux increments are injected at background apertures to empirically derive limiting magnitudes and recovery fractions for pre-SN detections.

conceptDetails.name: difference image photometry
conceptDetails.description: Photometry is performed on difference images (subtracted host-galaxy template) to isolate transient pre-explosion flux at the supernova site.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Detection significance is assessed via 3-sigma thresholds and 80% artificial-source recovery fractions to distinguish real pre-SN detections from noise.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly invalidates blackbody SED modeling and bolometric light curve parameters from the prior analysis, replacing them with more conservative validated detections.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Revised limiting magnitudes and photometric uncertainties are carefully quantified to correct overestimates of pre-SN detections in the original analysis.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Photometric calibration of griz-band light curves from the YSE/Pan-STARRS survey is central to producing reliable pre-explosion flux measurements.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The progenitor star's pre-explosion luminosity (~10^40 erg/s) and circumstellar mass loss constrain the final evolutionary stages of a massive red supergiant before core collapse.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: SN 2020tlf is classified as a Type II (hydrogen-rich) core-collapse supernova, providing context for interpreting the precursor emission properties.

title: Lake- and Surface-Based Detectors for Forward Neutrino Physics
abstract: We propose two medium-baseline, kiloton-scale neutrino experiments to study neutrinos from LHC proton-proton collisions: SINE, a surface-based scintillator panel detector observing muon neutrinos from the CMS interaction point, and UNDINE, a water Cherenkov detector submerged in lake Geneva observing all-flavor neutrinos from LHCb. Using a Monte Carlo simulation, we estimate millions of neutrino interactions during the high-luminosity LHC era. We show that these datasets can constrain neutrino cross sections, charm production in $pp$ collisions, and strangeness enhancement as a solution to the cosmic-ray muon puzzle. SINE and UNDINE thus offer a cost-effective medium-baseline complement to the proposed short-baseline forward physics facility.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is proposing two novel kiloton-scale neutrino detectors (SINE and UNDINE) to observe TeV neutrinos produced at LHC interaction points.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experiments exploit proton-proton collisions at the LHC to produce a forward neutrino beam, making collider physics the primary production mechanism studied.

conceptDetails.name: forward neutrino beams
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept describing the highly collimated neutrino flux produced along the LHC beam axis from hadron decays, enabling medium-baseline experiments.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulation is used to estimate millions of neutrino interactions during the HL-LHC era and compute detector acceptance and physics sensitivities.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper simulates detector response for both SINE (scintillator panels) and UNDINE (water Cherenkov) to estimate signal yields and backgrounds.

conceptDetails.name: water cherenkov detector
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept for UNDINE's detection technology: a submerged kiloton-scale water Cherenkov detector using lake Geneva as a natural medium for all-flavor neutrino detection.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The experiments constrain Standard Model processes including neutrino cross sections, charm production, and strangeness enhancement in pp collisions.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: SINE and UNDINE are sensitive to heavy neutral leptons, a beyond-Standard-Model signature accessible via the forward neutrino beam.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic-ray muon puzzle
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept referring to the observed excess of muons in cosmic-ray air showers relative to model predictions, which strangeness enhancement in pp collisions could help explain.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Neutrino cross section measurements at TeV energies probe effective field theory descriptions of weak interactions in a previously unmeasured regime.

title: Synthesis and Analysis of Data as Probability Measures with Entropy-Regularized Optimal Transport
abstract: We consider synthesis and analysis of probability measures using the entropy-regularized Wasserstein-2 cost and its unbiased version, the Sinkhorn divergence. The synthesis problem consists of computing the barycenter, with respect to these costs, of reference measures given a set of coefficients belonging to the simplex. The analysis problem consists of finding the coefficients for the closest barycenter in the Wasserstein-2 distance to a given measure. Under the weakest assumptions on the measures thus far in the literature, we compute the derivative of the entropy-regularized Wasserstein-2 cost. We leverage this to establish a characterization of barycenters with respect to the entropy-regularized Wasserstein-2 cost as solutions that correspond to a fixed point of an average of the entropy-regularized displacement maps. This characterization yields a finite-dimensional, convex, quadratic program for solving the analysis problem when the measure being analyzed is a barycenter with respect to the entropy-regularized Wasserstein-2 cost. We show that these coefficients, as well as the value of the barycenter functional, can be estimated from samples with dimension-independent rates of convergence, and that barycentric coefficients are stable with respect to perturbations in the Wasserstein-2 metric. We employ the barycentric coefficients as features for classification of corrupted point cloud data, and show that compared to neural network baselines, our approach is more efficient in small training data regimes.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops entropy-regularized optimal transport (Wasserstein-2 cost and Sinkhorn divergence) as the core mathematical framework for synthesis and analysis of probability measures.

conceptDetails.name: wasserstein barycenters
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a characterization of entropy-regularized Wasserstein-2 barycenters as fixed points of averaged displacement maps, enabling efficient synthesis and analysis.

conceptDetails.name: barycentric coordinates
conceptDetails.description: Novel use of entropy-regularized barycentric coefficients as low-dimensional features for downstream machine learning tasks such as point cloud classification.

conceptDetails.name: dimension-free convergence
conceptDetails.description: A key theoretical result showing that barycentric coefficients and the barycenter functional can be estimated from samples at rates independent of ambient dimension, avoiding the curse of dimensionality.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Barycentric coefficients derived from entropy-regularized optimal transport are used as features for classifying corrupted point cloud data.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Data points are modeled as probability measures, and the paper estimates barycentric coefficients and barycenter functionals from samples.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Entropy regularization enables parallelizable computation via the Sinkhorn-Knopp algorithm, overcoming the cubic complexity of standard optimal transport.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The analysis problem—finding barycentric coordinates of a measure with respect to reference measures—serves as a form of dimensionality reduction in the space of probability measures.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The Sinkhorn divergence and entropy-regularized OT costs relate to kernel-based distances and are used in the convex quadratic program for analysis.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Relative entropy (KL divergence) regularization is central to the entropy-regularized optimal transport formulation.

title: Effects of galactic environment on size and dark matter content in low-mass galaxies
abstract: We utilize the cosmological volume simulation, FIREbox, to investigate how a galaxy's environment influences its size and dark matter content. Our study focuses on approximately 1,200 galaxies (886 central and 332 satellite halos) in the low-mass regime, with stellar masses between $10^6$ to $10^9$ $M_{\odot}$. We analyze the size-mass relation ($r_{50} - M_{\star}$), inner dark matter mass-stellar mass ($M^{50}_{\rm DM} - M_{\star}$) relation, and the halo mass-stellar mass ($M_{\rm halo} - M_{\star}$) relation. At fixed stellar mass, we find the galaxies experiencing stronger tidal influences, indicated by higher Perturbation Indices (PI $>$ 1) are generally larger and have lower masses relative to their counterparts with lower Perturbation Indices (PI $<$ 1). Applying a Random Forest regression model, we show that both the environment (PI) and halo mass ($M_{rm halo}$) are significant predictors of a galaxy's relative size and dark matter content. Notably, because $M_{\rm halo}$ is also strongly affected by the environment, our findings indicate that environmental conditions not only influence galactic sizes and relative inner dark matter content directly, but also indirectly through their impact on halo mass. Our results highlight a critical interplay between environmental factors and halo mass in shaping galaxy properties, affirming the environment as a fundamental driver in galaxy formation and evolution.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The FIREbox cosmological volume simulation is the primary tool used to generate and analyze the sample of ~1,200 low-mass galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how galactic environment affects the inner dark matter content and halo mass of low-mass galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy size-mass relation
conceptDetails.description: The r50-M* relation is a central scaling relation analyzed to quantify how environment and halo mass shape galaxy sizes.

conceptDetails.name: halo mass-stellar mass relation
conceptDetails.description: The Mhalo-M* relation is analyzed to show that environment indirectly affects galaxy properties through its impact on halo mass.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: A Random Forest regression model is applied to quantify the relative importance of environment (Perturbation Index) and halo mass as predictors of galaxy size and dark matter content.

conceptDetails.name: tidal stripping
conceptDetails.description: Tidal stripping and ram pressure are identified as key environmental mechanisms that modulate the sizes and dark matter content of low-mass galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Random Forest, an ensemble learning method, is used to identify significant predictors of galaxy structural properties.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Galaxies are classified as central or satellite halos and by their Perturbation Index to study environmental effects on scaling relations.

conceptDetails.name: stellar feedback
conceptDetails.description: Supernova-driven outflows and stellar feedback are discussed as intrinsic processes that reshape low-mass galaxy structure alongside environmental effects.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Statistical inference methods are used to analyze scaling relations and disentangle the effects of halo mass and environment.

title: Predicting band gap from chemical composition: A simple learned model for a material property with atypical statistics
