abstract: In solid-state materials science, substantial efforts have been devoted to the calculation and modeling of the electronic band gap. While a wide range of ab initio methods and machine learning algorithms have been created that can predict this quantity, the development of new computational approaches for studying the band gap remains an active area of research. Here we introduce a simple machine learning model for predicting the band gap using only the chemical composition of the crystalline material. To motivate the form of the model, we first analyze the empirical distribution of the band gap, which sheds new light on its atypical statistics. Specifically, our analysis enables us to frame band gap prediction as a task of modeling a mixed random variable, and we design our model accordingly. Our model formulation incorporates thematic ideas from chemical heuristic models for other material properties in a manner that is suited towards the band gap modeling task. The model has exactly one parameter corresponding to each element, which is fit using data. To predict the band gap for a given material, the model computes a weighted average of the parameters associated with its constituent elements and then takes the maximum of this quantity and zero. The model provides heuristic chemical interpretability by intuitively capturing the associations between the band gap and individual chemical elements.

conceptDetails.name: mixed random variable modeling
conceptDetails.description: Novel framing that treats the band gap as a mixed random variable with a discrete probability mass at zero (metals) and a continuous density for positive values (semiconductors/insulators).

conceptDetails.name: elemental parameter model
conceptDetails.description: A novel architecture where each chemical element is assigned exactly one scalar parameter, and prediction is the ReLU of the stoichiometry-weighted average of constituent element parameters.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper reframes band gap prediction as a non-standard regression task by modeling the band gap as a mixed random variable rather than a purely continuous one.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A central design goal is heuristic chemical interpretability: each element has a single learned parameter that captures its tendency to contribute to a material's band gap.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: The model enables rapid band gap prediction from chemical composition alone, supporting high-throughput screening for materials with desired electronic properties.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The proposed model is maximally sparse — one scalar parameter per chemical element — making it highly interpretable and data-efficient.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained with a custom loss tailored to the mixed-distribution nature of the band gap, rather than standard mean squared error regression loss.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly contrasts its composition-only approach against structure-dependent methods (DFT, GNNs), highlighting band gap prediction without crystal structure input.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Element parameters serve as learned elemental features that are aggregated via a weighted average to represent a material's composition.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: GNNs are discussed as the dominant ML baseline for band gap prediction, incorporating both structural and compositional information, against which the simpler model is compared.

title: Learning Fricke signs from Maass form Coefficients
abstract: In this paper, we conduct a data-scientific investigation of Maass forms. We find that averaging the Fourier coefficients of Maass forms with the same Fricke sign reveals patterns analogous to the recently discovered "murmuration" phenomenon, and that these patterns become more pronounced when parity is incorporated as an additional feature. Approximately 43% of the forms in our dataset have an unknown Fricke sign. For the remaining forms, we employ Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) to machine learn their Fricke sign, achieving 96% (resp. 94%) accuracy for forms with even (resp. odd) parity. We apply the trained LDA model to forms with unknown Fricke signs to make predictions. The average values based on the predicted Fricke signs are computed and compared to those for forms with known signs to verify the reasonableness of the predictions. Additionally, a subset of these predictions is evaluated against heuristic guesses provided by Hejhal's algorithm, showing a match approximately 95% of the time. We also use neural networks to obtain results comparable to those from the LDA model.

conceptDetails.name: maass forms
conceptDetails.description: Smooth automorphic eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator on the upper half-plane, the primary mathematical objects whose Fricke signs are being predicted.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Linear Discriminant Analysis is used as the primary supervised classifier to predict Fricke signs of Maass forms from Fourier coefficients, achieving over 96% accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: murmuration phenomenon
conceptDetails.description: Statistical correlations between Fricke signs and averaged Fourier coefficients of Maass forms, analogous to murmurations previously observed for elliptic curve L-functions.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Fourier coefficients of Maass forms serve as input features for the classifiers, capturing statistical patterns correlated with Fricke signs.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The trained LDA model is applied to approximately 43% of Maass forms with unknown Fricke signs to generate predictions from unlabeled data.

conceptDetails.name: l-functions
conceptDetails.description: Dirichlet series associated to Maass forms whose root numbers encode Fricke signs and whose coefficient distributions underlie the observed murmuration patterns.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The spectral parameter R derived from the Laplace-Beltrami eigenvalue of Maass forms is incorporated as an additional input feature in neural network experiments.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses identifying which Fourier coefficient features most contribute to Fricke sign classification and understanding what the models learn beyond simple sign patterns.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: K-means clustering was tested as an unsupervised method to separate Maass forms by Fricke sign but failed to produce meaningful groupings.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: PCA was evaluated as an unsupervised technique for separating Maass forms by Fricke sign, but proved unsuccessful unlike supervised approaches.

title: Exoplanet Detection via Differentiable Rendering
abstract: Direct imaging of exoplanets is crucial for advancing our understanding of planetary systems beyond our solar system, but it faces significant challenges due to the high contrast between host stars and their planets. Wavefront aberrations introduce speckles in the telescope science images, which are patterns of diffracted starlight that can mimic the appearance of planets, complicating the detection of faint exoplanet signals. Traditional post-processing methods, operating primarily in the image intensity domain, do not integrate wavefront sensing data. These data, measured mainly for adaptive optics corrections, have been overlooked as a potential resource for post-processing, partly due to the challenge of the evolving nature of wavefront aberrations. In this paper, we present a differentiable rendering approach that leverages these wavefront sensing data to improve exoplanet detection. Our differentiable renderer models wave-based light propagation through a coronagraphic telescope system, allowing gradient-based optimization to significantly improve starlight subtraction and increase sensitivity to faint exoplanets. Simulation experiments based on the James Webb Space Telescope configuration demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, achieving substantial improvements in contrast and planet detection limits. Our results showcase how the computational advancements enabled by differentiable rendering can revitalize previously underexploited wavefront data, opening new avenues for enhancing exoplanet imaging and characterization.

conceptDetails.name: exoplanets
conceptDetails.description: The primary astronomical targets of the paper, with the goal of directly imaging faint exoplanets obscured by host star glare and instrumental speckles.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The core computational challenge: recovering wavefront aberration parameters from telescope science images to enable accurate PSF estimation and starlight subtraction.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable rendering
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution — a differentiable physics-based renderer modeling wave-optics light propagation through a coronagraphic telescope, enabling gradient-based optimization over wavefront parameters.

conceptDetails.name: wavefront aberration estimation
conceptDetails.description: The specific sub-problem of inferring time-varying optical aberrations from wavefront sensor data to correct speckle patterns that mimic planet signals.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The ultimate objective: detecting statistically significant faint exoplanet signals against a background of residual speckle noise after starlight subtraction.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The differentiable renderer acts as a differentiable surrogate for the full coronagraphic optical system, enabling efficient gradient-based parameter optimization.

conceptDetails.name: coronagraphic imaging
conceptDetails.description: The instrumental technique central to the paper, using coronagraphic optical elements to suppress starlight; the renderer explicitly models the coronagraph's effect on the PSF.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The differentiable renderer embeds physical wave-optics models (Fourier optics, Fresnel propagation) as differentiable modules, analogous to physics-informed modeling for gradient-based learning.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Experimental validation is conducted entirely via JWST-configuration simulations, using simulated speckle and planet data to benchmark detection performance.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The method achieves improved contrast and effective resolution of faint planet signals beyond what conventional PSF-subtraction methods can achieve.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Planet detection significance is evaluated using likelihood-ratio-based statistical tests, grounding detection claims in a probabilistic framework.

title: Robust resonant anomaly detection with NPLM
abstract: In this study, we investigate the application of the New Physics Learning Machine (NPLM) algorithm as an alternative to the standard CWoLa method with Boosted Decision Trees (BDTs), particularly for scenarios with rare signal events. NPLM offers an end-to-end approach to anomaly detection and hypothesis testing by utilizing an in-sample evaluation of a binary classifier to estimate a log-density ratio, which can improve detection performance without prior assumptions on the signal model. We examine two approaches: (1) a end-to-end NPLM application in cases with reliable background modelling and (2) an NPLM-based classifier used for signal selection when accurate background modelling is unavailable, with subsequent performance enhancement through a hyper-test on multiple values of the selection threshold. Our findings show that NPLM-based methods outperform BDT-based approaches in detection performance, particularly in low signal injection scenarios, while significantly reducing epistemic variance due to hyperparameter choices. This work highlights the potential of NPLM for robust resonant anomaly detection in particle physics, setting a foundation for future methods that enhance sensitivity and consistency under signal variability.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Core methodology of the paper: NPLM and BDT-based approaches detect anomalous events in collider data without prior assumptions on the signal model.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: NPLM performs end-to-end Neyman-Pearson likelihood-ratio hypothesis testing to statistically assess the presence of new physics signals.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: NPLM estimates the log-density ratio between experimental data and reference samples to construct the optimal Neyman-Pearson test statistic.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Primary application domain: model-agnostic resonant searches for beyond-Standard-Model signals in LHC collision data.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper evaluates sensitivity to rare signal injection across varying signal strengths to benchmark detection performance.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: Experimental setting: sliding-window bump-hunt searches for resonant peaks in invariant mass spectra from hadron collider data.

conceptDetails.name: weakly supervised anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Novel framing: CWoLa-based methods train classifiers using noisy region labels (signal region vs. sideband) as a proxy for signal vs. background.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classifiers (BDTs and NPLM neural networks) separate signal-enriched signal regions from background sidebands for anomaly selection.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: NPLM estimates the log-density ratio between data and a reference background distribution to score events for anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Gradient-Boosted Decision Trees serve as the baseline comparison method, demonstrating strong performance at low signal strengths.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A key contribution is demonstrating that NPLM-based methods significantly reduce epistemic variance arising from hyperparameter choices.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: NPLM provides a signal-agnostic goodness-of-fit test for the background hypothesis, complementing the anomaly selection stage.

title: Cosmological constraints from the Minkowski functionals of the BOSS CMASS galaxy sample
abstract: For the first time, we develop a simulation-based model for the Minkowski functionals (MFs) of large-scale structure, which allows us to extract the full information available from the MFs (including both the Gaussian and non-Gaussian part), and apply it to the BOSS DR12 CMASS galaxy sample. Our model is based on high-fidelity mock galaxy catalogs constructed from the \textsc{Abacus}\textsc{Summit} simulations using the halo occupation distribution (HOD) framework, which include the redshift-space distortions and Alcock-Paczynski distortions, incorporate survey realism, including survey geometry and veto masks, and account for angular plus radial selection effects. The cosmological and HOD parameter dependence of the MFs is captured with a neural network emulator trained from the galaxy mocks with various cosmological and HOD parameters. To benchmark the constraining power of the MFs, we also train an emulator for the galaxy 2-point correlation function (2PCF) using the same pipeline. Having validated our approach through successful parameter recovery tests on both internal and external mocks, including non-HOD forward models of the halo-galaxy connection, we apply our forward model to analyze the CMASS data in the redshift range $0.45<z<0.58$. We find the MFs provide stronger constraints on the cosmological parameters than the 2PCF. The combination of the two gives $ω_{\rm cdm}=0.1172^{+0.0020}_{-0.0023}$, $σ_8=0.783\pm 0.026$, and $n_s=0.966^{+0.019}_{-0.015}$, which are tighter by a factor of 2.0, 1.9, and 1.6 than the 2PCF alone. The derived constraint $fσ_8=0.453 \pm 0.016$ is also improved by a factor of 1.9, compared to the 2PCF, and agrees well with Planck 2018 predictions and other results from a series of studies in the literature.

conceptDetails.name: minkowski functionals
conceptDetails.description: Topological summary statistics (volume, surface area, mean curvature, Euler characteristic) of the galaxy density field that capture non-Gaussian large-scale structure information beyond the 2PCF.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The entire forward-modeling pipeline is simulation-based, using AbacusSummit N-body simulations and HOD galaxy mocks to build a likelihood-free inference framework for cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: Neural network emulators are trained on galaxy mocks to interpolate the cosmological and HOD parameter dependence of both the Minkowski functionals and the 2-point correlation function.

conceptDetails.name: non-gaussian statistics
conceptDetails.description: Extracting non-Gaussian information from the cosmic density field via Minkowski functionals is the central scientific advance over standard 2-point methods.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The neural network emulators serve as fast surrogate models replacing expensive N-body simulations during MCMC parameter sampling.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: A Bayesian MCMC framework is used to sample the posterior distribution of cosmological and HOD parameters given the observed Minkowski functional measurements.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives tight posterior constraints on ωcdm, σ8, ns, and fσ8 from the BOSS CMASS galaxy sample using MFs combined with 2PCF.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: AbacusSummit high-fidelity N-body simulations are used to generate the training set of galaxy mocks that underpin the entire forward model.

conceptDetails.name: halo occupation distribution
conceptDetails.description: The HOD framework is used to populate AbacusSummit dark matter halos with galaxies, creating realistic mock catalogs that include survey systematics and redshift-space distortions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC sampling is employed to explore the joint cosmological and HOD parameter space and obtain marginalized constraints.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: A Gaussian likelihood with a covariance matrix estimated from MD-Patchy mocks is constructed for both the Minkowski functionals and the 2PCF.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Cold dark matter density ωcdm is one of the primary cosmological parameters constrained, with the paper improving bounds by a factor of 2 over 2PCF alone.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: ΛCDM and its extensions (including dark energy equation-of-state parameters) are the cosmological models tested against BOSS CMASS data.

title: ORACLE: A Real-Time, Hierarchical, Deep-Learning Photometric Classifier for the LSST
authors: The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration
abstract: We present ORACLE, the first hierarchical deep-learning model for real-time, context-aware classification of transient and variable astrophysical phenomena. ORACLE is a recurrent neural network with Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs), and has been trained using a custom hierarchical cross-entropy loss function to provide high-confidence classifications along an observationally-driven taxonomy with as little as a single photometric observation. Contextual information for each object, including host galaxy photometric redshift, offset, ellipticity and brightness, is concatenated to the light curve embedding and used to make a final prediction. Training on $\sim$0.5M events from the Extended LSST Astronomical Time-Series Classification Challenge, we achieve a top-level (Transient vs Variable) macro-averaged precision of 0.96 using only 1 day of photometric observations after the first detection in addition to contextual information, for each event; this increases to $>$0.99 once 64 days of the light curve has been obtained, and 0.83 at 1024 days after first detection for 19-way classification (including supernova sub-types, active galactic nuclei, variable stars, microlensing events, and kilonovae). We also compare ORACLE with other state-of-the-art classifiers and report comparable performance for the 19-way classification task, in addition to delivering accurate top-level classifications much earlier. The code and model weights used in this work are publicly available at our associated GitHub repository (https://github.com/uiucsn/ELAsTiCC-Classification).

conceptDetails.name: hierarchical classification
conceptDetails.description: ORACLE's defining innovation is a taxonomy-driven hierarchical classification scheme where predictions become more granular as more light curve data accumulates, balancing early actionability with eventual precision.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: ORACLE is built on a GRU-based recurrent neural network that processes photometric light curves sequentially to produce time-evolving classification outputs.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: 19-way multi-class classification of transient and variable astrophysical phenomena is the central task of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A custom hierarchical cross-entropy loss function is introduced as a core methodological contribution to enforce taxonomically consistent predictions across classification levels.

conceptDetails.name: photometric light curves
conceptDetails.description: Time-series multi-band photometry (ugrizy filters) is the primary observational modality used in place of spectroscopy for real-time astrophysical classification.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Supernovae and their subtypes (Ia, Ib/c, II, calcium-rich, etc.) constitute the largest and most scientifically important class in ORACLE's 19-way taxonomy.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Photometric light curve observations are processed into a compact embedding, with contextual host galaxy features (redshift, offset, ellipticity, brightness) appended for final classification.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The GRU encoder produces a light-curve embedding that is concatenated with host galaxy context vectors before the classification head.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: ORACLE is designed to deliver high-confidence, well-calibrated classifications at every phase of a transient's evolution, emphasizing reliable probability outputs rather than just accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Host galaxy photometric properties (redshift, offset, ellipticity, brightness) are used as contextual features to improve transient classification.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The hierarchical model simultaneously optimizes classification at multiple granularity levels (transient vs. variable, then finer subtypes), which constitutes a form of multi-task learning.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: ORACLE is explicitly designed for real-time operation on the LSST alert stream, which will produce millions of transient events per night, requiring a scalable inference pipeline.

title: A Near-IR Search for Helium in the Superluminous Supernova SN 2024ahr
abstract: We present a detailed study of SN 2024ahr, a hydrogen-poor superluminous supernova (SLSN-I), for which we determine a redshift of $z=0.0861$. SN 2024ahr has a peak absolute magnitude of $M_g\approx M_r\approx -21$ mag, rest-frame rise and decline times (50$\%$ of peak) of about 40 and 80 days, respectively, and typical spectroscopic evolution in the optical band. Similarly, modeling of the UV/optical light curves with a magnetar spin-down engine leads to typical parameters: an initial spin period of $\approx 3.3$ ms, a magnetic field strength of $\approx 6\times 10^{13}$ G, and an ejecta mass of $\approx 9.5$ M$_\odot$. Due to its relatively low redshift we obtained a high signal-to-noise ratio near-IR spectrum about 43 rest-frame days post-peak to search for the presence of helium. We do not detect any significant feature at the location of the He I $\,λ2.058$ $μ$m feature, and place a conservative upper limit of $\sim 0.05$ M$_\odot$ on the mass of helium in the outer ejecta. We detect broad features of Mg I $\,λ1.575$ $μ$m and a blend of Co II $\,λ2.126$ $μ$m and Mg II, $λ2.136$ $μ$m, which are typical of Type Ic SNe, but with higher velocities. Examining the sample of SLSNe-I with NIR spectroscopy, we find that, unlike SN 2024ahr, these events are generally peculiar. This highlights the need for a large sample of prototypical SLSNe-I with NIR spectroscopy to constrain the fraction of progenitors with helium (Ib-like) and without helium (Ic-like) at the time of the explosion, and hence the evolutionary path(s) leading to the rare outcome of SLSNe-I.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies SN 2024ahr as a hydrogen-poor superluminous supernova (SLSN-I) and compares its properties to Type Ib/Ic SNe to constrain its classification and progenitor.

conceptDetails.name: nir spectroscopy
conceptDetails.description: Near-infrared spectroscopy at 2 µm is the primary observational technique used to search for the He I λ2.058 µm feature, providing the most robust helium diagnostic.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar spin-down
conceptDetails.description: A highly magnetized, rapidly spinning neutron star (magnetar) is modeled as the central power source driving the extreme luminosity of SN 2024ahr.

conceptDetails.name: helium shell detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper places an upper limit of ~0.05 solar masses on helium in the outer ejecta, constraining whether SLSNe-I progenitors retain a helium layer.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the evolutionary paths leading to SLSNe-I, specifically whether progenitors retain helium (Ib-like) or are fully stripped (Ic-like) at the time of explosion.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for the He I λ2.058 µm spectral feature in NIR spectra, placing an upper limit on helium mass when no significant detection is made.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Light curve modeling with a magnetar spin-down engine involves fitting model parameters (spin period, magnetic field, ejecta mass) to observational data using statistical inference.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Magnetar spin-down engine modeling serves as a physical surrogate to reproduce the observed UV/optical light curves of SN 2024ahr.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral modeling and parameter estimation for the magnetar engine and helium mass limits involve Monte Carlo sampling techniques.

title: Bootstrapped Reward Shaping
abstract: In reinforcement learning, especially in sparse-reward domains, many environment steps are required to observe reward information. In order to increase the frequency of such observations, "potential-based reward shaping" (PBRS) has been proposed as a method of providing a more dense reward signal while leaving the optimal policy invariant. However, the required "potential function" must be carefully designed with task-dependent knowledge to not deter training performance. In this work, we propose a "bootstrapped" method of reward shaping, termed BSRS, in which the agent's current estimate of the state-value function acts as the potential function for PBRS. We provide convergence proofs for the tabular setting, give insights into training dynamics for deep RL, and show that the proposed method improves training speed in the Atari suite.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The core framework of the paper, addressing how agents learn optimal policies through environment interaction, specifically targeting sparse-reward domains where reward signals are infrequent.

conceptDetails.name: bootstrapped reward shaping
conceptDetails.description: The novel method proposed (BSRS) that uses the agent's current estimate of the state-value function as the potential function for potential-based reward shaping, creating an adaptive, self-improving reward signal.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is BSRS, a dynamic reward shaping method that densifies reward signals while provably preserving the optimal policy, directly advancing reward optimization techniques.

conceptDetails.name: potential-based reward shaping
conceptDetails.description: The foundational prior method (PBRS) that this work extends; PBRS allows reward function modification without changing the optimal policy, but requires hand-crafted potential functions with task-specific knowledge.

conceptDetails.name: sparse reward
conceptDetails.description: The primary problem setting motivating BSRS, where the agent receives reward information rarely, making learning extremely sample-inefficient without auxiliary dense reward signals.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Markov Decision Processes form the mathematical foundation of the RL formulation, and the paper provides convergence proofs for the tabular setting using stochastic process theory.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key experimental contribution is demonstrating that BSRS scales to complex image-based Atari tasks using deep RL, requiring only a single line of code change in existing algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper modifies the effective training signal by reshaping rewards, analogous to loss function design; they analyze how the TD loss and training dynamics change under BSRS.

title: A Neural Network-Based Search for Unmodeled Transients in LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA's Third Observing Run
abstract: This paper presents the results of a Neural Network (NN)-based search for short-duration gravitational-wave transients in data from the third observing run of LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA. The search targets unmodeled transients with durations of milliseconds to a few seconds in the 30-1500 Hz frequency band, without assumptions about the incoming signal direction, polarization, or morphology. Using the Gravitational Wave Anomalous Knowledge (GWAK) method, three compact binary coalescences (CBCs) identified by existing pipelines are successfully detected, along with a range of detector glitches. The algorithm constructs a low-dimensional embedded space to capture the physical features of signals, enabling the detection of CBCs, detector glitches, and unmodeled transients. This study demonstrates GWAK's ability to enhance gravitational-wave searches beyond the limits of existing pipelines, laying the groundwork for future detection strategies.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for short-duration gravitational-wave transients in LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA O3 data using the GWAK neural network method.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: GWAK is fundamentally an anomaly detection framework that identifies unmodeled transients and novel signals without prior templates.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Multiple autoencoders are trained on different signal types to construct a low-dimensional embedded space capturing physical signal features.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The core scientific goal is detecting short-duration gravitational-wave bursts across diverse morphologies in real observatory data.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: GWAK uses semi-supervised learning to leverage both labeled signal examples and unlabeled background data for detection.

conceptDetails.name: unmodeled burst search
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept describing the all-sky, template-free search strategy targeting gravitational-wave bursts of arbitrary morphology.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The GWAK algorithm compresses gravitational-wave data into a low-dimensional embedded space to distinguish signal classes.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: GWAK targets signals that fall outside the templates of existing matched-filter pipelines, enabling detection of truly novel transients.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm learns compact representations of gravitational-wave signals to encode physical features in an embedded latent space.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: GWAK constructs a structured embedding space where different signal classes occupy distinct regions for classification and detection.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional neural network architectures are used within the GWAK autoencoder framework to process time-series gravitational-wave strain data.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Simulated signals are injected into real O3 background noise to train and evaluate the GWAK detection pipeline.

title: Data for Mathematical Copilots: Better Ways of Presenting Proofs for Machine Learning
abstract: The datasets and benchmarks commonly used to train and evaluate the mathematical capabilities of AI-based mathematical copilots (primarily large language models) exhibit several shortcomings and misdirections. These range from a restricted scope of mathematical complexity to limited fidelity in capturing aspects beyond the final, written proof (e.g. motivating the proof, or representing the thought processes leading to a proof). These issues are compounded by a dynamic reminiscent of Goodhart's law: as benchmark performance becomes the primary target for model development, the benchmarks themselves become less reliable indicators of genuine mathematical capability. We systematically explore these limitations and contend that enhancing the capabilities of large language models, or any forthcoming advancements in AI-based mathematical assistants (copilots or ``thought partners''), necessitates a course correction both in the design of mathematical datasets and the evaluation criteria of the models' mathematical ability. In particular, it is necessary for benchmarks to move beyond the existing result-based datasets that map theorem statements directly to proofs, and instead focus on datasets that translate the richer facets of mathematical research practice into data that LLMs can learn from. This includes benchmarks that supervise the proving process and the proof discovery process itself, and we advocate for mathematical dataset developers to consider the concept of "motivated proof", introduced by G. Pólya in 1949, which can serve as a blueprint for datasets that offer a better proof learning signal, alleviating some of the mentioned limitations.

conceptDetails.name: motivated proof
conceptDetails.description: The central proposed concept from Pólya (1949) advocating for datasets that capture the reasoning and discovery process behind proofs, not just the final written proof, as a blueprint for better LLM training data.

conceptDetails.name: mathematical benchmarking
conceptDetails.description: Systematic critique of existing mathematical benchmarks for LLMs, identifying shortcomings such as Goodhart's law dynamics, restricted complexity scope, and result-only evaluation.

conceptDetails.name: proof process supervision
conceptDetails.description: The paper's advocacy for training signals that supervise the proving and proof discovery process rather than mapping theorem statements directly to final proofs.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper argues that mathematical research workflows—including conjecture, exploration, and motivation—are systematically absent from current datasets and must be represented.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Large language models (transformer-based architectures) are the primary subject of evaluation and improvement throughout the paper as mathematical copilots.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper calls for models that can explain proof motivation and thought processes, going beyond opaque correct-answer outputs to human-interpretable reasoning traces.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: Discussed as a goal for mathematical copilots, including conjecture generation in domains such as knot theory, representation theory, and group theory.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Training and fine-tuning LLMs on curated mathematical datasets is the central use case motivating the paper's dataset design recommendations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Evaluation scalability is identified as a core challenge, particularly the difficulty of automated grading for open-ended proof-based outputs beyond boxed-answer formats.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: RL-based training approaches (e.g., process reward models, tool-integrated reasoning) are discussed as techniques for improving LLM mathematical capabilities.

conceptDetails.name: mixture of experts
conceptDetails.description: The mixture-of-experts paradigm is cited as one architectural approach for incorporating specialized mathematical reasoning modules into general-purpose LLMs.

title: Product Manifold Machine Learning for Physics
abstract: Physical data are representations of the fundamental laws governing the Universe, hiding complex compositional structures often well captured by hierarchical graphs. Hyperbolic spaces are endowed with a non-Euclidean geometry that naturally embeds those structures. To leverage the benefits of non-Euclidean geometries in representing natural data we develop machine learning on $\mathcal P \mathcal M$ spaces, Cartesian products of constant curvature Riemannian manifolds. As a use case we consider the classification of "jets", sprays of hadrons and other subatomic particles produced by the hadronization of quarks and gluons in collider experiments. We compare the performance of $\mathcal P \mathcal M$-MLP and $\mathcal P \mathcal M$-Transformer models across several possible representations. Our experiments show that $\mathcal P \mathcal M$ representations generally perform equal or better to fully Euclidean models of similar size, with the most significant gains found for highly hierarchical jets and small models. We discover significant correlation between the degree of hierarchical structure at a per-jet level and classification performance with the $\mathcal P \mathcal M$-Transformer in top tagging benchmarks. This is a promising result highlighting a potential direction for further improving machine learning model performance through tailoring geometric representation at a per-sample level in hierarchical datasets. These results reinforce the view of geometric representation as a key parameter in maximizing both performance and efficiency of machine learning on natural data.

conceptDetails.name: product manifold spaces
conceptDetails.description: Novel framework: Cartesian products of constant curvature Riemannian manifolds (hyperbolic, Euclidean, spherical) that jointly represent hierarchical and non-hierarchical data features.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops machine learning on PM spaces—Cartesian products of constant curvature Riemannian manifolds—as the central methodological framework.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: PM-MLP and PM-Transformer architectures are designed to operate natively on non-Euclidean Riemannian manifolds with mixed curvature geometries.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jet classification and top quark tagging benchmarks serve as the primary physics use case for validating the PM machine learning framework.

conceptDetails.name: hyperbolic embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Hyperbolic space components within the product manifold exploit exponential volume growth to efficiently embed the tree-like branching structure of jet formation.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically studies how the choice of geometric representation (hyperbolic, Euclidean, spherical, or product combinations) affects model performance on jet data.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A PM-Transformer architecture is developed that processes particle-level and jet-level representations on product manifolds using non-Euclidean attention mechanisms.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Particle-level and jet-level embeddings in product manifold spaces are the primary mechanism for encoding hierarchical jet structure.

conceptDetails.name: gromov-delta hyperbolicity
conceptDetails.description: A per-jet measure of hierarchical structure used to discover a significant correlation between a jet's geometric complexity and the performance gain of PM-Transformer over Euclidean models.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Multi-class jet classification and binary top-tagging are the machine learning tasks used to benchmark PM representations against Euclidean baselines.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: Jets produced by hadronization of quarks and gluons in high-energy collider experiments provide the dataset and physical motivation for the work.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The PM framework preserves geometric structure through non-Euclidean analogs of standard neural network operations consistent with the manifold's symmetry group.

title: Grayscale to Hyperspectral at Any Resolution Using a Phase-Only Lens
abstract: We consider the problem of reconstructing a HxWx31 hyperspectral image from a HxW grayscale snapshot measurement that is captured using only a single diffractive optic and a filterless panchromatic photosensor. This problem is severely ill-posed, but we present the first model that produces high-quality results. We make efficient use of limited data by training a conditional denoising diffusion model that operates on small patches in a shift-invariant manner. During inference, we synchronize per-patch hyperspectral predictions using guidance derived from the optical point spread function. Surprisingly, our experiments reveal that patch sizes as small as the PSFs support achieve excellent results, and they show that local optical cues are sufficient to capture full spectral information. Moreover, by drawing multiple samples, our model provides per-pixel uncertainty estimates that strongly correlate with reconstruction error. Our work lays the foundation for a new class of high-resolution snapshot hyperspectral imagers that are compact and light-efficient.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: A conditional denoising diffusion model trained on image patches is the core generative method used to reconstruct hyperspectral cubes from grayscale measurements.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses the severely ill-posed inverse problem of recovering a H×W×31 hyperspectral image from a single H×W grayscale measurement captured through a diffractive optic.

conceptDetails.name: psf-guided diffusion
conceptDetails.description: A novel guided sampling algorithm uses the optical point spread function to synchronize per-patch hyperspectral predictions into globally measurement-consistent full-field reconstructions.

conceptDetails.name: hyperspectral reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents the first method to reconstruct high-quality 31-channel hyperspectral images solely from chromatic aberration encoded in a single grayscale snapshot.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: By drawing multiple diffusion samples, the model produces per-pixel uncertainty estimates that strongly correlate with spectral reconstruction error.

conceptDetails.name: diffractive optical encoding
conceptDetails.description: A phase-only diffractive lens or metalens encodes spatial and spectral scene information into the grayscale measurement via purposeful chromatic aberration, enabling spectral recovery.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Multiple samples are drawn from the learned conditional distribution to characterize the posterior over hyperspectral reconstructions given a grayscale measurement.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The denoising diffusion framework used is closely related to score-based generative modeling, learning the score of the data distribution over hyperspectral patches.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The shift-invariant patch-based architecture relies on convolution operations, and the optical PSF is itself a convolution kernel central to the measurement model.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The model learns spatial-spectral priors directly from limited hyperspectral patch data, building compact representations that capture full spectral information from local optical cues.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The patch-based model scales to arbitrary image resolutions at inference, analogous to shift-invariant superresolution pipelines, enabling high-resolution HSI reconstruction beyond training patch sizes.

title: The Millennium and Astrid galaxies in effective field theory: comparison with galaxy-halo connection models at the field level
abstract: Cosmological analyses of redshift space clustering data are primarily based on using luminous ``red'' galaxies (LRGs) and ``blue'' emission line galaxies (ELGs) to trace underlying dark matter. Using the large high-fidelity high-resolution MillenniumTNG (MTNG) and Astrid simulations, we study these galaxies with the effective field theory (EFT)-based field level forward model. We confirm that both red and blue galaxies can be accurately modeled with EFT at the field level and their parameters match those of the phenomenological halo-based models. Specifically, we consider the state of the art Halo Occupation Distribution (HOD) and High Mass Quenched (HMQ) models for the red and blue galaxies, respectively. Our results explicitly confirm the validity of the halo-based models on large scales beyond the two-point statistics. In addition, we validate the field-level HOD/HMQ-based priors for EFT full-shape analysis. We find that the local bias parameters of the ELGs are in tension with the predictions of the LRG-like HOD models and present a simple analytic argument explaining this phenomenology. We also confirm that ELGs exhibit weaker non-linear redshift-space distortions (``fingers-of-God''), suggesting that a significant fraction of their data should be perturbative. We find that the response of EFT parameters to galaxy selection is sensitive to assumptions about baryonic feedback, suggesting that a detailed understanding of feedback processes is necessary for robust predictions of EFT parameters. Finally, using neural density estimation based on paired HOD-EFT parameter samples, we obtain optimal HOD models that reproduce the clustering of Astrid and MTNG galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The EFT-based field-level forward model is the central framework used to model galaxy clustering statistics for both red and blue galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The MillenniumTNG and Astrid hydrodynamical simulations provide the high-fidelity galaxy catalogs analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy-halo connection models
conceptDetails.description: Halo Occupation Distribution (HOD) and High Mass Quenched (HMQ) models are tested against EFT field-level results to validate their accuracy for red and blue galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy bias expansion
conceptDetails.description: The perturbative bias expansion relating galaxy density to the underlying matter field is central to connecting EFT parameters with HOD/HMQ model predictions.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Neural density estimation is used to map paired HOD-EFT parameter samples and derive optimal HOD priors for EFT full-shape analysis.

conceptDetails.name: redshift-space distortions
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates non-linear redshift-space distortions (fingers-of-God) and finds ELGs exhibit weaker signatures than LRGs, extending the perturbative regime.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Galaxies serve as biased tracers of the underlying dark matter distribution, and the galaxy-halo connection models describe how galaxies populate dark matter halos.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Field-level Bayesian inference is used to extract EFT bias parameters from simulated galaxy catalogs with cosmic-variance cancellation.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs simulation-based approaches combining hydrodynamical simulations with EFT and HOD models to validate priors for cosmological analyses.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper measures posterior distributions of EFT bias parameters conditioned on galaxy catalogs from both hydro simulations and HOD mocks.

conceptDetails.name: baryonic feedback
conceptDetails.description: The sensitivity of EFT bias parameters to baryonic feedback assumptions is quantified by comparing MTNG and Astrid simulations with different AGN implementations.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural density estimators act as surrogates to efficiently map HOD parameter space to EFT parameter distributions for prior construction.

title: Probing primordial non-Gaussianity by reconstructing the initial conditions
abstract: We propose to constrain the primordial (local-type) non-Gaussianity signal by first reconstructing the initial density field to remove the late time non-Gaussianities introduced by gravitational evolution. Our reconstruction algorithm combines perturbation theory on large scales with a convolutional neural network on small scales. We reconstruct the squared potential (that sources the non-Gaussian signal) out to $k=0.2\ h$/Mpc to an accuracy of 99.8%. We cross-correlate this squared potential field with the reconstructed density field and verify that this computationally inexpensive estimator has the same information content as the full matter bispectrum. As a proof of concept, our approach can yield up to a factor of three improvement in the $f_{\rm NL}$ constraints, although it does not yet include the complications of galaxy bias or imperfections in the reconstruction. These potential improvements make it a promising alternative to current approaches to constraining primordial non-Gaussianity.

conceptDetails.name: primordial non-gaussianity
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon being constrained — local-type PNG parameterized by fNL that encodes non-Gaussian signatures of inflation in the initial density field.

conceptDetails.name: initial conditions reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core method: reconstructing the primordial density field and squared gravitational potential from late-time observations to remove gravitational non-linearities.

conceptDetails.name: bispectrum estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a compressed cross-power estimator ⟨Φ²δ⟩ and proves it captures the same fNL information as the full matter bispectrum.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing the initial conditions from the evolved late-time density field is cast as an inverse problem combining perturbation theory and learned corrections.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A CNN is trained to reconstruct the small-scale (high-k) components of the primordial density field where perturbation theory breaks down.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: N-body simulations are used extensively to generate training data, validate reconstruction accuracy, and test fNL constraints as a proof of concept.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: An optimal, near-lossless estimator for fNL is derived by maximizing the likelihood of the cross-power spectrum ⟨Φ²δ⟩.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Fisher matrix forecasting is used to quantify expected fNL constraints and demonstrate the factor-of-three improvement over pre-reconstruction analyses.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The CNN functions as a surrogate for the full non-linear gravitational dynamics at small scales, replacing expensive N-body calculations during reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The CNN is trained as a regression model mapping the nonlinear density field to the primordial squared potential Φ² at each spatial location.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo realizations of simulations are used to estimate covariance matrices for the bispectrum and cross-power estimators.

title: SymbolFit: Automatic Parametric Modeling with Symbolic Regression
abstract: We introduce SymbolFit, a framework that automates parametric modeling by using symbolic regression to perform a machine-search for functions that fit the data while simultaneously providing uncertainty estimates in a single run. Traditionally, constructing a parametric model to accurately describe binned data has been a manual and iterative process, requiring an adequate functional form to be determined before the fit can be performed. The main challenge arises when the appropriate functional forms cannot be derived from first principles, especially when there is no underlying true closed-form function for the distribution. In this work, we develop a framework that automates and streamlines the process by utilizing symbolic regression, a machine learning technique that explores a vast space of candidate functions without requiring a predefined functional form because the functional form itself is treated as a trainable parameter, making the process far more efficient and effortless than traditional regression methods. We demonstrate the framework in high-energy physics experiments at the CERN Large Hadron Collider (LHC) using five real proton-proton collision datasets from new physics searches, including background modeling in resonance searches for high-mass dijet, trijet, paired-dijet, diphoton, and dimuon events. We show that our framework can flexibly and efficiently generate a wide range of candidate functions that fit a nontrivial distribution well using a simple fit configuration that varies only by random seed, and that the same fit configuration, which defines a vast function space, can also be applied to distributions of different shapes, whereas achieving a comparable result with traditional methods would have required extensive manual effort.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: The core ML technique used by SymbolFit to perform a machine-search over candidate functional forms, treating the function itself as a trainable parameter via genetic programming on expression trees.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A key contribution of SymbolFit is automatically re-optimizing and estimating uncertainties for all candidate functions found by symbolic regression, enabling their use in downstream statistical inference.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The fundamental task automated by SymbolFit—fitting parametric functions to binned data distributions—replacing traditional manual polynomial or empirical regression workflows.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain: SymbolFit is demonstrated on five CMS datasets from LHC resonance searches (dijet, trijet, paired-dijet, diphoton, dimuon) to model signal and background distributions.

conceptDetails.name: background parametric modeling
conceptDetails.description: Novel application task: automatically constructing smooth parametric functions to describe SM background distributions in LHC resonance searches without a known underlying true function.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental context—proton-proton collision data at the CERN LHC—in which SymbolFit performs parametric background modeling for invariant mass distributions.

conceptDetails.name: genetic programming
conceptDetails.description: Novel algorithmic method: expression trees encoding mathematical functions are evolved via mutation and crossover operations to explore the space of candidate functional forms.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The downstream statistical inference workflow for which SymbolFit's parametric models and uncertainty estimates are constructed, enabling signal-versus-background hypothesis tests.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: Used to evaluate and rank the candidate functions produced by symbolic regression, ensuring that selected parametric models accurately describe the data distributions.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: SymbolFit automates the previously manual, iterative process of discovering appropriate functional forms for data distributions, eliminating trial-and-error in parametric modeling.

conceptDetails.name: expression tree search
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the discrete combinatorial search over symbolic expression trees that defines SymbolFit's function-space exploration strategy, distinct from continuous optimization.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The candidate parametric functions found by SymbolFit serve as smooth surrogate descriptions of binned collision data for use in statistical analyses.

title: Type IIn Supernovae. I. Uniform Light Curve Characterization and a Bimodality in the Radiated Energy Distribution
abstract: We present the largest uniform study to date of Type IIn supernovae (SNe IIn), focusing in this first paper on the multi-band optical light curves of $487$ SNe IIn. The sample, constructed from multiple surveys, extends to $z \approx 0.8$, with the majority of events at $z \lesssim 0.3$. We construct uniform multi-band and bolometric light curves using Gaussian process regression, and determine key observed properties in the rest-frame (e.g., peak luminosity, timescales, radiated energy). We find that SNe IIn span broad ranges in peak luminosity ($\sim 10^{42}-10^{44}$ erg s$^{-1}$) and timescales ($\sim 20-300$ days above 50% of peak luminosity), but the sample divides into two clear groups in the luminosity-timescale phase-space around the median peak luminosity ($\approx 10^{43}$ erg s$^{-1}$): faint-fast and luminous-slow groups. This leads to a strong bimodality in the radiated energy distribution, with peaks at $\sim 10^{49}$ and $\sim 2\times10^{50}$ erg, with the latter events having a characteristic timescale of $\sim 100$ days, and the former appearing to bifurcate into two branches with timescales of $\sim 40$ and $\sim 70$ days. Therefore, SNe IIn exhibit at least two dominant groupings, and perhaps three, which are likely reflective of different progenitor and/or circumstellar medium formation pathways. We do not find any obvious transition in SN IIn properties at the arbitrary cut-off of $\approx -20$ mag used for the designation "Type II Superluminous Supernovae", and we argue that this classification should be abandoned. The absence of SNe IIn with timescales of $\lesssim 14$ days defines the region occupied by fast transients with evidence for interaction with hydrogen-poor circumstellar medium.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper performs large-scale uniform classification and characterization of Type IIn supernovae, challenging existing classification schemes like SLSN-II.

conceptDetails.name: bimodal energy distribution
conceptDetails.description: A novel observational finding of bimodality in radiated energy with peaks at ~10^49 and ~2x10^50 erg, suggesting at least two distinct physical populations.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: Shock interaction between supernova ejecta and pre-existing circumstellar medium is identified as the main power source driving the observed diversity.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian process regression is used to construct uniform multi-band and bolometric light curves across 487 SNe IIn.

conceptDetails.name: photometric survey analysis
conceptDetails.description: Multi-survey optical photometry from TNS, WISeREP, ZTF, and other facilities is uniformly processed to construct the largest SN IIn light curve sample to date.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies bimodal clustering of SNe IIn into faint-fast and luminous-slow groups in luminosity-timescale phase space.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian process regression, a stochastic process framework, is the core computational method for light curve interpolation and reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The bimodality in radiated energy distribution is interpreted as reflecting different progenitor channels and circumstellar medium formation pathways.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Statistical analysis of the radiated energy distribution bimodality is used to test whether SNe IIn form distinct physical subclasses.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The study identifies distinct groupings and outliers within the SN IIn population, including fast transients with hydrogen-poor CSM interaction.

title: A Twist on Heterotic Little String Duality
abstract: In this work, we significantly expand the web of T-dualities among heterotic NS5-brane theories with eight supercharges. This is achieved by introducing twists involving outer automorphisms of discrete gauge/flavor factors and tensor multiplet permutations along the compactification circle. We assemble field theory data that we propose as invariants across T-dual theories, comprised of twisted Coulomb branch dimensions, higher group structures and flavor symmetry ranks. Using this data, we establish a detailed field theory correspondence between singularities of the compactification space, the number five-branes in the theory, and the flavor symmetry factors. The twisted theories are realized via M-theory compactifications on non-compact genus-one fibered Calabi-Yau threefolds without section. This approach allows us to prove duality of twisted and (un-)twisted theories by leveraging M/F-theory duality and identifying inequivalent torus fibrations in the same geometry. We construct several new 5D theories, including a novel type of CHL-like twisted theory where the two M9 branes are identified. Using their field theory invariants, we also construct their dual theories.

conceptDetails.name: t-duality
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically expands the web of T-dualities among heterotic NS5-brane theories, including new twisted T-dual pairs.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper works within string theory, studying heterotic Little String Theories and their T-duality web via compactifications.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau compactification
conceptDetails.description: Twisted theories are geometrically realized via M-theory compactifications on non-compact genus-one fibered Calabi-Yau threefolds without section.

conceptDetails.name: brane physics
conceptDetails.description: Heterotic NS5-branes and M9-branes are the fundamental objects whose worldvolume theories define the Little String Theories studied.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Field theory data (Coulomb branch dimensions, higher group structures, flavor symmetry ranks) are assembled as T-duality invariants.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Outer automorphisms of gauge/flavor algebras and discrete symmetry groups (Z2, Z3) are central tools for constructing twisted compactifications.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Outer automorphism twists of discrete gauge/flavor factors implement a form of symmetry breaking when compactifying on a circle.

conceptDetails.name: generalized symmetries
conceptDetails.description: Higher group structures (2-group symmetry constants κR, κP) are used as T-duality invariants characterizing the twisted LSTs.

conceptDetails.name: moduli space
conceptDetails.description: Coulomb branch moduli spaces and their dimensions serve as key invariants for matching T-dual theories.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: Little String Theories are UV-complete 6D interacting theories sharing structural features with conformal theories, studied via their dualities.

title: Conversations and Deliberations: Non-Standard Cosmological Epochs and Expansion Histories
abstract: This document summarizes the discussions which took place during the PITT-PACC Workshop entitled "Non-Standard Cosmological Epochs and Expansion Histories," held in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, Sept. 5-7, 2024. Much like the non-standard cosmological epochs that were the subject of these discussions, the format of this workshop was also non-standard. Rather than consisting of a series of talks from participants, with each person presenting their own work, this workshop was instead organized around free-form discussion blocks, with each centered on a different overall theme and guided by a different set of Discussion Leaders. This document is not intended to serve as a comprehensive review of these topics, but rather as an informal record of the discussions that took place during the workshop, in the hope that the content and free-flowing spirit of these discussions may inspire new ideas and research directions.

conceptDetails.name: early matter domination
conceptDetails.description: Block IV is dedicated to early matter-dominated eras (EMDEs), covering their physical origin, observational signatures, and effects on structure formation.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: Block III dedicates an entire discussion to gravitational-wave signals as observational probes of modified expansion histories, including stochastic backgrounds from early-universe dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological stasis
conceptDetails.description: Block VIII introduces and debates cosmological stasis, a novel epoch where multiple species maintain fixed energy fractions over extended periods despite cosmological expansion.

conceptDetails.name: primordial black holes
conceptDetails.description: Block IX examines scenarios where primordial black holes dominate the early-universe energy budget, with distinct observational and theoretical consequences.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: The CMB is repeatedly cited as a primary observational anchor constraining non-standard cosmological epochs and benchmarking expansion history models.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Dark matter production mechanisms are discussed in the context of non-standard epochs, where modified expansion rates can alter relic abundances.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Scalar field dynamics and associated phase transitions are central to Blocks II and V, motivating non-standard expansion histories from first principles.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Top-down EFT frameworks (Block II and VII) provide theoretical justification for scalar-field-driven non-standard epochs within string theory and formal theory contexts.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Stochastic gravitational-wave backgrounds arising from modified cosmologies are analyzed as a key observational signal of pre-BBN dynamics.

title: Structure factor and topological bound of twisted bilayer semiconductors at fractional fillings
abstract: The structure factor is a useful observable for probing charge density correlations in real materials, and its long-wavelength behavior encapsulated by ``quantum weight'' has recently gained prominence in the study of quantum geometry and topological phases of matter. Here we employ the static structure factor, S(q), to explore the phase diagram of twisted transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), specifically tMoTe2, at filling factors n=1/3, 2/3 under varying displacement fields. Our results reveal a topological phase transition between a fractional Chern insulator (FCI) and a generalized Wigner crystal (GWC). This transition is marked by the appearance of Bragg peaks at charge-density-wave vectors, and simultaneously, large decrease of S(q) at small q which lowers the interaction energy. We further calculate the quantum weight of various FCI states, verifying the universal topological bound. Our findings provide new insights into the phase diagram of twisted TMDs and establish a general framework for characterizing topological phases through structure factor analysis.

conceptDetails.name: fractional chern insulator
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central subject: topological phases at fractional band fillings in twisted MoTe2 exhibiting fractional quantum anomalous Hall conductivity.

conceptDetails.name: quantum weight
conceptDetails.description: A ground state property encoding long-wavelength density correlations, whose universal topological lower bound K ≥ |C| is verified for FCI states.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies a topological phase transition between a fractional Chern insulator and a generalized Wigner crystal driven by displacement field tuning.

conceptDetails.name: moiré quantum geometry
conceptDetails.description: Many-body quantum geometry of fractional Chern insulators defined over twisted boundary conditions, directly related to quantum weight and structure factor.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Fractional Chern insulator and generalized Wigner crystal ground states are characterized through exact diagonalization and structure factor analysis.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The transition from FCI to generalized Wigner crystal involves spontaneous breaking of translational symmetry, signaled by Bragg peaks at CDW vectors.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The static structure factor S(q) serves as the primary spectral observable for identifying phase boundaries and charge density wave order.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Band-projected exact diagonalization is used to solve the many-body Hamiltonian and obtain ground state wavefunctions at fractional fillings.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The continuum model Hamiltonian for twisted TMDs provides an effective field theory framework capturing moiré band topology and interactions.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: Quantum weight and many-body quantum geometry characterize the ground state of fractional Chern insulators, connecting to broader quantum information concepts.

title: Adaptive Length Image Tokenization via Recurrent Allocation
abstract: Current vision systems typically assign fixed-length representations to images, regardless of the information content. This contrasts with human intelligence - and even large language models - which allocate varying representational capacities based on entropy, context and familiarity. Inspired by this, we propose an approach to learn variable-length token representations for 2D images. Our encoder-decoder architecture recursively processes 2D image tokens, distilling them into 1D latent tokens over multiple iterations of recurrent rollouts. Each iteration refines the 2D tokens, updates the existing 1D latent tokens, and adaptively increases representational capacity by adding new tokens. This enables compression of images into a variable number of tokens, ranging from 32 to 256. We validate our tokenizer using reconstruction loss and FID metrics, demonstrating that token count aligns with image entropy, familiarity and downstream task requirements. Recurrent token processing with increasing representational capacity in each iteration shows signs of token specialization, revealing potential for object / part discovery.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is learning variable-length visual representations that adapt to image content entropy and complexity.

conceptDetails.name: adaptive token allocation
conceptDetails.description: Novel method where representational capacity is dynamically increased per image by adding new latent tokens at each recurrent iteration based on content complexity.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: Recurrent computation drives the iterative token allocation process, where the same encoder-decoder is applied across multiple rollout iterations.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: ALIT uses an encoder-decoder architecture with reconstruction loss as the self-supervised training objective for learning adaptive tokenizations.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Cross-attention is used for latent-token distillation, enabling 2D image tokens to be compressed into 1D latent tokens at each recurrent step.

conceptDetails.name: perceiver architectures
conceptDetails.description: ALIT is directly inspired by and extends the Perceiver framework, performing latent-token distillation from high-dimensional 2D tokens to compact 1D tokens.

conceptDetails.name: dynamic halting
conceptDetails.description: Novel mechanism that masks 2D image tokens already well-reconstructed from further processing, enabling per-token early stopping during recurrent rollouts.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The architecture builds on transformer-based encoder-decoder designs, extending them with recurrent processing and adaptive token allocation.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Image reconstruction is used as a self-supervised objective to train the adaptive tokenizer without requiring labeled data.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The paper compresses 2D image tokens into variable-length 1D latent tokens, performing adaptive dimensionality reduction based on image content.

conceptDetails.name: token specialization
conceptDetails.description: Emergent phenomenon observed in ALIT where later-allocated tokens focus on specific objects or parts, suggesting implicit object/part discovery.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Variable-length tokenization reduces computational cost by allocating fewer tokens to simpler images, improving efficiency in downstream tasks.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The tokenizer is evaluated for compatibility with image generation tasks, including integration with LlamaGen for class-conditional generation.

title: The $\mathcal{D}$-Geometric Hilbert Scheme -- Part II: Hilbert and Quot DG-Schemes
abstract: This is the second in a series of two papers developing a moduli-theoretic framework for differential ideal sheaves associated with formally integrable, involutive systems of algebraic partial differential equations (PDEs). Building on earlier work, which established the existence of moduli stacks for such systems with prescribed regularity and stability conditions, we now construct a derived enhancement of these moduli spaces. We prove the derived $\mathcal{D}$-Quot functor admits a global differential graded refinement representable by a suitable differential graded $\mathcal{D}$-manifold. We further analyze the finiteness, representability, and functoriality properties of these derived moduli spaces, establishing foundations for a derived deformation theory of algebraic differential equations.

conceptDetails.name: derived d-geometry
conceptDetails.description: The primary mathematical framework of the paper, extending D-modules and algebraic geometry to derived/homotopical settings to parametrize solutions of algebraic PDEs.

conceptDetails.name: moduli of pdes
conceptDetails.description: The central problem: constructing derived Hilbert and Quot schemes that classify formally integrable, involutive algebraic differential systems with prescribed numerical polynomial.

conceptDetails.name: spencer cohomology
conceptDetails.description: The stability criterion (Spencer-stability) used to ensure well-behaved moduli spaces, serving as the PDE analog of Gieseker stability in algebraic geometry.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
