conceptDetails.description: Lie pseudogroups act naturally on jet spaces, and their invariant quotients are described as well-defined D-schemes central to the moduli construction.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Pseudogroup-invariant quotients and the preservation of Spencer regularity under restriction to non-characteristic subvarieties are key structural results.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper motivates its constructions as foundations for counting problems in gauge theory and enumerative algebraic D-geometry.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The moduli problem of classifying integrable differential systems from their symbolic data (Hilbert polynomial, characteristic module) is an instance of a geometric inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spencer cohomology and Postnikov towers provide filtration and spectral-sequence-type tools for analyzing finiteness and representability of the derived moduli functors.

title: DexHub and DART: Towards Internet Scale Robot Data Collection
abstract: The quest to build a generalist robotic system is impeded by the scarcity of diverse and high-quality data. While real-world data collection effort exist, requirements for robot hardware, physical environment setups, and frequent resets significantly impede the scalability needed for modern learning frameworks. We introduce DART, a teleoperation platform designed for crowdsourcing that reimagines robotic data collection by leveraging cloud-based simulation and augmented reality (AR) to address many limitations of prior data collection efforts. Our user studies highlight that DART enables higher data collection throughput and lower physical fatigue compared to real-world teleoperation. We also demonstrate that policies trained using DART-collected datasets successfully transfer to reality and are robust to unseen visual disturbances. All data collected through DART is automatically stored in our cloud-hosted database, DexHub, which will be made publicly available upon curation, paving the path for DexHub to become an ever-growing data hub for robot learning. Videos are available at: https://dexhub.ai/project

conceptDetails.name: crowdsourced teleoperation
conceptDetails.description: DART enables anyone worldwide to collect robot demonstrations via AR interfaces without physical hardware, enabling internet-scale crowdsourcing.

conceptDetails.name: augmented reality interface
conceptDetails.description: DART's AR interface provides high-fidelity scene visualization for teleoperation, reducing occlusion and improving operator throughput.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Addressing the scalability bottleneck in robot data collection is the central motivation for DART and DexHub.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: The core technical challenge addressed: policies learned in cloud-based simulation successfully transfer to physical robots.

conceptDetails.name: robot demonstration dataset
conceptDetails.description: DexHub serves as a publicly available, continuously growing cloud-hosted database of robot demonstrations for learning research.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Policies trained on DART-collected simulation data are transferred to real-world robot deployment, demonstrating sim-to-real transfer.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Simulation enables visual randomization and augmentation strategies to improve policy robustness beyond what real-world data collection allows.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Policies trained with DART data demonstrate robustness to unseen visual disturbances in real-world deployment.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper highlights simulation's advantage for applying RL to refine human-collected demonstrations through massively parallelizable environments.

title: Small-scale Hamiltonian optimization of interpolating operators for Lagrangian lattice quantum field theory
abstract: Lattice quantum field theory calculations may potentially combine the advantages of Hamiltonian formulations with the scalability and control of conventional Lagrangian frameworks. However, such hybrid approaches need to consider (1) the differences in renormalized coupling values between the two formulations, and (2) finite-volume and discretization effects when the Hamiltonian component of the calculation is characterized by a smaller volume or coarser lattice spacing than the Lagrangian component. This work investigates the role of both factors in the application of Hamiltonian-optimized interpolating operator constructions for the conventional Lagrangian framework. The numerical investigation is realized for the pseudoscalar meson in the Schwinger model, using tensor-network and Monte-Carlo calculations. It is demonstrated that tensor-network-optimized constructions are robust to both (1) and (2). In particular, accurate optimized constructions for the pseudoscalar meson can be obtained from calculations with a smaller number of Hamiltonian lattice sites, even when the meson mass itself receives significant finite-volume corrections. To the extent that these results generalize to theories with more complicated spectra, the method holds promise for near-term applications in large-scale calculations of lattice quantum field theory.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops and tests hybrid Hamiltonian-Lagrangian methods within lattice gauge theory, using the Schwinger model as a proof-of-concept system.

conceptDetails.name: interpolating operator optimization
conceptDetails.description: The central novel method: using small-scale Hamiltonian tensor-network calculations to optimize the operator constructions used in large-scale Lagrangian Monte Carlo calculations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Lattice quantum field theory is the overarching framework, with the paper investigating how Hamiltonian and Lagrangian formulations of QFT can be combined.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Tensor-network calculations are used to perform the Hamiltonian optimization of interpolating operators, leveraging efficient representations of low-lying quantum states.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The Hamiltonian formulation of lattice QFT is exploited for its ability to directly represent quantum states and avoid sign problems, enabling interpolating operator optimization.

conceptDetails.name: hybrid lattice approach
conceptDetails.description: A novel computational paradigm combining small-scale Hamiltonian calculations with large-scale Lagrangian Monte Carlo, demonstrated to be robust to formulation differences.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo calculations in the Lagrangian framework are the target application for which tensor-network-optimized interpolating operators are constructed and validated.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The conventional Lagrangian Monte Carlo framework provides the scalable large-scale calculation target that benefits from Hamiltonian-optimized operator constructions.

conceptDetails.name: finite-volume corrections
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically studies how finite-volume and discretization effects in small Hamiltonian systems affect the accuracy of optimized operators transferred to larger Lagrangian calculations.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Differences in renormalized gauge-field couplings and fermion masses between Hamiltonian and Lagrangian formulations are a key systematic effect investigated in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Explicit representations of interpolated quantum states in the Hamiltonian framework are leveraged to parametrically optimize interpolating operator constructions.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The work is motivated by near-term quantum simulation capabilities, with tensor networks serving as a proxy for small-scale quantum computations applied to lattice QFT.

title: Not So Flat Metrics
abstract: In order to be in control of the $α'$ derivative expansion, geometric string compactifications are understood in the context of a large volume approximation. In this letter, we consider the reduction of these higher derivative terms, and propose an improved estimate on the large volume approximation using numerical Calabi-Yau metrics obtained via machine learning methods. Further to this, we consider the $α'^3$ corrections to numerical Calabi-Yau metrics in the context of IIB string theory. This correction represents one of several important contributions for realistic string compactifications -- alongside, for example, the backreaction of fluxes and local sources -- all of which have important consequences for string phenomenology. As a simple application of the corrected metric, we compute the change to the spectrum of the scalar Laplacian.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper works within IIB string theory, computing α' corrections to Calabi-Yau metrics relevant for realistic string compactifications.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau metrics
conceptDetails.description: Numerical Ricci-flat Kähler metrics on Calabi-Yau threefolds are the central geometric objects computed and corrected throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: alpha-prime corrections
conceptDetails.description: The leading α'^3 higher-derivative corrections to the IIB string effective action and their effect on the compactification geometry are explicitly computed.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The α' derivative expansion of the 10D string effective action is the central EFT framework whose validity the paper tests and extends.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A projective neural network trained with the cymetric φ-model is used to obtain numerical Ricci-flat metrics on the Fermat quintic Calabi-Yau.

conceptDetails.name: large volume approximation
conceptDetails.description: The standard validity criterion for truncating the α' expansion is refined using pointwise curvature checks enabled by the numerical metric.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: Machine learning techniques are applied to learn the geometry of Calabi-Yau manifolds by approximating their Ricci-flat metrics.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The spectrum of the scalar Laplacian on the Calabi-Yau is computed as an application of the α'-corrected metric.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau CFTs provide the worldsheet description of string compactifications, and the α' corrections studied here are defining data of these CFTs.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural network-based numerical metrics serve as surrogates for exact analytic Calabi-Yau metrics that are generally unavailable.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Shifts to eigenvalues of the scalar Laplacian are calculated to quantify the physical effect of the metric correction.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the Ricci-flat metric satisfying the Monge-Ampère equation from a learned neural representation is an inverse geometric problem.

title: A Lorentz-Equivariant Transformer for All of the LHC
abstract: We show that the Lorentz-Equivariant Geometric Algebra Transformer (L-GATr) yields state-of-the-art performance for a wide range of machine learning tasks at the Large Hadron Collider. L-GATr represents data in a geometric algebra over space-time and is equivariant under Lorentz transformations. The underlying architecture is a versatile and scalable transformer, which is able to break symmetries if needed. We demonstrate the power of L-GATr for amplitude regression and jet classification, and then benchmark it as the first Lorentz-equivariant generative network. For all three LHC tasks, we find significant improvements over previous architectures.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: L-GATr is built with exact Lorentz equivariance as its core architectural principle, enabling the network to respect relativistic symmetry across all LHC tasks.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The architecture encodes data in a spacetime geometric algebra, using geometric products and multivectors to build equivariant network layers.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: L-GATr generalizes the Geometric Algebra Transformer to Lorentz symmetry, preserving the scalable attention-based transformer structure as its backbone.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically encodes Lorentz symmetry into every layer — linear maps, attention, and layer normalization — while also allowing controlled symmetry breaking via reference vectors.

conceptDetails.name: spacetime geometric algebra
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs the full Clifford algebra Cl(1,3) over Minkowski space as the internal representation space, unifying vectors, bivectors, and pseudoscalars under one framework.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: L-GATr is applied to precision surrogate modeling of scattering amplitudes up to Z+5 gluon final states, outperforming prior architectures at per-mille accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jet classification and multi-class tagging are key benchmarks, with L-GATr achieving state-of-the-art performance enhanced further by pre-training.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets the full breadth of LHC machine learning tasks — amplitude regression, jet tagging, and event generation — as a unified demonstration of L-GATr.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The Lorentz group structure and its subgroups are explicitly used to construct equivariant representations and handle cases of partial symmetry realization.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: L-GATr is embedded inside a diffusion-based generative network to produce the first Lorentz-equivariant event generator for LHC final states.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Event generation for high-multiplicity final states (e.g., tt̄+4 jets) is a primary application, with L-GATr outperforming all benchmark generative architectures.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: A Lorentz-equivariant attention mechanism over multivector tokens is a core component of L-GATr, enabling permutation-invariant processing of particle sets.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Pre-training L-GATr on broad datasets before fine-tuning on specific tagging tasks yields significant performance gains in jet classification.

title: Linearized Wasserstein Barycenters: Synthesis, Analysis, Representational Capacity, and Applications
abstract: We propose the linear barycentric coding model (LBCM) which utilizes the linear optimal transport (LOT) metric for analysis and synthesis of probability measures. We provide a closed-form solution to the variational problem characterizing the probability measures in the LBCM and establish equivalence of the LBCM to the set of 2-Wasserstein barycenters in the special case of compatible measures. Computational methods for synthesizing and analyzing measures in the LBCM are developed with finite sample guarantees. One of our main theoretical contributions is to identify an LBCM, expressed in terms of a simple family, which is sufficient to express all probability measures on the closed unit interval. We show that a natural analogous construction of an LBCM in 2 dimensions fails, and we leave it as an open problem to identify the proper extension in more than 1 dimension. We conclude by demonstrating the utility of LBCM for covariance estimation and data imputation.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The paper is fundamentally built on optimal transport theory, using 2-Wasserstein distances and transport maps as the geometric foundation for the LBCM framework.

conceptDetails.name: linearized wasserstein embedding
conceptDetails.description: A novel technique central to this paper: embedding measures into an L2 space via optimal transport maps from a base measure, enabling linear operations on distributions.

conceptDetails.name: barycentric coding
conceptDetails.description: The core model of the paper, representing probability measures as weighted barycenters of reference measures under the LOT or W2 metric.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The LBCM provides a low-dimensional coding model for probability measures, enabling both synthesis and analysis (encoding/decoding) of data distributions.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The analysis problem projects arbitrary measures onto a low-parameter barycentric coding model, effectively reducing the representation complexity.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The LBCM models and reconstructs probability measures from finite samples, with statistical guarantees on estimation error rates.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The LBCM exploits the geodesic geometry of the Wasserstein manifold to construct a linear approximation in transport map space.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Data imputation and image reconstruction are cast as inverse problems solved via the LBCM analysis problem.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The barycentric coding model enforces simplex constraints on coefficients, yielding parsimonious, low-parameter representations of probability measures.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The LOT metric induces a linearization of the Wasserstein space that shares structural properties with kernel-based embeddings into Hilbert spaces.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Finite-sample computational methods use empirical measures and plug-in OT map estimates, with statistical guarantees derived from sampling theory.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Covariance estimation experiments demonstrate LBCM as a prior-based generative model for distributions over positive definite matrices.

title: ASURA-FDPS-ML: Star-by-star Galaxy Simulations Accelerated by Surrogate Modeling for Supernova Feedback
abstract: We introduce new high-resolution galaxy simulations accelerated by a surrogate model that reduces the computation cost by approximately 75 percent. Massive stars with a Zero Age Main Sequence mass of more than about 10 $\mathrm{M_\odot}$ explode as core-collapse supernovae (CCSNe), which play a critical role in galaxy formation. The energy released by CCSNe is essential for regulating star formation and driving feedback processes in the interstellar medium (ISM). However, the short integration timesteps required for SNe feedback have presented significant bottlenecks in astrophysical simulations across various scales. Overcoming this challenge is crucial for enabling star-by-star galaxy simulations, which aim to capture the dynamics of individual stars and the inhomogeneous shell's expansion within the turbulent ISM. To address this, our new framework combines direct numerical simulations and surrogate modeling, including machine learning and Gibbs sampling. The star formation history and the time evolution of outflow rates in the galaxy match those obtained from resolved direct numerical simulations. Our new approach achieves high-resolution fidelity while reducing computational costs, effectively bridging the physical scale gap and enabling multi-scale simulations.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: A machine learning surrogate model replaces direct numerical integration of supernova feedback physics, reducing computational cost by ~75% while maintaining simulation fidelity.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops star-by-star galaxy simulations that resolve individual stellar feedback events in dwarf galaxy formation and evolution.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The ML framework emulates resolved direct numerical simulations of supernova remnant evolution to bypass costly small-timestep integration.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Core-collapse supernovae from massive stars (>10 solar masses) drive the feedback processes modeled and accelerated in this work.

conceptDetails.name: multi-scale simulation
conceptDetails.description: The framework bridges physical scale gaps between individual supernova remnant dynamics (~100 yr timescales) and galaxy-scale evolution (~Gyr timescales).

conceptDetails.name: ism turbulence feedback
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paper specifically models inhomogeneous shell expansion and turbulent ISM response to individual supernova explosions at star-by-star resolution.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Reducing computational cost by 75% enables high-resolution star-by-star galaxy simulations that were previously intractable at this scale.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Gibbs sampling, a Markov Chain Monte Carlo technique, is used within the surrogate modeling framework to sample physical states for SN feedback.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The surrogate model performs regression to predict the physical states of fluid tracer particles undergoing supernova blast-wave interactions.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The surrogate model trained on isolated molecular cloud simulations is applied to full dwarf galaxy simulations, representing domain transfer.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper contrasts its data-driven approach against PINNs, ultimately choosing data-driven surrogate modeling for galaxy formation simulations.

title: Multi-rigidity of Schubert classes in partial flag varieties
abstract: In this paper, we study the multi-rigidity problem in rational homogeneous spaces. A Schubert class is called multi-rigid if every multiple of it can only be represented by a union of Schubert varieties. We prove the multi-rigidity of Schubert classes in rational homogeneous spaces. In particular, we characterize the multi-rigid Schubert classes in partial flag varieties of type A, B and D. Moreover, for a general rational homogeneous space $G/P$, we deduce the rigidity and multi-rigidity from the corresponding generalized Grassmannians (correspond to maximal parabolics). When $G$ is semi-simple, we also deduce the rigidity and multi-rigidity from the simple cases.

conceptDetails.name: schubert calculus
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central mathematical framework: the study of Schubert classes in the integer cohomology of flag varieties, proving when multiples of these classes can only be represented by unions of Schubert varieties.

conceptDetails.name: flag variety geometry
conceptDetails.description: Partial flag varieties of Lie types A, B, and D are the primary geometric objects studied, with detailed combinatorial characterization of their Schubert indices and rigidity conditions.

conceptDetails.name: cohomology rigidity
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution: classifying which Schubert cohomology classes are multi-rigid, meaning every positive multiple is representable only by unions of Schubert subvarieties.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper fundamentally relies on Lie group theory, studying homogeneous spaces G/P where G is a semisimple algebraic group and P is a parabolic subgroup, with classification via Dynkin diagrams.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Rational homogeneous spaces G/P encode Lie group symmetries, and the rigidity/multi-rigidity of Schubert classes is analyzed through the lens of these symmetry structures.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Flag varieties and Schubert calculus appear naturally in the geometry underlying quantum field theory and string compactifications, providing foundational mathematical structure.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Co-author Yau's framework of Calabi-Yau geometry and co-author Sheshmani's work on mathematical string theory provide the broader physics context motivating study of these algebraic varieties.

title: Inferring the Morphology of the Galactic Center Excess with Gaussian Processes
abstract: Descriptions of the Galactic Center using Fermi gamma-ray data have so far modeled the Galactic Center Excess (GCE) as a template with fixed spatial morphology or as a linear combination of such templates. Although these templates are informed by various physical expectations, the morphology of the excess is a priori unknown. For the first time, we describe the GCE using a flexible, non-parametric machine learning model -- the Gaussian process (GP). We assess our model's performance on synthetic data, demonstrating that the model can recover the templates used to generate the data. We then fit the \Fermi data with our model in a single energy bin from 2-20 GeV (leaving a spectral GP analysis of the GCE for future work) using a variety of template models of diffuse gamma-ray emission to quantify our fits' systematic uncertainties associated with diffuse emission modeling. We interpret our best-fit GP in terms of GCE templates consisting of an NFW squared template and a bulge component to determine which bulge models can best describe the fitted GP and to what extent the best-fit GP is described better by an NFW squared template versus a bulge template. The best-fit GP contains morphological features that are typically not associated with traditional GCE studies. These include a localized bright source at around $(\ell,b) = (20^{\circ}, 0^{\circ})$ and a diagonal arm extending Northwest from the Galactic Center. In spite of these novel features, the fitted GP is explained best by a template-based model consisting of the bulge presented in Coleman et al. (2020) and a squared NFW component. Our results suggest that the physical interpretation of the GCE in terms of stellar bulge and NFW-like components is highly sensitive to the assumed morphologies, background models, and the region of the sky used for inference.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Two-dimensional spatial Gaussian processes serve as the core non-parametric model for describing the GCE morphology without assuming a fixed spatial template.

conceptDetails.name: galactic center excess
conceptDetails.description: The GCE is the specific anomalous gamma-ray emission from the Galactic Center whose morphology and physical origin this paper investigates.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses Bayesian inference to naturally quantify uncertainty in the predicted GCE morphology via the GP posterior.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Estimating the posterior distribution over the GCE spatial morphology is the primary inferential goal, achieved through stochastic variational inference.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The GCE is a candidate dark matter annihilation signal, and the paper investigates whether its morphology is consistent with an NFW-squared dark matter profile or a stellar bulge.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Systematic uncertainties from diffuse emission modeling and morphological assumptions are quantified across multiple template models and fitting configurations.

conceptDetails.name: sparse variational inference
conceptDetails.description: Stochastic variational inference with a sparse GP (inducing point) approximation is the key algorithmic contribution enabling scalable Bayesian inference for the GCE.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: GP kernel selection (e.g., Matérn, RBF) encodes prior smoothness assumptions about the GCE signal and is validated through stability tests.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Sparse GP approximations using inducing points and stochastic variational inference enable tractable inference over the O(10^3) spatial bins of Fermi gamma-ray data.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes a spatially extended gamma-ray excess signal at the Galactic Center, identifying novel morphological features not seen in template-based analyses.

conceptDetails.name: nfw profile
conceptDetails.description: The Navarro-Frenk-White dark matter halo density profile squared serves as a key competing template for interpreting the GCE morphology.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Extensive validation on synthetic data confirms the GP can recover known injected templates and is robust to mismodeling of diffuse emission components.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring the unknown spatial morphology of the GCE from observed gamma-ray photon counts is an ill-posed inverse problem addressed non-parametrically.

title: Relative Monoidal Bondal-Orlov
abstract: In this article we study a relative monoidal version of the Bondal-Orlov reconstruction theorem. We establish an uniqueness result for tensor triangulated category structures $(\boxtimes,\mathbb{1})$ on the derived category $D^{b}(X)$ of a variety $X$ which is smooth projective and faithfully flat over a quasi-compact quasi-separated base scheme $S$ in the case where the fibers $X_{s}$ over any point $s\in S$ all have ample (anti-)canonical bundles. To do so we construct a stack $Γ$ of dg-bifunctors which parametrize the local homotopical behaviour of $\boxtimes$, and we study some of its properties around the derived categories of the fibers $X_{s}$.

conceptDetails.name: tensor triangulated categories
conceptDetails.description: Tensor triangulated category structures — triangulated categories equipped with a compatible symmetric monoidal bifunctor — are the primary subject of the paper's uniqueness theorem.

conceptDetails.name: derived categories
conceptDetails.description: The bounded derived category D^b(X) of coherent sheaves on a smooth projective variety is the central mathematical object whose tensor triangulated structures are classified in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: algebraic geometry stacks
conceptDetails.description: Algebraic stacks — including the stack Γ of dg-bifunctors and the ∞-stack of dg-categories — are the primary geometric tool used to parametrize tensor structures and carry out descent arguments.

conceptDetails.name: dg-enhancements
conceptDetails.description: Differential graded enhancements of triangulated categories provide the homotopy-coherent framework used to lift tensor bifunctors and apply descent constructions across fibers.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Symmetric monoidal structures on triangulated categories rely on coherence data governed by group-theoretic symmetry axioms, central to both Balmer's spectrum construction and the paper's uniqueness argument.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Mirror symmetry, a key application domain discussed in the examples section, connects the paper's reconstruction results for derived categories to dualities in string theory.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The derived categorical machinery developed in this paper has foundational applications in topological and algebraic quantum field theory, particularly through mirror symmetry and Fourier-Mukai correspondences.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: The tensor bifunctor parametrized by the stack of dg-bifunctors shares structural parallels with tensor network decompositions, encoding local-to-global composition laws for derived objects.

title: A Cosmic-Scale Benchmark for Symmetry-Preserving Data Processing
abstract: Efficiently processing structured point cloud data while preserving multiscale information is a key challenge across domains, from graphics to atomistic modeling. Using a curated dataset of simulated galaxy positions and properties, represented as point clouds, we benchmark the ability of graph neural networks to simultaneously capture local clustering environments and long-range correlations. Given the homogeneous and isotropic nature of the Universe, the data exhibits a high degree of symmetry. We therefore focus on evaluating the performance of Euclidean symmetry-preserving ($E(3)$-equivariant) graph neural networks, showing that they can outperform non-equivariant counterparts and domain-specific information extraction techniques in downstream performance as well as simulation-efficiency. However, we find that current architectures fail to capture information from long-range correlations as effectively as domain-specific baselines, motivating future work on architectures better suited for extracting long-range information.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper benchmarks E(3)-equivariant GNNs that preserve Euclidean symmetry (translations, rotations, reflections) when processing galaxy point clouds.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: GNNs are the primary model class benchmarked for processing unordered galaxy point clouds and capturing multi-scale spatial correlations.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The benchmark specifically evaluates models that preserve the homogeneous and isotropic symmetry structure inherent in cosmological data.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy clustering benchmark
conceptDetails.description: A novel curated dataset of simulated galaxy positions representing a cosmic-scale point cloud benchmark for evaluating ML architectures.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The benchmark dataset is derived from the Quijote suite of N-body simulations, representing simulated galaxy positions and properties.

conceptDetails.name: long-range correlations
conceptDetails.description: A central finding is that current equivariant GNN architectures fail to capture long-range cosmological correlations as well as domain-specific baselines.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper situates its work within the geometric deep learning paradigm, applying symmetry-aware architectures to structured point cloud data.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Processing point clouds of O(10^4) galaxy points and simulation-efficiency are explicit evaluation criteria in the benchmark.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper evaluates how well GNN architectures learn compressed representations of galaxy clustering data for downstream inference.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The benchmark aims to assess ML methods as automated surrogates for traditional domain-specific summary statistics in cosmological data analysis.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Constraining the distribution and nature of dark matter is one of the downstream scientific tasks motivating the benchmark.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Inferring cosmological parameters related to dark energy and the Universe's expansion history is a key benchmark task.

title: Fermion Masses and Mixing in String-Inspired Models
abstract: We study a class of supersymmetric Froggatt-Nielsen (FN) models with multiple U(1) symmetries and Standard Model (SM) singlets inspired by heterotic string compactifications on Calabi-Yau threefolds. The string-theoretic origin imposes a particular charge pattern on the SM fields and FN singlets, dividing the latter into perturbative and non-perturbative types. Employing systematic and heuristic search strategies, such as genetic algorithms, we identify charge assignments and singlet VEVs that replicate the observed mass and mixing hierarchies in the quark sector, and subsequently refine the Yukawa matrix coefficients to accurately match the observed values for the Higgs VEV, the quark and charged lepton masses and the CKM matrix. This bottom-up approach complements top-down string constructions and our results demonstrate that string FN models possess a sufficiently rich structure to account for flavour physics. On the other hand, the limited number of distinct viable charge patterns identified here indicates that flavour physics imposes tight constraints on string theory models, adding new constraints on particle spectra that are essential for achieving a realistic phenomenology.

conceptDetails.name: froggatt-nielsen mechanism
conceptDetails.description: The core model-building framework of the paper, in which horizontal U(1) charges and singlet VEVs generate hierarchical Yukawa couplings explaining the fermion mass spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives its FN models from heterotic string compactifications on Calabi-Yau threefolds, with string-theoretic constraints directly shaping the charge patterns and singlet structure.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The authors construct four-dimensional N=1 supergravity effective field theories whose structure and operator content are constrained by the underlying string compactification.

conceptDetails.name: flavour hierarchy
conceptDetails.description: The observed pattern of quark and lepton mass ratios and mixing angles that the string FN models are designed to explain, imposing tight constraints on viable string constructions.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau compactification
conceptDetails.description: Heterotic string compactifications on smooth Calabi-Yau threefolds with split vector bundles provide the geometric origin of the U(1) symmetries and charge structures studied.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central goal is to reproduce the observed SM fermion mass hierarchies, quark mixing (CKM matrix), and Higgs VEV within string-inspired models.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Multiple horizontal U(1) symmetries are broken by singlet vacuum expectation values, generating the Yukawa coupling hierarchies responsible for fermion masses.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The horizontal symmetry group G = U(1)^{f-1} and its representations, labeled by integer charge vectors with equivalence classes, form the mathematical backbone of the FN charge assignment framework.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: Genetic algorithms are employed as a heuristic search strategy to explore the large landscape of FN charge assignments and identify phenomenologically viable models.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The bottom-up search for realistic string FN models constitutes a systematic exploration of particle spectra beyond the Standard Model that satisfy tight phenomenological constraints.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: A crude renormalization scheme is applied to run Yukawa couplings and match quark masses at a common scale, enabling comparison with observed values.

title: Blast: a Web Application for Characterizing the Host Galaxies of Astrophysical Transients
abstract: Characterizing the host galaxies of astrophysical transients is important to many areas of astrophysics, including constraining the progenitor systems of core-collapse supernovae, correcting Type Ia supernova distances, and probabilistically classifying transients without photometric or spectroscopic data. Given the increasing transient discovery rate in the coming years, there is substantial utility in providing public, transparent, reproducible, and automatic characterization for large samples of transient host galaxies. Here we present Blast, a web application that ingests live streams of transient alerts, matches transients to their host galaxies, and performs photometry on coincident archival imaging data of the host galaxy. The photometry is then used to infer both global host-galaxy properties and galaxy properties within 2 kpc of the transient location by using the Prospector Bayesian inference framework, with an acceleration in evaluation speed achieved via simulation-based inference. Blast provides host-galaxy properties to users via a web browser or an application program interface. The software can be extended to support alternative photometric or SED-fitting algorithms, and can be scaled via an asynchronous worker queue across multiple compute nodes to handle the processing of large volumes of transient alerts for upcoming transient surveys. Blast has been ingesting newly discovered transients from the Transient Name Server since mid-2024, and has currently measured SED parameters for more than 6000 transients. The service is publicly available at https://blast.scimma.org/.

conceptDetails.name: transient host matching
conceptDetails.description: Novel automated algorithm matching astrophysical transients to their host galaxies using archival imaging and probabilistic directional light radius methods.

conceptDetails.name: sed fitting
conceptDetails.description: Multi-wavelength spectral energy distribution fitting is the core method for inferring physical properties of transient host galaxies from photometric data.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Used to accelerate the Prospector Bayesian SED-fitting framework, replacing slow MCMC sampling with learned posteriors for host galaxy property estimation.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The Prospector framework uses Bayesian inference to infer global and local host galaxy properties from multi-wavelength photometry.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Blast estimates posterior distributions over galaxy SED parameters including stellar mass, star formation rate, and metallicity for each transient host.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Host galaxy characterization is used to probabilistically classify astrophysical transients, including supernovae, without requiring spectroscopic data.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Simulation-based inference acts as a surrogate for full Bayesian SED fitting, dramatically accelerating evaluation speed without sacrificing posterior quality.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Host galaxy properties are used to classify Type Ia and core-collapse supernovae and correct SN Ia distance measurements for cosmological applications.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: Blast provides a fully automated, reproducible, and publicly accessible pipeline for host galaxy characterization from alert ingestion to SED parameter delivery.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Blast propagates photometric uncertainties through SED fitting to provide reliable parameter uncertainties for host galaxy properties.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Blast uses an asynchronous worker queue across multiple compute nodes to handle high-volume transient alert processing for upcoming surveys like LSST.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Transient alert streams are ingested and matched to host galaxies, enabling automated flagging and characterization of newly discovered astrophysical events.

title: Decomposing The Dark Matter of Sparse Autoencoders
abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a promising technique for decomposing language model activations into interpretable linear features. However, current SAEs fall short of completely explaining model performance, resulting in "dark matter": unexplained variance in activations. This work investigates dark matter as an object of study in its own right. Surprisingly, we find that much of SAE dark matter -- about half of the error vector itself and >90% of its norm -- can be linearly predicted from the initial activation vector. Additionally, we find that the scaling behavior of SAE error norms at a per token level is remarkably predictable: larger SAEs mostly struggle to reconstruct the same contexts as smaller SAEs. We build on the linear representation hypothesis to propose models of activations that might lead to these observations. These insights imply that the part of the SAE error vector that cannot be linearly predicted ("nonlinear" error) might be fundamentally different from the linearly predictable component. To validate this hypothesis, we empirically analyze nonlinear SAE error and show that 1) it contains fewer not yet learned features, 2) SAEs trained on it are quantitatively worse, and 3) it is responsible for a proportional amount of the downstream increase in cross entropy loss when SAE activations are inserted into the model. Finally, we examine two methods to reduce nonlinear SAE error: inference time gradient pursuit, which leads to a very slight decrease in nonlinear error, and linear transformations from earlier layer SAE outputs, which leads to a larger reduction.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are the central object of study, used to decompose language model activations into interpretable linear features.

conceptDetails.name: sae dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The unexplained residual error of sparse autoencoders — the paper's central object of study — decomposed into linearly predictable and nonlinear components.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity is a defining constraint of SAEs, enabling decomposition of activations into a small number of active features per token.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Mechanistic interpretability is the overarching goal — understanding neural network internals via feature decomposition — motivating the investigation of SAE dark matter.

conceptDetails.name: linear representation hypothesis
conceptDetails.description: A novel framing central to the paper: the hypothesis that LM activations are sparse sums of linear feature directions, which the authors use to model SAE dark matter.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how language model hidden states are structured and how SAEs learn to represent them, including linear and nonlinear components.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: SAEs extract a dictionary of one-dimensional feature directions from model activations; this paper examines what fraction of variance remains unextracted.

conceptDetails.name: mechanistic interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The broader research program of reverse-engineering neural networks into circuits and features, whose ambitious goals motivate the need to fully account for SAE error.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The linear representation hypothesis — that activations decompose into sparse sums of independent linear directions — is the theoretical foundation the authors build on and test.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key finding is that SAE error scaling at the per-token level is highly predictable, with larger SAEs failing on the same contexts as smaller ones.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: SAEs project high-dimensional activation vectors into sparse low-dimensional feature representations; the error of this reduction is the focus of analysis.

title: Learning Efficient Representations of Neutrino Telescope Events
abstract: Neutrino telescopes detect rare interactions of particles produced in some of the most extreme environments in the Universe. This is accomplished by instrumenting a cubic-kilometer scale volume of naturally occurring transparent medium with light sensors. Given their substantial size and the high frequency of background interactions, these telescopes amass an enormous quantity of large variance, high-dimensional data. These attributes create substantial challenges for analyzing and reconstructing interactions, particularly when utilizing machine learning (ML) techniques. In this paper, we present a novel approach, called om2vec, that employs transformer-based variational autoencoders to efficiently represent the detected photon arrival time distributions of neutrino telescope events by learning compact and descriptive latent representations. We demonstrate that these latent representations offer enhanced flexibility and improved computational efficiency, thereby facilitating downstream tasks in data analysis.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The core architecture of om2vec, used to learn compact latent representations of photon arrival time distributions from neutrino telescope events.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The central contribution of the paper — learning efficient, fixed-size latent representations of variable-length neutrino telescope event data.

conceptDetails.name: photon arrival time encoding
conceptDetails.description: Novel term for the specific task of compressing per-optical-module photon arrival time distributions (PATDs) into fixed-size latent vectors.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Transformer layers are integrated into the VAE encoder-decoder to exploit the sequential nature of photon arrival time distributions.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The application domain — IceCube and next-generation neutrino telescopes that record photon arrival times from rare neutrino interactions.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: om2vec compresses high-dimensional, variable-length photon arrival time distributions into fixed-size compact latent vectors.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Downstream task that benefits from om2vec representations, including prediction of particle energy, direction, and type.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Input embedding layers and learned memory embeddings are used to encode photon arrival time distributions into the latent space.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Attention is used within transformer blocks to capture temporal dependencies in photon arrival time sequences.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key motivation for om2vec is runtime efficiency and scalability to handle the high data rates of current and future neutrino telescopes.

title: Hodge Theory for Entanglement Cohomology
abstract: We explore and extend the application of homological algebra to describe quantum entanglement, initiated in arXiv:1901.02011, focusing on the Hodge-theoretic structure of entanglement cohomology in finite-dimensional quantum systems. We construct analogues of the Hodge star operator, inner product, codifferential, and Laplacian for entanglement $k$-forms. We also prove that such $k$-forms obey versions of the Hodge isomorphism theorem and Hodge decomposition, and that they exhibit Hodge duality. As a corollary, we conclude that the dimensions of the $k$-th and $(n-k)$-th cohomologies coincide for entanglement in $n$-partite pure states, which explains a symmetry property ("Poincare duality") of the associated Poincare polynomials.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper: characterizing and classifying quantum entanglement in multipartite finite-dimensional systems using cohomological and Hodge-theoretic tools.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement cohomology
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central novel framework: a cochain complex structure on quantum states whose cohomology groups encode multipartite entanglement patterns.

conceptDetails.name: hodge decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves a Hodge decomposition theorem for entanglement k-forms, splitting them into harmonic, exact, and co-exact components analogous to differential forms on manifolds.

conceptDetails.name: homological algebra
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical backbone of the work: cochain complexes, cohomology groups, and related algebraic structures are applied to quantum entanglement.

conceptDetails.name: poincaré duality
conceptDetails.description: A novel corollary established in the paper: dimensions of k-th and (n-k)-th entanglement cohomology groups coincide, mirroring classical Poincaré duality.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes finite-dimensional quantum mechanical states, including bipartite and n-partite pure states, as the objects to which entanglement cohomology is applied.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The Hodge Laplacian and its spectral decomposition into harmonic, exact, and co-exact forms is a core mathematical tool developed for entanglement k-forms.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Hodge decomposition of the entanglement complex relies on eigenvalue structure of the Laplacian operator, directly connecting to eigenvalue decomposition methods.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Symmetry and duality properties (Poincaré duality, Hodge duality) of cohomology groups underpin the main results of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The motivation and broader context of entanglement cohomology connects to quantum field theory and quantum gravity, where entanglement patterns are physically significant.

title: Auriga Streams II: orbital properties of tidally disrupting satellites of Milky Way-mass galaxies
abstract: Galaxies like the Milky Way are surrounded by complex populations of satellites at all stages of tidal disruption. In this paper, we present a dynamical study of the disrupting satellite galaxies in the Auriga simulations that are orbiting 28 distinct Milky Way-mass hosts across three resolutions. We find that the satellite galaxy populations are highly disrupted. The majority of satellites that remain fully intact at present day were accreted recently without experiencing more than one pericentre ($n_{\rm peri} \lesssim 1$) and have large apocentres ($r_{\rm apo} \gtrsim 200$ kpc) and pericentres ($r_{\rm peri} \gtrsim 50$ kpc). The remaining satellites have experienced significant tidal disruption and, given full knowledge of the system, would be classified as stellar streams. We find stellar streams in Auriga across the range of pericentres and apocentres of the known Milky Way dwarf galaxy streams and, interestingly, overlapping significantly with the Milky Way intact satellite population. We find no significant change in satellite orbital distributions across resolution. However, we do see substantial halo-to-halo variance of $(r_\text{peri}, r_\text{apo})$ distributions across host galaxies, as well as a dependence of satellite orbits on host halo mass - systems disrupt at larger pericentres and apocentres in more massive hosts. Our results suggest that either cosmological simulations (including, but not limited to, Auriga) are disrupting satellites far too readily, or that the Milky Way's satellites are more disrupted than current imaging surveys have revealed. Future observing facilities and careful mock observations of these systems will be key to revealing the nature of this apparent discrepancy.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Auriga suite of hydrodynamic cosmological simulations provides the primary dataset, with 28 Milky Way-mass host galaxies across three resolutions used to study satellite disruption.

conceptDetails.name: tidal disruption
conceptDetails.description: The central physical process studied: tidal forces from the host galaxy's gravitational potential strip stars from satellite galaxies, forming streams and ultimately phase-mixed halos.

conceptDetails.name: stellar streams
conceptDetails.description: Coherent stellar structures formed from tidally disrupting satellites are catalogued and their orbital properties (pericentre, apocentre) compared between Auriga simulations and observed Milky Way streams.

conceptDetails.name: satellite orbital dynamics
conceptDetails.description: Orbital parameters including pericentre radius, apocentre radius, and number of pericentre passages are computed for all accreted satellites to characterize the disruption history of each system.

conceptDetails.name: halo-to-halo variance
conceptDetails.description: Substantial variation in satellite orbital distributions across the 28 host halos is quantified, highlighting the importance of host environment in determining disruption outcomes.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Accreted satellite systems are classified into three categories — intact satellites, coherent stellar streams, and phase-mixed remnants — based on their morphology and dynamical state.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The ΛCDM paradigm frames the entire study, with host halo mass and dark matter substructure driving satellite accretion histories and disruption rates.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Simulated orbital distributions from Auriga are systematically compared against observed Milky Way satellite and stream populations to test whether current cosmological models reproduce reality.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Orbital integrations and statistical sampling of satellite trajectories are used to characterize the phase-space distributions of disrupting systems across the simulation suite.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Control of Non-Markovian Cellular Population Dynamics
abstract: Many organisms and cell types, from bacteria to cancer cells, exhibit a remarkable ability to adapt to fluctuating environments. Additionally, cells can leverage a memory of past environments to better survive previously-encountered stressors. From a control perspective, this adaptability poses significant challenges in driving cell populations toward extinction, and thus poses an open question with great clinical significance. In this work, we focus on drug dosing in cell populations exhibiting phenotypic plasticity. For specific dynamical models switching between resistant and susceptible states, exact solutions are known. However, when the underlying system parameters are unknown, and for complex memory-based systems, obtaining the optimal solution is currently intractable. To address this challenge, we apply reinforcement learning (RL) to identify informed dosing strategies to control cell populations evolving under novel non-Markovian dynamics. We find that model-free deep RL is able to recover exact solutions and control cell populations even in the presence of long-range temporal dynamics. To further test our approach in more realistic settings, we demonstrate robust RL-based control strategies in environments with measurement noise and dynamic memory strength.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Model-free deep RL is the central method used to discover optimal drug dosing strategies without requiring known system parameters or tractable analytical solutions.

conceptDetails.name: non-markovian control
conceptDetails.description: A novel contribution of the paper: population dynamics with memory-dependent switching rates that violate the Markov assumption, requiring history-aware RL control.

conceptDetails.name: phenotypic switching
conceptDetails.description: The biological mechanism by which cells transition between drug-susceptible and drug-resistant states, constituting the adaptive dynamics the RL agent must control.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The non-Markovian cellular dynamics involve memory kernels and history-dependent phenotypic switching rates that constitute complex stochastic processes.

conceptDetails.name: bang-bang control
conceptDetails.description: Optimal control analysis demonstrates that the optimal drug dosing policy is binary (full dose or no dose), providing a principled simplification of the RL action space.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The RL agent optimizes a reward signal designed to drive cell populations toward extinction through adaptive dosing policies.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: Framestacking and recurrent architectures encode the history of past observations needed to handle the non-Markovian state space.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: RL-based control strategies are validated under measurement noise and varying dynamic memory strength to ensure clinical applicability.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Identifying optimal dosing strategies when underlying population model parameters are unknown constitutes an inverse problem addressed via model-free RL.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Optimal control theory and Hamiltonian formulations are used to derive the bang-bang structure of the optimal dosing policy.

title: Efficient Dictionary Learning with Switch Sparse Autoencoders
abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a recent technique for decomposing neural network activations into human-interpretable features. However, in order for SAEs to identify all features represented in frontier models, it will be necessary to scale them up to very high width, posing a computational challenge. In this work, we introduce Switch Sparse Autoencoders, a novel SAE architecture aimed at reducing the compute cost of training SAEs. Inspired by sparse mixture of experts models, Switch SAEs route activation vectors between smaller "expert" SAEs, enabling SAEs to efficiently scale to many more features. We present experiments comparing Switch SAEs with other SAE architectures, and find that Switch SAEs deliver a substantial Pareto improvement in the reconstruction vs. sparsity frontier for a given fixed training compute budget. We also study the geometry of features across experts, analyze features duplicated across experts, and verify that Switch SAE features are as interpretable as features found by other SAE architectures.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders are the foundational architecture this paper extends, used to decompose neural network activations into interpretable features via dictionary learning.

conceptDetails.name: mixture of experts
conceptDetails.description: The Switch SAE architecture is directly inspired by sparse mixture-of-experts models, routing activation vectors to smaller expert SAEs to reduce compute cost.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity is central to both the SAE training objective and the Switch layer routing mechanism, enabling efficient scaling by activating only a subset of parameters per input.

conceptDetails.name: expert routing
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the trainable routing network that assigns activation vectors to specific expert SAEs is a key architectural innovation controlling compute efficiency and feature specialization.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A primary motivation is mechanistic interpretability — verifying that Switch SAE features are as human-interpretable as those from standard SAE architectures.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses the computational bottleneck of scaling SAEs to the widths needed for frontier models, proposing Switch SAEs as a scalable solution.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Switch SAEs learn structured decompositions of LLM internal representations, mapping hidden states to monosemantic feature dictionaries.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: Switch SAEs aim to disentangle polysemantic neuron activations into monosemantic, human-interpretable features by leveraging superposition theory.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a load-balancing auxiliary loss to ensure uniform expert utilization during Switch SAE training, a critical training design choice.

conceptDetails.name: feature geometry
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paper analyzes the geometric structure of features across experts using t-SNE and cosine similarity, studying duplication and clustering patterns.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Dictionary learning via sparse autoencoders performs a form of dimensionality expansion followed by sparse projection, structurally related to dimensionality reduction techniques.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Switch SAEs are trained on activations from transformer language models, and the paper references attention-based architectures throughout as the target systems being interpreted.

title: The Geometry of Concepts: Sparse Autoencoder Feature Structure
abstract: Sparse autoencoders have recently produced dictionaries of high-dimensional vectors corresponding to the universe of concepts represented by large language models. We find that this concept universe has interesting structure at three levels: 1) The "atomic" small-scale structure contains "crystals" whose faces are parallelograms or trapezoids, generalizing well-known examples such as (man-woman-king-queen). We find that the quality of such parallelograms and associated function vectors improves greatly when projecting out global distractor directions such as word length, which is efficiently done with linear discriminant analysis. 2) The "brain" intermediate-scale structure has significant spatial modularity; for example, math and code features form a "lobe" akin to functional lobes seen in neural fMRI images. We quantify the spatial locality of these lobes with multiple metrics and find that clusters of co-occurring features, at coarse enough scale, also cluster together spatially far more than one would expect if feature geometry were random. 3) The "galaxy" scale large-scale structure of the feature point cloud is not isotropic, but instead has a power law of eigenvalues with steepest slope in middle layers. We also quantify how the clustering entropy depends on the layer.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders are the primary tool used to extract interpretable feature dictionaries from LLM activations, with their geometric structure being the central object of study.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse coding and sparse dictionary learning provide the theoretical foundation for why SAE features represent distinct concepts, with sparsity ensuring only a small subset of features activate at once.

conceptDetails.name: feature geometry
conceptDetails.description: Novel contribution characterizing the multi-scale geometric structure of SAE concept dictionaries, introducing crystal, brain-lobe, and galaxy-scale analogies.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes how large language models internally represent the universe of concepts through high-dimensional feature vectors at multiple geometric scales.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Mechanistic interpretability of LLMs is the primary motivation, using SAE feature geometry to understand and audit internal model representations for AI safety.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: High-dimensional feature vectors (dictionary elements) produced by SAEs form the point clouds whose geometry is analyzed at atomic, brain, and galaxy scales.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Cluster analysis is applied at intermediate scale to identify spatially co-located functional modules (e.g., math/code lobes) and measure clustering entropy across layers.

conceptDetails.name: linear representation hypothesis
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical assumption that neural networks represent features as additive one-dimensional directions in activation space, which the paper empirically probes through SAE geometry.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Eigenvalue analysis of the feature point cloud reveals a power-law spectrum with layer-dependent slope, characterizing the large-scale anisotropic 'galaxy' structure.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral analysis of the covariance structure of SAE features is used to quantify the global geometry and isotropy of the feature space.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Linear discriminant analysis is applied to project out global distractor directions (e.g., word length), improving the quality of detected parallelogram analogies.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The paper tests whether SAE features form cleanly separable geometric structures (parallelograms, functional lobes) analogous to disentangled latent factors.

title: Dirac Traces and the Tutte Polynomial
abstract: Perturbative calculations involving fermion loops in quantum field theories require tracing over Dirac matrices. A simple way to regulate the divergences that generically appear in these calculations is dimensional regularisation, which has the consequence of replacing 4-dimensional Dirac matrices with d-dimensional counterparts for arbitrary complex values of d. In this work, a connection between traces of d-dimensional Dirac matrices and computations of the Tutte polynomial of associated graphs is proven. The time complexity of computing Dirac traces is analysed by this connection, and improvements to algorithms for computing Dirac traces are proposed.

conceptDetails.name: tutte polynomial
conceptDetails.description: The central mathematical object of the paper: d-dimensional Dirac traces are proven to equal specific evaluations of the Tutte polynomial of an associated intersection graph.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within QFT, specifically addressing fermion loop calculations in perturbative expansions of QFTs like QED.

conceptDetails.name: deletion-contraction
conceptDetails.description: The anticommutation relations of d-dimensional Dirac matrices are reformulated as a deletion-contraction recurrence on graphs, directly mirroring the defining recursion of the Tutte polynomial.

conceptDetails.name: dimensional regularisation
conceptDetails.description: The technique of analytically continuing spacetime dimension to complex d that motivates the study of d-dimensional Dirac traces and their polynomial structure.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Dirac traces arise in the computation of Feynman diagrams contributing to scattering amplitudes, such as the two-loop QED photon vacuum polarization.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Dimensional regularisation is used to regulate divergences in loop integrals, a core technique in renormalization of QFTs.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Graph-theoretic spectral concepts such as the bicycle space and connected components of intersection graphs underpin the main theorems relating Dirac traces to Tutte polynomial evaluations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Runtime comparisons between Tutte-polynomial-based and existing computer algebra Dirac trace implementations were benchmarked over randomly contracted gamma-matrix strings.

title: Investigating Representation Universality: Case Study on Genealogical Representations
abstract: Motivated by interpretability and reliability, we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) deploy universal geometric structures to encode discrete, graph-structured knowledge. To this end, we present two complementary experimental evidence that might support universality of graph representations. First, on an in-context genealogy Q&A task, we train a cone probe to isolate a tree-like subspace in residual stream activations and use activation patching to verify its causal effect in answering related questions. We validate our findings across five different models. Second, we conduct model stitching experiments across models of diverse architectures and parameter counts (OPT, Pythia, Mistral, and LLaMA, 410 million to 8 billion parameters), quantifying representational alignment via relative degradation in the next-token prediction loss. Generally, we conclude that the lack of ground truth representations of graphs makes it challenging to study how LLMs represent them. Ultimately, improving our understanding of LLM representations could facilitate the development of more interpretable, robust, and controllable AI systems.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates what geometric representations LLMs learn to encode graph-structured knowledge, specifically testing whether these representations are universal across architectures.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the geometric structure of object embeddings in LLM residual streams, proposing cone embeddings as the optimal representation for tree-structured relationships.

conceptDetails.name: cone embedding
conceptDetails.description: A novel geometric structure proposed as the optimal representation for tree-structured (descendant-of) relationships, where a node's descendants lie within a fixed cone emanating from it.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Mechanistic interpretability is the primary motivation, seeking to understand how LLMs internally encode relational knowledge to improve transparency and reliability.

conceptDetails.name: activation patching
conceptDetails.description: A causal intervention technique used to verify that the identified tree-like subspace in residual stream activations causally mediates genealogical question answering.

conceptDetails.name: model stitching
conceptDetails.description: A black-box cross-model alignment technique that splices layers from different LLMs via linear adapters to quantify representational similarity via degradation in next-token prediction loss.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies transformer-based LLMs (OPT, Pythia, Mistral, LLaMA) as the architectural substrate for investigating universal graph representations.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes the geometric manifold structure (cone embeddings) that emerges in LLM representation spaces for encoding hierarchical knowledge.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: PCA is applied to learned embeddings to visualize and analyze the two-dimensional geometric structure of cone embeddings across random seeds.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Model stitching uses trainable linear adapters to splice early layers of one model onto late layers of another, probing representational alignment across architectures.

title: RelitLRM: Generative Relightable Radiance for Large Reconstruction Models
abstract: We propose RelitLRM, a Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) for generating high-quality Gaussian splatting representations of 3D objects under novel illuminations from sparse (4-8) posed images captured under unknown static lighting. Unlike prior inverse rendering methods requiring dense captures and slow optimization, often causing artifacts like incorrect highlights or shadow baking, RelitLRM adopts a feed-forward transformer-based model with a novel combination of a geometry reconstructor and a relightable appearance generator based on diffusion. The model is trained end-to-end on synthetic multi-view renderings of objects under varying known illuminations. This architecture design enables to effectively decompose geometry and appearance, resolve the ambiguity between material and lighting, and capture the multi-modal distribution of shadows and specularity in the relit appearance. We show our sparse-view feed-forward RelitLRM offers competitive relighting results to state-of-the-art dense-view optimization-based baselines while being significantly faster. Our project page is available at: https://relit-lrm.github.io/.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Core architecture for both the geometry reconstructor and appearance generator, processing tokenized multi-view images and lighting conditions.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Used as the appearance generator component to model the multi-modal distribution of relit appearances including shadows and specular highlights.

conceptDetails.name: 3d gaussian splatting
conceptDetails.description: The output representation of the model — 3D Gaussian primitives encode geometry and appearance for fast differentiable rendering under novel illuminations.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The architecture explicitly disentangles geometry, appearance, and illumination to resolve material-lighting ambiguities in inverse rendering.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The overall framework is a generative approach combining regression-based geometry and probabilistic appearance synthesis for relightable 3D reconstruction.
