conceptDetails.name: feed-forward reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Unlike optimization-based inverse rendering, RelitLRM performs a single forward pass to reconstruct relightable objects in approximately one second.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Relightable 3D reconstruction from sparse images is a classic inverse problem requiring recovery of geometry, material, and lighting from observations.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Cross-attention and self-attention are used within the transformer blocks to aggregate multi-view image tokens into 3D Gaussian parameters.

conceptDetails.name: novel view synthesis
conceptDetails.description: RelitLRM enables rendering from arbitrary viewpoints under novel lighting by reconstructing a full 3D Gaussian scene representation.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The model is pretrained on large-scale synthetic datasets and transferred to real-world sparse-view relighting scenarios.

title: Transformers are Efficient Compilers, Provably
abstract: Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated surprisingly robust performance across a wide range of language-related tasks, including programming language understanding and generation. In this paper, we take the first steps towards a formal investigation of using transformers as compilers from an expressive power perspective. To this end, we introduce a representative programming language, Mini-Husky, which encapsulates key features of modern C-like languages. We show that if the input code sequence has a bounded depth in both the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) and type inference (reasonable assumptions based on the clean code principle), then the number of parameters required by transformers depends only on the logarithm of the input sequence length to handle compilation tasks, such as AST construction, symbol resolution, and type analysis. A significant technical challenge stems from the fact that transformers operate at a low level, where each layer processes the input sequence as raw vectors without explicitly associating them with predefined structure or meaning. In contrast, high-level compiler tasks necessitate managing intricate relationships and structured program information. Our primary technical contribution is the development of a domain-specific language, Cybertron, which generates formal proofs of the transformer's expressive power, scaling to address compiler tasks. We further establish that recurrent neural networks (RNNs) require at least a linear number of parameters relative to the input sequence, leading to an exponential separation between transformers and RNNs. Finally, we empirically validate our theoretical results by comparing transformers and RNNs on compiler tasks within Mini-Husky.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The paper formally proves transformers can perform compilation tasks (AST construction, symbol resolution, type analysis) with logarithmic parameter scaling.

conceptDetails.name: expressive power theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes the expressive power of transformers for compiler tasks, proving logarithmic vs. linear parameter separation from RNNs.

conceptDetails.name: domain-specific language for proofs
conceptDetails.description: Cybertron, a novel DSL, is introduced as a proof vehicle to formally verify transformer expressivity for compiler tasks.

conceptDetails.name: formal expressivity bounds
conceptDetails.description: The paper establishes provable upper bounds (transformers) and lower bounds (RNNs) on parameters needed for compilation, representing a new theoretical framework.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Self-attention is the core mechanism analyzed to show how transformers efficiently handle structured program information.

conceptDetails.name: abstract syntax tree compilation
conceptDetails.description: AST construction is one of the three core compilation tasks proven to be efficiently solvable by transformers.

conceptDetails.name: type inference analysis
conceptDetails.description: Type inference and type checking are the hardest compilation tasks studied, and the source of the exponential transformer-RNN separation.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: RNNs are compared against transformers, with a proven lower bound showing they require linearly many parameters for type analysis.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central result is that transformer parameter counts scale only logarithmically with input sequence length under bounded AST/type inference depth.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The work aims to deepen understanding of what transformers can formally compute, contributing to mechanistic interpretability of LLMs on structured tasks.

title: SPECTER: Efficient Evaluation of the Spectral EMD
abstract: The Energy Mover's Distance (EMD) has seen use in collider physics as a metric between events and as a geometric method of defining infrared and collinear safe observables. Recently, the Spectral Energy Mover's Distance (SEMD) has been proposed as a more analytically tractable alternative to the EMD. In this work, we obtain a closed-form expression for the Riemannian-like p = 2 SEMD metric between events, eliminating the need to numerically solve an optimal transport problem. Additionally, we show how the SEMD can be used to define event and jet shape observables by minimizing the distance between events and parameterized energy flows (similar to the EMD), and we obtain closed-form expressions for several of these observables. We also present the SPECTER framework, an efficient and highly parallelized implementation of the SEMD metric and SEMD-derived shape observables as an analogue of the previously-introduced SHAPER for EMD-based computations. We demonstrate that computing the SEMD with SPECTER can be up to a thousand times faster than computing the EMD with standard optimal transport libraries.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The SEMD is defined through optimal transport between spectral function representations of events, and the paper's core contribution is eliminating the need to numerically solve this problem via a closed-form expression.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The SEMD and SPECTER framework are developed specifically for particle physics collider events, with applications to LHC data analysis and jet/event characterization.

conceptDetails.name: spectral event metric
conceptDetails.description: The p=2 Spectral Energy Mover's Distance is the novel metric introduced in this paper, providing the first closed-form, numerically exact metric between collision events.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives closed-form spectral jet shape observables including spRinginess, spLineliness, and sDiskiness for characterizing jet substructure.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The Spectral EMD is built on spectral function representations of collision events, encoding pairwise particle angles and energy products as one-dimensional distributions.

conceptDetails.name: irc-safe observables
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs a family of infrared and collinear safe event and jet shape observables (sThrust, spIsotropy, N-sPronginess, etc.) derived from the SEMD.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: SPECTER achieves up to 1000x speedup over standard optimal transport libraries through parallelized GPU implementation and O(N² log N) algorithm complexity.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: SPECTER provides efficient reconstruction and characterization of collision events through SEMD-based distance metrics and shape observables.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: IRC-safe observables derived from the SEMD are calculable in perturbative QCD, connecting the geometric framework to quantum field theory predictions.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The spectral representation is automatically invariant to isometries of compared events, preserving the geometric symmetries of the celestial sphere.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The SEMD defines a Riemannian-like metric geometry on the space of collider events, enabling geometric analysis of the event manifold.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: SPECTER serves as an efficient surrogate for optimal transport computations, enabling applications like gradient descent parameter optimization and trigger-level studies.

title: Exploring gauge-fixing conditions with gradient-based optimization
abstract: Lattice gauge fixing is required to compute gauge-variant quantities, for example those used in RI-MOM renormalization schemes or as objects of comparison for model calculations. Recently, gauge-variant quantities have also been found to be more amenable to signal-to-noise optimization using contour deformations. These applications motivate systematic parameterization and exploration of gauge-fixing schemes. This work introduces a differentiable parameterization of gauge fixing which is broad enough to cover Landau gauge, Coulomb gauge, and maximal tree gauges. The adjoint state method allows gradient-based optimization to select gauge-fixing schemes that minimize an arbitrary target loss function.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within lattice gauge theory, developing new gauge-fixing methods for use in lattice field theory calculations.

conceptDetails.name: adjoint state method
conceptDetails.description: The adjoint state method is the central algorithmic contribution, enabling efficient gradient computation through implicit gauge-fixing constraints.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable gauge fixing
conceptDetails.description: A novel differentiable parameterization of gauge-fixing schemes spanning Landau, Coulomb, and maximal tree gauges is introduced.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces gradient-based optimization with an arbitrary differentiable loss function to select optimal gauge-fixing schemes.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Gauge fixing is required for RI-MOM renormalization schemes, one of the primary motivations for this work.

conceptDetails.name: signal-to-noise optimization
conceptDetails.description: Improving statistical signal-to-noise ratios in lattice calculations via optimized gauge fixing is a key application target.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Selecting optimal gauge-fixing parameters to minimize a target loss is framed as an inverse/regression problem solved via gradient methods.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The gauge-fixing schemes explored are applied in the context of lattice Monte Carlo calculations to reduce statistical noise.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The differentiable parameterization and adjoint state method reflect physics-informed, gradient-based optimization approaches.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Contour deformations related to normalizing flows are cited as motivation for improved gauge-fixing schemes to reduce signal-to-noise problems.

title: Formation of Representations in Neural Networks
abstract: Understanding neural representations will help open the black box of neural networks and advance our scientific understanding of modern AI systems. However, how complex, structured, and transferable representations emerge in modern neural networks has remained a mystery. Building on previous results, we propose the Canonical Representation Hypothesis (CRH), which posits a set of six alignment relations to universally govern the formation of representations in most hidden layers of a neural network. Under the CRH, the latent representations (R), weights (W), and neuron gradients (G) become mutually aligned during training. This alignment implies that neural networks naturally learn compact representations, where neurons and weights are invariant to task-irrelevant transformations. We then show that the breaking of CRH leads to the emergence of reciprocal power-law relations between R, W, and G, which we refer to as the Polynomial Alignment Hypothesis (PAH). We present a minimal-assumption theory proving that the balance between gradient noise and regularization is crucial for the emergence of the canonical representation. The CRH and PAH lead to an exciting possibility of unifying major key deep learning phenomena, including neural collapse and the neural feature ansatz, in a single framework.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central focus is explaining how structured, compact, and transferable representations emerge in neural network hidden layers during training.

conceptDetails.name: canonical representation hypothesis
conceptDetails.description: A novel theoretical framework proposing six alignment relations between latent representations, weights, and neuron gradients that universally govern representation formation.

conceptDetails.name: polynomial alignment hypothesis
conceptDetails.description: A novel hypothesis describing reciprocal power-law relations between representations, weights, and gradients that emerge when the canonical representation breaks.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The CRH framework advances interpretability by revealing universal alignment equations that govern internal neural network structure, helping open the black box of deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how neurons and weights extract task-relevant features while becoming invariant to task-irrelevant transformations under the canonical representation.

conceptDetails.name: neural collapse
conceptDetails.description: An existing phenomenon that the CRH generalizes, where structured low-rank representations emerge in classification models at the penultimate layer.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Spectral analysis via eigenvalue decomposition of representation, weight, and gradient covariance matrices underpins the alignment relations in the CRH.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The Polynomial Alignment Hypothesis identifies power-law scaling relations between representations, weights, and gradients across network layers and scales.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The balance between gradient noise and regularization in the loss landscape is proven to be crucial for the emergence of canonical representations.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The compactness and invariance properties of canonical representations directly relate to why representations transfer across tasks and architectures.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The neural feature ansatz and neural tangent kernel literature are unified under the CRH framework, connecting kernel-based analysis to representation alignment.

title: ${T\overline{T}}$-like Flows of Yang-Mills Theories
abstract: We study ${T\overline{T}}$-like deformations of $d>2$ Yang-Mills theories. The standard ${T\overline{T}}$ flows lead to multi-trace Lagrangians, and the non-Abelian gauge structures make it challenging to find Lagrangians in a closed form. However, within the geometric approach to ${T\overline{T}}$, we obtain the closed-form solution to the metric flow and stress-energy tensor, and show that instanton solutions are undeformed. We also introduce new symmetrised single-trace ${T\overline{T}}$-like deformations, whose solutions in $d=4$ include the non-Abelian Born-Infeld Lagrangian proposed by Tseytlin in 1997.

conceptDetails.name: tt-bar deformation
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: The core method — an irrelevant deformation of field theories driven by a bilinear of stress-energy tensors — extended here to non-Abelian Yang-Mills in d>2.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates entirely within QFT, studying deformations of Yang-Mills gauge theories through stress-energy tensor flows in arbitrary spacetime dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The central challenge is finding closed-form Lagrangians for TT-bar-deformed Yang-Mills theories; the geometric approach to metric flows provides the key solution strategy.

conceptDetails.name: born-infeld theory
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: The non-linear gauge theory that emerges as the solution to TT-bar flows; the paper recovers the non-Abelian Born-Infeld Lagrangian proposed by Tseytlin via symmetrized single-trace deformations.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Non-Abelian gauge group structure (structure constants, traces, adjoint representations) is central to the algebraic complexity of the deformation and distinguishes this work from the Abelian case.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: TT-bar deformations originated as irrelevant deformations of 2d CFTs; the paper generalizes these to higher-dimensional non-Abelian gauge theories, with the 2d case providing key benchmarks.

conceptDetails.name: instanton solutions
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: Self-dual topological solutions of Yang-Mills theory shown to be undeformed by TT-bar flows, providing a key structural result about the deformation.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Born-Infeld theory arises as the open string effective action, and Tseytlin's 1997 non-Abelian BI proposal (recovered here) was motivated by string theory considerations.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge invariance and Lorentz symmetry must be maintained throughout the TT-bar flow; the paper systematically constructs gauge-invariant Lorentz scalars as the basis for the deformed Lagrangians.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The deformed Yang-Mills Lagrangians constitute non-linear EFTs organized as perturbative expansions in the deformation parameter λ.

title: Optimal Quantum Purity Amplification
abstract: Quantum purity amplification (QPA) provides a novel approach to counteracting the pervasive noise that degrades quantum states. We present the optimal QPA protocol for general quantum systems and global noise, resolving a two-decade open problem. Under strong depolarization, our protocol achieves an exponential reduction in sample complexity over the best-known methods. We provide an efficient implementation of the protocol based on generalized quantum phase estimation. Additionally, we introduce SWAPNET, a sparse and shallow circuit that enables QPA for near-term experiments. Simulations in both digital and analog quantum settings, along with experiments on superconducting quantum processors, confirm the protocol's robustness and practical utility. Our findings suggest that QPA could improve the performance of quantum information processing tasks, particularly in the context of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices, where reducing the effect of noise with limited resources is critical.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops optimal quantum purity amplification protocols for NISQ quantum processors, directly advancing quantum computing capabilities.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: QPA operates on noisy quantum states to amplify their purity, with the paper deriving optimal protocols for general d-dimensional mixed states.

conceptDetails.name: quantum purity amplification
conceptDetails.description: The central novel contribution: an optimal protocol that consumes multiple copies of noisy quantum states to produce higher-purity output states, resolving a two-decade open problem.

conceptDetails.name: depolarizing noise
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes QPA under strong depolarization, achieving exponential reduction in sample complexity over prior methods.

conceptDetails.name: sample complexity
conceptDetails.description: A key theoretical result is deriving optimal sample complexity in terms of infidelity for generic noisy inputs beyond depolarizing noise.

conceptDetails.name: sparse circuit design
conceptDetails.description: SWAPNET is introduced as a sparse and shallow quantum circuit enabling near-term experimental implementation of QPA.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper validates SWAPNET and QPA protocols through both digital and analog quantum simulations on superconducting processors.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The protocol leverages unitary invariance and Schur-Weyl duality to convert the optimization into a symmetric, tractable form.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The optimal protocol involves Schur sampling and spectral decomposition of mixed states to identify and amplify the principal eigenstate.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: QPA uses entangled operations across multiple copies of quantum states to extract and amplify coherent information.

conceptDetails.name: physics-motivated optimization
conceptDetails.description: The optimization of the QPA channel is cast as a semidefinite program (SDP) exploiting unitary symmetry and combinatorial structure.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses the challenge of scaling QPA to qubit hardware and near-term devices with limited resources.

title: Seeing Faces in Things: A Model and Dataset for Pareidolia
abstract: The human visual system is well-tuned to detect faces of all shapes and sizes. While this brings obvious survival advantages, such as a better chance of spotting unknown predators in the bush, it also leads to spurious face detections. ``Face pareidolia'' describes the perception of face-like structure among otherwise random stimuli: seeing faces in coffee stains or clouds in the sky. In this paper, we study face pareidolia from a computer vision perspective. We present an image dataset of ``Faces in Things'', consisting of five thousand web images with human-annotated pareidolic faces. Using this dataset, we examine the extent to which a state-of-the-art human face detector exhibits pareidolia, and find a significant behavioral gap between humans and machines. We find that the evolutionary need for humans to detect animal faces, as well as human faces, may explain some of this gap. Finally, we propose a simple statistical model of pareidolia in images. Through studies on human subjects and our pareidolic face detectors we confirm a key prediction of our model regarding what image conditions are most likely to induce pareidolia. Dataset and Website: https://aka.ms/faces-in-things

conceptDetails.name: face pareidolia
conceptDetails.description: The core phenomenon studied: the tendency to perceive face-like structure in random or unrelated stimuli such as clouds, coffee stains, and textured surfaces.

conceptDetails.name: human-machine perceptual gap
conceptDetails.description: A novel quantification of the behavioral discrepancy between human and machine pareidolic face detection, shown to be partially explained by animal face training.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: RetinaFace and other CNN-based detectors trained on WIDER FACE are used as the primary machine face detection baselines evaluated for pareidolic behavior.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Fine-tuning RetinaFace on the Faces in Things training set is the main supervised approach to boosting pareidolic face detection performance.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Models fine-tuned on animal face datasets show improved pareidolic detection, suggesting evolutionary cross-species face generalization as a transfer mechanism.

conceptDetails.name: visual psychophysics
conceptDetails.description: Controlled human subject experiments validate the Goldilocks zone prediction of the statistical model, linking computational predictions to human perceptual behavior.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: A central finding is the significant behavioral robustness gap between humans and state-of-the-art detectors on pareidolic stimuli even at relaxed detection thresholds.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Mid-level learned features from human face detectors are found to partially generalize to pareidolic facial keypoints, bridging human and machine pareidolia.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates what learned visual representations enable or inhibit pareidolic face perception in neural networks compared to humans.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: A Gaussian process model is proposed to characterize the statistical image conditions that constitute a 'Goldilocks zone' for inducing pareidolia.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Face detection is framed as a binary classification task, and performance metrics on pareidolic versus non-pareidolic images are compared across human and machine detectors.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Image augmentation techniques are tested as one of several interventions to improve machine pareidolic detection without supervised pareidolic training data.

title: StreamGen: Connecting Populations of Streams and Shells to Their Host Galaxies
abstract: In this work, we study how the abundance and dynamics of populations of disrupting satellite galaxies change systematically as a function of host galaxy properties. We apply a theoretical model of the phase-mixing process to classify intact satellite galaxies, stellar stream-like and shell-like debris in ~1500 Milky Way-mass systems generated by a semi-analytic galaxy formation code, SatGen. In particular, we test the effect of host galaxy halo mass, disk mass, ratio of disk scale height to length, and stellar feedback model on disrupting satellite populations. We find that the counts of tidal debris are consistent across all host galaxy models, within a given host mass range, and that all models can have stream-like debris on low-energy orbits, consistent with those observed around the Milky Way. However, we find a preference for stream-like debris on lower-energy orbits in models with a thicker (lower-density) host disk or on higher-energy orbits in models with a more-massive host disk. Importantly, we observe significant halo-to-halo variance across all models. These results highlight the importance of simulating and observing large samples of Milky Way-mass galaxies and accounting for variations in host properties when using disrupting satellites in studies of near-field cosmology.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses SatGen, a semi-analytic galaxy formation code, to generate ~1500 Milky Way-mass systems for studying satellite disruption populations.

conceptDetails.name: stellar tidal streams
conceptDetails.description: Coherent structures of tidally stripped stars following progenitor orbits, the primary observational phenomenon being modeled and classified in this work.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: A phase-mixing theoretical model is applied to classify tidal debris into intact satellites, stream-like, and shell-like morphological categories.

conceptDetails.name: phase-space mixing
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework of phase-mixing as a function of orbit energy and interaction time is the core model used to distinguish stream-like from shell-like debris.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Tidal disruption rates and debris morphology are used to constrain dark matter properties and density profiles of satellite galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: SatGen serves as a computationally efficient surrogate for expensive cosmological hydrodynamical simulations, enabling rapid exploration of host galaxy parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: near-field cosmology
conceptDetails.description: The application domain in which disrupting satellites and tidal debris around Milky Way-mass galaxies serve as probes of small-scale structure and dark matter physics.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly quantifies halo-to-halo variance and systematic uncertainties arising from host disk mass, disk geometry, and stellar feedback models.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The semi-analytic framework emulates the orbital and disruption physics of satellite galaxies to reproduce observable debris populations without full N-body integration.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Statistical sampling across ~1500 independent galaxy realizations is used to characterize halo-to-halo variance in tidal debris populations.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Halo-to-halo variance and the stochastic nature of satellite accretion histories are central to interpreting differences between simulated and observed stream populations.

title: A Field Guide to Event-Shape Observables Using Optimal Transport
abstract: We lay out the phenomenological behavior of event-shape observables evaluated by solving optimal transport problems between collider events and reference geometries -- which we name 'manifold distances' -- to provide guidance regarding their use in future studies. This discussion considers several choices related to the metric used to quantify these distances. We explore the differences between the various options, using a combination of analytical studies and simulated minimum-bias and multi-jet events. Making judicious choices when defining the metric and reference geometry can improve sensitivity to interesting signal features and reduce sensitivity to non-perturbative effects in QCD. The goal of this article is to provide a 'field guide' that can inform how choices made when defining a manifold distance can be tailored for the analysis at-hand.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The Earth Mover's/Wasserstein distance framework is the mathematical foundation for all event-shape observables defined in this paper, computing minimum-work transport maps between collider events and reference geometries.

conceptDetails.name: manifold distances
conceptDetails.description: The paper's coined term for event-shape observables computed as EMD distances between a collider event and a symmetric reference geometry on an event manifold.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies phenomenological behavior of event-shape observables in high-energy proton-proton collisions at the LHC, analyzing minimum-bias and multi-jet events.

conceptDetails.name: energy mover's distance
conceptDetails.description: The particle-physics adaptation of the Wasserstein/Earth Mover's Distance that serves as the computational engine for all manifold distance observables in this work.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Multi-jet final states and jet substructure are central analysis targets, with jet multiplicity and topology used to benchmark observable sensitivity.

conceptDetails.name: event isotropy
conceptDetails.description: A specific manifold distance observable measuring how close an event's radiation pattern is to a uniform isotropic distribution, studied extensively as a key benchmark case.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Sensitivity to beyond-Standard-Model signatures—including SUEPs, black holes, and SUSY multi-jet topologies—motivates the development of tailored manifold distance observables.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Simulated minimum-bias and multi-jet events from Parton Shower Monte Carlo generators are used to benchmark and compare observable distributions.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: QCD properties including the strong coupling αs, hadronization, and non-perturbative effects are the primary Standard Model physics characterized through these observables.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Reference geometries with specific symmetries—isotropic uniform rings and back-to-back dipole configurations—are chosen to encode physical symmetry priors into the observables.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The EMD framework is contextualized within unsupervised anomaly detection and novel triggering strategies at the LHC as a key downstream application.

title: Conformal Fields from Neural Networks
abstract: We use the embedding formalism to construct conformal fields in $D$ dimensions, by restricting Lorentz-invariant ensembles of homogeneous neural networks in $(D+2)$ dimensions to the projective null cone. Conformal correlators may be computed using the parameter space description of the neural network. Exact four-point correlators are computed in a number of examples, and we perform a 4D conformal block decomposition that elucidates the spectrum. In some examples the analysis is facilitated by recent approaches to Feynman integrals. Generalized free CFTs are constructed using the infinite-width Gaussian process limit of the neural network, enabling a realization of the free boson. The extension to deep networks constructs conformal fields at each subsequent layer, with recursion relations relating their conformal dimensions and four-point functions. Numerical approaches are discussed.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is constructing conformal fields and computing conformal correlators using neural network architectures restricted to the projective null cone.

conceptDetails.name: neural network field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a novel use of neural network field theory — defining fields via parameter-space ensembles — to directly construct conformal fields through the embedding formalism.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Neural network field theory is used as the underlying framework, defining partition functions and expectation values for homogeneous fields in (D+2)-dimensional Minkowski space.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Lorentz invariance of the neural network ensembles in (D+2) dimensions is the key symmetry constraint that generates conformal symmetry upon restriction to the projective null cone.

conceptDetails.name: conformal block decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Four-point correlators computed from neural network architectures are decomposed into conformal blocks to extract OPE coefficients and operator spectra.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The SO(D+1,1) conformal group structure and its identification with the Lorentz group via the embedding formalism is central to the paper's mathematical framework.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The Gaussian process limit of infinite-width neural networks is used to construct generalized free CFTs and realize the free boson.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Modern Feynman integral and amplitudes techniques are applied to compute exact four-point correlators in the neural network CFT construction.

conceptDetails.name: deep network recursion
conceptDetails.description: Deep neural networks generate towers of conformal fields layer by layer, with recursion relations connecting conformal dimensions and four-point functions across layers.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Renormalization group flows between CFT fixed points provide context for the paper's construction, with conformal dimensions playing the role of critical exponents.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Numerical approaches using Monte Carlo sampling over neural network parameter space are discussed for computing conformal correlators.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: CFTs describing phase transitions such as the Wilson-Fisher fixed point motivate the physical relevance of the constructions presented.

title: Full-shape analysis with simulation-based priors: cosmological parameters and the structure growth anomaly
abstract: We explore full-shape analysis with simulation-based priors, which is the simplest approach to galaxy clustering data analysis that combines effective field theory (EFT) on large scales and numerical simulations on small scales. The core ingredient of our approach is the prior density of EFT parameters which we extract from a suite of 10500 galaxy simulations based on the halo occupation distribution (HOD) model. We measure the EFT parameters with the field-level forward model, which enables us to cancel cosmic variance. On the theory side, we develop a new efficient approach to calculate field-level transfer functions using time-sliced perturbation theory and the logarithmic fast Fourier transform. We find that the cosmology dependence of EFT parameters of galaxies is approximately degenerate with the HOD parameters, and hence it can be ignored for the purpose of prior generation. We use neural density estimation to model the measured distribution of EFT parameters. Our distribution model is then used as a prior in a reanalysis of the BOSS full-shape galaxy power spectrum data. Assuming the $Λ$CDM model, we find significant ($\approx 30\%$ and $\approx 60\%$) improvements for the matter density fraction and the mass fluctuation amplitude, which are constrained to $Ω_{m}= 0.315 \pm 0.010$ and $σ_8 = 0.671 \pm 0.027$. The value of the Hubble constant does not change, $H_0= 68.7\pm 1.1$~km/s/Mpc. This reaffirms earlier reports of the structure growth tension from the BOSS data. Finally, we use the measured EFT parameters to constrain the galaxy-dark matter connection.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core approach combines EFT with simulation-based priors extracted from 10500 HOD galaxy simulations to improve cosmological parameter constraints.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: EFT of large scale structure provides the theoretical framework for modeling galaxy clustering on large scales, with parameters constrained by simulation-based priors.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: A suite of 10500 galaxy simulations based on the HOD model is used to extract the prior density of EFT parameters.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Neural density estimation is used to model the measured distribution of EFT parameters from simulations, which then serves as a prior in the BOSS data analysis.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The method uses simulation-derived prior distributions in a Bayesian reanalysis of the BOSS galaxy power spectrum to improve posterior constraints on cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy power spectrum
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the galaxy clustering power spectrum from BOSS data is the primary observable used in the full-shape analysis.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper improves posterior estimates of matter density fraction and mass fluctuation amplitude by incorporating simulation-based priors.

conceptDetails.name: structure growth tension
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the σ8 tension between BOSS large-scale structure data and CMB predictions, which this analysis reaffirms at improved precision.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The halo occupation distribution framework describes galaxy-dark matter connections, and the analysis constrains the galaxy-dark matter connection using measured EFT parameters.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: The ΛCDM model with cosmological constant is the assumed framework, and the analysis constrains Ωm and σ8 relevant to the dark energy sector.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC sampling is used throughout the parameter inference pipeline for both EFT parameter measurement and cosmological constraints.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The field-level forward model with time-sliced perturbation theory and logarithmic FFT serves as an efficient surrogate for computing transfer functions.

title: Unveiling the Diversity of Type IIn Supernovae via Systematic Light Curve Modeling
abstract: Type IIn supernovae (SNeIIn) are a highly heterogeneous subclass of core-collapse supernovae, spectroscopically characterized by signatures of interaction with a dense circumstellar medium (CSM). Here we systematically model the light curves of 142 archival SNeIIn using MOSFiT (the Modular Open Source Fitter for Transients). We find that the observed and inferred properties of SNIIn are diverse, but there are some trends. The typical SN CSM is dense ($\sim$10$^{-12}$gcm$^{-3}$) with highly diverse CSM geometry, with a median CSM mass of $\sim$1M$_\odot$. The ejecta are typically massive ($\gtrsim10$M$_\odot$), suggesting massive progenitor systems. We find positive correlations between the CSM mass and the rise and fall times of SNeIIn. Furthermore there are positive correlations between the rise time and fall times and the $r$-band luminosity. We estimate the mass-loss rates of our sample (where spectroscopy is available) and find a high median mass-loss rate of $\sim$10$^{-2}$M$_\odot$yr$^{-1}$, with a range between 10$^{-4}$--1M$_\odot$yr$^{-1}$. These mass-loss rates are most similar to the mass loss from great eruptions of luminous blue variables, consistent with the direct progenitor detections in the literature. We also discuss the role that binary interactions may play, concluding that at least some of our SNeIIn may be from massive binary systems. Finally, we estimate a detection rate of 1.6$\times$10$^5$yr$^{-1}$ in the upcoming Legacy Survey of Space and Time at the Vera C. Rubin Observatory.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on Type IIn supernovae as a heterogeneous subclass of core-collapse supernovae, analyzing their photometric and spectroscopic properties.

conceptDetails.name: csm interaction modeling
conceptDetails.description: A novel aspect of this work is the systematic application of circumstellar medium interaction light curve models to the largest archival SNe IIn sample to date.

conceptDetails.name: mass-loss rate inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a methodology to estimate progenitor mass-loss rates from fitted CSM parameters and spectroscopic wind velocities across a large sample.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The study investigates progenitor systems including luminous blue variables, red supergiants, and binary systems to understand the stellar evolution pathways leading to SNe IIn.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: MOSFiT uses Bayesian inference with MCMC sampling to fit light curve models and infer posterior distributions of physical parameters for each supernova.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives posterior probability distributions for CSM mass, ejecta mass, mass-loss rates, and other physical parameters from the light curve fits.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: MOSFiT employs semi-analytic models as surrogate representations of supernova light curve physics to enable efficient fitting across 142 events.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC techniques are used within MOSFiT to sample the posterior parameter space for each of the 142 SNe IIn light curves.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Correlation analyses are performed between inferred CSM properties, rise/fall times, and luminosities to identify statistical trends across the sample.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The systematic light curve modeling approach uses forward-modeled transient simulations fit to observed data to infer physical parameters of the CSM interaction.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The paper validates CSM interaction models by comparing inferred mass-loss rates and progenitor properties against direct progenitor detections and environmental studies.

conceptDetails.name: transient survey forecasting
conceptDetails.description: The study estimates a detection rate of 1.6×10^5 yr^-1 for SNe IIn in the upcoming LSST/Rubin Observatory survey based on the inferred population properties.

title: Auxiliary Field Deformations of (Semi-)Symmetric Space Sigma Models
abstract: We generalize the auxiliary field deformations of the principal chiral model (PCM) introduced in arXiv:2405.05899 and arXiv:2407.16338 to sigma models whose target manifolds are symmetric or semi-symmetric spaces, including a Wess-Zumino term in the latter case. This gives rise to a new infinite family of classically integrable $\mathbb{Z}_2$ and $\mathbb{Z}_4$ coset models of the form which are of interest in applications of integrability to worldsheet string theory and holography. We demonstrate that every theory in this infinite class admits a zero-curvature representation for its equations of motion by exhibiting a Lax connection.

conceptDetails.name: integrable deformations
conceptDetails.description: The central contribution is a new infinite family of classically integrable deformations of symmetric and semi-symmetric space sigma models via auxiliary fields.

conceptDetails.name: lax connection
conceptDetails.description: A zero-curvature Lax connection is explicitly constructed for every theory in the new family, proving classical integrability via the flatness condition.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops integrable sigma models directly relevant to worldsheet string theory on AdS backgrounds, a central motivation for the work.

conceptDetails.name: wess-zumino term
conceptDetails.description: The Wess-Zumino term is incorporated into the semi-symmetric space sigma model and shown to be compatible with auxiliary field deformations and integrability.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The auxiliary field sigma models are two-dimensional quantum field theories whose classical integrability and equations of motion are analyzed in depth.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs explicit Lagrangians for the auxiliary field deformed sigma models and derives their equations of motion via variational principles.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Lie group and coset space theory underpins the construction of symmetric and semi-symmetric space sigma models throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The coset space structure of symmetric and semi-symmetric spaces is defined via Z2 and Z4 graded decompositions of Lie algebras, encoding symmetry quotients central to the construction.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: AdS/CFT holography and dual gauge theories such as N=4 super Yang-Mills provide key applications motivating the integrable models studied.

title: How DREAMS are made: Emulating Satellite Galaxy and Subhalo Populations with Diffusion Models and Point Clouds
abstract: The connection between galaxies and their host dark matter (DM) halos is critical to our understanding of cosmology, galaxy formation, and DM physics. To maximize the return of upcoming cosmological surveys, we need an accurate way to model this complex relationship. Many techniques have been developed to model this connection, from Halo Occupation Distribution (HOD) to empirical and semi-analytic models to hydrodynamic. Hydrodynamic simulations can incorporate more detailed astrophysical processes but are computationally expensive; HODs, on the other hand, are computationally cheap but have limited accuracy. In this work, we present NeHOD, a generative framework based on variational diffusion model and Transformer, for painting galaxies/subhalos on top of DM with an accuracy of hydrodynamic simulations but at a computational cost similar to HOD. By modeling galaxies/subhalos as point clouds, instead of binning or voxelization, we can resolve small spatial scales down to the resolution of the simulations. For each halo, NeHOD predicts the positions, velocities, masses, and concentrations of its central and satellite galaxies. We train NeHOD on the TNG-Warm DM suite of the DREAMS project, which consists of 1024 high-resolution zoom-in hydrodynamic simulations of Milky Way-mass halos with varying warm DM mass and astrophysical parameters. We show that our model captures the complex relationships between subhalo properties as a function of the simulation parameters, including the mass functions, stellar-halo mass relations, concentration-mass relations, and spatial clustering. Our method can be used for a large variety of downstream applications, from galaxy clustering to strong lensing studies.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: NeHOD uses a variational diffusion model as its core generative framework to paint galaxies and subhalos onto dark matter halos.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper models the galaxy-halo connection for dark matter halos, including warm dark matter physics affecting subhalo populations.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: NeHOD serves as an emulator that reproduces hydrodynamic simulation accuracy at the computational cost of analytic HOD models.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy-halo connection
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses modeling the statistical relationship between galaxies and their host dark matter halos across varying physics parameters.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents NeHOD as a generative framework that synthesizes realistic galaxy/subhalo populations conditioned on simulation parameters.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: NeHOD is trained on the DREAMS/TNG-WDM suite of 1024 high-resolution zoom-in hydrodynamic simulations of Milky Way-mass halos.

conceptDetails.name: point cloud modeling
conceptDetails.description: Galaxies and subhalos are represented as unordered point clouds rather than voxelized grids, preserving small-scale spatial resolution.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A Transformer architecture is combined with the diffusion model to process point cloud representations of galaxy populations.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The framework acts as a surrogate for expensive hydrodynamic simulations, enabling rapid exploration of astrophysical and DM parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The method enables downstream simulation-based inference for cosmological and dark matter parameter constraints from galaxy surveys.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The generative model implicitly learns the posterior distribution over galaxy/subhalo properties conditioned on halo and simulation parameters.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key goal of NeHOD is achieving hydrodynamic-level accuracy at HOD-like computational cost, enabling large-scale survey analysis.

title: Double "acct": a distinct double-peaked supernova matching pulsational pair-instability models
abstract: We present multi-wavelength data of SN2020acct, a double-peaked stripped-envelope supernova (SN) in NGC2981 at ~150 Mpc. The two peaks are temporally distinct, with maxima separated by 58 rest-frame days, and a factor of 20 reduction in flux between. The first is luminous (M$_{r}$ = -18.00 $\pm$ 0.02 mag), blue (g - r = 0.27 $\pm$ 0.03 mag), and displays spectroscopic signatures of interaction with hydrogen-free circumstellar material. The second peak is fainter (M$_{r}$ = -17.29 $\pm$ 0.03 mag), and spectroscopically similar to an evolved stripped-envelope SNe, with strong blended forbidden [Ca II] and [O II] features. No other known double-peak SN exhibits a light curve similar to that of SN 2020acct. We find the likelihood of two individual SNe occurring in the same star-forming region within that time to be highly improbable, while an implausibly fine-tuned configuration would be required to produce two SNe from a single binary system. We find that the peculiar properties of SN2020acct match models of pulsational pair instability (PPI), in which the initial peak is produced by collisions of shells of ejected material, shortly followed by a terminal explosion. Pulsations from a star with a 72 M$_{\odot}$ helium core provide an excellent match to the double-peaked light curve. The local galactic environment has a metallicity of 0.4 Z$_{\odot}$, a level where massive single stars are not expected retain enough mass to encounter the PPI. However, late binary mergers or a low-metallicity pocket may allow the required core mass. We measure the rate of SN 2020acct-like events to be $<3.3\times10^{-8}$ Mpc$^{-3}$ yr$^{-1}$ at z = 0.07, or <0.1% of the total core-collapse SN rate.

conceptDetails.name: pulsational pair instability
conceptDetails.description: The central physical mechanism proposed to explain SN 2020acct, where a massive helium core undergoes repeated pulsations ejecting shells of material before a terminal explosion produces the two observed peaks.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the evolutionary pathway of a massive star with a 72 solar mass helium core through pulsational pair instability, connecting progenitor properties to the observed supernova behavior.

conceptDetails.name: light curve modeling
conceptDetails.description: Multi-band photometric light curves are modeled to characterize the two temporally distinct peaks and match them to pulsational pair-instability theoretical predictions.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: SN 2020acct is analyzed as a stripped-envelope supernova with a unique double-peaked light curve that defies existing classification schemes, requiring a new category of pulsational pair-instability events.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: The first peak of SN 2020acct is attributed to collisions between ejected shells of hydrogen-free circumstellar material, producing the luminous, blue, spectrally distinct initial maximum.

conceptDetails.name: rate estimation
conceptDetails.description: The volumetric rate of SN 2020acct-like pulsational pair-instability events is measured to be less than 3.3e-8 Mpc^-3 yr^-1, placing it at under 0.1% of the total core-collapse supernova rate.

conceptDetails.name: binary stellar merger
conceptDetails.description: Late binary mergers are proposed as a pathway to produce the required 72 solar mass helium core at the observed sub-solar metallicity where single stars cannot retain sufficient mass.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The pair-instability mechanism involves a physical phase transition in the stellar core where electron-positron pair production softens the equation of state, triggering runaway pulsations.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Multi-wavelength photometric and spectroscopic observations are used to detect and characterize the rare double-peaked transient signal across optical and UV bands.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies SN 2020acct as a statistical outlier with no known analogs among double-peaked supernovae, effectively treating it as an anomalous transient in the broader supernova population.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Statistical inference methods are used to estimate the rate of SN 2020acct-like events and assess the probability of alternative explanations such as two independent supernovae occurring in the same region.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations are used to model the pulsational pair-instability light curves and match them against the observed double-peaked photometry of SN 2020acct.

title: SN 2021foa: The "Flip-Flop" Type IIn / Ibn supernova
abstract: We present a comprehensive analysis of the photometric and spectroscopic evolution of SN~2021foa, unique among the class of transitional supernovae for repeatedly changing its spectroscopic appearance from hydrogen-to-helium-to-hydrogen-dominated (IIn-to-Ibn-to-IIn) within 50 days past peak brightness. The spectra exhibit multiple narrow ($\approx$ 300--600~km~s$^{-1}$) absorption lines of hydrogen, helium, calcium and iron together with broad helium emission lines with a full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) of $\sim 6000$~km~s$^{-1}$. For a steady, wind-mass loss regime, light curve modeling results in an ejecta mass of $\sim 8$ M$_{\odot}$ and CSM mass below 1 M$_{\odot}$, and an ejecta velocity consistent with the FWHM of the broad helium lines. We obtain a mass-loss rate of $\approx 2$ M$_{\odot} {\rm yr}^{-1}$. This mass-loss rate is three orders of magnitude larger than derived for normal Type II SNe. We estimate that the bulk of the CSM of SN~2021foa must have been expelled within half a year, about 15 years ago. Our analysis suggests that SN~2021foa had a helium rich ejecta which swept up a dense shell of hydrogen rich CSM shortly after explosion. At about 60 days past peak brightness, the photosphere recedes through the dense ejecta-CSM region, occulting much of the red-shifted emission of the hydrogen and helium lines, which results in observed blue-shift ($\sim -3000$~km~s$^{-1}$). Strong mass loss activity prior to explosion, such as those seen in SN~2009ip-like objects and SN~2021foa as precursor emission, are the likely origin of a complex, multiple-shell CSM close to the progenitor star.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on classifying SN 2021foa as it repeatedly transitions between Type IIn and Type Ibn spectroscopic appearances within 50 days of peak brightness.

conceptDetails.name: transitional supernovae
conceptDetails.description: SN 2021foa exemplifies a rare IIn/Ibn transitional event; the paper characterizes the physical mechanism driving its flip-flop spectral behavior as helium-rich ejecta sweeping a hydrogen-rich CSM shell.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: The spectral and photometric evolution of SN 2021foa is driven entirely by ejecta-CSM interaction, producing narrow emission lines, P-Cygni profiles, and late-time blue-shifted asymmetries.

conceptDetails.name: pre-explosion mass loss
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives a mass-loss rate ~2 solar masses/yr and infers that the bulk of the complex, multi-shell CSM was expelled ~12–15 years before explosion via precursor-like activity analogous to SN 2009ip.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the late-stage evolution of the massive progenitor star, including pre-explosion mass-loss phases, Wolf-Rayet and LBV progenitor scenarios, and their role in shaping the circumstellar environment.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Multi-epoch spectroscopic analysis identifies and tracks narrow absorption and broad emission lines of H, He, Ca, and Fe, including detecting the velocity shift from ~0 to ~−3000 km/s at 60 days post-peak.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Light curve modeling under a steady wind-mass-loss regime is used to derive ejecta mass (~8 solar masses), CSM mass, and ejecta velocity from photometric data.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Physical progenitor and CSM parameters are inferred from observed light curves and spectra, constituting an inverse problem of recovering source conditions from integrated line-of-sight observables.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Statistical sampling methods are used in light curve fitting and parameter estimation to characterize uncertainties in ejecta and CSM properties.

title: Maven: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Supernova Science
abstract: A common setting in astronomy is the availability of a small number of high-quality observations, and larger amounts of either lower-quality observations or synthetic data from simplified models. Time-domain astrophysics is a canonical example of this imbalance, with the number of supernovae observed photometrically outpacing the number observed spectroscopically by multiple orders of magnitude. At the same time, no data-driven models exist to understand these photometric and spectroscopic observables in a common context. Contrastive learning objectives, which have grown in popularity for aligning distinct data modalities in a shared embedding space, provide a potential solution to extract information from these modalities. We present Maven, the first foundation model for supernova science. To construct Maven, we first pre-train our model to align photometry and spectroscopy from 0.5M synthetic supernovae using a constrastive objective. We then fine-tune the model on 4,702 observed supernovae from the Zwicky Transient Facility. Maven reaches state-of-the-art performance on both classification and redshift estimation, despite the embeddings not being explicitly optimized for these tasks. Through ablation studies, we show that pre-training with synthetic data improves overall performance. In the upcoming era of the Vera C. Rubin Observatory, Maven serves as a Rosetta Stone for leveraging large, unlabeled and multimodal time-domain datasets.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: Core pre-training objective used to align photometric and spectroscopic embeddings of synthetic supernovae into a shared latent space.

conceptDetails.name: multimodal foundation model
conceptDetails.description: Maven is the first foundation model for supernova science that jointly encodes heterogeneous photometric and spectroscopic data modalities.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Maven learns joint representations of supernova photometry and spectroscopy to enable downstream classification and redshift estimation.

conceptDetails.name: synthetic pre-training
conceptDetails.description: Novel two-stage training strategy where simulated supernova data is used to pre-train before fine-tuning on real observations.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Primary downstream evaluation task where Maven classifies supernova types from photometric light curves.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Pre-training on 0.5M synthetic supernova pairs without explicit task labels to learn general-purpose embeddings.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Shared embedding space where photometric and spectroscopic modalities are aligned via contrastive objectives.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Maven is fine-tuned on 4,702 observed ZTF supernovae after synthetic pre-training to adapt to real observational data.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Supernova type classification is one of two main downstream benchmarks used to evaluate Maven's embeddings.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Knowledge from synthetic supernova pre-training transfers to improve performance on real observed supernova tasks.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Transformer-based encoder architectures are used to independently encode photometric light curves and spectra.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Photometric redshift estimation is framed as a regression task and evaluated as the second downstream benchmark.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Maven leverages large unlabeled photometric datasets alongside small labeled spectroscopic samples to improve generalization.

title: Remove Symmetries to Control Model Expressivity and Improve Optimization
abstract: When symmetry is present in the loss function, the model is likely to be trapped in a low-capacity state that is sometimes known as a "collapse". Being trapped in these low-capacity states can be a major obstacle to training across many scenarios where deep learning technology is applied. We first prove two concrete mechanisms through which symmetries lead to reduced capacities and ignored features during training and inference. We then propose a simple and theoretically justified algorithm, syre, to remove almost all symmetry-induced low-capacity states in neural networks. When this type of entrapment is especially a concern, removing symmetries with the proposed method is shown to correlate well with improved optimization or performance. A remarkable merit of the proposed method is that it is model-agnostic and does not require any knowledge of the symmetry.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry removal
conceptDetails.description: Novel contribution: the syre algorithm that provably eliminates almost all discrete reflection symmetries from a neural network's loss landscape, enabling escape from symmetry-induced degenerate solutions.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper formalizes reflection symmetries as discrete symmetry groups (including Z2 subgroups) to characterize how loss function invariances arise and how they can be systematically removed.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is deliberately breaking symmetries in neural network loss functions to prevent entrapment in low-capacity states, inverting the usual physics goal of symmetry preservation.

conceptDetails.name: capacity collapse
conceptDetails.description: Novel phenomenon formalized in the paper: the mechanism by which reflection symmetries trap models in low-rank, low-capacity saddle points where features are ignored during training and inference.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The syre algorithm modifies the loss function to remove reflection symmetries without requiring knowledge of the symmetry structure, implemented in a single line of code.

conceptDetails.name: saddle point escape
conceptDetails.description: Novel focus: the paper addresses the previously unsolved problem of fully escaping symmetry-induced saddle points, distinguishing syre from prior work that only characterized their dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Projection matrices defining reflection symmetries are analyzed via their spectral structure, and the method exploits pseudo-inverses and orthonormal column decompositions to identify and remove symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Serves as important conceptual contrast: where equivariant networks enforce symmetry, this paper argues that removing symmetry from the loss unlocks model expressivity and escapes degenerate solutions.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Symmetric saddle points in the loss landscape act analogously to phase transition points where the model becomes stuck in a lower-capacity phase, drawing on physics intuition about symmetry-broken states.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The broader framework motivating the study of symmetry groups in deep learning, situating syre within the landscape of geometry-aware training methods.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key merit of syre is that it is model-agnostic and architecture-independent, making it broadly applicable across deep learning settings without symmetry-specific tuning.

title: Low-Budget Simulation-Based Inference with Bayesian Neural Networks
abstract: Simulation-based inference methods have been shown to be inaccurate in the data-poor regime, when training simulations are limited or expensive. Under these circumstances, the inference network is particularly prone to overfitting, and using it without accounting for the computational uncertainty arising from the lack of identifiability of the network weights can lead to unreliable results. To address this issue, we propose using Bayesian neural networks in low-budget simulation-based inference, thereby explicitly accounting for the computational uncertainty of the posterior approximation. We design a family of Bayesian neural network priors that are tailored for inference and show that they lead to well-calibrated posteriors on tested benchmarks, even when as few as $O(10)$ simulations are available. This opens up the possibility of performing reliable simulation-based inference using very expensive simulators, as we demonstrate on a problem from the field of cosmology where single simulations are computationally expensive. We show that Bayesian neural networks produce informative and well-calibrated posterior estimates with only a few hundred simulations.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The central methodology of the paper, which approximates posterior distributions using neural networks trained on synthetic data from stochastic simulators without tractable likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The foundational framework used to treat neural network weights as random variables and marginalize over them to produce principled uncertainty-aware posterior approximations.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly targets computational uncertainty arising from weight non-identifiability in low-budget SBI, using BNNs to quantify and propagate this uncertainty.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Expected coverage is the primary evaluation metric; the paper demonstrates that BNN priors yield well-calibrated, conservative posteriors even with O(10) simulations.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: BNNs are used to approximate the Bayesian posterior over parameters given observations, accounting for both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in the approximation.

conceptDetails.name: bnn weight priors
conceptDetails.description: A novel contribution: the paper designs a family of BNN priors specifically tailored to produce conservative, calibrated posteriors for SBI tasks in the low-data regime.

conceptDetails.name: epistemic uncertainty
conceptDetails.description: The paper distinguishes and explicitly addresses epistemic uncertainty from neural network weight non-identifiability, which dominates in the low-simulation-budget setting.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: SBI methods in the paper estimate the likelihood or posterior density using neural networks trained on simulator outputs.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian model averaging over BNN weight samples is computed via Monte Carlo sampling, enabling tractable approximate inference.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Neural network ensembles are used as a baseline comparison representing a crude BNN approximation, against which the proposed BNN priors are evaluated.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: A computationally expensive cosmological simulator is the real-world application demonstrating that the proposed method enables reliable inference with only a few hundred simulations.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Coverage diagnostics and simulation-based calibration tests are used to validate that the BNN posteriors are not overconfident.

title: LINX: A Fast, Differentiable, and Extensible Big Bang Nucleosynthesis Package
abstract: We introduce LINX (Light Isotope Nucleosynthesis with JAX), a new differentiable public Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) code designed for fast parameter estimation. By leveraging JAX, LINX achieves both speed and differentiability, enabling the use of Bayesian inference, including gradient-based methods. We discuss the formalism used in LINX for rapid primordial elemental abundance predictions and give examples of how LINX can be used. When combined with differentiable Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) power spectrum emulators, LINX can be used for joint CMB and BBN analyses without requiring extensive computational resources, including on personal hardware.

conceptDetails.name: primordial nucleosynthesis
conceptDetails.description: LINX directly models the network of nuclear reactions in the early Universe that determines the primordial abundances of light isotopes.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable bbn
conceptDetails.description: LINX introduces full differentiability to Big Bang Nucleosynthesis codes via JAX, enabling gradient-based inference methods for primordial abundance predictions.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: LINX is designed to enable fast Bayesian parameter estimation for BBN, including gradient-based posterior sampling methods like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: LINX enables joint CMB and BBN analyses by combining with differentiable CMB power spectrum emulators like CosmoPower.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo samplers are central to the parameter inference workflows that LINX is designed to support, including nested sampling for joint CMB+BBN fits.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The core application of LINX is efficient posterior estimation over cosmological parameters from BBN and CMB data.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: LINX facilitates likelihood-based and simulation-based cosmological inference by providing fast, differentiable forward modeling of primordial nucleosynthesis.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: LINX acts as a fast surrogate/emulator for BBN calculations, and is designed to work alongside differentiable CMB emulators in joint analysis pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: LINX serves as a fast, accurate surrogate for expensive BBN calculations, enabling its use in large-scale parameter sweeps and inference pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: LINX leverages JAX compilation and vectorization to achieve high computational speed, enabling analyses on personal hardware.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The effective number of neutrino species N_eff is a key parameter constrained by LINX through joint BBN and CMB analyses.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: LINX is designed to be extensible to new physics scenarios, including models with non-standard particles like dark matter that affect BBN.

title: Cosmological Parameter Estimation with a Joint-Likelihood Analysis of the Cosmic Microwave Background and Big Bang Nucleosynthesis
abstract: We present the first joint-likelihood analysis of Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) and Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) data. Bayesian inference is performed on the baryon abundance and the effective number of neutrino species, $N_{\rm eff}$, using a CMB Boltzmann solver in combination with LINX, a new flexible and efficient BBN code. We marginalize over Planck nuisance parameters and nuclear rates to find $N_{\rm{eff}} = 3.08_{-0.13}^{+0.13},\,2.94 _{-0.16}^{+0.16},$ or $2.98_{-0.13}^{+0.14}$, for three separate reaction networks. This framework enables robust testing of the Lambda Cold Dark Matter paradigm and its variants with CMB and BBN data.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: The CMB power spectrum is one of the two primary data sources used for joint-likelihood cosmological parameter estimation, with CLASS Boltzmann solver providing predictions.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The core statistical framework used to infer cosmological parameters (baryon abundance, N_eff) by combining CMB and BBN likelihoods with MCMC sampling.

conceptDetails.name: big bang nucleosynthesis
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept: the primordial nucleosynthesis epoch providing independent constraints on baryon abundance and N_eff through light element abundance predictions.

conceptDetails.name: joint likelihood analysis
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept: the first simultaneous combination of CMB and BBN likelihoods with correlated nuisance parameters for unified cosmological parameter estimation.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Markov-Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) and nested sampling are used to explore the high-dimensional parameter space of cosmological and nuisance parameters.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The joint posterior over cosmological parameters, Planck nuisance parameters, and BBN nuclear rate nuisance parameters is the primary output of the analysis.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Nuclear reaction rate uncertainties are modeled as log-normal nuisance parameters and marginalized over, enabling rigorous uncertainty propagation in cosmological constraints.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear rate marginalization
