conceptDetails.description: A novel concept: systematic marginalization over nuclear reaction rate uncertainties as nuisance parameters within a full cosmological MCMC analysis.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: LINX, a JAX-based BBN code, acts as a differentiable simulator enabling efficient likelihood evaluation for Bayesian parameter estimation within standard MCMC frameworks.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The effective number of neutrino species N_eff is a primary inference target, constraining the neutrino sector and potential beyond-Standard-Model relativistic species.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The Lambda Cold Dark Matter (ΛCDM) paradigm and its variants are the cosmological models being tested and constrained by the joint CMB+BBN analysis.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: LINX serves as an efficient surrogate for BBN abundance predictions, replacing slower codes to make joint MCMC analysis computationally feasible.

title: Finding the Fuse: Prospects for the Detection and Characterization of Hydrogen-Rich Core-Collapse Supernova Precursor Emission with the LSST
abstract: Enhanced emission in the months to years preceding explosion has been detected for several core-collapse supernovae (SNe). Though the physical mechanisms driving the emission remain hotly debated, the light curves of detected events show long-lived ($\geq$50 days), plateau-like behavior, suggesting hydrogen recombination may significantly contribute to the total energy budget. The Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) will provide a decade-long photometric baseline to search for this emission, both in binned pre-explosion observations after an SN is detected and in single-visit observations prior to the SN explosion. In anticipation of these searches, we simulate a range of eruptive precursor models to core-collapse SNe and forecast the discovery rates of these phenomena in LSST data. We find a detection rate of ~40-130 yr$^{-1}$ for SN IIP/IIL precursors and ~110 yr$^{-1}$ for SN IIn precursors in single-epoch photometry. Considering the first three years of observations with the effects of rolling and observing triplets included, this number grows to a total of 150-400 in binned photometry, with the highest number recovered when binning in 100-day bins for 2020tlf-like precursors and in 20-day bins for other recombination-driven models from the literature. We quantify the impact of using templates contaminated by residual light (from either long-lived or separate precursor emission) on these detection rates, and explore strategies for estimating baseline flux to mitigate these issues. Spectroscopic follow-up of the eruptions preceding core-collapse SNe and detected with LSST will offer important clues to the underlying drivers of terminal-stage mass loss in massive stars.

conceptDetails.name: precursor emission
conceptDetails.description: Enhanced optical emission occurring months to years before core-collapse supernova explosion, driven by eruptive mass loss, which is the central phenomenon studied in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar material
conceptDetails.description: Dense hydrogen-rich material ejected by massive stars in their terminal phase that powers precursor light curves via recombination and CSM interaction.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Core task of identifying pre-explosion precursor emission signals in LSST difference-image photometry above detection thresholds in both single-epoch and binned data.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Terminal-stage evolution of massive red supergiant stars including eruptive mass-loss mechanisms and their observational signatures preceding core-collapse supernovae.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Used to simulate populations of supernova precursor events across a range of eruptive models to forecast discovery rates in LSST survey data.

conceptDetails.name: photometric binning strategy
conceptDetails.description: Time-averaging of photometric observations at varying cadences to recover sub-threshold precursor flux, with quantified impact of template contamination.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Hydrogen-rich SN IIP/IIL and SN IIn subtypes are analyzed separately based on their distinct precursor emission characteristics and detection rates.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: Optimization of LSST photometric binning strategies (20-day vs. 100-day bins, rolling cadence, triplet observations) to maximize precursor detection rates.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Eruptive precursor emission models from the literature are constructed as surrogates for the unknown physical emission mechanisms to forecast LSST-observable signatures.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Pre-explosion brightening events are identified as anomalous transients in difference imaging relative to quiescent host-galaxy baselines.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Simulated precursor light curves under multiple physical models are used to infer detection statistics and physical constraints without closed-form likelihoods.

title: Multiple testing for signal-agnostic searches of new physics with machine learning
abstract: In this work, we address the question of how to enhance signal-agnostic searches by leveraging multiple testing strategies. Specifically, we consider hypothesis tests relying on machine learning, where model selection can introduce a bias towards specific families of new physics signals. We show that it is beneficial to combine different tests, characterised by distinct choices of hyperparameters, and that performances comparable to the best available test are generally achieved while providing a more uniform response to various types of anomalies. Focusing on the New Physics Learning Machine, a methodology to perform a signal-agnostic likelihood-ratio test, we explore a number of approaches to multiple testing, such as combining p-values and aggregating test statistics.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on designing and combining signal-agnostic hypothesis tests to detect new physics without assuming a specific signal model.

conceptDetails.name: multiple testing
conceptDetails.description: Novel framework combining outputs from many hypothesis tests with distinct hyperparameters to reduce bias toward specific signal families while accounting for the look-elsewhere effect.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: NPLM approximates the maximum-likelihood-ratio test using machine learning to construct a data-driven alternative hypothesis.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics application is model-independent searches for Beyond the Standard Model signals at colliders.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The signal-agnostic search framework is fundamentally an anomaly detection problem—identifying deviations from the Standard Model reference distribution.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Kernel-based classifiers are used to parametrize the likelihood-ratio function in the NPLM methodology.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: The two-sample test is framed as a goodness-of-fit test assessing whether the reference model describes the observed data.

conceptDetails.name: p-value combination
conceptDetails.description: Several methods for combining p-values across multiple tests are explored as strategies to aggregate evidence from different models.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Multiple tests with different hyperparameters are combined via ensemble strategies to achieve more uniform sensitivity across signal types.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The methodology targets high-energy collider data analysis, comparing observed events against Standard Model Monte Carlo references.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Controlling false positive rates and quantifying p-values and Z-scores are central to the statistical validity of the tests.

title: KAN 2.0: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks Meet Science
abstract: A major challenge of AI + Science lies in their inherent incompatibility: today's AI is primarily based on connectionism, while science depends on symbolism. To bridge the two worlds, we propose a framework to seamlessly synergize Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) and science. The framework highlights KANs' usage for three aspects of scientific discovery: identifying relevant features, revealing modular structures, and discovering symbolic formulas. The synergy is bidirectional: science to KAN (incorporating scientific knowledge into KANs), and KAN to science (extracting scientific insights from KANs). We highlight major new functionalities in the pykan package: (1) MultKAN: KANs with multiplication nodes. (2) kanpiler: a KAN compiler that compiles symbolic formulas into KANs. (3) tree converter: convert KANs (or any neural networks) to tree graphs. Based on these tools, we demonstrate KANs' capability to discover various types of physical laws, including conserved quantities, Lagrangians, symmetries, and constitutive laws.

conceptDetails.name: kolmogorov-arnold networks
conceptDetails.description: The central neural architecture of the paper, featuring learnable activation functions on edges rather than fixed activations on nodes, enabling interpretable function decomposition.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A core goal of the framework, enabling extraction of human-understandable scientific knowledge including features, modular structures, and symbolic formulas from trained networks.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the process of fitting learned KAN activation functions to closed-form symbolic expressions to discover analytic physical laws from data.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: One of the demonstrated physical discovery tasks, where KANs identify conserved quantities from trajectory data.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: KANs are applied to discover Lagrangian formulations of physical systems, recovering equations of motion from observational data.

conceptDetails.name: multiplicative nodes
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the MultKAN extension adding explicit multiplication nodes to KANs, improving expressiveness and interpretability for product-structured physical laws.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The 'science to KAN' direction of the framework, embedding prior physical knowledge such as symmetries, sparsity constraints, and known functional forms into KAN architecture and training.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: KANs are used to discover and enforce symmetries in physical systems, including identifying which variables a function depends on symmetrically.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The coarsest level of scientific explanation supported by KANs, identifying which input variables are relevant to a target quantity.

conceptDetails.name: modular structure discovery
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: a middle tier of scientific explanation where KANs reveal how variables contribute independently or in coupled groups, bridging feature selection and symbolic formula extraction.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsification and pruning of KAN edges is used to reveal modular structure and identify important features, underpinning interpretability.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes a bidirectional framework integrating KANs into the scientific discovery workflow, covering inductive bias injection and knowledge extraction.

title: Enhancing Events in Neutrino Telescopes through Deep Learning-Driven Super-Resolution
abstract: Recent discoveries by neutrino telescopes, such as the IceCube Neutrino Observatory, relied extensively on machine learning (ML) tools to infer physical quantities from the raw photon hits detected. Neutrino telescope reconstruction algorithms are limited by the sparse sampling of photons by the optical modules due to the relatively large spacing ($10-100\,{\rm m})$ between them. In this letter, we propose a novel technique that learns photon transport through the detector medium through the use of deep learning-driven super-resolution of data events. These ``improved'' events can then be reconstructed using traditional or ML techniques, resulting in improved resolution. Our strategy arranges additional ``virtual'' optical modules within an existing detector geometry and trains a convolutional neural network to predict the hits on these virtual optical modules. We show that this technique improves the angular reconstruction of muons in a generic ice-based neutrino telescope. Our results readily extend to water-based neutrino telescopes and other event morphologies.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The core technique of this paper, applying image super-resolution concepts to neutrino telescope data by predicting photon hits on virtual optical modules between physical ones.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets improvement of neutrino telescope performance, specifically angular reconstruction of neutrino-induced muon tracks in IceCube-like detectors.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A UNet++ convolutional neural network architecture is trained to predict time series data on virtual optical modules from the surrounding physical modules.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The super-resolved events with virtual optical module data are shown to improve angular reconstruction resolution for muon tracks beyond what is achievable with the sparse physical detector alone.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A VAE encodes the photon hit time series on each optical module into a compact 64-parameter latent vector, enabling the UNet to operate in latent space.

conceptDetails.name: photon transport emulation
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the network implicitly learns the physics of photon transport through ice or water, effectively emulating the detector medium's optical properties.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Prometheus open-source toolkit is used to simulate neutrino telescope events in arbitrary detector geometries to generate training and test datasets.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The VAE learns a compressed latent representation of photon arrival time series, which the UNet then operates on to predict virtual sensor responses.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring the dense photon deposition field from sparse detector measurements is an inverse problem that the super-resolution network addresses.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The pre-trained VAE encoding is reused as a fixed component within the super-resolution pipeline, enabling efficient training of the UNet on the latent space.

title: Improving Radial Velocities by Marginalizing over Stars and Sky: Achieving 30 m/s RV Precision for APOGEE in the Plate Era
abstract: The radial velocity catalog from the Apache Point Observatory Galactic Evolution Experiment (APOGEE) is unique in its simultaneously large volume and high precision as a result of its decade-long survey duration, multiplexing (600 fibers), and spectral resolution of $R \sim 22,500$. However, previous data reductions of APOGEE have not fully realized the potential radial velocity (RV) precision of the instrument. Here we present an RV catalog based on a new reduction of all 2.6 million visits of APOGEE DR17 and validate it against improved estimates for the theoretical RV performance. The core ideas of the new reduction are the simultaneous modeling of all components in the spectra, rather than a separate subtraction of point estimates for the sky, and a marginalization over stellar types, rather than a grid search for an optimum. We show that this catalog, when restricted to RVs measured with the same fiber, achieves noise-limited precision down to 30 m/s and delivers well-calibrated uncertainties. We also introduce a general method for calibrating fiber-to-fiber constant RV offsets and demonstrate its importance for high RV precision work in multi-fiber spectrographs. After calibration, we achieve 47 m/s RV precision on the combined catalog with RVs measured with different fibers. This degradation in precision relative to measurements with only a single fiber suggests that refining line spread function models should be a focus in SDSS-V to improve the fiber-unified RV catalog.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper marginalizes over stellar types using Bayesian marginalization rather than grid search, which is central to the improved RV precision method.

conceptDetails.name: spectral component separation
conceptDetails.description: Novel method simultaneously modeling stellar spectra, sky lines, and telluric absorption rather than sequential point-estimate subtraction.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces methods for calibrating fiber-to-fiber constant RV offsets and demonstrates well-calibrated uncertainty estimates.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A key contribution is delivering well-calibrated uncertainties and validating them against theoretical lower bounds on RV precision.

conceptDetails.name: fiber systematics calibration
conceptDetails.description: A general method introduced for calibrating fiber-to-fiber constant RV offsets in multi-fiber spectrographs, critical for combining multi-fiber observations.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering radial velocities from spectral observations is an inverse problem, and the paper presents a novel component separation approach.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The method improves detection of stellar radial velocity signals by simultaneously modeling stellar and sky components rather than sequential subtraction.

conceptDetails.name: line spread function modeling
conceptDetails.description: Characterizing and modeling variations in the line spread function across fibers is identified as a key limiting factor for RV precision.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The apMADGICS component separation framework uses sparse modeling techniques to decompose spectra into stellar and sky components.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The APOGEE RV catalog enables Galactic archaeology and study of stellar populations, directly informing our understanding of stellar evolution.

conceptDetails.name: exoplanets
conceptDetails.description: High-precision radial velocities are essential for exoplanet detection, and the 30 m/s precision achieved approaches exoplanet-detection thresholds.

title: Learning the Simplicity of Scattering Amplitudes
abstract: The simplification and reorganization of complex expressions lies at the core of scientific progress, particularly in theoretical high-energy physics. This work explores the application of machine learning to a particular facet of this challenge: the task of simplifying scattering amplitudes expressed in terms of spinor-helicity variables. We demonstrate that an encoder-decoder transformer architecture achieves impressive simplification capabilities for expressions composed of handfuls of terms. Lengthier expressions are implemented in an additional embedding network, trained using contrastive learning, which isolates subexpressions that are more likely to simplify. The resulting framework is capable of reducing expressions with hundreds of terms - a regular occurrence in quantum field theory calculations - to vastly simpler equivalent expressions. Starting from lengthy input expressions, our networks can generate the Parke-Taylor formula for five-point gluon scattering, as well as new compact expressions for five-point amplitudes involving scalars and gravitons. An interactive demonstration can be found at https://spinorhelicity.streamlit.app .

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics object being simplified; the paper develops ML methods to reduce complex multi-term spinor-helicity expressions for QFT scattering processes to compact forms like the Parke-Taylor formula.

conceptDetails.name: spinor-helicity formalism
conceptDetails.description: The specific mathematical language of on-shell scattering amplitudes, using angle and square brackets, Schouten identities, and momentum conservation as the symbolic algebra the ML system operates over.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: An encoder-decoder transformer architecture is the core ML model used for one-shot simplification of short-to-medium length spinor-helicity expressions.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic expression simplification
conceptDetails.description: The core ML task: mapping a large, algebraically complex symbolic expression to a provably equivalent but vastly shorter form, validated by numerical evaluation.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: Used to train an embedding network that groups subexpressions likely to simplify together in latent space, enabling sequential simplification of very long expressions.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: A learned embedding space over spinor-helicity terms is central to identifying which subsets of a long expression are likely to combine and simplify.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper learns compact latent representations for symbolic mathematical expressions so that semantic similarity (simplifiability) is reflected in embedding geometry.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The broader theoretical framework motivating the work; QFT calculations routinely produce the complex multi-term amplitude expressions that the ML system is designed to tame.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Self-attention within the transformer processes variable-length sequences of spinor-helicity tokens, enabling the model to identify cancellations across terms.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The contrastive embedding network is trained without external labels, using automatically generated pairs of equivalent and inequivalent expressions as supervision signal.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Cosine similarity analysis of the learned embedding space provides insight into why certain subexpressions are grouped and what structural features drive simplification.

title: Finite Temperature at Finite Places
abstract: This paper studies AdS/CFT in its $p$-adic version (at the ``finite place") in the setting where the bulk geometry is made up of the Tate curve, a discrete quotient of the Bruhat-Tits tree. Generalizing a classic result due to Zabrodin, the boundary dual of the free massive bulk theory is explicitly derived. Introducing perturbative interactions, the Wittens diagrams for the two-point and three-point correlators are computed for generic scaling dimensions at one-loop and tree level respectively. The answers obtained demonstrate how $p$-adic AdS/CFT on the Tate curve provides a useful toy model for real CFTs at finite temperature.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives boundary duals and computes two- and three-point correlators for p-adic CFTs at finite temperature, providing a toy model for real thermal CFTs.

conceptDetails.name: p-adic ads/cft
conceptDetails.description: The central framework of the paper, extending the holographic correspondence to p-adic number fields using discrete tree geometries as the bulk.

conceptDetails.name: tate curve
conceptDetails.description: The discrete quotient of the Bruhat-Tits tree used as the bulk geometry that introduces a thermal cycle analogous to finite temperature in real AdS/CFT.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: A free massive scalar field theory in the p-adic bulk is analyzed, and perturbative interactions are introduced to compute loop-level and tree-level correlators.

conceptDetails.name: bruhat-tits tree
conceptDetails.description: The p-adic analog of hyperbolic space that serves as the zero-temperature bulk geometry, from which the Tate curve is constructed by a discrete quotient.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Witten diagrams for two-point and three-point correlators at one-loop and tree level are explicitly computed, paralleling amplitude computations in real AdS/CFT.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: One-loop Witten diagrams are evaluated for generic scaling dimensions, requiring regularization techniques analogous to those in standard QFT loop calculations.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper situates p-adic AdS/CFT within the broader context of p-adic string theory and uses insights from string amplitudes to motivate the computational approach.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Finite temperature breaks zero-temperature conformal symmetry, and the Tate curve geometry encodes the thermal state analogous to black holes in real AdS/CFT.

title: AT2023vto: An Exceptionally Luminous Helium Tidal Disruption Event from a Massive Star
abstract: We present optical/UV observations and the spectroscopic classification of the transient AT2023vto as a tidal disruption event (TDE) at z = 0.4846. The spectrum is dominated by a broad He II $λ$4686 emission line, with a width of ~ $3.76 \times 10^4$ km/s and a blueshift of ~ $1.05 \times 10^4$ km/s, classifying it as a member of the TDE-He class. The light curve exhibits a long rise and decline timescale, with a large peak absolute magnitude of M$_g$ ~ -23.6, making it the most luminous of the classical optical TDEs (H, H+He, He) discovered to date by about 2 mag (and ~ 4 mag compared to the mean of the population). The light curve exhibits a persistent blue color of g - r ~ -0.4 mag throughout its evolution, similar to other TDEs, but distinct from supernovae. We identify the host galaxy of AT2023vto in archival Pan-STARRS images and find that the transient is located at the galaxy center, and that its inferred central black hole mass is ~ $10^7~M_{\odot}$. Modeling the light curves of AT2023vto, we find that it resulted from the disruption of a ~ 9 $M_{\odot}$ star by a ~$10^7~M_{\odot}$ supermassive black hole. The star mass is about 5 times larger than the highest star masses previously inferred in TDEs, and the black hole mass is at the high end of the distribution. AT2023vto is comparable in luminosity and timescale to some putative TDEs (with a blue featureless continuum), as well as to the mean of the recently identified population of ambiguous nuclear transients (ANTs), although the latter are spectroscopically distinct and tend to have longer timescales. ANTs have been speculated to arise from tidal disruptions of massive stars, perhaps in active galactic nuclei, and AT2023vto may represent a similar case but in a dormant black hole, thereby bridging the TDE and ANT populations. We anticipate that Rubin Observatory / LSST will uncover similar luminous TDEs to z ~ 3.

conceptDetails.name: tidal disruption event
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central subject is AT2023vto, classified as the most luminous classical optical TDE discovered to date, with analysis of its unique physical properties.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the disruption of a ~9 solar mass star, situating AT2023vto within the context of stellar mass distributions in TDE populations.

conceptDetails.name: accretion disk physics
conceptDetails.description: The luminosity and timescale of AT2023vto are interpreted through models of mass fallback and accretion onto a supermassive black hole.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Spectroscopic detection and classification of the broad He II emission line is central to establishing AT2023vto as a TDE-He event.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Light curve modeling uses Bayesian fitting (MOSFiT) to infer physical parameters such as black hole mass and disrupted star mass.

conceptDetails.name: spectral energy distribution fitting
conceptDetails.description: Prospector SED modeling of the host galaxy is used to infer stellar mass and hence the central black hole mass.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper distinguishes AT2023vto from superluminous supernovae and ambiguous nuclear transients through spectroscopic and photometric criteria.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: MOSFiT serves as a physical surrogate model to fit UV/optical light curves and infer system parameters for AT2023vto.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Host galaxy identification and characterization via Pan-STARRS archival imaging is used to localize AT2023vto at the galactic nucleus.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Identifying AT2023vto as an exceptionally luminous outlier among known TDE populations requires statistical comparison and outlier characterization.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Statistical light curve fitting employs likelihood-based parameter estimation to constrain the physical properties of the disruption event.

title: On the Generality and Persistence of Cosmological Stasis
abstract: Hierarchical decays of $N$ matter species to radiation may balance against Hubble expansion to yield stasis, a new phase of cosmological evolution with constant matter and radiation abundances. We analyze stasis with various machine learning techniques on the full $2N$-dimensional space of decay rates and abundances, which serve as inputs to the system of Boltzmann equations that governs the dynamics. We construct a differentiable Boltzmann solver to maximize the number of stasis $e$-folds $\mathcal{N}$. High-stasis configurations obtained by gradient ascent motivate log-uniform distributions on rates and abundances to accompany power-law distributions of previous works. We demonstrate that random configurations drawn from these families of distributions regularly exhibit many $e$-folds of stasis. We additionally use them as priors in a Bayesian analysis conditioned on stasis, using stochastic variational inference with normalizing flows to model the posterior. All three numerical analyses demonstrate the generality of stasis and point to a new model in which the rates and abundances are exponential in the species index. We show that the exponential model solves the exact stasis equations, is an attractor, and satisfies $\mathcal{N}\propto N$, exhibiting inflation-level $e$-folding with a relatively low number of species. This is contrasted with the $\mathcal{N}\propto \log(N)$ scaling of power-law models. Finally, we discuss implications for the emergent string conjecture and string axiverse.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological stasis
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon studied: a new phase of cosmological evolution where matter and radiation abundances remain constant, driven by hierarchical decays of N matter species balancing Hubble expansion.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Neural network-based generative models used to represent the posterior distribution over stasis configurations in the Bayesian analysis via stochastic variational inference.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: A stasis-conditioned posterior analysis is performed over the 2N-dimensional space of decay rates and abundances, using normalizing flows to model the posterior.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable boltzmann solver
conceptDetails.description: A novel differentiable numerical solver for the system of Boltzmann equations governing stasis dynamics, enabling gradient-based optimization of stasis e-folds.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are trained via stochastic variational inference to model the posterior over configurations conditioned on achieving stasis.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The differentiable Boltzmann solver acts as a differentiable simulator enabling gradient ascent and posterior inference over the high-dimensional stasis parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Implications of the exponential stasis model are discussed in the context of the emergent string conjecture, string axiverse, and Kaluza-Klein and string towers.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Log-uniform and power-law probability distributions over decay rates and abundances are studied; normalizing flows provide flexible density estimates for the posterior.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Random configurations are sampled from log-uniform and power-law distributions to demonstrate the generality of stasis across the parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Vacuum energy (Λ) components enter in alternative stasis flavors (Λ→M and Λ→γ stasis), extending the cosmological energy density framework analyzed.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Beyond-Standard-Model theories predicting towers of unstable states provide the physical motivation for the N matter species framework underlying stasis.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The Boltzmann equations governing the time evolution of energy densities and the Hubble parameter constitute a stochastic dynamical system analyzed numerically.

title: Attractors, Geodesics, and the Geometry of Moduli Spaces
abstract: We connect recent conjectures and observations pertaining to geodesics, attractor flows, Laplacian eigenvalues and the geometry of moduli spaces by using that attractor flows are geodesics. For toroidal compactifications, attractor points are related to (degenerate) masses of the Laplacian on the target space, and also to the Laplacian on the moduli space. We also explore compactifications of M-Theory to $5$D on a Calabi-Yau threefold and argue that geodesics are unique in a special set of classes, providing further evidence for a recent conjecture by Raman and Vafa. Finally, we describe the role of the marked moduli space in $4$d $\mathcal{N} = 2$ compactifications. We study split attractor flows in an explicit example of the one-parameter family of quintics and discuss setups where flops to isomorphic Calabi-Yau manifolds exist.

conceptDetails.name: attractor flows
conceptDetails.description: Gradient flows of the black hole potential to attractor points in moduli space; the paper's central result is identifying these flows as geodesics of the Weil-Petersson metric.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies attractor flows and geodesics arising from Calabi-Yau compactifications of M-Theory and string theory, including BPS states and central charges.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau moduli space
conceptDetails.description: The Weil-Petersson geometry of Calabi-Yau moduli spaces is the primary setting where geodesic uniqueness, split attractor flows, and flop transitions are studied.

conceptDetails.name: swampland conjecture
conceptDetails.description: The paper contributes to the swampland program by providing evidence for the Raman-Vafa geodesic uniqueness conjecture using attractor flow arguments.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Laplacian eigenvalues on tori and on Calabi-Yau moduli spaces are shown to be related to attractor points and particle masses, connecting spectral geometry to the swampland program.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Degenerate eigenvalues of the scalar Laplacian on target and moduli spaces are identified with attractor points, providing a spectral characterization of these special loci.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The 4d N=2 and 5D N=1 supergravity theories arising from compactification provide the field-theoretic framework for attractor flows and moduli spaces.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: M-Theory compactified to 5D and type II compactified to 4D yield effective supergravity theories whose moduli spaces are the central objects of study.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: BPS symmetry conditions constrain the attractor flow equations and ensure the constant-length gradient property that implies the geodesic equation.

title: TASI Lectures on Physics for Machine Learning
abstract: These notes are based on lectures I gave at TASI 2024 on Physics for Machine Learning. The focus is on neural network theory, organized according to network expressivity, statistics, and dynamics. I present classic results such as the universal approximation theorem and neural network / Gaussian process correspondence, and also more recent results such as the neural tangent kernel, feature learning with the maximal update parameterization, and Kolmogorov-Arnold networks. The exposition on neural network theory emphasizes a field theoretic perspective familiar to theoretical physicists. I elaborate on connections between the two, including a neural network approach to field theory.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a direct correspondence between neural networks and quantum field theories, using field-theoretic language (path integrals, partition functions, phi^4 theory) as the primary lens for understanding NN statistics and dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: neural tangent kernel
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept governing gradient-descent training dynamics: in the infinite-width NTK regime networks are exactly solvable linear models, while departures enable feature learning.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The NNGP correspondence shows that infinite-width neural networks at initialization are Gaussian processes; the paper extends this to non-Gaussian processes for finite-width corrections.

conceptDetails.name: neural network field theory
conceptDetails.description: The NN-FT correspondence: the paper establishes that neural network ensembles are statistical field theories, enabling tools from QFT (partition functions, Feynman diagrams) to analyze NNs.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The Neural Tangent Kernel characterizes gradient descent dynamics in the infinite-width limit, enabling exact solvable models of NN training.

conceptDetails.name: kolmogorov-arnold networks
conceptDetails.description: Presented as a recent architecture grounded in the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, offering an alternative to standard MLPs with improved expressivity properties.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Feature learning with the maximal update parameterization (muP) is covered as the mechanism by which networks escape the lazy training regime and adapt internal representations.

conceptDetails.name: maximal update parameterization
conceptDetails.description: The muP initialization scheme that scales weights so that features evolve non-trivially with width, enabling a distinct training regime beyond the NTK lazy-learning limit.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Symmetries of neural network distributions are analyzed in the statistics section, connecting invariance properties of weight initializations to resulting function-space symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Renormalization group concepts inform the field-theoretic treatment of neural networks, particularly in the phi^4 theory analogy and the treatment of perturbative corrections.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Width scaling and the infinite-width limit are central to deriving analytical results; the paper examines how NN behavior changes as a function of network width.

title: A Generative Modeling Approach to Reconstructing 21-cm Tomographic Data
abstract: Analyses of the cosmic 21-cm signal are hampered by astrophysical foregrounds that are far stronger than the signal itself. These foregrounds, typically confined to a wedge-shaped region in Fourier space, often necessitate the removal of a vast majority of modes, thereby degrading the quality of the data anisotropically. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel deep generative model based on stochastic interpolants to reconstruct the 21-cm data lost to wedge filtering. Our method leverages the non-Gaussian nature of the 21-cm signal to effectively map wedge-filtered 3D lightcones to samples from the conditional distribution of wedge-recovered lightcones. We demonstrate how our method is able to restore spatial information effectively, considering both varying cosmological initial conditions and astrophysics. Furthermore, we discuss a number of future avenues where this approach could be applied in analyses of the 21-cm signal, potentially offering new opportunities to improve our understanding of the Universe during the epochs of cosmic dawn and reionization.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a deep generative model based on stochastic interpolants to reconstruct 21-cm modes lost to foreground wedge filtering, sampling from the conditional distribution of recovered lightcones.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: Stochastic interpolants, the core method used, are explicitly identified as a variant of flow matching and rectified flows that transport wedge-filtered to wedge-recovered distributions.

conceptDetails.name: 21-cm foreground wedge
conceptDetails.description: The foreground wedge is the wedge-shaped contaminated region in Fourier space caused by spectrally smooth astrophysical foregrounds and interferometer chromaticity, whose removal motivates the entire reconstruction task.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: A stochastic differential equation is solved at inference time, with the trained neural network as the drift function, to generate samples from the conditional distribution of recovered lightcones.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method produces samples from the conditional posterior of wedge-recovered lightcones given a wedge-filtered input, enabling probabilistic reconstruction rather than a single deterministic output.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the full 21-cm brightness temperature field from anisotropically filtered (wedge-removed) Fourier modes is framed and solved as a conditional inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: lightcone tomography
conceptDetails.description: 3D 21-cm lightcones encoding the redshift evolution of neutral hydrogen are the input and output data format, enabling reconstruction across a range of cosmic epochs including cosmic dawn and reionization.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The stochastic interpolant framework unifies diffusion- and flow-based generative models, and the paper draws on this unified class to construct the reconstruction approach.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: By sampling multiple realizations from the learned conditional distribution, the method propagates reconstruction uncertainty into downstream tasks such as power spectrum estimation.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Training pairs of original and wedge-filtered 21-cm lightcones are generated by running 21-cm simulations across varied cosmological initial conditions and astrophysical parameters.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: Restoring spatial information lost to anisotropic wedge filtering is conceptually analogous to superresolution, recovering high-fidelity field structure from degraded observations.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional neural network architecture is used to learn the time-dependent velocity field that transports the wedge-filtered distribution to the recovered distribution.

title: Relaxed Equivariant Graph Neural Networks
abstract: 3D Euclidean symmetry equivariant neural networks have demonstrated notable success in modeling complex physical systems. We introduce a framework for relaxed $E(3)$ graph equivariant neural networks that can learn and represent symmetry breaking within continuous groups. Building on the existing e3nn framework, we propose the use of relaxed weights to allow for controlled symmetry breaking. We show empirically that these relaxed weights learn the correct amount of symmetry breaking.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends E(3) equivariant neural networks by introducing relaxed weights that allow controlled symmetry breaking while building on the e3nn framework.

conceptDetails.name: relaxed equivariance
conceptDetails.description: The novel framework introduced in this paper: a generalization of strict equivariance to continuous groups where weights are allowed to vary in a controlled, learnable manner to capture symmetry breaking.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Group representations, irreducible representations of O(3)/SO(3), and Clebsch-Gordan coefficients form the mathematical backbone of the relaxed equivariant convolution framework.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is a framework that learns and parametrizes symmetry-breaking factors within continuous groups, analogous to order parameters in Landau theory.

conceptDetails.name: relaxed weights
conceptDetails.description: The specific technical mechanism proposed — modifying the radial weight functions in tensor product decompositions to break equivariance in a parametric, data-driven way.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The work falls within geometric deep learning, extending symmetry-constrained architectures to handle approximate and broken symmetries in physical systems.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The relaxed equivariant architecture is implemented as a graph neural network where nodes carry irrep-typed features and edges encode geometric relationships via spherical harmonics.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Standard E(3) equivariance is the baseline the paper generalizes from, with relaxed weights recovering full equivariance when no symmetry breaking is present.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Crystal phase transitions and Landau theory are cited as key physical motivations for needing models that can parametrize symmetry-breaking factors.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Features are typed by irreducible representations of O(3), and the network learns representations that capture the degree of symmetry breaking in the data.

conceptDetails.name: molecular dynamics
conceptDetails.description: Learning ab initio molecular dynamics is cited as a primary application domain where E(3) equivariant networks excel and where symmetry breaking is physically relevant.

title: Automatic Environment Shaping is the Next Frontier in RL
abstract: Many roboticists dream of presenting a robot with a task in the evening and returning the next morning to find the robot capable of solving the task. What is preventing us from achieving this? Sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved impressive performance on challenging robotics tasks, but requires substantial human effort to set up the task in a way that is amenable to RL. It's our position that algorithmic improvements in policy optimization and other ideas should be guided towards resolving the primary bottleneck of shaping the training environment, i.e., designing observations, actions, rewards and simulation dynamics. Most practitioners don't tune the RL algorithm, but other environment parameters to obtain a desirable controller. We posit that scaling RL to diverse robotic tasks will only be achieved if the community focuses on automating environment shaping procedures.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The core framework examined in this paper, specifically sim-to-real RL for robotics, where the authors argue that environment shaping is the primary bottleneck to scaling.

conceptDetails.name: environment shaping
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to this paper: the holistic set of manual design choices (observations, actions, rewards, dynamics, curriculum) required to make RL training tractable for a given robotic task.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: The paradigm of training robot controllers in simulation and deploying them in reality, which the paper identifies as state-of-the-art but bottlenecked by manual environment shaping.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Reward function design is identified as a critical component of environment shaping that requires automation, as manually crafted rewards are a major source of human effort.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper advocates for automating the environment shaping process (observations, actions, rewards, dynamics) to enable autonomous RL scaling across diverse robotic tasks.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Scaling RL to diverse robotic tasks is the central challenge motivating the paper's position on automating environment shaping.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Sim-to-real transfer is a central paradigm discussed, where policies trained in simulation are deployed in real-world robotic environments.

conceptDetails.name: automatic curriculum design
conceptDetails.description: One of the key sub-problems within environment shaping that the paper highlights as needing automation to reduce human effort in RL training pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Reward shaping and observation/action space design are analogous to loss function design choices that currently require substantial manual tuning.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Simulation as a surrogate for real-world environments is foundational to the sim-to-real RL pipeline analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: Curriculum design and adaptive training distributions are discussed as environment shaping components that could benefit from automation.

title: Moment Unfolding
abstract: Deconvolving ("unfolding'') detector distortions is a critical step in the comparison of cross section measurements with theoretical predictions in particle and nuclear physics. However, most existing approaches require histogram binning while many theoretical predictions are at the level of statistical moments. We develop a new approach to directly unfold distribution moments as a function of another observable without having to first discretize the data. Our Moment Unfolding technique uses machine learning and is inspired by Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). We demonstrate the performance of this approach using jet substructure measurements in collider physics. With this illustrative example, we find that our Moment Unfolding protocol is more precise than bin-based approaches and is as or more precise than completely unbinned methods.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution of the paper: a new method to directly unfold statistical moments of distributions without histogram binning, correcting for detector distortions in particle physics measurements.

conceptDetails.name: boltzmann reweighting
conceptDetails.description: A novel technical innovation: the reweighting function is parameterized as a Boltzmann weight factor, enabling its parameters to be directly identified with statistical moments.

conceptDetails.name: moment estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a dedicated framework for estimating statistical moments of observable distributions as a function of another variable, bypassing full distribution unfolding.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Detector unfolding is fundamentally an inverse problem of recovering particle-level distributions from distorted detector-level observations.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: The Moment Unfolding architecture is directly inspired by GANs, using an adversarial discriminator to optimize reweighting such that reweighted simulation matches target data.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The method is demonstrated and benchmarked on jet substructure observables, a primary application domain where moment-level predictions from QCD are available.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper is motivated by and applied to cross-section measurements at particle colliders, where detector corrections are essential for comparing data to theory.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The method uses simulated detector-level events paired with particle-level truth to learn the reweighting function that corrects for detector effects.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: Reweighting particle-level simulations involves estimating likelihood ratios between data and simulation distributions, a key statistical component of the method.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations are used throughout as the reference simulation for training and evaluating the unfolding procedure.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: A response matrix or learned model of detector smearing is central to the unfolding problem the paper addresses.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The adversarial optimization objective is carefully designed so that the generator parameters correspond directly to the unfolded distribution moments.

title: The Type I Superluminous Supernova Catalog I: Light Curve Properties, Models, and Catalog Description
abstract: We present the most comprehensive catalog to date of Type I Superluminous Supernovae (SLSNe), a class of stripped envelope supernovae (SNe) characterized by exceptionally high luminosities. We have compiled a sample of 262 SLSNe reported through 2022 December 31. We verified the spectroscopic classification of each SLSN and collated an exhaustive data set of UV, optical and IR photometry from both publicly available data and our own FLEET observational follow-up program, totaling over 30,000 photometric detections. Using these data we derive observational parameters such as the peak absolute magnitudes, rise and decline timescales, as well as bolometric luminosities, temperature and photospheric radius evolution for all SLSNe. Additionally, we model all light curves using a hybrid model that includes contributions from both a magnetar central engine and the radioactive decay of $^{56}$Ni. We explore correlations among various physical and observational parameters, and recover the previously found relation between ejecta mass and magnetar spin, as well as the overall progenitor pre-explosion mass distribution with a peak at $\approx 6.5$ M$_\odot$. We find no significant redshift dependence for any parameter, and no evidence for distinct sub-types of SLSNe. We find that $< 3$\% of SLSNe are best fit with a significant contribution from radioactive decay $\gtrsim 50$\%, representing a set of relatively dim and slowly declining SNe. We provide several analytical tools designed to simulate typical SLSN light curves across a broad range of wavelengths and phases, enabling accurate K-corrections, bolometric scaling calculations, and inclusion of SLSNe in survey simulations or future comparison works. The complete catalog, including all of the photometry, models, and derived parameters, is made available as an open-source resource on GitHub.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper verifies spectroscopic classifications for 262 Type I SLSNe and explores whether distinct sub-types exist within the population.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar central engine
conceptDetails.description: A millisecond magnetar spin-down model is the primary power source fitted to the light curves, with spin period and magnetic field strength as free parameters.

conceptDetails.name: bolometric light curves
conceptDetails.description: Bolometric luminosity, photospheric temperature, and radius evolution are derived for all 262 SLSNe from multi-band UV/optical/IR photometry.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian parameter estimation is used to fit the hybrid magnetar+radioactive decay light curve model and derive posterior distributions for physical parameters.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The progenitor pre-explosion mass distribution is derived, with a peak at approximately 6.5 solar masses, connecting SLSN properties to massive star evolution.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Analytical tools are developed to simulate typical SLSN light curves across broad wavelength and phase ranges, serving as fast surrogates for survey simulations and K-corrections.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The hybrid magnetar+56Ni model is validated against the full sample, finding that fewer than 3% of SLSNe require significant radioactive decay contribution.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Correlations among observational and physical parameters (ejecta mass, magnetar spin, peak magnitude, timescales) are quantified through statistical regression analysis.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: K-corrections and bolometric scaling relations are carefully calibrated using the multi-wavelength photometry, enabling cross-survey comparisons.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling techniques underpin the light curve modeling and uncertainty estimation across the full catalog of 262 events.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The catalog tools enable inclusion of SLSNe in survey simulations, requiring forward modeling of light curve observables from physical parameters.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The catalog analysis identifies outlier SLSNe that deviate from the dominant magnetar-powered population, including dim, slowly-declining events.

title: Find the haystacks, then look for needles: The rate of strongly lensed transients in galaxy-galaxy strong gravitational lenses
abstract: The time delay between appearances of multiple images of a gravitationally lensed supernova (glSN) is sensitive to the Hubble constant, $H_0$. As well as time delays, a lensed host galaxy is needed to enable precise inference of $H_0$. In this work we investigate the connection between discoverable lensed transients and their host galaxies. We find that LSST will discover 88 glSNe per year, of which $54\%$ will also have a strongly lensed host. The rates can change by approximately 30 percent uncertainty depending primarily on the choice of unlensed SN population and uncertainties in the redshift evolution of the deflector population, but the fraction of glSNe with a lensed host is consistently around a half. LSST will discover 20 glSNe per year in systems that could plausibly have been identified by Euclid as galaxy-galaxy lenses before the discovery of the glSN. Such systems have preferentially longer time delays and therefore are well suited for cosmography. We define a golden sample of glSNe Ia with time delays over 10 days, image separations greater than 0.8 arcseconds, and a multiply imaged host. For this golden sample, we find $91\%$ occur in systems that should already be discoverable as galaxy-galaxy lenses in Euclid. For cosmology with glSNe, monitoring Euclid lenses is a plausible alternative to searching the entire LSST alert stream.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational lensing time delays
conceptDetails.description: Time delays between multiply-imaged lensed supernova appearances are the primary observable used to constrain H0 in this work.

conceptDetails.name: strong gravitational lensing rates
conceptDetails.description: Novel computation of the rate of strongly lensed supernovae with lensed hosts discoverable by LSST and pre-identified by Euclid as galaxy-galaxy lens systems.

conceptDetails.name: survey optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper evaluates monitoring Euclid-identified galaxy-galaxy lenses as a targeted alternative to searching the full LSST alert stream for glSNe.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations are used to model populations of gravitationally lensed supernovae and their host galaxies across redshift distributions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling is employed to generate simulated populations of lensed transients and compute discovery rates for LSST and Euclid surveys.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: Type Ia supernovae are identified as the golden sample for cosmography due to their standardizable luminosity and sensitivity to Hubble constant measurements.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: The Hubble constant tension between early- and late-time measurements motivates this study as strong lensing time delays provide an independent cosmological probe.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detection thresholds for lensed host galaxies and glSN image separations are modeled to define the golden cosmographic sample.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically quantifies ~30% uncertainties in glSN discovery rates arising from SN population choices and deflector redshift evolution.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Lens modelling and H0 inference from time-delay measurements rely on Bayesian frameworks to constrain cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Supernova occurrence is modeled as a stochastic process tied to star formation rates and galaxy stellar mass functions across redshift.

title: A multicategory jet image classification framework using deep neural network
abstract: Jet point cloud images are high dimensional data structures that needs to be transformed to a separable feature space for machine learning algorithms to distinguish them with simple decision boundaries. In this article, the authors focus on jet category separability by particle and jet feature extraction, resulting in more efficient training of a simple deep neural network, resulting in a computational efficient interpretable model for jet classification. The methodology is tested with three to five categories of jets from the JetNet benchmark jet tagging dataset, resulting in comparable performance to particle flow network. This work demonstrates that high dimensional datasets represented in separable latent spaces lead to simpler architectures for jet classification.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core application domain, classifying jets from LHC proton-proton collisions into categories including top quarks, W/Z bosons, gluons, and light quarks.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The central methodological contribution — extracting particle and jet features using a point cloud transformer and 1D CNN autoencoder to create separable latent representations.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The primary task: multicategory jet classification using a deep neural network trained on extracted features from the JetNet benchmark dataset.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A 1D CNN-based autoencoder is used for jet-level feature extraction, transforming raw jet observables into compact representations.

conceptDetails.name: point cloud classification
conceptDetails.description: A novel method treating jets as point clouds of particles and applying transformer-based feature extraction to enable image-free classification.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Multihead attention is employed in the point cloud transformer for particle feature embedding to capture inter-particle relationships within a jet.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The method transforms high-dimensional jet point cloud data into a separable low-dimensional latent space to enable simpler classification boundaries.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A 1D convolutional autoencoder is used to compress jet-level features into a compact latent representation for downstream classification.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Particle feature embeddings via a point cloud transformer map raw four-momentum vectors into a structured feature space for improved class separability.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The work is grounded in LHC pp collision data analysis, with jets being the primary physics objects produced and studied.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Jet reconstruction from detector signals is the physics context in which the proposed ML classification framework operates.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The authors explicitly aim for a computationally efficient and interpretable model as a goal of the simpler DNN architecture trained on extracted features.

title: Anomaly-aware summary statistic from data batches
abstract: Signal-agnostic data exploration based on machine learning could unveil very subtle statistical deviations of collider data from the expected Standard Model of particle physics. The beneficial impact of a large training sample on machine learning solutions motivates the exploration of increasingly large and inclusive samples of acquired data with resource efficient computational methods. In this work we consider the New Physics Learning Machine (NPLM), a multivariate goodness-of-fit test built on the Neyman-Pearson maximum-likelihood-ratio construction, and we address the problem of testing large size samples under computational and storage resource constraints. We propose to perform parallel NPLM routines over batches of the data, and to combine them by locally aggregating over the data-to-reference density ratios learnt by each batch. The resulting data hypothesis defining the likelihood-ratio test is thus shared over the batches, and complies with the assumption that the expected rate of new physical processes is time invariant. We show that this method outperforms the simple sum of the independent tests run over the batches, and can recover, or even surpass, the sensitivity of the single test run over the full data. Beside the significant advantage for the offline application of NPLM to large size samples, the proposed approach offers new prospects toward the use of NPLM to construct anomaly-aware summary statistics in quasi-online data streaming scenarios.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central problem addressed, specifically statistical anomaly detection to identify distributional deviations of collider data from Standard Model expectations using the NPLM algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: batch aggregation
conceptDetails.description: Novel contribution combining density ratios from parallel NPLM instances over data batches into a single shared data hypothesis for the global likelihood-ratio test.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: NPLM is explicitly framed as a multivariate goodness-of-fit test, and the paper extends its applicability to large datasets via batch processing.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The Neyman-Pearson maximum-likelihood-ratio construction is the statistical foundation of NPLM, with the proposed method aggregating local density ratios learned by each batch.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Classical Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing underpins the NPLM test statistic comparing observed data to a reference distribution.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics motivation is signal-agnostic searches for rare new physical processes beyond the Standard Model at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Each parallel NPLM instance learns a data-to-reference density ratio over its batch, and these ratios are locally aggregated to form the combined test statistic.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm is validated on realistic LHC datasets including a dimuon final state and 24-dimensional CMS L1 trigger data.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A core contribution is addressing computational and storage constraints by parallelizing NPLM over data batches to handle large-scale datasets.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops signal-agnostic techniques for detecting subtle rare anomalous signals without prior knowledge of new physics signatures.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The method is applied to large-scale CMS L1 trigger data and motivates quasi-online streaming scenarios for anomaly detection at the trigger level.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Standard Model reference samples are generated via Monte Carlo simulation for use as the nominal model in two-sample goodness-of-fit testing.

title: Boosting Soft Q-Learning by Bounding
abstract: An agent's ability to leverage past experience is critical for efficiently solving new tasks. Prior work has focused on using value function estimates to obtain zero-shot approximations for solutions to a new task. In soft Q-learning, we show how any value function estimate can also be used to derive double-sided bounds on the optimal value function. The derived bounds lead to new approaches for boosting training performance which we validate experimentally. Notably, we find that the proposed framework suggests an alternative method for updating the Q-function, leading to boosted performance.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The core framework in which agents learn optimal policies through interaction with an environment, extended here with entropy regularization for robust soft Q-learning.

conceptDetails.name: value function bounding
conceptDetails.description: The central novel contribution: deriving double-sided bounds on the optimal Q-function from any arbitrary (suboptimal) prior estimate, enabling improved training.

conceptDetails.name: entropy-regularized rl
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework extending standard RL with an entropic control cost relative to a reference policy, which enables the exact analytic results used to derive the bounds.

conceptDetails.name: soft q-learning
conceptDetails.description: The specific entropy-regularized Q-learning algorithm that is boosted by the proposed bounding framework and clipping methods.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses optimizing the soft Q-function (action-value function) under an entropy-regularized reward objective to derive tighter bounds on optimal values.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes novel hard-clipping and soft-clip loss variants that incorporate derived value function bounds into the Bellman training objective.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper leverages prior value function estimates from previous tasks as bounds to accelerate learning on new tasks, a form of knowledge transfer across RL tasks.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Entropy-regularized MDPs yield stochastic optimal policies that are provably robust to perturbations in rewards and dynamics, motivating the soft Q-learning framework.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The MDP framework underlying the paper models transitions as stochastic processes, and the optimal policy is a Boltzmann distribution over actions.

title: Simulating moiré quantum matter with neural network
abstract: Moiré materials provide an ideal platform for exploring quantum phases of matter. However, solving the many-electron problem in moiré systems is challenging due to strong correlation effects. We introduce a powerful variational representation of quantum states, many-body neural Bloch wavefunction, to solve many-electron problems in moiré materials accurately and efficiently. Applying our method to the semiconductor heterobilayer WSe2/WS2 , we obtain a generalized Wigner crystal at filling factor n = 1/3, a Mott insulator n = 1, and a correlated insulator with local magnetic moments and antiferromagnetic spin correlation at n = 2. Our neural network approach improves the simulation accuracy of strongly interacting moiré materials and paves the way for discovery of new quantum phases with variational learning principle in a unified framework.

conceptDetails.name: moiré neural wavefunction
conceptDetails.description: A novel variational ansatz combining Bloch basis functions, neural network backflow, and neural momentum transformation specifically designed for periodic moiré systems.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a many-body neural Bloch wavefunction as a variational representation of quantum states to solve the many-electron problem in moiré materials.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is simulating moiré quantum matter — including Wigner crystals and Mott insulators — using neural network-based quantum simulation.

conceptDetails.name: correlated electron phases
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies and characterizes strongly correlated phases — Wigner crystals, Mott insulators, and antiferromagnetic correlated insulators — in semiconductor moiré heterostructures.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The continuum model Hamiltonian for TMD heterobilayers — encoding kinetic energy, moiré potential, and Coulomb interactions — is the starting point for all simulations.

conceptDetails.name: fermionic neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Building on FermiNet and PauliNet architectures, the method extends fermionic neural network wavefunctions to periodic moiré lattice systems.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Message passing graph networks are used to implement neural network backflow, enabling the model to capture many-body correlations among electrons.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper maps out a rich phase diagram of moiré materials as a function of filling factor, identifying distinct quantum phases including insulators and electron solids.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The many-body neural Bloch wavefunction is constructed to preserve the periodicity of the moiré lattice and the antisymmetry of fermionic wavefunctions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo sampling is used to evaluate expectation values of observables and optimize the neural wavefunction parameters.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Neural network backflow and momentum transformation learn to represent strongly correlated many-body quantum states beyond mean-field approximations.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The method uses a first-principles variational learning approach to optimize the neural wavefunction parameters against the moiré Hamiltonian.

title: Derived Moduli Spaces of Nonlinear PDEs II: Variational Tricomplex and BV Formalism
abstract: This paper is the second in a series of works dedicated to studying non-linear partial differential equations via derived geometric methods. We study a natural derived enhancement of the de Rham complex of a non-linear PDE via algebro-geometric techniques and examine its consequences for the functional differential calculus on the space of solutions. Applications to the BV-formalism with and without boundary conditions are discussed.

conceptDetails.name: derived algebraic geometry
conceptDetails.description: The primary mathematical framework of the paper, used to construct homotopy-invariant analogs of the variational bicomplex for nonlinear PDEs via algebro-geometric techniques.

conceptDetails.name: variational tricomplex
