conceptDetails.description: The paper's main novel contribution: a derived enhancement of the classical variational bicomplex encoding up-to-homotopy differential calculus on solution spaces of nonlinear PDEs.

conceptDetails.name: bv formalism
conceptDetails.description: The Batalin-Vilkovisky formalism for quantizing gauge field theories is the central application target, with the derived variational tricomplex providing its rigorous geometric foundation.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops geometric tools for classical and quantum field theories, including non-Lagrangian theories such as higher-spin gauge theories and superconformal theories.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational calculus and Euler-Lagrange systems are studied through the derived variational tricomplex, extending classical Lagrangian field theory to a homotopical setting.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Shifted symplectic structures on derived solution spaces provide the analog of Hamiltonian/symplectic geometry for nonlinear PDEs in the D-geometric setting.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge symmetries and their intrinsic treatment via D-geometry are central, enabling a coordinate-free account of symmetry reductions and gauge-invariant constructions.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Gauge groups acting on solution spaces are encoded through dg-Lie algebras and Chevalley-Eilenberg algebras within the derived deformation-theoretic framework.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Vinogradov's C-spectral sequence and derived spectral sequences for the variational bicomplex are used to compute cohomological invariants of nonlinear PDEs.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau geometry, Gromov-Witten theory, and Donaldson-Thomas theory on Calabi-Yau 3,4-folds motivate the shifted symplectic framework applied to PDE moduli spaces.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Formal neighbourhood constructions around classical solutions capture perturbative (infinitesimal) aspects of field theories, connecting to effective field theory expansions.

title: Demonstration of neutron identification in neutrino interactions in the MicroBooNE liquid argon time projection chamber
abstract: A significant challenge in measurements of neutrino oscillations is reconstructing the incoming neutrino energies. While modern fully-active tracking calorimeters such as liquid argon time projection chambers in principle allow the measurement of all final state particles above some detection threshold, undetected neutrons remain a considerable source of missing energy with little to no data constraining their production rates and kinematics. We present the first demonstration of tagging neutrino-induced neutrons in liquid argon time projection chambers using secondary protons emitted from neutron-argon interactions in the MicroBooNE detector. We describe the method developed to identify neutrino-induced neutrons and demonstrate its performance using neutrons produced in muon-neutrino charged current interactions. The method is validated using a small subset of MicroBooNE's total dataset. The selection yields a sample with $60\%$ of selected tracks corresponding to neutron-induced secondary protons.

conceptDetails.name: neutron tagging
conceptDetails.description: Novel technique of identifying neutrino-induced neutrons in a LArTPC by reconstructing secondary proton tracks from neutron-argon inelastic scatters, demonstrated here for the first time.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents the first demonstration of identifying neutrino-induced neutrons in a liquid argon TPC using secondary protons from neutron-argon inelastic scatters.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing the full kinematics of muon-neutrino charged current interactions including identifying displaced neutron candidate tracks among final-state particles.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino-nucleus interactions
conceptDetails.description: Measurements of muon-neutrino charged current interactions with argon nuclei constrain neutron production rates and kinematics needed for precision oscillation experiments.

conceptDetails.name: missing energy reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Addresses the systematic bias in neutrino energy reconstruction caused by undetected neutrons carrying away kinetic energy in oscillation measurements.

conceptDetails.name: particle tracking
conceptDetails.description: Tracking secondary proton candidates produced by neutron-argon inelastic scatters spatially displaced from the primary neutrino interaction vertex within the LArTPC.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations of neutrino interactions and detector response are used to develop and validate the neutron identification method against data.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detecting the neutron-induced secondary proton signal above backgrounds in the MicroBooNE dataset by exploiting the spatial displacement and kinematics of candidate tracks.

conceptDetails.name: calorimetry
conceptDetails.description: The LArTPC functions as a fully-active tracking calorimeter capable of measuring all charged final-state particles above threshold, motivating neutron identification to recover missing energy.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Simulation-based methods model neutrino-nucleus interactions, neutron transport in argon, and detector response for predicting signal and background yields.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Selection criteria are developed to classify candidate tracks as neutron-induced secondary protons versus background processes, achieving 60% signal purity.

title: Improving neutrino energy estimation of charged-current interaction events with recurrent neural networks in MicroBooNE
abstract: We present a deep learning-based method for estimating the neutrino energy of charged-current neutrino-argon interactions. We employ a recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture for neutrino energy estimation in the MicroBooNE experiment, utilizing liquid argon time projection chamber (LArTPC) detector technology. Traditional energy estimation approaches in LArTPCs, which largely rely on reconstructing and summing visible energies, often experience sizable biases and resolution smearing because of the complex nature of neutrino interactions and the detector response. The estimation of neutrino energy can be improved after considering the kinematics information of reconstructed final-state particles. Utilizing kinematic information of reconstructed particles, the deep learning-based approach shows improved resolution and reduced bias for the muon neutrino Monte Carlo simulation sample compared to the traditional approach. In order to address the common concern about the effectiveness of this method on experimental data, the RNN-based energy estimator is further examined and validated with dedicated data-simulation consistency tests using MicroBooNE data. We also assess its potential impact on a neutrino oscillation study after accounting for all statistical and systematic uncertainties and show that it enhances physics sensitivity. This method has good potential to improve the performance of other physics analyses.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: An RNN architecture is the core deep learning method used to estimate neutrino energy from sequences of reconstructed final-state particle kinematics.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino energy reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Novel deep learning approach to reconstructing neutrino energy that reduces bias and improves resolution compared to traditional visible-energy summation methods.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Reconstruction of final-state particles and their kinematics from LArTPC detector data is the primary input to the energy estimation pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The MicroBooNE experiment detects muon neutrino charged-current interactions in liquid argon, providing the experimental context for this work.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Neutrino energy estimation is formulated as a regression task, predicting a continuous energy value from reconstructed particle kinematics.

conceptDetails.name: lartpc detector
conceptDetails.description: The liquid argon time projection chamber technology used in MicroBooNE presents unique challenges for energy reconstruction that motivate the RNN approach.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulation of the MicroBooNE LArTPC detector response is used to train and validate the RNN-based energy estimator.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Dedicated data-simulation consistency tests are performed to validate the RNN-based estimator on real MicroBooNE experimental data.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Statistical and systematic uncertainties are fully accounted for when assessing the RNN estimator's impact on neutrino oscillation sensitivity.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulation samples are used extensively for training, evaluation, and systematic uncertainty estimation throughout the analysis.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: Neutrino oscillations and charged-current interactions probed in this work are studied as extensions and tests of the Standard Model.

title: QCD constraints on isospin-dense matter and the nuclear equation of state
abstract: Understanding the behavior of dense hadronic matter is a central goal in nuclear physics as it governs the nature and dynamics of astrophysical objects such as supernovae and neutron stars. Because of the non-perturbative nature of quantum chromodynamics (QCD), little is known rigorously about hadronic matter in these extreme conditions. Here, lattice QCD calculations are used to compute thermodynamic quantities and the equation of state of QCD over a wide range of isospin chemical potentials with controlled systematic uncertainties. Agreement is seen with chiral perturbation theory when the chemical potential is small. Comparison to perturbative QCD at large chemical potential allows for an estimate of the gap in the superconducting phase, and this quantity is seen to agree with perturbative determinations. Since the partition function for an isospin chemical potential, $μ_I$, bounds the partition function for a baryon chemical potential $μ_B=3μ_I/2$, these calculations also provide rigorous non-perturbative QCD bounds on the symmetric nuclear matter equation of state over a wide range of baryon densities for the first time.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary computational method used to perform non-perturbative QCD calculations of thermodynamic quantities and the equation of state at finite isospin chemical potential.

conceptDetails.name: isospin chemical potential
conceptDetails.description: The central thermodynamic variable over which calculations are performed, enabling access to dense QCD matter while circumventing the sign problem.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear equation of state
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides the first rigorous non-perturbative QCD bounds on the symmetric nuclear matter equation of state over a wide range of baryon densities.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Chiral perturbation theory is used as an effective field theory to validate lattice QCD results at small isospin chemical potential.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: QCD as a quantum field theory is the foundational framework for all calculations of hadronic matter properties.

conceptDetails.name: color superconductivity
conceptDetails.description: The superconducting gap in the color-superconducting phase is determined by comparing lattice QCD results to perturbative QCD predictions at large isospin chemical potential.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the color-superconducting phase transition and the QCD phase diagram as a function of isospin and baryon chemical potentials.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: A Bayesian model mixing approach is used to combine chiral perturbation theory, lattice QCD, and perturbative QCD to determine the full equation of state.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides the first systematically-controlled, non-perturbative QCD constraints on the nuclear equation of state with complete quantification of systematic uncertainties.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling is used in the lattice QCD calculations to compute path integrals and extract thermodynamic observables.

title: An Elliptic Kernel Unsupervised Autoencoder-Graph Convolutional Network Ensemble Model for Hyperspectral Unmixing
abstract: Spectral Unmixing is an important technique in remote sensing used to analyze hyperspectral images to identify endmembers and estimate abundance maps. Over the past few decades, performance of techniques for endmember extraction and fractional abundance map estimation have significantly improved. This article presents an ensemble model workflow called Autoencoder Graph Ensemble Model (AEGEM) designed to extract endmembers and fractional abundance maps. An elliptical kernel is applied to measure spectral distances, generating the adjacency matrix within the elliptical neighborhood. This information is used to construct an elliptical graph, with centroids as senders and remaining pixels within the geometry as receivers. The next step involves stacking abundance maps, senders, and receivers as inputs to a Graph Convolutional Network, which processes this input to refine abundance maps. Finally, an ensemble decision-making process determines the best abundance maps based on root mean square error metric. The proposed AEGEM is assessed with benchmark datasets such as Samson, Jasper, and Urban, outperforming results obtained by baseline algorithms. For the Samson dataset, AEGEM excels in three abundance maps: water, tree and soil yielding values of 0.081, 0.158, and 0.182, respectively. For the Jasper dataset, results are improved for the tree and water endmembers with values of 0.035 and 0.060 in that order, as well as for the mean average of the spectral angle distance metric 0.109. For the Urban dataset, AEGEM outperforms previous results for the abundance maps of roof and asphalt, achieving values of 0.135 and 0.240, respectively. Additionally, for the endmembers of grass and roof, AEGEM achieves values of 0.063 and 0.094.

conceptDetails.name: hyperspectral unmixing
conceptDetails.description: The central task of the paper: decomposing hyperspectral image pixels into constituent material endmembers and their fractional abundances.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional autoencoder forms the first stage of AEGEM, performing unsupervised endmember extraction and initial abundance map estimation.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A Graph Convolutional Network refines the abundance maps by processing spatial-spectral relationships encoded in an elliptical graph structure.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: The core AEGEM workflow uses an ensemble decision-making process to select the best abundance maps across multiple model outputs using RMSE.

conceptDetails.name: elliptical graph construction
conceptDetails.description: A novel graph structure built using elliptical kernel neighborhoods, with pixel centroids as senders and surrounding pixels as receivers.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional layers are used in both the autoencoder and GCN components to capture local spectral-spatial features in hyperspectral images.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: An elliptical kernel measures spectral distances between pixels to construct the adjacency matrix for the graph representation.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Spectral unmixing inherently reduces high-dimensional hyperspectral data into compact endmember signatures and fractional abundance maps.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The autoencoder learns latent representations of spectral signatures that encode endmember and abundance information without supervision.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Spectral unmixing is formulated as an inverse problem: recovering pure material spectra and their fractional contributions from mixed pixel observations.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: AEGEM extracts spatial and spectral features at multiple stages to progressively refine endmember and abundance map estimates.

title: Separating the "Chirp" from the "Chat": Self-supervised Visual Grounding of Sound and Language
abstract: We present DenseAV, a novel dual encoder grounding architecture that learns high-resolution, semantically meaningful, and audio-visually aligned features solely through watching videos. We show that DenseAV can discover the ``meaning'' of words and the ``location'' of sounds without explicit localization supervision. Furthermore, it automatically discovers and distinguishes between these two types of associations without supervision. We show that DenseAV's localization abilities arise from a new multi-head feature aggregation operator that directly compares dense image and audio representations for contrastive learning. In contrast, many other systems that learn ``global'' audio and video representations cannot localize words and sound. Finally, we contribute two new datasets to improve the evaluation of AV representations through speech and sound prompted semantic segmentation. On these and other datasets we show DenseAV dramatically outperforms the prior art on speech and sound prompted semantic segmentation. DenseAV outperforms the previous state-of-the-art, ImageBind, on cross-modal retrieval using fewer than half of the parameters. Project Page: \href{https://aka.ms/denseav}{https://aka.ms/denseav}

conceptDetails.name: audio-visual grounding
conceptDetails.description: The central task of associating spoken words and environmental sounds with the specific spatial regions of a video frame that produce or correspond to them.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: DenseAV learns audio-visual correspondences solely from watching unlabeled videos, without any explicit localization or semantic supervision.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The core training objective aligns paired audio-visual representations while pushing apart unpaired signals using a dense similarity-based contrastive loss.

conceptDetails.name: dense cross-modal alignment
conceptDetails.description: A key technical contribution that compares full spatial feature maps (rather than global pooled tokens) across modalities to enable localization-aware contrastive learning.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper learns high-resolution, semantically meaningful audio-visual features that support downstream tasks like segmentation and retrieval.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: DenseAV's multi-head aggregator automatically separates speech-semantic (language meaning) from non-speech (sound location) audio-visual associations without supervision.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: A novel multi-head feature aggregation operator generalizes multi-head attention to form dense similarity volumes across spatial and temporal audio-visual dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Modality-specific backbones produce dense local feature maps from audio and visual inputs that preserve spatial and temporal structure for fine-grained grounding.

conceptDetails.name: multimodal segmentation
conceptDetails.description: Two new benchmark datasets introduced for evaluating speech- and sound-prompted semantic segmentation using audio-visual representations as queries.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Cross-modal audio and visual embeddings are compared at the local feature level to enable pixel-level localization of words and sounds.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Dense similarity volume visualizations reveal which image regions correspond to specific spoken words or ambient sounds, providing interpretable cross-modal grounding.

title: A Heterotic Kähler Gravity and the Distance Conjecture
abstract: Deformations of the heterotic superpotential give rise to a topological holomorphic theory with similarities to both Kodaira-Spencer gravity and holomorphic Chern-Simons theory. Although the action is cubic, it is only quadratic in the complex structure deformations (the Beltrami differential). Treated separately, for large fluxes, or alternatively at large distances in the background complex structure moduli space, these fields can be integrated out to obtain a new field theory in the remaining fields, which describe the complexified hermitian and gauge degrees of freedom. We investigate properties of this new holomorphic theory, and in particular connections to the swampland distance conjecture in the context of heterotic string theory. In the process, we define a new type of symplectic cohomology theory, where the background complex structure Beltrami differential plays the role of the symplectic form.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper works entirely within heterotic string theory, deriving topological field theories from the heterotic superpotential and studying moduli spaces of heterotic compactifications.

conceptDetails.name: swampland distance conjecture
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the swampland distance conjecture in heterotic string theory, finding an exponentially light tower of states at infinite geodesic distance in the complex structure moduli space consistent with the conjecture.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The central technical operation of the paper is integrating out complex structure deformations at large flux or large distance to obtain a new effective field theory in the remaining Kähler and gauge degrees of freedom.

conceptDetails.name: heterotic kähler gravity
conceptDetails.description: A new topological holomorphic field theory derived from the heterotic superpotential that describes complexified hermitian and gauge degrees of freedom after integrating out complex structure deformations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs and analyzes topological holomorphic quantum field theories with features of both Kodaira-Spencer gravity and holomorphic Chern-Simons theory.

conceptDetails.name: symplectic cohomology
conceptDetails.description: A new cohomology theory defined in the paper where the background complex structure Beltrami differential plays the role of the symplectic form, arising naturally from the gauge structure of the effective action.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Holomorphic Courant algebroids provide the algebraic and geometric framework governing the symmetries of heterotic geometries and the Maurer-Cartan equation structure of the theory.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Elliptic differential complexes are constructed and their ellipticity is proved to establish well-definedness of the gauge structure, enabling quantization and study of topological invariants.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The gauge symmetry structure of the new effective theories is analyzed, including how it forms elliptic complexes whose breaking patterns connect to anomalies and topological invariants.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: UV completion with gravity is the overarching motivation, and the paper studies how EFT descriptions break down at infinite distance in moduli space.

title: Stochastic logic in biased coupled photonic probabilistic bits
abstract: Optical computing often employs tailor-made hardware to implement specific algorithms, trading generality for improved performance in key aspects like speed and power efficiency. An important computing approach that is still missing its corresponding optical hardware is probabilistic computing, used e.g. for solving difficult combinatorial optimization problems. In this study, we propose an experimentally viable photonic approach to solve arbitrary probabilistic computing problems. Our method relies on the insight that coherent Ising machines composed of coupled and biased optical parametric oscillators can emulate stochastic logic. We demonstrate the feasibility of our approach by using numerical simulations equivalent to the full density matrix formulation of coupled optical parametric oscillators.

conceptDetails.name: optical parametric oscillators
conceptDetails.description: Networks of coupled and biased OPOs are the core physical hardware proposed to realize probabilistic bits and stochastic logic in the optical domain.

conceptDetails.name: probabilistic computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes a photonic hardware platform specifically designed to implement probabilistic computing using p-bits and stochastic logic gates.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The paper models OPO networks using stochastic differential equations to emulate probabilistic bit behavior and stochastic logic circuits.

conceptDetails.name: coherent ising machines
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends coherent Ising machines by incorporating a Zeeman bias term, enabling full stochastic logic beyond previous coupling-only implementations.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The Ising Hamiltonian with coupling and Zeeman terms is the central mathematical framework mapping stochastic logic problems to OPO network dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: OPOs undergo a bifurcation (phase transition) above threshold where they randomly choose between two steady-state phases, which is exploited to implement p-bits.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The invertible logic circuit concept enables circuits to infer inputs from outputs, a form of inverse problem solving central to p-computing.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Numerical simulations based on the full density matrix formulation are used to validate the feasibility of the photonic probabilistic computing approach.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Probabilistic computing applications demonstrated include quantum Monte Carlo and classical annealing, contextualizing the platform's utility.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Probabilistic computing and p-bits are demonstrated to be applicable to Bayesian inference tasks, one of the key use cases motivating the platform.

title: OccamLLM: Fast and Exact Language Model Arithmetic in a Single Step
abstract: Despite significant advancements in text generation and reasoning, Large Language Models (LLMs) still face challenges in accurately performing complex arithmetic operations. Language model systems often enable LLMs to generate code for arithmetic operations to achieve accurate calculations. However, this approach compromises speed and security, and fine-tuning risks the language model losing prior capabilities. We propose a framework that enables exact arithmetic in a single autoregressive step, providing faster, more secure, and more interpretable LLM systems with arithmetic capabilities. We use the hidden states of a LLM to control a symbolic architecture that performs arithmetic. Our implementation using Llama 3 with OccamNet as a symbolic model (OccamLlama) achieves 100\% accuracy on single arithmetic operations ($+,-,\times,÷,\sin{},\cos{},\log{},\exp{},\sqrt{}$), outperforming GPT 4o with and without a code interpreter. Furthermore, OccamLlama outperforms GPT 4o with and without a code interpreter on average across a range of mathematical problem solving benchmarks, demonstrating that OccamLLMs can excel in arithmetic tasks, even surpassing much larger models. We will make our code public shortly.

conceptDetails.name: single-step arithmetic
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary contribution is enabling exact arithmetic computation within a single autoregressive decoding step, eliminating multi-step code generation overhead.

conceptDetails.name: neurosymbolic integration
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is a hybrid architecture combining a neural LLM with a symbolic arithmetic system (OccamNet), bridging neural and symbolic AI paradigms.

conceptDetails.name: hidden state control
conceptDetails.description: LLM internal hidden states are used to dynamically configure and control the weights of the symbolic OccamNet module during each autoregressive step.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: OccamLLM provides an interpretable arithmetic process by making the symbolic computation steps visible and auditable, in contrast to opaque neural approaches.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: OccamNet uses sparse function representations to select and execute only the relevant arithmetic operations from a symbolic function space.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: OccamNet acts as a surrogate symbolic module controlled by LLM hidden states to perform exact arithmetic that the neural model cannot reliably compute.

conceptDetails.name: transferlearning
conceptDetails.description: The LLM backbone (Llama 3) is kept frozen throughout training, preserving its general language capabilities while the symbolic decoder is trained separately.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly contrasts OccamLLM with fine-tuning approaches, showing that fine-tuning risks catastrophic forgetting of prior LLM capabilities.

conceptDetails.name: mixture of experts
conceptDetails.description: The decoder module routes computation between the language model output and the symbolic OccamNet output, acting as a learned gating mechanism analogous to mixture-of-experts routing.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper explores custom loss functions for training the decoder to correctly route between LLM and symbolic model outputs.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The framework is motivated by enabling LLM-powered automated scientific discovery where reliable arithmetic is essential.

title: Towards Universal Unfolding of Detector Effects in High-Energy Physics using Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models
abstract: Correcting for detector effects in experimental data, particularly through unfolding, is critical for enabling precision measurements in high-energy physics. However, traditional unfolding methods face challenges in scalability, flexibility, and dependence on simulations. We introduce a novel approach to multidimensional object-wise unfolding using conditional Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (cDDPM). Our method utilizes the cDDPM for a non-iterative, flexible posterior sampling approach, incorporating distribution moments as conditioning information, which exhibits a strong inductive bias that allows it to generalize to unseen physics processes without explicitly assuming the underlying distribution. Our results highlight the potential of this method as a step towards a "universal" unfolding tool that reduces dependence on truth-level assumptions, while enabling the unfolding of a wide range of measured distributions with improved adaptability and accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Conditional Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (cDDPM) are the core generative architecture used to sample from the posterior distribution P(x|y) for non-iterative unfolding.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: The central problem addressed: correcting detector distortions in HEP data to recover true particle-level distributions from observed reconstructed-level data.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The cDDPM learns to sample from the Bayesian posterior P(x|y), enabling flexible and unbiased recovery of truth-level particle kinematics from detector-level observations.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Unfolding is formulated as an inverse problem—recovering the true distribution f_true(x) from the detector-level observation f_det(y) via inversion of a Fredholm integral equation.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The proposed method belongs to the class of generative approaches to unfolding, contrasted with reweighting and traditional binned methods.

conceptDetails.name: distribution moment conditioning
conceptDetails.description: Novel conditioning strategy using statistical moments of distributions as inductive bias, allowing the model to generalize across different underlying physics processes.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayes' theorem is the theoretical foundation for expressing the unfolding posterior P(x|y) in terms of the detector response P(y|x) and the prior f_true(x).

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Detector simulation defines the response function P(y|x) and is used to generate training data; the paper aims to reduce dependence on simulation-specific assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The application domain is proton-proton collisions at the LHC, with benchmark processes including W+jets, tt-bar, and leptoquark events.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Object-wise unfolding reconstructs individual particle or jet kinematic properties, preserving correlations between observables rather than operating on binned histograms.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The generalized unfolder exhibits transfer learning by generalizing to unseen physics processes after training on a diverse mixture of known processes.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The method is situated within the broader landscape of simulation-based inference approaches, where training relies on paired simulated particle-level and detector-level samples.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: A key motivation for universal unfolding is enabling searches for undiscovered particles by unfolding distributions for processes not seen during training.

title: Sheaf stable pairs, Quot-schemes, and birational geometry
abstract: In this paper we build bridges between moduli theory of sheaf stable pairs on one hand and birational geometry on the other hand. We will in particular treat moduli of sheaf stable pairs on smooth projective curves in detail and present some calculations in low degrees. We will also outline problems in various directions.

conceptDetails.name: moduli spaces
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs and studies moduli spaces MZ(r,n) of sheaf stable pairs on smooth projective curves, proving they are smooth projective varieties with explicit fiber descriptions.

conceptDetails.name: birational geometry
conceptDetails.description: The paper establishes a bridge between moduli of sheaf stable pairs and birational geometry via stable minimal models, with the moduli space parametrizing the birational procedure between models.

conceptDetails.name: quot-schemes
conceptDetails.description: Quot-schemes serve as the foundational parameterization tool for flat families of quotient sheaves, embedded into Grassmannians via Plücker maps to enable GIT quotient constructions.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: GLn(k) group actions on Quot-schemes with linearized ample line bundles are central to constructing moduli spaces of semi-stable coherent sheaves as GIT quotients.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Moduli spaces of sheaves and the enumerative geometry program outlined in the paper are deeply connected to string theory compactifications and Donaldson-Thomas invariants.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Moduli spaces of coherent sheaves of the type studied arise naturally in gauge theories and topological quantum field theories, providing physics motivation for the mathematical program.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The algebraic-geometric structures of projective varieties and Grassmannians studied here underpin the geometric spaces that appear in geometric deep learning frameworks.

title: QuanTA: Efficient High-Rank Fine-Tuning of LLMs with Quantum-Informed Tensor Adaptation
abstract: We propose Quantum-informed Tensor Adaptation (QuanTA), a novel, easy-to-implement, fine-tuning method with no inference overhead for large-scale pre-trained language models. By leveraging quantum-inspired methods derived from quantum circuit structures, QuanTA enables efficient high-rank fine-tuning, surpassing the limitations of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)--low-rank approximation may fail for complicated downstream tasks. Our approach is theoretically supported by the universality theorem and the rank representation theorem to achieve efficient high-rank adaptations. Experiments demonstrate that QuanTA significantly enhances commonsense reasoning, arithmetic reasoning, and scalability compared to traditional methods. Furthermore, QuanTA shows superior performance with fewer trainable parameters compared to other approaches and can be designed to integrate with existing fine-tuning algorithms for further improvement, providing a scalable and efficient solution for fine-tuning large language models and advancing state-of-the-art in natural language processing.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that enables high-rank weight updates for adapting pre-trained LLMs to downstream tasks without inference overhead.

conceptDetails.name: high-rank adaptation
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA's central novel contribution: a theoretically grounded method for efficient high-rank weight update parameterization that overcomes LoRA's low-rank bottleneck.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA uses tensor operations analogous to quantum circuit structures to parameterize high-rank weight updates, directly drawing on tensor network formalism from quantum information.

conceptDetails.name: parameter-efficient fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA belongs to and advances the PEFT family of methods, targeting the regime where only a small fraction of parameters are updated during adaptation.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: Quantum circuit structures inspire the design of QuanTA's tensor operations, with the universality of quantum circuits motivating the method's theoretical guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: quantum-inspired tensors
conceptDetails.description: The specific methodological innovation of applying quantum circuit-derived tensor decompositions as a blueprint for constructing expressive, parameter-efficient weight updates.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA targets transformer-based large language models, inserting quantum-inspired tensor adaptations into attention and MLP weight matrices.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A core claim of QuanTA is achieving better scalability than LoRA by enabling high-rank adaptations with fewer trainable parameters as model size grows.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA is a parameter-efficient approach to transferring pre-trained LLM capabilities to new downstream tasks such as commonsense and arithmetic reasoning.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The rank representation theorem underpins QuanTA's ability to parameterize arbitrary high-rank matrices, directly addressing representational capacity limitations of LoRA.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: QuanTA achieves competitive performance with far fewer trainable parameters than full fine-tuning, embodying the parameter-sparsity goals of PEFT methods.

title: Exploratory Preference Optimization: Harnessing Implicit Q*-Approximation for Sample-Efficient RLHF
abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a central tool for language model alignment. We consider online exploration in RLHF, which exploits interactive access to human or AI feedback by deliberately encouraging the model to produce diverse, maximally informative responses. By allowing RLHF to confidently stray from the pre-trained model, online exploration offers the possibility of novel, potentially super-human capabilities, but its full potential as a paradigm for language model training has yet to be realized, owing to computational and statistical bottlenecks in directly adapting existing reinforcement learning techniques. We propose a new algorithm for online exploration in RLHF, Exploratory Preference Optimization (XPO), which is simple and practical -- a one-line change to (online) Direct Preference Optimization (DPO; Rafailov et al., 2023) -- yet enjoys the strongest known provable guarantees and promising empirical performance. XPO augments the DPO objective with a novel and principled exploration bonus, empowering the algorithm to explore outside the support of the initial model and human feedback data. In theory, we show that XPO is provably sample-efficient and converges to a near-optimal language model policy under natural exploration conditions, irrespective of whether the initial model has good coverage. Our analysis, which builds on the observation that DPO implicitly performs a form of $Q^{\star}$-approximation (or, Bellman error minimization), combines previously disparate techniques from language modeling and theoretical reinforcement learning in a serendipitous fashion through the perspective of KL-regularized Markov decision processes. Empirically, we find that XPO is more sample-efficient than non-exploratory DPO variants in a preliminary evaluation.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames RLHF as a KL-regularized Markov decision process and develops provably sample-efficient RL algorithms for language model alignment.

conceptDetails.name: exploratory preference optimization
conceptDetails.description: XPO is the novel algorithm proposed, augmenting DPO with a principled exploration bonus for provably sample-efficient online RLHF with general function approximation.

conceptDetails.name: kl-regularized mdp
conceptDetails.description: XPO's theoretical analysis frames language model training as a KL-regularized Markov decision process, enabling the connection between DPO and Q*-approximation.

conceptDetails.name: q*-approximation
conceptDetails.description: The paper shows DPO implicitly performs Bellman error minimization to approximate the optimal Q* function, a key insight enabling the XPO exploration bonus design.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: XPO optimizes language model policies toward human-preferred outputs by augmenting DPO with an exploration bonus to maximize reward signal efficiently.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: Online exploration in RLHF actively queries human or AI feedback for maximally informative responses, mirroring active learning principles for sample efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: XPO introduces a principled exploration bonus added to the DPO objective, representing a novel loss function modification with theoretical guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: XPO is applied as a fine-tuning method for pre-trained language models using preference feedback to align model outputs with human values.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central motivation is overcoming the sample complexity bottleneck in RLHF to scale alignment to super-human capabilities with less human feedback.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework involves stochastic MDP transitions, regret decompositions, and concentration arguments central to the sample complexity proofs.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides a novel theoretical interpretation of DPO as implicit Q*-approximation, revealing previously unrecognized connections between preference optimization and RL theory.

title: Harmonic $1$-forms on real loci of Calabi-Yau manifolds
abstract: We numerically study whether there exist nowhere vanishing harmonic $1$-forms on the real locus of some carefully constructed examples of Calabi-Yau manifolds, which would then give rise to potentially new examples of $G_2$-manifolds and an explicit description of their metrics. We do this in two steps: first, we use a neural network to compute an approximate Calabi-Yau metric on each manifold. Second, we use another neural network to compute an approximately harmonic $1$-form with respect to the approximate metric, and then inspect the found solution. On two manifolds existence of a nowhere vanishing harmonic $1$-form can be ruled out using differential geometry. The real locus of a third manifold is diffeomorphic to $S^1 \times S^2$, and our numerics suggest that when the Calabi-Yau metric is close to a singular limit, then it admits a nowhere vanishing harmonic $1$-form. We explain how such an approximate solution could potentially be used in a numerically verified proof for the fact that our example manifold must admit a nowhere vanishing harmonic $1$-form.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau metric learning
conceptDetails.description: A novel application of neural networks to numerically approximate Ricci-flat metrics on complete intersection Calabi-Yau manifolds of higher codimension, extending prior hypersurface methods.

conceptDetails.name: g2-manifold construction
conceptDetails.description: The central mathematical goal of the paper is to provide numerical evidence for the existence of harmonic 1-forms that would enable a new construction of G2-holonomy Riemannian 7-manifolds.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks are used to compute approximate Calabi-Yau metrics by minimizing violations of the complex Monge-Ampère equation, embedding physical constraints directly into the learning objective.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau manifolds are central objects in superstring compactification, and the G2-manifolds constructed in this paper are relevant to M-theory, situating the work within formal string/M-theory.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo integration is used throughout the numerical pipeline to evaluate integrals over Calabi-Yau manifolds and their real loci when computing approximate metrics and harmonic forms.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks serve as surrogate models for the intractable exact Calabi-Yau metric, providing approximate solutions that are used to study the existence of harmonic 1-forms.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Finding a harmonic 1-form consistent with an approximate Calabi-Yau metric is treated as an inverse problem, where the network learns the form by minimizing a residual functional.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Bihomogeneous neural networks with symmetry-respecting architectures are used to approximate the Calabi-Yau metric, exploiting the geometric structure of the manifold.

conceptDetails.name: computer-assisted proof
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses how a sufficiently precise approximate harmonic 1-form could be used in a numerically verified (interval-arithmetic) proof of existence of a genuine harmonic 1-form.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The approach leverages geometric structure of complex manifolds and their real loci to design network architectures and polynomial bases suited to differential-geometric computations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The motivation for computing explicit Calabi-Yau metrics includes calculating particle masses and couplings in string-derived QFTs, connecting the numerical work to physics applications.

title: From Neurons to Neutrons: A Case Study in Interpretability
abstract: Mechanistic Interpretability (MI) promises a path toward fully understanding how neural networks make their predictions. Prior work demonstrates that even when trained to perform simple arithmetic, models can implement a variety of algorithms (sometimes concurrently) depending on initialization and hyperparameters. Does this mean neuron-level interpretability techniques have limited applicability? We argue that high-dimensional neural networks can learn low-dimensional representations of their training data that are useful beyond simply making good predictions. Such representations can be understood through the mechanistic interpretability lens and provide insights that are surprisingly faithful to human-derived domain knowledge. This indicates that such approaches to interpretability can be useful for deriving a new understanding of a problem from models trained to solve it. As a case study, we extract nuclear physics concepts by studying models trained to reproduce nuclear data.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: using mechanistic interpretability to extract domain knowledge (nuclear physics concepts) from trained neural networks, expanding MI beyond model debugging.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The core mechanism studied — neural networks trained on nuclear data learn low-dimensional representations that encode physically meaningful structure without explicit supervision.

conceptDetails.name: mechanistic interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The specific subfield of interpretability focused on uncovering the algorithms implemented by neural networks at the neuron/circuit level, applied here to a physical science domain.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: PCA is applied throughout to project high-dimensional neuron embeddings into interpretable subspaces that reveal helical and shell structures corresponding to nuclear physics.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The paper argues that networks learn representations where latent dimensions correspond to semantically meaningful physical factors, enabling extraction of nuclear shell model concepts.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Input embeddings for proton/neutron numbers are analyzed via PCA to uncover the spiral helix structure that mirrors human-derived nuclear shell model structure.

conceptDetails.name: latent space topography
conceptDetails.description: A novel visualization technique introduced in the paper: uniformly sampling a 2D latent subspace and inverting the projection to map model outputs across the embedding manifold.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The manifold hypothesis — that natural data lies on a low-dimensional manifold — is the theoretical foundation motivating why neural network representations can encode physical laws.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear magic numbers
conceptDetails.description: The nuclear shell closure numbers (2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126) that spontaneously emerge in the model's learned representations, validating that MI recovers established nuclear physics.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: PCA via eigendecomposition of activation covariance matrices is the primary analytical tool used to identify principal components encoding nuclear structure.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The method extracts physically interpretable features (magic numbers, shell closures, pairing effects) from intermediate network activations trained on nuclear binding energy data.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: MLPs are trained as surrogate models of nuclear binding energies, serving as the learned domain experts from which physical concepts are then extracted.

title: Survival of the Fittest Representation: A Case Study with Modular Addition
abstract: When a neural network can learn multiple distinct algorithms to solve a task, how does it "choose" between them during training? To approach this question, we take inspiration from ecology: when multiple species coexist, they eventually reach an equilibrium where some survive while others die out. Analogously, we suggest that a neural network at initialization contains many solutions (representations and algorithms), which compete with each other under pressure from resource constraints, with the "fittest" ultimately prevailing. To investigate this Survival of the Fittest hypothesis, we conduct a case study on neural networks performing modular addition, and find that these networks' multiple circular representations at different Fourier frequencies undergo such competitive dynamics, with only a few circles surviving at the end. We find that the frequencies with high initial signals and gradients, the "fittest," are more likely to survive. By increasing the embedding dimension, we also observe more surviving frequencies. Inspired by the Lotka-Volterra equations describing the dynamics between species, we find that the dynamics of the circles can be nicely characterized by a set of linear differential equations. Our results with modular addition show that it is possible to decompose complicated representations into simpler components, along with their basic interactions, to offer insight on the training dynamics of representations.

conceptDetails.name: training dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: modeling how competing circular representations evolve and survive during neural network training using differential equations.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how neural networks form and select among multiple circular representations when solving modular addition.

conceptDetails.name: representation competition
conceptDetails.description: Novel hypothesis that multiple representations initialized in a network compete under resource constraints, with only the 'fittest' surviving training.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The work is framed as mechanistic interpretability, reverse-engineering which algorithms and representations networks choose to learn and why.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The embedding matrix is the primary object of study; its Fourier decomposition reveals competing circular representations at different frequencies.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Fourier analysis of the embedding matrix is used to identify and track circular representations across training steps.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The paper shows complex high-dimensional embeddings can be decomposed into independent low-dimensional circular components with simple pairwise interactions.

conceptDetails.name: grokking
conceptDetails.description: The modular addition models studied exhibit grokking—sudden late generalization—which is induced by weight decay and shapes which representations survive.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Lasso regression is applied to recover sparse interaction matrices in the Lotka-Volterra-inspired differential equations governing representation dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Modular arithmetic (ℤ/pℤ) provides the group structure that determines the Fourier frequencies available to circular representations.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Survival of specific frequencies is probabilistic across random seeds, motivating statistical rather than deterministic analysis of which circles survive.

title: Improved Distribution Matching Distillation for Fast Image Synthesis
abstract: Recent approaches have shown promises distilling diffusion models into efficient one-step generators. Among them, Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) produces one-step generators that match their teacher in distribution, without enforcing a one-to-one correspondence with the sampling trajectories of their teachers. However, to ensure stable training, DMD requires an additional regression loss computed using a large set of noise-image pairs generated by the teacher with many steps of a deterministic sampler. This is costly for large-scale text-to-image synthesis and limits the student's quality, tying it too closely to the teacher's original sampling paths. We introduce DMD2, a set of techniques that lift this limitation and improve DMD training. First, we eliminate the regression loss and the need for expensive dataset construction. We show that the resulting instability is due to the fake critic not estimating the distribution of generated samples accurately and propose a two time-scale update rule as a remedy. Second, we integrate a GAN loss into the distillation procedure, discriminating between generated samples and real images. This lets us train the student model on real data, mitigating the imperfect real score estimation from the teacher model, and enhancing quality. Lastly, we modify the training procedure to enable multi-step sampling. We identify and address the training-inference input mismatch problem in this setting, by simulating inference-time generator samples during training time. Taken together, our improvements set new benchmarks in one-step image generation, with FID scores of 1.28 on ImageNet-64x64 and 8.35 on zero-shot COCO 2014, surpassing the original teacher despite a 500X reduction in inference cost. Further, we show our approach can generate megapixel images by distilling SDXL, demonstrating exceptional visual quality among few-step methods.

conceptDetails.name: distribution matching distillation
conceptDetails.description: The novel core method (DMD2) that trains one-step/few-step student generators to match teacher distribution without regression loss or paired trajectory data.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Serve as the teacher models being distilled; DMD2 accelerates diffusion model inference by training efficient student generators that match the teacher's output distribution.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Core contribution: eliminating the regression loss required by original DMD, replacing it with a two time-scale update rule and GAN objective for stable, high-quality training.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: A GAN loss is integrated into the DMD2 distillation framework, with a discriminator trained to distinguish student-generated samples from real images, enhancing output quality.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: DMD2 uses two score functions — one fixed for the target distribution and one dynamically trained for the generator's output — to compute the approximate KL divergence gradient.

conceptDetails.name: backward simulation
conceptDetails.description: Novel training procedure introduced in DMD2 that simulates inference-time generator inputs during training to resolve the training-inference input mismatch in multi-step student models.

conceptDetails.name: two time-scale update
conceptDetails.description: Stability technique that updates the fake diffusion critic more frequently than the generator, ensuring accurate distribution estimation and preventing training collapse without regression loss.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Distribution matching between student and teacher is framed as minimizing approximate KL divergence, making accurate density/distribution estimation central to DMD2's objective.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: DMD2 eliminates costly dataset pre-generation and scales to megapixel image synthesis by distilling SDXL, demonstrating practical applicability at large scale.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: Related to ODE trajectory sampling used by teacher diffusion models; DMD2 avoids enforcing one-to-one correspondence with these trajectories, enabling distribution-level alignment instead.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The distribution matching objective minimizes KL and Jensen-Shannon divergences between student and teacher output distributions, connecting to optimal transport principles.

title: Not All Language Model Features Are One-Dimensionally Linear
abstract: Recent work has proposed that language models perform computation by manipulating one-dimensional representations of concepts ("features") in activation space. In contrast, we explore whether some language model representations may be inherently multi-dimensional. We begin by developing a rigorous definition of irreducible multi-dimensional features based on whether they can be decomposed into either independent or non-co-occurring lower-dimensional features. Motivated by these definitions, we design a scalable method that uses sparse autoencoders to automatically find multi-dimensional features in GPT-2 and Mistral 7B. These auto-discovered features include strikingly interpretable examples, e.g. circular features representing days of the week and months of the year. We identify tasks where these exact circles are used to solve computational problems involving modular arithmetic in days of the week and months of the year. Next, we provide evidence that these circular features are indeed the fundamental unit of computation in these tasks with intervention experiments on Mistral 7B and Llama 3 8B, and we examine the continuity of the days of the week feature in Mistral 7B. Overall, our work argues that understanding multi-dimensional features is necessary to mechanistically decompose some model behaviors.

conceptDetails.name: multi-dimensional features
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution: a formal definition and empirical discovery of irreducible features that span more than one dimension in a language model's activation space.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how language models learn to represent concepts in activation space, specifically challenging the assumption that all representations are one-dimensional.

conceptDetails.name: circular representations
conceptDetails.description: A specific class of multi-dimensional features discovered in GPT-2 and Mistral 7B where concepts like days of the week and months of the year are encoded as circles in 2D subspaces.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are the primary method used to automatically discover and identify multi-dimensional features in GPT-2 and Mistral 7B.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper is a mechanistic interpretability work that seeks to reverse-engineer how language models compute with multi-dimensional feature representations.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders are central to the methodology, leveraging sparsity to decompose activation spaces into candidate features that are then tested for irreducibility.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The study analyzes multi-dimensional features in GPT-2-small, Mistral 7B, and Llama 3 8B transformer language models.

conceptDetails.name: modular arithmetic circuits
conceptDetails.description: Causal intervention experiments show that circular representations are the fundamental computational unit used by Mistral 7B and Llama 3 8B to perform modular arithmetic over cyclic concepts.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: PCA is used extensively to visualize and verify circular structure in multi-dimensional feature representations across model layers.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The formal definition of irreducible multi-dimensional features is grounded in whether a representation can be decomposed into independent or non-co-occurring lower-dimensional components.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Clustering of SAE dictionary elements is the scalable automated method proposed to discover multi-dimensional feature groupings in large language models.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies the geometry of activation-space embeddings to characterize whether features occupy one-dimensional or higher-dimensional subspaces.

title: Lorentz-Equivariant Geometric Algebra Transformers for High-Energy Physics
abstract: Extracting scientific understanding from particle-physics experiments requires solving diverse learning problems with high precision and good data efficiency. We propose the Lorentz Geometric Algebra Transformer (L-GATr), a new multi-purpose architecture for high-energy physics. L-GATr represents high-energy data in a geometric algebra over four-dimensional space-time and is equivariant under Lorentz transformations, the symmetry group of relativistic kinematics. At the same time, the architecture is a Transformer, which makes it versatile and scalable to large systems. L-GATr is first demonstrated on regression and classification tasks from particle physics. We then construct the first Lorentz-equivariant generative model: a continuous normalizing flow based on an L-GATr network, trained with Riemannian flow matching. Across our experiments, L-GATr is on par with or outperforms strong domain-specific baselines.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: L-GATr is built to be equivariant under Lorentz transformations, encoding the symmetry group of relativistic kinematics directly into the architecture.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The architecture represents data in a geometric (Clifford) algebra over four-dimensional spacetime, extending scalar and four-vector representations to higher-order geometric objects.

conceptDetails.name: geometric algebra
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses the Clifford algebra over Minkowski spacetime as the representation space, a novel choice that lifts four-vectors to a richer multivector structure.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: L-GATr is a Transformer, enabling variable-length inputs, scalability to large systems, and compatibility with optimized attention backends like Flash Attention.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: The first Lorentz-equivariant generative model is trained using Riemannian flow matching, which allows encoding phase-space geometry and hard-coding detector boundaries.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a Lorentz-equivariant attention mechanism as a core layer of L-GATr, adapting scaled dot-product attention to the Lorentz-symmetric geometric algebra setting.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The Lorentz group SO(1,3) is the symmetry group exploited throughout the architecture design, from equivariant linear maps to layer normalization.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: A continuous normalizing flow built on the L-GATr network serves as the backbone of the Lorentz-equivariant generative model for particle-physics data.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces the first Lorentz-equivariant generative model, targeting particle-level density estimation with sharp edges and low-density tails.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Neural surrogates for quantum field theory scattering amplitudes are one of three benchmark tasks, requiring high precision regression.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: All experiments target LHC data-analysis tasks, from amplitude regression to event generation and top tagging, situating the work in collider phenomenology.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: L-GATr is applied to learn fast neural surrogates for expensive quantum field theory amplitude computations, replacing costly Monte Carlo evaluations.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Top quark tagging, a canonical jet classification benchmark at the LHC, is used to evaluate L-GATr's discriminative performance against domain-specific baselines.

title: Multistable Shape from Shading Emerges from Patch Diffusion
abstract: Models for inferring monocular shape of surfaces with diffuse reflection -- shape from shading -- ought to produce distributions of outputs, because there are fundamental mathematical ambiguities of both continuous (e.g., bas-relief) and discrete (e.g., convex/concave) types that are also experienced by humans. Yet, the outputs of current models are limited to point estimates or tight distributions around single modes, which prevent them from capturing these effects. We introduce a model that reconstructs a multimodal distribution of shapes from a single shading image, which aligns with the human experience of multistable perception. We train a small denoising diffusion process to generate surface normal fields from $16\times 16$ patches of synthetic images of everyday 3D objects. We deploy this model patch-wise at multiple scales, with guidance from inter-patch shape consistency constraints. Despite its relatively small parameter count and predominantly bottom-up structure, we show that multistable shape explanations emerge from this model for ambiguous test images that humans experience as being multistable. At the same time, the model produces veridical shape estimates for object-like images that include distinctive occluding contours and appear less ambiguous. This may inspire new architectures for stochastic 3D shape perception that are more efficient and better aligned with human experience.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: A small denoising diffusion process is trained to generate surface normal fields from 16x16 image patches, forming the core generative backbone of the model.

conceptDetails.name: multistable perception
conceptDetails.description: The target phenomenon being modeled: human visual experience where a single ambiguous image alternates between two or more competing 3D shape interpretations.

conceptDetails.name: shape from shading
conceptDetails.description: The classical inverse problem of inferring 3D surface geometry from monocular shading images under diffuse Lambertian reflectance assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: patch-based diffusion
conceptDetails.description: The key architectural contribution: training and deploying the diffusion model on small 16x16 patches with inter-patch consistency constraints rather than full images.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Shape from shading is framed as an ill-posed inverse problem with fundamental continuous and discrete mathematical ambiguities that motivate a distributional output.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model reconstructs a multimodal posterior distribution over surface normal fields rather than a single point estimate, capturing multiple valid shape explanations.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Multimodal output distributions explicitly represent inherent shape ambiguities, enabling the model to express multiple competing interpretations of ambiguous images.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Monocular shape inference is reframed as a conditional generative process to enable distributional outputs aligned with human perceptual ambiguity.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Multi-scale diffusion sampling is coordinated across patches via a V-cycle fine-coarse-fine noise schedule to find globally consistent shape explanations.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A pixel-based diffusion UNet operating on small patches serves as the lightweight backbone, requiring only 10MB of model weights.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The patch diffusion model uses score-based denoising principles to sample from the learned distribution over surface normals conditioned on image intensity.

title: How Do Transformers "Do" Physics? Investigating the Simple Harmonic Oscillator
abstract: How do transformers model physics? Do transformers model systems with interpretable analytical solutions, or do they create "alien physics" that are difficult for humans to decipher? We take a step in demystifying this larger puzzle by investigating the simple harmonic oscillator (SHO), $\ddot{x}+2γ\dot{x}+ω_0^2x=0$, one of the most fundamental systems in physics. Our goal is to identify the methods transformers use to model the SHO, and to do so we hypothesize and evaluate possible methods by analyzing the encoding of these methods' intermediates. We develop four criteria for the use of a method within the simple testbed of linear regression, where our method is $y = wx$ and our intermediate is $w$: (1) Can the intermediate be predicted from hidden states? (2) Is the intermediate's encoding quality correlated with model performance? (3) Can the majority of variance in hidden states be explained by the intermediate? (4) Can we intervene on hidden states to produce predictable outcomes? Armed with these two correlational (1,2), weak causal (3) and strong causal (4) criteria, we determine that transformers use known numerical methods to model trajectories of the simple harmonic oscillator, specifically the matrix exponential method. Our analysis framework can conveniently extend to high-dimensional linear systems and nonlinear systems, which we hope will help reveal the "world model" hidden in transformers.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The neural architecture whose internal computations are studied to determine how it models simple harmonic oscillator trajectories.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The core goal of the paper: developing causal and correlational criteria to reveal what numerical methods transformers use internally to model physics.

conceptDetails.name: mechanistic interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A novel probing framework using four criteria (linear probe, correlation with performance, variance explanation, and causal intervention) to identify specific methods encoded in transformer hidden states.

conceptDetails.name: in-context learning
conceptDetails.description: The learning paradigm studied, where transformers infer physical parameters (damping γ, frequency ω₀) from context trajectory points to predict future states.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: In-context linear regression (y = wx) is used as a simplified testbed for developing and validating the intermediate-probing criteria before applying them to the SHO.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Central to the matrix exponential method found to be used by transformers; the SHO system matrix is diagonalized to compute state transitions.

conceptDetails.name: world models
conceptDetails.description: The broader research goal: determining whether transformers build internal representations of physical laws rather than producing opaque 'alien physics' predictions.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The simple harmonic oscillator is a canonical Hamiltonian system, and the paper's framework is positioned to extend to broader classes of Hamiltonian dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Probing transformer hidden states to extract encoded intermediates (e.g., the weight w in linear regression or γ, ω₀ in the SHO) using linear and Taylor probes.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The matrix exponential numerical method identified as the transformer's strategy relies on spectral decomposition of the system's state-transition matrix.

title: Unsupervised Searches for Cosmological Parity Violation: Improving Detection Power with the Neural Field Scattering Transform
abstract: Recent studies using four-point correlations suggest a parity violation in the galaxy distribution, though the significance of these detections is sensitive to the choice of simulation used to model the noise properties of the galaxy distribution. In a recent paper, we introduce an unsupervised learning approach which offers an alternative method that avoids the dependence on mock catalogs, by learning parity violation directly from observational data. However, the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model utilized by our previous unsupervised approach struggles to extend to more realistic scenarios where data is limited.
