We propose a novel method, the Neural Field Scattering Transform (NFST), which enhances the Wavelet Scattering Transform (WST) technique by adding trainable filters, parameterized as a neural field. We first tune the NFST model to detect parity violation in a simplified dataset, then compare its performance against WST and CNN benchmarks across varied training set sizes. We find the NFST can detect parity violation with $4\times$ less data than the CNN and $32\times$ less than the WST. Furthermore, in cases with limited data the NFST can detect parity violation with up to $6σ$ confidence, where the WST and CNN fail to make any detection. We identify that the added flexibility of the NFST, and particularly the ability to learn asymmetric filters, as well as the specific symmetries built into the NFST architecture, contribute to its improved performance over the benchmark models. We further demonstrate that the NFST is readily interpretable, which is valuable for physical applications such as the detection of parity violation.

conceptDetails.name: parity violation
conceptDetails.description: The core physics phenomenon under investigation — a statistical asymmetry in the galaxy distribution under spatial inversion — which the NFST is designed to detect without relying on mock catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: wavelet scattering transform
conceptDetails.description: The baseline signal-summarization technique that the NFST extends by replacing fixed wavelets with trainable neural-field-parameterized filters, retaining its built-in translation and rotation invariances.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary scientific goal: detecting the parity-violation signal in galaxy distributions at high statistical confidence (up to 6σ) using data-efficient learned representations.

conceptDetails.name: neural field
conceptDetails.description: A continuous implicit neural representation used to parameterize the learnable filters in the NFST, enabling asymmetric and task-adapted filter shapes that improve data efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Key design principle of the NFST architecture, which encodes translation and rotation symmetries to achieve robustness to noise and improved generalization from limited cosmological data.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The unsupervised training paradigm used — the model learns to distinguish galaxy fields from their parity-inverted counterparts using the data itself as supervision, avoiding dependence on labeled mock catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Statistical significance testing (reported in σ units) is used throughout to quantify whether the model's detection of parity asymmetry exceeds noise thresholds across different training set sizes.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Both WST and NFST compress galaxy density fields into compact summary statistics via iterative wavelet convolutions and modulus nonlinearities, enabling downstream parity detection.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly demonstrates that the learned NFST filters can be visualized and physically interpreted, a property highlighted as critical for validating detections in cosmological applications.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNN serves as a primary benchmark whose data-hungry nature in 3D motivated the development of the more sample-efficient NFST approach.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Wavelet-domain convolution and scattering operations form the mathematical backbone of WST and NFST, capturing multi-scale frequency information from the galaxy density field.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Mock galaxy catalogs from N-body simulations (e.g., MultiDark-Patchy, Uchuu-GLAM) represent the noise-modeling tool that the unsupervised NFST approach is designed to circumvent.

title: Resonant Neutrino Flavor Conversion in the Atmosphere
abstract: Neutrinos produced in the atmosphere traverse a column density of air before being detected at neutrino observatories like IceCube or KM3NeT. In this work, we extend the neutrino flavor evolution in the {nuSQuIDS} code accounting for the varying height of neutrino production and the variable air density in the atmosphere. These effects can lead to sizeable spectral distortions in standard neutrino oscillations and are crucial to accurately describe some new physics scenarios. As an example, we study a model of quasi-sterile neutrinos that induce resonant flavor conversions at neutrino energies of ${O}(300)\text{ MeV}$ in matter densities of $1 \text{ g/cm}^3$. In atmospheric air densities, the same resonance is then realized at neutrino energies of ${O}(300- 700)$~GeV. We find that the new resonance can deplete the $ν_μ+ \overlineν_μ$ flux at the IceCube Neutrino Observatory by as much as $10\%$ in the direction of the horizon.

conceptDetails.name: msw resonance
conceptDetails.description: The Mikheev-Smirnov-Wolfenstein matter effect drives resonant flavor conversion of quasi-sterile neutrinos in the atmospheric density profile.

conceptDetails.name: atmospheric neutrino oscillations
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends nuSQuIDS to accurately model neutrino flavor oscillations accounting for variable production height and air density in the atmosphere.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: IceCube and KM3NeT observatories are the primary detection contexts for observing the predicted resonant flavor conversion signatures in atmospheric neutrinos.

conceptDetails.name: quasi-sterile neutrinos
conceptDetails.description: A novel beyond-Standard-Model state with no weak charge but sizeable matter interactions, inducing the resonant conversions studied in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for exotic neutrino flavor conversions as signatures of physics beyond the Standard Model, specifically quasi-sterile neutrino states.

conceptDetails.name: spectral distortions
conceptDetails.description: Sizeable spectral distortions in neutrino flux arise from accurately modeling variable production height and atmospheric density in oscillation codes.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Quasi-sterile neutrino models with new matter potentials are framed within effective field theory approaches to explain short-baseline anomalies.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper quantifies the predicted 10% depletion of the muon neutrino flux near the horizon as a detectable signal at IceCube.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCEq (Matrix Cascade Equations) and nuSQuIDS numerical integration are used to simulate atmospheric neutrino production and flavor evolution.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The three-neutrino paradigm as an extension of the Standard Model provides the baseline against which new resonant flavor conversion effects are measured.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: nuSQuIDS serves as an efficient numerical solver (surrogate) for the full quantum integro-differential equations governing neutrino flavor evolution.

title: Diffusion-HMC: Parameter Inference with Diffusion-model-driven Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
abstract: Diffusion generative models have excelled at diverse image generation and reconstruction tasks across fields. A less explored avenue is their application to discriminative tasks involving regression or classification problems. The cornerstone of modern cosmology is the ability to generate predictions for observed astrophysical fields from theory and constrain physical models from observations using these predictions. This work uses a single diffusion generative model to address these interlinked objectives -- as a surrogate model or emulator for cold dark matter density fields conditional on input cosmological parameters, and as a parameter inference model that solves the inverse problem of constraining the cosmological parameters of an input field. The model is able to emulate fields with summary statistics consistent with those of the simulated target distribution. We then leverage the approximate likelihood of the diffusion generative model to derive tight constraints on cosmology by using the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo method to sample the posterior on cosmological parameters for a given test image. Finally, we demonstrate that this parameter inference approach is more robust to small perturbations of noise to the field than baseline parameter inference networks.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The core generative architecture used both as an emulator of cold dark matter density fields and as the source of approximate likelihood for parameter inference.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo is used to sample the posterior on cosmological parameters by exploiting gradients from the diffusion model likelihood.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central inference goal is to derive tight posterior distributions on cosmological parameters (Ω_m, σ_8) given an observed density field.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion models are framed as score-based generative models; the learned score function is directly leveraged to compute gradients for Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The full Bayesian posterior over cosmological parameters is sampled using HMC driven by the diffusion model's approximate likelihood.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Constraining cosmological parameters from an observed field is cast as an inverse problem solved via diffusion-model-driven posterior sampling.

conceptDetails.name: field-level inference
conceptDetails.description: Parameter inference is performed directly on full 2D density fields rather than compressed summary statistics like the power spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The approximate conditional likelihood of the diffusion model is used as a surrogate for the true intractable likelihood during posterior sampling.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Field-level inference is performed without explicit analytic likelihoods by learning a neural likelihood model from cosmological simulations.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The diffusion model acts as a surrogate/emulator for costly N-body simulations, generating dark matter density fields conditioned on cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Cold dark matter density fields from IllustrisTNG/CAMELS are the target data distribution that the model emulates and infers parameters from.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The CAMELS Multifield Dataset derived from IllustrisTNG N-body simulations provides training data across 1000 cosmologies.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion-HMC is demonstrated to be more robust to uncorrelated noise perturbations than discriminative baseline networks for parameter estimation.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A U-Net with circular convolutions (to respect periodic boundary conditions) serves as the score/noise-predictor backbone of the diffusion model.

title: OptPDE: Discovering Novel Integrable Systems via AI-Human Collaboration
abstract: Integrable partial differential equation (PDE) systems are of great interest in natural science, but are exceedingly rare and difficult to discover. To solve this, we introduce OptPDE, a first-of-its-kind machine learning approach that Optimizes PDEs' coefficients to maximize their number of conserved quantities, $n_{\rm CQ}$, and thus discover new integrable systems. We discover four families of integrable PDEs, one of which was previously known, and three of which have at least one conserved quantity but are new to the literature to the best of our knowledge. We investigate more deeply the properties of one of these novel PDE families, $u_t = (u_x+a^2u_{xxx})^3$. Our paper offers a promising schema of AI-human collaboration for integrable system discovery: machine learning generates interpretable hypotheses for possible integrable systems, which human scientists can verify and analyze, to truly close the discovery loop.

conceptDetails.name: integrable system discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary novel contribution: a first-of-its-kind ML pipeline for systematically finding new PDE families with one or more conserved quantities.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: The central objective of OptPDE: maximizing the number of conserved quantities (nCQ) of PDEs serves as the signal for identifying integrable systems.

conceptDetails.name: conserved quantity optimization
conceptDetails.description: The novel algorithmic framing of treating nCQ as a differentiable objective function to maximize over the space of PDE coefficients.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: OptPDE automates the search for integrable PDE systems by running 5000 random initializations and optimizing coefficients to maximize nCQ.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Singular value decomposition of the constraint matrix G is the core numerical method in CQFinder for finding the null space that encodes conserved quantities.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: CQFinder produces sparse, symbolic formulas for conserved quantities so human scientists can read, verify, and interpret discovered systems.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a structured AI-human collaboration workflow where ML generates candidate integrable systems and human scientists verify and analyze them.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: CQFinder applies sparsification to solutions of the linear system to return minimal basis representations of conserved quantities for human interpretability.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: OptPDE frames integrable system discovery as an inverse problem: find PDE coefficients that achieve a desired property (maximal number of conserved quantities).

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A differentiable surrogate for nCQ is constructed in PyTorch to enable gradient-based optimization of PDE coefficients via automatic differentiation.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Applied to cluster the high-dimensional solution set from 5000 OptPDE runs, revealing four distinct families of integrable PDEs.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The integrable systems discovered are defined by their conserved quantities, which are intimately connected to underlying symmetries of the PDE dynamics.

title: Bounds and Dualities of Type II Little String Theories
abstract: We explore the symmetry structure of Type II Little String Theories and their T-dualities. We construct these theories both from the bottom-up perspective starting with seed Superconformal Field Theories, and from the top-down using F-/M-theory. By exploiting anomaly inflow and unitarity of the LST worldsheet theory, we derive strong conditions on the possible 6D bulk theories and their flavor algebras. These constraints continue to apply if gravity is coupled to the theory. We also study the higher form symmetry structure of these theories and show how they get exchanged under T-duality. Finally, we comment on seemingly consistent bottom-up Little String Theories that cannot be constructed from the top-down approach.

conceptDetails.name: little string theory
conceptDetails.description: The central objects of study: non-gravitational 6D theories with a Hagedorn density of states set by the string scale M_s, classified by pairs of ADE singularities.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Type II Little String Theories are constructed from Type IIA/B string theory and geometrically engineered via F-theory and M-theory compactifications.

conceptDetails.name: t-duality
conceptDetails.description: The key duality of Type II LSTs, shown to act as an exchange of the two defining singularities (g_F ↔ g_B) and correspondingly swapping higher-form symmetry sectors.

conceptDetails.name: higher form symmetry
conceptDetails.description: Generalized (1-form and 2-form) symmetries of Type II LSTs are mapped to the centers of the defining ADE algebras and shown to be exchanged under T-duality.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: Superconformal Field Theories (SCFTs) serve as the bottom-up seed theories from which LSTs are constructed via 'affinization'.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly inflow
conceptDetails.description: Used to derive rigorous bounds on the 6D bulk theories and their flavor algebras by exploiting unitarity and anomaly cancellation on the LST worldsheet.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies six-dimensional supersymmetric QFTs, deriving consistency conditions on their bulk theories, flavor algebras, and UV completions.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: ADE Lie algebras and their centers classify the defining singularities of Type II LSTs and determine their higher-form symmetry sectors.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The exchange of higher-form symmetry sectors under T-duality and Higgs branch deformations illuminate how symmetry structures transform across the LST landscape.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The tensor branch description below the string scale M_s provides an effective field theory in terms of vector, hyper-, and tensor multiplets.

title: A Parameter-Masked Mock Data Challenge for Beyond-Two-Point Galaxy Clustering Statistics
abstract: The last few years have seen the emergence of a wide array of novel techniques for analyzing high-precision data from upcoming galaxy surveys, which aim to extend the statistical analysis of galaxy clustering data beyond the linear regime and the canonical two-point (2pt) statistics. We test and benchmark some of these new techniques in a community data challenge "Beyond-2pt", initiated during the Aspen 2022 Summer Program "Large-Scale Structure Cosmology beyond 2-Point Statistics," whose first round of results we present here. The challenge dataset consists of high-precision mock galaxy catalogs for clustering in real space, redshift space, and on a light cone. Participants in the challenge have developed end-to-end pipelines to analyze mock catalogs and extract unknown ("masked") cosmological parameters of the underlying $Λ$CDM models with their methods. The methods represented are density-split clustering, nearest neighbor statistics, BACCO power spectrum emulator, void statistics, LEFTfield field-level inference using effective field theory (EFT), and joint power spectrum and bispectrum analyses using both EFT and simulation-based inference. In this work, we review the results of the challenge, focusing on problems solved, lessons learned, and future research needed to perfect the emerging beyond-2pt approaches. The unbiased parameter recovery demonstrated in this challenge by multiple statistics and the associated modeling and inference frameworks supports the credibility of cosmology constraints from these methods. The challenge data set is publicly available and we welcome future submissions from methods that are not yet represented.

conceptDetails.name: beyond-2pt statistics
conceptDetails.description: Novel galaxy clustering statistics beyond the two-point function — including bispectrum, void statistics, nearest-neighbor, and density-split — are the central subject of the challenge.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: High-precision mock galaxy catalogs generated from N-body simulations form the challenge dataset used to benchmark all methods.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Used as one of the primary inference frameworks for extracting cosmological parameters from beyond-2pt statistics without explicit likelihood functions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: EFT is used both in LEFTfield field-level inference and joint power spectrum/bispectrum analyses as a perturbative framework for modeling galaxy clustering.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: All participating methods use Bayesian parameter estimation pipelines to recover masked cosmological parameters from mock catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The parameter-masked challenge design enables rigorous validation of modeling and inference pipelines by checking for unbiased parameter recovery.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The challenge evaluates each method's ability to produce unbiased posterior distributions over ΛCDM cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: field-level inference
conceptDetails.description: LEFTfield performs inference at the level of the full density field using EFT, extracting maximal information from galaxy survey data.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The BACCO power spectrum emulator provides fast surrogate predictions for galaxy clustering statistics across cosmological parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Constraining dark energy properties is a primary science goal driving the development of beyond-2pt statistics for galaxy surveys.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Density-split clustering and field-level inference methods rely on estimating the local density field from galaxy positions.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The large-scale structure traced by galaxies reflects the underlying dark matter distribution, and the challenge aims to constrain ΛCDM parameters including dark matter density.

title: Measurement of atmospheric neutrino oscillation parameters using convolutional neural networks with 9.3 years of data in IceCube DeepCore
abstract: The DeepCore sub-detector of the IceCube Neutrino Observatory provides access to neutrinos with energies above approximately 5 GeV. Data taken between 2012-2021 (3,387 days) are utilized for an atmospheric $ν_μ$ disappearance analysis that studied 150,257 neutrino-candidate events with reconstructed energies between 5-100 GeV. An advanced reconstruction based on a convolutional neural network is applied, providing increased signal efficiency and background suppression, resulting in a measurement with both significantly increased statistics compared to previous DeepCore oscillation results and high neutrino purity. For the normal neutrino mass ordering, the atmospheric neutrino oscillation parameters and their 1$σ$ errors are measured to be $Δ$m$^2_{32}$ = $2.40\substack{+0.05 \\ -0.04} \times 10^{-3} \textrm{ eV}^2$ and sin$^2$$θ_{23}$=$0.54\substack{+0.04 \\ -0.03}$. The results are the most precise to date using atmospheric neutrinos, and are compatible with measurements from other neutrino detectors including long-baseline accelerator experiments.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The IceCube DeepCore sub-detector is used to detect atmospheric neutrinos with energies above 5 GeV over 9.3 years of operation.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino oscillation parameters
conceptDetails.description: The paper measures Δm²₃₂ and sin²θ₂₃ — the atmospheric neutrino mass splitting and mixing angle — to the highest precision achieved with atmospheric neutrinos.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional neural network is used for advanced neutrino event reconstruction, providing increased signal efficiency and background suppression.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: CNN-based reconstruction of neutrino energy and direction replaces previous methods to improve precision of oscillation parameter measurements.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Machine learning classification is used to separate neutrino signal events from muon and noise backgrounds in the detector data.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulation of atmospheric neutrino fluxes, interactions, and IceCube detector response underpins the analysis framework.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian statistical methods are employed to extract oscillation parameters and their uncertainties from the observed event distributions.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations of neutrino interactions and detector response are used to construct expected event distributions for parameter fitting.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: A likelihood ratio test statistic is constructed from binned event distributions to fit neutrino oscillation parameters Δm²₃₂ and sin²θ₂₃.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Systematic uncertainties from neutrino flux models, cross-sections, and detector effects are profiled and quantified in the oscillation parameter measurement.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Likelihood-ratio tests are used to evaluate the significance of the neutrino oscillation signal and constrain the oscillation parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The CNN extracts discriminating features from raw photon arrival time and charge patterns recorded by DeepCore digital optical modules.

title: Multi-filter UV to NIR Data-driven Light Curve Templates for Stripped Envelope Supernovae
abstract: While the spectroscopic classification scheme for Stripped envelope supernovae (SESNe) is clear, and we know that they originate from massive stars that lost some or all their envelopes of Hydrogen and Helium, the photometric evolution of classes within this family is not fully characterized. Photometric surveys, like the Vera C. Rubin Legacy Survey of Space and Time, will discover tens of thousands of transients each night and spectroscopic follow-up will be limited, prompting the need for photometric classification and inference based solely on photometry. We have generated 54 data-driven photometric templates for SESNe of subtypes IIb, Ib, Ic, Ic-bl, and Ibn in U/u, B, g, V, R/r, I/i, J, H, Ks, and Swift w2, m2, w1 bands using Gaussian Processes and a multi-survey dataset composed of all well-sampled open-access light curves (165 SESNe, 29531 data points) from the Open Supernova Catalog. We use our new templates to assess the photometric diversity of SESNe by comparing final per-band subtype templates with each other and with individual, unusual and prototypical SESNe. We find that SNe Ibns and Ic-bl exhibit a distinctly faster rise and decline compared to other subtypes. We also evaluate the behavior of SESNe in the PLAsTiCC and ELAsTiCC simulations of LSST light curves highlighting differences that can bias photometric classification models trained on the simulated light curves. Finally, we investigate in detail the behavior of fast-evolving SESNe (including SNe Ibn) and the implications of the frequently observed presence of two peaks in their light curves.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops photometric templates for classifying stripped envelope supernovae subtypes (IIb, Ib, Ic, Ic-bl, Ibn) based solely on light curve data.

conceptDetails.name: multi-band light curve templates
conceptDetails.description: A novel contribution: 54 data-driven photometric templates spanning UV to NIR covering 13 photometric bands for five SESNe subtypes.

conceptDetails.name: gaussian processes
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian Processes are the core statistical method used to generate the 54 data-driven photometric templates from heterogeneous multi-survey light curve data.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Photometric classification of transients is a central application, with the templates enabling classification without spectroscopic follow-up.

conceptDetails.name: photometric survey bias
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies and quantifies systematic differences between observed SESNe light curves and those in LSST simulations that can bias machine learning classifiers.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates SESNe as endpoints of massive star evolution, specifically stars that lost their hydrogen and helium envelopes before core collapse.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper evaluates PLAsTiCC and ELAsTiCC simulations of LSST light curves and their biases when used to train photometric classification models.

conceptDetails.name: double-peaked light curves
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates the physical implications of frequently observed double-peaked light curves in fast-evolving SESNe including SNe Ibn.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian Process templates inherently provide uncertainty estimates, and the paper addresses biases and uncertainties in template construction and photometric diversity assessment.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies unusual and fast-evolving SESNe (e.g., SNe Ibn, Ic-bl) as outliers from typical photometric evolution, effectively performing anomaly characterization.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian Process regression relies on kernel functions to model correlations in the light curve data across time and photometric bands.

title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation
abstract: Controlling contact forces during interactions is critical for locomotion and manipulation tasks. While sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) has succeeded in many contact-rich problems, current RL methods achieve forceful interactions implicitly without explicitly regulating forces. We propose a method for training RL policies for direct force control without requiring access to force sensing. We showcase our method on a whole-body control platform of a quadruped robot with an arm. Such force control enables us to perform gravity compensation and impedance control, unlocking compliant whole-body manipulation. The learned whole-body controller with variable compliance makes it intuitive for humans to teleoperate the robot by only commanding the manipulator, and the robot's body adjusts automatically to achieve the desired position and force. Consequently, a human teleoperator can easily demonstrate a wide variety of loco-manipulation tasks. To the best of our knowledge, we provide the first deployment of learned whole-body force control in legged manipulators, paving the way for more versatile and adaptable legged robots.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The core training paradigm used to learn force-tracking policies for whole-body control of legged manipulators without requiring force sensors.

conceptDetails.name: contact force regulation
conceptDetails.description: Explicit regulation of end-effector contact forces is the primary contribution, enabling gravity compensation and compliant interaction without force sensors.

conceptDetails.name: whole-body loco-manipulation
conceptDetails.description: The central robotics task domain requiring coordinated control of a quadruped body and mounted arm to perform contact-rich tasks across a large workspace.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: The method relies on simulation-trained policies that generalize to real quadruped-arm systems, avoiding the need for physical force-torque sensors during training.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Custom reward functions combining force tracking objectives with locomotion regularization terms are designed to train the force control policy.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Policies are trained entirely in simulation and transferred to physical hardware (sim-to-real), avoiding the need for real-world force sensor data.

conceptDetails.name: impedance control
conceptDetails.description: Closing the force control loop enables impedance control, allowing the teleoperator to modulate end-effector compliance and stiffness during manipulation.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A novel objective combining force tracking error with standard RL regularization terms replaces classical control formulations to achieve direct force command following.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The policy simultaneously handles locomotion velocity control and end-effector force/position tracking in a unified whole-body framework.

title: Introducing the DREAMS Project: DaRk mattEr and Astrophysics with Machine learning and Simulations
abstract: We introduce the DREAMS project, an innovative approach to understanding the astrophysical implications of alternative dark matter models and their effects on galaxy formation and evolution. The DREAMS project will ultimately comprise thousands of cosmological hydrodynamic simulations that simultaneously vary over dark matter physics, astrophysics, and cosmology in modeling a range of systems -- from galaxy clusters to ultra-faint satellites. Such extensive simulation suites can provide adequate training sets for machine-learning-based analyses. This paper introduces two new cosmological hydrodynamical suites of Warm Dark Matter, each comprised of 1024 simulations generated using the Arepo code. One suite consists of uniform-box simulations covering a $(25~h^{-1}~{\rm M}_\odot)^3$ volume, while the other consists of Milky Way zoom-ins with sufficient resolution to capture the properties of classical satellites. For each simulation, the Warm Dark Matter particle mass is varied along with the initial density field and several parameters controlling the strength of baryonic feedback within the IllustrisTNG model. We provide two examples, separately utilizing emulators and Convolutional Neural Networks, to demonstrate how such simulation suites can be used to disentangle the effects of dark matter and baryonic physics on galactic properties. The DREAMS project can be extended further to include different dark matter models, galaxy formation physics, and astrophysical targets. In this way, it will provide an unparalleled opportunity to characterize uncertainties on predictions for small-scale observables, leading to robust predictions for testing the particle physics nature of dark matter on these scales.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces the DREAMS project specifically to study alternative dark matter models (Warm Dark Matter, Cold Dark Matter) and their astrophysical implications.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The core methodology involves 1024+ hydrodynamic cosmological simulations using the Arepo code to vary dark matter and baryonic physics parameters.

conceptDetails.name: warm dark matter models
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paper specifically introduces Warm Dark Matter simulation suites varying particle mass as the primary dark matter alternative explored.

conceptDetails.name: baryonic feedback
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: supernova and AGN feedback parameters from IllustrisTNG are systematically varied to disentangle their effects from dark matter physics signatures.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: Emulators are used as one of two demonstration ML approaches to interpolate simulation outputs and predict galactic properties across parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional Neural Networks are used to disentangle the effects of dark matter and baryonic physics on galactic properties from simulation images.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The project enables simulation-based inference by generating large training sets to constrain dark matter physics from astrophysical observables.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A primary goal is characterizing astrophysical uncertainties from baryonic feedback to produce robust predictions for dark matter observables.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Emulators serve as surrogate models for the expensive cosmological simulations, enabling rapid parameter space exploration.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework aims to enable Bayesian posterior estimation of dark matter and baryonic physics parameters from observed galaxy properties.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: ML models are trained to classify and characterize galaxy properties as functions of dark matter and baryonic physics parameters.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Cosmological parameters including dark energy are varied across simulations to account for cosmological uncertainties in the DREAMS suite.

title: KAN: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
abstract: Inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, we propose Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) as promising alternatives to Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs). While MLPs have fixed activation functions on nodes ("neurons"), KANs have learnable activation functions on edges ("weights"). KANs have no linear weights at all -- every weight parameter is replaced by a univariate function parametrized as a spline. We show that this seemingly simple change makes KANs outperform MLPs in terms of accuracy and interpretability. For accuracy, much smaller KANs can achieve comparable or better accuracy than much larger MLPs in data fitting and PDE solving. Theoretically and empirically, KANs possess faster neural scaling laws than MLPs. For interpretability, KANs can be intuitively visualized and can easily interact with human users. Through two examples in mathematics and physics, KANs are shown to be useful collaborators helping scientists (re)discover mathematical and physical laws. In summary, KANs are promising alternatives for MLPs, opening opportunities for further improving today's deep learning models which rely heavily on MLPs.

conceptDetails.name: kolmogorov-arnold networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: a new neural network architecture replacing fixed node activations with learnable edge activation functions parametrized as splines, inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: KANs are shown to be intrinsically interpretable through visualization and symbolic simplification, enabling scientists to extract human-readable mathematical and physical laws directly from the network.

conceptDetails.name: spline activation functions
conceptDetails.description: The core architectural innovation: each learnable weight in a KAN is a B-spline function, enabling accurate low-dimensional function approximation while inheriting MLP's compositional learning.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: KANs act as 'collaborators' that help scientists rediscover known laws (Anderson localization, knot theory invariants) and potentially discover new ones through interactive network analysis.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Function fitting and data regression are the primary benchmarks used to demonstrate KAN accuracy advantages over MLPs, including high-dimensional compositional functions.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: KANs can be post-processed to extract exact symbolic mathematical expressions, bridging neural network approximation and closed-form scientific law discovery.

conceptDetails.name: neural scaling laws
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives and measures how KAN test loss scales with model size, demonstrating theoretically superior scaling exponents compared to MLPs for structured compositional functions.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Neural scaling laws for KANs are derived theoretically and validated empirically, showing KANs achieve faster scaling (better accuracy per parameter) than MLPs.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: KANs are applied to PDE solving (Poisson equation) as a demonstration of their accuracy benefits over MLPs in scientific computing contexts.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes an interactive human-AI workflow where scientists iteratively prune, visualize, and query KANs to extract scientific knowledge from data.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Network sparsification and pruning techniques are introduced to simplify KANs into minimal interpretable structures that reveal underlying symbolic relationships.

title: SN 2024ggi in NGC 3621: Rising Ionization in a Nearby, CSM-Interacting Type II Supernova
abstract: We present UV/optical/NIR observations and modeling of supernova (SN) 2024ggi, a type II supernova (SN II) located in NGC 3621 at 7.2 Mpc. Early-time ("flash") spectroscopy of SN 2024ggi within +0.8 days of discovery shows emission lines of H I, He I, C III, and N III with a narrow core and broad, symmetric wings (i.e., IIn-like) arising from the photoionized, optically-thick, unshocked circumstellar material (CSM) that surrounded the progenitor star at shock breakout. By the next spectral epoch at +1.5 days, SN 2024ggi showed a rise in ionization as emission lines of He II, C IV, N IV/V and O V became visible. This phenomenon is temporally consistent with a blueward shift in the UV/optical colors, both likely the result of shock breakout in an extended, dense CSM. The IIn-like features in SN 2024ggi persist on a timescale of $t_{\rm IIn} = 3.8 \pm 1.6$ days at which time a reduction in CSM density allows the detection of Doppler broadened features from the fastest SN material. SN 2024ggi has peak UV/optical absolute magnitudes of $M_{\rm w2} = -18.7$ mag and $M_{\rm g} = -18.1$ mag that are consistent with the known population of CSM-interacting SNe II. Comparison of SN 2024ggi with a grid of radiation hydrodynamics and non-local thermodynamic equilibrium (nLTE) radiative-transfer simulations suggests a progenitor mass-loss rate of $\dot{M} = 10^{-2}$M$_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ ($v_w$ = 50 km/s), confined to a distance of $r < 5\times 10^{14}$ cm. Assuming a wind velocity of $v_w$ = 50 km/s, the progenitor star underwent an enhanced mass-loss episode in the last ~3 years before explosion.

conceptDetails.name: flash spectroscopy
conceptDetails.description: Ultra-rapid early-time spectroscopy within hours of discovery used to capture shock breakout signatures and photoionized CSM emission lines in SN 2024ggi.

conceptDetails.name: csm interaction
conceptDetails.description: The central physical phenomenon driving the observed IIn-like spectral features, rising ionization, and UV/optical light curve behavior as supernova ejecta interact with dense circumstellar material.

conceptDetails.name: shock breakout
conceptDetails.description: The radiation event probed by early flash spectroscopy in which the shock front escapes through optically-thick extended CSM, producing the ionization rise and blueward UV color shift observed in SN 2024ggi.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper constrains the red supergiant progenitor's mass-loss history, finding an enhanced episode of ~10^-2 solar masses per year in the final ~3 years before explosion.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: A grid of radiation hydrodynamics and nLTE radiative-transfer simulations is compared against observations to infer progenitor mass-loss rate and CSM geometry.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Observed UV/optical/NIR light curves and spectra are inverted through forward simulation grids to recover progenitor and CSM properties.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The CMFGEN nLTE radiative-transfer code grid serves as a surrogate physical model enabling parameter space exploration of CSM density and mass-loss rates.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes SN 2024ggi as a Type II SN showing transient IIn-like spectral features, discussing its classification within the CSM-interacting SN II population.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detection and characterization of narrow emission lines, Lorentzian electron-scattering wings, and high-ionization species (He II, N IV/V, O V) at very early epochs is central to the observational methodology.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Multi-epoch UV/optical/NIR spectral analysis is used to track the ionization evolution and identify the timescale of IIn-like feature persistence in SN 2024ggi.

title: Position-space renormalization schemes for four-quark operators in HQET
abstract: X-space schemes are gauge-invariant, regulator-independent renormalization schemes that are defined by requiring position-space correlation functions of gauge invariant operators to be equal to their noninteracting values at particular kinematic points. These schemes can be used to nonperturbatively renormalize composite operators in Lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (LQCD), and by computing matching coefficients between the X-space scheme and MSbar in the dimensionally-regulated continuum, matrix elements calculated with LQCD can be converted to MSbar-renormalized matrix elements. Using X-space schemes for Heavy Quark Effective Theory (HQET) operators has the additional benefit that appropriate ratios of position-space correlation functions cancel the power divergent static-quark self-energy of Lattice HQET nonperturbatively. This work presents the O($α_S$) matching coefficients between X-space renormalized four-quark flavor-nonsinglet HQET operators relevant for the lifetimes of charm- and bottom-hadrons, and four-quark HQET operators relevant for mixing between neutral mesons containing a heavy quark, such as B-Bbar mixing.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops O(α_S) matching coefficients between X-space renormalization schemes and MS-bar for four-quark HQET operators, with the X-space scheme defined by position-space correlation function conditions.

conceptDetails.name: x-space renormalization
conceptDetails.description: The central method of the paper: a gauge-invariant, regulator-independent scheme defined by requiring position-space correlation functions of composite operators to match their noninteracting values at fixed spacetime separation.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: LQCD is the primary computational framework these X-space renormalization schemes are designed to serve, enabling nonperturbative renormalization of composite operators without gauge-fixing artifacts.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Heavy Quark Effective Theory (HQET) is the specific EFT whose four-quark operators are being renormalized, exploiting the static-limit simplifications for charm and bottom hadron systems.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The lattice gauge theory formulation introduces the power-divergent static-quark self-energy that the proposed correlation-function ratio scheme cancels nonperturbatively.

conceptDetails.name: b-meson mixing
conceptDetails.description: One of the two key physics applications targeted: the ΔQ=2 four-quark HQET operators whose renormalized matrix elements govern B–B-bar oscillation amplitudes.

conceptDetails.name: static quark power divergence
conceptDetails.description: A distinctive complication of lattice HQET in which the static-quark self-energy diverges as O(α_S/a); this paper shows how ratios of three-point to two-point correlation functions cancel it nonperturbatively.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Perturbative QFT techniques including dimensional regularization and one-loop integral evaluation are used to compute the continuum matching coefficients between X-space and MS-bar schemes.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The four-quark operators renormalized in this work are directly relevant to precision Standard Model tests in the flavor sector, including hadron lifetime ratios and neutral B-meson oscillations.

title: Learning Galaxy Intrinsic Alignment Correlations
abstract: The intrinsic alignments (IA) of galaxies, regarded as a contaminant in weak lensing analyses, represents the correlation of galaxy shapes due to gravitational tidal interactions and galaxy formation processes. As such, understanding IA is paramount for accurate cosmological inferences from weak lensing surveys; however, one limitation to our understanding and mitigation of IA is expensive simulation-based modeling. In this work, we present a deep learning approach to emulate galaxy position-position ($ξ$), position-orientation ($ω$), and orientation-orientation ($η$) correlation function measurements and uncertainties from halo occupation distribution-based mock galaxy catalogs. We find strong Pearson correlation values with the model across all three correlation functions and further predict aleatoric uncertainties through a mean-variance estimation training procedure. $ξ(r)$ predictions are generally accurate to $\leq10\%$. Our model also successfully captures the underlying signal of the noisier correlations $ω(r)$ and $η(r)$, although with a lower average accuracy. We find that the model performance is inhibited by the stochasticity of the data, and will benefit from correlations averaged over multiple data realizations. Our code will be made open source upon journal publication.

conceptDetails.name: intrinsic alignment
conceptDetails.description: The central physical phenomenon being modeled: correlations between galaxy shapes due to gravitational tidal interactions, which this paper emulates via deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary contribution is a neural network emulator that replaces expensive simulation-based computation of IA correlation functions.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The encoder-decoder network acts as a surrogate for HOD-based N-body simulation pipelines, enabling rapid inference of correlation statistics.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Aleatoric uncertainties in the correlation function predictions are learned via mean-variance estimation during training.

conceptDetails.name: halo occupation distribution
conceptDetails.description: The generative framework used to produce mock galaxy catalogs from N-body simulations; HOD parameters serve as direct inputs to the emulator.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: N-body simulations provide the training data from which galaxy catalogs and correlation statistics are derived.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The emulator is designed to accelerate Monte Carlo parameter inference from simulation-derived observables, a core use case highlighted in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: weak gravitational lensing
conceptDetails.description: The primary cosmological application context: IA is a systematic contaminant of weak lensing signals that this work helps characterize and mitigate.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Dark matter halos and their structural properties are the foundation of the HOD model and govern the tidal fields driving intrinsic alignments.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The supervised learning task is continuous regression of correlation function amplitudes across multiple separation bins from HOD parameter inputs.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The model simultaneously predicts all three correlation functions (ξ, ω, η) and their uncertainties in a single forward pass.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The emulator is explicitly intended to enable efficient Monte Carlo-based cosmological parameter inference by replacing slow simulation evaluations.

title: PhysDreamer: Physics-Based Interaction with 3D Objects via Video Generation
abstract: Realistic object interactions are crucial for creating immersive virtual experiences, yet synthesizing realistic 3D object dynamics in response to novel interactions remains a significant challenge. Unlike unconditional or text-conditioned dynamics generation, action-conditioned dynamics requires perceiving the physical material properties of objects and grounding the 3D motion prediction on these properties, such as object stiffness. However, estimating physical material properties is an open problem due to the lack of material ground-truth data, as measuring these properties for real objects is highly difficult. We present PhysDreamer, a physics-based approach that endows static 3D objects with interactive dynamics by leveraging the object dynamics priors learned by video generation models. By distilling these priors, PhysDreamer enables the synthesis of realistic object responses to novel interactions, such as external forces or agent manipulations. We demonstrate our approach on diverse examples of elastic objects and evaluate the realism of the synthesized interactions through a user study. PhysDreamer takes a step towards more engaging and realistic virtual experiences by enabling static 3D objects to dynamically respond to interactive stimuli in a physically plausible manner. See our project page at https://physdreamer.github.io/.

conceptDetails.name: video prior distillation
conceptDetails.description: Novel core method that transfers implicit physics knowledge from video generation models into a 3D object's material field by matching rendered dynamics to video model predictions.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Video diffusion models serve as the primary source of physics dynamics priors, whose learned knowledge about object behavior is distilled into material property estimation.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) from video diffusion models is used to optimize the physical material field by matching synthesized dynamics to video model predictions.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A neural field encodes spatially-varying physical material properties and is jointly optimized with a differentiable physics simulator to produce realistic elastic dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Estimating physical material properties (e.g., stiffness fields) from video observations without ground-truth measurements is the central inverse problem PhysDreamer solves.

conceptDetails.name: material point method
conceptDetails.description: A hybrid Lagrangian-Eulerian simulation technique used as the differentiable physics engine to simulate elastic deformation under arbitrary applied forces.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Pre-trained video generation models provide implicit physical world knowledge that is leveraged to supervise material property estimation for 3D objects.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The Material Point Method (MPM), a Lagrangian-Eulerian simulation technique, models elastic object dynamics and is made differentiable to enable gradient-based material optimization.

conceptDetails.name: 3d gaussian splatting
conceptDetails.description: Explicit 3D Gaussian representation used to reconstruct static objects and render synthesized dynamics from arbitrary viewpoints with high visual quality.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Differentiable physics simulation is integrated with neural rendering to infer material parameters by backpropagating through both the simulator and renderer.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: 3D Gaussians represent object geometry and appearance while a neural field represents the material property distribution, together enabling physically-grounded 3D dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The learned neural material field acts as a continuous surrogate for spatially-varying physical properties that cannot be directly measured in real objects.

title: Constraints on the finite volume two-nucleon spectrum at $m_π\approx 806$ MeV
abstract: The low-energy finite-volume spectrum of the two-nucleon system at a quark mass corresponding to a pion mass of $m_π\approx 806$ MeV is studied with lattice quantum chromodynamics (LQCD) using variational methods. The interpolating-operator sets used in [Phys.Rev.D 107 (2023) 9, 094508] are extended by including a complete basis of local hexaquark operators, as well as plane-wave dibaryon operators built from products of both positive- and negative-parity nucleon operators. Results are presented for the isosinglet and isotriplet two-nucleon channels. In both channels, noticably weaker variational bounds on the lowest few energy eigenvalues are obtained from operator sets which contain only hexaquark operators or operators constructed from the product of two negative-parity nucleons, while other operator sets produce low-energy variational bounds which are consistent within statistical uncertainties. The consequences of these studies for the LQCD understanding of the two-nucleon spectrum are investigated.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses LQCD as its primary computational framework to study the finite-volume two-nucleon spectrum at mπ ≈ 806 MeV from first principles.

conceptDetails.name: finite-volume spectroscopy
conceptDetails.description: The central methodology of the paper: extracting constraints on the two-nucleon energy spectrum in a finite spatial volume to infer infinite-volume scattering parameters.

conceptDetails.name: variational operator basis
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary contribution is the systematic construction and comparison of extended operator bases including hexaquark, dibaryon, and negative-parity nucleon operators to tighten variational bounds.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The generalized eigenvalue problem (GEVP) is the core variational technique used to extract upper bounds on energy eigenvalues from sets of interpolating operators.

conceptDetails.name: hexaquark operators
conceptDetails.description: A complete basis of local hexaquark operators (six-quark operators not factorizable into two color-singlet baryons) is newly introduced and assessed for its variational coverage of the two-nucleon spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Finite-volume spectroscopy is used to extract energy levels of the isosinglet and isotriplet two-nucleon channels from Euclidean two-point correlation functions.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The paper aims to constrain two-nucleon scattering phase shifts and mixing angles via the finite-volume energy spectrum through the Lüscher formalism.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Nuclear EFTs provide the low-energy matching framework that connects LQCD results to nuclear observables and inspire the quasi-local operator constructions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: LQCD computations rely on Monte Carlo importance sampling over gauge field configurations to evaluate path integrals non-perturbatively.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: QCD as a quantum field theory provides the theoretical foundation for the nucleon-nucleon interaction calculations performed on the lattice.

title: Practical applications of machine-learned flows on gauge fields
abstract: Normalizing flows are machine-learned maps between different lattice theories which can be used as components in exact sampling and inference schemes. Ongoing work yields increasingly expressive flows on gauge fields, but it remains an open question how flows can improve lattice QCD at state-of-the-art scales. We discuss and demonstrate two applications of flows in replica exchange (parallel tempering) sampling, aimed at improving topological mixing, which are viable with iterative improvements upon presently available flows.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Machine-learned invertible maps between gauge field distributions, used as approximate bridges between base and target theories for exact sampling.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics application domain, with flows aimed at improving Monte Carlo sampling of QCD field configurations at state-of-the-art scales.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The formal framework of discretized gauge theories (pure-gauge SU(3) with Wilson action) on which the flows are trained and tested.

conceptDetails.name: parallel tempering
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the replica exchange algorithm framework in which flows bridge between replicas at different action parameters to accelerate topological mixing.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The baseline sampling algorithms (heatbath, overrelaxation, HMC) being augmented and improved via flow-based proposals and replica exchange.

conceptDetails.name: topological freezing
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the critical sampling problem in lattice QCD where MCMC gets stuck in topological sectors at fine lattice spacings, which this work targets.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Tractable evaluation of the model distribution q(U) via the flow Jacobian determinant, enabling reweighting and Metropolis corrections for exact inference.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The flow architecture employs equivariant smearing of gauge links to preserve gauge symmetry throughout the learned transformation.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Flows are used as components in exact inference schemes, correcting approximate samples via importance reweighting or independence Metropolis.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Integrated autocorrelation time of Markov chains is the key metric used to quantify sampling efficiency improvements from flow-augmented algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Models are transferred across different lattice volumes and action parameters to reduce training cost in the replica exchange demonstrations.

title: Multiscale Normalizing Flows for Gauge Theories
abstract: Scale separation is an important physical principle that has previously enabled algorithmic advances such as multigrid solvers. Previous work on normalizing flows has been able to utilize scale separation in the context of scalar field theories, but the principle has been largely unexploited in the context of gauge theories. This work gives an overview of a new method for generating gauge fields using hierarchical normalizing flow models. This method builds gauge fields from the outside in, allowing different parts of the model to focus on different scales of the problem. Numerical results are presented for $U(1)$ and $SU(3)$ gauge theories in 2, 3, and 4 spacetime dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core ML framework used to construct expressive learned samplers for gauge field configurations via bijective transformations.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The physical framework in which the method is applied, targeting U(1) and SU(3) gauge theories on discrete spacetime lattices.

conceptDetails.name: multiscale hierarchy
conceptDetails.description: Novel hierarchical construction where gauge fields are built coarse-to-fine via successive doubling layers, enabling scale-separated modeling.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops hierarchical generative models for sampling gauge field configurations in lattice field theory.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Multiscale normalizing flows are used as improved samplers within Monte Carlo frameworks, addressing critical slowing down and topological freezing.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Gauge-equivariant architectures are employed within the flow model to preserve gauge symmetry throughout the sampling procedure.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Maintaining gauge symmetry (U(1) and SU(3)) is a core architectural constraint imposed on the multiscale flow layers.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within the theoretical framework of quantum field theory, targeting path integral sampling for gauge theories.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The flow model learns to estimate and sample from the target Boltzmann density of the gauge theory.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A primary motivation of the multiscale approach is to scale normalizing flow samplers to larger, physically relevant lattice volumes.

conceptDetails.name: topological freezing
conceptDetails.description: A key pathology in lattice field theory sampling that normalizing flows, including the multiscale approach, aim to mitigate.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Reverse KL divergence minimization and reweighting techniques are used to align the model density with the target posterior.

title: TENG: Time-Evolving Natural Gradient for Solving PDEs With Deep Neural Nets Toward Machine Precision
abstract: Partial differential equations (PDEs) are instrumental for modeling dynamical systems in science and engineering. The advent of neural networks has initiated a significant shift in tackling these complexities though challenges in accuracy persist, especially for initial value problems. In this paper, we introduce the $\textit{Time-Evolving Natural Gradient (TENG)}$, generalizing time-dependent variational principles and optimization-based time integration, leveraging natural gradient optimization to obtain high accuracy in neural-network-based PDE solutions. Our comprehensive development includes algorithms like TENG-Euler and its high-order variants, such as TENG-Heun, tailored for enhanced precision and efficiency. TENG's effectiveness is further validated through its performance, surpassing current leading methods and achieving $\textit{machine precision}$ in step-by-step optimizations across a spectrum of PDEs, including the heat equation, Allen-Cahn equation, and Burgers' equation.

conceptDetails.name: natural gradient optimization
conceptDetails.description: The core innovation of TENG is leveraging natural gradient (Fisher matrix-based second-order optimization) to achieve machine precision in PDE solving.

conceptDetails.name: sequential-in-time optimization
conceptDetails.description: TENG operates step-by-step in time, updating neural network parameters at each time step rather than globally, enabling high per-step accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: time-dependent variational principle
conceptDetails.description: TENG generalizes TDVP, which derives parameter-space ODEs from function-space PDEs via Dirac-Frenkel projection onto the neural network manifold.

conceptDetails.name: neural operators
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks serve as function approximators parameterizing PDE solutions at each time step, central to the TENG framework.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: TENG defines a novel loss function directly in u-space and optimizes via repeated projections to the tangent space of the neural network manifold.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks act as surrogate models for PDE solutions, approximating complex dynamical systems across spatial and temporal domains.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: TENG is compared against PINNs as a baseline global-in-time optimization method for PDE solving, demonstrating superior accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: TENG employs sparse update strategies for efficient realization of the natural gradient algorithms, reducing computational cost.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The time-dependent variational principle (TDVP), a key method generalized by TENG, has deep roots in Hamiltonian and variational mechanics.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses the inverse problem of finding neural network parameters that accurately reproduce PDE dynamics through sequential optimization.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling methods are used to estimate integrals appearing in the natural gradient computation within TENG.

title: The Frozen Phase of Heterotic F-theory Duality
abstract: We study the duality between the Spin$(32)/\mathbb{Z}_2$ heterotic string without vector structure and F-theory with frozen singularities. We give a complete description in theories with $6$d $\mathcal{N}=(1,0)$ supersymmetry and identify the duals of Spin$(32)/\mathbb{Z}_2$-instantons on ADE singularities without vector structure in the frozen phase of F-theory using an ansatz introduced by Bhardwaj, Morrison, Tachikawa, and Tomasiello. As a consequence, we obtain a strongly coupled description of orbifold phases of type I string theory without vector structure, substantially expanding the list of known examples of $6$d F-theory compactifications with frozen singularities. Supergravity theories can be fused from these instanton theories, in a way that commutes with switching off vector structure, which we use to propose new consistency checks via neutral hypermultiplet counting. Finally, we describe various Higgsings of this duality, and comment on constraints on higher form symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies string theory dualities, specifically between heterotic string theory and F-theory, as the central theoretical framework.

conceptDetails.name: frozen singularities
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central novel contribution: F-theory compactifications with frozen O7+ orientifold planes that cannot be deformed, expanding the known landscape of 6d vacua.

conceptDetails.name: heterotic-f-theory duality
conceptDetails.description: The duality between Spin(32)/Z2 heterotic string without vector structure and F-theory with frozen singularities is the primary subject of study.

conceptDetails.name: vector structure
conceptDetails.description: The absence of vector structure in Spin(32)/Z2 compactifications is a key structural feature distinguishing the theories studied.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: 6d N=(1,0) supersymmetric quantum field theories are the physical theories constructed and analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The frozen versus unfrozen phases of F-theory compactifications represent distinct phases, and transitions between them are studied.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: ADE singularity classifications, Spin(32)/Z2 gauge groups, and discrete center 1-form symmetries are analyzed using group-theoretic methods.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Higgsings of the SO(32) gauge group and transitions between different phases are analyzed as symmetry breaking mechanisms.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs effective supergravity theories in 6d from string compactifications as low-energy descriptions.

conceptDetails.name: higher form symmetries
conceptDetails.description: Constraints on discrete center 1-form gauge symmetries in the frozen phase are analyzed as part of the gauge group topology discussion.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly cancellation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge, gravitational, and mixed anomaly cancellation conditions are used as consistency checks for the constructed frozen F-theory vacua.

conceptDetails.name: swampland program
conceptDetails.description: The paper situates its results in the context of the swampland program, using F-theory constructions to test and inform swampland conjectures.

title: Is Model Collapse Inevitable? Breaking the Curse of Recursion by Accumulating Real and Synthetic Data
abstract: The proliferation of generative models, combined with pretraining on web-scale data, raises a timely question: what happens when these models are trained on their own generated outputs? Recent investigations into model-data feedback loops proposed that such loops would lead to a phenomenon termed model collapse, under which performance progressively degrades with each model-data feedback iteration until fitted models become useless. However, those studies largely assumed that new data replace old data over time, where an arguably more realistic assumption is that data accumulate over time. In this paper, we ask: what effect does accumulating data have on model collapse? We empirically study this question by pretraining sequences of language models on text corpora. We confirm that replacing the original real data by each generation's synthetic data does indeed tend towards model collapse, then demonstrate that accumulating the successive generations of synthetic data alongside the original real data avoids model collapse; these results hold across a range of model sizes, architectures, and hyperparameters. We obtain similar results for deep generative models on other types of real data: diffusion models for molecule conformation generation and variational autoencoders for image generation. To understand why accumulating data can avoid model collapse, we use an analytically tractable framework introduced by prior work in which a sequence of linear models are fit to the previous models' outputs. Previous work used this framework to show that if data are replaced, the test error increases with the number of model-fitting iterations; we extend this argument to prove that if data instead accumulate, the test error has a finite upper bound independent of the number of iterations, meaning model collapse no longer occurs.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies sequences of generative models (transformers, diffusion models, VAEs) trained iteratively on their own outputs to analyze model collapse behavior.

conceptDetails.name: model collapse
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon studied: progressive degradation of generative model performance when models are trained on their own synthetic outputs across iterations.

conceptDetails.name: synthetic data accumulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's key intervention and finding: retaining all prior generations of synthetic data alongside real data provably avoids model collapse with a finite upper bound on test error.

conceptDetails.name: model-data feedback loops
conceptDetails.description: The recursive training dynamic where a model generates synthetic data used to train the next model generation, creating iterative feedback that can cause collapse or stable convergence depending on the data retention strategy.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Causal transformer language models (GPT-2 and Llama2 variants) serve as the primary empirical setting for studying model collapse under data replacement vs. accumulation.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The analytical framework uses sequences of linear regression models to prove that accumulated data yields bounded test error, providing theoretical grounding for the empirical findings.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion models for molecule conformation generation are one of three deep generative model classes used to empirically validate that data accumulation avoids model collapse.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: VAEs for image generation are used as one of the three empirical testbeds to demonstrate that accumulating synthetic data prevents model collapse.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Data accumulation—retaining all prior real and synthetic data across training iterations—functions as a form of augmentation that stabilizes training and prevents model collapse.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Experiments are conducted across a range of model sizes (9M to 125M parameters), architectures, and hyperparameters to verify that results scale broadly.

conceptDetails.name: molecular dynamics
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion models for molecular conformation generation are trained and evaluated as one modality, connecting the findings to molecular science applications.

title: Anomaly Detection and Approximate Similarity Searches of Transients in Real-time Data Streams
