abstract: We present LAISS (Lightcurve Anomaly Identification and Similarity Search), an automated pipeline to detect anomalous astrophysical transients in real-time data streams. We deploy our anomaly detection model on the nightly ZTF Alert Stream via the ANTARES broker, identifying a manageable $\sim$1-5 candidates per night for expert vetting and coordinating follow-up observations. Our method leverages statistical light-curve and contextual host-galaxy features within a random forest classifier, tagging transients of rare classes (spectroscopic anomalies), of uncommon host-galaxy environments (contextual anomalies), and of peculiar or interaction-powered phenomena (behavioral anomalies). Moreover, we demonstrate the power of a low-latency ($\sim$ms) approximate similarity search method to find transient analogs with similar light-curve evolution and host-galaxy environments. We use analogs for data-driven discovery, characterization, (re-)classification, and imputation in retrospective and real-time searches. To date we have identified $\sim$50 previously known and previously missed rare transients from real-time and retrospective searches, including but not limited to: SLSNe, TDEs, SNe IIn, SNe IIb, SNe Ia-CSM, SNe Ia-91bg-like, SNe Ib, SNe Ic, SNe Ic-BL, and M31 novae. Lastly, we report the discovery of 325 total transients, all observed between 2018-2021 and absent from public catalogs ($\sim$1% of all ZTF Astronomical Transient reports to the Transient Name Server through 2021). These methods enable a systematic approach to finding the "needle in the haystack" in large-volume data streams. Because of its integration with the ANTARES broker, LAISS is built to detect exciting transients in Rubin data.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The core method of LAISS, using a random forest classifier to identify spectroscopic, contextual, and behavioral anomalies among astrophysical transients in real-time ZTF alert streams.

conceptDetails.name: approximate similarity search
conceptDetails.description: A novel low-latency (~ms) approximate nearest-neighbor search over light-curve and host-galaxy feature spaces is used to retrieve photometric analogs for characterization and imputation.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: A random forest classifier is employed to combine statistical light-curve and host-galaxy features for anomaly scoring and transient classification.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: LAISS identifies and classifies diverse rare supernova subtypes (SLSNe, SNe IIn, SNe Ia-CSM, SNe Ic-BL, etc.) from photometric light curves without full spectroscopic data.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Statistical light-curve features and contextual host-galaxy features are extracted to build the feature vectors used in anomaly detection and similarity search.

conceptDetails.name: real-time alert broker
conceptDetails.description: Integration with the ANTARES broker enables LAISS to process nightly ZTF alert streams in real time, a key infrastructure contribution for scalability to Rubin/LSST.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Transients are embedded into a feature space enabling approximate nearest-neighbor similarity searches to find photometric and contextual analogs.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Beyond anomaly detection, LAISS performs (re-)classification of transients using data-driven analog matching in lieu of spectroscopic confirmation.

conceptDetails.name: novelty detection
conceptDetails.description: LAISS is designed to identify previously unknown or uncataloged transients, including 325 objects absent from public catalogs, enabling discovery of genuinely new phenomena.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: LAISS is an end-to-end automated pipeline integrated with the ANTARES broker for real-time ingestion, scoring, and follow-up coordination of ZTF and future Rubin/LSST alerts.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames transient discovery as detecting rare signals in high-volume data streams, directly addressing the 'needle in the haystack' problem for large photometric surveys.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline prioritizes ~1-5 candidates per night for expert vetting and coordinated follow-up, implementing an active human-in-the-loop discovery workflow.

title: Feature Splatting: Language-Driven Physics-Based Scene Synthesis and Editing
abstract: Scene representations using 3D Gaussian primitives have produced excellent results in modeling the appearance of static and dynamic 3D scenes. Many graphics applications, however, demand the ability to manipulate both the appearance and the physical properties of objects. We introduce Feature Splatting, an approach that unifies physics-based dynamic scene synthesis with rich semantics from vision language foundation models that are grounded by natural language. Our first contribution is a way to distill high-quality, object-centric vision-language features into 3D Gaussians, that enables semi-automatic scene decomposition using text queries. Our second contribution is a way to synthesize physics-based dynamics from an otherwise static scene using a particle-based simulator, in which material properties are assigned automatically via text queries. We ablate key techniques used in this pipeline, to illustrate the challenge and opportunities in using feature-carrying 3D Gaussians as a unified format for appearance, geometry, material properties and semantics grounded on natural language. Project website: https://feature-splatting.github.io/

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Feature Splatting learns a unified 3D Gaussian representation that encodes appearance, geometry, semantics, and physics simultaneously through distillation from 2D foundation models.

conceptDetails.name: 3d gaussian splatting
conceptDetails.description: An explicit scene representation using 3D Gaussian primitives that serves as the foundation for Feature Splatting's unified appearance, semantics, and physics pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The method extracts high-quality, object-centric vision-language features from CLIP, DINOv2, and SAM foundation models and fuses them for accurate scene decomposition.

conceptDetails.name: language-grounded scene editing
conceptDetails.description: Open-vocabulary text queries drive both semi-automatic scene decomposition and automatic material property assignment, enabling natural-language-driven physical scene synthesis.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Vision-language embeddings from 2D foundation models are distilled into 3D Gaussian primitives to enable language-grounded semantic queries on 3D scenes.

conceptDetails.name: material point method
conceptDetails.description: MPM-based particle physics simulation is adapted to the 3D Gaussian representation to synthesize physically realistic dynamics for segmented scene objects.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The method operates on explicit 3D Gaussian primitives as geometric structures, requiring specialized handling of rotations and deformations under physical simulation.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Foundation models like DINOv2 and CLIP, trained via self-supervised and contrastive objectives, provide the semantic features that are distilled into the 3D representation.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: A particle-based MPM physics engine serves as a surrogate for real-world physical dynamics, enabling plausible simulation of material behavior within the Gaussian scene.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Feature Splatting solves the inverse problem of reconstructing a complete 3D scene representation—including semantics and physics properties—from 2D image observations.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Language grounding provides a human-interpretable interface to the 3D scene, allowing users to query, decompose, and assign physical properties via natural language.

title: Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Interpretable Causal Graphs in Language Models
abstract: We introduce methods for discovering and applying sparse feature circuits. These are causally implicated subnetworks of human-interpretable features for explaining language model behaviors. Circuits identified in prior work consist of polysemantic and difficult-to-interpret units like attention heads or neurons, rendering them unsuitable for many downstream applications. In contrast, sparse feature circuits enable detailed understanding of unanticipated mechanisms. Because they are based on fine-grained units, sparse feature circuits are useful for downstream tasks: We introduce SHIFT, where we improve the generalization of a classifier by ablating features that a human judges to be task-irrelevant. Finally, we demonstrate an entirely unsupervised and scalable interpretability pipeline by discovering thousands of sparse feature circuits for automatically discovered model behaviors.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a scalable pipeline for discovering human-interpretable causal subnetworks (sparse feature circuits) in language models.

conceptDetails.name: causal circuit discovery
conceptDetails.description: Novel method combining SAE features with attribution patching to discover sparse causal subgraphs that explain specific language model behaviors.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are used to decompose language model activations into interpretable feature directions, forming the foundation for circuit discovery.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity is a core design principle: sparse feature activations from SAEs and sparse circuit graphs enable tractable, interpretable representations of model behavior.

conceptDetails.name: feature attribution
conceptDetails.description: Linear approximations to indirect effects (attribution patching) are used to scalably score the causal importance of thousands of SAE features.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: SAE features provide disentangled, monosemantic directions in LM latent spaces, replacing polysemantic neurons with interpretable units.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The method is applied to transformer language models (Pythia-70M, Gemma-2-2B), discovering circuits across attention layers, MLPs, and residual streams.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: SAE-based feature extraction identifies fine-grained, human-interpretable units from LM latent spaces for use in causal circuit analysis.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses the scalability challenge of circuit discovery over large numbers of fine-grained units using linear approximations and unsupervised pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Attention heads are analyzed as components within sparse feature circuits, replacing prior coarse-grained attention-head-level interpretability methods.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Clustering of LM behaviors (via Michaud et al.'s approach) enables unsupervised discovery of thousands of narrow behaviors for automated circuit analysis.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The SHIFT technique identifies and removes spurious/unintended signals (e.g., gender bias) from classifiers using interpretability-guided feature ablation.

title: A machine-learning pipeline for real-time detection of gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences
abstract: The promise of multi-messenger astronomy relies on the rapid detection of gravitational waves at very low latencies ($\mathcal{O}$(1\,s)) in order to maximize the amount of time available for follow-up observations. In recent years, neural-networks have demonstrated robust non-linear modeling capabilities and millisecond-scale inference at a comparatively small computational footprint, making them an attractive family of algorithms in this context. However, integration of these algorithms into the gravitational-wave astrophysics research ecosystem has proven non-trivial. Here, we present the first fully machine learning-based pipeline for the detection of gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences (CBCs) running in low-latency. We demonstrate this pipeline to have a fraction of the latency of traditional matched filtering search pipelines while achieving state-of-the-art sensitivity to higher-mass stellar binary black holes.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary target: detecting gravitational wave signals from compact binary coalescences in real-time LIGO interferometer data.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A 1D ResNet34-based convolutional neural network maps gravitational wave strain time-series from two interferometers to a scalar detection statistic analogous to matched-filter SNR.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline's core task is detecting gravitational wave signals embedded in non-stationary, transient detector noise at millisecond inference latency.

conceptDetails.name: low-latency gw pipeline
conceptDetails.description: The first fully ML-based CBC detection pipeline running in production low-latency mode, achieving a fraction of the latency of traditional matched filtering searches.

conceptDetails.name: inference-as-a-service
conceptDetails.description: A novel IaaS deployment paradigm decouples neural-network inference into a dedicated TensorRT-accelerated service, enabling real-time sub-second detection latency.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Aggressive data augmentation during training enables competitive sensitivity to binary black hole mergers without relying on matched filtering templates.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: An inference-as-a-service computing model using Triton Inference Server allows independent scaling of pipeline stages across heterogeneous computing resources.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The network performs binary signal-versus-noise classification trained with binary cross-entropy loss and an Adam optimizer with cosine annealing.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Astrophysically meaningful sensitivity metrics and benchmarking against traditional matched-filter pipelines validate deployment readiness on real O4 detector data.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The Aframe framework provides end-to-end tooling for implementing, testing, and deploying ML models into the gravitational-wave astrophysics research ecosystem.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Distinguishing genuine gravitational wave events from non-Gaussian transient noise glitches in detector data is a central challenge the pipeline must address.

title: The Unreasonable Ineffectiveness of the Deeper Layers
abstract: How is knowledge stored in an LLM's weights? We study this via layer pruning: if removing a certain layer does not affect model performance in common question-answering benchmarks, then the weights in that layer are not necessary for storing the knowledge needed to answer those questions. To find these unnecessary parameters, we identify the optimal block of layers to prune by considering similarity across layers; then, to "heal" the damage, we perform a small amount of finetuning. Surprisingly, with this method we find minimal degradation of performance until after a large fraction (up to half) of the layers are removed for some common open-weight models. From a scientific perspective, the robustness of these LLMs to the deletion of layers implies either that current pretraining methods are not properly leveraging the parameters in the deeper layers of the network or that the shallow layers play a critical role in storing knowledge. For our study, we use parameter-efficient finetuning (PEFT) methods, specifically quantization and Low Rank Adapters (QLoRA), such that each of our experiments can be performed on a single 40GB A100 GPU.

conceptDetails.name: layer pruning
conceptDetails.description: The core novel method: identifying and removing contiguous blocks of transformer layers based on inter-layer representation similarity, then healing with QLoRA.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: QLoRA fine-tuning is used to 'heal' performance degradation after layer removal, enabling recovery with minimal compute.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies layer pruning specifically in transformer-based LLMs, exploiting the residual stream structure of transformer architectures.

conceptDetails.name: knowledge localization
conceptDetails.description: A key finding that LLM knowledge is disproportionately stored in shallow layers, with deeper layers contributing redundantly.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Layer similarity is measured via angular distance between hidden representations, revealing that deeper layers produce qualitatively more similar representations.

conceptDetails.name: parameter efficiency
conceptDetails.description: QLoRA (quantization + Low Rank Adapters) enables healing of pruned models within single-GPU memory budgets.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The work probes how knowledge is stored in LLM weights by identifying which layers are necessary for downstream task performance.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Layer pruning produces a structurally sparse (shallower) model by removing entire blocks, reducing parameter count without weight-level sparsity.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The method is designed to run on a single 40GB A100 GPU using quantization and LoRA, making large-model compression accessible at scale.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The healing phase re-adapts pruned models to downstream tasks via parameter-efficient fine-tuning, a form of transfer learning.

title: Debiasing with Diffusion: Probabilistic reconstruction of Dark Matter fields from galaxies with CAMELS
abstract: Galaxies are biased tracers of the underlying cosmic web, which is dominated by dark matter components that cannot be directly observed. Galaxy formation simulations can be used to study the relationship between dark matter density fields and galaxy distributions. However, this relationship can be sensitive to assumptions in cosmology and astrophysical processes embedded in the galaxy formation models, that remain uncertain in many aspects. In this work, we develop a diffusion generative model to reconstruct dark matter fields from galaxies. The diffusion model is trained on the CAMELS simulation suite that contains thousands of state-of-the-art galaxy formation simulations with varying cosmological parameters and sub-grid astrophysics. We demonstrate that the diffusion model can predict the unbiased posterior distribution of the underlying dark matter fields from the given stellar mass fields, while being able to marginalize over uncertainties in cosmological and astrophysical models. Interestingly, the model generalizes to simulation volumes approximately 500 times larger than those it was trained on, and across different galaxy formation models. Code for reproducing these results can be found at https://github.com/victoriaono/variational-diffusion-cdm

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: A conditional diffusion generative model is the core method used to reconstruct dark matter density fields from stellar mass fields by learning the posterior distribution p(x_DM|x_stars).

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model predicts the full unbiased posterior distribution of dark matter fields conditioned on observed stellar mass fields, enabling probabilistic inference rather than point estimates.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The primary reconstruction target — the paper develops a method to recover unobservable dark matter density fields from biased galaxy tracers.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The CAMELS suite of over 8000 galaxy formation simulations with varying cosmological and astrophysical parameters provides the training data for the diffusion model.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The model explicitly marginalizes over uncertainties in cosmological parameters and sub-grid astrophysics models embedded in galaxy formation simulations.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The diffusion model functions as a conditional generative model capable of sampling multiple plausible dark matter field realizations consistent with observed galaxy distributions.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy bias debiasing
conceptDetails.description: A novel framing of the problem: correcting for the systematic bias that makes galaxies imperfect tracers of the underlying dark matter cosmic web.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing the dark matter density field from biased observable galaxy tracers is formulated as a probabilistic inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained on simulation outputs from CAMELS to learn the mapping between observables and latent dark matter fields, enabling inference on real or larger-scale data.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework is explicitly Bayesian, producing posterior samples of dark matter fields that encode epistemic uncertainty from galaxy formation model assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A U-Net convolutional architecture serves as the backbone of the diffusion model's noise prediction network for processing 2D density map inputs.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The trained model is evaluated on IllustrisTNG-300 volumes approximately 500 times larger than training volumes and across different galaxy formation models, demonstrating strong out-of-distribution generalization.

title: FeatUp: A Model-Agnostic Framework for Features at Any Resolution
abstract: Deep features are a cornerstone of computer vision research, capturing image semantics and enabling the community to solve downstream tasks even in the zero- or few-shot regime. However, these features often lack the spatial resolution to directly perform dense prediction tasks like segmentation and depth prediction because models aggressively pool information over large areas. In this work, we introduce FeatUp, a task- and model-agnostic framework to restore lost spatial information in deep features. We introduce two variants of FeatUp: one that guides features with high-resolution signal in a single forward pass, and one that fits an implicit model to a single image to reconstruct features at any resolution. Both approaches use a multi-view consistency loss with deep analogies to NeRFs. Our features retain their original semantics and can be swapped into existing applications to yield resolution and performance gains even without re-training. We show that FeatUp significantly outperforms other feature upsampling and image super-resolution approaches in class activation map generation, transfer learning for segmentation and depth prediction, and end-to-end training for semantic segmentation.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: FeatUp directly addresses the spatial resolution limitations of deep feature extractors from any vision backbone, restoring lost spatial information while preserving semantic content.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: FeatUp performs feature-level super-resolution, upsampling low-resolution deep feature maps to arbitrary resolutions guided by high-resolution image signals.

conceptDetails.name: multi-view consistency
conceptDetails.description: The core training principle: slight spatial transformations (jitter, flip, crop) of an input image produce multiple low-resolution feature views whose consistency supervises high-resolution feature learning.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper builds on learned deep representations and proposes methods to enhance their spatial fidelity without altering their semantic meaning.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The multi-view consistency loss is the central training signal, enforcing that upsampled features downsampled back to low resolution match the original model outputs across jittered views.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: FeatUp trains using a multi-view consistency loss derived from jittered image views, requiring no labeled data or task-specific supervision.

conceptDetails.name: implicit feature network
conceptDetails.description: One FeatUp variant overfits a deep implicit neural network to a single image, enabling arbitrary-resolution feature reconstruction analogous to NeRF implicit representations.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Upsampled FeatUp features are evaluated as drop-in replacements for standard features in transfer learning benchmarks for segmentation and depth prediction.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing high-resolution feature maps from multiple low-resolution views is framed as an inverse problem, analogous to NeRF-style 3D reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Vision Transformers (ViTs) are a primary backbone supported by FeatUp, and the paper addresses their inherent spatial resolution reduction from patch-based processing.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: FeatUp's attention downsampler uses predicted salience maps combined with spatially invariant kernels to better model nonlinear and dynamic receptive fields.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNNs such as ResNet-50 are key backbone models supported by FeatUp, and the feedforward upsampler is itself a convolutional architecture.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: FeatUp improves class activation map (CAM) generation, enabling higher-resolution model explanations without complex relevance propagation methods.

title: Moments of Clarity: Streamlining Latent Spaces in Machine Learning using Moment Pooling
abstract: Many machine learning applications involve learning a latent representation of data, which is often high-dimensional and difficult to directly interpret. In this work, we propose "Moment Pooling", a natural extension of Deep Sets networks which drastically decrease latent space dimensionality of these networks while maintaining or even improving performance. Moment Pooling generalizes the summation in Deep Sets to arbitrary multivariate moments, which enables the model to achieve a much higher effective latent dimensionality for a fixed latent dimension. We demonstrate Moment Pooling on the collider physics task of quark/gluon jet classification by extending Energy Flow Networks (EFNs) to Moment EFNs. We find that Moment EFNs with latent dimensions as small as 1 perform similarly to ordinary EFNs with higher latent dimension. This small latent dimension allows for the internal representation to be directly visualized and interpreted, which in turn enables the learned internal jet representation to be extracted in closed form.

conceptDetails.name: moment pooling
conceptDetails.description: The core novel contribution of this paper: a generalization of the Deep Sets summation to arbitrary multivariate moments, enabling high effective latent dimensionality with low actual latent dimension.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates that Moment Pooling drastically reduces latent space dimensionality (to as small as L=1) while maintaining or improving classification performance.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on learning compact, interpretable latent representations of particle collision events via the Φ network in Moment EFNs.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A key outcome of the work is that the small latent dimension enables direct visualization and closed-form analytic extraction of the learned internal jet representation.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Quark/gluon jet classification and jet angularity observables are the primary physics application domain used to validate and interpret Moment EFNs.

conceptDetails.name: energy flow networks
conceptDetails.description: The existing EFN architecture, a weighted Deep Sets model for IRC-safe jet observables, which this paper extends to Moment EFNs via Moment Pooling.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper situates its ML contribution within the broader context of collider physics and jet substructure, benchmarking on high-energy particle collision data.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Quark/gluon discrimination is the benchmark classification task used to measure performance of Moment EFNs across varying latent dimensions and moment orders.

conceptDetails.name: deep sets
conceptDetails.description: The foundational permutation-invariant architecture whose summation aggregation step is generalized by Moment Pooling to higher-order multivariate moments.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The per-particle Φ network acts as a learned feature extractor mapping particle kinematics to latent space, whose moments are then aggregated for event-level inference.

conceptDetails.name: effective latent dimension
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept introduced in the paper quantifying how many independent directions a Moment EFN's latent space effectively spans, explaining why low L suffices.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Energy Flow Networks enforce infrared and collinear (IRC) safety as a symmetry constraint, which is inherited and preserved by the Moment EFN extension.

title: PAPERCLIP: Associating Astronomical Observations and Natural Language with Multi-Modal Models
abstract: We present PAPERCLIP (Proposal Abstracts Provide an Effective Representation for Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training), a method which associates astronomical observations imaged by telescopes with natural language using a neural network model. The model is fine-tuned from a pre-trained Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) model using successful observing proposal abstracts and corresponding downstream observations, with the abstracts optionally summarized via guided generation using large language models (LLMs). Using observations from the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) as an example, we show that the fine-tuned model embodies a meaningful joint representation between observations and natural language through tests targeting image retrieval (i.e., finding the most relevant observations using natural language queries) and description retrieval (i.e., querying for astrophysical object classes and use cases most relevant to a given observation). Our study demonstrates the potential for using generalist foundation models rather than task-specific models for interacting with astronomical data by leveraging text as an interface.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The core training paradigm used to align astronomical image and text embeddings via CLIP-style contrastive objectives.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Pre-trained CLIP model is fine-tuned on Hubble observation–proposal abstract pairs to adapt it to the astronomical domain.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The method learns a joint embedding space between astronomical images and natural language descriptions.

conceptDetails.name: multi-modal foundation model
conceptDetails.description: PAPERCLIP leverages and extends a generalist multi-modal foundation model to interact with astronomical survey data via natural language.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Joint image-text embedding space is constructed and evaluated for retrieval quality across astronomical observations.

conceptDetails.name: astronomical image retrieval
conceptDetails.description: A key downstream task where natural language queries are used to find the most relevant Hubble telescope observations.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: A generalist foundation model (CLIP) trained on broad web data is transferred to the specialized astrophysics domain.

conceptDetails.name: guided llm generation
conceptDetails.description: Large language models with constrained (structured) generation are used to summarize raw proposal abstracts into compact captions for training.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Separate image and text encoders extract features that are aligned in a shared latent space during fine-tuning.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Text retrieval tasks include querying for astrophysical object classes such as spiral galaxies, used to evaluate model quality.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The broader learning paradigm underlying CLIP, enabling powerful representations without manual labels by using paired data.

title: Superphot+: Realtime Fitting and Classification of Supernova Light Curves
abstract: Photometric classifications of supernova (SN) light curves have become necessary to utilize the full potential of large samples of observations obtained from wide-field photometric surveys, such as the Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) and the Vera C. Rubin Observatory. Here, we present a photometric classifier for SN light curves that does not rely on redshift information and still maintains comparable accuracy to redshift-dependent classifiers. Our new package, Superphot+, uses a parametric model to extract meaningful features from multiband SN light curves. We train a gradient-boosted machine with fit parameters from 6,061 ZTF SNe that pass data quality cuts and are spectroscopically classified as one of five classes: SN Ia, SN II, SN Ib/c, SN IIn, and SLSN-I. Without redshift information, our classifier yields a class-averaged F1-score of 0.61 +/- 0.02 and a total accuracy of 0.83 +/- 0.01. Including redshift information improves these metrics to 0.71 +/- 0.02 and 0.88 +/- 0.01, respectively. We assign new class probabilities to 3,558 ZTF transients that show SN-like characteristics (based on the ALeRCE Broker light curve and stamp classifiers), but lack spectroscopic classifications. Finally, we compare our predicted SN labels with those generated by the ALeRCE light curve classifier, finding that the two classifiers agree on photometric labels for 82 +/- 2% of light curves with spectroscopic labels and 72% of light curves without spectroscopic labels. Superphot+ is currently classifying ZTF SNe in real time via the ANTARES Broker, and is designed for simple adaptation to six-band Rubin light curves in the future.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is a photometric classifier distinguishing five SN types (Ia, II, Ib/c, IIn, SLSN-I) from ZTF light curves without requiring spectroscopic data.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Gradient-boosted machine (GBM) classification is the primary ML method used to categorize supernovae from extracted light curve features.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: A parametric model fits multiband SN light curves to extract meaningful features (best-fit parameters) used as inputs to the classifier.

conceptDetails.name: real-time transient classification
conceptDetails.description: Superphot+ is deployed in real time via the ANTARES Broker to classify ZTF supernovae as new photometric observations arrive.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Gradient boosting is an ensemble learning method that combines multiple weak learners; it is the classifier architecture chosen for Superphot+.

conceptDetails.name: photometric redshift-independence
conceptDetails.description: A key innovation is achieving classification accuracy comparable to redshift-dependent classifiers without using any redshift information.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Nested sampling (a Bayesian posterior estimation technique) is used to fit the parametric light curve model and obtain parameter posteriors.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The fitting stage uses nested sampling to estimate posterior distributions over parametric model parameters for each supernova light curve.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The paper evaluates classifier probability calibration by examining accuracy as a function of classification confidence score.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The classifier assigns photometric labels to 3,558 ZTF transients lacking spectroscopic classification, extending supervised predictions to unlabeled data.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: The training dataset is oversampled to address class imbalance among the five SN types, improving classifier performance on minority classes.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline processes real-time alert streams from ANTARES Broker to identify and classify SN-like transients as they are detected.

title: Re-Simulation-based Self-Supervised Learning for Pre-Training Foundation Models
abstract: Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) is at the core of training modern large machine learning models, providing a scheme for learning powerful representations that can be used in a variety of downstream tasks. However, SSL strategies must be adapted to the type of training data and downstream tasks required. We propose RS3L ("Re-simulation-based self-supervised representation learning"), a novel simulation-based SSL strategy that employs a method of re-simulation to drive data augmentation for contrastive learning in the physical sciences, particularly, in fields that rely on stochastic simulators. By intervening in the middle of the simulation process and re-running simulation components downstream of the intervention, we generate multiple realizations of an event, thus producing a set of augmentations covering all physics-driven variations available in the simulator. Using experiments from high-energy physics, we explore how this strategy may enable the development of a foundation model; we show how RS3L pre-training enables powerful performance in downstream tasks such as discrimination of a variety of objects and uncertainty mitigation. In addition to our results, we make the RS3L dataset publicly available for further studies on how to improve SSL strategies.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: RS3L is fundamentally a self-supervised learning strategy that uses re-simulation to generate pseudo-labeled augmentations for pre-training foundation models without human annotation.

conceptDetails.name: re-simulation augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Novel core contribution: intervening mid-simulation to fix upstream latent state and re-run downstream stochastic components, producing domain-complete physics-driven augmentations.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses SimCLR-based contrastive learning as the core training objective, mapping re-simulated augmentations of the same event to similar representations while pushing different events apart.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Re-simulation serves as a physics-driven, domain-complete data augmentation strategy, replacing hand-crafted augmentations with stochastic simulator outputs.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The goal of RS3L pre-training is to learn powerful latent representations of jets that capture physically salient features and transfer to diverse downstream tasks.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jets are the primary physics objects studied; RS3L learns representations for jet tagging tasks including Higgs, top quark, and QCD jet discrimination at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: physics foundation model
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: a model pre-trained on generic physics tasks via SSL that can be fine-tuned across diverse HEP classification and analysis tasks.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: RS3L pre-trained models are fine-tuned for downstream classification and uncertainty mitigation tasks, demonstrating the transfer learning paradigm central to foundation models.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Re-simulation intervenes in the HEP simulation chain—covering parton showering, hadronization, and detector interaction—to generate physically motivated augmentations.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: RS3L mitigates systematic uncertainties from domain shift between simulation and real data by re-simulating with varied simulator settings during augmentation.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The method leverages stochastic simulators as the generative mechanism for augmentation, closely related to simulation-based inference paradigms in physics.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs transformer-based architectures as the backbone for encoding jet constituents in the SSL pre-training framework.

title: On classical de Sitter solutions and parametric control
abstract: Finding string backgrounds with de Sitter spacetime, where all approximations and corrections are controlled, is an open problem. We revisit the search for de Sitter solutions in the classical regime for specific type IIB supergravity compactifications on group manifolds, an under-explored corner of the landscape that offers an interesting testing ground for swampland conjectures. While the supergravity de Sitter solutions we obtain numerically are ambiguous in terms of their classicality, we find an analytic scaling that makes four out of six compactification radii, as well as the overall volume, arbitrarily large. This potentially provides parametric control over corrections. If we could show that these solutions, or others to be found, are fully classical, they would constitute a counterexample to conjectures stating that asymptotic de Sitter solutions do not exist. We discuss this point in great detail.

conceptDetails.name: de sitter solutions
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study — finding parametrically controlled de Sitter spacetime backgrounds in type IIB supergravity compactifications on group manifolds.

conceptDetails.name: parametric control
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept describing the existence of a scaling parameter that makes string coupling and compactification radii arbitrarily favorable for classicality.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates de Sitter solutions as classical string backgrounds, requiring string theory inputs like flux quantization and source quantization to validate classicality.

conceptDetails.name: flux compactification
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to this paper — the compactification of extra dimensions with quantized fluxes in type IIB supergravity, required for de Sitter solutions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Type IIB supergravity compactifications serve as effective field theories derived from string theory, used to study de Sitter solutions in the classical regime.

conceptDetails.name: swampland conjectures
conceptDetails.description: The paper tests and contextualizes swampland conjectures — specifically the de Sitter conjecture — using concrete supergravity solutions on group manifolds.

conceptDetails.name: landscape exploration
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept referring to the systematic search through the string landscape of compactification solutions to identify de Sitter candidates.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Group manifolds (6d Lie group quotients) are the compactification spaces used, with their algebraic structure directly constraining the solution landscape.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Flux quantization and lattice conditions impose discrete symmetry constraints that determine the classicality of supergravity solutions.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: Systematic and automated numerical searches are used to explore the landscape of de Sitter solutions across group manifold compactifications.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Numerical searches for supergravity solutions serve as computational surrogates for analytic solution-finding in a high-dimensional parameter space.

title: Photonic probabilistic machine learning using quantum vacuum noise
abstract: Probabilistic machine learning utilizes controllable sources of randomness to encode uncertainty and enable statistical modeling. Harnessing the pure randomness of quantum vacuum noise, which stems from fluctuating electromagnetic fields, has shown promise for high speed and energy-efficient stochastic photonic elements. Nevertheless, photonic computing hardware which can control these stochastic elements to program probabilistic machine learning algorithms has been limited. Here, we implement a photonic probabilistic computer consisting of a controllable stochastic photonic element - a photonic probabilistic neuron (PPN). Our PPN is implemented in a bistable optical parametric oscillator (OPO) with vacuum-level injected bias fields. We then program a measurement-and-feedback loop for time-multiplexed PPNs with electronic processors (FPGA or GPU) to solve certain probabilistic machine learning tasks. We showcase probabilistic inference and image generation of MNIST-handwritten digits, which are representative examples of discriminative and generative models. In both implementations, quantum vacuum noise is used as a random seed to encode classification uncertainty or probabilistic generation of samples. In addition, we propose a path towards an all-optical probabilistic computing platform, with an estimated sampling rate of ~ 1 Gbps and energy consumption of ~ 5 fJ/MAC. Our work paves the way for scalable, ultrafast, and energy-efficient probabilistic machine learning hardware.

conceptDetails.name: photonic probabilistic neuron
conceptDetails.description: A novel stochastic photonic element implemented as a biased degenerate optical parametric oscillator that generates controllable probability distributions from quantum vacuum noise.

conceptDetails.name: optical parametric oscillator
conceptDetails.description: A bistable degenerate OPO serves as the core hardware element whose phase bistability and vacuum noise sensitivity enable programmable stochastic binary outputs.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The photonic probabilistic neuron exploits inherently stochastic quantum vacuum fluctuations to implement controllable stochastic sampling for machine learning algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper implements a pixelCNN generative model on photonic hardware to generate MNIST handwritten digit images using quantum vacuum noise as a random seed.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates probabilistic inference with a stochastic binary neural network that uses quantum vacuum noise to encode classification uncertainty in a Bayesian manner.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Quantum vacuum noise is used as a controllable random source to quantify and encode classification uncertainty in discriminative neural network models.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Quantum vacuum noise arising from fluctuating electromagnetic fields provides the fundamental physical randomness exploited by the optical parametric oscillator.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic binary neural network
conceptDetails.description: A neural network architecture with stochastic binary weights or activations implemented on photonic hardware to perform probabilistic discriminative inference.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Statistical sampling from learned probability distributions is central to both the discriminative and generative model implementations on the photonic platform.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A pixel convolutional neural network (pixelCNN) is used as the generative model architecture for MNIST image generation in the photonic probabilistic computing system.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Generative models on the photonic platform learn and sample from the underlying probability density of the MNIST training dataset.

title: Operator Learning Renormalization Group
abstract: In this paper, we present a general framework for quantum many-body simulations called the operator learning renormalization group (OLRG). Inspired by machine learning perspectives, OLRG is a generalization of Wilson's numerical renormalization group and White's density matrix renormalization group, which recursively builds a simulatable system to approximate a target system of the same number of sites via operator maps. OLRG uses a loss function to minimize the error of a target property directly by learning the operator map in lieu of a state ansatz. This loss function is designed by a scaling consistency condition that also provides a provable bound for real-time evolution. We implement two versions of the operator maps for classical and quantum simulations. The former, which we call the Operator Matrix Map, can be implemented via neural networks on classical computers. The latter, which we call the Hamiltonian Expression Map, generates device pulse sequences to leverage the capabilities of quantum computing hardware. We illustrate the performance of both maps for calculating time-dependent quantities in the quantum Ising model Hamiltonian.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: OLRG directly generalizes Wilson's NRG and White's DMRG by replacing linear operator maps with arbitrary learned operator maps, extending the renormalization group paradigm.

conceptDetails.name: operator map learning
conceptDetails.description: The core algorithmic idea of OLRG: learning arbitrary operator maps between system sizes instead of parameterizing quantum state ansatze.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain: OLRG is designed to simulate quantum many-body systems, demonstrated on the transverse-field Ising model for real-time dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: scaling consistency condition
conceptDetails.description: A novel mathematical principle introduced in this paper that constrains the loss function design and provides provable error bounds for real-time evolution across system sizes.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A central innovation: the scaling consistency condition provides a principled, end-to-end loss that directly minimizes error on the target property rather than an intermediate surrogate.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Matrix product states and DMRG—core tensor network methods—are the direct predecessors that OLRG generalizes by replacing linear operator maps with expressive neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The Hamiltonian Expression Map variant compiles OLRG operator maps into device pulse sequences for near-term digital-analog quantum hardware.

conceptDetails.name: neural operators
conceptDetails.description: The Operator Matrix Map implements the learned operator map as a neural network acting on operator spaces rather than function spaces, making it a neural operator analogue.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian expression map
conceptDetails.description: A novel quantum-hardware variant of OLRG that maps a target problem Hamiltonian to a device Hamiltonian expressed as pulse sequences for near-term quantum computers.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The Operator Matrix Map uses neural networks constrained by the physics-derived scaling consistency condition, embedding physical structure into the learning objective.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The transverse-field Ising model—a paradigmatic system exhibiting quantum phase transitions—is used as the benchmark for evaluating OLRG performance.

title: Quantum Many-Body Physics Calculations with Large Language Models
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated an unprecedented ability to perform complex tasks in multiple domains, including mathematical and scientific reasoning. We demonstrate that with carefully designed prompts, LLMs can accurately carry out key calculations in research papers in theoretical physics. We focus on a broadly used approximation method in quantum physics: the Hartree-Fock method, requiring an analytic multi-step calculation deriving approximate Hamiltonian and corresponding self-consistency equations. To carry out the calculations using LLMs, we design multi-step prompt templates that break down the analytic calculation into standardized steps with placeholders for problem-specific information. We evaluate GPT-4's performance in executing the calculation for 15 research papers from the past decade, demonstrating that, with correction of intermediate steps, it can correctly derive the final Hartree-Fock Hamiltonian in 13 cases and makes minor errors in 2 cases. Aggregating across all research papers, we find an average score of 87.5 (out of 100) on the execution of individual calculation steps. Overall, the requisite skill for doing these calculations is at the graduate level in quantum condensed matter theory. We further use LLMs to mitigate the two primary bottlenecks in this evaluation process: (i) extracting information from papers to fill in templates and (ii) automatic scoring of the calculation steps, demonstrating good results in both cases. The strong performance is the first step for developing algorithms that automatically explore theoretical hypotheses at an unprecedented scale.

conceptDetails.name: hartree-fock method
conceptDetails.description: The core physics calculation automated in this work: a mean-field approximation deriving the HF Hamiltonian and self-consistency equations for quantum many-body systems.

conceptDetails.name: prompt engineering
conceptDetails.description: Multi-step prompt templates with problem-specific placeholders are the key methodological contribution enabling GPT-4 to execute graduate-level physics calculations.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on deriving the Hartree-Fock Hamiltonian HHF, making Hamiltonian formulation the primary mathematical object throughout.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: GPT-4, a large transformer-based language model, is the AI system evaluated for its ability to carry out multi-step theoretical physics derivations.

conceptDetails.name: llm scientific reasoning
conceptDetails.description: Novel evaluation framework assessing GPT-4 on research-level, multi-step analytic derivations in quantum condensed matter theory — the first such benchmark.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The HF template encodes a structured, reproducible multi-step scientific workflow that the LLM executes end-to-end.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames its goal as building algorithms that automatically explore theoretical physics hypotheses at unprecedented scale.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Symmetry-breaking channels and order parameter structures are central to the HF derivation steps the LLM must correctly identify and handle.

conceptDetails.name: test-time scaling
conceptDetails.description: Interactive correction of intermediate steps at inference time is used to improve final accuracy, an instance of test-time compute strategies.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Automatic scoring of intermediate calculation steps probes whether LLM outputs are mathematically correct and physically meaningful.

title: New Pathways in Neutrino Physics via Quantum-Encoded Data Analysis
abstract: Ever-increasing amount of data is produced by particle detectors in their quest to unveil the laws of Nature. The large data rate requires the use of specialized triggers that promptly reduce the data rate to a manageable level; however, in doing so, unexpected new phenomena may escape detection. Additionally, the large data rate is increasingly difficult to analyze effectively, which has led to a recent revolution on machine learning techniques. Here, we present a methodology based on recent quantum compression techniques that has the capacity to store exponentially more amount of information than classically available methods. To demonstrate this, we encode the full neutrino telescope event information using parity observables in an IBM quantum processor using 8 qubits. Then we show that we can recover the information stored on the quantum computer with a fidelity of 84%. Finally, we illustrate the use of our protocol by performing a classification task that separates electron-neutrino events to muon-neutrinos events in a neutrino telescope. This new capability would eventually allow us to solve the street light effect in particle physics, where we only record signatures of particles with which we are familiar.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses an IBM quantum processor with 8 qubits to encode neutrino telescope event data via parity observables, demonstrating exponential classical-to-quantum data compression.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Parity operators acting on multi-qubit quantum states are the core mechanism for encoding classical bitstrings into the quantum system.

conceptDetails.name: quantum data encoding
conceptDetails.description: A novel protocol mapping classical bitstrings to parity observable expectation values on ensembles of n-qubit states, enabling exponential compression of particle physics event data.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: IceCube neutrino telescope event data—including electron-neutrino and muon-neutrino interactions—serves as the physical use case for the quantum encoding and classification protocol.

conceptDetails.name: parity observables
conceptDetails.description: Products of single-qubit spin measurements along x, y, or z axes used as the basis for encoding and decoding classical bits in the quantum system.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper motivates quantum compression as a solution to the streetlight effect caused by classical trigger systems that filter out unfamiliar particle signatures.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A downstream classification task distinguishing electron-neutrino from muon-neutrino events is used to demonstrate the practical utility of the quantum-encoded data representation.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Quantum compression achieves exponential reduction in the classical storage required to represent high-dimensional particle detector event data.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Quantum entanglement and superposition are the resources that allow the encoding protocol to store exponentially more information than classical methods.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The broader goal of the methodology is to enable detection of novel, previously unrecorded particle signatures that classical triggers would discard.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Parity observables act as the feature representation of classical detector data within the quantum system.

title: Renormalization Group flow, Optimal Transport and Diffusion-based Generative Model
abstract: Diffusion-based generative models represent a forefront direction in generative AI research today. Recent studies in physics have suggested that the renormalization group (RG) can be conceptualized as a diffusion process. This insight motivates us to develop a novel diffusion-based generative model by reversing the momentum-space RG flow. We establish a framework that interprets RG flow as optimal transport gradient flow, which minimizes a functional analogous to the Kullback-Leibler divergence, thereby bridging statistical physics and information theory. Our model applies forward and reverse diffusion processes in Fourier space, exploiting the sparse representation of natural images in this domain to efficiently separate signal from noise and manage image features across scales. By introducing a scale-dependent noise schedule informed by a dispersion relation, the model optimizes denoising performance and image generation in Fourier space, taking advantage of the distinct separation of macro and microscale features. Experimental validations on standard datasets demonstrate the model's capability to generate high-quality images while significantly reducing training time compared to existing image-domain diffusion models. This approach not only enhances our understanding of the generative processes in images but also opens new pathways for research in generative AI, leveraging the convergence of theoretical physics, optimal transport, and machine learning principles.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is a novel diffusion-based generative model operating in Fourier space, inspired by reversing renormalization group flow.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: RG flow is reinterpreted as an optimal transport gradient flow minimizing a KL-divergence-like functional, forming the mathematical backbone of the framework.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Renormalization group flow serves as the physical analogy driving the forward diffusion process, with image generation achieved by reversing RG.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a new class of generative model that bridges statistical physics and machine learning for high-quality image synthesis.

conceptDetails.name: fourier-space diffusion
conceptDetails.description: Novel method performing both forward and reverse diffusion entirely in Fourier space to exploit sparse signal representations and reduce training time.

conceptDetails.name: scale-dependent noise schedule
conceptDetails.description: A dispersion-relation-informed noise schedule that differentially treats image features at different spatial scales during diffusion.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Concepts from quantum field theory, particularly RG flow and holographic duality, provide the theoretical physics foundation for the model architecture.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The model exploits Fourier-space (spectral) representations of images to separate macro and microscale features efficiently.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The reverse diffusion process involves score-based denoising in Fourier space to reconstruct clean images from noise.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The forward and reverse diffusion dynamics are formulated as stochastic processes governed by scale-dependent noise schedules.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained by optimizing the likelihood of data given the diffusion process, enabling tractable density estimation.

title: Seeing Double: Calibrating Two Jets at Once
abstract: Jet energy calibration is an important aspect of many measurements and searches at the LHC. Currently, these calibrations are performed on a per-jet basis, i.e. agnostic to the properties of other jets in the same event. In this work, we propose taking advantage of the correlations induced by momentum conservation between jets in order to improve their jet energy calibration. By fitting the $p_T$ asymmetry of dijet events in simulation, while remaining agnostic to the $p_T$ spectra themselves, we are able to obtain correlation-improved maximum likelihood estimates. This approach is demonstrated with simulated jets from the CMS Detector, yielding a $3$-$5\%$ relative improvement in the jet energy resolution, corresponding to a quadrature improvement of approximately 35\%.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on improving jet energy calibration for dijet events at the LHC, which is the central application.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Jet energy calibration is the primary goal, with the paper proposing correlation-improved calibration methods.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Correlation-improved maximum likelihood estimates are the core statistical methodology introduced in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The method is demonstrated using CMS Detector simulations from the LHC, a flagship collider experiment.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: Momentum conservation between jets in the center-of-mass frame is the physical constraint exploited to improve calibration.

conceptDetails.name: dijet momentum asymmetry
conceptDetails.description: Novel use of the pT asymmetry distribution of dijet pairs as a prior-independent constraint for joint calibration.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Simulated jets from the CMS Detector are used to validate the correlation-improved calibration approach.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper quantifies improvements in jet energy resolution and provides calibration uncertainty estimates.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayes' rule is used to incorporate prior knowledge of jet correlations into the likelihood framework.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The method contributes to event-level reconstruction by jointly calibrating both jets in a dijet event.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Fitting the pT asymmetry of dijet events is a regression task used to extract the correlation parameter Λ.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations of QCD dijet events are used to demonstrate and validate the proposed method.

title: Rigor with Machine Learning from Field Theory to the Poincaré Conjecture
abstract: Machine learning techniques are increasingly powerful, leading to many breakthroughs in the natural sciences, but they are often stochastic, error-prone, and blackbox. How, then, should they be utilized in fields such as theoretical physics and pure mathematics that place a premium on rigor and understanding? In this Perspective we discuss techniques for obtaining rigor in the natural sciences with machine learning. Non-rigorous methods may lead to rigorous results via conjecture generation or verification by reinforcement learning. We survey applications of these techniques-for-rigor ranging from string theory to the smooth $4$d Poincaré conjecture in low-dimensional topology. One can also imagine building direct bridges between machine learning theory and either mathematics or theoretical physics. As examples, we describe a new approach to field theory motivated by neural network theory, and a theory of Riemannian metric flows induced by neural network gradient descent, which encompasses Perelman's formulation of the Ricci flow that was utilized to resolve the $3$d Poincaré conjecture.

conceptDetails.name: neural network field theory
conceptDetails.description: A novel correspondence introduced in the paper between neural network statistics and quantum field theory, offering a new non-perturbative formulation.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: A central subject of the paper's NN-FT correspondence, proposing neural networks as a non-perturbative definition of QFT in the continuum.

conceptDetails.name: conjecture generation
conceptDetails.description: A technique where ML models assist domain experts in formulating mathematical conjectures that can subsequently be rigorously proven.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Used to obtain rigorous mathematical results by defining games whose solutions correspond to verified mathematical facts, such as establishing ribbonness of knots.

conceptDetails.name: ricci flow
conceptDetails.description: Perelman's Ricci flow, which resolved the 3d Poincaré conjecture, is shown to be a special case of metric flows induced by neural network gradient descent.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: A primary application domain for ML-driven conjecture generation and rigorous results, including algebraic geometry and Calabi-Yau metrics.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper surveys ML-driven conjecture generation as a form of automated scientific and mathematical discovery.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Attribution and interpretable AI techniques are essential for translating ML model predictions into rigorous mathematical conjectures.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Neural tangent kernel theory is used to develop a theory of metric flows generalizing known mathematical flows including Ricci flow.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Connected to renormalization group flows discussed in the context of neural network gradient descent and metric flows.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: Linked to renormalization group flows and Bayesian inference in the paper's framework connecting ML theory to theoretical physics.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Connected to renormalization group flows and optimal transport in the paper's unified framework bridging ML theory and physics.

title: Full-shape analysis with simulation-based priors: constraints on single field inflation from BOSS
abstract: Perturbative, or effective field theory (EFT)-based, full-shape analyses of galaxy clustering data involve ``nuisance parameters'' to capture various observational effects such as the galaxy-dark matter connection (galaxy bias). We present an efficient approach to set informative physically motivated priors on these parameters. We extract these priors from simulated galaxy catalogs based on halo occupation distribution (HOD) models. First, we build a joint distribution between EFT galaxy bias and HOD parameters from a set of 10,500 HOD mock catalogs. We use the field level EFT technique that allows for cosmic variance cancellation, enabling a precision calibration of EFT parameters from computationally inexpensive small-volume simulations. Second, we use neural density estimators -- normalizing flows -- to model the marginal probability density of the EFT parameters, which can be used as a prior distribution in full shape analyses. As a first application, we use our HOD-based priors in a new analysis of galaxy power spectra and bispectra from the BOSS survey in the context of single field primordial non-Gaussianity. We find that our priors lead to a reduction of the posterior volume of bias parameters by an order of magnitude. We also find $f_{\rm NL}^{\rm equil} = 320\pm 300$ and $f_{\rm NL}^{\rm ortho} = 100\pm 130$ (at 68\% CL) in a combined two-template analysis, representing a $\approx 40\%$ improvement in constraints on single field primordial non-Gaussianity, equivalent to doubling the survey volume.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The EFT of large-scale structure provides the perturbative framework for modeling galaxy clustering, with bias parameters that are the primary targets for prior calibration.

conceptDetails.name: primordial non-gaussianity
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paper derives new constraints on equilateral and orthogonal single-field PNG templates (f_NL) using HOD-based priors on BOSS data.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Used as neural density estimators to model the marginal probability density of EFT bias parameters, enabling their use as informative priors in full-shape analyses.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Priors on EFT nuisance parameters are extracted from large sets of HOD simulated galaxy catalogs rather than analytic approximations.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy bias calibration
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: precise determination of EFT galaxy bias parameters from HOD mocks using field-level EFT with cosmic variance cancellation.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Core methodology for building the joint and marginal distributions of EFT and HOD parameters from 10,500 mock catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Framework for incorporating HOD-based simulation priors into posterior estimation of cosmological and bias parameters from BOSS galaxy clustering data.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates a ~40% reduction in posterior volume of primordial non-Gaussianity constraints and an order-of-magnitude reduction in bias parameter posterior volume.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: 10,500 HOD mock catalogs are used to build the EFT-HOD parameter distribution and calibrate simulation-based priors.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The galaxy-dark matter connection (galaxy bias) is the central nuisance parameter problem addressed by the HOD-based prior framework.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The normalizing flow serves as a surrogate for the complex high-dimensional EFT parameter distribution learned from simulations.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: The BOSS full-shape analysis targets cosmological parameters including dark energy constraints within the ΛCDM and beyond-ΛCDM context.

title: Real-time Dynamics of the Schwinger Model as an Open Quantum System with Neural Density Operators
abstract: Ab-initio simulations of multiple heavy quarks propagating in a Quark-Gluon Plasma are computationally difficult to perform due to the large dimension of the space of density matrices. This work develops machine learning algorithms to overcome this difficulty by approximating exact quantum states with neural network parametrisations, specifically Neural Density Operators. As a proof of principle demonstration in a QCD-like theory, the approach is applied to solve the Lindblad master equation in the 1+1d lattice Schwinger Model as an open quantum system. Neural Density Operators enable the study of in-medium dynamics on large lattice volumes, where multiple-string interactions and their effects on string-breaking and recombination phenomena can be studied. Thermal properties of the system at equilibrium can also be probed with these methods by variationally constructing the steady state of the Lindblad master equation. Scaling of this approach with system size is studied, and numerical demonstrations on up to 32 spatial lattice sites and with up to 3 interacting strings are performed.

conceptDetails.name: neural density operators
conceptDetails.description: The core ML contribution: neural network parametrisations of quantum density matrices that enable scalable simulation of open quantum system dynamics without exponential memory cost.

conceptDetails.name: lindblad dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The governing first-order master equation describing non-unitary evolution of the Schwinger model as an open quantum system coupled to a thermal bath, solved variationally with NDOs.

conceptDetails.name: open quantum systems
conceptDetails.description: The Schwinger model is treated as a quantum system coupled to a thermal environment, with the environment traced out to yield Lindbladian evolution of the subsystem.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The 1+1d Schwinger model is discretised as a lattice U(1) gauge theory, serving as a QCD-like testbed for the neural network open quantum system methods.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops and benchmarks ab-initio simulation methods for real-time quantum field theory dynamics, including string breaking and recombination in up to 32-site lattices.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Density matrices representing mixed quantum states are the fundamental objects parametrised by neural networks, replacing exact exponentially-large representations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
