conceptDetails.description: Time-dependent Variational Monte Carlo (tVMC) is the primary algorithm used to evolve the NDO parameters and simulate Lindbladian dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The work targets QFT in the open quantum system framework, with the Schwinger model as a proof-of-principle for future QCD Quark-Gluon Plasma simulations.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The staggered-fermion lattice Hamiltonian governs the unitary part of the Schwinger model dynamics, and its structure determines the Lindblad operators and steady-state thermal properties.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Chiral symmetry breaking is a key QCD-like feature shared by the Schwinger model, contextualising why it is the chosen testbed for these methods.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Confinement and deconfinement phase transitions motivate the study of heavy quark dynamics in the Quark-Gluon Plasma, the ultimate application of these methods.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Mentioned as a competing approach to NDOs for parametrising density matrices in open quantum system simulations, motivating the neural network alternative.

title: GenEFT: Understanding Statics and Dynamics of Model Generalization via Effective Theory
abstract: We present GenEFT: an effective theory framework for shedding light on the statics and dynamics of neural network generalization, and illustrate it with graph learning examples. We first investigate the generalization phase transition as data size increases, comparing experimental results with information-theory-based approximations. We find generalization in a Goldilocks zone where the decoder is neither too weak nor too powerful. We then introduce an effective theory for the dynamics of representation learning, where latent-space representations are modeled as interacting particles (repons), and find that it explains our experimentally observed phase transition between generalization and overfitting as encoder and decoder learning rates are scanned. This highlights the power of physics-inspired effective theories for bridging the gap between theoretical predictions and practice in machine learning.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The core framework of the paper — GenEFT adapts the physics concept of effective field theory to approximate and predict neural network generalization behavior.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies and characterizes a generalization/overfitting phase transition as a function of data size and learning rates, analogous to physical phase transitions.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The dynamics section models how latent-space representations evolve during training, framing their interactions as a particle system to predict generalization behavior.

conceptDetails.name: interacting repon theory
conceptDetails.description: A novel particle-physics-inspired model introduced in this paper where latent representations ('repons') are treated as interacting particles whose dynamics predict phase transitions in learning.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The encoder-decoder autoencoder is the primary model architecture used throughout all experiments to study generalization statics and dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Latent-space embedding vectors are the central object of the dynamics theory, modeled as interacting particles ('repons') whose configuration determines generalization.

conceptDetails.name: generalization statics
conceptDetails.description: The information-theoretic sub-framework of GenEFT that derives the minimum training data fraction for full generalization from the entropy of the input distribution.

conceptDetails.name: induction gap
conceptDetails.description: A novel quantity introduced to quantify the extra data bits required to learn task-specific inductive biases (graph structure properties) beyond the minimum information-theoretic threshold.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Knowledge graph learning tasks (e.g., modular arithmetic relations, bipartite graphs) serve as the illustrative benchmark domain for validating GenEFT predictions.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A primary motivation of GenEFT is to provide human-interpretable estimates of critical hyperparameters (learning rate, minimum data size) rather than black-box bounds.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The interacting repon theory is analyzed as a function of encoder and decoder learning rates, directly connecting optimization choices to the generalization/overfitting transition.

title: Opening the AI black box: program synthesis via mechanistic interpretability
abstract: We present MIPS, a novel method for program synthesis based on automated mechanistic interpretability of neural networks trained to perform the desired task, auto-distilling the learned algorithm into Python code. We test MIPS on a benchmark of 62 algorithmic tasks that can be learned by an RNN and find it highly complementary to GPT-4: MIPS solves 32 of them, including 13 that are not solved by GPT-4 (which also solves 30). MIPS uses an integer autoencoder to convert the RNN into a finite state machine, then applies Boolean or integer symbolic regression to capture the learned algorithm. As opposed to large language models, this program synthesis technique makes no use of (and is therefore not limited by) human training data such as algorithms and code from GitHub. We discuss opportunities and challenges for scaling up this approach to make machine-learned models more interpretable and trustworthy.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The entire MIPS pipeline is built around automated mechanistic interpretability — reverse-engineering what algorithm a neural network has learned in order to extract it as explicit code.

conceptDetails.name: program synthesis
conceptDetails.description: The central task of MIPS: automatically generating correct Python code for algorithmic tasks by distilling learned neural network behavior rather than searching over code or prompting LLMs.

conceptDetails.name: mechanistic interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The research paradigm — reverse-engineering neural network computations at a mechanistic level — that MIPS automates and extends to produce executable code.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: An integer autoencoder is the core component of MIPS that converts continuous RNN hidden states into discrete integer or Boolean representations, enabling finite state machine extraction.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: RNNs are the neural architecture trained on algorithmic tasks and subsequently distilled; MIPS exploits their sequential hidden-state structure to extract finite state machines.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Boolean and integer symbolic regression are applied to the discretized hidden states to discover exact algebraic rules that capture the learned algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: finite state machine extraction
conceptDetails.description: A novel pipeline step in MIPS that converts a continuous RNN into a discrete finite state machine by discretizing hidden states, enabling exact lookup-table and symbolic descriptions.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: MIPS discovers structured integer and Boolean representations of RNN hidden states, revealing that learned algorithms are encoded in geometric lattice structures within activation space.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: MIPS fully automates the mechanistic interpretability pipeline — from architecture search through state extraction to code generation — without requiring human analysts.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Hidden-state clustering compresses the exponentially large set of visited activations into a small number of representative states, reducing the effective state space before symbolic regression.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: AutoML-style architecture search minimizes RNN hidden-state dimensionality to find the simplest network that solves each task, facilitating downstream algorithm extraction.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse autoencoders are mentioned as a related prior approach to labeling neural network features, contextualizing MIPS within the broader automated interpretability literature.

title: A Resource Model For Neural Scaling Law
abstract: Neural scaling laws characterize how model performance improves as the model size scales up. Inspired by empirical observations, we introduce a resource model of neural scaling. A task is usually composite hence can be decomposed into many subtasks, which compete for resources (measured by the number of neurons allocated to subtasks). On toy problems, we empirically find that: (1) The loss of a subtask is inversely proportional to its allocated neurons. (2) When multiple subtasks are present in a composite task, the resources acquired by each subtask uniformly grow as models get larger, keeping the ratios of acquired resources constants. We hypothesize these findings to be generally true and build a model to predict neural scaling laws for general composite tasks, which successfully replicates the neural scaling law of Chinchilla models reported in arXiv:2203.15556. We believe that the notion of resource used in this paper will be a useful tool for characterizing and diagnosing neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central focus is explaining and predicting neural scaling laws — how model loss scales as a power law with model size, parameters, and compute.

conceptDetails.name: neural scaling law
conceptDetails.description: The core novel contribution: a resource-based model that analytically derives power-law scaling of loss with neuron count and model parameters.

conceptDetails.name: resource allocation
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces neurons as a quantifiable resource that subtasks compete for, and shows allocation ratios remain constant as models grow.

conceptDetails.name: modular subnetworks
conceptDetails.description: The framework identifies distinct subnetworks (modules) within a neural network, each responsible for a specific subtask, as the structural basis of resource allocation.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity regularization (L1/L2 penalties) is used to induce modular structure in networks, enabling identification of neuron allocations per subtask.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The resource model treats composite tasks as combinations of subtasks competing for neuron resources, directly relating to multi-task learning dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses mechanistic interpretability concepts (circuits, modules, subnetworks) to ground the resource allocation framework.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The training objective combines task loss with sparsity regularization to control resource allocation and reveal module structure.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Toy regression tasks (e.g., predicting x²) serve as the empirical testbed for validating the single-task scaling hypothesis.

title: LtU-ILI: An All-in-One Framework for Implicit Inference in Astrophysics and Cosmology
abstract: This paper presents the Learning the Universe Implicit Likelihood Inference (LtU-ILI) pipeline, a codebase for rapid, user-friendly, and cutting-edge machine learning (ML) inference in astrophysics and cosmology. The pipeline includes software for implementing various neural architectures, training schemata, priors, and density estimators in a manner easily adaptable to any research workflow. It includes comprehensive validation metrics to assess posterior estimate coverage, enhancing the reliability of inferred results. Additionally, the pipeline is easily parallelizable and is designed for efficient exploration of modeling hyperparameters. To demonstrate its capabilities, we present real applications across a range of astrophysics and cosmology problems, such as: estimating galaxy cluster masses from X-ray photometry; inferring cosmology from matter power spectra and halo point clouds; characterizing progenitors in gravitational wave signals; capturing physical dust parameters from galaxy colors and luminosities; and establishing properties of semi-analytic models of galaxy formation. We also include exhaustive benchmarking and comparisons of all implemented methods as well as discussions about the challenges and pitfalls of ML inference in astronomical sciences. All code and examples are made publicly available at https://github.com/maho3/ltu-ili.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is the LtU-ILI pipeline, which implements implicit likelihood inference (ILI/SBI) to learn posterior distributions over model parameters directly from simulations without analytic likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline trains neural networks to emulate full posterior probability distributions over physical parameters given observed data, enabling robust Bayesian parameter constraints.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Neural density estimators (NPE, NLE, NRE variants) are the primary algorithmic machinery used to learn the mapping between observations and parameter posteriors.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: ILI is framed as a rigorous extension of Bayesian statistics, incorporating priors and producing calibrated posterior credible intervals for physical model parameters.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are implemented as core density estimator architectures (e.g., masked autoregressive flows) for modeling complex, high-dimensional posterior distributions.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline provides comprehensive coverage diagnostics (TARP, expected coverage) to validate that inferred posteriors are statistically calibrated and reliable.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Epistemic uncertainty from network weights is addressed through dropout marginalization, model ensembling, and stochastic weight averaging to produce reliable credible intervals.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Exhaustive benchmarking and validation metrics (posterior coverage, predictive checks) are central design features ensuring responsible application of ML inference.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: ILI effectively builds a learned surrogate for the intractable likelihood function, enabling fast posterior sampling without expensive forward model evaluations at inference time.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: One of the five real-world demonstration applications involves using the pipeline to characterize progenitor parameters from gravitational wave signals.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Training sets drawn from N-body and hydrodynamical simulations (halo point clouds, matter power spectra) are used as forward models for cosmological parameter inference.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Model ensembling is used both as an approximation for Bayesian marginalization over network weights and as a hyperparameter-search strategy to improve posterior reliability.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Graph neural networks are employed to process halo point cloud data for cosmological inference, enabling permutation-invariant summary extraction.

title: Equivariant Symmetry Breaking Sets
abstract: Equivariant neural networks (ENNs) have been shown to be extremely effective in applications involving underlying symmetries. By construction ENNs cannot produce lower symmetry outputs given a higher symmetry input. However, symmetry breaking occurs in many physical systems and we may obtain a less symmetric stable state from an initial highly symmetric one. Hence, it is imperative that we understand how to systematically break symmetry in ENNs. In this work, we propose a novel symmetry breaking framework that is fully equivariant and is the first which fully addresses spontaneous symmetry breaking. We emphasize that our approach is general and applicable to equivariance under any group. To achieve this, we introduce the idea of symmetry breaking sets (SBS). Rather than redesign existing networks, we design sets of symmetry breaking objects which we feed into our network based on the symmetry of our inputs and outputs. We show there is a natural way to define equivariance on these sets, which gives an additional constraint. Minimizing the size of these sets equates to data efficiency. We prove that minimizing these sets translates to a well studied group theory problem, and tabulate solutions to this problem for the point groups. Finally, we provide some examples of symmetry breaking to demonstrate how our approach works in practice. The code for these examples is available at \url{https://github.com/atomicarchitects/equivariant-SBS}.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The core architecture studied in this paper, which the authors extend to handle symmetry breaking inputs and outputs while preserving equivariance.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon addressed — specifically spontaneous symmetry breaking where a lower-symmetry output arises from a higher-symmetry input.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking sets
conceptDetails.description: The novel contribution of this paper — designed sets of symmetry breaking objects fed into equivariant networks to enable lower-symmetry outputs.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical framework used to prove that minimizing symmetry breaking sets reduces to a group complement problem, with solutions tabulated for point groups.

conceptDetails.name: normalizer constraint
conceptDetails.description: A novel theoretical result showing that equivariance of symmetry breaking sets requires closure under the group normalizer.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The broader field this work contributes to, addressing how geometric and symmetry constraints can be systematically handled in neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: point group symmetry
conceptDetails.description: The specific class of groups for which the authors tabulate minimal symmetry breaking set solutions as concrete results.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Contrasted with symmetry breaking throughout the paper; the default behavior of ENNs that the authors work around while maintaining full equivariance.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: A key physical application domain motivating the work, as spontaneous symmetry breaking underlies phase transitions in physical systems.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: One of the primary application examples — crystal distortion from high-symmetry to low-symmetry structures is used to motivate and demonstrate the framework.

title: Substructure Detection in Realistic Strong Lensing Systems with Machine Learning
abstract: Tens of thousands of galaxy-galaxy strong lensing systems are expected to be discovered by the end of the decade. These will form a vast new dataset that can be used to probe subgalactic dark matter structures through its gravitational effects, which will in turn allow us to study the nature of dark matter at small length scales. This work shows how we can leverage machine learning to search through the data and identify which systems are most likely to contain dark matter substructure and thus can be studied in greater depth. We use a UNet, an image segmentation architecture, on a simulated strongly-lensed dataset with realistic sources (COSMOS galaxies), lenses (power-law elliptical profiles with multipoles and external shear), and noise. Our machine learning algorithm is able to quickly detect most substructure at high image resolution and subhalo concentration. At a false positive rate of $10\%$, we are able to identify systems with substructure at a true positive rate of $71\%$ for a subhalo mass range of $10^{9}\text{-}10^{9.5}\,M_\odot$. While recent detections are consistent with higher concentrations, we find that our algorithm fails at detecting subhalos with lower concentrations (expected from $Λ$CDM simulations).

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary scientific goal is detecting dark matter substructure (subhalos) in galaxy-galaxy lensing systems to constrain the nature of dark matter at small scales.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational lensing
conceptDetails.description: Strong gravitational lensing of background galaxies by foreground mass distributions is the physical phenomenon exploited to probe subgalactic dark matter structures.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A UNet, a convolutional encoder-decoder architecture, is the core ML model used to perform pixel-level segmentation of simulated strong lensing images.

conceptDetails.name: image segmentation
conceptDetails.description: The UNet is applied as an image segmentation architecture to identify spatial regions in lensing images likely to harbor dark matter substructure.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The ML pipeline classifies lensing systems as containing detectable subhalos or not, evaluated via true positive rate and false positive rate metrics.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The method flags which lensing systems are most likely to contain individually detectable subhalo signals for downstream detailed analysis.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained on a large simulated dataset with realistic COSMOS sources, power-law elliptical lenses, multipoles, external shear, and noise rather than analytic posteriors.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering subhalo mass and position without prior knowledge of the unlensed source or lens mass distribution is the fundamental inverse problem motivating the ML approach.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: ΛCDM-motivated NFW subhalo profiles are drawn from realistic mass and concentration distributions to generate the training and test datasets.

conceptDetails.name: subhalo concentration
conceptDetails.description: The NFW concentration parameter is identified as the key physical variable controlling detection success, with low-concentration subhalos proving undetectable by the algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Subhalos introduce subtle perturbations to the smooth lens model; detecting them can be framed as identifying anomalous deviations from the expected Einstein ring morphology.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The paper evaluates the trained model via ROC-style analysis across subhalo mass and concentration ranges to characterize detection completeness and false alarm rates.

title: Rigid Schubert classes in partial flag varieties
abstract: A Schubert class is called rigid if it can only be represented by Schubert varieties. The rigid Schubert classes have been classified in Grassmannians and orthogonal Grassmannians. In this paper, we study the rigidity problem in partial flag varieties (type A) and orthogonal partial flag varieties (type B and type D). In particular, we give numerical conditions that ensure a Schubert class is rigid.

conceptDetails.name: schubert variety rigidity
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: classifying which Schubert classes in partial flag varieties can only be represented by Schubert varieties.

conceptDetails.name: partial flag variety
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: The geometric spaces parametrizing chains of subspaces that form the primary objects of study throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: rational equivalence
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: The algebraic equivalence relation used to define Schubert classes and determine when different subvarieties represent the same class.

conceptDetails.name: schubert calculus
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: The intersection theory on flag varieties that provides the algebraic framework for studying Schubert classes and their rigidity.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within the framework of classical Lie groups (type A, B, D) and their associated flag variety geometry.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The orthogonal partial flag varieties involve isotropic subspaces under symmetric bilinear forms, connecting to symmetry structures in quantum field theory.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Numerical conditions involving dimension counts and index sequences are used to characterize rigidity, analogous to spectral classification problems.

title: Applications of flow models to the generation of correlated lattice QCD ensembles
abstract: Machine-learned normalizing flows can be used in the context of lattice quantum field theory to generate statistically correlated ensembles of lattice gauge fields at different action parameters. This work demonstrates how these correlations can be exploited for variance reduction in the computation of observables. Three different proof-of-concept applications are demonstrated using a novel residual flow architecture: continuum limits of gauge theories, the mass dependence of QCD observables, and hadronic matrix elements based on the Feynman-Hellmann approach. In all three cases, it is shown that statistical uncertainties are significantly reduced when machine-learned flows are incorporated as compared with the same calculations performed with uncorrelated ensembles or direct reweighting.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core ML method used to learn invertible maps between lattice gauge field distributions at different action parameters, enabling correlated ensemble generation.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics framework; flow models are applied to generate and relate lattice QCD gauge field configurations across different action parameters.

conceptDetails.name: correlated ensembles
conceptDetails.description: A novel application mode where flow maps produce statistically correlated gauge field samples at different action parameters, enabling cancellation of fluctuations in finite-difference derivatives.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical setting in which flow architectures must respect gauge symmetry, motivating equivariant construction of the learned maps.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The broader class of ML models to which normalizing flows belong; the paper positions flows as generative tools for producing physically correlated ensembles.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central goal is reducing statistical uncertainties in QCD observables by exploiting correlations introduced by the flow maps.

conceptDetails.name: residual flow architecture
conceptDetails.description: A novel invertible neural network architecture introduced in this paper for mapping between lattice gauge field distributions with improved expressiveness.

conceptDetails.name: feynman-hellmann method
conceptDetails.description: A technique for computing hadronic matrix elements via derivatives of the action with respect to source parameters, demonstrated here with flow-based variance reduction.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The standard sampling baseline against which the flow-based correlated ensembles are compared for variance reduction.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The flow architectures are constructed to respect gauge symmetry, making gauge-equivariant network design essential to the approach.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge invariance of the lattice action must be preserved by the flow transformations, constraining the architecture design throughout the work.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Flow models are trained to approximate target lattice action distributions, relying on exact density evaluation via the change-of-variables formula.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Continuum extrapolation of gradient flow scales requires understanding how observables run with the lattice spacing, connecting to renormalization group ideas.

title: SymbolNet: Neural Symbolic Regression with Adaptive Dynamic Pruning for Compression
abstract: Compact symbolic expressions have been shown to be more efficient than neural network models in terms of resource consumption and inference speed when implemented on custom hardware such as FPGAs, while maintaining comparable accuracy~\cite{tsoi2023symbolic}. These capabilities are highly valuable in environments with stringent computational resource constraints, such as high-energy physics experiments at the CERN Large Hadron Collider. However, finding compact expressions for high-dimensional datasets remains challenging due to the inherent limitations of genetic programming, the search algorithm of most symbolic regression methods. Contrary to genetic programming, the neural network approach to symbolic regression offers scalability to high-dimensional inputs and leverages gradient methods for faster equation searching. Common ways of constraining expression complexity often involve multistage pruning with fine-tuning, which can result in significant performance loss. In this work, we propose $\tt{SymbolNet}$, a neural network approach to symbolic regression specifically designed as a model compression technique, aimed at enabling low-latency inference for high-dimensional inputs on custom hardware such as FPGAs. This framework allows dynamic pruning of model weights, input features, and mathematical operators in a single training process, where both training loss and expression complexity are optimized simultaneously. We introduce a sparsity regularization term for each pruning type, which can adaptively adjust its strength, leading to convergence at a target sparsity ratio. Unlike most existing symbolic regression methods that struggle with datasets containing more than $\mathcal{O}(10)$ inputs, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our model on the LHC jet tagging task (16 inputs), MNIST (784 inputs), and SVHN (3072 inputs).

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: The core method of the paper: searching for compact analytic expressions that best fit data without a pre-defined functional form, extended here via a neural network framework to high-dimensional datasets.

conceptDetails.name: dynamic pruning
conceptDetails.description: SymbolNet's novel single-phase pruning framework that simultaneously prunes model weights, input features, and mathematical operators using trainable thresholds, eliminating the need for multi-stage fine-tuning.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity is the central optimization target; the paper introduces self-adaptive sparsity regularization terms for each pruning type that converge to user-specified sparsity ratios.

conceptDetails.name: model compression
conceptDetails.description: SymbolNet is explicitly framed as a model compression technique, distilling neural networks into compact symbolic expressions for low-latency FPGA deployment.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic expressions produced by SymbolNet are human-readable and interpretable, contrasting with opaque black-box deep learning models.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: LHC jet tagging (16 inputs) is the primary physics benchmark, demonstrating SymbolNet's practical utility in high-energy physics classification tasks.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Input feature pruning within SymbolNet performs automated feature selection, eliminating irrelevant inputs during the single training phase.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key contribution is scaling symbolic regression beyond O(10) inputs to high-dimensional datasets including MNIST (784) and SVHN (3072 inputs).

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: FPGA-based low-latency inference targets LHC real-time trigger systems, where resource efficiency and nanosecond latency are hard constraints.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression is the supervised learning task being solved, with the model optimizing both predictive accuracy and expression complexity simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The self-adaptive regularization term adjusts its strength relative to training loss dynamically, ensuring co-optimization of accuracy and sparsity without manual tuning.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The motivating application domain is high-energy physics experiments at the CERN Large Hadron Collider, which impose strict computational resource constraints.

title: A Physics-Informed Variational Autoencoder for Rapid Galaxy Inference and Anomaly Detection
abstract: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory is slated to observe nearly 20 billion galaxies during its decade-long Legacy Survey of Space and Time. The rich imaging data it collects will be an invaluable resource for probing galaxy evolution across cosmic time, characterizing the host galaxies of transient phenomena, and identifying novel populations of anomalous systems. To facilitate these studies, we introduce a convolutional variational autoencoder trained to estimate the redshift, stellar mass, and star-formation rates of galaxies from multi-band imaging data. We train and test our physics-informed CVAE on a spectroscopic sample of $\sim$26,000 galaxies within $z<1$ imaged through the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey. We show that our model can infer redshift and stellar mass more accurately than the latest image-based self-supervised learning approaches, and is >100x faster than more computationally-intensive SED-fitting techniques. Using a small sample of Green Pea and Red Spiral galaxies reported in the literature, we further demonstrate how this CVAE can be used to rapidly identify rare galaxy populations and interpret what makes them unique.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The CVAE is the core architecture, encoding galaxy images into a 5-dimensional physics-guided latent space for parameter inference.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A physics-informed loss term constrains the first four latent dimensions to correspond directly to physical galaxy properties (redshift, stellar mass, SFR, orientation).

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The model explicitly separates intrinsic galaxy properties from observational artifacts (orientation) in the latent space to enable meaningful anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The CVAE is used to identify rare galaxy populations (Green Pea and Red Spiral galaxies) by locating outliers in the physics-guided latent space.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Three convolutional encoding and three deconvolutional decoding layers form the backbone of the CVAE for processing multi-band galaxy images.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: The model characterizes galaxies by estimating physical parameters from DECaLS multi-band imaging, enabling rapid population-level analysis for surveys like Rubin LSST.

conceptDetails.name: photometric redshift estimation
conceptDetails.description: Inferring galaxy redshift from multi-band photometric imaging is a central prediction task, with the CVAE outperforming prior image-based methods in R² accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: Rare galaxy sub-populations are identified as out-of-distribution examples in the physically-constrained latent manifold rather than via raw reconstruction error.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper benchmarks its CVAE image embeddings against prior self-supervised image embedding approaches for predicting galaxy physical properties.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Survey-reported 1-sigma uncertainties on SED-derived parameters are incorporated directly into the physics loss term during training.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: State-of-the-art image-based self-supervised learning methods serve as the primary comparison baseline for redshift and stellar mass prediction accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: SED fitting—a computationally intensive simulation-based inference technique—is the high-accuracy baseline the CVAE aims to replace at >100x speed improvement.

title: Applications of Lipschitz neural networks to the Run 3 LHCb trigger system
abstract: The operating conditions defining the current data taking campaign at the Large Hadron Collider, known as Run 3, present unparalleled challenges for the real-time data acquisition workflow of the LHCb experiment at CERN. To address the anticipated surge in luminosity and consequent event rate, the LHCb experiment is transitioning to a fully software-based trigger system. This evolution necessitated innovations in hardware configurations, software paradigms, and algorithmic design. A significant advancement is the integration of monotonic Lipschitz neural networks into the LHCb trigger system. These deep learning models offer certified robustness against detector instabilities, and the ability to encode domain-specific inductive biases. Such properties are crucial for the inclusive heavy-flavour triggers and, most notably, for the topological triggers designed to inclusively select $b$-hadron candidates by exploiting the unique kinematic and decay topologies of beauty decays. This paper describes the recent progress in integrating Lipschitz neural networks into the topological triggers, highlighting the resulting enhanced sensitivity to highly displaced multi-body candidates produced within the LHCb acceptance.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary application is integrating Lipschitz neural networks into the LHCb Run 3 fully software-based trigger system for real-time event selection.

conceptDetails.name: lipschitz neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A novel architecture constraint bounding the Lipschitz constant of the learned decision boundary to provide certified robustness and monotonicity simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Certified robustness against detector instabilities and simulation inaccuracies is a central motivation for using Lipschitz-constrained networks in the trigger.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The Lipschitz neural networks function as classifiers to distinguish b-hadron signal candidates from combinatorial and QCD backgrounds.

conceptDetails.name: monotonic constraints
conceptDetails.description: Architectural enforcement of monotonic classifier response with respect to selected input features encodes domain-specific physics priors in the trigger models.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The work is set within LHC Run 3 proton-proton collisions at unprecedented luminosity, defining the experimental context.

conceptDetails.name: particle tracking
conceptDetails.description: The topological triggers reconstruct secondary-vertex candidates from charged-track combinations, relying on track-level input features.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: HLT2 operates on offline-quality full-event reconstruction observables to select b-hadron decay candidates.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Monotonic Lipschitz networks enhance sensitivity to feebly interacting BSM states and dark-portal candidates in the LHCb acceptance.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Monotonic response guarantees provide physics-motivated interpretability, encoding domain-specific inductive biases about b-hadron kinematics.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Monotonicity constraints encode known physical relationships (e.g., higher displacement implies stronger signal) as architectural inductive biases.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Monotonic networks improve retention of outlier signal candidates absent from training data, enabling sensitivity to novel undiscovered particles.

title: First search for dark-trident processes using the MicroBooNE detector
abstract: We present a first search for dark-trident scattering in a neutrino beam using a data set corresponding to $7.2 \times 10^{20}$ protons on target taken with the MicroBooNE detector at Fermilab. Proton interactions in the neutrino target at the Main Injector produce $π^0$ and $η$ mesons, which could decay into dark-matter (DM) particles mediated via a dark photon $A^\prime$. A convolutional neural network is trained to identify interactions of the DM particles in the liquid-argon time projection chamber (LArTPC) exploiting its image-like reconstruction capability. In the absence of a DM signal, we provide limits at the $90\%$ confidence level on the squared kinematic mixing parameter $\varepsilon^2$ as a function of the dark-photon mass in the range $10\le M_{A^\prime}\le 400$ MeV. The limits cover previously unconstrained parameter space for the production of fermion or scalar DM particles $χ$ for two benchmark models with mass ratios $M_χ/M_{A^\prime}=0.6$ and $2$ and for dark fine-structure constants $0.1\leα_D\le 1$.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for dark-matter particles produced via dark photon-mediated meson decays and scattering in the MicroBooNE LArTPC detector.

conceptDetails.name: dark-trident scattering
conceptDetails.description: A novel process where a dark-matter particle scatters off an argon nucleus producing an on-shell dark photon that decays to an e+e- pair.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: This is a first search for the dark-trident process, constraining previously unconstrained dark sector parameter space using a neutrino beam experiment.

conceptDetails.name: dark photon
conceptDetails.description: The dark photon A′ mediates both dark-matter production from meson decays and the trident scattering process inside the detector, decaying to e+e- with branching ratio 1.

conceptDetails.name: kinematic mixing
conceptDetails.description: The squared kinematic mixing parameter ε² coupling the dark photon to Standard Model photons is the primary quantity being constrained by this search.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional neural network is trained to identify dark-matter interactions exploiting the image-like reconstruction capability of the LArTPC.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The analysis is designed to detect or exclude a dark-trident signal above Standard Model backgrounds in LArTPC data.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Reconstruction of e+e- pair topology, energies, and opening angles in the liquid-argon detector is central to identifying dark-trident candidates.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: 90% confidence level limits on the kinematic mixing parameter ε² are derived using statistical hypothesis testing in the absence of a signal.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulation of signal and background processes including detector response is used to train the CNN and estimate expected yields.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The CNN performs binary classification to distinguish dark-trident signal events from neutrino and cosmic-ray backgrounds.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The MicroBooNE LArTPC neutrino detector at Fermilab provides the infrastructure and data set used in this dark matter search.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Dark sector benchmark models parameterized by dark fine-structure constant αD and mass ratios Mχ/MA′ provide the theoretical framework for the search.

title: Inhomogeneous Energy Injection in the 21-cm Power Spectrum: Sensitivity to Dark Matter Decay
abstract: The 21-cm signal provides a novel avenue to measure the thermal state of the universe during cosmic dawn and reionization (redshifts $z\sim 5-30$), and thus to probe energy injection from decaying or annihilating dark matter (DM). These DM processes are inherently inhomogeneous: both decay and annihilation are density dependent, and furthermore the fraction of injected energy that is deposited at each point depends on the gas ionization and density, leading to further anisotropies in absorption and propagation. In this work, we develop a new framework for modeling the impact of spatially inhomogeneous energy injection and deposition during cosmic dawn, accounting for ionization and baryon density dependence, as well as the attenuation of propagating photons. We showcase how this first completely inhomogeneous treatment affects the predicted 21-cm power spectrum in the presence of exotic sources of energy injection, and forecast the constraints that upcoming HERA measurements of the 21-cm power spectrum will set on DM decays to photons and to electron/positron pairs. These projected constraints considerably surpass those derived from CMB and Lyman-$α$ measurements, and for decays to electron/positron pairs they exceed all existing constraints in the sub-GeV mass range, reaching lifetimes of $\sim 10^{28}\,\mathrm{s}$. Our analysis demonstrates the unprecedented sensitivity of 21-cm cosmology to exotic sources of energy injection during the cosmic dark ages. Our code, $\mathtt{DM21cm}$, includes all these effects and is publicly available in an accompanying release.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central subject: forecasting sensitivity to DM decay lifetimes via 21-cm power spectrum observations, surpassing existing CMB and Lyman-alpha constraints.

conceptDetails.name: 21-cm power spectrum
conceptDetails.description: The primary observable: spatial power spectrum of the redshifted hydrogen hyperfine signal, used to constrain DM energy injection across redshifts z~5-30.

conceptDetails.name: inhomogeneous energy injection
conceptDetails.description: The paper's key methodological contribution: a framework for spatially resolved DM energy deposition accounting for ionization, baryon density, and photon attenuation.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The DM21cm code couples 21cmFAST semi-numerical simulations with DarkHistory to simulate spatially inhomogeneous exotic energy injection during cosmic dawn.

conceptDetails.name: reionization modeling
conceptDetails.description: Modeling the epoch of cosmic reionization including exotic DM contributions to ionization and heating of the intergalactic medium alongside standard stellar sources.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The framework enables detection of DM-induced signatures in the 21-cm power spectrum by accurately modeling inhomogeneous energy deposition morphology.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Simulated 21-cm power spectra from DM21cm feed a Fisher matrix and MCMC pipeline to forecast projected HERA constraints on DM decay parameters.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: Existing CMB-derived constraints on DM energy injection serve as the benchmark that the 21-cm forecasts are shown to substantially surpass.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian statistical framework underlies the parameter constraint forecasts, deriving posterior distributions on DM lifetime from simulated observations.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: Semi-numerical simulation serves as a computationally efficient emulator of full radiative transfer, enabling large parameter-space surveys of DM models.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural network or interpolation-based surrogate models of the 21-cm power spectrum reduce computational cost when scanning DM parameter space for forecasts.

title: Cosmological Field Emulation and Parameter Inference with Diffusion Models
abstract: Cosmological simulations play a crucial role in elucidating the effect of physical parameters on the statistics of fields and on constraining parameters given information on density fields. We leverage diffusion generative models to address two tasks of importance to cosmology -- as an emulator for cold dark matter density fields conditional on input cosmological parameters $Ω_m$ and $σ_8$, and as a parameter inference model that can return constraints on the cosmological parameters of an input field. We show that the model is able to generate fields with power spectra that are consistent with those of the simulated target distribution, and capture the subtle effect of each parameter on modulations in the power spectrum. We additionally explore their utility as parameter inference models and find that we can obtain tight constraints on cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The core method used to emulate cold dark matter density fields conditioned on cosmological parameters and to perform parameter inference via variational lower bounds.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses CAMELS/IllustrisTNG simulated cold dark matter density fields as training data and benchmarks generated fields against these simulations.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: One of the two primary tasks addressed: using the diffusion model as a surrogate emulator to generate cosmological density fields conditioned on Ω_m and σ_8 without running full simulations.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The diffusion model's variational lower bound is used to derive constraints on cosmological parameters from input density fields, effectively performing posterior estimation.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper specifically emulates cold dark matter density fields from cosmological simulations as its primary physical target.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The diffusion model acts as a fast surrogate for expensive N-body cosmological simulations, enabling sampling across parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework underlying the diffusion generative models used in this work, involving learning to reverse a stochastic noising process.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The parameter inference approach leverages the trained generative model to constrain cosmological parameters from observed fields, a form of simulation-based inference.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The variational lower bound on the log likelihood is computed and used as an objective for parameter inference from cosmological fields.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The U-Net backbone with ResNet blocks and circular convolutions (for periodic boundary conditions) forms the score model architecture.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Power spectra (P(k)) are used as the primary summary statistics to validate generated fields and assess how cosmological parameters modulate field statistics.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Attention layers are incorporated in the U-Net architecture used as the diffusion model's denoising network.

title: Derived Moduli Spaces of Nonlinear PDEs I: Singular Propagations
abstract: We construct a sheaf theoretic and derived geometric machinery to study nonlinear partial differential equations and their singular supports. We establish a notion of derived microlocalization for solution spaces of non-linear equations and develop a formalism to pose and solve singular non-linear Cauchy problems globally. Using this approach we estimate the domains of propagation for the solutions of non-linear systems. It is achieved by exploiting the fact that one may greatly enrich and simplify the study of derived non-linear PDEs over a space $X$ by studying its derived linearization which is a module over the sheaf of functions on the $S^1$-equivariant derived loop stack $\mathcal{L}X$.

conceptDetails.name: derived algebraic geometry
conceptDetails.description: The entire paper is built on derived algebraic and analytic geometry, using (∞,1)-categories, derived stacks, and sheaf theory as the foundational framework.

conceptDetails.name: nonlinear pde theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary subject is the study of nonlinear PDEs via derived geometric methods, including their solution spaces, singularities, and propagation.

conceptDetails.name: microlocal analysis
conceptDetails.description: Derived microlocalization and characteristic varieties for nonlinear PDEs are central contributions, generalizing classical microlocal analysis to the derived nonlinear setting.

conceptDetails.name: derived loop stacks
conceptDetails.description: The S1-equivariant derived loop stack LX is used to encode linearizations of nonlinear PDEs and is a key geometric object throughout.

conceptDetails.name: sheaf theory
conceptDetails.description: Sheaf-theoretic constructions, including ind-coherent sheaves and solution sheaves, provide the technical backbone for propagation and singular support results.

conceptDetails.name: hodge theory
conceptDetails.description: Simpson's non-abelian Hodge theory and the Hodge stack XHod are used to lift linearization data and derive vanishing criteria for solution sheaves.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops derived algebraic geometry machinery applicable to QFT contexts, including derived loop stacks and characteristic varieties central to field-theoretic constructions.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Characteristic varieties and singular supports are computed via spectral/microlocal techniques adapted to the derived nonlinear setting.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Equivariant structures under S1-action on the derived loop stack play a fundamental role in the microlocalization framework developed in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The global Cauchy problem framework addresses well-posedness of singular initial value problems, a canonical class of inverse/forward PDE problems.

title: Generating Interpretable Networks using Hypernetworks
abstract: An essential goal in mechanistic interpretability to decode a network, i.e., to convert a neural network's raw weights to an interpretable algorithm. Given the difficulty of the decoding problem, progress has been made to understand the easier encoding problem, i.e., to convert an interpretable algorithm into network weights. Previous works focus on encoding existing algorithms into networks, which are interpretable by definition. However, focusing on encoding limits the possibility of discovering new algorithms that humans have never stumbled upon, but that are nevertheless interpretable. In this work, we explore the possibility of using hypernetworks to generate interpretable networks whose underlying algorithms are not yet known. The hypernetwork is carefully designed such that it can control network complexity, leading to a diverse family of interpretable algorithms ranked by their complexity. All of them are interpretable in hindsight, although some of them are less intuitive to humans, hence providing new insights regarding how to "think" like a neural network. For the task of computing L1 norms, hypernetworks find three algorithms: (a) the double-sided algorithm, (b) the convexity algorithm, (c) the pudding algorithm, although only the first algorithm was expected by the authors before experiments. We automatically classify these algorithms and analyze how these algorithmic phases develop during training, as well as how they are affected by complexity control. Furthermore, we show that a trained hypernetwork can correctly construct models for input dimensions not seen in training, demonstrating systematic generalization.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The central goal of the paper is to generate neural networks whose underlying algorithms are interpretable and can be decoded by humans.

conceptDetails.name: hypernetworks
conceptDetails.description: Hypernetworks are used to generate families of interpretable networks with controlled complexity, enabling systematic exploration of algorithmic space.

conceptDetails.name: algorithmic phase discovery
conceptDetails.description: Novel phenomenon: the emergence and transition between distinct algorithmic strategies (double-sided, pudding, convexity) as a function of complexity parameter β.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The hypernetwork framework enables automated discovery of novel algorithms (pudding, convexity) that humans had not previously identified.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Order parameters are defined to auto-classify algorithms and reveal phase transitions between different algorithmic regimes during training and complexity variation.

conceptDetails.name: weight space generalization
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the hypernetwork demonstrates systematic generalization by constructing valid models for input dimensions unseen during training.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Complexity control via KL divergence encourages sparse, simple weight structures that yield interpretable algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A KL-divergence-based complexity penalty is incorporated into the loss to balance model performance against interpretability.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames decoding (weights to algorithm) as the hard inverse of encoding (algorithm to weights), motivating the hypernetwork approach.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Force-directed graph drawings reveal symmetry structures in learned networks that distinguish different algorithmic classes.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Two-layer MLPs with swish activations serve as the target networks generated by the hypernetwork for L1 norm computation experiments.

title: Shifted symplectic structures on derived Quot-stacks II -- Derived Quot-schemes as dg manifolds
abstract: It is proved that derived Quot-schemes, as defined by Ciocan-Fontanine and Kapranov, are represented by dg manifolds of finite type. This is the second part if a work aimed to analyze shifted symplectic structures on moduli spaces of coherent sheaves on Calabi--Yau manifolds. The first part related dg manifolds to derived schemes as defined by Toën and Vezzosi.

conceptDetails.name: derived algebraic geometry
conceptDetails.description: The entire paper operates within derived algebraic geometry, proving that derived Quot-stacks are representable by dg manifolds via Toën-Vezzosi style derived schemes.

conceptDetails.name: moduli spaces of sheaves
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly analyzes derived moduli spaces of coherent sheaves and their Quot-scheme compactifications on projective varieties.

conceptDetails.name: dg lie algebras
conceptDetails.description: Differential graded Lie algebras parametrize A∞-structures on the graded modules, and Koszul duality is used to construct affine dg manifolds from them.

conceptDetails.name: maurer-cartan equations
conceptDetails.description: The Maurer-Cartan equations govern the solutions to the A∞-structure classification problem and are used to propagate injectivity conditions across the stretch.

conceptDetails.name: homotopy limit
conceptDetails.description: The derived Quot-stack is defined as a homotopy limit of a sequence of dg manifold fibrations, and the paper shows this limit is representable by a finite-type dg manifold.

conceptDetails.name: koszul duality
conceptDetails.description: Koszul and linear duality are used to translate between dg Lie algebras and affine dg manifolds of finite type in the construction.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs dg manifolds of finite type to represent derived Quot-schemes, a core result about the geometry of these moduli spaces.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs group actions (C× quotients) and invariant theory to construct geometric and algebraic quotients of derived spaces.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies moduli spaces of coherent sheaves on Calabi-Yau manifolds, which are central objects in quantum field theory and string theory.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau manifolds and their sheaf moduli spaces are foundational to string theory compactifications, providing the physical motivation for this work.

title: Anomaly Detection in Collider Physics via Factorized Observables
abstract: To maximize the discovery potential of high-energy colliders, experimental searches should be sensitive to unforeseen new physics scenarios. This goal has motivated the use of machine learning for unsupervised anomaly detection. In this paper, we introduce a new anomaly detection strategy called FORCE: factorized observables for regressing conditional expectations. Our approach is based on the inductive bias of factorization, which is the idea that the physics governing different energy scales can be treated as approximately independent. Assuming factorization holds separately for signal and background processes, the appearance of non-trivial correlations between low- and high-energy observables is a robust indicator of new physics. Under the most restrictive form of factorization, a machine-learned model trained to identify such correlations will in fact converge to the optimal new physics classifier. We test FORCE on a benchmark anomaly detection task for the Large Hadron Collider involving collimated sprays of particles called jets. By teasing out correlations between the kinematics and substructure of jets, our method can reliably extract percent-level signal fractions. This strategy for uncovering new physics adds to the growing toolbox of anomaly detection methods for collider physics with a complementary set of assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: FORCE is a new anomaly detection strategy that uses factorized observables to identify non-trivial correlations as indicators of new physics at colliders.

conceptDetails.name: factorization-based anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Novel method exploiting the inductive bias of QCD factorization to define anomalies as violations of independence between observables at different energy scales.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The method targets high-energy collider experiments, specifically the LHC, to discover unforeseen new physics beyond the Standard Model.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jets serve as the benchmark test case for FORCE, exploiting the factorization of jet kinematics and substructure to detect anomalous signals.

conceptDetails.name: conditional expectation regression
conceptDetails.description: The core FORCE technique of learning E[pT|O] via mean-squared error minimization, which provably recovers the optimal signal-background classifier.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: FORCE is explicitly designed to maximize discovery potential for unforeseen new physics scenarios at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The Neyman-Pearson likelihood ratio is shown to be the optimal classifier and is provably recovered by FORCE under strict factorization assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: FORCE trains a model to regress conditional expectations (predicted jet pT from substructure), with non-trivial regression outputs signaling new physics.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Factorization in QCD, which underpins FORCE, is a key concept from effective field theory separating physics at different energy scales.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames anomaly detection as a statistical hypothesis test, leveraging the optimal decision boundaries derived from factorization.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: FORCE operates in a semi-supervised regime, requiring no labeled signal events and training directly on unlabeled collider data.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: FORCE implicitly estimates conditional probability densities to identify correlations between jet kinematics and substructure.

title: One-step Diffusion with Distribution Matching Distillation
abstract: Diffusion models generate high-quality images but require dozens of forward passes. We introduce Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD), a procedure to transform a diffusion model into a one-step image generator with minimal impact on image quality. We enforce the one-step image generator match the diffusion model at distribution level, by minimizing an approximate KL divergence whose gradient can be expressed as the difference between 2 score functions, one of the target distribution and the other of the synthetic distribution being produced by our one-step generator. The score functions are parameterized as two diffusion models trained separately on each distribution. Combined with a simple regression loss matching the large-scale structure of the multi-step diffusion outputs, our method outperforms all published few-step diffusion approaches, reaching 2.62 FID on ImageNet 64x64 and 11.49 FID on zero-shot COCO-30k, comparable to Stable Diffusion but orders of magnitude faster. Utilizing FP16 inference, our model generates images at 20 FPS on modern hardware.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The teacher model framework being distilled; DMD transforms multi-step diffusion sampling into a single forward pass while preserving distribution quality.

conceptDetails.name: distribution matching distillation
conceptDetails.description: Novel distillation procedure that enforces a one-step generator to match the teacher diffusion model at the distribution level via approximate KL divergence minimization.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: Two diffusion models are trained as score function estimators — one on real data, one on synthetic outputs — whose difference forms the distribution matching gradient.

conceptDetails.name: one-step generation
conceptDetails.description: The core capability delivered by DMD: mapping Gaussian noise to a high-fidelity image in a single forward pass, enabling interactive and real-time applications.

conceptDetails.name: fake score estimation
conceptDetails.description: A novel component where a diffusion model is continuously trained on the generator's own outputs to estimate the score of the evolving synthetic distribution.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: DMD combines a KL-divergence-based distribution matching gradient with a regression loss anchoring large-scale structure to multi-step diffusion outputs.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: DMD advances the state of the art in generative modeling by enabling real-time single-step synthesis competitive with costly iterative models.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: A pretrained Stable Diffusion model is finetuned continuously on synthetic images to estimate the fake distribution's score function.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The primary engineering motivation is reducing hundreds of neural network evaluations to a single pass, enabling 20 FPS generation on modern hardware.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: KL divergence between the target and synthetic distributions is approximated using score function differences from the two trained diffusion models.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: GANs provide key conceptual inspiration for the distribution-matching objective and the real/fake scoring framework underlying DMD.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The one-step student generator is initialized from the pretrained Stable Diffusion weights, leveraging learned representations from large-scale text-to-image training.

title: Learning an Effective Evolution Equation for Particle-Mesh Simulations Across Cosmologies
abstract: Particle-mesh simulations trade small-scale accuracy for speed compared to traditional, computationally expensive N-body codes in cosmological simulations. In this work, we show how a data-driven model could be used to learn an effective evolution equation for the particles, by correcting the errors of the particle-mesh potential incurred on small scales during simulations. We find that our learnt correction yields evolution equations that generalize well to new, unseen initial conditions and cosmologies. We further demonstrate that the resulting corrected maps can be used in a simulation-based inference framework to yield an unbiased inference of cosmological parameters. The model, a network implemented in Fourier space, is exclusively trained on the particle positions and velocities.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly improves particle-mesh cosmological simulations by learning a neural correction to the gravitational potential, enabling faster yet accurate large-scale structure modeling.

conceptDetails.name: particle-mesh correction
conceptDetails.description: Novel core contribution: a data-driven correction to the PM gravitational potential at small scales, learned as a B-spline coefficient network that generalizes across cosmologies.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The corrected particle-mesh maps are validated within an SBI framework to demonstrate unbiased inference of cosmological parameters Ω_m and σ_8.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable simulation
conceptDetails.description: JaxPM formulates the PM solver as a system of ODEs in JAX, enabling gradient backpropagation through the full simulation for end-to-end neural network training.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The correction network is an isotropic filter in Fourier space with sinusoidal activations, explicitly preserving translational and rotational symmetries of the underlying physics.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The neural network acts as a surrogate for the small-scale force corrections missing from the particle-mesh approximation, recovering N-body accuracy at PM cost.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The Fourier-space architecture enforces isotropy so the learned correction respects the physical symmetries of the gravitational potential.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The simulations model collisionless dark matter particle dynamics, using CAMELS IllustrisTNG dark-matter-only runs as training and validation data.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The work is explicitly inspired by the effective field theory of large-scale structure, framing the neural correction as learning the coarse-grained effective evolution equation.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The potential correction is computed entirely in Fourier space using FFTs, and the neural network operates on wavenumber magnitudes |k| to produce spectral corrections.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: SBI applied to the corrected density maps yields unbiased posterior distributions over cosmological parameters, validating the physical fidelity of the learned correction.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A key methodological contribution is training exclusively on particle positions and velocities with L2 norms rather than summary statistics like power spectra, enforcing conservation of momenta.

title: A point cloud approach to generative modeling for galaxy surveys at the field level
abstract: We introduce a diffusion-based generative model to describe the distribution of galaxies in our Universe directly as a collection of points in 3-D space (coordinates) optionally with associated attributes (e.g., velocities and masses), without resorting to binning or voxelization. The custom diffusion model can be used both for emulation, reproducing essential summary statistics of the galaxy distribution, as well as inference, by computing the conditional likelihood of a galaxy field. We demonstrate a first application to massive dark matter haloes in the Quijote simulation suite. This approach can be extended to enable a comprehensive analysis of cosmological data, circumventing limitations inherent to summary statistic -- as well as neural simulation-based inference methods.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The core methodology of the paper, adapting score-based diffusion to model galaxy distributions as point clouds by learning to denoise corrupted spatial configurations.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary contribution is a generative model that captures the full statistical distribution of galaxies at the field level for both emulation and inference.

conceptDetails.name: point cloud diffusion
conceptDetails.description: Novel combination of diffusion generative modeling with point cloud representations for cosmological field-level analysis, avoiding voxelization artifacts.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model computes the conditional likelihood p(x|θ) of an observed galaxy field, enabling parameter inference and hypothesis testing.

conceptDetails.name: field-level inference
conceptDetails.description: The approach enables inference from the full galaxy field rather than compressed summary statistics, preserving information at all scales.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: One of the two neural network backbones used to process point cloud data by constructing graphs over galaxy positions for denoising.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Quijote N-body simulation suite provides training data of dark matter halo catalogs across varying cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The second backbone architecture evaluated, using attention mechanisms to model pairwise interactions between point cloud elements.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Massive dark matter halos from the Quijote simulations are the primary physical object modeled, serving as proxies for observable galaxy distributions.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: One of the two primary use cases: the model reproduces essential summary statistics of the galaxy distribution as a surrogate for expensive simulations.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model learns the joint probability density of galaxy positions and attributes, capturing complex non-Gaussian clustering statistics.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The point cloud representation of galaxies leverages geometric deep learning principles to process permutation-invariant, variable-size spatial data.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly positions its approach as circumventing limitations of neural simulation-based inference methods by providing direct likelihood access.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The conditional likelihood from the generative model is used within a Bayesian framework to estimate cosmological parameter posteriors.

title: Symphony: Symmetry-Equivariant Point-Centered Spherical Harmonics for 3D Molecule Generation
abstract: We present Symphony, an $E(3)$-equivariant autoregressive generative model for 3D molecular geometries that iteratively builds a molecule from molecular fragments.
