Existing autoregressive models such as G-SchNet and G-SphereNet for molecules utilize rotationally invariant features to respect the 3D symmetries of molecules. In contrast, Symphony uses message-passing with higher-degree $E(3)$-equivariant features. This allows a novel representation of probability distributions via spherical harmonic signals to efficiently model the 3D geometry of molecules. We show that Symphony is able to accurately generate small molecules from the QM9 dataset, outperforming existing autoregressive models and approaching the performance of diffusion models.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Symphony's core architecture uses E(3)-equivariant message-passing with higher-degree irreducible representations (Wigner D-matrices) to respect 3D rotational and translational symmetries of molecules.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The model explicitly preserves E(3) symmetry (rotations, reflections, translations) throughout generation, ensuring predictions are physically consistent regardless of molecular orientation.

conceptDetails.name: spherical harmonic signals
conceptDetails.description: A novel representation introduced in this paper where probability distributions over atom positions are encoded as truncated spherical harmonic expansions on radial shells around a focus atom.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Symphony is an autoregressive generative model that iteratively builds 3D molecular geometries by predicting atom types and positions conditioned on previously placed fragments.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies geometric deep learning principles to encode 3D molecular structure, using point-cloud representations and equivariant feature transformations.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The E(3) and SO(3) group structures, Wigner D-matrices, and irreducible representations form the mathematical backbone of the equivariant feature transformations in Symphony.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spherical harmonics serve as an orthonormal basis on S², enabling Symphony to parameterize angular probability distributions of new atom positions as spectral coefficients.

conceptDetails.name: fragment-based generation
conceptDetails.description: Symphony's unique autoregressive strategy constructs molecules by sequentially appending atoms to growing molecular fragments, using a minimum-spanning-tree ordering of training molecules.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Symphony employs equivariant message-passing over molecular graphs to aggregate neighborhood information and compute atom-centered feature embeddings.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model estimates joint radial-angular probability distributions over candidate atom positions using spherical harmonic signal projections at each generation step.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion models serve as the primary performance benchmark, with Symphony approaching their accuracy while using an autoregressive rather than denoising-based framework.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: The molecular generation capability of Symphony is motivated by applications in materials design and drug discovery requiring diverse, chemically valid 3D structures.

title: Quantum Inception Score
abstract: Motivated by the great success of classical generative models in machine learning, enthusiastic exploration of their quantum version has recently started. To depart on this journey, it is important to develop a relevant metric to evaluate the quality of quantum generative models; in the classical case, one such example is the (classical) inception score (cIS). In this paper, as a natural extension of cIS, we propose the quantum inception score (qIS) for quantum generators. Importantly, qIS relates the quality to the Holevo information of the quantum channel that classifies a given dataset. In this context, we show several properties of qIS. First, qIS is greater than or equal to the corresponding cIS, which is defined through projection measurements on the system output. Second, the difference between qIS and cIS arises from the presence of quantum coherence, as characterized by the resource theory of asymmetry. Third, when a set of entangled generators is prepared, there exists a classifying process leading to the further enhancement of qIS. Fourth, we harness the quantum fluctuation theorem to characterize the physical limitation of qIS. Finally, we apply qIS to assess the quality of the one-dimensional spin chain model as a quantum generative model, with the quantum convolutional neural network as a quantum classifier, for the phase classification problem in the quantum many-body physics.

conceptDetails.name: quantum inception score
conceptDetails.description: The central contribution of this paper: a novel metric extending the classical inception score to evaluate the quality and diversity of quantum generative models via Holevo information.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes qIS specifically to evaluate quantum generative models (qGANs, VQAEs, spin chain models), motivated by the success of classical generative modeling.

conceptDetails.name: holevo information
conceptDetails.description: The quantum information-theoretic quantity central to the definition of qIS, linking generative model quality to the channel capacity of the quantum classifier.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates entirely within the quantum computing paradigm, analyzing quantum generators and classifiers as quantum channels and circuits.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Entanglement between generators is shown to enable superadditive channel capacity, leading to further enhancement of qIS beyond the single-generator case.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Quantum states are the outputs of quantum generators being evaluated; the paper tracks how coherence in these states determines the gap between qIS and cIS.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A quantum classifier (here a QCNN) is a core component of the qIS framework, analogous to the Inception network in the classical inception score.

conceptDetails.name: quantum efficacy
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept from the quantum fluctuation theorem framework used to characterize the physical limitation on qIS imposed by decoherence in the quantum classifier.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The quantum convolutional neural network (QCNN) is employed as the quantum classifier in the spin-chain phase classification application of qIS.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Phase classification in a 1D quantum many-body spin chain serves as the demonstration application for evaluating qIS in practice.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: A 1D spin chain model is used as a quantum generative model, situating qIS within the context of quantum simulation of physical systems.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Information-theoretic measures related to the mutual information and channel capacity underpin the qIS formulation, connecting to density and likelihood estimation frameworks.

title: Super Gromov-Witten Invariants via torus localization
abstract: In this article we propose a definition of super Gromov-Witten invariants by postulating a torus localization property for the odd directions of the moduli spaces of super stable maps and super stable curves of genus zero. That is, we define super Gromov-Witten invariants as the integral over the pullback of homology classes along the evaluation maps divided by the equivariant Euler class of the normal bundle of the embedding of the moduli space of stable spin maps into the moduli space of super stable maps. This definition sidesteps the difficulties of defining a supergeometric intersection theory and works with classical intersection theory only. The properties of the normal bundles, known from the differential geometric construction of the moduli space of super stable maps, imply that super Gromov-Witten invariants satisfy a generalization of Kontsevich-Manin axioms and allow for the construction of a super small quantum cohomology ring. We describe a method to calculate super Gromov-Witten invariants of $\mathbb{P}^n$ of genus zero by a further geometric torus localization and give explicit numbers in degree one when dimension and number of marked points are small.

conceptDetails.name: super gromov-witten invariants
conceptDetails.description: The central novel contribution: a definition of supergeometric enumerative invariants via torus localization on moduli spaces of super stable maps.

conceptDetails.name: supergeometric moduli spaces
conceptDetails.description: Moduli spaces of super stable maps and super stable curves of genus zero form the geometric foundation of the paper's construction.

conceptDetails.name: torus localization
conceptDetails.description: The equivariant torus localization theorem is the key technique used to define and compute the super Gromov-Witten invariants without a full supergeometric intersection theory.

conceptDetails.name: quantum cohomology
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs a super small quantum cohomology ring extending classical quantum cohomology as a consequence of the generalized Kontsevich-Manin axioms.

conceptDetails.name: spin structures
conceptDetails.description: Stable spin maps and spin moduli spaces are the classical loci inside the super moduli spaces used to define the invariants via normal bundle data.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Torus actions and equivariant Euler classes are central to the localization technique used to define the invariants.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Super Riemann surfaces and super stable maps are directly motivated by the worldsheet formulation of superstring theory.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends Gromov-Witten theory into a supergeometric setting motivated by superstring theory and supersymmetric quantum field theory.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The C* torus group action on moduli spaces of super stable maps underpins the localization formula for super Gromov-Witten invariants.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Gromov-Witten invariants and their super generalizations are related to counting problems arising in string-theoretic amplitude computations.

title: Probabilistic reconstruction of Dark Matter fields from biased tracers using diffusion models
abstract: Galaxies are biased tracers of the underlying cosmic web, which is dominated by dark matter components that cannot be directly observed. The relationship between dark matter density fields and galaxy distributions can be sensitive to assumptions in cosmology and astrophysical processes embedded in the galaxy formation models, that remain uncertain in many aspects. Based on state-of-the-art galaxy formation simulation suites with varied cosmological parameters and sub-grid astrophysics, we develop a diffusion generative model to predict the unbiased posterior distribution of the underlying dark matter fields from the given stellar mass fields, while being able to marginalize over the uncertainties in cosmology and galaxy formation.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: A variational diffusion model is the core generative architecture used to reconstruct dark matter density fields from stellar mass fields through iterative denoising.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The primary target of reconstruction — the paper aims to recover unobservable dark matter density fields from biased galaxy/stellar tracers.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The model predicts the full posterior distribution p(x_DM | x_stars), enabling probabilistic uncertainty quantification over dark matter field reconstructions.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The CAMELS simulation suite (IllustrisTNG) with 1000+ varied cosmological and astrophysical parameter configurations provides the training data.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The model explicitly marginalizes over uncertainties in cosmological parameters and galaxy formation sub-grid physics to produce calibrated posterior samples.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy-dark matter bias
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly addresses the galaxy bias problem — the complex, uncertain relationship between observable galaxy distributions and underlying dark matter structure.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a conditional generative model framework to synthesize plausible dark matter field realizations consistent with observed stellar fields.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing the unobservable dark matter field from biased galaxy/stellar tracers is fundamentally an ill-posed inverse problem addressed probabilistically.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A U-Net convolutional architecture with downsampling, group normalization, and residual connections serves as the denoising backbone of the diffusion model.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The approach leverages large simulation suites to learn the mapping between observables and latent fields, enabling inference without an explicit likelihood.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Random translations, flips, and permutations with periodic boundary conditions are used to increase effective training data from the simulation suite.

title: Safe but Incalculable: Energy-weighting is not all you need
abstract: Infrared and collinear (IRC) safety has long been used a proxy for robustness when developing new jet substructure observables. This guiding philosophy has been carried into the deep learning era, where IRC-safe neural networks have been used for many jet studies. For graph-based neural networks, the most straightforward way to achieve IRC safety is to weight particle inputs by their energies. However, energy-weighting by itself does not guarantee that perturbative calculations of machine-learned observables will enjoy small non-perturbative corrections. In this paper, we demonstrate the sensitivity of IRC-safe networks to non-perturbative effects, by training an energy flow network (EFN) to maximize its sensitivity to hadronization. We then show how to construct Lipschitz Energy Flow Networks (L-EFNs), which are both IRC safe and relatively insensitive to non-perturbative corrections. We demonstrate the performance of L-EFNs on generated samples of quark and gluon jets, and showcase fascinating differences between the learned latent representations of EFNs and L-EFNs.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on jet substructure observables, specifically developing IRC-safe neural network architectures for quark/gluon jet classification that are robust to non-perturbative effects.

conceptDetails.name: irc safety
conceptDetails.description: Infrared and collinear safety is the foundational theoretical property under investigation; the paper demonstrates that IRC safety via energy-weighting alone is insufficient for perturbative calculability.

conceptDetails.name: non-perturbative corrections
conceptDetails.description: The paper quantifies and minimizes sensitivity to hadronization and other non-perturbative QCD effects, showing EFNs can be maximally sensitive despite being IRC safe.

conceptDetails.name: lipschitz constraint
conceptDetails.description: The central technical innovation of the paper is constraining the Lipschitz norm of EFN sub-networks via spectral normalization, yielding L-EFNs with bounded sensitivity to non-perturbative corrections.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The Energy Mover's Distance, an optimal transport metric, provides the theoretical framework bounding the sensitivity of L-EFNs to non-perturbative corrections via Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The L-EFN architecture is explicitly designed to be robust against non-perturbative effects by bounding the Lipschitz norm, making observables more trustworthy for perturbative QCD calculations.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Energy Flow Networks are built on the deep sets/graph-based framework, and the paper extends this architecture with Lipschitz constraints to create L-EFNs.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates and contrasts the learned latent representations of standard EFNs versus L-EFNs, revealing how the Lipschitz constraint fundamentally changes what the network encodes.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The work is motivated by and applied to high-energy collider physics, where jet substructure observables are used in both measurements and new physics searches.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral normalization of weight matrices is the practical mechanism used to enforce the Lipschitz constraint during training of L-EFNs.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Quark/gluon jet discrimination and parton-level vs. hadron-level classification serve as the empirical benchmarks for evaluating EFN versus L-EFN performance.

title: Two Watts is All You Need: Enabling In-Detector Real-Time Machine Learning for Neutrino Telescopes Via Edge Computing
abstract: The use of machine learning techniques has significantly increased the physics discovery potential of neutrino telescopes. In the upcoming years, we are expecting upgrade of currently existing detectors and new telescopes with novel experimental hardware, yielding more statistics as well as more complicated data signals. This calls out for an upgrade on the software side needed to handle this more complex data in a more efficient way. Specifically, we seek low power and fast software methods to achieve real-time signal processing, where current machine learning methods are too expensive to be deployed in the resource-constrained regions where these experiments are located. We present the first attempt at and a proof-of-concept for enabling machine learning methods to be deployed in-detector for water/ice neutrino telescopes via quantization and deployment on Google Edge Tensor Processing Units (TPUs). We design a recursive neural network with a residual convolutional embedding, and adapt a quantization process to deploy the algorithm on a Google Edge TPU. This algorithm can achieve similar reconstruction accuracy compared with traditional GPU-based machine learning solutions while requiring the same amount of power compared with CPU-based regression solutions, combining the high accuracy and low power advantages and enabling real-time in-detector machine learning in even the most power-restricted environments.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets water/ice Cherenkov neutrino telescopes (IceCube, KM3NeT, etc.) as the primary deployment environment for real-time ML inference.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The core task is reconstructing neutrino event parameters (energy, direction) in real time directly within the detector.

conceptDetails.name: in-detector ml deployment
conceptDetails.description: The novel contribution of running trained ML models physically inside the detector in real time, eliminating the need for off-site GPU clusters.

conceptDetails.name: model quantization
conceptDetails.description: Post-training and quantization-aware quantization of weights and activations to 8-bit integers is the key technique enabling Edge TPU deployment.

conceptDetails.name: edge tpu inference
conceptDetails.description: Google Edge TPU hardware is used as the low-power (2 W) inference accelerator, representing the paper's primary hardware contribution.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: The authors design a recursive neural network (RNN) as the backbone architecture for sequential neutrino hit processing.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A residual convolutional embedding encodes raw detector hits before passing them to the recurrent backbone.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: Enabling real-time in-detector triggering is a primary motivation, replacing slow CPU-based regression triggers with accurate ML inference.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: A quantization-aware fine-tuning procedure is applied after initial training to adapt the model for integer-only Edge TPU execution.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The residual convolutional module acts as a learned feature extractor that compresses raw optical module data into dense representations.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Simulated WaterHex and IceHex detector geometries using the Prometheus tool provide training and evaluation data for the network.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Deploying within 2 W power budgets addresses scalability to resource-constrained next-generation detectors like IceCube-Gen2 and TRIDENT.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The model performs regression over neutrino energy and direction, benchmarked against traditional CPU-based regression solutions.

title: T-Duality and Flavor Symmetries in Little String Theories
abstract: We explore the T-duality web of 6D Heterotic Little String Theories, focusing on flavor algebra reducing deformations. A careful analysis of the full flavor algebra, including Abelian factors, shows that the flavor rank is preserved under T-duality. This suggests a new T-duality invariant in addition to the Coulomb branch dimension and the two-group structure constants. We also engineer Little String Theories with non-simply laced flavor algebras, whose appearance we attribute to certain discrete 3-form fluxes in M-theory. Geometrically, these theories are engineered in F-theory with non-Kähler favorable K3 fibers. This geometric origin leads us to propose that freezing fluxes are preserved across T-duality. Along the way, we discuss various exotic models, including two inequivalent $\text{Spin(32)}/\mathbb{Z}_2$ models that are dual to the same $\text{E}_8 \times \text{E}_8$ theory, and a family of self-T-dual models.

conceptDetails.name: t-duality
conceptDetails.description: The central focus of the paper; the authors map the full T-duality web of 6D heterotic LSTs and identify new invariants including flavor rank preservation.

conceptDetails.name: little string theories
conceptDetails.description: Non-gravitational UV-complete theories at string scale in 6D exhibiting T-duality and global symmetries; the primary objects of study throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within heterotic string theory, F-theory, and M-theory frameworks to geometrically engineer and analyze 6D Little String Theories and their T-duality webs.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Lie algebra classification—including simply and non-simply laced algebras, Abelian factors, and 2-group symmetries—is central to characterizing flavor symmetries and their T-duality invariance.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies 6D supersymmetric quantum field theories with eight supercharges, analyzing their anomaly polynomials, moduli spaces, and UV completions.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Flavor algebra reduction via Wilson line deformations and holonomies along compactification circles is a primary mechanism studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: freezing fluxes
conceptDetails.description: Discrete 3-form fluxes in M-theory responsible for engineering non-simply laced flavor algebras in LSTs; the paper proposes these are preserved across T-duality.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: 6D superconformal field theories are analyzed as a closely related class and as decoupling limits of LSTs, informing the broader classification.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: LSTs are characterized through their low-energy effective descriptions including tensor multiplet spectra, gauge sectors, and anomaly-constrained structures.

title: Pairing-based graph neural network for simulating quantum materials
abstract: We develop a pairing-based graph neural network for simulating quantum many-body systems. Our architecture augments a BCS-type geminal wavefunction with a generalized pair amplitude parameterized by a graph neural network. Variational Monte Carlo with our neural network simultaneously provides an accurate, flexible, and scalable method for simulating many-electron systems. We apply this method to two-dimensional semiconductor electron-hole bilayers and obtain accurate results on a variety of interaction-induced phases, including the exciton Bose-Einstein condensate, electron-hole superconductor, and bilayer Wigner crystal. Our study demonstrates the potential of physically-motivated neural network wavefunctions for quantum materials simulations.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Core neural architecture used to parameterize the generalized pair amplitude in the geminal wavefunction, enabling flexible and scalable quantum state representation.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The primary goal of the paper: simulating quantum many-body systems, specifically 2D electron-hole bilayers with interaction-induced phases.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo is the central optimization framework used to minimize energy of the neural network wavefunction ansatz.

conceptDetails.name: geminal wavefunction
conceptDetails.description: The BCS-type number-projected pair wavefunction (Geminal/GemiNet) that serves as the physics-motivated structural backbone of the neural network ansatz.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Neural network wavefunctions are used to represent complex many-body quantum states including exciton condensates, superconductors, and Wigner crystals.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The architecture is explicitly motivated by BCS mean-field physics, augmenting a geminal wavefunction with a GNN-parameterized pair amplitude.

conceptDetails.name: neural network wavefunction
conceptDetails.description: Neural-network-parameterized quantum wavefunctions that can represent arbitrary many-body states including strongly correlated phases without handcrafted bias.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper maps out BEC-BCS crossover and Wigner crystal phase transitions in the electron-hole bilayer phase diagram.

conceptDetails.name: electron-hole bilayer
conceptDetails.description: The 2D semiconductor quantum material system studied, featuring separately tunable electron and hole layers with rich interaction-induced phases.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Physical symmetries (translational invariance, exchange antisymmetry) are built into the wavefunction ansatz to ensure physically valid quantum states.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Transfer learning enables scalability of the trained GNN wavefunction to larger system sizes without retraining from scratch.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key claimed advantage of the GemiNet architecture over conventional VMC methods is its scalability to large many-electron systems.

title: E(2) Equivariant Neural Networks for Robust Galaxy Morphology Classification
abstract: We propose the use of group convolutional neural network architectures (GCNNs) equivariant to the 2D Euclidean group, $E(2)$, for the task of galaxy morphology classification by utilizing symmetries of the data present in galaxy images as an inductive bias in the architecture. We conduct robustness studies by introducing artificial perturbations via Poisson noise insertion and one-pixel adversarial attacks to simulate the effects of limited observational capabilities. We train, validate, and test GCNNs equivariant to discrete subgroups of $E(2)$ - the cyclic and dihedral groups of order $N$ - on the Galaxy10 DECals dataset and find that GCNNs achieve higher classification accuracy and are consistently more robust than their non-equivariant counterparts, with an architecture equivariant to the group $D_{16}$ achieving a $95.52 \pm 0.18\%$ test-set accuracy. We also find that the model loses $<6\%$ accuracy on a $50\%$-noise dataset and all GCNNs are less susceptible to one-pixel perturbations than an identically constructed CNN. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/snehjp2/GCNNMorphology.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs GCNNs equivariant to discrete subgroups of E(2) as the core architectural contribution for galaxy morphology classification.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The cyclic and dihedral groups CN and DN are used as discrete subgroups of E(2) to define the symmetry structure of the network architectures.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: The primary application task is classifying galaxy morphology into 10 classes using the Galaxy10 DECals dataset.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The work applies geometric deep learning principles by encoding spatial symmetries of galaxy images as inductive biases in the neural architecture.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Symmetries of the 2D Euclidean group (rotations, translations, reflections) are preserved as inductive biases to improve generalization and robustness.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The paper extensively studies model robustness to Poisson noise insertion and one-pixel adversarial attacks, finding GCNNs consistently outperform CNNs under perturbations.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Group convolutional neural networks (GCNNs) with specialized equivariant filters form the backbone architecture compared against standard CNN baselines.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Multi-class image classification of galaxy morphology is the central supervised learning task evaluated across all model variants.

conceptDetails.name: adversarial robustness
conceptDetails.description: One-pixel adversarial attacks via a differentiable genetic algorithm are used to probe model vulnerability to worst-case single-pixel perturbations.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: Robustness studies simulate out-of-distribution scenarios via artificial perturbations to evaluate model reliability under realistic observational degradation.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Random rotations, translations, and center-crops are applied during training to ensure fair comparison between equivariant and non-equivariant models.

title: Learning to See Physical Properties with Active Sensing Motor Policies
abstract: Knowledge of terrain's physical properties inferred from color images can aid in making efficient robotic locomotion plans. However, unlike image classification, it is unintuitive for humans to label image patches with physical properties. Without labeled data, building a vision system that takes as input the observed terrain and predicts physical properties remains challenging. We present a method that overcomes this challenge by self-supervised labeling of images captured by robots during real-world traversal with physical property estimators trained in simulation. To ensure accurate labeling, we introduce Active Sensing Motor Policies (ASMP), which are trained to explore locomotion behaviors that increase the accuracy of estimating physical parameters. For instance, the quadruped robot learns to swipe its foot against the ground to estimate the friction coefficient accurately. We show that the visual system trained with a small amount of real-world traversal data accurately predicts physical parameters. The trained system is robust and works even with overhead images captured by a drone despite being trained on data collected by cameras attached to a quadruped robot walking on the ground.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Core methodology for generating terrain property labels from proprioceptive traversal data without human annotation.

conceptDetails.name: active terrain sensing
conceptDetails.description: Novel policy paradigm where the robot adopts emergent behaviors (e.g., foot swiping) specifically to improve physical property estimation accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Used to train Active Sensing Motor Policies that optimize locomotion behaviors for maximizing terrain property estimation accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: The Active Sensing Motor Policy actively selects informative locomotion behaviors to improve the quality of proprioceptive terrain labels.

conceptDetails.name: proprioceptive state estimation
conceptDetails.description: Robot proprioceptive sensor history is used to infer terrain dynamics parameters such as friction coefficient and roughness.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: Physical property estimators and motor policies trained in simulation are applied to real-world robot traversal data.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: An estimation reward signal incentivizes the policy to adopt behaviors that maximize terrain parameter observability.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: A visual perception module learns to map terrain images to physical property representations grounded in real-world physics.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Estimating terrain physical parameters (friction, roughness) from proprioceptive sensor history is an inverse problem solved via sim-trained estimators.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The vision model trained on robot-mounted camera data transfers successfully to overhead drone imagery from novel viewpoints.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Simulation rollouts are used to learn cost functions mapping terrain dynamics parameters to performance metrics for planning.

title: Metric Flows with Neural Networks
abstract: We develop a general theory of flows in the space of Riemannian metrics induced by neural network gradient descent. This is motivated in part by recent advances in approximating Calabi-Yau metrics with neural networks and is enabled by recent advances in understanding flows in the space of neural networks. We derive the corresponding metric flow equations, which are governed by a metric neural tangent kernel, a complicated, non-local object that evolves in time. However, many architectures admit an infinite-width limit in which the kernel becomes fixed and the dynamics simplify. Additional assumptions can induce locality in the flow, which allows for the realization of Perelman's formulation of Ricci flow that was used to resolve the 3d Poincaré conjecture. We demonstrate that such fixed kernel regimes lead to poor learning of numerical Calabi-Yau metrics, as is expected since the associated neural networks do not learn features. Conversely, we demonstrate that well-learned numerical metrics at finite-width exhibit an evolving metric-NTK, associated with feature learning. Our theory of neural network metric flows therefore explains why neural networks are better at learning Calabi-Yau metrics than fixed kernel methods, such as the Ricci flow.

conceptDetails.name: metric neural tangent kernel
conceptDetails.description: A novel kernel object derived in this paper that governs metric flows induced by neural network gradient descent; it is non-local and time-dependent in general, becoming fixed in the infinite-width limit.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau metric learning
conceptDetails.description: Numerical approximation of Ricci-flat Kähler metrics on compact manifolds using neural networks, the primary empirical application driving the theoretical development.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Fixed kernel methods, including the infinite-width NTK regime, are analyzed and shown to underperform finite-width neural networks for Calabi-Yau metric learning due to lack of feature learning.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau metrics are central objects in string theory compactification, providing the primary physical motivation for learning Riemannian metrics with neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies deep learning to the space of Riemannian metrics, developing a mathematical framework connecting neural network optimization to differential geometry.

conceptDetails.name: neural ricci flow
conceptDetails.description: Perelman's formulation of Ricci flow is shown to emerge as a special case of neural network metric flows under infinite-width and locality assumptions, contextualizing classical geometric flows within the new theory.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Feature learning in finite-width networks is identified as the key mechanism enabling superior Calabi-Yau metric approximation over fixed-kernel methods that do not learn features.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper is fundamentally concerned with learning and representing metrics on Riemannian manifolds, connecting neural network representations to the geometry of metric spaces.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks are used to represent and approximate physical geometric objects (metrics), with loss functionals encoding differential-geometric constraints such as Ricci-flatness.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Neural network parameterization of Riemannian metrics is studied through the lens of representation learning, distinguishing fixed-kernel (no feature learning) from finite-width (feature learning) regimes.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The choice of loss functional is shown to determine which geometric flow is realized; specific choices recover Perelman's Ricci flow gradient flow formulation.

title: Precise Cosmological Constraints from BOSS Galaxy Clustering with a Simulation-Based Emulator of the Wavelet Scattering Transform
abstract: We perform a reanalysis of the BOSS CMASS DR12 galaxy dataset using a simulation-based emulator for the Wavelet Scattering Transform (WST) coefficients. Moving beyond our previous works, which laid the foundation for the first galaxy clustering application of this estimator, we construct a neural net-based emulator for the cosmological dependence of the WST coefficients and the 2-point correlation function multipoles, trained from the state-of-the-art suite of \textsc{AbacusSummit} simulations combined with a flexible Halo Occupation Distribution (HOD) galaxy model. In order to confirm the accuracy of our pipeline, we subject it to a series of thorough internal and external mock parameter recovery tests, before applying it to reanalyze the CMASS observations in the redshift range $0.46<z<0.57$. We find that a joint WST + 2-point correlation function likelihood analysis allows us to obtain marginalized 1$σ$ errors on the $Λ$CDM parameters that are tighter by a factor of $2.5-6$, compared to the 2-point correlation function, and by a factor of $1.4-2.5$ compared to the WST-only results. This corresponds to a competitive $0.9\%$, $2.3\%$ and $1\%$ level of determination for parameters $ω_c$, $σ_8$ $\&$ $n_s$, respectively, and also to a $0.7\%$ $\&$ $2.5 \%$ constraint on derived parameters h and $f(z)σ_8(z)$, in agreement with the \textit{Planck} 2018 results. Our results reaffirm the constraining power of the WST and highlight the exciting prospect of employing higher-order statistics in order to fully exploit the power of upcoming Stage-IV spectroscopic observations.

conceptDetails.name: wavelet scattering transform
conceptDetails.description: The WST is the central novel statistical estimator applied to galaxy clustering data, providing higher-order non-Gaussian summary statistics that substantially tighten cosmological parameter constraints.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses simulation-based emulation to model the cosmological dependence of WST coefficients, replacing analytic approximations with neural net-based forward models trained on AbacusSummit simulations.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: A neural network emulator is constructed to efficiently predict WST coefficients and 2-point correlation function multipoles across the cosmological parameter space without running full simulations at inference time.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling is used within a Bayesian framework to obtain posterior constraints on ΛCDM cosmological parameters from the WST and 2PCF data vectors.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The Wavelet Scattering Transform extracts interpretable higher-order summary statistics from the galaxy density field, capturing non-Gaussian information beyond the 2-point function.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The AbacusSummit suite of N-body simulations provides the training data for the emulator, spanning a broad grid of cosmological and HOD parameters.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline produces marginalized posterior distributions on ΛCDM and nuisance parameters, enabling precise cosmological inference from galaxy survey data.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Extensive internal and external mock parameter recovery tests are performed to validate the emulator accuracy before applying it to real BOSS CMASS data.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Constraining ΛCDM parameters including those related to dark energy is a primary science goal of the BOSS galaxy clustering reanalysis.

conceptDetails.name: halo occupation distribution
conceptDetails.description: A flexible HOD galaxy-halo connection model is used to marginalize over galaxy formation uncertainties when inferring cosmological parameters from the simulations.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The cold dark matter density parameter ωc is one of the key cosmological parameters constrained with sub-percent precision in this analysis.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The WST is architecturally analogous to a CNN, performing successive wavelet convolutions and modulus operations, providing an interpretable middle ground between traditional statistics and deep learning.

title: Feature Learning and Generalization in Deep Networks with Orthogonal Weights
abstract: Fully-connected deep neural networks with weights initialized from independent Gaussian distributions can be tuned to criticality, which prevents the exponential growth or decay of signals propagating through the network. However, such networks still exhibit fluctuations that grow linearly with the depth of the network, which may impair the training of networks with width comparable to depth. We show analytically that rectangular networks with tanh activations and weights initialized from the ensemble of orthogonal matrices have corresponding preactivation fluctuations which are independent of depth, to leading order in inverse width. Moreover, we demonstrate numerically that, at initialization, all correlators involving the neural tangent kernel (NTK) and its descendants at leading order in inverse width -- which govern the evolution of observables during training -- saturate at a depth of $\sim 20$, rather than growing without bound as in the case of Gaussian initializations. We speculate that this structure preserves finite-width feature learning while reducing overall noise, thus improving both generalization and training speed in deep networks with depth comparable to width. We provide some experimental justification by relating empirical measurements of the NTK to the superior performance of deep nonlinear orthogonal networks trained under full-batch gradient descent on the MNIST and CIFAR-10 classification tasks.

conceptDetails.name: orthogonal initialization
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: initializing MLP weights from orthogonal matrix ensembles to suppress depth-dependent fluctuations and improve training in deep networks.

conceptDetails.name: neural tangent kernel
conceptDetails.description: The NTK and its descendant correlators are the primary observables studied, governing gradient descent dynamics and feature learning at finite width.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The NTK framework is a kernel-based description of neural network training dynamics that the paper analyzes under orthogonal vs. Gaussian initialization.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Finite-width feature learning—captured by NTK evolution during training—is argued to be preserved under orthogonal initialization while overall noise is reduced.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses how performance and fluctuations scale with depth-to-width ratio L/n, identifying regimes where orthogonal initialization outperforms Gaussian.

conceptDetails.name: critical initialization
conceptDetails.description: The concept of tuning initialization hyperparameters to criticality to prevent exploding/vanishing gradients is the baseline framework this paper extends.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Orthogonal matrix ensembles are characterized by their spectral properties, and the analysis leverages singular value structure of weight matrices and the input-output Jacobian.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The paper performs ensemble-averaged statistical analysis of preactivation distributions and correlators over randomly initialized networks.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: MNIST and CIFAR-10 classification benchmarks are used to empirically validate the theoretical predictions about generalization and training speed.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Theoretical results are framed as expectations over an ensemble of networks with fixed data, enabling analytic computation of multi-point correlators.

title: Growing Brains: Co-emergence of Anatomical and Functional Modularity in Recurrent Neural Networks
abstract: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) trained on compositional tasks can exhibit functional modularity, in which neurons can be clustered by activity similarity and participation in shared computational subtasks. Unlike brains, these RNNs do not exhibit anatomical modularity, in which functional clustering is correlated with strong recurrent coupling and spatial localization of functional clusters. Contrasting with functional modularity, which can be ephemerally dependent on the input, anatomically modular networks form a robust substrate for solving the same subtasks in the future. To examine whether it is possible to grow brain-like anatomical modularity, we apply a recent machine learning method, brain-inspired modular training (BIMT), to a network being trained to solve a set of compositional cognitive tasks. We find that functional and anatomical clustering emerge together, such that functionally similar neurons also become spatially localized and interconnected. Moreover, compared to standard $L_1$ or no regularization settings, the model exhibits superior performance by optimally balancing task performance and network sparsity. In addition to achieving brain-like organization in RNNs, our findings also suggest that BIMT holds promise for applications in neuromorphic computing and enhancing the interpretability of neural network architectures.

conceptDetails.name: anatomical modularity
conceptDetails.description: The co-emergence of spatial localization and recurrent connectivity alignment with functional clusters is the central phenomenon demonstrated in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: brain-inspired modular training
conceptDetails.description: BIMT, which uses distance-dependent weight regularization and neuron swapping to enforce spatial locality, is the core method applied to grow brain-like organization in RNNs.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: RNNs are the primary architecture studied, trained on compositional cognitive tasks to observe the emergence of anatomical and functional modularity.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The RNN is trained simultaneously on 20 compositional cognitive tasks drawn from a shared pool of computational primitives to induce functional specialization.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Neurons are clustered by activity similarity and spatial position to identify functional and anatomical modules that co-emerge under BIMT.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: BIMT achieves network sparsity by pruning peripheral neurons and minimizing wiring length, outperforming standard L1 regularization on the performance–sparsity Pareto frontier.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The BIMT loss combines prediction loss with a connection cost penalty (vanilla L1 plus distance-aware regularization) to enforce local connectivity.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Anatomically modular organization produced by BIMT increases the interpretability of neural network computations by spatially segregating functional sub-circuits.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Neurons learn spatially structured representations in which proximity in an embedded 2D grid correlates with functional similarity across tasks.

conceptDetails.name: neuromorphic computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes BIMT-trained anatomically modular RNNs as a promising substrate for neuromorphic hardware implementations.

title: Search for heavy neutral leptons in electron-positron and neutral-pion final states with the MicroBooNE detector
abstract: We present the first search for heavy neutral leptons (HNL) decaying into $νe^+e^-$ or $νπ^0$ final states in a liquid-argon time projection chamber using data collected with the MicroBooNE detector. The data were recorded synchronously with the NuMI neutrino beam from Fermilab's Main Injector corresponding to a total exposure of $7.01 \times 10^{20}$ protons on target. We set upper limits at the $90\%$ confidence level on the mixing parameter $\lvert U_{μ4}\rvert^2$ in the mass ranges $10\le m_{\rm HNL}\le 150$ MeV for the $νe^+e^-$ channel and $150\le m_{\rm HNL}\le 245$ MeV for the $νπ^0$ channel, assuming $\lvert U_{e 4}\rvert^2 = \lvert U_{τ4}\rvert^2 = 0$. These limits represent the most stringent constraints in the mass range $35<m_{\rm HNL}<175$ MeV and the first constraints from a direct search for $νπ^0$ decays.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core purpose is searching for heavy neutral leptons as signatures of physics beyond the Standard Model using the MicroBooNE detector.

conceptDetails.name: heavy neutral leptons
conceptDetails.description: Novel BSM particles introduced via PMNS matrix extension that can explain neutrino masses, leptogenesis, and dark matter, and are the target of this search.

conceptDetails.name: lepton mixing parameters
conceptDetails.description: The primary result of the paper is setting the most stringent constraints on the HNL-muon mixing parameter |Uμ4|² in the mass range 35–175 MeV.

conceptDetails.name: liquid-argon tpc
conceptDetails.description: The liquid-argon time projection chamber technology provides precision 3D imaging enabling the full reconstruction of HNL decay topologies, making this search possible.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: MicroBooNE is a liquid-argon TPC designed for neutrino detection, and the NuMI neutrino beam provides the source of potential HNL production for this search.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Full 3D reconstruction of electromagnetic shower topologies (νe+e- and νπ0 final states) in the liquid-argon TPC is central to identifying HNL decay candidates.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Boosted decision trees (BDTs) are the primary analysis tool used to discriminate HNL signal from Standard Model backgrounds in both decay channels.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Upper limits at 90% confidence level on the mixing parameter |Uμ4|² are derived using statistical hypothesis testing across the HNL mass range.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: BDTs, which are ensemble classifiers, are employed within a unified analysis framework to handle the topological similarity between νe+e- and νπ0 final states.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations of detector response are essential for modeling signal efficiency and background contributions in the LArTPC.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo event generators and detector simulations are used extensively to predict signal and background yields for the statistical analysis.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for minimal SM extensions via the PMNS matrix that introduce HNL mass eigenstates with suppressed mixing to active neutrino states.

title: Functional renormalization group for signal detection and stochastic ergodicity breaking
abstract: Signal detection is one of the main challenges of data science. As it often happens in data analysis, the signal in the data may be corrupted by noise. There is a wide range of techniques aimed at extracting the relevant degrees of freedom from data. However, some problems remain difficult. It is notably the case of signal detection in almost continuous spectra when the signal-to-noise ratio is small enough. This paper follows a recent bibliographic line which tackles this issue with field-theoretical methods. Previous analysis focused on equilibrium Boltzmann distributions for some effective field representing the degrees of freedom of data. It was possible to establish a relation between signal detection and $\mathbb{Z}_2$-symmetry breaking. In this paper, we consider a stochastic field framework inspiring by the so-called "Model A", and show that the ability to reach or not an equilibrium state is correlated with the shape of the dataset. In particular, studying the renormalization group of the model, we show that the weak ergodicity prescription is always broken for signals small enough, when the data distribution is close to the Marchenko-Pastur (MP) law. This, in particular, enables the definition of a detection threshold in the regime where the signal-to-noise ratio is small enough.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: The functional renormalization group (FRG) is the central method used to study how effective couplings evolve with scale, enabling detection thresholds to be identified.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The primary application: detecting signals corrupted by noise in nearly continuous spectra where signal-to-noise ratio is small.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic ergodicity breaking
conceptDetails.description: Novel phenomenon where weak ergodicity is always broken for small enough signals near the Marchenko-Pastur distribution, defining a detection threshold.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Signal detection is mapped onto a symmetry-breaking phase transition (Z2-symmetry breaking), with ergodicity breaking as a new phase transition marker.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs a stochastic field framework inspired by Model A dynamics, using the Martin-Siggia-Rose formalism for path integral representation.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Z2-symmetry breaking serves as the order parameter distinguishing signal from noise in the equilibrium field theory framework.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Eigenvalue spectra of data covariance matrices and their proximity to the Marchenko-Pastur law are central to characterizing the noise distribution.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: An effective field theory is constructed for data statistics, with the 2-point function identified with the data covariance matrix.

conceptDetails.name: marchenko-pastur universality
conceptDetails.description: The universal spectral law of large Wishart random matrices that characterizes purely noisy data and anchors the field theory construction.

conceptDetails.name: martin-siggia-rose formalism
conceptDetails.description: Path integral formalism used to represent the stochastic dynamics of the field model, enabling functional RG analysis of non-equilibrium behavior.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Signal detection in noisy spectra is framed as identifying anomalous structure (signal) against a universal noise background.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Numerical flow analysis is performed to track RG trajectories and validate analytical results about ergodicity breaking.

title: The Geometry of Truth: Emergent Linear Structure in Large Language Model Representations of True/False Datasets
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have impressive capabilities, but are prone to outputting falsehoods. Recent work has developed techniques for inferring whether a LLM is telling the truth by training probes on the LLM's internal activations. However, this line of work is controversial, with some authors pointing out failures of these probes to generalize in basic ways, among other conceptual issues. In this work, we use high-quality datasets of simple true/false statements to study in detail the structure of LLM representations of truth, drawing on three lines of evidence: 1. Visualizations of LLM true/false statement representations, which reveal clear linear structure. 2. Transfer experiments in which probes trained on one dataset generalize to different datasets. 3. Causal evidence obtained by surgically intervening in a LLM's forward pass, causing it to treat false statements as true and vice versa. Overall, we present evidence that at sufficient scale, LLMs linearly represent the truth or falsehood of factual statements. We also show that simple difference-in-mean probes generalize as well as other probing techniques while identifying directions which are more causally implicated in model outputs.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how LLMs internally represent the truth or falsehood of factual statements as linear directions in their latent space.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is mechanistic interpretability analysis of how truth is encoded in LLM activations using probes and causal interventions.

conceptDetails.name: truth geometry
conceptDetails.description: The emergent linear geometric structure of truth representations in high-dimensional LLM activation space is the central phenomenon studied.

conceptDetails.name: linear truth probing
conceptDetails.description: Novel finding that difference-in-mean linear probes identify causally implicated truth directions that generalize across diverse datasets.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The study uses LLaMA-2 autoregressive transformers as the primary model architecture for investigating truth representations.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies the geometry of hidden state embeddings to identify a 'truth direction' in LLM representation space.

conceptDetails.name: causal intervention
conceptDetails.description: Surgical interventions in the LLM forward pass (activation patching) are used to causally verify that identified truth directions control model outputs.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Linear probes are trained to classify the truth or falsehood of statements from LLM activations.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: PCA is used to visualize the structure of LLM true/false statement representations, revealing clear linear geometry.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Probe generalization experiments test whether truth-probes trained on one dataset transfer to structurally and topically different datasets.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper finds that linear truth representations emerge with model scale, with larger models having more abstract and generalizable notions of truth.

title: Grokking as Compression: A Nonlinear Complexity Perspective
abstract: We attribute grokking, the phenomenon where generalization is much delayed after memorization, to compression. To do so, we define linear mapping number (LMN) to measure network complexity, which is a generalized version of linear region number for ReLU networks. LMN can nicely characterize neural network compression before generalization. Although the $L_2$ norm has been a popular choice for characterizing model complexity, we argue in favor of LMN for a number of reasons: (1) LMN can be naturally interpreted as information/computation, while $L_2$ cannot. (2) In the compression phase, LMN has linear relations with test losses, while $L_2$ is correlated with test losses in a complicated nonlinear way. (3) LMN also reveals an intriguing phenomenon of the XOR network switching between two generalization solutions, while $L_2$ does not. Besides explaining grokking, we argue that LMN is a promising candidate as the neural network version of the Kolmogorov complexity since it explicitly considers local or conditioned linear computations aligned with the nature of modern artificial neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: linear mapping number
conceptDetails.description: The core novel metric introduced in this paper, measuring network nonlinear complexity as a generalized linear region count using Von Neumann entropy of a linear connectivity matrix.

conceptDetails.name: grokking
conceptDetails.description: The phenomenon of delayed generalization long after memorization, which this paper attributes to a compression phase measurable by LMN.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Von Neumann entropy is computed from the eigenvalue spectrum of the linear connectivity matrix to derive LMN as a soft cluster count.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: LMN is proposed as a more interpretable complexity measure than L2 norm, admitting a natural information/computation interpretation for neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Eigenvalue structure of the linear connectivity matrix is analyzed to quantify the nonlinear complexity of the network at different training stages.

conceptDetails.name: nonlinear complexity
conceptDetails.description: A novel framework connecting LMN to Kolmogorov complexity, providing an information-theoretic grounding for measuring effective neural network complexity.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Counting linear mappings is cast as a soft clustering problem on the linear connectivity similarity matrix, motivating the entropy-based LMN formula.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Pearson r² from linear regression is used to measure linear connectivity between sample pairs in input-output space, forming the entries of the connectivity matrix.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates how LMN and L2 norm correlate with test loss dynamics during the memorization-to-generalization transition.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Generalization emerges as the network compresses toward a sparser, more efficient solution, which LMN captures as decreasing linear mapping count.

title: A Neural Scaling Law from Lottery Ticket Ensembling
abstract: Neural scaling laws (NSL) refer to the phenomenon where model performance improves with scale. Sharma & Kaplan analyzed NSL using approximation theory and predict that MSE losses decay as $N^{-α}$, $α=4/d$, where $N$ is the number of model parameters, and $d$ is the intrinsic input dimension. Although their theory works well for some cases (e.g., ReLU networks), we surprisingly find that a simple 1D problem $y=x^2$ manifests a different scaling law ($α=1$) from their predictions ($α=4$). We opened the neural networks and found that the new scaling law originates from lottery ticket ensembling: a wider network on average has more "lottery tickets", which are ensembled to reduce the variance of outputs. We support the ensembling mechanism by mechanistically interpreting single neural networks, as well as studying them statistically. We attribute the $N^{-1}$ scaling law to the "central limit theorem" of lottery tickets. Finally, we discuss its potential implications for large language models and statistical physics-type theories of learning.

conceptDetails.name: neural scaling laws
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon under study: MSE loss decays as N^{-α} with model size, and the paper identifies a novel α=1 regime unexplained by existing approximation or quanta theories.

conceptDetails.name: lottery ticket ensembling
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core proposed mechanism: wider networks implicitly contain more lottery ticket sub-networks whose outputs are ensembled during training, driving the N^{-1} scaling law.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Aggregation of multiple lottery ticket sub-network predictions reduces output variance analogously to bagging, but emerges organically from training rather than being manually designed.

conceptDetails.name: variance reduction
conceptDetails.description: The N^{-1} scaling is attributed to variance reduction from ensembling n∝N lottery tickets, with the central limit theorem predicting n^{-1} variance decay.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Mechanistic reverse engineering of trained networks—identifying symmetric neuron pairs and loss histogram peaks—is the primary investigative method used to uncover the lottery ticket structure.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically studies how training loss scales with network width N over several orders of magnitude, characterizing the scaling exponent α under different activation functions.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Lottery tickets are sparse sub-networks within a larger network that independently achieve good performance; their prevalence and structure underpin the new scaling mechanism.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: A central-limit-theorem analysis of lottery ticket populations, treating each ticket as an independent random variable, provides the statistical foundation for the N^{-1} scaling prediction.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Fitting y=x² with a two-layer network under MSE loss serves as the minimal benchmark that empirically reveals the new scaling law and enables clean mechanistic analysis.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: ReLU networks exhibit a crossover from N^{-4} to N^{-1} scaling as width increases, which the paper frames in the context of statistical physics-like theories of learning.

title: Discovering Symmetry Breaking in Physical Systems with Relaxed Group Convolution
abstract: Modeling symmetry breaking is essential for understanding the fundamental changes in the behaviors and properties of physical systems, from microscopic particle interactions to macroscopic phenomena like fluid dynamics and cosmic structures. Thus, identifying sources of asymmetry is an important tool for understanding physical systems. In this paper, we focus on learning asymmetries of data using relaxed group convolutions. We provide both theoretical and empirical evidence that this flexible convolution technique allows the model to maintain the highest level of equivariance that is consistent with data and discover the subtle symmetry-breaking factors in various physical systems. We employ various relaxed group convolution architectures to uncover various symmetry-breaking factors that are interpretable and physically meaningful in different physical systems, including the phase transition of crystal structure, the isotropy and homogeneity breaking in turbulent flow, and the time-reversal symmetry breaking in pendulum systems.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The core architectural framework extended in this paper; relaxed group convolutions generalize strict equivariant networks to allow data-driven symmetry breaking detection.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon studied; the paper categorizes explicit, spontaneous, functional, and distributional symmetry breaking and provides methods to discover it from data.

conceptDetails.name: relaxed group convolution
conceptDetails.description: The core novel method: group convolutions with trainable equivariant weights that relax strict weight-sharing, enabling models to learn approximate symmetries and detect symmetry breaking.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical foundation for relaxed group convolutions, weight-sharing schemes, and the formal treatment of equivariance under symmetry groups.

conceptDetails.name: approximate equivariance
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical concept formalized in this work; relaxed weights allow a model to interpolate between full equivariance and no symmetry constraint, adapting to the data's true symmetry level.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: One of the key physical applications; relaxed group convolutions are used to detect and characterize crystal structure phase transitions as a symmetry-breaking phenomenon.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The broader field in which this work sits, designing neural architectures that respect geometric symmetries of physical data.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The relaxed weights are interpreted post-training to identify physically meaningful symmetry-breaking factors such as gravity, boundary conditions, and time-reversal asymmetry.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The base architectural building block; group convolutions on regular grids are extended to relaxed group convolutions that learn approximate symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: One of three physical systems studied; the paper uses relaxed group convolution to discover symmetry-breaking factors during crystal phase transitions.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper shows relaxed group convolutions maintain the highest level of equivariance consistent with data, preserving symmetry where appropriate rather than fully breaking it.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: A downstream task used to benchmark relaxed group convolution; fluid super-resolution on 3D channel flow and isotropic turbulence demonstrates performance gains.

title: Cosmological constraints from density-split clustering in the BOSS CMASS galaxy sample
abstract: We present a clustering analysis of the BOSS DR12 CMASS galaxy sample, combining measurements of the galaxy two-point correlation function and density-split clustering down to a scale of $1\,h^{-1}{\rm Mpc}$. Our theoretical framework is based on emulators trained on high-fidelity mock galaxy catalogues that forward model the cosmological dependence of the clustering statistics within an extended-$Λ$CDM framework, including redshift-space and Alcock-Paczynski distortions. Our base-$Λ$CDM analysis finds $ω_{\rm cdm} = 0.1201\pm 0.0022$, $σ_8 = 0.792\pm 0.034$, and $n_s = 0.970\pm 0.018$, corresponding to $fσ_8 = 0.462\pm 0.020$ at $z \approx 0.525$, which is in agreement with Planck 2018 predictions and various clustering studies in the literature. We test single-parameter extensions to base-$Λ$CDM, varying the running of the spectral index, the dark energy equation of state, and the density of massless relic neutrinos, finding no compelling evidence for deviations from the base model. We model the galaxy-halo connection using a halo occupation distribution framework, finding signatures of environment-based assembly bias in the data. We validate our pipeline against mock catalogues that match the clustering and selection properties of CMASS, showing that we can recover unbiased cosmological constraints even with a volume 84 times larger than the one used in this study.

conceptDetails.name: density-split clustering
conceptDetails.description: A novel summary statistic that splits galaxies by local environment density to extract non-Gaussian clustering information beyond the two-point function.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: Neural network emulators replace expensive simulation calls to predict clustering statistics as a function of cosmological and HOD parameters during MCMC sampling.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework uses emulators trained on simulations to forward model clustering statistics and infer cosmological parameters without explicit likelihood functions.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Posterior distributions over cosmological parameters are obtained via MCMC sampling using the emulator-based likelihood framework.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Cold dark matter density (ω_cdm) is a primary cosmological parameter being constrained through galaxy clustering statistics.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: High-fidelity N-body simulations are used to generate mock galaxy catalogues for training emulators and validating the analysis pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives joint posterior constraints on ΛCDM parameters including σ_8, ω_cdm, and n_s from combined clustering statistics.

conceptDetails.name: halo occupation distribution

conceptDetails.name: dark energy

conceptDetails.name: assembly bias
