conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper constrains the density of massless relic neutrinos as a single-parameter extension to base-ΛCDM, testing their effect on structure growth.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Emulators are trained as regression models mapping cosmological and HOD parameters to summary statistics of galaxy clustering.

title: SUNBIRD: A simulation-based model for full-shape density-split clustering
abstract: Combining galaxy clustering information from regions of different environmental densities can help break cosmological parameter degeneracies and access non-Gaussian information from the density field that is not readily captured by the standard two-point correlation function (2PCF) analyses. However, modelling these density-dependent statistics down to the non-linear regime has so far remained challenging. We present a simulation-based model that is able to capture the cosmological dependence of the full shape of the density-split clustering (DSC) statistics down to intra-halo scales. Our models are based on neural-network emulators that are trained on high-fidelity mock galaxy catalogues within an extended-$Λ$CDM framework, incorporating the effects of redshift-space, Alcock-Paczynski distortions and models of the halo-galaxy connection. Our models reach sub-percent level accuracy down to $1\,h^{-1}{\rm Mpc}$ and are robust against different choices of galaxy-halo connection modelling. When combined with the galaxy 2PCF, DSC can tighten the constraints on $ω_{\rm cdm}$, $σ_8$, and $n_s$ by factors of 2.9, 1.9, and 2.1, respectively, compared to a 2PCF-only analysis. DSC additionally puts strong constraints on environment-based assembly bias parameters. Our code is made publicly available on Github.

conceptDetails.name: density-split clustering
conceptDetails.description: A novel two-point statistic that partitions the galaxy field by local environmental density, capturing non-Gaussian information beyond the standard 2PCF.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper builds neural-network emulators trained on simulation suites to forward-model density-split clustering statistics and perform cosmological inference without analytic likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: SUNBIRD uses neural-network emulators to accurately interpolate density-split clustering statistics across cosmological parameter space at sub-percent accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: High-fidelity N-body simulations and mock galaxy catalogues within an extended-ΛCDM framework form the training data for the emulators.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural-network surrogate models replace expensive simulation evaluations to enable rapid parameter inference over the full DSC statistic shape.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Posterior distributions over cosmological and HOD parameters are obtained via MCMC sampling using the emulator as a likelihood surrogate.

conceptDetails.name: halo-galaxy connection
conceptDetails.description: Halo occupation distribution and assembly bias models parameterize how galaxies populate dark matter halos, tested for robustness in the emulator.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Dark matter halo structure and halo occupation distribution models are central to connecting simulations to observed galaxy clustering.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives joint posteriors on cosmological parameters combining DSC and 2PCF data vectors through emulator-based likelihood evaluation.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Constraining cosmological parameters related to dark energy (e.g., ω_cdm, σ_8) is a primary science goal of the density-split clustering analysis.

conceptDetails.name: assembly bias
conceptDetails.description: Environment-based assembly bias parameters describing secondary halo properties that affect galaxy clustering are constrained by DSC statistics.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC chains are used to sample the posterior over cosmological and nuisance parameters given the emulated summary statistics.

title: Chained Quantile Morphing with Normalizing Flows
abstract: Accounting for inaccuracies in Monte Carlo simulations is a crucial step in any high energy physics analysis. It becomes especially important when training machine learning models, which can amplify simulation inaccuracies and introduce large discrepancies and systematic uncertainties when the model is applied to data. In this paper, we introduce a method to transform simulated events to better match data using normalizing flows, a class of deep learning-based density estimation models. Our proposal uses a technique called chained quantile morphing, which corrects a set of observables by iteratively shifting each entry according to a conditonal cumulative density function. We demonstrate the technique on a realistic particle physics dataset, and compare it to a neural network-based reweighting method. We also introduce a new contrastive learning technique to correct high dimensional particle-level inputs, which naively cannot be efficiently corrected with morphing strategies.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core deep learning architecture used to learn conditional CDFs for chained quantile morphing, enabling continuous and precise distribution transformation.

conceptDetails.name: chained quantile morphing
conceptDetails.description: The novel iterative technique introduced in this paper that corrects multivariate observables by sequentially applying conditional CDF transformations along each dimension.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows learn conditional probability densities of observables, which are used to compute the CDFs required for morphing simulated distributions to match data.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain is LHC data analysis, where Monte Carlo simulation corrections are essential for measurements and searches.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The simulated event samples being corrected; the paper addresses systematic inaccuracies in MC simulations of detector interactions and hadronization.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: CQM is motivated by and demonstrated on correcting mis-modeled detector responses, such as photon energy reconstruction in CMS.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: A novel contrastive learning technique is introduced to embed high-dimensional particle-level inputs into a low-dimensional latent space amenable to morphing.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Reducing simulation-induced systematic uncertainties is the primary physics motivation for the method, and the paper evaluates uncertainty reduction quantitatively.

conceptDetails.name: distribution morphing
conceptDetails.description: The broader class of correction strategies (as opposed to reweighting) that physically shift observable values to align simulated and data distributions.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: Quantile morphing guarantees optimal transport for each corrected sample, providing a theoretical foundation for the 1D case of the morphing transformation.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Particle-level jet representations (e.g., ParticleNet, LundNet inputs) form the high-dimensional use case motivating the contrastive learning extension.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Contrastive learning is used to reduce high-dimensional particle-level inputs to a low-dimensional space where morphing strategies can be efficiently applied.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The broader methodological context in which CQM operates — using learned models to bridge the gap between simulation and real experimental data.

title: GWAK: Gravitational-Wave Anomalous Knowledge with Recurrent Autoencoders
abstract: Matched-filtering detection techniques for gravitational-wave (GW) signals in ground-based interferometers rely on having well-modeled templates of the GW emission. Such techniques have been traditionally used in searches for compact binary coalescences (CBCs), and have been employed in all known GW detections so far. However, interesting science cases aside from compact mergers do not yet have accurate enough modeling to make matched filtering possible, including core-collapse supernovae and sources where stochasticity may be involved. Therefore the development of techniques to identify sources of these types is of significant interest. In this paper, we present a method of anomaly detection based on deep recurrent autoencoders to enhance the search region to unmodeled transients. We use a semi-supervised strategy that we name Gravitational Wave Anomalous Knowledge (GWAK). While the semi-supervised nature of the problem comes with a cost in terms of accuracy as compared to supervised techniques, there is a qualitative advantage in generalizing experimental sensitivity beyond pre-computed signal templates. We construct a low-dimensional embedded space using the GWAK method, capturing the physical signatures of distinct signals on each axis of the space. By introducing signal priors that capture some of the salient features of GW signals, we allow for the recovery of sensitivity even when an unmodeled anomaly is encountered. We show that regions of the GWAK space can identify CBCs, detector glitches and also a variety of unmodeled astrophysical sources.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The core method of GWAK, using recurrent autoencoders to identify gravitational-wave signals that deviate from background noise without requiring precise signal templates.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Deep recurrent autoencoders form the backbone of GWAK, trained on signal priors to construct low-dimensional embedded spaces for anomalous transient identification.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The physical domain of the paper, targeting detection of both modeled (CBC) and unmodeled (CCSN, cosmic strings) gravitational-wave transients in interferometer data.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: GWAK's training strategy that combines unlabeled background data with partial signal priors, enabling generalization to unmodeled astrophysical sources.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: LSTM-based recurrent neural networks are used as the encoder-decoder architecture to process time-series gravitational-wave strain data.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: GWAK constructs a low-dimensional embedded space where distinct physical signal types occupy interpretable, separable regions along each axis.

conceptDetails.name: unmodeled transient search
conceptDetails.description: Novel contribution: a semi-supervised pipeline specifically targeting gravitational-wave transients that lack accurate theoretical waveform models.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The GWAK space is a learned low-dimensional embedding that captures physical signatures of GW signals, glitches, and anomalies as geometrically separable regions.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: A key goal of GWAK is detecting signals outside the training distribution, generalizing sensitivity beyond pre-computed waveform templates.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: GWAK compresses high-dimensional interferometer time-series into a compact embedded space to enable efficient anomaly search and signal classification.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets detection of weak astrophysical transients in noisy interferometer data, including CCSN, cosmic strings, and neutron star glitches.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Transformer architectures are explored alongside recurrent networks as alternative encoders for constructing the GWAK embedded space.

title: Simulation-based Inference for Exoplanet Atmospheric Retrieval: Insights from winning the Ariel Data Challenge 2023 using Normalizing Flows
abstract:
Advancements in space telescopes have opened new avenues for gathering vast amounts of data on exoplanet atmosphere spectra. However, accurately extracting chemical and physical properties from these spectra poses significant challenges due to the non-linear nature of the underlying physics.
  This paper presents novel machine learning models developed by the AstroAI team for the Ariel Data Challenge 2023, where one of the models secured the top position among 293 competitors. Leveraging Normalizing Flows, our models predict the posterior probability distribution of atmospheric parameters under different atmospheric assumptions.
  Moreover, we introduce an alternative model that exhibits higher performance potential than the winning model, despite scoring lower in the challenge. These findings highlight the need to reevaluate the evaluation metric and prompt further exploration of more efficient and accurate approaches for exoplanet atmosphere spectra analysis.
  Finally, we present recommendations to enhance the challenge and models, providing valuable insights for future applications on real observational data. These advancements pave the way for more effective and timely analysis of exoplanet atmospheric properties, advancing our understanding of these distant worlds.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The primary generative model used to predict posterior probability distributions of exoplanet atmospheric parameters from transmission spectra.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The core methodology replacing traditional nested sampling with fast ML-based inference of atmospheric properties from simulated spectra.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The models predict full posterior distributions over 7 atmospheric parameters rather than point estimates, capturing degeneracies in the inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: exoplanets
conceptDetails.description: The scientific domain of the paper — retrieving chemical and physical properties of exoplanet atmospheres from transmission spectra.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Atmospheric retrieval is framed as an inverse problem: inferring physical parameters from observed spectra given a known forward radiative transfer model.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework underpinning the retrieval task; ML models are trained to approximate Bayesian posteriors previously computed via nested sampling.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling

conceptDetails.name: transmission spectroscopy

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing

title: Advances in machine-learning-based sampling motivated by lattice quantum chromodynamics
abstract: Sampling from known probability distributions is a ubiquitous task in computational science, underlying calculations in domains from linguistics to biology and physics. Generative machine-learning (ML) models have emerged as a promising tool in this space, building on the success of this approach in applications such as image, text, and audio generation. Often, however, generative tasks in scientific domains have unique structures and features -- such as complex symmetries and the requirement of exactness guarantees -- that present both challenges and opportunities for ML. This Perspective outlines the advances in ML-based sampling motivated by lattice quantum field theory, in particular for the theory of quantum chromodynamics. Enabling calculations of the structure and interactions of matter from our most fundamental understanding of particle physics, lattice quantum chromodynamics is one of the main consumers of open-science supercomputing worldwide. The design of ML algorithms for this application faces profound challenges, including the necessity of scaling custom ML architectures to the largest supercomputers, but also promises immense benefits, and is spurring a wave of development in ML-based sampling more broadly. In lattice field theory, if this approach can realize its early promise it will be a transformative step towards first-principles physics calculations in particle, nuclear and condensed matter physics that are intractable with traditional approaches.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain driving all ML-based sampling developments in this paper, requiring exact, symmetry-preserving generative models for field configurations.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core ML architecture used to learn exact, invertible samplers for lattice field theory distributions with tractable likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The traditional sampling baseline that ML approaches seek to augment or replace, including Markov chain Monte Carlo and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural architectures that exactly encode the gauge and spacetime symmetries of lattice QCD into the ML sampling models.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: A hard requirement for ML samplers in this domain, ensuring exact incorporation of gauge symmetry and other lattice symmetries into flow architectures.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The broader theoretical framework encompassing lattice QCD, whose symmetry structures (gauge invariance, Lorentz symmetry) constrain ML architecture design.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The general class of ML models explored for producing independent field configuration samples from the QCD path integral measure.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The foundational theoretical framework from which the path integral sampling problem is derived, motivating the entire ML development program.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central challenge addressed in the paper: scaling ML sampling architectures to the large lattice volumes and supercomputing resources required for physical QCD calculations.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The problem of approximating the QCD Boltzmann distribution p[Φ] = e^{-S[Φ]}/Z with a tractable ML model for use in importance sampling.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: A modern generative modeling technique discussed as a promising direction for constructing continuous normalizing flows for field theory sampling.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Underpins the Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithms that ML-based samplers are designed to improve upon by generating independent samples.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory

title: Signal-to-noise improvement through neural network contour deformations for 3D $SU(2)$ lattice gauge theory
abstract: Complex contour deformations of the path integral have been demonstrated to significantly improve the signal-to-noise ratio of observables in previous studies of two-dimensional gauge theories with open boundary conditions. In this work, new developments based on gauge fixing and a neural network definition of the deformation are introduced, which enable an effective application to theories in higher dimensions and with generic boundary conditions. Improvements of the signal-to-noise ratio by up to three orders of magnitude for Wilson loop measurements are shown in $SU(2)$ lattice gauge theory in three spacetime dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies complex contour deformations to SU(2) lattice gauge theory in 3D to improve signal-to-noise ratios of Wilson loop measurements.

conceptDetails.name: signal-to-noise improvement
conceptDetails.description: The central contribution is achieving up to three orders of magnitude improvement in signal-to-noise ratio for Wilson loop observables via optimized contour deformations.

conceptDetails.name: complex contour deformation
conceptDetails.description: Novel application of Cauchy's theorem to deform the path integral into the complexified gauge field domain, defining a family of equivalent observables with reduced variance.

conceptDetails.name: wilson loops
conceptDetails.description: Rectangular Wilson loops are the primary observables studied, serving as probes of confinement and exhibiting the area-law signal-to-noise problem targeted by this work.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo sampling is the core computational method used to estimate path integral expectation values, and the signal-to-noise problem addressed here is a fundamental limitation of this approach.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within the Euclidean path integral formalism of quantum field theory, deforming integration contours in the complexified field space.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The neural network deformation is constructed to respect gauge symmetry of the lattice theory, making gauge-equivariance a key design constraint.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge fixing is introduced to handle the gauge redundancy and enable effective neural network deformations while preserving physical symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Neural network-parameterized maps define the contour deformation, conceptually similar to normalizing flows in that they transform integration domains while tracking Jacobian factors.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The signal-to-noise problem arises from statistical fluctuations in Monte Carlo estimators; the work targets variance reduction in these stochastic estimates.

title: Data Compression and Inference in Cosmology with Self-Supervised Machine Learning
abstract: The influx of massive amounts of data from current and upcoming cosmological surveys necessitates compression schemes that can efficiently summarize the data with minimal loss of information. We introduce a method that leverages the paradigm of self-supervised machine learning in a novel manner to construct representative summaries of massive datasets using simulation-based augmentations. Deploying the method on hydrodynamical cosmological simulations, we show that it can deliver highly informative summaries, which can be used for a variety of downstream tasks, including precise and accurate parameter inference. We demonstrate how this paradigm can be used to construct summary representations that are insensitive to prescribed systematic effects, such as the influence of baryonic physics. Our results indicate that self-supervised machine learning techniques offer a promising new approach for compression of cosmological data as well its analysis.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The core method of the paper, using physically-motivated simulation-based augmentations to learn informative data summaries without explicit labels.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper learns compressed low-dimensional representations of cosmological datasets that retain maximal information for downstream tasks.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Novel technique using pairs of simulations with varied nuisance parameters (e.g., baryonic physics) as augmentations to drive self-supervised learning.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The learned summaries are used for likelihood-free parameter inference, and the method integrates with sequential simulation-based inference pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The method compresses high-dimensional cosmological data (density maps) into compact low-dimensional summary statistics.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: VICReg, the specific self-supervised framework used, is a contrastive/variance-invariance-covariance regularization method for learning representations.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The method is evaluated on hydrodynamical cosmological simulations from the CAMELS suite, including matter density maps.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The compressed summaries are used to perform precise cosmological parameter posterior estimation via simulation-based inference.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Physically-motivated simulation-based augmentations are the key innovation enabling self-supervised learning in cosmological settings.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The method constructs summaries that are insensitive to systematic effects such as baryonic physics while preserving cosmological information.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies compression for sequential simulation-based inference via generative emulation as a downstream application.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Cosmological parameters including dark energy density are among the target parameters for inference using the learned summaries.

title: Subhalo effective density slope measurements from HST strong lensing data with neural likelihood-ratio estimation
abstract: Examining the properties of subhalos with strong gravitational lensing images can shed light on the nature of dark matter. From upcoming large-scale surveys, we expect to discover orders of magnitude more strong lens systems that can be used for subhalo studies. To optimally extract information from a large number of strong lensing images, machine learning provides promising avenues for efficient analysis that is unachievable with traditional analysis methods, but application of machine learning techniques to real observations is still limited. We build upon previous work, which uses a neural likelihood-ratio estimator, to constrain the effective density slopes of subhalos and demonstrate the feasibility of this method on real strong lensing observations. To do this, we implement significant improvements to the forward simulation pipeline and undertake careful model evaluation using simulated images. Ultimately, we use our trained model to predict the effective subhalo density slope from combining a set of strong lensing images taken by the \textit{Hubble Space Telescope}. We found the subhalo slope measurement of this set of observations to be steeper than the slope predictions of cold dark matter subhalos. Our result adds to several previous works that also measured high subhalo slopes in observations. Although a possible explanation for this is that subhalos with steeper slopes are easier to detect due to selection effects and thus contribute to statistical bias, our result nevertheless points to the need for careful analysis of more strong lensing observations from future surveys.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper constrains subhalo properties to probe the nature of dark matter, comparing CDM predictions against WDM and SIDM models through effective density slope measurements.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: A forward simulation pipeline generates mock strong lensing images that train the neural likelihood-ratio estimator, enabling inference without a tractable likelihood function.

conceptDetails.name: strong gravitational lensing
conceptDetails.description: The observational technique by which background galaxy light is deflected by a foreground lens galaxy, enabling detection of dark matter substructure through image perturbations.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: A neural likelihood-ratio estimator is the core machine learning technique used to constrain effective subhalo density slopes from strong lensing images.

conceptDetails.name: subhalo density slope
conceptDetails.description: The effective power-law slope of the subhalo mass density profile, directly related to concentration, serves as the key observable distinguishing dark matter models.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering subhalo density slope parameters from observed gravitational lensing images constitutes an ill-posed inverse problem that the neural estimator addresses.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method estimates the posterior distribution over subhalo effective density slopes by combining evidence from multiple HST strong lensing observations.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The forward simulation pipeline generates realistic mock HST strong lensing images incorporating source galaxies, lens galaxies, subhalo populations, and line-of-sight halos.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional neural network architecture processes the strong lensing images to learn discriminative features for likelihood-ratio estimation.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Careful evaluation of the trained model on simulated images with known ground-truth slopes is performed to verify inference quality before applying to real observations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework is Bayesian, combining the neural likelihood-ratio estimates across multiple lens systems to produce a joint posterior on the subhalo slope parameter.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The neural likelihood-ratio estimator is evaluated for calibration to ensure that predicted posteriors are reliable before deployment on HST observations.

title: Reconstructing $S$-matrix Phases with Machine Learning
abstract: An important element of the $S$-matrix bootstrap program is the relationship between the modulus of an $S$-matrix element and its phase. Unitarity relates them by an integral equation. Even in the simplest case of elastic scattering, this integral equation cannot be solved analytically and numerical approaches are required. We apply modern machine learning techniques to studying the unitarity constraint. We find that for a given modulus, when a phase exists it can generally be reconstructed to good accuracy with machine learning. Moreover, the loss of the reconstruction algorithm provides a good proxy for whether a given modulus can be consistent with unitarity at all. In addition, we study the question of whether multiple phases can be consistent with a single modulus, finding novel phase-ambiguous solutions. In particular, we find a new phase-ambiguous solution which pushes the known limit on such solutions significantly beyond the previous bound.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly studies S-matrix elements, reconstructing their phases from moduli using ML, central to the S-matrix bootstrap program.

conceptDetails.name: phase ambiguity
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paper discovers new phase-ambiguous solutions where multiple distinct phases yield the same modulus, pushing known bounds on such solutions.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the complex phase of a scattering amplitude from its modulus (the differential cross section) is an inverse problem solved here with ML.

conceptDetails.name: s-matrix bootstrap
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the broader program of constraining scattering amplitudes from first principles (unitarity, analyticity, crossing symmetry) that this work contributes to.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The unitarity constraint on the S-matrix is a fundamental QFT requirement that defines the integral equation the ML algorithm must satisfy.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks are trained to satisfy the unitarity integral equation as a physical constraint, making this a physics-informed approach to phase reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A repulsive loss is designed to discover phase-ambiguous solutions by penalizing proximity to already-found phases, enabling exploration of the solution landscape.

conceptDetails.name: loss landscape
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: systematic scanning of the ML loss landscape over modulus space to classify which moduli admit valid unitary phases.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The core ML task is regression: mapping a given amplitude modulus B(z) to its corresponding phase ϕ(z) as a continuous function.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The reconstruction loss serves as a proxy for whether a given modulus is consistent with unitarity, providing a data-driven uncertainty or feasibility signal.

title: Gravitational action for a massive Majorana fermion in 2d quantum gravity
abstract: We compute the gravitational action of a free massive Majorana fermion coupled to two-dimensional gravity on compact Riemann surfaces of arbitrary genus. The structure is similar to the case of the massive scalar. The small-mass expansion of the gravitational yields the Liouville action at zeroth order, and we can identify the Mabuchi action at first order. While the massive Majorana action is a conformal deformation of the massless Majorana CFT, we find an action different from the one given by the David-Distler-Kawai (DDK) ansatz.

conceptDetails.name: majorana fermion
conceptDetails.description: The central matter field studied: a free massive Majorana fermion in two dimensions, equivalent to the massive Ising model on flat space.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper computes the gravitational action by evaluating the functional integral of a massive Majorana fermion coupled to 2d gravity, a core QFT computation.

conceptDetails.name: liouville gravity
conceptDetails.description: The gravitational action reduces to the Liouville action at zeroth order in the small-mass expansion, connecting to Polyakov's foundational result for 2d quantum gravity.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The massless Majorana fermion is a CFT with central charge c=1/2 (Ising model), and the massive theory is analyzed as a conformal deformation of this CFT.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral analysis including zeta functions, heat kernels, and Green functions is the primary mathematical tool used to derive the gravitational action.

conceptDetails.name: functional determinant
conceptDetails.description: The gravitational action is expressed as a functional determinant of the massive Dirac operator, evaluated via zeta-function regularization.

conceptDetails.name: mabuchi action
conceptDetails.description: A geometric functional identified at first order in the small-mass expansion of the gravitational action, analogous to the massive scalar case.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: The DDK ansatz and beta-function analysis relate to quantum renormalization of the coupled gravity-matter system and conformal invariance at the quantum level.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The departure from conformal invariance due to mass constitutes a symmetry breaking that drives the main distinction from the DDK ansatz prediction.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The small-mass expansion of the gravitational action provides an effective description organized by powers of the mass parameter.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Two-dimensional quantum gravity and the Liouville field are directly connected to string theory via the Polyakov action and worldsheet formulation.

title: An Extensive $\textit{Hubble Space Telescope}$ Study of the Offset and Host Light Distributions of Type I Superluminous Supernovae
abstract: We present an extensive $\textit{Hubble Space Telescope}$ ($\textit{HST}$) rest-frame ultraviolet (UV) imaging study of the locations of Type I superluminous supernovae (SLSNe) within their host galaxies. The sample includes 65 SLSNe with detected host galaxies in the redshift range $z\approx 0.05-2$. Using precise astrometric matching with SN images, we determine the distributions of physical and host-normalized offsets relative to the host centers, as well as the fractional flux distribution relative to the underlying UV light distribution. We find that the host-normalized offsets of SLSNe roughly track an exponential disk profile, but exhibit an overabundance of sources with large offsets of $1.5-4$ times their host half-light radius. The SLSNe normalized offsets are systematically larger than those of long gamma-ray bursts (LGRBs), and even Type Ib/c and II SNe. Furthermore, we find that about 40\% of all SLSNe occur in the dimmest regions of their host galaxies (fractional flux of 0), in stark contrast to LGRBs and Type Ib/c and II SNe. We do not detect any significant trends in the locations of SLSNe as a function of redshift, or as a function of explosion and magnetar engine parameters inferred from modeling of their optical lights curves. The significant difference in SLSN locations compared to LGRBs (and normal core-collapse SNe) suggests that at least some of their progenitors follow a different evolutionary path. We speculate that SLSNe arise from massive runaway stars from disrupted binary systems, with velocities of $\sim 10^2$ km s$^{-1}$.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The study focuses specifically on Type I superluminous supernovae (SLSNe), comparing their sub-galactic locations to other transient classes including LGRBs and Type Ib/c and II SNe.

conceptDetails.name: runaway star progenitors
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes that SLSNe arise from massive runaway stars ejected from disrupted binary systems, explaining their large offsets from host galaxy light.

conceptDetails.name: fractional flux distribution
conceptDetails.description: The fraction of host UV light underlying each SLSN location is measured and compared across transient classes to probe the star-forming environments of progenitors.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates SLSN progenitor systems through analysis of host galaxy environments, connecting offset distributions to massive star evolutionary pathways including binary systems and runaway stars.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar engine
conceptDetails.description: A rapidly spinning, highly magnetized neutron star central engine is the leading power source model for SLSNe, and its inferred parameters are tested for correlation with host offset distributions.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Statistical tests are applied to compare offset and fractional flux distributions of SLSNe against LGRBs and core-collapse SNe to determine if populations are drawn from the same underlying distribution.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Host galaxies of SLSNe are characterized as low-metallicity dwarf galaxies with high specific star formation rates, distinguishing them from hosts of other transient types.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Precise astrometric matching between SLSN positions and HST host galaxy images is used to detect and localize transient events within their host light distributions.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The study identifies an overabundance of SLSNe at large host-normalized offsets and in dim host regions, flagging these as statistically anomalous compared to other transient populations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Magnetar engine parameters are inferred from optical light curve modeling using statistical inference techniques to connect explosion properties to host galaxy locations.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: A flat ΛCDM cosmology with Planck 2018 parameters is assumed to convert angular to physical offsets across the redshift range z≈0.05–2.

title: FLORAH: A generative model for halo assembly histories
abstract: The mass assembly history (MAH) of dark matter halos plays a crucial role in shaping the formation and evolution of galaxies. MAHs are used extensively in semi-analytic and empirical models of galaxy formation, yet current analytic methods to generate them are inaccurate and unable to capture their relationship with the halo internal structure and large-scale environment. This paper introduces FLORAH, a machine-learning framework for generating assembly histories of ensembles of dark matter halos. We train FLORAH on the assembly histories from the GUREFT and VSMDPL N-body simulations and demonstrate its ability to recover key properties such as the time evolution of mass and concentration. We obtain similar results for the galaxy stellar mass versus halo mass relation and its residuals when we run the Santa Cruz semi-analytic model on FLORAH-generated assembly histories and halo formation histories extracted from an N-body simulation. We further show that FLORAH also reproduces the dependence of clustering on properties other than mass (assembly bias), which is not captured by other analytic methods. By combining multiple networks trained on a suite of simulations with different redshift ranges and mass resolutions, we are able to construct accurate main progenitor branches (MPBs) with a wide dynamic mass range from $z=0$ up to an ultra-high redshift $z \approx 20$, currently far beyond that of a single N-body simulation. FLORAH is the first step towards a machine learning-based framework for planting full merger trees; this will enable the exploration of different galaxy formation scenarios with great computational efficiency at unprecedented accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: FLORAH is fundamentally a deep generative model that learns the distribution of dark matter halo assembly histories from N-body simulations and generates statistically consistent new samples.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper models the mass assembly histories and concentration histories of dark matter halos, which are the foundational structures governing galaxy formation in the ΛCDM paradigm.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: FLORAH combines a recurrent network with a normalizing flow to learn and sample from the full conditional distribution of possible assembly histories for halos of given mass and concentration.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: A recurrent neural network encodes the time-ordered sequence structure of main progenitor branches, capturing temporal dependencies in halo mass and concentration evolution.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: FLORAH is trained on N-body simulations (GUREFT and VSMDPL) and validated against them, serving as an efficient emulator that extends their reach to ultra-high redshifts z≈20.

conceptDetails.name: halo assembly bias
conceptDetails.description: FLORAH reproduces the dependence of halo clustering on formation history and structural properties beyond mass—a key failure mode of analytic EPS-based methods.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: FLORAH emulates the statistical outputs of expensive N-body simulations, enabling generation of halo assembly histories at mass ranges and redshifts inaccessible to any single simulation.

conceptDetails.name: merger trees
conceptDetails.description: The paper positions FLORAH as the first step toward a machine-learning framework for generating full halo merger trees, the hierarchical progenitor-descendant structure used by galaxy formation models.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The framework acts as a surrogate for full N-body simulations within the galaxy formation pipeline, enabling semi-analytic models to run at orders-of-magnitude lower computational cost.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are used to estimate the probability density of assembly history trajectories conditioned on current halo properties, enabling faithful stochastic generation.

conceptDetails.name: semi-analytic models
conceptDetails.description: The Santa Cruz SAM is run on FLORAH-generated histories to validate that downstream galaxy observables such as the stellar mass–halo mass relation are faithfully reproduced.

title: First application of a liquid argon time projection chamber for the search for intranuclear neutron-antineutron transitions and annihilation in $^{40}$Ar using the MicroBooNE detector
abstract: We present a novel methodology to search for intranuclear neutron-antineutron transition ($n\rightarrow\bar{n}$) followed by $\bar{n}$-nucleon annihilation within an $^{40}$Ar nucleus, using the MicroBooNE liquid argon time projection chamber (LArTPC) detector. A discovery of $n\rightarrow\bar{n}$ transition or a new best limit on the lifetime of this process would either constitute physics beyond the Standard Model or greatly constrain theories of baryogenesis, respectively. The approach presented in this paper makes use of deep learning methods to select $n\rightarrow\bar{n}$ events based on their unique features and differentiate them from cosmogenic backgrounds. The achieved signal and background efficiencies are (70.22$\pm$6.04)\% and (0.0020$\pm$0.0003)\%, respectively. A demonstration of a search is performed with a data set corresponding to an exposure of $3.32 \times10^{26}\,$neutron-years, and where the background rate is constrained through direct measurement, assuming the presence of a negligible signal. With this approach, no excess of events over the background prediction is observed, setting a demonstrative lower bound on the $n\rightarrow\bar{n}$ lifetime in $^{40}$Ar of $τ_{\textrm{m}} \gtrsim 1.1\times10^{26}\,$years, and on the free $n\rightarrow\bar{n}$ transition time of $τ_{\textrm{\nnbar}} \gtrsim 2.6\times10^{5}\,$s, each at the $90\%$ confidence level. This analysis represents a first-ever proof-of-principle demonstration of the ability to search for this rare process in LArTPCs with high efficiency and low background.

conceptDetails.name: neutron-antineutron oscillation
conceptDetails.description: The core physics process studied: intranuclear n→n̄ transition violating baryon number by two units, motivating extensions to the Standard Model.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for baryon-number-violating neutron-antineutron transitions as a probe of physics beyond the Standard Model and baryogenesis theories.

conceptDetails.name: lartpc imaging
conceptDetails.description: The MicroBooNE liquid argon time projection chamber provides high-resolution 2D wire-plane images used as input to the deep learning event selection pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A CNN-based selection stage is used to classify neutron-antineutron annihilation events from wire-plane images of the LArTPC detector, achieving 70% signal efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates a proof-of-principle signal detection methodology for rare intranuclear n→n̄ transitions in liquid argon with high signal efficiency and ultra-low background.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Multi-stage classification combining BDT preselection, CNN-based selection, and topological cuts separates signal from cosmogenic background.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing particle tracks, vertices, and topological features from LArTPC wire-plane data is central to identifying n→n̄ annihilation event signatures.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: A frequentist hypothesis test at 90% confidence level is used to set lower bounds on the n→n̄ transition lifetime and free transition time.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: GENIE and Geant4-based simulations model signal events and detector response, with associated systematic uncertainties evaluated for the analysis.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations are used to model both signal and background processes, and to evaluate systematic uncertainties from nuclear models and detector effects.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Systematic uncertainties from GENIE nuclear model parameters, Geant4 hadronic interactions, and detector effects are propagated through the analysis to constrain sensitivity.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The analysis searches for an excess of rare annihilation events over background, effectively treating signal as an anomaly in a background-dominated dataset.

title: A parsec-scale Galactic 3D dust map out to 1.25 kpc from the Sun
abstract: High-resolution 3D maps of interstellar dust are critical for probing the underlying physics shaping the structure of the interstellar medium, and for foreground correction of astrophysical observations affected by dust. We aim to construct a new 3D map of the spatial distribution of interstellar dust extinction out to a distance of 1.25 kpc from the Sun. We leveraged distance and extinction estimates to 54 million nearby stars derived from the Gaia BP/RP spectra. Using the stellar distance and extinction information, we inferred the spatial distribution of dust extinction. We modeled the logarithmic dust extinction with a Gaussian process in a spherical coordinate system via iterative charted refinement and a correlation kernel inferred in previous work. In total, our posterior has over 661 million degrees of freedom. We probed the posterior distribution using the variational inference method MGVI. Our 3D dust map has an angular resolution of up to 14' (Nside = 256), and we achieve parsec-scale distance resolution, sampling the dust in 516 logarithmically spaced distance bins spanning 69 pc to 1250 pc. We generated 12 samples from the variational posterior of the 3D dust distribution and release the samples alongside the mean 3D dust map and its corresponding uncertainty. Our map resolves the internal structure of hundreds of molecular clouds in the solar neighborhood and will be broadly useful for studies of star formation, Galactic structure, and young stellar populations. It is available for download in a variety of coordinate systems online and can also be queried via the publicly available dustmaps Python package.

conceptDetails.name: 3d dust mapping
conceptDetails.description: Novel methodology combining spherical coordinate Gaussian processes with MGVI to produce a parsec-scale resolution 3D map of interstellar dust extinction out to 1.25 kpc.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses Bayesian inference to infer the 3D spatial distribution of dust extinction from stellar distance and extinction measurements, with a full posterior over 661 million degrees of freedom.

conceptDetails.name: variational inference
conceptDetails.description: Metric Gaussian Variational Inference (MGVI) is the core inference algorithm used to approximate the intractable posterior over the high-dimensional dust distribution.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Variational inference (MGVI) is used to probe the high-dimensional posterior distribution of the 3D dust map, generating samples from the variational posterior.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: A Gaussian process with a correlation kernel in spherical coordinates is used as the prior model for the logarithmic dust extinction distribution.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing the 3D dust distribution from integrated line-of-sight extinction measurements is fundamentally an ill-posed inverse problem requiring regularization.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The map releases uncertainty estimates alongside the mean dust map, with 12 posterior samples enabling probabilistic characterization of the reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: interstellar medium structure
conceptDetails.description: The map resolves the internal structure of hundreds of molecular clouds in the solar neighborhood, enabling studies of star formation and Galactic structure.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Iterative charted refinement is employed to make Gaussian process inference tractable at over 661 million degrees of freedom on a spherical grid.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The method achieves parsec-scale distance resolution despite the sparse and noisy stellar extinction measurements, effectively resolving fine structure in molecular clouds.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper leverages stellar photometric and spectroscopic data from Gaia to infer individual stellar distances and extinctions, which feed the dust map construction.

title: Distilled Feature Fields Enable Few-Shot Language-Guided Manipulation
abstract: Self-supervised and language-supervised image models contain rich knowledge of the world that is important for generalization. Many robotic tasks, however, require a detailed understanding of 3D geometry, which is often lacking in 2D image features. This work bridges this 2D-to-3D gap for robotic manipulation by leveraging distilled feature fields to combine accurate 3D geometry with rich semantics from 2D foundation models. We present a few-shot learning method for 6-DOF grasping and placing that harnesses these strong spatial and semantic priors to achieve in-the-wild generalization to unseen objects. Using features distilled from a vision-language model, CLIP, we present a way to designate novel objects for manipulation via free-text natural language, and demonstrate its ability to generalize to unseen expressions and novel categories of objects.

conceptDetails.name: distilled feature fields
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution: extending neural radiance fields with additional outputs that reconstruct dense 2D feature maps from vision foundation models, embedding semantic knowledge into a 3D volumetric representation.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper is fundamentally about learning rich 3D scene representations by distilling 2D image features from pre-trained models into spatially-consistent volumetric feature fields.

conceptDetails.name: neural radiance fields
conceptDetails.description: NeRF provides the underlying 3D geometry backbone that is extended with semantic feature rendering integrals to produce the Distilled Feature Field (DFF) scene representation.

conceptDetails.name: few-shot manipulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents a few-shot learning framework for 6-DOF robotic grasping and placing that generalizes to unseen objects given only a handful of demonstrations.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: 2D image embeddings from CLIP and DINO ViT are distilled into a 3D volumetric feature field, enabling spatial queries over semantic descriptors for manipulation planning.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Dense patch-level feature extraction from CLIP (via MaskCLIP reparameterization) and DINO ViT is central to constructing the 2D supervision signal for the distillation pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: DINO ViT, a self-supervised vision transformer, provides semantically rich visual descriptors that serve as effective out-of-the-box dense correspondence features for manipulation.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Internet-scale pre-trained representations from CLIP and DINO are transferred to a new 3D robotic manipulation domain, enabling open-set generalization without task-specific retraining.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: CLIP, trained via contrastive learning on image-text pairs, provides the language-grounded features used to identify and query objects for manipulation via free-text natural language.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures underlie both DINO and CLIP, whose patch-level attention features are leveraged for dense 3D scene understanding.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: The feature field and grasp pose network are optimized on scene-specific image data using hierarchical hashgrids to reduce fitting time from hours to minutes.

title: Score-based Diffusion Models for Generating Liquid Argon Time Projection Chamber Images
abstract: For the first time, we show high-fidelity generation of LArTPC-like data using a generative neural network. This demonstrates that methods developed for natural images do transfer to LArTPC-produced images, which, in contrast to natural images, are globally sparse but locally dense. We present the score-based diffusion method employed. We evaluate the fidelity of the generated images using several quality metrics, including modified measures used to evaluate natural images, comparisons between high-dimensional distributions, and comparisons relevant to LArTPC experiments.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The specific diffusion model formulation using stochastic differential equations and score function estimation to learn and reverse a noising process for LArTPC image generation.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: The generative modeling framework employed to produce high-fidelity synthetic LArTPC detector images by learning to reverse a forward noising diffusion process.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: The primary application target: replacing slow Geant4-based Monte Carlo simulation of LArTPC detector readout with a fast neural generative surrogate.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The broader class of deep learning methods—including GANs, normalizing flows, VQ-VAEs, and diffusion models—surveyed and compared for LArTPC data generation.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The overarching goal of using the trained diffusion model as a fast surrogate for physics-based Monte Carlo detector simulation pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The physics domain driving the need for LArTPC simulation; LArTPCs are the primary detector technology for current and future neutrino experiments.

conceptDetails.name: sparse image generation
conceptDetails.description: Novel challenge central to this work: generating images that are globally sparse but locally dense, a regime not well-studied in standard diffusion model literature.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The conventional simulation approach (Geant4-based MC) whose computational bottleneck motivates the development of generative neural network surrogates.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The neural architecture backbone (U-Net-style convolutional network) used as the score estimator within the diffusion model.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: A downstream application noted by the authors: using the generative model for likelihood estimation of low-level detector data to enable novel inference approaches.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's key demonstration that diffusion model methods developed for natural images successfully transfer to the structurally distinct sparse LArTPC image domain.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Implicit objective of the score-based model—learning the probability distribution over LArTPC image space to enable faithful sampling.

title: Polarization Multi-Image Synthesis with Birefringent Metasurfaces
abstract:
Optical metasurfaces composed of precisely engineered nanostructures have gained significant attention for their ability to manipulate light and implement distinct functionalities based on the properties of the incident field. Computational imaging systems have started harnessing this capability to produce sets of coded measurements that benefit certain tasks when paired with digital post-processing. Inspired by these works, we introduce a new system that uses a birefringent metasurface with a polarizer-mosaicked photosensor to capture four optically-coded measurements in a single exposure. We apply this system to the task of incoherent opto-electronic filtering, where digital spatial-filtering operations are replaced by simpler, per-pixel sums across the four polarization channels, independent of the spatial filter size. In contrast to previous work on incoherent opto-electronic filtering that can realize only one spatial filter, our approach can realize a continuous family of filters from a single capture, with filters being selected from the family by adjusting the post-capture digital summation weights. To find a metasurface that can realize a set of user-specified spatial filters, we introduce a form of gradient descent with a novel regularizer that encourages light efficiency and a high signal-to-noise ratio. We demonstrate several examples in simulation and with fabricated prototypes, including some with spatial filters that have prescribed variations with respect to depth and wavelength.
  Visit the Project Page at https://deanhazineh.github.io/publications/Multi_Image_Synthesis/MIS_Home.html

conceptDetails.name: birefringent metasurface
conceptDetails.description: An engineered array of nanofins that imparts polarization-dependent phase delays to incident light, enabling simultaneous multi-coded snapshot imaging.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Designing metasurface nanostructure layouts from user-specified spatial filter targets is formulated as an inverse problem solved via gradient descent.

conceptDetails.name: opto-electronic filtering
conceptDetails.description: A computational imaging paradigm in which digital spatial filtering is replaced by optical encoding followed by simple per-pixel weighted sums across polarization channels.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A novel regularizer is introduced during gradient descent to encourage light efficiency and high signal-to-noise ratio in the fabricated metasurface.

conceptDetails.name: polarization-coded imaging
conceptDetails.description: A snapshot imaging modality using a polarizer-mosaicked photosensor to capture four optically-coded measurements in a single exposure.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: A precomputed phase-delay library from a field solver serves as a differentiable surrogate for nanostructure optical response during optimization.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Opto-electronic filtering replaces spatially-varying digital convolutions with pixel-wise summations that are computationally independent of filter size.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: The system co-designs birefringent optics with downstream digital post-processing weights to achieve a continuous family of target image-synthesis functions.

title: Neural Network Field Theories: Non-Gaussianity, Actions, and Locality
abstract: Both the path integral measure in field theory and ensembles of neural networks describe distributions over functions. When the central limit theorem can be applied in the infinite-width (infinite-$N$) limit, the ensemble of networks corresponds to a free field theory. Although an expansion in $1/N$ corresponds to interactions in the field theory, others, such as in a small breaking of the statistical independence of network parameters, can also lead to interacting theories. These other expansions can be advantageous over the $1/N$-expansion, for example by improved behavior with respect to the universal approximation theorem. Given the connected correlators of a field theory, one can systematically reconstruct the action order-by-order in the expansion parameter, using a new Feynman diagram prescription whose vertices are the connected correlators. This method is motivated by the Edgeworth expansion and allows one to derive actions for neural network field theories. Conversely, the correspondence allows one to engineer architectures realizing a given field theory by representing action deformations as deformations of neural network parameter densities. As an example, $φ^4$ theory is realized as an infinite-$N$ neural network field theory.

conceptDetails.name: nn-ft correspondence
conceptDetails.description: The central novel framework establishing that neural network ensembles define field theories, with parameter-space descriptions mapping to path-integral descriptions via connected correlators.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a systematic correspondence between ensembles of neural networks and quantum field theories, deriving actions and Feynman rules from neural network statistics.

conceptDetails.name: connected correlators
conceptDetails.description: Statistical cumulants of the field distribution that serve as the bridge between neural network statistics and interacting field theory actions, used as vertices in a new Feynman diagram prescription.

conceptDetails.name: edgeworth expansion
conceptDetails.description: A statistical series expansion around a Gaussian that motivates the method for systematically reconstructing field theory actions order-by-order from connected correlators.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks at initialization define random functions over parameter draws; the paper analyzes these as stochastic processes whose statistics encode field theory structure.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs actions order-by-order in expansion parameters (1/N or independence-breaking), analogous to effective field theory expansions, including realizing φ⁴ theory.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Statistical independence breaking of network parameters is analyzed as the mechanism generating non-Gaussianity and interacting field theories beyond the 1/N expansion.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses how to estimate and engineer the field density P(φ) by deforming neural network parameter distributions to realize target field theories.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The infinite-width Gaussian process limit of neural networks is a Bayesian prior over functions; the paper extends beyond this Gaussian regime to interacting priors.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Action reconstruction from correlators and the locality analysis of engineered field theories connect to renormalization-group ideas about which interactions survive at long distances.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian processes—the infinite-width NN limit—are characterized by kernel functions (two-point correlators), which the paper generalizes via non-Gaussian deformations.

title: From Discovery to the First Month of the Type II Supernova 2023ixf: High and Variable Mass Loss in the Final Year before Explosion
abstract: We present the discovery of the Type II supernova SN 2023ixf in M101 and follow-up photometric and spectroscopic observations, respectively, in the first month and week of its evolution. Our discovery was made within a day of estimated first light, and the following light curve is characterized by a rapid rise ($\approx5$ days) to a luminous peak ($M_V\approx-18.2$ mag) and plateau ($M_V\approx-17.6$ mag) extending to $30$ days with a fast decline rate of $\approx0.03$ mag day$^{-1}$. During the rising phase, $U-V$ color shows blueward evolution, followed by redward evolution in the plateau phase. Prominent flash features of hydrogen, helium, carbon, and nitrogen dominate the spectra up to $\approx5$ days after first light, with a transition to a higher ionization state in the first $\approx2$ days. Both the $U-V$ color and flash ionization states suggest a rise in the temperature, indicative of a delayed shock breakout inside dense circumstellar material (CSM). From the timescales of CSM interaction, we estimate its compact radial extent of $\sim(3-7)\times10^{14}$ cm. We then construct numerical light-curve models based on both continuous and eruptive mass-loss scenarios shortly before explosion. For the continuous mass-loss scenario, we infer a range of mass-loss history with $0.1-1.0\,M_\odot\,{\rm yr}^{-1}$ in the final $2-1$ yr before explosion, with a potentially decreasing mass loss of $0.01-0.1\,M_\odot\,{\rm yr}^{-1}$ in $\sim0.7-0.4$ yr toward the explosion. For the eruptive mass-loss scenario, we favor eruptions releasing $0.3-1\,M_\odot$ of the envelope at about a year before explosion, which result in CSM with mass and extent similar to the continuous scenario. We discuss the implications of the available multiwavelength constraints obtained thus far on the progenitor candidate and SN 2023ixf to our variable CSM models.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: SN 2023ixf is classified and analyzed as a Type II core-collapse supernova with detailed photometric and spectroscopic characterization.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: SN-CSM shock interaction drives early light curve excess, flash spectral features, and X-ray/radio emission analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies the final stages of a red supergiant's evolution, including mass-loss history in the year before core collapse.

conceptDetails.name: shock breakout
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to this paper: delayed shock breakout inside dense CSM is inferred from flash ionization and UV color evolution.

conceptDetails.name: flash spectroscopy
conceptDetails.description: Early high-ionization narrow emission features (flash features) of H, He, C, N are used to probe the immediate pre-explosion CSM structure.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Mass-loss history and CSM properties are inferred by inverting observed light curves and spectral features against numerical models.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Numerical light-curve models are constructed to simulate continuous and eruptive mass-loss scenarios and match observed photometric evolution.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Discovery and early detection of SN 2023ixf within one day of first light enables time-sensitive follow-up across multiple wavelengths.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Parameter estimation for mass-loss rates and CSM properties is performed by comparing models to multiwavelength observational constraints.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Eruptive and variable mass-loss scenarios before explosion are modeled as discrete or stochastic outburst events from the progenitor star.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: Multiwavelength constraints including non-detections at millimeter wavelengths are discussed in the context of the explosion mechanism.

title: The Clock and the Pizza: Two Stories in Mechanistic Explanation of Neural Networks
abstract: Do neural networks, trained on well-understood algorithmic tasks, reliably rediscover known algorithms for solving those tasks? Several recent studies, on tasks ranging from group arithmetic to in-context linear regression, have suggested that the answer is yes. Using modular addition as a prototypical problem, we show that algorithm discovery in neural networks is sometimes more complex. Small changes to model hyperparameters and initializations can induce the discovery of qualitatively different algorithms from a fixed training set, and even parallel implementations of multiple such algorithms. Some networks trained to perform modular addition implement a familiar Clock algorithm; others implement a previously undescribed, less intuitive, but comprehensible procedure which we term the Pizza algorithm, or a variety of even more complex procedures. Our results show that even simple learning problems can admit a surprising diversity of solutions, motivating the development of new tools for characterizing the behavior of neural networks across their algorithmic phase space.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central goal is mechanistically reverse-engineering which algorithms neural networks discover when trained on modular arithmetic, identifying the Clock and Pizza procedures.

conceptDetails.name: mechanistic explanation
conceptDetails.description: A novel methodology for decomposing network behavior into discrete algorithmic steps (embedding, computation, readout) to determine which mathematical procedure a trained network implements.

conceptDetails.name: algorithmic phase space
conceptDetails.description: A novel framing introduced by the paper to characterize the full landscape of algorithms a network can discover as hyperparameters vary, rather than assuming a single canonical solution.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper documents sharp algorithmic phase transitions between the Clock and Pizza algorithms as model width and attention strength are varied.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Circular token embeddings (representing integers as angles on a unit circle) are the shared geometric substrate underlying both the Clock and Pizza algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Presence or absence of learned attention is the key architectural factor determining whether a network implements the Clock (attention-based multiplication) or Pizza (mean-based) algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: One-layer ReLU transformers are the primary model architecture trained on modular addition, with variants differing in whether attention weights are learned or fixed.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Networks spontaneously learn to represent integers as points on a circle in embedding space; the paper analyzes how this representation supports different downstream computational strategies.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gradient symmetry (permutation symmetry of logit gradients w.r.t. input embeddings) is used as a diagnostic metric to distinguish Clock from Pizza implementations.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Modular addition (Z/pZ) is the group-theoretic task used as the prototypical benchmark for studying algorithm discovery in neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Principal component analysis of embedding matrices is used throughout to visualize the geometric structure of learned representations and project gradient information.

title: Hierarchical Neural Simulation-Based Inference Over Event Ensembles
abstract: When analyzing real-world data it is common to work with event ensembles, which comprise sets of observations that collectively constrain the parameters of an underlying model of interest. Such models often have a hierarchical structure, where "local" parameters impact individual events and "global" parameters influence the entire dataset. We introduce practical approaches for frequentist and Bayesian dataset-wide probabilistic inference in cases where the likelihood is intractable, but simulations can be realized via a hierarchical forward model. We construct neural estimators for the likelihood(-ratio) or posterior and show that explicitly accounting for the model's hierarchical structure can lead to significantly tighter parameter constraints. We ground our discussion using case studies from the physical sciences, focusing on examples from particle physics and cosmology.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The central paradigm of the paper, enabling inference in models where the likelihood is intractable but simulations via a forward model are possible.

conceptDetails.name: hierarchical forward model
conceptDetails.description: A novel generative structure where local per-event parameters and global dataset-wide parameters jointly govern the data-generating process, enabling more efficient inference.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Neural estimators are constructed to approximate the dataset-wide posterior distribution over global and local model parameters.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: Neural likelihood-ratio estimators are developed as the frequentist alternative to posterior estimation for hypothesis testing over event ensembles.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
