conceptDetails.description: The paper develops Bayesian dataset-wide inference methods that exploit the hierarchical structure of the forward model for tighter parameter constraints.

conceptDetails.name: event ensemble learning
conceptDetails.description: A novel approach to learning from sets of observations collectively rather than individually, leveraging dataset-wide structure for improved parameter estimation.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Used as flexible neural density estimators to parameterize the learned likelihood or posterior distributions in the hierarchical inference framework.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Neural density estimation is used to approximate intractable likelihoods and posteriors across event ensembles of variable cardinality.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Permutation-invariant set-based architectures using attention (e.g., Deep Sets) are employed to aggregate information across variable-sized event ensembles.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates that hierarchy-aware inference produces significantly tighter parameter constraints than naive per-event combination approaches.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: Particle collider event data serves as one of the primary case studies, where hierarchical SBI is applied to constrain physics model parameters.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Strong gravitational lensing images are used as an astrophysics case study to constrain dark matter substructure properties via hierarchical inference.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Traditional MCMC methods serve as baselines against which the neural inference approaches are compared in speed and accuracy.

title: Development of the Topological Trigger for LHCb Run 3
abstract: The data-taking conditions expected in Run 3 of the LHCb experiment at CERN are unprecedented and challenging for the software and computing systems. Despite that, the LHCb collaboration pioneers the use of a software-only trigger system to cope with the increased event rate efficiently. The beauty physics programme of LHCb is heavily reliant on topological triggers. These are devoted to selecting beauty-hadron candidates inclusively, based on the characteristic decay topology and kinematic properties expected from beauty decays. The following proceeding describes the current progress of the Run 3 implementation of the topological triggers using Lipschitz monotonic neural networks. This architecture offers robustness under varying detector conditions and sensitivity to long-lived candidates, improving the possibility of discovering New Physics at LHCb.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central subject is the LHCb Run 3 software-only topological trigger that selects beauty-hadron decay candidates at 30 MHz input rate.

conceptDetails.name: lipschitz neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A neural network architecture whose output is constrained by a Lipschitz constant, bounding the maximum change in classifier response per unit change in input features.

conceptDetails.name: monotonic neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks with monotonicity constraints on selected input features, ensuring that more signal-like feature values always yield equal or higher classifier output.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Lipschitz constraints on the neural network response ensure robustness against varying detector conditions by bounding sensitivity to input perturbations.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The monotonic neural network architecture is designed to retain sensitivity to BSM candidates not present in training data, enabling discovery potential.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The trigger operates on proton-proton and heavy-ion collisions at the LHC, selecting beauty-hadron decays within the LHCb forward spectrometer.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Composite candidate reconstruction via vertex fitting and kinematic criteria is the preprocessing step that feeds into the neural network classifier.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The topological trigger is fundamentally a binary classifier distinguishing beauty-hadron decay candidates from backgrounds including charm, soft-QCD, and combinatorial events.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: Monotonicity ensures that candidates with features more extreme than training signal are scored higher, enabling detection of unknown long-lived BSM particles.

conceptDetails.name: particle tracking
conceptDetails.description: Track reconstruction from the LHCb tracking system provides the input features (momenta, impact parameters, vertex positions) used by the trigger algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Real-time alignment and calibration between HLT1 and HLT2 ensures the neural network operates on well-calibrated detector observables.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Boosted decision trees served as the Run 1 and Run 2 topological trigger baseline against which the new neural network architecture is compared.

title: Torus Actions on Moduli Spaces of Super Stable Maps of Genus Zero
abstract: We construct smooth $\mathbb{C}^*$-actions on the moduli spaces of super $J$-holomorphic curves as well as super stable curves and super stable maps of genus zero and fixed tree type such that their reduced spaces are torus invariant. Furthermore, we give explicit descriptions of the normal bundles to the fixed loci in terms of spinor bundles and their sections. Main steps to the construction of the $\mathbb{C}^*$-action are the proof that the charts of the moduli space of super $J$-holomorphic curves obtained by the implicit function theorem yield a smooth split atlas and a detailed study of the superconformal automorphism group of $\mathbb{P}_{\mathbb{C}}^{1|1}$ and its action on component fields.

conceptDetails.name: super moduli spaces
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central objects are moduli spaces of super J-holomorphic curves and super stable maps, extending classical moduli theory to the super geometric setting.

conceptDetails.name: supergeometry
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs the full machinery of supergeometry including super Riemann surfaces, supermanifolds, and split atlases throughout its constructions.

conceptDetails.name: torus localization
conceptDetails.description: C*-torus actions are constructed on super moduli spaces to enable localization techniques, motivated by extending Gromov-Witten invariants to the super case.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops super stable maps and super J-holomorphic curves as foundational tools for extending Gromov-Witten theory to the super setting, directly relevant to string theory's worldsheet formalism.

conceptDetails.name: spinor bundles
conceptDetails.description: Normal bundles to the fixed loci of the torus action are described explicitly in terms of spinor bundles and their sections, playing a key geometric role.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The superconformal automorphism group of projective superspace and its decomposition into SL(2,C) and supersymmetry transformations is a central object of study.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Super Riemann surfaces and superconformal automorphism groups are core objects from superstring/superconformal quantum field theory.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how torus symmetry actions interact with the structure of super moduli spaces and their reduced (classical) counterparts.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: The Gromov-Witten invariants and their super extensions, motivating this work, are closely connected to amplitude computations in string theory.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs and analyzes the geometry of super moduli spaces as supermanifolds, studying their atlases, split structures, and embeddings.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: Equivariant structures and torus actions on moduli spaces reflect the geometric symmetry-preservation themes common to geometric learning frameworks.

title: NuCLR: Nuclear Co-Learned Representations
abstract: We introduce Nuclear Co-Learned Representations (NuCLR), a deep learning model that predicts various nuclear observables, including binding and decay energies, and nuclear charge radii. The model is trained using a multi-task approach with shared representations and obtains state-of-the-art performance, achieving levels of precision that are crucial for understanding fundamental phenomena in nuclear (astro)physics. We also report an intriguing finding that the learned representation of NuCLR exhibits the prominent emergence of crucial aspects of the nuclear shell model, namely the shell structure, including the well-known magic numbers, and the Pauli Exclusion Principle. This suggests that the model is capable of capturing the underlying physical principles and that our approach has the potential to offer valuable insights into nuclear theory.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Core contribution of NuCLR: learning a shared, task-independent nuclear representation that captures underlying physical structure.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear shell emergence
conceptDetails.description: Novel finding: magic numbers, shell structure, and the Pauli Exclusion Principle spontaneously emerge in NuCLR's learned representations without being explicitly encoded.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The model jointly trains on binding energies, decay energies, and charge radii with shared representations to improve generalization.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Proton and neutron numbers are encoded as learnable high-dimensional embedding vectors, analogous to token embeddings in language models.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Key finding: the learned latent representations spontaneously recover the nuclear shell model, magic numbers, and the Pauli Exclusion Principle.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear observables
conceptDetails.description: The set of predicted physical quantities—binding energies, decay energies, and charge radii—that characterize atomic nuclei.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Principal component analysis applied to learned embeddings to visualize and interpret emergent shell structure.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: All nuclear observable prediction tasks (binding energy, charge radii) are formulated as regression problems.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: NuCLR acts as a computationally tractable surrogate for intractable ab initio QCD calculations of nuclear properties.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: The shared latent space separates proton and neutron contributions while encoding coupled shell structure across tasks.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Shared encoder weights transfer nuclear structure information across binding energy, decay energy, and charge radii tasks.

title: Quantum Computation and Simulation using Fermion-Pair Registers
abstract: We propose and analyze an approach to realize quantum computation and simulation using fermionic particles under quantum gas microscopes. Our work is inspired by a recent experimental demonstration of large-scale quantum registers, where tightly localized fermion pairs are used to encode qubits exhibiting long coherence time and robustness against laser intensity noise. We describe how to engineer the SWAP gate and high-fidelity controlled-phase gates by adjusting the fermion hopping as well as Feshbach interaction strengths. Combined with previously demonstrated single-qubit rotations, these gates establish the computational universality of the system. Furthermore, we show that 2D quantum Ising Hamiltonians with tunable transverse and longitudinal fields can be efficient simulated by modulating Feshbach interaction strengths. We present a sample-efficient protocol to characterize engineered gates and Hamiltonian dynamics based on an improved classical shadow process tomography that requires minimal experimental controls. Our work opens up new opportunities to harness existing ultracold quantum gases for quantum information sciences.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes and analyzes a complete universal quantum computation scheme using fermion-pair registers, establishing a universal gate set via SWAP and controlled-phase gates.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops schemes for efficiently simulating 2D quantum Ising Hamiltonians with tunable transverse and longitudinal fields by modulating Feshbach interaction strengths.

conceptDetails.name: fermion-pair qubits
conceptDetails.description: A novel qubit encoding using the common and relative motional degrees of freedom of tightly localized fermion pairs in optical lattice sites.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Qubits are encoded in the vibrational states of fermion pairs in anharmonic traps, exploiting motional degrees of freedom as the logical basis.

conceptDetails.name: feshbach resonance
conceptDetails.description: Magnetically tunable s-wave interactions between atoms, used as the primary control knob for engineering two-qubit gates and driving qubit rotations.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Two-qubit entangling gates (CPHASE) are central to achieving computational universality, creating quantum correlations between adjacent fermion-pair qubits.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The Fermi-Hubbard model and derived effective Hamiltonians are the core theoretical framework used to engineer quantum gates and simulate many-body physics.

conceptDetails.name: shadow process tomography
conceptDetails.description: An improved classical shadow-based protocol for characterizing quantum processes (gates and dynamics) with provable sample efficiency and minimal experimental overhead.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Fermionic exchange symmetry protects qubit coherence and robustness against laser intensity noise, making the register long-lived.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: A sample-efficient classical shadow process tomography protocol is designed to characterize gates and Hamiltonian dynamics with minimal experimental controls.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The 2D quantum Ising model simulated in this work hosts quantum phase transitions governed by tunable transverse and longitudinal fields.

title: Discovering New Interpretable Conservation Laws as Sparse Invariants
abstract: Discovering conservation laws for a given dynamical system is important but challenging. In a theorist setup (differential equations and basis functions are both known), we propose the Sparse Invariant Detector (SID), an algorithm that auto-discovers conservation laws from differential equations. Its algorithmic simplicity allows robustness and interpretability of the discovered conserved quantities. We show that SID is able to rediscover known and even discover new conservation laws in a variety of systems. For two examples in fluid mechanics and atmospheric chemistry, SID discovers 14 and 3 conserved quantities, respectively, where only 12 and 2 were previously known to domain experts.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: The primary target of discovery — SID automatically finds complete, independent, interpretable conserved quantities from differential equations.

conceptDetails.name: sparse invariant detection
conceptDetails.description: Novel algorithmic contribution — SID combines null-space solving with sparse orthogonal rotation to find minimal, interpretable conservation laws.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparsity is the core technique used to enforce interpretability of discovered conservation laws via L1-norm minimization over orthogonal rotations.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: SID is an algorithm for automatic discovery of conservation laws without human enumeration of specific candidates.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: SVD of the constraint matrix G is used to identify the null space, which spans all conserved quantities in the given basis.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A central design goal — SID produces symbolic, sparse formulas for conserved quantities rather than black-box representations.

conceptDetails.name: null space enumeration
conceptDetails.description: Novel technique — completeness of discovered conservation laws is guaranteed by exhaustive enumeration of the SVD null space of the constraint matrix.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Hamiltonian mechanics is one of the canonical test cases for SID, and the framework is explicitly shown to subsume Hamiltonian systems.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Discovering conservation laws from known dynamics is an inverse problem — inferring structure (invariants) from equations of motion.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Conservation laws are deeply connected to symmetries (Noether's theorem), and SID operates in this theoretical context.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: SID is framed as a tool that augments human scientists by handling hypothesis testing while scientists focus on hypothesis formulation.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The basis-function parametrization of H(x) acts as a structured surrogate for the space of possible conserved quantities.

title: Learning Linear Groups in Neural Networks
abstract: Employing equivariance in neural networks leads to greater parameter efficiency and improved generalization performance through the encoding of domain knowledge in the architecture; however, the majority of existing approaches require an a priori specification of the desired symmetries. We present a neural network architecture, Linear Group Networks (LGNs), for learning linear groups acting on the weight space of neural networks. Linear groups are desirable due to their inherent interpretability, as they can be represented as finite matrices. LGNs learn groups without any supervision or knowledge of the hidden symmetries in the data and the groups can be mapped to well known operations in machine learning. We use LGNs to learn groups on multiple datasets while considering different downstream tasks; we demonstrate that the linear group structure depends on both the data distribution and the considered task.

conceptDetails.name: linear group learning
conceptDetails.description: Novel framework for learning elements of the general linear group GL_d(K) to construct cyclic groups acting on neural network weight spaces without prior symmetry specification.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper builds on and extends equivariant neural network theory by learning the equivariant structure (the group itself) from data rather than specifying it a priori.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Linear groups, cyclic groups, and the general linear group GL_d(K) form the mathematical foundation of the entire LGN framework.

conceptDetails.name: weight space symmetry
conceptDetails.description: The paper uniquely situates group actions in the weight space of networks rather than the input space, enabling symmetry discovery in single-task settings.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: LGNs discover and encode hidden symmetries present in datasets without prior knowledge, enabling data-driven symmetry learning.

conceptDetails.name: cyclic group construction
conceptDetails.description: LGNs construct finite cyclic groups from a single learned generator matrix, ensuring valid group structure by design.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper contributes to geometric deep learning by providing a constructive framework for learning group structure that generalizes across architectures.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A central motivation for using linear groups is their interpretability as finite matrices whose actions can be visualized and mapped to known ML operations.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional networks serve as the motivating architecture and experimental backbone; LGNs learn filter sets that generalize convolution via group actions.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: LGNs learn structured representations of weight-space transformations that capture task- and distribution-dependent symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: LGNs learn group structure without any supervision or labels about the hidden symmetries, making the approach fully unsupervised.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Unfolded sparse coding networks are used as the experimental architecture because their per-layer data-space filters make group actions human-interpretable.

title: Machine Learning for Quantum-Enhanced Gravitational-Wave Observatories
abstract: Machine learning has become an effective tool for processing the extensive data sets produced by large physics experiments. Gravitational-wave detectors are now listening to the universe with quantum-enhanced sensitivity, accomplished with the injection of squeezed vacuum states. Squeezed state preparation and injection is operationally complicated, as well as highly sensitive to environmental fluctuations and variations in the interferometer state. Achieving and maintaining optimal squeezing levels is a challenging problem and will require development of new techniques to reach the lofty targets set by design goals for future observing runs and next-generation detectors. We use machine learning techniques to predict the squeezing level during the third observing run of the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) based on auxiliary data streams, and offer interpretations of our models to identify and quantify salient sources of squeezing degradation. The development of these techniques lays the groundwork for future efforts to optimize squeezed state injection in gravitational-wave detectors, with the goal of enabling closed-loop control of the squeezer subsystem by an agent based on machine learning.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies ML to optimize quantum noise reduction in LIGO gravitational-wave detectors during the third observing run (O3).

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Squeezed vacuum states are the central physical phenomenon; the paper predicts and optimizes their injection into the interferometer readout port.

conceptDetails.name: squeezed state optimization
conceptDetails.description: Novel task of using ML to maintain optimal squeezing levels against environmental and interferometer fluctuations in real detector operations.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Sensitivity analysis on trained neural networks is used to identify and quantify salient sources of squeezing degradation in the LIGO squeezer subsystem.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural network models serve as surrogates that predict squeezing level from auxiliary witness channels without direct physical measurement.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The core ML task is predicting a continuous squeezing level value from squeezer and environmental monitor channels.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: A genetic algorithm selects the most statistically relevant auxiliary data channels from hundreds of monitor streams as model inputs.

conceptDetails.name: genetic feature selection
conceptDetails.description: Genetic algorithm applied specifically to rank and select witness channels based on their correlation with squeezing performance.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: The sensing-side ML framework is designed to evolve into a closed-loop active control system that actuates on the squeezer subsystem.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Explicitly named as the target future architecture for a closed-loop ML agent that will autonomously control the squeezer subsystem.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Auxiliary witness channels serve as indirect observational signals whose correlations with squeezing degradation are extracted and analyzed.

title: Materialistic: Selecting Similar Materials in Images
abstract: Separating an image into meaningful underlying components is a crucial first step for both editing and understanding images. We present a method capable of selecting the regions of a photograph exhibiting the same material as an artist-chosen area. Our proposed approach is robust to shading, specular highlights, and cast shadows, enabling selection in real images. As we do not rely on semantic segmentation (different woods or metal should not be selected together), we formulate the problem as a similarity-based grouping problem based on a user-provided image location. In particular, we propose to leverage the unsupervised DINO features coupled with a proposed Cross-Similarity module and an MLP head to extract material similarities in an image. We train our model on a new synthetic image dataset, that we release. We show that our method generalizes well to real-world images. We carefully analyze our model's behavior on varying material properties and lighting. Additionally, we evaluate it against a hand-annotated benchmark of 50 real photographs. We further demonstrate our model on a set of applications, including material editing, in-video selection, and retrieval of object photographs with similar materials.

conceptDetails.name: cross-similarity feature weighting
conceptDetails.description: A novel module proposed in the paper that modulates multi-scale features conditioned on a user query pixel to produce material similarity scores.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: DINO features are adapted and specialized through a multi-scale neural network to learn material-specific representations robust to shading and geometric variations.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper leverages DINO, an unsupervised self-supervised vision transformer, as a fixed feature extractor to compute patch-level image representations for material similarity.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline extracts multi-scale patch-level features from images using a pretrained DINO backbone as the core feature extraction mechanism.

conceptDetails.name: cross-image material similarity
conceptDetails.description: A novel capability allowing a query embedding from one image to select matching materials in other images, enabling video material selection and image retrieval.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: The architecture is inspired by vision transformers (ViT) and employs DINO (a self-supervised ViT) as the backbone for image feature extraction.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Query embeddings extracted from a user-selected pixel are used to compute similarity scores across all image patches, enabling cross-image material retrieval.

conceptDetails.name: synthetic-to-real generalization
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained exclusively on 50,000 synthetic physically-based rendered images but generalizes to real-world photographs, a key contribution of the work.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: The Cross-Similarity Feature Weighting module uses attention-like mechanisms to modulate features at different resolutions based on a user-provided query location.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Pretrained DINO features trained on natural images are transferred to the material selection task, enabling generalization from synthetic training data to real photographs.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The model is trained to distinguish same-material from different-material regions, implicitly relying on contrastive similarity objectives.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Material selection is framed as an ill-posed inverse problem: recovering material identity from pixels that conflate geometry, illumination, and reflectance.

title: Multiple Peaks and a Long Precursor in the Type IIn Supernova 2021qqp: An Energetic Explosion in a Complex Circumstellar Environment
abstract: We present optical photometry and spectroscopy of the Type IIn supernova (SN) 2021qqp. Its unusual light curve is marked by a long precursor for $\approx300$ days, a rapid increase in brightness for $\approx60$ days, and then a sharp increase of $\approx1.6$ mag in only a few days to a first peak of $M_r \approx -19.5$ mag. The light curve then declines rapidly until it re-brightens to a second distinct peak of $M_r \approx -17.3$ mag centered at $\approx335$ days after the first peak. The spectra are dominated by Balmer lines with a complex morphology, including a narrow component with a width of $\approx 1300$ km s$^{-1}$ (first peak) and $\approx 2500$ km s$^{-1}$ (second peak) that we associate with the circumstellar medium (CSM) and a P Cygni component with an absorption velocity of $\approx 8500$ km s$^{-1}$ (first peak) and $\approx 5600$ km s$^{-1}$ (second peak) that we associate with the SN-CSM interaction shell. Using the luminosity and velocity evolution, we construct a flexible analytical model, finding two significant mass-loss episodes with peak mass loss rates of $\approx 10$ and $\approx 5\,M_{\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$ about $0.8$ and $2$ yr before explosion, respectively, with a total CSM mass of $\approx 2-4\,M_{\odot}$. We show that the most recent mass-loss episode could explain the precursor for the year preceding the explosion. The SN ejecta mass is constrained to be $\approx 5-30\,M_{\odot}$ for an explosion energy of $\approx (3-10)\times10^{51}$ erg. We discuss eruptive massive stars (luminous blue variable, pulsational pair instability) and an extreme stellar merger with a compact object as possible progenitor channels.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: The dominant power source of SN 2021qqp is shock interaction between SN ejecta and complex circumstellar medium, producing the observed spectral features and multi-peaked light curve.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents detailed observational analysis of Type IIn supernova 2021qqp, contributing to understanding of the heterogeneous SN IIn class defined by narrow Balmer emission lines from CSM interaction.

conceptDetails.name: mass-loss episodes
conceptDetails.description: Two distinct eruptive mass-loss episodes with peak rates of ~10 and ~5 solar masses per year occurring 0.8 and 2 years before explosion are identified and modeled as the origin of the complex CSM.

conceptDetails.name: multi-peak light curve
conceptDetails.description: The double-peaked light curve with a precursor spanning ~300 days is the defining observational feature of SN 2021qqp, directly encoding the episodic pre-explosion mass-loss history.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates progenitor channels including luminous blue variables, pulsational pair instability, and stellar mergers with compact objects to explain the pre-explosion mass-loss history of SN 2021qqp.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring pre-explosion mass-loss history, CSM structure, and progenitor properties from observed light curves and spectra constitutes an inverse problem central to the paper's methodology.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian methods are used to constrain model parameters including CSM mass, ejecta mass, explosion energy, and mass-loss rates from the light curve and spectral evolution.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: A flexible analytical model is constructed to extract CSM and SN properties from combined photometric and spectroscopic data, serving as a surrogate for full radiation hydrodynamics simulations.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Analytical model fitting to photometric and spectroscopic observables is used to regress physical parameters such as CSM density profile, ejecta mass, and explosion energy.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The unusual multi-peak light curve and long precursor of SN 2021qqp represent an anomalous transient event that motivates detailed follow-up and modeling to understand its physical origin.

title: Constraint of pionless EFT using two-nucleon spectra from lattice QCD
abstract: Finite-volume pionless effective field theory (FVEFT$_{ π\!/ }$) at next-to-leading order (NLO) is used to analyze the two-nucleon lattice QCD spectrum of Ref.~\cite{Amarasinghe:2021lqa}, performed at quark masses corresponding to a pion mass of approximately $800 $ MeV. Specifically, the effective theory is formulated in finite volume, and variational sets of wave functions are optimized using differential programming. Using these wave functions projected to the appropriate finite-volume symmetry group, variational bounds from FVEFT$_{π\!/ }$ are obtained for the ground state, as well as excited states. By comparison with the lattice QCD GEVP spectrum, different low energy constants (LECs) are constrained. Relativistic corrections are incorporated, allowing for the extractions of NLO LECs, as well as the leading $s$-$d$-wave mixing term in the deuteron channel.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The two-nucleon LQCD spectrum at 800 MeV pion mass serves as the primary dataset for constraining pionless EFT low energy constants.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Pionless EFT at NLO is the core theoretical framework, providing a systematic low-momentum expansion for nucleon-nucleon interactions.

conceptDetails.name: finite-volume eft
conceptDetails.description: Pionless EFT is directly formulated in a finite volume to match LQCD calculations without requiring an infinite-volume extrapolation.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The Hamiltonian formulation of pionless EFT enables variational energy bounds via optimized correlated Gaussian wave function ansätze.

conceptDetails.name: variational wave functions
conceptDetails.description: Correlated Gaussian ansätze optimized via differential programming provide variational bounds on ground and excited state two-nucleon energies.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Wave functions are projected onto irreducible representations of the finite-volume cubic symmetry group to match LQCD symmetry sectors.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The Generalized Eigenvalue Problem (GEVP) is used to extract the multi-level two-nucleon energy spectrum from lattice QCD correlation matrices.

conceptDetails.name: differential programming
conceptDetails.description: Automatic differentiation optimizes variational wave functions with respect to EFT energy bounds, replacing manual tuning of ansatz parameters.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The NLO EFT Lagrangian with contact interactions and two-derivative operators defines the operator basis used to constrain low energy constants.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Regularization and renormalization of contact interactions in the pionless EFT Hamiltonian are required to handle ultraviolet divergences.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Statistical parameter estimation is used to constrain NLO low energy constants by comparing variational EFT energy bounds to the LQCD GEVP spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Underlying lattice QCD calculations employ Monte Carlo importance sampling to evaluate path integrals on the discrete spacetime lattice.

title: Symbolic Regression on FPGAs for Fast Machine Learning Inference
abstract: The high-energy physics community is investigating the potential of deploying machine-learning-based solutions on Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) to enhance physics sensitivity while still meeting data processing time constraints. In this contribution, we introduce a novel end-to-end procedure that utilizes a machine learning technique called symbolic regression (SR). It searches the equation space to discover algebraic relations approximating a dataset. We use PySR (a software to uncover these expressions based on an evolutionary algorithm) and extend the functionality of hls4ml (a package for machine learning inference in FPGAs) to support PySR-generated expressions for resource-constrained production environments. Deep learning models often optimize the top metric by pinning the network size because the vast hyperparameter space prevents an extensive search for neural architecture. Conversely, SR selects a set of models on the Pareto front, which allows for optimizing the performance-resource trade-off directly. By embedding symbolic forms, our implementation can dramatically reduce the computational resources needed to perform critical tasks. We validate our method on a physics benchmark: the multiclass classification of jets produced in simulated proton-proton collisions at the CERN Large Hadron Collider. We show that our approach can approximate a 3-layer neural network using an inference model that achieves up to a 13-fold decrease in execution time, down to 5 ns, while still preserving more than 90% approximation accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The physics benchmark used to validate the approach: multiclass tagging of jets from quark, gluon, W, Z, and top quark origins at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The real-time data filtering system at the LHC that imposes sub-microsecond latency constraints motivating the use of lightweight FPGA-based inference.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental context of proton-proton collisions at the CERN LHC operating at 40 MHz, driving the need for resource-efficient inference.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A key advantage of symbolic regression over deep learning: the discovered models are human-readable algebraic equations providing physical insight.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression is used as a surrogate to approximate a 3-layer neural network while achieving a 13-fold reduction in execution time.

conceptDetails.name: classification

conceptDetails.name: pareto front optimization
conceptDetails.description: PySR selects models on the Pareto front of accuracy versus complexity, enabling direct optimization of the performance-resource trade-off for FPGA deployment.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central motivation of the work: reducing computational resource usage dramatically to meet the tight latency and hardware budgets of LHC trigger systems.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic expressions produce compact, sparse algebraic models that require far fewer computational resources than dense neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Sixteen physics-motivated high-level jet substructure features serve as inputs to the symbolic regression models.

title: A Spectral Metric for Collider Geometry
abstract: By quantifying the distance between two collider events, one can triangulate a metric space and reframe collider data analysis as computational geometry. One popular geometric approach is to first represent events as an energy flow on an idealized celestial sphere and then define the metric in terms of optimal transport in two dimensions. In this paper, we advocate for representing events in terms of a spectral function that encodes pairwise particle angles and products of particle energies, which enables a metric distance defined in terms of one-dimensional optimal transport. This approach has the advantage of automatically incorporating obvious isometries of the data, like rotations about the colliding beam axis. It also facilitates first-principles calculations, since there are simple closed-form expressions for optimal transport in one dimension. Up to isometries and event sets of measure zero, the spectral representation is unique, so the metric on the space of spectral functions is a metric on the space of events. At lowest order in perturbation theory in electron-positron collisions, our metric is simply the summed squared invariant masses of the two event hemispheres. Going to higher orders, we present predictions for the distribution of metric distances between jets in fixed-order and resummed perturbation theory as well as in parton-shower generators. Finally, we speculate on whether the spectral approach could furnish a useful metric on the space of quantum field theories.

conceptDetails.name: spectral emd
conceptDetails.description: Novel metric introduced in this paper: the Earth Mover's Distance defined on one-dimensional spectral functions of collider events.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The spectral EMD is defined via one-dimensional optimal transport using quantile matching, which admits closed-form expressions unlike the two-dimensional case.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is representing collider events as spectral functions encoding pairwise particle angles and energy products, reducing the metric problem to 1D.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The spectral metric is developed and validated in the context of high-energy collider experiments, with explicit calculations for electron-positron and LHC collisions.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jets serve as the primary testbed for the spectral EMD, with perturbative calculations and parton-shower comparisons performed on jet pairs.

conceptDetails.name: collider event geometry
conceptDetails.description: The overarching framework of treating the space of collider events as a metric space amenable to computational geometry techniques.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: A key advantage of the spectral approach is that it automatically incorporates isometries such as azimuthal rotations about the beam axis.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames collider data analysis as computational geometry by defining a metric on the manifold of collider events.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper speculates that the spectral approach could furnish a useful metric on the space of quantum field theories via a spectral sigma-MD.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning

title: Seeing is Believing: Brain-Inspired Modular Training for Mechanistic Interpretability
abstract: We introduce Brain-Inspired Modular Training (BIMT), a method for making neural networks more modular and interpretable. Inspired by brains, BIMT embeds neurons in a geometric space and augments the loss function with a cost proportional to the length of each neuron connection. We demonstrate that BIMT discovers useful modular neural networks for many simple tasks, revealing compositional structures in symbolic formulas, interpretable decision boundaries and features for classification, and mathematical structure in algorithmic datasets. The ability to directly see modules with the naked eye can complement current mechanistic interpretability strategies such as probes, interventions or staring at all weights.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: BIMT is explicitly designed for mechanistic interpretability, enabling direct visual identification of modular circuits in trained networks.

conceptDetails.name: modular training
conceptDetails.description: BIMT is a novel training paradigm that induces emergent modularity in non-modular networks without requiring prior knowledge of module structure.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: BIMT augments the standard prediction loss with a locality regularization term proportional to connection length times weight magnitude, driving the core method.

conceptDetails.name: locality regularization
conceptDetails.description: A novel regularization that penalizes long-range neuron connections proportionally to distance, encouraging spatially local computation.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: BIMT combines L1 sparsity with locality regularization, producing sparse modular networks as a primary output.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: Neurons are embedded in a Riemannian geometric space (2D/3D Euclidean) so that spatial distances define connection costs and drive modularity.

conceptDetails.name: neuron swapping
conceptDetails.description: A discrete optimization step that permutes neurons within layers to minimize connection cost, complementing continuous gradient descent.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: BIMT learns structured, modular representations that expose compositional and independence structure in symbolic formulas and algorithmic datasets.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression on Feynman-style formulas is a primary benchmark, where BIMT reveals compositional and independence structure.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: BIMT is applied to classification tasks, producing interpretable decision boundaries and disentangled features.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: BIMT is generalized to transformer architectures, demonstrating its applicability beyond fully-connected networks.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Algorithmic modular arithmetic tasks are analyzed through the lens of group representations, with BIMT revealing voting-based mechanisms consistent with group structure.

title: Normalizing flows for lattice gauge theory in arbitrary space-time dimension
abstract: Applications of normalizing flows to the sampling of field configurations in lattice gauge theory have so far been explored almost exclusively in two space-time dimensions. We report new algorithmic developments of gauge-equivariant flow architectures facilitating the generalization to higher-dimensional lattice geometries. Specifically, we discuss masked autoregressive transformations with tractable and unbiased Jacobian determinants, a key ingredient for scalable and asymptotically exact flow-based sampling algorithms. For concreteness, results from a proof-of-principle application to SU(3) lattice gauge theory in four space-time dimensions are reported.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core generative model framework used to learn and sample from lattice gauge field configuration distributions, extended here to higher-dimensional geometries.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain, with the paper developing gauge-equivariant flow architectures for SU(N) gauge fields in arbitrary space-time dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Gauge-equivariant flow architectures that respect local gauge symmetry are the central methodological contribution enabling generalization to higher dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: Four-dimensional SU(3) gauge theory serves as the proof-of-principle application motivating all algorithmic developments.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC sampling with Metropolis-Hastings acceptance is the target sampling framework that flow-based proposals interface with for asymptotically exact results.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Maintaining gauge invariance and equivariance throughout every flow layer is an explicit architectural design constraint.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Tractable and unbiased computation of the model density q(U) via Jacobian determinants is the key ingredient enabling exact likelihood evaluation.

conceptDetails.name: masked coupling layers
conceptDetails.description: Novel application of masking patterns to decompose gauge link transformations into tractably invertible conditional element-wise autoregressive operations.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: SU(N) Lie group structure governs the manifold of gauge links and constrains the allowable invertible flow transformations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework underpinning path integrals, partition functions, Wilson loops, and the continuum limit of lattice formulations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Scaling flow-based sampling from two to four space-time dimensions with tractable Jacobians is the primary algorithmic challenge addressed.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral flows transform eigenvalue spectra of untraced Wilson loops as a gauge-equivariant mechanism for updating gauge links.

conceptDetails.name: topological freezing
conceptDetails.description: The critical slowing-down problem in LQCD where topological sectors freeze at fine lattice spacings, providing the core physics motivation for flow-based sampling.

title: Dynamic Sparse Training with Structured Sparsity
abstract: Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) methods achieve state-of-the-art results in sparse neural network training, matching the generalization of dense models while enabling sparse training and inference. Although the resulting models are highly sparse and theoretically less computationally expensive, achieving speedups with unstructured sparsity on real-world hardware is challenging. In this work, we propose a sparse-to-sparse DST method, Structured RigL (SRigL), to learn a variant of fine-grained structured N:M sparsity by imposing a constant fan-in constraint. Using our empirical analysis of existing DST methods at high sparsity, we additionally employ a neuron ablation method which enables SRigL to achieve state-of-the-art sparse-to-sparse structured DST performance on a variety of Neural Network (NN) architectures. Using a 90% sparse linear layer, we demonstrate a real-world acceleration of 3.4x/2.5x on CPU for online inference and 1.7x/13.0x on GPU for inference with a batch size of 256 when compared to equivalent dense/unstructured (CSR) sparse layers, respectively.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is a structured sparse neural network training method that achieves hardware-efficient sparsity patterns.

conceptDetails.name: dynamic sparse training
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends the RigL dynamic sparse training framework to learn structured sparsity patterns during training rather than after.

conceptDetails.name: structured n:m sparsity
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces constant fan-in structured sparsity as a variant of N:M sparsity that enables real-world hardware acceleration.

conceptDetails.name: neuron ablation
conceptDetails.description: A novel method employed in SRigL to handle high-sparsity regimes where entire neurons become inactive, matching unstructured DST generalization.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: SRigL demonstrates real-world inference speedups (3.4x CPU, 13x GPU) by translating theoretical sparsity into practical hardware acceleration.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The method employs saliency-based grow/prune criteria and neuron ablation strategies to guide sparse mask updates during training.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: SRigL is evaluated across multiple neural network architectures including convolutional networks (ResNet, etc.).

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: The method builds on existing DST algorithms (RigL) and adapts them with structured constraints, representing a form of algorithmic refinement.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Empirical analysis of neuron output-norm variance provides theoretical grounding for why constant fan-in improves stability and inference efficiency.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Comparison against multiple DST baselines (SET, RigL, SNFS, DSR, Top-KAST, MEST) situates SRigL within the broader sparse training landscape.

title: Pileup and Infrared Radiation Annihilation (PIRANHA): A Paradigm for Continuous Jet Grooming
abstract: Jet grooming is an important strategy for analyzing relativistic particle collisions in the presence of contaminating radiation. Most jet grooming techniques introduce hard cutoffs to remove soft radiation, leading to discontinuous behavior and associated experimental and theoretical challenges. In this paper, we introduce Pileup and Infrared Radiation Annihilation (PIRANHA), a paradigm for continuous jet grooming that overcomes the discontinuity and infrared sensitivity of hard-cutoff grooming procedures. We motivate PIRANHA from the perspective of optimal transport and the Energy Mover's Distance and review Apollonius Subtraction and Iterated Voronoi Subtraction as examples of PIRANHA-style grooming. We then introduce a new tree-based implementation of PIRANHA, Recursive Subtraction, with reduced computational costs. Finally, we demonstrate the performance of Recursive Subtraction in mitigating sensitivity to soft distortions from hadronization and detector effects, and additive contamination from pileup and the underlying event.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central focus is jet grooming — techniques to clean hadronic jets produced in high-energy collisions by removing soft contamination and pileup.

conceptDetails.name: continuous jet grooming
conceptDetails.description: PIRANHA introduces a new paradigm of grooming that avoids hard cutoffs, ensuring continuity in event space and reducing sensitivity to soft pollution.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: PIRANHA is motivated from the perspective of optimal transport theory, using the Energy Mover's Distance as a geometric foundation for continuous grooming.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses challenges in analyzing particle collisions at colliders, including pileup mitigation and jet substructure measurement.

conceptDetails.name: energy mover's distance
conceptDetails.description: The Energy Mover's Distance, an optimal transport metric between collider events, provides the geometric motivation for the PIRANHA grooming paradigm.

conceptDetails.name: pileup mitigation
conceptDetails.description: A primary application of PIRANHA is removing additive contamination from overlapping proton-proton collisions (pileup) while preserving jet properties.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper performs perturbative QCD calculations and leading-logarithm resummation to analytically characterize the behavior of grooming algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: Discontinuities in grooming algorithms cause difficulties in experimental unfolding, which PIRANHA aims to alleviate through continuous behavior.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations (e.g., Pythia) are used extensively to evaluate grooming performance under hadronization, pileup, and underlying event conditions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Soft-collinear effective theory concepts underpin the perturbative and resummed calculations used to study grooming discontinuities.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Jet grooming and substructure techniques are used to distinguish signal jets (W, top) from QCD background, relating to signal/anomaly detection in collider data.

title: Prometheus: An Open-Source Neutrino Telescope Simulation
abstract: Neutrino telescopes are gigaton-scale neutrino detectors comprised of individual light-detection units. Though constructed from simple building blocks, they have opened a new window to the Universe and are able to probe center-of-mass energies that are comparable to those of collider experiments. \prometheus{} is a new, open-source simulation tailored for this kind of detector. Our package, which is written in a combination of \texttt{C++} and \texttt{Python} provides a balance of ease of use and performance and allows the user to simulate a neutrino telescope with arbitrary geometry deployed in ice or water. \prometheus{} simulates the neutrino interactions in the volume surrounding the detector, computes the light yield of the hadronic shower and the out-going lepton, propagates the photons in the medium, and records their arrival times and position in user-defined regions. Finally, \prometheus{} events are serialized into a \texttt{parquet} file, which is a compact and interoperational file format that allows prompt access to the events for further analysis.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Prometheus is an open-source simulation framework for neutrino telescopes, modeling the full chain from neutrino interaction to photon detection.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper is centered on simulating gigaton-scale neutrino detectors that detect Cherenkov photons from neutrino interactions in ice or water.

conceptDetails.name: open-source neutrino simulator
conceptDetails.description: Prometheus is a novel publicly available, modular simulation package supporting arbitrary detector geometries in water and ice, filling a gap left by closed-source tools.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Prometheus uses Monte Carlo simulation throughout, including for neutrino event injection, lepton propagation, light yield computation, and photon transport.

conceptDetails.name: photon propagation
conceptDetails.description: Prometheus implements ray tracing routines (PPC for ice, Hyperion for water) to propagate Cherenkov photons from interaction to optical modules.

conceptDetails.name: cherenkov light modeling
conceptDetails.description: The framework explicitly models Cherenkov photon emission from charged leptons and hadronic showers as the primary detection mechanism in neutrino telescopes.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Prometheus generates simulated events in a standardized format to facilitate the development of reconstruction techniques applicable across different neutrino telescope experiments.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: Prometheus provides an end-to-end, modular simulation workflow serialized to interoperable parquet files, enabling integration with broader analysis and ML pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The simulator produces structured output enabling downstream inference and reconstruction, supporting machine-learning-based analysis pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The Fennel module emulates hadronic and electromagnetic shower light yields based on parameterizations of full GEANT4 simulations.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Parameterized light yield models derived from GEANT4 simulations serve as surrogate models for shower emission within Prometheus.

title: Searching for ribbons with machine learning
abstract: We apply Bayesian optimization and reinforcement learning to a problem in topology: the question of when a knot bounds a ribbon disk. This question is relevant in an approach to disproving the four-dimensional smooth Poincaré conjecture; using our programs, we rule out many potential counterexamples to the conjecture. We also show that the programs are successful in detecting many ribbon knots in the range of up to 70 crossings.

conceptDetails.name: knot ribbon detection
conceptDetails.description: Novel application domain: determining whether a knot bounds a ribbon disk using ML search over move sequences.

conceptDetails.name: low-dimensional topology
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical field of study — knots, ribbon disks, and the smooth 4-dimensional Poincaré conjecture — that motivates the entire paper.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Used to search for sequences of knot diagram moves (Reidemeister and band moves) that prove a knot is ribbon, framed as a game-playing problem.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian optimization is applied to tune the random walker's move selection strategy for finding ribbon disks, outperforming RL on custom benchmarks.

conceptDetails.name: poincaré conjecture search
conceptDetails.description: Novel application: using ribbon detection to systematically eliminate potential counterexamples to the smooth 4D Poincaré conjecture.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The programs automatically discover ribbon disk certificates for hundreds of knots, producing rigorous mathematical results beyond paper-and-pencil techniques.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: A Bayesian-optimized random walker (stochastic search) is central to the ribbon detection approach.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline integrates ML search with topological verification to rule out counterexamples to the smooth 4D Poincaré conjecture at scale.

title: Correlation function distributions for O(N) lattice field theories in the disordered phase
abstract: Numerical computations in strongly-interacting quantum field theories are often performed using Monte-Carlo sampling methods. A key task in these calculations is to estimate the value of a given physical quantity from the distribution of stochastic samples that are generated using the Monte-Carlo method. Typically, the sample mean and sample variance are used to define the expectation values and uncertainties of computed quantities. However, the Monte-Carlo sample distribution contains more information than these basic properties and it is useful to investigate it more generally. In this work, the exact form of the probability distributions of two-point correlation functions at zero momentum in O(N) lattice field theories in the disordered phase and in infinite volume are determined. These distributions allow for a robust investigation of the efficacy of the Monte-Carlo sampling procedure and are shown also to allow for improved estimators of the target physical quantity to be constructed. The theoretical expectations are shown to agree with numerical calculations in the O(2) model.

conceptDetails.name: correlation function pdf
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: exact analytic probability distribution functions for two-point correlation functions in O(N) models at zero momentum.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the statistical properties of Monte Carlo sampling in lattice field theories, constructing exact probability distributions of correlation function samples.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: O(N) lattice field theories are the primary theoretical framework studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper works within the lattice field theory formalism, discretizing spacetime to study O(N) models numerically.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The work characterizes the stochastic nature of Monte Carlo samples through exact probability distribution functions.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops improved estimators and statistical tests for expectation values and uncertainties derived from Monte Carlo samples.

conceptDetails.name: improved estimators
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: construction of minimum-variance estimators for correlation function means using known PDF structure.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The O(N) models are studied in the disordered phase, with reference to phenomena like the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses the signal-to-noise problem in lattice QFT, where correlation functions degrade exponentially with separation.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The O(N) symmetry structure of the models determines the form of the correlation function distributions derived.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: The derived PDFs enable statistical tests of thermalization and autocorrelation in Monte Carlo samples.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Knowledge of exact PDFs enables construction of improved estimators analogous to Bayesian minimum-variance estimation.

title: GenPhys: From Physical Processes to Generative Models
abstract: Since diffusion models (DM) and the more recent Poisson flow generative models (PFGM) are inspired by physical processes, it is reasonable to ask: Can physical processes offer additional new generative models? We show that the answer is yes. We introduce a general family, Generative Models from Physical Processes (GenPhys), where we translate partial differential equations (PDEs) describing physical processes to generative models. We show that generative models can be constructed from s-generative PDEs (s for smooth). GenPhys subsume the two existing generative models (DM and PFGM) and even give rise to new families of generative models, e.g., "Yukawa Generative Models" inspired from weak interactions. On the other hand, some physical processes by default do not belong to the GenPhys family, e.g., the wave equation and the Schrödinger equation, but could be made into the GenPhys family with some modifications. Our goal with GenPhys is to explore and expand the design space of generative models.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces GenPhys, a unified framework for constructing generative models from physical PDEs, subsuming diffusion models and Poisson flow generative models.

conceptDetails.name: s-generative pdes
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept introduced in this paper: a class of PDEs satisfying two conditions (density flow equivalence and smoothing over time) that can be converted into valid generative models.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion models are one of the two existing generative models shown to be a special case of the GenPhys framework, derived from the diffusion (heat) equation.

conceptDetails.name: dispersion relations
conceptDetails.description: A novel use of dispersion relations from physics to rigorously determine whether a PDE is s-generative by analyzing the decay rate of high-frequency vs. low-frequency modes.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks in GenPhys are trained to approximate velocity fields and source/sink terms derived from physical PDEs, connecting physics simulation to deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: yukawa generative models
conceptDetails.description: A novel family of generative models derived from the Yukawa (screened Poisson) equation inspired by weak nuclear interactions, introduced as a new contribution of GenPhys.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: Score-based modeling underlies the neural network training step in GenPhys, where a network is trained to approximate the velocity field derived from the physical PDE.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: GenPhys constructs generative models that implicitly define probability densities through PDE-driven density flows, enabling sampling from complex data distributions.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: GenPhys generalizes flow matching by deriving continuous normalizing flows from physical PDEs, including non-conservative (branching) density flows.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The GenPhys framework incorporates branching stochastic processes to handle non-conservative PDEs where probability mass is created or destroyed during generation.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Generating samples by running PDE dynamics backward from a simple prior to a complex data distribution is an inverse problem central to the GenPhys framework.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The Yukawa interaction from particle physics (screened Poisson equation) and the Schrödinger and Dirac equations from quantum mechanics are analyzed as candidate physical PDEs for generative models.

title: ANTN: Bridging Autoregressive Neural Networks and Tensor Networks for Quantum Many-Body Simulation
abstract: Quantum many-body physics simulation has important impacts on understanding fundamental science and has applications to quantum materials design and quantum technology. However, due to the exponentially growing size of the Hilbert space with respect to the particle number, a direct simulation is intractable. While representing quantum states with tensor networks and neural networks are the two state-of-the-art methods for approximate simulations, each has its own limitations in terms of expressivity and inductive bias. To address these challenges, we develop a novel architecture, Autoregressive Neural TensorNet (ANTN), which bridges tensor networks and autoregressive neural networks. We show that Autoregressive Neural TensorNet parameterizes normalized wavefunctions, allows for exact sampling, generalizes the expressivity of tensor networks and autoregressive neural networks, and inherits a variety of symmetries from autoregressive neural networks. We demonstrate our approach on quantum state learning as well as finding the ground state of the challenging 2D $J_1$-$J_2$ Heisenberg model with different systems sizes and coupling parameters, outperforming both tensor networks and autoregressive neural networks. Our work opens up new opportunities for quantum many-body physics simulation, quantum technology design, and generative modeling in artificial intelligence.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: ANTN generalizes tensor network representations (MPS, PEPS, MERA) by embedding them within an autoregressive framework to overcome expressivity limitations in 2D systems.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary application is approximate simulation of quantum many-body systems, specifically finding ground states of the J1-J2 Heisenberg model.

conceptDetails.name: autoregressive wavefunction
conceptDetails.description: The core architectural contribution — using autoregressive factorization with tensor network layers to produce normalized, exactly sampleable quantum wavefunctions.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: ANTN parameterizes normalized complex-valued wavefunctions over exponentially large Hilbert spaces, enabling efficient quantum state learning and variational optimization.

conceptDetails.name: neural network quantum states
conceptDetails.description: ANTN is a novel class of neural network quantum state that unifies tensor network and autoregressive neural network ansatzes for wavefunction representation.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) is the core optimization procedure used to minimize ground state energy via stochastic sampling from the ANTN wavefunction.

conceptDetails.name: variational ansatz
conceptDetails.description: ANTN serves as a variational ansatz for the wavefunction that provably generalizes expressivity of both tensor networks and autoregressive neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Tensor networks capture entanglement structure of physical systems, and ANTN extends this capacity to handle higher entanglement regimes that challenge standard tensor networks.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Autoregressive neural networks within ANTN function as generative models over spin configurations, enabling exact normalized sampling without rejection.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: ANTN inherits symmetry properties (e.g., U(1), translation, spin-flip) from autoregressive neural networks, encoding physical inductive biases into the wavefunction ansatz.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The autoregressive component of ANTN performs exact density estimation over the computational basis, enabling unbiased gradient estimation for variational optimization.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: Autoregressive neural networks used in ANTN share architectural similarities with RNNs, enabling sequential factorization of the joint wavefunction probability.

title: Neural Volumetric Memory for Visual Locomotion Control
abstract: Legged robots have the potential to expand the reach of autonomy beyond paved roads. In this work, we consider the difficult problem of locomotion on challenging terrains using a single forward-facing depth camera. Due to the partial observability of the problem, the robot has to rely on past observations to infer the terrain currently beneath it. To solve this problem, we follow the paradigm in computer vision that explicitly models the 3D geometry of the scene and propose Neural Volumetric Memory (NVM), a geometric memory architecture that explicitly accounts for the SE(3) equivariance of the 3D world. NVM aggregates feature volumes from multiple camera views by first bringing them back to the ego-centric frame of the robot. We test the learned visual-locomotion policy on a physical robot and show that our approach, which explicitly introduces geometric priors during training, offers superior performance than more naïve methods. We also include ablation studies and show that the representations stored in the neural volumetric memory capture sufficient geometric information to reconstruct the scene. Our project page with videos is https://rchalyang.github.io/NVM .

conceptDetails.name: neural volumetric memory
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution: a geometric memory architecture that aggregates 3D feature volumes from sequential depth images into a coherent ego-centric scene representation for locomotion control.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: NVM explicitly encodes SE(3) equivariance by aligning past feature volumes to the robot's current ego-centric frame using estimated relative poses, making the architecture invariant to camera pose changes.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The entire NVM architecture is motivated by and built around geometric priors—explicitly modeling 3D scene structure and rigid-body transformations rather than relying on naive frame-stacking.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: A model-free RL teacher policy is trained in simulation using privileged state information (elevation maps, ground-truth velocity) to produce robust locomotion behaviors across challenging terrains.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: NVM learns compact volumetric representations of the 3D environment from depth image sequences, shown via ablation to capture sufficient geometric information for scene reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: visuomotor distillation
conceptDetails.description: A teacher-student behavior cloning stage that transfers the RL teacher's privileged-state policy into a vision-based visuomotor policy operating solely on depth camera input through the NVM.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: All policies are trained entirely in simulation and transferred to the physical UniTree A1 robot without any fine-tuning, demonstrating robust sim-to-real generalization.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A 2D-to-3D feature volume encoder based on convolutional operations lifts monocular depth images into 3D feature grids that populate the volumetric memory.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Per-frame depth images are encoded into latent feature volumes that are then spatially aligned and aggregated across timesteps to form the full NVM representation.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
