conceptDetails.description: An auxiliary novel-view consistency objective provides self-supervised training signal to improve the geometric quality of representations stored in the volumetric memory.

title: DribbleBot: Dynamic Legged Manipulation in the Wild
abstract: DribbleBot (Dexterous Ball Manipulation with a Legged Robot) is a legged robotic system that can dribble a soccer ball under the same real-world conditions as humans (i.e., in-the-wild). We adopt the paradigm of training policies in simulation using reinforcement learning and transferring them into the real world. We overcome critical challenges of accounting for variable ball motion dynamics on different terrains and perceiving the ball using body-mounted cameras under the constraints of onboard computing. Our results provide evidence that current quadruped platforms are well-suited for studying dynamic whole-body control problems involving simultaneous locomotion and manipulation directly from sensory observations.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is used to train the dribbling control policy entirely in simulation before real-world deployment.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Sim-to-real transfer is the central methodological challenge, requiring the policy trained in Isaac Gym to generalize to real-world terrain and sensor noise.

conceptDetails.name: legged locomotion
conceptDetails.description: Quadruped locomotion over diverse natural terrains is a core subproblem tightly integrated with the ball manipulation task.

conceptDetails.name: whole-body control
conceptDetails.description: The policy coordinates all twelve joint motors simultaneously to kick, pursue, and balance, exemplifying whole-body control under dynamic constraints.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Custom reward functions are designed to shape dribbling behavior, balancing ball velocity tracking, locomotion stability, and kick coordination.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The system must robustly handle out-of-distribution terrains (snow, mud, gravel) and recover from falls using a dedicated recovery policy.

conceptDetails.name: domain randomization
conceptDetails.description: A custom ball drag model and randomized physical parameters across terrains are introduced so the policy generalizes beyond flat-ground simulation.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The policy must simultaneously solve locomotion and ball manipulation, making this a compound control problem trained with a unified objective.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Domain randomization over physical parameters (ball drag, terrain friction, robot dynamics) is used to bridge the sim-to-real gap.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A fine-tuned YOLOv7 model performs real-time ball detection from fisheye camera images, providing ball position estimates to the control policy.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Ball position in the robot's reference frame is extracted from raw fisheye images as a compact state representation for the control policy.

title: Level Crossings, Attractor Points and Complex Multiplication
abstract: We study the complex structure moduli dependence of the scalar Laplacian eigenmodes for one-parameter families of Calabi-Yau $n$-folds in P^{n+1}. It was previously observed that some eigenmodes get lighter while others get heavier as a function of these moduli, which leads to eigenvalue crossing. We identify the cause for this behavior for the torus. We then show that at points in a sublocus of complex structure moduli space where Laplacian eigenmodes cross, the torus has complex multiplication. We speculate that the generalization to arbitrary Calabi-Yau manifolds could be that level crossing is related to rank one attractor points. To test this, we compute the eigenmodes numerically for the quartic K3 and the quintic threefold, and match crossings to CM and attractor points in these varieties. To quantify the error of our numerical methods, we also study the dependence of the numerical spectrum on the quality of the Calabi-Yau metric approximation, the number of points sampled from the Calabi-Yau variety, the truncation of the eigenbasis, and the the distance from degeneration points in complex structure moduli space.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The scalar Laplacian eigenspectrum on Calabi-Yau manifolds is the central object of study, with eigenvalue crossings as the primary phenomenon being explained.

conceptDetails.name: complex multiplication
conceptDetails.description: Complex multiplication is identified as the number-theoretic property of tori at level crossing points, with its generalization to higher-dimensional CY manifolds via middle cohomology conditions being the paper's central conjecture.

conceptDetails.name: attractor points
conceptDetails.description: Rank-one attractor points in complex structure moduli space are proposed and numerically verified as the generalization of CM points that governs level crossings for higher-dimensional Calabi-Yau manifolds.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies Calabi-Yau compactifications in string theory, examining how Laplacian eigenmodes and their crossings relate to special points in complex structure moduli space relevant to string phenomenology.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau moduli
conceptDetails.description: The dependence of the Laplacian eigenspectrum on complex structure moduli parametrizing one-parameter families of CY n-folds is the geometric framework underlying the entire analysis.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Numerical computation of the Laplacian eigenspectrum on quartic K3 and quintic threefold CY manifolds is the main computational methodology, with careful error analysis across approximation parameters.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Discrete symmetry groups of one-parameter CY families organize eigenmodes into irreducible representations, and enhanced symmetries at crossing points are analyzed using group-theoretic tools.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks are used to approximate the Ricci-flat metric on Calabi-Yau manifolds, enabling numerical computation of the Laplacian eigenmodes required to detect level crossings.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Enhanced discrete symmetries at specific moduli space loci explain the degeneracy structure of eigenvalue crossings, connecting geometric symmetries to the spectral behavior.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Point sampling from the Calabi-Yau variety is performed to construct numerical approximations of the metric and Laplacian, with the paper analyzing how the number of sampled points affects eigenspectrum accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses connections between CM points, rational conformal field theories, and the André-Oort conjecture regarding the scarcity of special points in moduli space.

title: Noisy dynamical systems evolve error correcting codes and modularity
abstract: Noise is a ubiquitous feature of the physical world. As a result, the first prerequisite of life is fault tolerance: maintaining integrity of state despite external bombardment. Recent experimental advances have revealed that biological systems achieve fault tolerance by implementing mathematically intricate error-correcting codes and by organizing in a modular fashion that physically separates functionally distinct subsystems. These elaborate structures represent a vanishing volume in the massive genetic configuration space. How is it possible that the primitive process of evolution, by which all biological systems evolved, achieved such unusual results? In this work, through experiments in Boolean networks, we show that the simultaneous presence of error correction and modularity in biological systems is no coincidence. Rather, it is a typical co-occurrence in noisy dynamic systems undergoing evolution. From this, we deduce the principle of error correction enhanced evolvability: systems possessing error-correcting codes are more effectively improved by evolution than those without.

conceptDetails.name: error-correcting codes
conceptDetails.description: The primary finding is that Boolean networks evolving under noise spontaneously develop error-correcting codes as a typical outcome.

conceptDetails.name: modularity
conceptDetails.description: Evolved Boolean networks under composite tasks develop modular structure co-emergent with error correction, mirroring biological organization.

conceptDetails.name: evolvability
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces the principle of error correction-enhanced evolvability, showing that error-correcting systems explore fitness landscapes more effectively.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Fault tolerance and robustness to noise are the central fitness criteria that biological systems evolve toward in this work.

conceptDetails.name: boolean network evolution
conceptDetails.description: Boolean networks serve as the primary experimental model for studying how error correction and modularity emerge through evolutionary dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Noise modeled as stochastic perturbations drives the evolutionary dynamics and selection pressure studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: fitness landscapes
conceptDetails.description: Error correction smooths fitness landscape valleys by neutralizing lethal mutations, enabling broader genome configuration space search.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: Fault-tolerant quantum computing provides the conceptual framework for error-correcting codes that the paper applies to biological and evolutionary systems.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper references Kauffman's edge-of-chaos phase transition as a related evolvability principle, situating the work within that theoretical landscape.

title: The Quantization Model of Neural Scaling
abstract: We propose the Quantization Model of neural scaling laws, explaining both the observed power law dropoff of loss with model and data size, and also the sudden emergence of new capabilities with scale. We derive this model from what we call the Quantization Hypothesis, where network knowledge and skills are "quantized" into discrete chunks ($\textbf{quanta}$). We show that when quanta are learned in order of decreasing use frequency, then a power law in use frequencies explains observed power law scaling of loss. We validate this prediction on toy datasets, then study how scaling curves decompose for large language models. Using language model gradients, we automatically decompose model behavior into a diverse set of skills (quanta). We tentatively find that the frequency at which these quanta are used in the training distribution roughly follows a power law corresponding with the empirical scaling exponent for language models, a prediction of our theory.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central focus is explaining why loss decreases as a power law with model parameters and training data size, and deriving this from first principles.

conceptDetails.name: neural scaling laws
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon under study: empirical power law relationships between model/data size and loss, unified with discrete capability emergence by the quantization model.

conceptDetails.name: knowledge quantization
conceptDetails.description: The paper's novel hypothesis that network knowledge and skills are organized into discrete, indivisible computational modules (quanta) analogous to energy quanta in physics.

conceptDetails.name: emergent capabilities
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides a mechanistic explanation for why individual capabilities appear abruptly at scale while aggregate loss changes smoothly — a quantum is either learned or not.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Mechanistic interpretability techniques, particularly using model gradients, are used to automatically decompose LLM behavior into discrete skill clusters (quanta).

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Gradient-based clustering is the core method for automatically discovering quanta — coherent behavioral units that correspond to identifiable model skills.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The Quantization Hypothesis relies on decomposability and sparsity: only a small subset of quanta are active for any given input, enabling modular analysis.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Large language models based on the transformer architecture are the primary empirical subject for validating the quantization model and discovering quanta via gradients.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: A Zipfian (power law) distribution over quanta use frequencies is the key statistical assumption that generates observed neural scaling exponents.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes how cross-entropy loss decomposes across quanta and how per-quantum loss curves sum to produce the observed aggregate power law scaling.

title: Artificial intelligence for artificial materials: moiré atom
abstract: Moiré engineering in atomically thin van der Waals heterostructures creates artificial quantum materials with designer properties. We solve the many-body problem of interacting electrons confined to a moiré superlattice potential minimum (the moiré atom) using a 2D fermionic neural network. We show that strong Coulomb interactions in combination with the anisotropic moiré potential lead to striking ``Wigner molecule" charge density distributions observable with scanning tunneling microscopy.

conceptDetails.name: fermionic neural network
conceptDetails.description: A 2D FermiNet-based variational wavefunction ansatz used to solve the many-body Schrödinger equation for interacting electrons in a moiré atom.

conceptDetails.name: moiré superlattice
conceptDetails.description: The periodic potential landscape arising from twisted van der Waals heterostructures that defines the artificial quantum material studied in this work.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Many-body ground state wavefunctions of interacting electrons in moiré atoms are the primary objects computed and analyzed.

conceptDetails.name: wigner molecule
conceptDetails.description: A strongly-correlated charge density distribution predicted for multi-electron moiré atoms, observable via scanning tunneling microscopy.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Fermionic antisymmetry of the many-electron wavefunction is enforced by the neural network architecture through parameterized Slater determinants.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo is used to optimize the fermionic neural network wavefunction and compute ground-state energies and charge densities.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper simulates the quantum many-body physics of interacting electrons confined to moiré potential minima using a continuum model.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: A continuum Hamiltonian with a periodic moiré potential serves as the low-energy effective model for semiconductor moiré materials.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Formation of Wigner molecule and Wigner crystal phases driven by strong Coulomb interactions relative to kinetic energy is a central physical phenomenon.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: The framework enables rational design and prediction of properties in artificial quantum materials based on moiré engineering.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Contrasted with neural network quantum states to motivate the higher expressivity of fermionic neural networks for highly entangled states.

title: Exploring the CP-violating Dashen phase in the Schwinger model with tensor networks
abstract: We numerically study the phase structure of the two-flavor Schwinger model with matrix product states, focusing on the (1+1)-dimensional analog of the CP-violating Dashen phase in QCD. We simulate the two-flavor Schwinger model around the point where the positive mass of one fermion flavor corresponds to the negative mass of the other fermion flavor, which is a sign-problem afflicted regime for conventional Monte Carlo techniques. Our results indicate that the model undergoes a CP-violating Dashen phase transition at this point, which manifests itself in abrupt changes of the average electric field and the analog of the pion condensate in the model. Studying the scaling of the bipartite entanglement entropy as a function of the volume, we find clear indications that this transition is not of first order.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The central subject is the CP-violating Dashen phase transition in the two-flavor Schwinger model, including its order and observables.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Matrix product states (MPS), a specific class of tensor networks, are the primary numerical method used to simulate the model's ground state.

conceptDetails.name: matrix product states
conceptDetails.description: MPS are the variational tensor network ansatz used to compute ground states and observables of the two-flavor Schwinger model.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The Schwinger model is formulated as a (1+1)-dimensional lattice gauge theory using Kogut-Susskind staggered fermions and Hamiltonian lattice methods.

conceptDetails.name: cp violation
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies spontaneous CP symmetry violation at the Dashen phase transition, analogous to the θ=π transition in QCD.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The Dashen phase transition involves spontaneous breaking of CP symmetry, with pion condensation as the order parameter.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The Schwinger model is a (1+1)-dimensional quantum electrodynamics used as a tractable analog for QCD phenomena.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Bipartite entanglement entropy scaling with volume is used to characterize the order of the Dashen phase transition.

conceptDetails.name: sign problem
conceptDetails.description: The sign problem afflicts conventional Monte Carlo in the mass regime studied (opposite-sign quark masses), making tensor networks essential.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper numerically simulates a quantum field theory in a sign-problem afflicted regime inaccessible to classical Monte Carlo methods.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: A Hamiltonian lattice formulation is adopted for the Schwinger model, enabling MPS-based ground state computation.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Conventional Monte Carlo methods are the baseline approach that fails in the sign-problem regime studied here, motivating the tensor network approach.

title: Multi-Symmetry Ensembles: Improving Diversity and Generalization via Opposing Symmetries
abstract: Deep ensembles (DE) have been successful in improving model performance by learning diverse members via the stochasticity of random initialization. While recent works have attempted to promote further diversity in DE via hyperparameters or regularizing loss functions, these methods primarily still rely on a stochastic approach to explore the hypothesis space. In this work, we present Multi-Symmetry Ensembles (MSE), a framework for constructing diverse ensembles by capturing the multiplicity of hypotheses along symmetry axes, which explore the hypothesis space beyond stochastic perturbations of model weights and hyperparameters. We leverage recent advances in contrastive representation learning to create models that separately capture opposing hypotheses of invariant and equivariant functional classes and present a simple ensembling approach to efficiently combine appropriate hypotheses for a given task. We show that MSE effectively captures the multiplicity of conflicting hypotheses that is often required in large, diverse datasets like ImageNet. As a result of their inherent diversity, MSE improves classification performance, uncertainty quantification, and generalization across a series of transfer tasks.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is Multi-Symmetry Ensembles (MSE), a novel framework for constructing diverse neural network ensembles guided by symmetry axes rather than stochastic initialization.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: MSE leverages contrastive representation learning to train models that separately capture invariant and equivariant hypotheses around symmetry groups.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly trains ensemble members to encode equivariant representations as opposing hypotheses to invariant models, using symmetry group structure.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis space diversity
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: MSE explicitly explores opposing regions of the hypothesis space along symmetry axes, going beyond stochastic perturbations to achieve structural diversity.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The framework is built around the concept of symmetry axes (e.g., rotational symmetry), guiding diversity in ensembles by capturing opposing invariant and equivariant symmetry hypotheses.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: MSE trains diverse representations that individually respect different symmetries, enabling task-agnostic transfer to downstream tasks.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: MSE is evaluated on uncertainty quantification benchmarks, showing improved calibration and reliability over standard deep ensembles.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The inherent diversity of MSE members improves generalization across a series of transfer tasks to new datasets.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The invariant and equivariant models in MSE are trained using self-supervised contrastive objectives before fine-tuning for downstream classification.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The framework is formalized using group-theoretic concepts of invariance and equivariance with respect to transformation groups acting on inputs.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Symmetry transformations (rotations, color jitter, etc.) are used as augmentations to define invariant vs. equivariant training objectives within MSE.

title: Via Machinae 2.0: Full-Sky, Model-Agnostic Search for Stellar Streams in Gaia DR2
abstract: We present an update to Via Machinae, an automated stellar stream-finding algorithm based on the deep learning anomaly detector ANODE. Via Machinae identifies stellar streams within Gaia, using only angular positions, proper motions, and photometry, without reference to a model of the Milky Way potential for orbit integration or stellar distances. This new version, Via Machinae 2.0, includes many improvements and refinements to nearly every step of the algorithm, that altogether result in more robust and visually distinct stream candidates than our original formulation. In this work, we also provide a quantitative estimate of the false positive rate of Via Machinae 2.0 by applying it to a simulated Gaia-mock catalog based on Galaxia, a smooth model of the Milky Way that does not contain substructure or stellar streams. Finally, we perform the first full-sky search for stellar streams with Via Machinae 2.0, identifying 102 streams at high significance within the Gaia Data Release 2, of which only 10 have been previously identified. While follow-up observations for further confirmation are required, taking into account the false positive rate presented in this work, we expect approximately 90 of these stream candidates to correspond to real stellar structures.

conceptDetails.name: stellar streams
conceptDetails.description: Tidally stripped structures from globular clusters and dwarf galaxies that Via Machinae 2.0 searches for as elongated, kinematically coherent overdensities in Gaia data.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The ANODE algorithm forms the core of Via Machinae, scoring each star by how anomalous it is relative to a background density estimate to identify stellar stream candidates.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: ANODE uses normalizing flows to estimate the probability density of stars in signal and control regions, enabling model-agnostic background estimation.

conceptDetails.name: model-agnostic stream search
conceptDetails.description: Via Machinae requires no assumption about the Galactic potential, orbit integration, or isochrones, distinguishing it from prior stream-finding methods.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm computes probability densities in signal and sideband regions to separate anomalous overdensities from the smooth Milky Way background.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The anomaly score R(x) = p_SR(x)/p_bg(x) is a likelihood ratio that quantifies how much more probable a star is in the signal region versus background.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Via Machinae 2.0 detects 102 high-significance stellar stream candidates above a smooth Milky Way background in the full Gaia DR2 sky.

conceptDetails.name: novelty detection
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline identifies previously unknown stellar structures, with 92 of 102 stream candidates being new discoveries not in prior catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The false positive rate is quantified by applying Via Machinae 2.0 to a Galaxia-based simulated Gaia mock catalog containing no real substructure.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Regions of interest (ROIs) flagged by ANODE are clustered across overlapping sky patches and proper motion scans to form coherent stream candidates.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Stellar streams are sensitive probes of dark matter substructure in the Milky Way halo, motivating the search for new streams.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: Stars anomalous with respect to the smooth stellar background are flagged as potential stream members, analogous to out-of-distribution detection.

title: Computational Mirror Symmetry
abstract: We present an efficient algorithm for computing the prepotential in compactifications of type II string theory on mirror pairs of Calabi-Yau threefolds in toric varieties. Applying this method, we exhibit the first systematic computation of genus-zero Gopakumar-Vafa invariants in compact threefolds with many moduli, including examples with up to 491 vector multiplets.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau geometry
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses exclusively on mirror pairs of Calabi-Yau threefold hypersurfaces in toric varieties as the geometric setting.

conceptDetails.name: gopakumar-vafa invariants
conceptDetails.description: The first systematic large-scale computation of genus-zero GV invariants in compact threefolds with many moduli is the paper's central result.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops algorithms for computing prepotentials in type II string theory compactifications on Calabi-Yau threefolds.

conceptDetails.name: picard-fuchs equations
conceptDetails.description: The Picard-Fuchs differential system governing period integrals is central to computing the prepotential via the HKTY method generalized here.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Mirror symmetry between Calabi-Yau pairs is the fundamental mathematical symmetry exploited throughout the algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: toric geometry
conceptDetails.description: Toric varieties and Batyrev's construction provide the combinatorial framework for constructing mirror Calabi-Yau hypersurfaces computationally.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The prepotential encodes quantum corrections (worldsheet instantons) to the effective field theory arising from string compactification.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Gopakumar-Vafa invariants encode genus-zero BPS state counts related to curve-counting and amplitude computations in the compactified theory.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The mirror map inverts the relationship between complex structure periods and Kähler moduli to extract enumerative invariants of the original manifold.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The efficient computational algorithm serves as a practical surrogate for otherwise intractable analytic mirror symmetry computations in high-moduli cases.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Computational sampling and numerical methods are used to systematically compute period integrals and enumerative invariants across large moduli spaces.

title: SHAPER: Can You Hear the Shape of a Jet?
abstract: The identification of interesting substructures within jets is an important tool for searching for new physics and probing the Standard Model at colliders. Many of these substructure tools have previously been shown to take the form of optimal transport problems, in particular the Energy Mover's Distance (EMD). In this work, we show that the EMD is in fact the natural structure for comparing collider events, which accounts for its recent success in understanding event and jet substructure. We then present a Shape Hunting Algorithm using Parameterized Energy Reconstruction (SHAPER), which is a general framework for defining and computing shape-based observables. SHAPER generalizes N-jettiness from point clusters to any extended, parametrizable shape. This is accomplished by efficiently minimizing the EMD between events and parameterized manifolds of energy flows representing idealized shapes, implemented using the dual-potential Sinkhorn approximation of the Wasserstein metric. We show how the geometric language of observables as manifolds can be used to define novel observables with built-in infrared-and-collinear safety. We demonstrate the efficacy of the SHAPER framework by performing empirical jet substructure studies using several examples of new shape-based observables.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The Energy Mover's Distance (EMD), a special case of the Wasserstein metric, is shown to be the unique natural metric for comparing collider events and forms the mathematical foundation of the entire SHAPER framework.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: SHAPER is developed specifically to define and compute shape-based jet substructure observables, extending N-jettiness to arbitrary geometric shapes for probing jet topology.

conceptDetails.name: energy mover's distance
conceptDetails.description: A novel application of the Wasserstein metric to collider events that measures the 'work' needed to rearrange one energy flow into another, proven here to be the unique faithful and continuous metric on collider event space.

conceptDetails.name: irc safety
conceptDetails.description: Infrared-and-collinear safety is geometrized within the SHAPER framework: observables defined as manifold-distance problems over energy flows are shown to automatically satisfy IRC safety constraints.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets LHC event analysis, developing observables to probe Standard Model structure and search for new physics signatures in high-multiplicity hadronic final states.

conceptDetails.name: sinkhorn divergence
conceptDetails.description: The dual-potential Sinkhorn approximation of the Wasserstein metric is the core computational technique enabling efficient minimization of the EMD over parameterized shape manifolds.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: Shape observables are formalized as distances from events to parameterized manifolds of energy flows in an infinite-dimensional measure space, framing observable design as a geometric problem.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: A geometric language for observables—encoding symmetry, IRC safety, and spatial structure via manifold parameterizations—is central to the theoretical contributions of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: SHAPER recovers shape parameters (radii, eccentricity, orientation) and dynamic jet energies from raw particle-level events as part of the observable evaluation pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: SHAPER generalizes k-means-style point clustering (as in XCone and N-jettiness) to clustering onto arbitrary extended geometric shapes such as rings, disks, and ellipses.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Shape-based observables from SHAPER are framed as tools for identifying anomalous jet substructure that could signal physics beyond the Standard Model at colliders.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives and justifies the Sinkhorn divergence as the appropriate loss for shape fitting, analyzing trade-offs between the entropic regularization parameter and computational cost.

title: Q-Flow: Generative Modeling for Differential Equations of Open Quantum Dynamics with Normalizing Flows
abstract: Studying the dynamics of open quantum systems can enable breakthroughs both in fundamental physics and applications to quantum engineering and quantum computation. Since the density matrix $ρ$, which is the fundamental description for the dynamics of such systems, is high-dimensional, customized deep generative neural networks have been instrumental in modeling $ρ$. However, the complex-valued nature and normalization constraints of $ρ$, as well as its complicated dynamics, prohibit a seamless connection between open quantum systems and the recent advances in deep generative modeling. Here we lift that limitation by utilizing a reformulation of open quantum system dynamics to a partial differential equation (PDE) for a corresponding probability distribution $Q$, the Husimi Q function. Thus, we model the Q function seamlessly with off-the-shelf deep generative models such as normalizing flows. Additionally, we develop novel methods for learning normalizing flow evolution governed by high-dimensional PDEs based on the Euler method and the application of the time-dependent variational principle. We name the resulting approach $Q$-$Flow$ and demonstrate the scalability and efficiency of Q-Flow on open quantum system simulations, including the dissipative harmonic oscillator and the dissipative bosonic model. Q-Flow is superior to conventional PDE solvers and state-of-the-art physics-informed neural network solvers, especially in high-dimensional systems.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core generative model used to represent the Husimi Q function as a probability distribution, enabling off-the-shelf modeling of open quantum dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: husimi q function
conceptDetails.description: A quantum quasiprobability distribution used to reformulate the complex-valued density matrix dynamics into a real-valued PDE amenable to generative modeling.

conceptDetails.name: open quantum dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The physics domain of study — systems interacting with an environment, governed by the Lindblad master equation — that Q-Flow is designed to simulate.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: Q-Flow is demonstrated on simulations of dissipative harmonic oscillators and bosonic models, directly advancing open quantum system simulation capability.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The density matrix and its Q function reformulation are the quantum state representations at the center of this work's computational approach.

conceptDetails.name: euler-kl method
conceptDetails.description: A novel training algorithm introduced in this paper that discretizes PDE time evolution via forward Euler steps and matches distributions using KL divergence.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The broader class of deep generative neural networks applied to model quantum state representations, bridging continuous-variable quantum dynamics and modern ML.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows provide exact density evaluation of the Q function, which is essential for computing quantum observables and training objectives.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: PINNs serve as the primary ML baseline against which Q-Flow is benchmarked, and are shown to be outperformed especially in high-dimensional settings.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The Euler-KL training method minimizes KL divergence between the evolved and parameterized distributions, relying on log-likelihood from the normalizing flow.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Stochastic sampling from the normalizing flow is used to estimate the loss and gradients in the Euler-KL and TDVP training procedures.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Mentioned as a related generative modeling paradigm; the Time-Dependent Variational Principle (TDVP) draws conceptual parallels to variational inference methods.

title: PFGM++: Unlocking the Potential of Physics-Inspired Generative Models
abstract: We introduce a new family of physics-inspired generative models termed PFGM++ that unifies diffusion models and Poisson Flow Generative Models (PFGM). These models realize generative trajectories for $N$ dimensional data by embedding paths in $N{+}D$ dimensional space while still controlling the progression with a simple scalar norm of the $D$ additional variables. The new models reduce to PFGM when $D{=}1$ and to diffusion models when $D{\to}\infty$. The flexibility of choosing $D$ allows us to trade off robustness against rigidity as increasing $D$ results in more concentrated coupling between the data and the additional variable norms. We dispense with the biased large batch field targets used in PFGM and instead provide an unbiased perturbation-based objective similar to diffusion models. To explore different choices of $D$, we provide a direct alignment method for transferring well-tuned hyperparameters from diffusion models ($D{\to} \infty$) to any finite $D$ values. Our experiments show that models with finite $D$ can be superior to previous state-of-the-art diffusion models on CIFAR-10/FFHQ $64{\times}64$ datasets, with FID scores of $1.91/2.43$ when $D{=}2048/128$. In class-conditional setting, $D{=}2048$ yields current state-of-the-art FID of $1.74$ on CIFAR-10. In addition, we demonstrate that models with smaller $D$ exhibit improved robustness against modeling errors. Code is available at https://github.com/Newbeeer/pfgmpp

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: PFGM++ introduces a new family of physics-inspired generative models parameterized by augmentation dimension D, unifying multiple generative modeling approaches.

conceptDetails.name: poisson flow augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Novel method of augmenting N-dimensional data with D additional dimensions and using Poisson equation-derived electric field lines to define generative trajectories.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: PFGM++ unifies diffusion models as a special case when augmentation dimension D→∞, and outperforms state-of-the-art diffusion models on image generation benchmarks.

conceptDetails.name: dimension interpolation
conceptDetails.description: The augmentation dimension D serves as a continuous hyperparameter interpolating between PFGM (D=1) and diffusion models (D→∞), enabling robustness-rigidity tradeoffs.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Rotational symmetry of the electric field on the D-dimensional cylinder is exploited to reduce the generative trajectory to a scalar norm, enabling tractable sampling.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives a perturbation-based training objective analogous to denoising score matching, connecting PFGM++ to score-based generative modeling.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The model leverages physical principles from electrostatics and Poisson equations to define the generative field and training objective.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Smaller values of D provide improved robustness against modeling errors, large step sizes, and post-training quantization.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The generative sampling process follows probability flow ODEs and stochastic differential equations in the augmented N+D dimensional space.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: PFGM++ defines generative trajectories via electric field lines in augmented space, related to flow-based sampling processes.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: PFGM++ models the data distribution by learning electric field lines that define a bijection between an easy prior and the data distribution.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: An alignment method is provided to transfer well-tuned hyperparameters from diffusion models (D→∞) to any finite D, enabling efficient model adaptation.

title: Geometry of contact: contact planning for multi-legged robots via spin models duality
abstract: Contact planning is crucial in locomoting systems.Specifically, appropriate contact planning can enable versatile behaviors (e.g., sidewinding in limbless locomotors) and facilitate speed-dependent gait transitions (e.g., walk-trot-gallop in quadrupedal locomotors). The challenges of contact planning include determining not only the sequence by which contact is made and broken between the locomotor and the environments, but also the sequence of internal shape changes (e.g., body bending and limb shoulder joint oscillation). Most state-of-art contact planning algorithms focused on conventional robots (e.g.biped and quadruped) and conventional tasks (e.g. forward locomotion), and there is a lack of study on general contact planning in multi-legged robots. In this paper, we show that using geometric mechanics framework, we can obtain the global optimal contact sequence given the internal shape changes sequence. Therefore, we simplify the contact planning problem to a graph optimization problem to identify the internal shape changes. Taking advantages of the spatio-temporal symmetry in locomotion, we map the graph optimization problem to special cases of spin models, which allows us to obtain the global optima in polynomial time. We apply our approach to develop new forward and sidewinding behaviors in a hexapod and a 12-legged centipede. We verify our predictions using numerical and robophysical models, and obtain novel and effective locomotion behaviors.

conceptDetails.name: contact sequence optimization
conceptDetails.description: Novel formulation reducing multi-legged robot gait design to global optimization of foot contact patterns via spin model duality.

conceptDetails.name: geometric mechanics
conceptDetails.description: Kinematic reconstruction equations and local connection matrices encode robot-substrate interactions, forming the theoretical backbone of the contact planning framework.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses a geometric mechanics framework to model multi-legged robot locomotion and derive optimal contact sequences from internal shape changes.

conceptDetails.name: spin model duality
conceptDetails.description: The graph optimization problem for locomotion is mapped to special cases of spin models (e.g., Ising model variants), enabling polynomial-time global optimization.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Spatio-temporal symmetry properties of locomotion are exploited to map the graph optimization problem to spin models, enabling polynomial-time solutions.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Spatio-temporal symmetry in locomotion gaits is leveraged to reduce the search space and obtain global optima in polynomial time.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Spin models from condensed matter physics (Ising-like models) are used as the mathematical dual to solve the contact planning optimization problem.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Contact planning is formulated as a graph optimization problem over internal shape change sequences for multi-legged robots.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Resistive force theory provides a surrogate physical model for ground reaction forces enabling analytical computation of the local connection matrix.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The optimization of contact sequences maps to spin model energy minimization, analogous to finding globally optimal transport paths in configuration space.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Compared and contrasted with the proposed geometric approach as an alternative anchor design method for legged robot locomotion control.

title: EPiC-GAN: Equivariant Point Cloud Generation for Particle Jets
abstract: With the vast data-collecting capabilities of current and future high-energy collider experiments, there is an increasing demand for computationally efficient simulations. Generative machine learning models enable fast event generation, yet so far these approaches are largely constrained to fixed data structures and rigid detector geometries. In this paper, we introduce EPiC-GAN - equivariant point cloud generative adversarial network - which can produce point clouds of variable multiplicity. This flexible framework is based on deep sets and is well suited for simulating sprays of particles called jets. The generator and discriminator utilize multiple EPiC layers with an interpretable global latent vector. Crucially, the EPiC layers do not rely on pairwise information sharing between particles, which leads to a significant speed-up over graph- and transformer-based approaches with more complex relation diagrams. We demonstrate that EPiC-GAN scales well to large particle multiplicities and achieves high generation fidelity on benchmark jet generation tasks.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: The EPiC-GAN uses a GAN framework with equivariant generator and discriminator to produce realistic particle jet point clouds.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: EPiC layers are designed to be permutation equivariant by construction, ensuring the model respects the unordered nature of particle sets.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The model is benchmarked on the JetNet dataset for generating hadronized particle jets at the LHC, demonstrating high-fidelity jet observable distributions.

conceptDetails.name: point cloud generation
conceptDetails.description: EPiC-GAN introduces a novel approach for generating variable-multiplicity point clouds without relying on fixed data structures or pairwise edge features.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: EPiC-GAN targets fast simulation of particle detector readouts, replacing computationally expensive Monte Carlo methods for collider experiments.

conceptDetails.name: deep sets
conceptDetails.description: The EPiC architecture is grounded in the deep sets framework (particle flow networks in HEP), using global and local attribute aggregation for permutation-invariant modeling.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The work is motivated by the simulation demands of the HL-LHC and future collider experiments with increasing data volumes.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The architecture leverages geometric structure of point clouds and deep sets to handle variable-cardinality, unordered particle data.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A core contribution is that EPiC layers scale linearly with particle multiplicity, enabling efficient generation at large jet multiplicities (up to 150 particles).

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Permutation symmetry of the unordered particle set is explicitly preserved in the EPiC layer design via equivariant aggregation.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Message-passing GNNs serve as the primary baseline comparison; EPiC-GAN achieves comparable fidelity while avoiding quadratic pairwise edge computations.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The global latent vector in each EPiC layer is shown to correlate with known physical observables, providing an interpretable latent space.

title: First demonstration of neural sensing and control in a kilometer-scale gravitational wave observatory
abstract: Suspended optics in gravitational wave (GW) observatories are susceptible to alignment perturbations, particularly slow drifts over time, due to variations in temperature and seismic levels. Such misalignments affect the coupling of the incident laser beam into the optical cavities, degrade both circulating power and optomechanical photon squeezing and thus decrease the astrophysical sensitivity to merging binaries. Traditional alignment techniques involve differential wavefront sensing using multiple quadrant photodiodes but are often restricted in bandwidth and are limited by the sensing noise. We present the first-ever successful implementation of neural network-based sensing and control at a gravitational wave observatory and demonstrate low-frequency control of the signal recycling mirror at the GEO 600 detector. Alignment information for three critical optics is simultaneously extracted from the interferometric dark port camera images via a CNN-LSTM network architecture and is then used for MIMO control using soft actor-critic-based deep reinforcement learning. Overall sensitivity improvement achieved using our scheme demonstrates deep learning's capabilities as a viable tool for real-time sensing and control for current and next-generation GW interferometers.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: Soft actor-critic (SAC) deep RL is used for MIMO control of the signal recycling mirror alignment, learning an optimal control policy from interferometer feedback.

conceptDetails.name: optical cavity control
conceptDetails.description: Real-time neural control of the signal recycling cavity mirror alignment to maintain optimal resonance and photon squeezing in the interferometer.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The GEO 600 gravitational wave observatory is the deployment target; the neural control system is evaluated by its improvement to GW strain sensitivity.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A CNN processes dark port camera images to extract spatial alignment features for three critical interferometer optics simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: An LSTM layer follows the CNN to capture temporal dynamics of mirror drift, forming the CNN-LSTM sensing network.

conceptDetails.name: mimo control
conceptDetails.description: The RL-based controller implements multiple-input multiple-output control, simultaneously actuating several mirrors based on joint alignment estimates.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Alignment state information for three optics is simultaneously extracted from interferometric camera images, replacing traditional quadrant photodiode sensing.

conceptDetails.name: dark port imaging
conceptDetails.description: Camera images at the interferometer's dark (output) port serve as the novel sensing modality, replacing or augmenting classical wavefront sensors.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring mirror angular alignment from intensity patterns in dark port images is an ill-posed inverse problem solved by the neural sensing network.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The CNN-LSTM acts as a learned surrogate for the classical differential wavefront sensing system, providing richer, lower-noise alignment estimates.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detecting subtle misalignment signals embedded in interferometric camera frames is the core sensing task motivating the neural approach.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The network simultaneously predicts alignment states for three distinct optics from a single image stream, a multi-output regression task.

title: Comparing Point Cloud Strategies for Collider Event Classification
abstract: In this paper, we compare several event classification architectures defined on the point cloud representation of collider events. These approaches, which are based on the frameworks of deep sets and edge convolutions, circumvent many of the difficulties associated with traditional feature engineering. To benchmark our architectures against more traditional event classification strategies, we perform a case study involving Higgs boson decays to tau leptons. We find a 2.5 times increase in performance compared to a baseline ATLAS analysis with engineered features. Our point cloud architectures can be viewed as simplified versions of graph neural networks, where each particle in the event corresponds to a graph node. In our case study, we find the best balance of performance and computational cost for simple pairwise architectures, which are based on learned edge features.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses event classification at the LHC, specifically Higgs boson decays to tau leptons as a benchmark case study.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary signal/background classification of collider events is the core task, benchmarked against ATLAS baseline strategies.

conceptDetails.name: point cloud event representation
conceptDetails.description: The use of variable-length unordered sets of particle four-vectors as the native collider event representation, eliminating combinatorial and truncation ambiguities.

conceptDetails.name: edge convolution
conceptDetails.description: EdgeConv-based architectures compute local pairwise particle features and are compared systematically against deep-set-based approaches.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Point cloud architectures are framed as simplified graph neural networks where each particle is a graph node, providing the conceptual foundation for the work.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: Deep sets and edge convolutions provide permutation-symmetric and permutation-equivariant processing of unordered particle sets, a core application of geometric deep learning principles.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The point cloud approach circumvents traditional hand-engineered feature extraction, with the pairwise architecture's latent features compared to conventional engineered features.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Permutation equivariant deep sets are reviewed and compared to nested concatenation as an alternative with equivalent expressivity.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructed particle four-momenta and identities form the raw inputs to the point cloud architectures, replacing traditional fixed-size event representations.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Particle jets and their constituents form the point cloud inputs, and jet-level features are among the hand-engineered baselines compared against.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulated events from ATLAS detector conditions are used to train and evaluate all classifiers.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Latent dimension studies and correlation analysis between learned and hand-engineered features are used to interpret what the pairwise architecture has learned.

title: Non-parametric Lagrangian biasing from the insights of neural nets
abstract: We present a Lagrangian model of galaxy clustering bias in which we train a neural net using the local properties of the smoothed initial density field to predict the late-time mass-weighted halo field. By fitting the mass-weighted halo field in the AbacusSummit simulations at z=0.5, we find that including three coarsely spaced smoothing scales gives the best recovery of the halo power spectrum. Adding more smoothing scales may lead to 2-5% underestimation of the large-scale power and can cause the neural net to overfit. We find that the fitted halo-to-mass ratio can be well described by two directions in the original high-dimension feature space. Projecting the original features into these two principal components and re-training the neural net either reproduces the original training result, or outperforms it with a better match of the halo power spectrum. The elements of the principal components are unlikely to be assigned physical meanings, partly owing to the features being highly correlated between different smoothing scales. Our work illustrates a potential need to include multiple smoothing scales when studying galaxy bias, and this can be done easily with machine-learning methods that can take in high dimensional input feature space.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian biasing
conceptDetails.description: A novel non-parametric Lagrangian framework that predicts halo overdensity from initial-condition particle features without assuming a polynomial bias expansion form.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Principal component analysis is applied to reduce the high-dimensional feature space to two principal directions that capture the halo-to-mass ratio behavior.

conceptDetails.name: multi-scale smoothing
conceptDetails.description: The method uses multiple coarsely spaced smoothing radii applied to the initial density field to capture scale-dependent information for halo formation.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses AbacusSummit N-body simulations to train and validate the neural network model for predicting halo fields at z=0.5.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Local properties of the smoothed initial density field (overdensity, Laplacian, tidal shear) are extracted as particle features across multiple smoothing scales.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper models dark-matter halo formation and clustering as a function of the initial Lagrangian density field to understand large-scale structure.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Eigenvalue decomposition of the feature covariance matrix is used to orthogonalize correlated features and identify the principal components of the gradient.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The neural network serves as a surrogate for the non-parametric halo bias function, replacing traditional polynomial bias expansions.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The neural network is trained as a regression model to predict the halo-to-mass weight function f given particle features from the initial density field.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates whether principal components of the learned function can be assigned physical meanings, finding that high feature correlations prevent clear interpretation.

conceptDetails.name: overfitting detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies that adding too many smoothing scales leads to 2-5% power underestimation and neural network overfitting, guiding optimal feature selection.

title: Simulating 2+1D Lattice Quantum Electrodynamics at Finite Density with Neural Flow Wavefunctions
abstract: We present a neural flow wavefunction, Gauge-Fermion FlowNet, and use it to simulate 2+1D lattice compact quantum electrodynamics with finite density dynamical fermions. The gauge field is represented by a neural network which parameterizes a discretized flow-based transformation of the amplitude while the fermionic sign structure is represented by a neural net backflow. This approach directly represents the $U(1)$ degree of freedom without any truncation, obeys Guass's law by construction, samples autoregressively avoiding any equilibration time, and variationally simulates Gauge-Fermion systems with sign problems accurately. In this model, we investigate confinement and string breaking phenomena in different fermion density and hopping regimes. We study the phase transition from the charge crystal phase to the vacuum phase at zero density, and observe the phase seperation and the net charge penetration blocking effect under magnetic interaction at finite density. In addition, we investigate a magnetic phase transition due to the competition effect between the kinetic energy of fermions and the magnetic energy of the gauge field. With our method, we further note potential differences on the order of the phase transitions between a continuous $U(1)$ system and one with finite truncation. Our state-of-the-art neural network approach opens up new possibilities to study different gauge theories coupled to dynamical matter in higher dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper simulates 2+1D lattice compact QED using the Kogut-Susskind Hamiltonian formulation, which is central to the entire work.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The Gauge-Fermion FlowNet uses a flow-based generative model to map a Gaussian distribution to gauge and fermionic degrees of freedom.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Neural flow wavefunctions are used as variational quantum states to represent the ground state of the lattice QED system.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates multiple phase transitions including charge crystal to vacuum, magnetic phase transitions, and confinement-deconfinement transitions.

conceptDetails.name: neural backflow
conceptDetails.description: A neural net backflow represents the fermionic sign structure of the wavefunction, enabling accurate simulation of systems with sign problems.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo is used to optimize the neural network wavefunction and compute observables, with the autoregressive construction avoiding sign problems.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauss's law (gauge symmetry) is enforced by construction in the autoregressive neural network architecture.

conceptDetails.name: sign problem
conceptDetails.description: The fermionic sign problem arising from finite-density dynamical fermions is directly addressed by the neural backflow component of the architecture.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper works within the framework of quantum electrodynamics as a quantum field theory discretized on a lattice.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: A flow-based generative model forms the core of the GFFN architecture, generating configurations of gauge and fermionic fields.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents a classical neural network approach to simulate quantum gauge-matter systems that are intractable with conventional methods.

conceptDetails.name: confinement
conceptDetails.description: String breaking and confinement phenomena in the lattice QED model are investigated across different fermion density and hopping regimes.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Tensor networks are discussed as a competing approach whose limitations in higher dimensions motivate the neural network method.

title: Stellar Reddening Based Extinction Maps for Cosmological Applications
abstract: Cosmological surveys must correct their observations for the reddening of extragalactic objects by Galactic dust. Existing dust maps, however, have been found to have spatial correlations with the large-scale structure of the Universe. Errors in extinction maps can propagate systematic biases into samples of dereddened extragalactic objects and into cosmological measurements such as correlation functions between foreground lenses and background objects and the primordial non-gaussianity parameter $f_{NL}$. Emission-based maps are contaminated by the cosmic infrared background, while maps inferred from stellar-reddenings suffer from imperfect removal of quasars and galaxies from stellar catalogs. Thus, stellar-reddening based maps using catalogs without extragalactic objects offer a promising path to making dust maps with minimal correlations with large-scale structure. We present two high-latitude integrated extinction maps based on stellar reddenings, with a point spread function of full-width half-maximum 6.1' and 15'. We employ a strict selection of catalog objects to filter out galaxies and quasars and measure the spatial correlation of our extinction maps with extragalactic structure. Our galactic extinction maps have reduced spatial correlation with large scale structure relative to most existing stellar-reddening based and emission-based extinction maps.

conceptDetails.name: dust extinction mapping
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution: constructing high-latitude integrated maps of galactic dust extinction with reduced spatial correlation with extragalactic large-scale structure.

conceptDetails.name: large-scale structure contamination
conceptDetails.description: The spurious spatial correlation between extinction maps and the cosmic large-scale structure that biases cosmological measurements including fNL and lensing correlations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The Bayestar framework uses Bayesian inference to estimate per-star posteriors on reddening and distance modulus from photometric and astrometric data.

conceptDetails.name: stellar catalog cleaning
conceptDetails.description: The novel methodology of strictly filtering extragalactic contaminants (galaxies, quasars) from stellar photometric catalogs prior to dust map construction.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Per-star posterior distributions over reddening and distance are the fundamental inputs used to construct the integrated extinction maps.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Distinguishing stars from galaxies and quasars in photometric catalogs is a central challenge addressed by strict morphological and color selection cuts.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Reconstructing the three-dimensional dust distribution along lines of sight from noisy, indirect stellar reddening measurements is an ill-posed inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Kernel-weighted regression is used to aggregate per-star reddening posteriors into spatially smooth integrated extinction maps.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Photometric calibration of Pan-STARRS1 and 2MASS magnitudes to the millimagnitude level is essential for accurate reddening inference.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian kernel smoothing is applied to combine stellar reddening estimates into continuous maps with a defined point spread function.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Identifying and excluding outlier objects (quasars, galaxies) from stellar catalogs mirrors anomaly detection, using morphological and color-based selection criteria.

title: Measuring the 8621 Å Diffuse Interstellar Band in Gaia DR3 RVS Spectra: Obtaining a Clean Catalog by Marginalizing over Stellar Types
abstract: Diffuse interstellar bands (DIBs) are broad absorption features associated with interstellar dust and can serve as chemical and kinematic tracers. Conventional measurements of DIBs in stellar spectra are complicated by residuals between observations and best-fit stellar models. To overcome this, we simultaneously model the spectrum as a combination of stellar, dust, and residual components, with full posteriors on the joint distribution of the components. This decomposition is obtained by modeling each component as a draw from a high-dimensional Gaussian distribution in the data-space (the observed spectrum) -- a method we call "Marginalized Analytic Data-space Gaussian Inference for Component Separation" (MADGICS). We use a data-driven prior for the stellar component, which avoids missing stellar features not well-modeled by synthetic spectra. This technique provides statistically rigorous uncertainties and detection thresholds, which are required to work in the low signal-to-noise regime that is commonplace for dusty lines of sight. We reprocess all public Gaia DR3 RVS spectra and present an improved 8621 Å DIB catalog, free of detectable stellar line contamination. We constrain the rest-frame wavelength to $8623.14 \pm 0.087$ Å (vacuum), find no significant evidence for DIBs in the Local Bubble from the $1/6^{\rm{th}}$ of RVS spectra that are public, and show unprecedented correlation with kinematic substructure in Galactic CO maps. We validate the catalog, its reported uncertainties, and biases using synthetic injection tests. We believe MADGICS provides a viable path forward for large-scale spectral line measurements in the presence of complex spectral contamination.

conceptDetails.name: spectral component separation
conceptDetails.description: Novel MADGICS method simultaneously decomposes each spectrum into stellar, dust (DIB), and residual components in the data space.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: MADGICS performs full Bayesian inference to obtain joint posteriors over stellar, dust, and residual spectral components simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: diffuse interstellar band mapping
conceptDetails.description: Large-scale catalog of 8621 Å DIB detections used to trace ISM kinematics and correlate with Galactic CO kinematic substructure.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The core task is detecting the 8621 Å diffuse interstellar band absorption feature in individual stellar spectra from the Gaia DR3 RVS dataset.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method computes full posteriors on the joint distribution of spectral components, enabling statistically rigorous uncertainties and detection thresholds.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: MADGICS provides statistically rigorous uncertainties and detection thresholds essential for low SNR regimes common in dusty lines of sight.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Decomposing an observed spectrum into stellar, DIB, and residual components is an ill-posed inverse problem solved analytically via Gaussian marginalization.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: A data-driven high-dimensional Gaussian prior over stellar spectra is constructed to capture the distribution of stellar spectral features without relying on synthetic models.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Synthetic injection tests are used to validate the catalog, reported uncertainties, and biases of the MADGICS method.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: Chi-squared goodness-of-fit cuts are used to validate spectral decompositions and exclude poor fits from the DIB catalog.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses measurement contamination from stellar spectral features and uses data-driven stellar priors to marginalize over stellar types.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The spectral data-space Gaussian prior implicitly performs dimensionality reduction by capturing the principal modes of stellar spectral variation.

title: Walk These Ways: Tuning Robot Control for Generalization with Multiplicity of Behavior
abstract: Learned locomotion policies can rapidly adapt to diverse environments similar to those experienced during training but lack a mechanism for fast tuning when they fail in an out-of-distribution test environment. This necessitates a slow and iterative cycle of reward and environment redesign to achieve good performance on a new task. As an alternative, we propose learning a single policy that encodes a structured family of locomotion strategies that solve training tasks in different ways, resulting in Multiplicity of Behavior (MoB). Different strategies generalize differently and can be chosen in real-time for new tasks or environments, bypassing the need for time-consuming retraining. We release a fast, robust open-source MoB locomotion controller, Walk These Ways, that can execute diverse gaits with variable footswing, posture, and speed, unlocking diverse downstream tasks: crouching, hopping, high-speed running, stair traversal, bracing against shoves, rhythmic dance, and more. Video and code release: https://gmargo11.github.io/walk-these-ways/

conceptDetails.name: multiplicity of behavior
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core novel contribution: a single policy encodes a structured family of locomotion strategies with equivalent training performance but differing generalization biases, enabling real-time behavioral selection.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The primary training paradigm used to learn locomotion policies in simulation that transfer to real quadruped robots.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The central goal of the paper: enabling a policy trained on flat ground to generalize to out-of-distribution terrains and dynamics without retraining.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: The end-to-end pipeline of training locomotion policies in physics simulation and successfully deploying them on physical quadruped robots in diverse real-world conditions.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: A single policy is trained to encode a structured family of locomotion strategies that can solve diverse downstream tasks including stair traversal, crouching, and dance.

conceptDetails.name: behavior parameterization
conceptDetails.description: A compact set of interpretable parameters (gait frequency, footswing height, stance width, body height) exposed as a high-level control interface for human-in-the-loop tuning.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Sim-to-real transfer is a core challenge addressed, with the policy trained entirely in simulation and deployed on physical hardware.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Auxiliary reward terms are used to shape gait properties such as foot clearance, action smoothness, and contact schedules to bridge the sim-to-real gap.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: Behavior parameters (footswing, posture, speed, gait frequency) form a structured, interpretable latent space that disentangles distinct locomotion strategies.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The framework explicitly targets out-of-distribution scenarios—environments not modeled during training—and provides a mechanism to adapt without retraining.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Careful design of auxiliary reward components is used to bias the learned policy toward behaviors that generalize across unseen environments.

title: Yang-Mills glueball masses from spectral reconstruction
abstract: We compute masses of the two lightest glueballs from spectral reconstructions of timelike interaction channels of the four-gluon vertex in Landau gauge Yang-Mills theory. The Euclidean spacelike dressings of the vertex are calculated with the functional renormalisation group. For the spectral reconstruction of these Euclidean data, we employ Gaussian process regression. The glueball resonances can be identified straightforwardly and we obtain $m_{sc} = 1870(75)~$ MeV as well as $m_{ps} = 2700(120)~$ MeV, in accordance with functional bound state and lattice calculations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper works within Yang-Mills quantum field theory to compute glueball bound state masses from gauge-fixed correlation functions.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral reconstruction of timelike correlation functions from Euclidean data is the central computational technique used to identify glueball resonances.
