conceptDetails.name: glueball spectroscopy
conceptDetails.description: Novel application: computing masses of purely gluonic bound states (scalar and pseudo-scalar glueballs) via overlap with resonant interaction channels of gauge-fixed correlators.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: The functional renormalization group (fRG) is the primary framework used to compute Euclidean spacelike dressings of the four-gluon vertex.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The inversion of the Källén-Lehmann spectral representation from Euclidean correlators is explicitly characterized as an ill-conditioned inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: functional renormalization group
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the fRG framework used to non-perturbatively compute four-gluon vertex dressings in Euclidean space as input for spectral reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian process regression, a Bayesian non-parametric method, is employed to perform the spectral reconstruction from Euclidean data.

conceptDetails.name: kallen-lehmann representation
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the spectral integral representation relating Euclidean propagators and vertex dressings to their Minkowski-space spectral functions.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian process regression is the specific machine learning technique used to reconstruct spectral functions from finite, noisy Euclidean input data.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian process regression relies on kernel functions to encode prior assumptions about the spectral function's smoothness and structure.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The GPR framework provides principled uncertainty estimates, yielding mass predictions with explicit error bounds (e.g., 1870±75 MeV).

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: Lattice Yang-Mills and lattice QCD calculations serve as key benchmarks for validating the glueball mass predictions obtained in this work.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: Lattice QCD glueball calculations are used for comparison and validation of the functional approach results.

title: Variational Neural-Network Ansatz for Continuum Quantum Field Theory
abstract: Physicists dating back to Feynman have lamented the difficulties of applying the variational principle to quantum field theories. In non-relativistic quantum field theories, the challenge is to parameterize and optimize over the infinitely many $n$-particle wave functions comprising the state's Fock space representation. Here we approach this problem by introducing neural-network quantum field states, a deep learning ansatz that enables application of the variational principle to non-relativistic quantum field theories in the continuum. Our ansatz uses the Deep Sets neural network architecture to simultaneously parameterize all of the $n$-particle wave functions comprising a quantum field state. We employ our ansatz to approximate ground states of various field theories, including an inhomogeneous system and a system with long-range interactions, thus demonstrating a powerful new tool for probing quantum field theories.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central subject: NQFS provides the first variational neural-network ansatz directly applicable to non-relativistic continuum QFTs.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: NQFS parameterizes quantum field states via their Fock space representation as superpositions of n-particle wave functions.

conceptDetails.name: neural-network quantum field states
conceptDetails.description: Novel deep learning ansatz introduced in this paper that uses Deep Sets to simultaneously parameterize all n-particle wave functions in a Fock space.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo in Fock space is the core algorithm used to estimate and optimize the energy of the quantum field state.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Bosonic permutation invariance is a hard architectural constraint, realized through the Deep Sets construction to correctly model indistinguishable particles.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Continuous matrix product states (cMPS) are the primary baseline comparison; NQFS is shown to overcome cMPS limitations in inhomogeneous systems and long-range interactions.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The Deep Sets architecture enforces permutation equivariance/invariance, enabling the network to accept variable-length particle-position inputs.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: NQFS is benchmarked by approximating ground states of the Lieb-Liniger, Calogero-Sutherland, and Klein-Gordon field theory models.

conceptDetails.name: fock space variational ansatz
conceptDetails.description: A novel variadic parameterization strategy that represents a quantum field state as a direct sum over symmetrized n-particle Hilbert spaces for arbitrary n.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Theoretical expressivity proofs cited show that neural-network quantum states can efficiently represent highly entangled states, motivating the NQFS approach.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The work follows the broader paradigm of embedding physical structure (Fock space, permutation symmetry) directly into neural network design.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Regularization of UV divergences is discussed as a limitation of higher-dimensional extensions of cMPS and as a consideration for the Klein-Gordon benchmark.

title: Search for Higgs boson and observation of Z boson through their decay into a charm quark-antiquark pair in boosted topologies in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s}$ = 13 TeV
abstract: A search for the standard model (SM) Higgs boson (H) produced with transverse momentum greater than 450 GeV and decaying to a charm quark-antiquark ($\mathrm{c\bar{c}}$) pair is presented. The search is performed using proton-proton collision data collected at $\sqrt{s}$ = 13 TeV by the CMS experiment at the LHC, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 138 fb$^{-1}$. Boosted H $\to$ $\mathrm{c\bar{c}}$ decay products are reconstructed as a single large-radius jet and identified using a deep neural network charm tagging technique. The method is validated by measuring the Z $\to$ $\mathrm{c\bar{c}}$ decay process, which is observed in association with jets at high $p_\mathrm{T}$ for the first time with a signal strength of 1.00 $_{-0.14}^{+0.17}$ (syst) $\pm$ 0.08 (theo) $\pm$ 0.06 (stat), defined as the ratio of the observed process rate to the standard model expectation. The observed (expected) upper limit on $σ$(H) $\mathcal{B}$(H $\to$ $\mathrm{c\bar{c}}$) is set at 47 (39) times the SM prediction at 95% confidence level.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for Higgs and Z boson decays to charm quark pairs in proton-proton collisions at the LHC at 13 TeV center-of-mass energy.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Boosted decay products are reconstructed as single large-radius AK8 jets using soft-drop mass and substructure observables central to the analysis.

conceptDetails.name: charm jet tagging
conceptDetails.description: A novel mass-decorrelated deep neural network discriminator identifies boosted jets from charm quark-antiquark decays, central to the analysis strategy.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The analysis sets upper limits on the Higgs boson decay to charm quarks, probing second-generation Yukawa couplings beyond current observations.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The search targets the SM Higgs boson decay branching fraction and validates Z boson decays against SM predictions as a control process.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Deep neural network discriminators classify jets as charm-enriched signal versus QCD multijet background using flavor tagging scores.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The analysis extracts signal production cross sections via simultaneous fits to jet mass distributions in signal and control regions.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The paper reports observed and expected upper limits at 95% confidence level using likelihood-based statistical tests.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo event generators interfaced with GEANT4 model detector response for signal and background processes.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Multiple MC generators (MADGRAPH5, POWHEG, PYTHIA) are used to simulate signal and background processes at various perturbative orders.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: High-pT jet triggers are used for online event selection, reaching 90% efficiency at 450 GeV relative to offline selection.

title: Learning Integrable Dynamics with Action-Angle Networks
abstract: Machine learning has become increasingly popular for efficiently modelling the dynamics of complex physical systems, demonstrating a capability to learn effective models for dynamics which ignore redundant degrees of freedom. Learned simulators typically predict the evolution of the system in a step-by-step manner with numerical integration techniques. However, such models often suffer from instability over long roll-outs due to the accumulation of both estimation and integration error at each prediction step. Here, we propose an alternative construction for learned physical simulators that are inspired by the concept of action-angle coordinates from classical mechanics for describing integrable systems. We propose Action-Angle Networks, which learn a nonlinear transformation from input coordinates to the action-angle space, where evolution of the system is linear. Unlike traditional learned simulators, Action-Angle Networks do not employ any higher-order numerical integration methods, making them extremely efficient at modelling the dynamics of integrable physical systems.

conceptDetails.name: action-angle coordinates
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to the paper: the classical mechanics coordinate system where integrable Hamiltonian dynamics become linear, used as the latent space of the network.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on learning dynamics of Hamiltonian systems, using the Hamiltonian framework to motivate the action-angle coordinate representation.

conceptDetails.name: integrable systems
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept defining the class of physical systems targeted — those with sufficient symmetry to admit action-angle coordinates and linear dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Symplectic normalizing flows (G-SympNet layers) are used as the encoder to learn the invertible transformation from canonical to action-angle coordinates.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The encoder is constrained to learn only symplectic transformations, preserving the symplectic structure of Hamiltonian mechanics throughout the network.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Action-Angle Networks learn a nonlinear transformation to a latent action-angle coordinate space where system dynamics become linear.

conceptDetails.name: symplectic structure
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept describing the geometric property of Hamiltonian phase space that the encoder is explicitly constrained to preserve via symplectic normalizing flows.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The architecture incorporates inductive bias from classical mechanics (action-angle coordinates) to constrain the learned simulator to integrable dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Action-Angle Networks serve as efficient surrogate simulators for integrable physical systems, bypassing costly numerical integration.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: The constancy of action variables across trajectories encodes conservation laws of integrable systems, which the network explicitly respects.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Learning the action-angle transformation from observed canonical coordinate trajectories is an inverse problem — inferring the latent structure from data.

title: Can denoising diffusion probabilistic models generate realistic astrophysical fields?
abstract: Score-based generative models have emerged as alternatives to generative adversarial networks (GANs) and normalizing flows for tasks involving learning and sampling from complex image distributions. In this work we investigate the ability of these models to generate fields in two astrophysical contexts: dark matter mass density fields from cosmological simulations and images of interstellar dust. We examine the fidelity of the sampled cosmological fields relative to the true fields using three different metrics, and identify potential issues to address. We demonstrate a proof-of-concept application of the model trained on dust in denoising dust images. To our knowledge, this is the first application of this class of models to the interstellar medium.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) are the central method investigated for generating realistic astrophysical fields.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: DDPMs belong to the broader class of score-based generative models, which learn the gradient of the data probability density to model complex distributions.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The CAMELS Multifield Dataset from IllustrisTNG hydrodynamical simulations provides the dark matter density field training data.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper benchmarks generative model approaches for learning and sampling from astrophysical field distributions.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Cold dark matter mass density fields from cosmological simulations are one of the two primary application domains evaluated.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The long-term goal of the work is building emulators that generate cosmological fields conditional on input parameter vectors, replacing expensive simulations.

conceptDetails.name: minkowski functionals
conceptDetails.description: Topological descriptors sensitive to higher-order correlations used as evaluation metrics to compare generated and real cosmological field statistics.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Denoising dust images using the trained DDPM is framed as an inverse problem: reconstructing a clean image from a noisy observation.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A U-Net convolutional architecture with ResNet blocks, group normalization, and attention is used as the backbone for the denoising neural network.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: GANs are presented as the primary prior alternative to score-based models for astrophysical field generation, motivating the study.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The power spectrum is one of three summary statistics used to evaluate the fidelity of generated cosmological fields against ground truth.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are cited as another alternative generative approach that DDPMs are compared against conceptually.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: CMB B-mode searches are a motivating application for dust field modeling, as component separation requires accurate statistical descriptions of interstellar dust.

title: Visual Dexterity: In-Hand Reorientation of Novel and Complex Object Shapes
abstract: In-hand object reorientation is necessary for performing many dexterous manipulation tasks, such as tool use in less structured environments that remain beyond the reach of current robots. Prior works built reorientation systems assuming one or many of the following: reorienting only specific objects with simple shapes, limited range of reorientation, slow or quasistatic manipulation, simulation-only results, the need for specialized and costly sensor suites, and other constraints which make the system infeasible for real-world deployment. We present a general object reorientation controller that does not make these assumptions. It uses readings from a single commodity depth camera to dynamically reorient complex and new object shapes by any rotation in real-time, with the median reorientation time being close to seven seconds. The controller is trained using reinforcement learning in simulation and evaluated in the real world on new object shapes not used for training, including the most challenging scenario of reorienting objects held in the air by a downward-facing hand that must counteract gravity during reorientation. Our hardware platform only uses open-source components that cost less than five thousand dollars. Although we demonstrate the ability to overcome assumptions in prior work, there is ample scope for improving absolute performance. For instance, the challenging duck-shaped object not used for training was dropped in 56 percent of the trials. When it was not dropped, our controller reoriented the object within 0.4 radians (23 degrees) 75 percent of the time. Videos are available at: https://taochenshh.github.io/projects/visual-dexterity.

conceptDetails.name: dexterous manipulation
conceptDetails.description: The core robotics task: dynamically reorienting complex, novel objects to any target rotation using a multi-fingered hand, including the gravity-counteracting downward-facing configuration.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper trains a teacher control policy via RL in simulation with full state information, then distills it into a vision-based student policy for real-world deployment.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: A central challenge of the paper is transferring a policy trained purely in simulation to real-world hardware, addressed via domain randomization and a two-stage vision training pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: A teacher-student training paradigm is used where an RL-trained teacher with privileged state information supervises a student policy that learns from visual (point cloud) inputs.

conceptDetails.name: point cloud policy
conceptDetails.description: A novel policy architecture that directly maps raw 3D point cloud observations from a commodity depth camera to robot actions, enabling object-agnostic generalization.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A sparse 3D convolutional neural network processes point clouds at 12Hz for real-time feedback control, enabling fast inference over complex object geometries.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The policy learns task-relevant representations directly from raw point clouds, bypassing the need for explicit per-object pose estimation and enabling generalization to novel shapes.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: The vision policy is first trained on fast synthetic point clouds, then fine-tuned with rendered point clouds to close the sim-to-real gap while reducing total training time by 5x.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Depth camera readings are converted to point clouds from which the policy extracts spatial features sufficient for dynamic dexterous control without explicit state estimation.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Reward shaping guides the RL teacher to achieve full-rotation reorientation across diverse object shapes while encouraging stable grasp and gravity compensation.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Domain randomization over object properties, physics parameters, and visual inputs is applied to reduce the sim-to-real gap for dynamic manipulation.

title: Characterizing 4-string contact interaction using machine learning
abstract: The geometry of 4-string contact interaction of closed string field theory is characterized using machine learning. We obtain Strebel quadratic differentials on 4-punctured spheres as a neural network by performing unsupervised learning with a custom-built loss function. This allows us to solve for local coordinates and compute their associated mapping radii numerically. We also train a neural network distinguishing vertex from Feynman region. As a check, 4-tachyon contact term in the tachyon potential is computed and a good agreement with the results in the literature is observed. We argue that our algorithm is manifestly independent of number of punctures and scaling it to characterize the geometry of $n$-string contact interaction is feasible.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly characterizes the geometry of closed string field theory contact interactions using machine learning methods.

conceptDetails.name: strebel quadratic differentials
conceptDetails.description: The central mathematical object of the paper: meromorphic quadratic differentials whose critical graph decomposes punctured spheres into cylinders, here represented as neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A custom loss function encoding the Strebel condition is used to train neural networks without labeled data, embedding the physics directly into the learning objective.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A novel unsupervised loss function minimized only when a quadratic differential satisfies the Strebel property is the central algorithmic contribution.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: Closed string field theory, a non-polynomial quantum field theory, provides the physical framework whose geometry the paper resolves.

conceptDetails.name: string vertex region
conceptDetails.description: The sub-region of moduli space where string contact interactions contribute, implicitly defined by non-contractible curve lengths and learned by the indicator network.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The accessory parameter neural network performs non-parametric regression to represent the Strebel quadratic differential as a function over moduli space.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A second neural network is trained as an indicator function to classify punctured spheres as belonging to the vertex region or Feynman region of moduli space.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Trained neural networks act as fast surrogates for costly numerical evaluations of Strebel differentials and vertex region boundaries.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Finding the Strebel differential satisfying boundary conditions at punctures without prior knowledge of the critical graph constitutes an inverse problem solved via ML.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm is deliberately designed to be independent of the number of punctures, enabling future scaling to n-string contact interactions.

conceptDetails.name: tachyon potential
conceptDetails.description: The 4-tachyon contact term in the bosonic closed string tachyon potential is computed numerically as a validation benchmark against prior literature results.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo integration is discussed as the required approach for higher-dimensional moduli integrals in the n-punctured generalization.

title: Aspects of scaling and scalability for flow-based sampling of lattice QCD
abstract: Recent applications of machine-learned normalizing flows to sampling in lattice field theory suggest that such methods may be able to mitigate critical slowing down and topological freezing. However, these demonstrations have been at the scale of toy models, and it remains to be determined whether they can be applied to state-of-the-art lattice quantum chromodynamics calculations. Assessing the viability of sampling algorithms for lattice field theory at scale has traditionally been accomplished using simple cost scaling laws, but as we discuss in this work, their utility is limited for flow-based approaches. We conclude that flow-based approaches to sampling are better thought of as a broad family of algorithms with different scaling properties, and that scalability must be assessed experimentally.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The primary ML method studied for generating lattice field configurations by learning a diffeomorphic map from a simple base distribution to the target lattice QCD distribution.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The target theory for which flow-based sampling scalability is assessed, representing the state-of-the-art application lattice field theory calculations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Central topic of the paper — analyzing whether and how flow-based sampling approaches can scale to state-of-the-art lattice QCD volumes and parameters.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The baseline framework (HMC/MCMC) against which flow-based sampling is compared, and the broader class of methods within which flow approaches operate.

conceptDetails.name: critical slowing down
conceptDetails.description: A key algorithmic failure mode of HMC near the continuum limit that flow-based methods aim to mitigate, motivating the scalability analysis.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The broader theoretical framework encompassing lattice QCD, including gauge symmetries and topological structure that flow models must handle.

conceptDetails.name: topological freezing
conceptDetails.description: The problem of MCMC chains becoming trapped in distinct topological sectors, which normalizing flows may alleviate by directly modeling multi-modal distributions.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Underpins the Hybrid/Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) algorithm used as the state-of-the-art baseline for gauge field configuration generation.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Flow models are trained to approximate the target lattice field distribution p∝exp(-S), with reweighting factors evaluated between the learned and true densities.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Gauge-equivariant flow transformations are highlighted as one architectural approach for incorporating gauge symmetry into flow models for lattice QCD.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: MCMC-based generation of gauge field configurations is the foundational stochastic framework within which both HMC and flow-based methods are analyzed.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge symmetry must be preserved by flow architectures; the paper discusses strategies such as equivariant transformations and their impact on scaling.

title: Limits on Simultaneous and Delayed Optical Emission from Well-localized Fast Radio Bursts
abstract: We present the largest compilation to date of optical observations during and following fast radio bursts (FRBs). The data set includes our dedicated simultaneous and follow-up observations, as well as serendipitous archival survey observations, for a sample of 15 well-localized FRBs: eight repeating and seven one-off sources. Our simultaneous (and nearly simultaneous with a $0.4$ s delay) optical observations of 13 (1) bursts from the repeating FRB 20220912A provide the deepest such limits to date for any extragalactic FRB, reaching a luminosity limit of $νL_ν\lesssim 10^{42}$ erg s$^{-1}$ ($\lesssim 2\times10^{41}$ erg s$^{-1}$) with $15-400$ s exposures; an optical-flux-to-radio-fluence ratio of $f_{\rm opt}/F_{\rm radio}\lesssim 10^{-7}$ ms$^{-1}$ ($\lesssim 10^{-8}$ ms$^{-1}$); and flux ratio of $f_{\rm opt}/f_{\rm radio}\lesssim 0.02-\lesssim 2\times 10^{-5}$ ($\lesssim 10^{-6}$) on millisecond to second timescales. These simultaneous limits provide useful constraints in the context of FRB emission models, such as the pulsar magnetosphere and pulsar nebula models. Interpreting all available optical limits in the context of the synchrotron maser model, we find that they constrain the flare energies to $\lesssim 10^{43}-10^{49}$ erg (depending on the distances of the various repeating FRBs, with $\lesssim 10^{39}$ erg for the Galactic SGR 1935+2154). These limits are generally at least an order of magnitude larger than those inferred from the FRBs themselves, although in the case of FRB 20220912A our simultaneous and rapid follow-up observations severely restrict the model parameter space. We conclude by exploring the potential of future simultaneous and rapid-response observations with large optical telescopes.

conceptDetails.name: fast radio bursts
conceptDetails.description: Millisecond-duration extragalactic radio pulses that are the central objects of study; 15 well-localized FRBs (eight repeating, seven one-off) are analysed for simultaneous and delayed optical counterpart emission.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Core methodology of the paper: deep optical searches for counterparts to FRBs, establishing the deepest simultaneous luminosity and flux ratio upper limits to date for any extragalactic FRB.

conceptDetails.name: synchrotron maser emission
conceptDetails.description: A leading FRB emission mechanism involving synchrotron maser radiation from relativistic magnetized shocks; its parameter space is directly constrained by the compiled optical upper limits.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar outbursts
conceptDetails.description: Energetic flares from highly magnetized neutron stars invoked as FRB progenitors, with flare energies constrained to ≲10^43–10^49 erg by the multi-epoch optical non-detections.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Statistical framework for deriving upper limits on optical emission from non-detections and using those limits to constrain FRB emission model parameter spaces.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Used to interpret non-detection constraints within emission model frameworks, bounding flare energies, circumburst densities, and other model parameters.

conceptDetails.name: goodness-of-fit testing
conceptDetails.description: Evaluating the consistency of the synchrotron maser, pulsar magnetosphere, and pulsar nebula models with the observed optical upper limits across the FRB sample.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Systematic treatment of observational uncertainties, distance-dependent limits, and exposure-time effects when converting flux upper limits to luminosity constraints.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Neutron star and magnetar formation channels—accretion-induced collapse of white dwarfs and neutron star mergers—are discussed as candidate FRB progenitor pathways.

title: Gauge Equivariant Neural Networks for 2+1D U(1) Gauge Theory Simulations in Hamiltonian Formulation
abstract: Gauge Theory plays a crucial role in many areas in science, including high energy physics, condensed matter physics and quantum information science. In quantum simulations of lattice gauge theory, an important step is to construct a wave function that obeys gauge symmetry. In this paper, we have developed gauge equivariant neural network wave function techniques for simulating continuous-variable quantum lattice gauge theories in the Hamiltonian formulation. We have applied the gauge equivariant neural network approach to find the ground state of 2+1-dimensional lattice gauge theory with U(1) gauge group using variational Monte Carlo. We have benchmarked our approach against the state-of-the-art complex Gaussian wave functions, demonstrating improved performance in the strong coupling regime and comparable results in the weak coupling regime.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution — constructing neural network wave functions that transform correctly under local U(1) gauge transformations, encoding the gauge principle directly into the architecture.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The physical framework studied: a 2+1D compact U(1) Kogut-Susskind model defined on a square lattice with periodic boundary conditions in Hamiltonian formulation.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge invariance and equivariance are enforced by construction in the neural network ansatz, ensuring the wave function satisfies the physical constraint g·ψ = ψ.

conceptDetails.name: neural network quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paradigm of using neural networks as variational wave function ansätze, extended here to continuous-variable gauge theories with exact symmetry enforcement.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets quantum simulation of continuous-variable lattice gauge theories, seeking the ground state wave function via a variational approach.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Neural network quantum states (NNQS) are used as compact, trainable ansätze for the exponentially large Hilbert space of the lattice gauge theory.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo is used to optimize the gauge equivariant neural network wave function by minimizing the energy expectation value.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The paper works in Hamiltonian lattice gauge theory, in contrast to the Lagrangian path-integral approach, defining the problem as ground-state eigenvalue estimation of H.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The U(1) Lie group structure dictates how gauge transformations act on field configurations and wave functions, forming the mathematical backbone of the equivariant architecture.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper is grounded in quantum field theory, specifically compact U(1) gauge theory as a prototype for understanding strongly coupled gauge theories.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The gauge equivariant blocks are implemented as CNN-like architectures that respect lattice translation symmetry and gauge equivariance simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The architecture leverages geometric deep learning principles — defining equivariant maps on the graph of lattice sites — to generalize CNNs to gauge-covariant settings.

title: QuACK: Accelerating Gradient-Based Quantum Optimization with Koopman Operator Learning
abstract: Quantum optimization, a key application of quantum computing, has traditionally been stymied by the linearly increasing complexity of gradient calculations with an increasing number of parameters. This work bridges the gap between Koopman operator theory, which has found utility in applications because it allows for a linear representation of nonlinear dynamical systems, and natural gradient methods in quantum optimization, leading to a significant acceleration of gradient-based quantum optimization. We present Quantum-circuit Alternating Controlled Koopman learning (QuACK), a novel framework that leverages an alternating algorithm for efficient prediction of gradient dynamics on quantum computers. We demonstrate QuACK's remarkable ability to accelerate gradient-based optimization across a range of applications in quantum optimization and machine learning. In fact, our empirical studies, spanning quantum chemistry, quantum condensed matter, quantum machine learning, and noisy environments, have shown accelerations of more than 200x speedup in the overparameterized regime, 10x speedup in the smooth regime, and 3x speedup in the non-smooth regime. With QuACK, we offer a robust advancement that harnesses the advantage of gradient-based quantum optimization for practical benefits.

conceptDetails.name: koopman operator learning
conceptDetails.description: The core novel method: learns a linear operator that embeds nonlinear quantum optimization dynamics into a higher-dimensional space with approximately linear evolution, enabling efficient prediction.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets variational quantum algorithms (VQAs) running on quantum computers, accelerating their gradient-based optimization to make them practically viable.

conceptDetails.name: variational quantum algorithms
conceptDetails.description: The application framework (VQAs/VQE/QAOA) whose gradient-based optimization is being accelerated by QuACK across all experiments in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The central problem addressed is that gradient computation scales linearly with the number of parameters; QuACK achieves speedups of 10x–200x by decoupling prediction cost from parameter count.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: QuACK is validated on quantum chemistry (H2, LiH, BeH2) and quantum condensed matter (Ising, Heisenberg models) simulations as primary application domains.

conceptDetails.name: quantum natural gradient
conceptDetails.description: The specific gradient method whose geometric properties are theoretically linked to Koopman dynamics in this work, motivating the QuACK framework.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Quantum systems are described by Hamiltonians; the VQE minimizes expectation values of these Hamiltonians, and QuACK accelerates this optimization.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The learned Koopman operator acts as a surrogate model for gradient dynamics, predicting parameter updates without executing full quantum circuit evaluations.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Koopman operator learning relies on eigendecomposition of the linear evolution operator to identify invariant subspaces and predict gradient dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Dynamic Mode Decomposition and Koopman mode decomposition are spectral techniques used to extract linear dynamics from the quantum optimization trajectory.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Quantum natural gradient employs the quantum Fisher information matrix (QFIM), which plays an analogous role to a kernel metric defining the geometry of parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The noisy quantum environment regime is tested, modeling parameter update trajectories under stochastic noise typical of near-term quantum hardware.

title: A Solvable Model of Neural Scaling Laws
abstract: Large language models with a huge number of parameters, when trained on near internet-sized number of tokens, have been empirically shown to obey neural scaling laws: specifically, their performance behaves predictably as a power law in either parameters or dataset size until bottlenecked by the other resource. To understand this better, we first identify the necessary properties allowing such scaling laws to arise and then propose a statistical model -- a joint generative data model and random feature model -- that captures this neural scaling phenomenology. By solving this model in the dual limit of large training set size and large number of parameters, we gain insight into (i) the statistical structure of datasets and tasks that lead to scaling laws, (ii) the way nonlinear feature maps, such as those provided by neural networks, enable scaling laws when trained on these datasets, (iii) the optimality of the equiparameterization scaling of training sets and parameters, and (iv) whether such scaling laws can break down and how they behave when they do. Key findings are the manner in which the power laws that occur in the statistics of natural datasets are extended by nonlinear random feature maps and then translated into power-law scalings of the test loss and how the finite extent of the data's spectral power law causes the model's performance to plateau.

conceptDetails.name: neural scaling laws
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon studied: power-law scaling of test loss with model parameters and dataset size in large language models, which this paper provides a theoretical explanation for.

conceptDetails.name: random feature model
conceptDetails.description: The core theoretical framework used — a joint generative data and random feature model that is analytically solved to reproduce neural scaling law phenomenology.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral power-law structure of natural datasets is identified as the necessary condition for scaling laws to emerge, and how nonlinear feature maps extend this spectrum is a key result.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses how model performance scales with parameters and dataset size, identifying optimal resource allocation strategies for training large models.

conceptDetails.name: equiparameterization
conceptDetails.description: A novel finding that exact equiparameterization — scaling the number of features identically with training set size — is provably optimal in the model's scaling regime.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Nonlinear feature maps (as provided by neural networks) are shown to extend spectral power laws in data representations, enabling scaling behavior that linear models cannot achieve.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Random feature models are closely related to kernel/RKHS methods; the paper's analytical solutions connect to kernel regression theory and the Marchenko-Pastur distribution.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Generalized linear models and ridge regression serve as the tractable base case for analysis, with results extended to nonlinear feature maps in the main results.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The spectral decomposition of the data covariance matrix and its power-law eigenvalue spectrum is a core mathematical object in deriving the scaling law predictions.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes how different feature representations (linear vs. nonlinear) affect a model's capacity to exhibit scaling laws on structured datasets.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Large transformer-based language models are the primary empirical motivation, with scaling laws from GPT-3, PaLM, and Chinchilla guiding the theoretical targets of the model.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The generative data model is formulated as a statistical/stochastic process whose spectral properties are analytically characterized in the large-sample limit.

title: Deep Learning Detection and Classification of Gravitational Waves from Neutron Star-Black Hole Mergers
abstract: The Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) and Virgo Interferometer Collaborations have now detected all three classes of compact binary mergers: binary black hole (BBH), binary neutron star (BNS), and neutron star-black hole (NSBH). For coalescences involving neutron stars, the simultaneous observation of gravitational and electromagnetic radiation produced by an event, has broader potential to enhance our understanding of these events, and also to probe the equation of state (EOS) of dense matter. However, electromagnetic follow-up to gravitational wave (GW) events requires rapid real-time detection and classification of GW signals, and conventional detection approaches are computationally prohibitive for the anticipated rate of detection of next-generation GW detectors. In this work, we present the first deep learning based results of classification of GW signals from NSBH mergers in \textit{real} LIGO data. We show for the first time that a deep neural network can successfully distinguish all three classes of compact binary mergers and separate them from detector noise. Specifically, we train a convolutional neural network (CNN) on $\sim 500,000$ data samples of real LIGO noise with injected BBH, BNS, and NSBH GW signals, and we show that our network has high sensitivity and accuracy. Most importantly, we successfully recover the two confirmed NSBH events to-date (GW200105 and GW200115) and the two confirmed BNS mergers to-date (GW170817 and GW190425), together with $\approx 90\%$ of all BBH candidate events from the third Gravitational Wave Transient Catalog, GWTC-3. These results are an important step towards low-latency real-time GW detection, enabling multi-messenger astronomy.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses on detection and classification of gravitational wave signals from compact binary mergers using deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A CNN is trained on ~500,000 real LIGO noise samples with injected GW signals to classify BBH, BNS, and NSBH merger events.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The core task is multi-class classification distinguishing BBH, BNS, NSBH signals and detector noise.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The network performs real-time low-latency detection of gravitational wave signals embedded in non-stationary, non-Gaussian LIGO noise.

conceptDetails.name: compact binary coalescence
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the paper addresses all three classes of compact binary mergers (BBH, BNS, NSBH) as the physical source of GW signals.

conceptDetails.name: multi-messenger astronomy
conceptDetails.description: Rapid GW classification enables prompt electromagnetic follow-up, directly supporting multi-messenger astrophysics observations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The deep learning approach is motivated by its computational efficiency compared to matched-filtering, enabling scalability to next-generation detector rates.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Large training datasets are constructed by injecting simulated waveforms into real LIGO noise to augment training data.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The CNN automatically extracts discriminative time-frequency features from whitened gravitational wave strain data.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The network must separate genuine GW signals from detector noise artifacts, effectively detecting anomalous astrophysical signals.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The model leverages patterns learned from simulated injections to generalize to real observed GW events from GWTC-3.

title: Large-time correlation functions in bosonic lattice field theories
abstract: Large-time correlation functions have a pivotal role in extracting particle masses from Euclidean lattice field theory calculations, however little is known about the statistical properties of these quantities. In this work, the asymptotic form of the distributions of the correlation functions at vanishing momentum is determined for bosonic interacting lattice field theories with a unique gapped vacuum. It is demonstrated that the deviations from the asymptotic form at large Euclidean times can be utilized to determine the spectrum of the theory.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The work operates within the framework of Euclidean quantum field theory, analyzing statistical properties of path-integral-defined correlation functions.

conceptDetails.name: correlation function distributions
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives the exact asymptotic probability distributions of bosonic two-point correlation functions, a novel analytical result for lattice field theory.

conceptDetails.name: signal-to-noise problem
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses the Parisi-Lepage signal-to-noise problem, where correlation function estimator variance grows exponentially relative to the signal at large times.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops analytical tools for correlation functions in bosonic lattice field theories, directly relevant to lattice QCD calculations of particle masses.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives asymptotic distributions of correlation functions, characterizing the stochastic fluctuations that arise in Monte Carlo lattice calculations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Lattice QFT relies on stochastic Monte Carlo sampling of path integrals; the paper addresses statistical limitations of these simulations at large Euclidean times.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A central goal is characterizing the statistical distributions of large-time correlators to enable rigorous uncertainty quantification in lattice field theory.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper shows that deviations from the asymptotic distribution encode excited-state contributions, providing a spectral method for determining the energy spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The derivation uses eigenvalue decomposition of the lattice Klein-Gordon operator to compute characteristic functions and resulting probability distributions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The asymptotic analysis leverages effective field theory reasoning to isolate ground-state and excited-state contributions at large Euclidean times.

title: Precision Machine Learning
abstract: We explore unique considerations involved in fitting ML models to data with very high precision, as is often required for science applications. We empirically compare various function approximation methods and study how they scale with increasing parameters and data. We find that neural networks can often outperform classical approximation methods on high-dimensional examples, by auto-discovering and exploiting modular structures therein. However, neural networks trained with common optimizers are less powerful for low-dimensional cases, which motivates us to study the unique properties of neural network loss landscapes and the corresponding optimization challenges that arise in the high precision regime. To address the optimization issue in low dimensions, we develop training tricks which enable us to train neural networks to extremely low loss, close to the limits allowed by numerical precision.

conceptDetails.name: precision machine learning
conceptDetails.description: A novel regime introduced by the paper: fitting ML models to data with loss approaching numerical precision limits (~1e-16), distinct from standard ML practice.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames precision ML as a supervised regression task, fitting functions to datasets of symbolic formulas from physics.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central focus is how approximation error scales as a power-law with number of model parameters and training data size.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies loss landscape geometry and develops novel training tricks to push loss to numerical precision limits.

conceptDetails.name: modular structure discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper finds that neural networks auto-discover and exploit compositional/modular structure in high-dimensional symbolic functions to outperform classical methods.

conceptDetails.name: loss landscape geometry
conceptDetails.description: The paper characterizes unique properties of neural network loss landscapes in the high-precision regime that obstruct standard optimizers.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets symbolic regression and formula discovery, which requires models that produce interpretable, exact functional forms.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression—recovering exact analytic formulas from data—is framed as a core science inverse problem motivating this work.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Classical sparse approximation methods are compared against neural networks as baselines for high-precision function fitting.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Precision ML is motivated by scientific surrogate modeling needs where fitting symbolic physics functions to very low error is required.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Kernel-based approximation methods (e.g., Gaussian processes) are included in empirical comparisons of function approximators.

title: The First Two Years of FLEET: an Active Search for Superluminous Supernovae
abstract: In November 2019 we began operating FLEET (Finding Luminous and Exotic Extragalactic Transients), a machine learning algorithm designed to photometrically identify Type I superluminous supernovae (SLSNe) in transient alert streams. Using FLEET, we spectroscopically classified 21 of the 50 SLSNe identified worldwide between November 2019 and January 2022. Based on our original algorithm, we anticipated that FLEET would achieve a purity of about 50\% for transients with a probability of being a SLSN, \pslsn$>0.5$; the true on-sky purity we obtained is closer to 80\%. Similarly, we anticipated FLEET could reach a completeness of about 30\%, and we indeed measure an upper limit on the completeness of $\approx 33$\%. Here, we present FLEET 2.0, an updated version of FLEET trained on 4,780 transients (almost 3 times more than in FLEET 1.0). FLEET 2.0 has a similar predicted purity to FLEET 1.0, but outperforms FLEET 1.0 in terms of completeness, which is now closer to $\approx 40$\% for transients with \pslsn$>0.5$. Additionally, we explore possible systematics that might arise from the use of FLEET for target selection. We find that the population of SLSNe recovered by FLEET is mostly indistinguishable from the overall SLSN population, in terms of physical and most observational parameters. We provide FLEET as an open source package on GitHub https://github.com/gmzsebastian/FLEET

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: FLEET photometrically classifies Type I superluminous supernovae from transient alert streams without requiring spectroscopy.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A random forest machine learning classifier assigns each transient a probability of being a SLSN using light curve and host galaxy features.

conceptDetails.name: photometric transient classification
conceptDetails.description: Classification of transient type from photometric light curves alone, prior to expensive spectroscopic confirmation.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: FLEET operates as an active search tool, selecting the most promising SLSN candidates for spectroscopic follow-up in real time.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Predicted purity and completeness from FLEET are empirically calibrated against on-sky performance over two years of operations.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: FLEET 2.0 is validated by comparing predicted performance metrics against actual classification outcomes from 50 real SLSNe discovered worldwide.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Light curve shape parameters and host galaxy properties are extracted as features for the random forest classifier.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: A random forest ensemble classifier is the core algorithm powering FLEET's SLSN probability estimates.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: FLEET identifies rare SLSNe (approximately 1.5% of all transients) within high-volume photometric alert streams.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: FLEET guides spectroscopic telescope allocation decisions by ranking candidates for follow-up, maximizing efficient use of limited resources.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detecting genuine SLSN signals against a large background of other transient types from wide-field time-domain surveys.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: Selection bias analysis examines whether SLSNe recovered by FLEET differ systematically from the general SLSN population.

title: Identifying Tidal Disruption Events with an Expansion of the FLEET Machine Learning Algorithm
abstract: We present an expansion of FLEET, a machine learning algorithm optimized to select transients that are most likely to be tidal disruption events (TDEs). FLEET is based on a random forest algorithm trained on the light curves and host galaxy information of 4,779 spectroscopically classified transients. For transients with a probability of being a TDE, \ptde$>0.5$, we can successfully recover TDEs with a $\approx40$\% completeness and a $\approx30$\% purity when using the first 20 days of photometry, or a similar completeness and $\approx50$\% purity when including 40 days of photometry. We find that the most relevant features for differentiating TDEs from other transients are the normalized host separation, and the light curve $(g-r)$ color during peak. Additionally, we use FLEET to produce a list of the 39 most likely TDE candidates discovered by the Zwicky Transient Facility that remain currently unclassified. We explore the use of FLEET for the Legacy Survey of Space and Time on the Vera C. Rubin Observatory (\textit{Rubin}) and the \textit{Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope} (\textit{Roman}). We simulate the \textit{Rubin} and \textit{Roman} survey strategies and estimate that $\sim 10^4$ TDEs could be discovered every year by \textit{Rubin}, and $\sim200$ TDEs per year by \textit{Roman}. Finally, we run FLEET on the TDEs in our \textit{Rubin} survey simulation and find that we can recover $\sim 30$\% of those at a redshift $z <0.5$ with \ptde$>0.5$. This translates to $\sim3,000$ TDEs per year that FLEET could uncover from \textit{Rubin}. FLEET is provided as a open source package on GitHub https://github.com/gmzsebastian/FLEET

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: FLEET uses a random forest classifier trained on 4,779 spectroscopically classified transients to assign probabilities of a transient being a TDE.

conceptDetails.name: tidal disruption events
conceptDetails.description: The central astrophysical phenomenon—stars torn apart by supermassive black holes producing bright optical transients—that FLEET is optimized to identify.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: The random forest algorithm at FLEET's core is an ensemble method that aggregates many decision trees to produce robust classification probabilities.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Light curve properties (g-r color at peak, rise/fade times) and host galaxy parameters (normalized separation, photometric redshift) are extracted as discriminating features for TDE classification.

conceptDetails.name: photometric transient classification
conceptDetails.description: FLEET classifies transients from photometry alone (without spectroscopy), enabling scalable candidate selection for costly spectroscopic follow-up.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: A core challenge is distinguishing TDEs from supernovae (Ia, Ibc, II, IIn, SLSN-I), which constitute the vast majority of the training set.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: FLEET was originally designed to identify superluminous supernovae and is here retrained and adapted to target tidal disruption events instead.

conceptDetails.name: survey simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Rubin LSST and Nancy Grace Roman survey cadences are simulated to forecast TDE discovery rates (~10,000/year and ~200/year respectively) and FLEET recovery fractions.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: TDEs represent only ~0.5% of classified transients, making their identification an extreme class-imbalance and rare-event detection problem.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Completeness and purity metrics are rigorously evaluated as a function of probability threshold and photometric baseline length to characterize classifier reliability.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: FLEET is applied to ZTF photometric streams to flag unclassified TDE candidates and is projected for use on future Rubin LSST data streams.

conceptDetails.name: galaxy classification
conceptDetails.description: Host galaxy properties, especially normalized offset from the nucleus, are identified as the most discriminating features for TDE identification.

title: Symmetries of Calabi-Yau Prepotentials with Isomorphic Flops
abstract: Calabi-Yau threefolds with infinitely many flops to isomorphic manifolds have an extended Kahler cone made up from an infinite number of individual Kahler cones. These cones are related by reflection symmetries across flop walls. We study the implications of this cone structure for mirror symmetry, by considering the instanton part of the prepotential in Calabi-Yau threefolds. We show that such isomorphic flops across facets of the Kahler cone boundary give rise to symmetry groups isomorphic to Coxeter groups. In the dual Mori cone, non-flopping curve classes that are identified under these groups have the same Gopakumar-Vafa invariants. This leads to instanton prepotentials invariant under Coxeter groups, which we make manifest by introducing appropriate invariant functions. For some cases, these functions can be expressed in terms of theta functions whose appearance can be linked to an elliptic fibration structure of the Calabi-Yau manifold.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau geometry
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses specifically on Calabi-Yau threefolds, their Kähler cones, flop transitions, mirror symmetry, and the structure of their moduli spaces.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Coxeter groups generated by reflection symmetries across flop walls are central to the paper's mathematical framework for classifying symmetries of the prepotential.

conceptDetails.name: gopakumar-vafa invariants
conceptDetails.description: The paper shows that non-flopping curve classes related by Coxeter group elements share the same Gopakumar-Vafa invariants, which is key to establishing prepotential symmetry.

conceptDetails.name: instanton prepotential
conceptDetails.description: The instanton contribution to the Kähler moduli prepotential is shown to be invariant under Coxeter groups arising from isomorphic flops, and is rewritten using invariant functions.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies Coxeter group symmetries arising from isomorphic flop transitions and constructs invariant functions that make these symmetries manifest in the prepotential.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies Calabi-Yau compactifications within string theory, analyzing how flop transitions and their symmetry structures affect the physical prepotential.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The instanton prepotential for Kähler moduli, a key object in the effective field theory arising from string compactification, is the main quantity analyzed for symmetry properties.

conceptDetails.name: mirror symmetry
conceptDetails.description: The extended Kähler cone arising from infinitely many isomorphic flops is mirror-dual to the complex structure moduli space of the mirror manifold, motivating the symmetry analysis.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the low-energy effective theory arising from Calabi-Yau compactification, focusing on the Kähler moduli prepotential and its invariance properties.

conceptDetails.name: elliptic fibration
conceptDetails.description: For special cases, the Coxeter-invariant functions in the prepotential can be expressed as Jacobi theta functions, whose appearance is traced to an elliptic fibration structure of the Calabi-Yau manifold.

title: Electric-Magnetic Duality in a Class of $G_2$-Compactifications of M-theory
abstract: We study electric-magnetic duality in compactifications of M-theory on twisted connected sum (TCS) $G_2$ manifolds via duality with F-theory. Specifically, we study the physics of the D3-branes in F-theory compactified on a Calabi-Yau fourfold $Y$, dual to a compactification of M-theory on a TCS $G_2$ manifold $X$. $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetry is restored in an appropriate geometric limit. In that limit, we demonstrate that the dual of D3-branes probing seven-branes corresponds to the shrinking of certain surfaces and curves, yielding light particles that may carry both electric and magnetic charges. We provide evidence that the Minahan-Nemeschansky theories with $E_n$ flavor symmetry may be realized in this way. The $SL(2,\mathbb{Z})$ monodromy of the 3/7-brane system is dual to a Fourier-Mukai transform of the dual IIA/M-theory geometry in this limit, and we extrapolate this monodromy action to the global compactification. Away from the limit, the theory is broken to $\mathcal{N}=1$ supersymmetry by a D-term.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates entirely within M-theory and F-theory compactification frameworks, studying dualities between these two string-theoretic descriptions.

conceptDetails.name: g2 compactification
conceptDetails.description: Compactification of M-theory on twisted connected sum G2 manifolds is the primary geometric setting studied, with detailed analysis of moduli space singularities and dual physics.

conceptDetails.name: electric-magnetic duality
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central topic: identifying particles carrying simultaneous electric and magnetic charges in G2-compactifications and characterizing the SL(2,Z) monodromy of the 3/7-brane system.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: The Minahan-Nemeschansky theories with En flavor symmetry are superconformal field theories whose realization via shrinking surfaces in G2-compactifications is a central result.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau geometry
conceptDetails.description: F-theory compactified on elliptic Calabi-Yau fourfolds provides the dual description used to extract concrete physics of the G2-compactification.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The SL(2,Z) duality group and its monodromy action on charged states, as well as En flavor symmetry groups, are fundamental mathematical structures throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates that N=2 supersymmetry is broken to N=1 away from the Kovalev limit via a D-term, providing explicit analysis of this supersymmetry breaking mechanism.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The low-energy four-dimensional effective physics arising from M-theory compactification on G2-manifolds is studied via a supergravity approximation in the large volume limit.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: General QFT framework underpins the analysis of gauge dynamics, charged particle spectra, and Coulomb branch physics in the four-dimensional compactified theory.

title: Learning to Optimize Quasi-Newton Methods
abstract: Fast gradient-based optimization algorithms have become increasingly essential for the computationally efficient training of machine learning models. One technique is to multiply the gradient by a preconditioner matrix to produce a step, but it is unclear what the best preconditioner matrix is. This paper introduces a novel machine learning optimizer called LODO, which tries to online meta-learn the best preconditioner during optimization. Specifically, our optimizer merges Learning to Optimize (L2O) techniques with quasi-Newton methods to learn preconditioners parameterized as neural networks; they are more flexible than preconditioners in other quasi-Newton methods. Unlike other L2O methods, LODO does not require any meta-training on a training task distribution, and instead learns to optimize on the fly while optimizing on the test task, adapting to the local characteristics of the loss landscape while traversing it. Theoretically, we show that our optimizer approximates the inverse Hessian in noisy loss landscapes and is capable of representing a wide range of inverse Hessians. We experimentally verify that our algorithm can optimize in noisy settings, and show that simpler alternatives for representing the inverse Hessians worsen performance. Lastly, we use our optimizer to train a semi-realistic deep neural network with 95k parameters at speeds comparable to those of standard neural network optimizers.

conceptDetails.name: quasi-newton methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends quasi-Newton optimization by replacing handcrafted inverse Hessian estimators with a neural network preconditioner learned online during optimization.

conceptDetails.name: learning to optimize
conceptDetails.description: LODO merges L2O techniques with quasi-Newton methods, enabling an optimizer to meta-learn its own update rule on the fly without offline meta-training.

conceptDetails.name: hypergradient descent
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses hypergradient descent as the mechanism for the meta-optimizer to update the neural network preconditioner during optimization.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Approximating the inverse Hessian of the loss landscape is framed as an inverse problem solved online by a neural network surrogate.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: A neural network acts as a flexible surrogate for the inverse Hessian matrix, replacing closed-form quasi-Newton estimators like L-BFGS.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates LODO on a 95k-parameter network at speeds comparable to standard optimizers, addressing the scalability bottleneck of Newton-based methods.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Theoretical analysis shows LODO correctly approximates the inverse Hessian in stochastic/noisy loss landscapes, a key practical requirement.

conceptDetails.name: test-time scaling
conceptDetails.description: Unlike offline L2O methods, LODO adapts its optimization strategy during the test task itself, scaling its performance through online learning.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The expressiveness of the neural preconditioner is analyzed via its capacity to represent a wide range of inverse Hessian spectra.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The meta-learning framing of LODO relates to multi-task optimization, though LODO uniquely requires no separate meta-training task distribution.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: Prior L2O baselines (e.g., L2L-BGDBGD) use LSTMs as the learned optimizer, providing the architectural context against which LODO is compared.

title: On the Importance of Calibration in Semi-supervised Learning
abstract: State-of-the-art (SOTA) semi-supervised learning (SSL) methods have been highly successful in leveraging a mix of labeled and unlabeled data by combining techniques of consistency regularization and pseudo-labeling. During pseudo-labeling, the model's predictions on unlabeled data are used for training and thus, model calibration is important in mitigating confirmation bias. Yet, many SOTA methods are optimized for model performance, with little focus directed to improve model calibration. In this work, we empirically demonstrate that model calibration is strongly correlated with model performance and propose to improve calibration via approximate Bayesian techniques. We introduce a family of new SSL models that optimizes for calibration and demonstrate their effectiveness across standard vision benchmarks of CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 and ImageNet, giving up to 15.9% improvement in test accuracy. Furthermore, we also demonstrate their effectiveness in additional realistic and challenging problems, such as class-imbalanced datasets and in photonics science.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The core framework of the paper, combining labeled and unlabeled data for training vision models.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The central contribution: improving alignment between model confidence and ground-truth accuracy to reduce confirmation bias in SSL.

conceptDetails.name: pseudo-labeling
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to the paper: assigning artificial labels to unlabeled data, whose quality depends directly on model calibration.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper leverages uncertainty estimates via Bayesian methods to improve pseudo-label quality and model calibration.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Approximate Bayesian techniques (variational BNNs, weight ensembling) are the proposed mechanism for improving calibration.

conceptDetails.name: confirmation bias mitigation
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept: the problem of SSL models reinforcing incorrect predictions, which proper calibration is designed to prevent.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian model averaging and weight-averaging ensembles are used as practical approximations to full Bayesian marginalization.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Consistency regularization via augmented views of unlabeled data is a core component of the SSL methods studied.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: One family of SSL methods studied (PAWS) uses self-supervised representation learning as a first training stage.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The downstream task evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet benchmarks throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Representation learning methods from self-supervised learning are adopted as one class of SSL baselines.

title: Omnigrok: Grokking Beyond Algorithmic Data
abstract: Grokking, the unusual phenomenon for algorithmic datasets where generalization happens long after overfitting the training data, has remained elusive. We aim to understand grokking by analyzing the loss landscapes of neural networks, identifying the mismatch between training and test losses as the cause for grokking. We refer to this as the "LU mechanism" because training and test losses (against model weight norm) typically resemble "L" and "U", respectively. This simple mechanism can nicely explain many aspects of grokking: data size dependence, weight decay dependence, the emergence of representations, etc. Guided by the intuitive picture, we are able to induce grokking on tasks involving images, language and molecules. In the reverse direction, we are able to eliminate grokking for algorithmic datasets. We attribute the dramatic nature of grokking for algorithmic datasets to representation learning.

conceptDetails.name: grokking
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon studied: delayed generalization long after overfitting training data, which this paper generalizes beyond algorithmic datasets to images, language, and molecules.

conceptDetails.name: lu mechanism
conceptDetails.description: The paper's novel mechanistic explanation for grokking, where training and test losses form L-shaped and U-shaped curves against model weight norm, creating a mismatch that drives delayed generalization.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper attributes the dramatic nature of grokking on algorithmic datasets to representation learning, where the formation of good discrete representations shifts the Goldilocks zone inward and causes grokking.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides mechanistic understanding of grokking by analyzing loss landscapes and weight norm dynamics, explaining data size and weight decay dependence.

conceptDetails.name: weight norm dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The study of how model weight norm evolution under regularization drives the transition from overfitting to generalization, determining whether and how fast grokking occurs.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core analysis examines the geometry of training and test loss landscapes as functions of weight norm, revealing the L and U shapes responsible for grokking dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies the emergence of discrete, structured internal representations (embeddings) in neural networks during the grokking transition, particularly for algorithmic tasks.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
