conceptDetails.description: The dependence of grokking on training data size is systematically studied as part of the LU mechanism framework, explaining why larger datasets reduce or eliminate grokking.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Image classification (MNIST), sentiment analysis (IMDb), and molecular property prediction (QM9) serve as non-algorithmic test domains in which grokking is successfully induced.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The reduced 1D loss landscape construction, obtained by minimizing over angular directions at fixed weight norm, serves as a tractable surrogate for analyzing high-dimensional optimization dynamics.

title: AI-Assisted Discovery of Quantitative and Formal Models in Social Science
abstract: In social science, formal and quantitative models, such as ones describing economic growth and collective action, are used to formulate mechanistic explanations, provide predictions, and uncover questions about observed phenomena. Here, we demonstrate the use of a machine learning system to aid the discovery of symbolic models that capture nonlinear and dynamical relationships in social science datasets. By extending neuro-symbolic methods to find compact functions and differential equations in noisy and longitudinal data, we show that our system can be used to discover interpretable models from real-world data in economics and sociology. Augmenting existing workflows with symbolic regression can help uncover novel relationships and explore counterfactual models during the scientific process. We propose that this AI-assisted framework can bridge parametric and non-parametric models commonly employed in social science research by systematically exploring the space of nonlinear models and enabling fine-grained control over expressivity and interpretability.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: The core method used to discover compact symbolic expressions and differential equations from social science datasets.

conceptDetails.name: neuro-symbolic integration
conceptDetails.description: OccamNet combines neural network optimization with symbolic expression search to perform efficient symbolic regression.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A key goal of the framework, producing human-readable symbolic models rather than black-box predictions.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The system automates the search over the space of symbolic models to uncover novel relationships in social science data.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes an AI-assisted workflow that augments existing social science research processes with symbolic regression.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Complexity regularization is used to find parsimonious models that balance expressivity and simplicity.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression extends traditional regression to discover nonlinear functional forms from data.

conceptDetails.name: longitudinal panel learning
conceptDetails.description: Novel weight-sharing technique to exploit shared structure across panel datasets common in social science.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: Weight-sharing across longitudinal panels enables a single model to be trained on multiple datasets simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: complexity-accuracy tradeoff
conceptDetails.description: The paper maps Pareto fronts of model complexity versus accuracy to help researchers select among competing symbolic models.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Discovered symbolic models serve as interpretable surrogates for complex social phenomena, replacing black-box approaches.

title: Finding NEEMo: Geometric Fitting using Neural Estimation of the Energy Mover's Distance
abstract: A novel neural architecture was recently developed that enforces an exact upper bound on the Lipschitz constant of the model by constraining the norm of its weights in a minimal way, resulting in higher expressiveness compared to other techniques. We present a new and interesting direction for this architecture: estimation of the Wasserstein metric (Earth Mover's Distance) in optimal transport by employing the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality to enable its use in geometric fitting applications. Specifically, we focus on the field of high-energy particle physics, where it has been shown that a metric for the space of particle-collider events can be defined based on the Wasserstein metric, referred to as the Energy Mover's Distance (EMD). This metrization has the potential to revolutionize data-driven collider phenomenology. The work presented here represents a major step towards realizing this goal by providing a differentiable way of directly calculating the EMD. We show how the flexibility that our approach enables can be used to develop novel clustering algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The Wasserstein metric and optimal transport theory form the mathematical foundation of the EMD, which NEEMo estimates via Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality.

conceptDetails.name: energy mover's distance
conceptDetails.description: A Wasserstein-based metric for particle-collider event spaces that NEEMo computes exactly and differentiably, enabling gradient-based geometric fitting.

conceptDetails.name: lipschitz networks
conceptDetails.description: A neural architecture that enforces exact Lipschitz bounds by constraining weight norms, used here to parameterize the Kantorovich potential for EMD estimation.

conceptDetails.name: kantorovich-rubinstein duality
conceptDetails.description: The duality that reformulates the Wasserstein-1 problem as an optimization over 1-Lipschitz functions, which NEEMo exploits for differentiable EMD computation.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The primary HEP application domain, where the EMD connects to infrared-collinear-safe observables and particle clustering into jets.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets data-driven phenomenology of high-energy particle-collision events, with applications at the LHC and future Electron-Ion Collider.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: NEEMo enables novel differentiable clustering algorithms that fit geometric shapes to particle distributions by minimizing the EMD.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The SHAPER-inspired framework uses the EMD to define and fit geometric observables over collider event distributions.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper formulates a minimax optimization objective that jointly optimizes the Kantorovich potential and geometric shape parameters to minimize EMD.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The architecture encodes physical constraints (Lipschitz continuity required by KR duality) directly into network structure rather than as soft regularization.

title: Poisson Flow Generative Models
abstract: We propose a new "Poisson flow" generative model (PFGM) that maps a uniform distribution on a high-dimensional hemisphere into any data distribution. We interpret the data points as electrical charges on the $z=0$ hyperplane in a space augmented with an additional dimension $z$, generating a high-dimensional electric field (the gradient of the solution to Poisson equation). We prove that if these charges flow upward along electric field lines, their initial distribution in the $z=0$ plane transforms into a distribution on the hemisphere of radius $r$ that becomes uniform in the $r \to\infty$ limit. To learn the bijective transformation, we estimate the normalized field in the augmented space. For sampling, we devise a backward ODE that is anchored by the physically meaningful additional dimension: the samples hit the unaugmented data manifold when the $z$ reaches zero. Experimentally, PFGM achieves current state-of-the-art performance among the normalizing flow models on CIFAR-10, with an Inception score of $9.68$ and a FID score of $2.35$. It also performs on par with the state-of-the-art SDE approaches while offering $10\times $ to $20 \times$ acceleration on image generation tasks. Additionally, PFGM appears more tolerant of estimation errors on a weaker network architecture and robust to the step size in the Euler method. The code is available at https://github.com/Newbeeer/poisson_flow .

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: PFGM is a novel generative model that maps a uniform hemisphere distribution to any data distribution via Poisson field dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: poisson field
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept introduced in this paper: the N-dimensional generalization of the electric field derived from the Poisson equation, used as the generative flow.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: PFGM is framed as a continuous normalizing flow, achieving state-of-the-art performance among normalizing flow models on CIFAR-10.

conceptDetails.name: augmented dimension sampling
conceptDetails.description: A novel technique using a physically meaningful extra z-dimension to anchor the backward ODE, improving robustness over time-variable-based approaches.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The model leverages the Poisson equation and N-dimensional electrostatics to define the generative field, grounding the architecture in physical laws.

conceptDetails.name: score-based models
conceptDetails.description: PFGM is compared extensively to score-based SDE methods, matching their quality while offering 10-20x sampling acceleration.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The forward and backward ODEs induced by the Poisson electric field define the probabilistic flow process central to PFGM.

conceptDetails.name: diffusion models
conceptDetails.description: PFGM is positioned as an alternative to diffusion/SDE-based generative models, sharing iterative sampling but using a physically-grounded ODE.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: PFGM supports likelihood evaluation via its invertible ODE, enabling exact density estimation on the data manifold.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The backward ODE solves the inverse problem of recovering data-distributed samples from a simple uniform hemisphere distribution.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The bijective mapping between the hemisphere and data distribution is conceptually related to optimal transport between probability measures.

title: Discovering Conservation Laws using Optimal Transport and Manifold Learning
abstract: Conservation laws are key theoretical and practical tools for understanding, characterizing, and modeling nonlinear dynamical systems. However, for many complex systems, the corresponding conserved quantities are difficult to identify, making it hard to analyze their dynamics and build stable predictive models. Current approaches for discovering conservation laws often depend on detailed dynamical information or rely on black box parametric deep learning methods. We instead reformulate this task as a manifold learning problem and propose a non-parametric approach for discovering conserved quantities. We test this new approach on a variety of physical systems and demonstrate that our method is able to both identify the number of conserved quantities and extract their values. Using tools from optimal transport theory and manifold learning, our proposed method provides a direct geometric approach to identifying conservation laws that is both robust and interpretable without requiring an explicit model of the system nor accurate time information.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper reformulates conservation law discovery as a manifold learning problem, using diffusion maps to embed the space of phase space isosurfaces.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The 2-Wasserstein distance from optimal transport theory is used to compute distances between pairs of sampled phase space isosurfaces.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper — the method discovers and extracts conserved quantities from trajectory data of dynamical systems.

conceptDetails.name: phase space isosurface embedding
conceptDetails.description: Novel technique: embedding the manifold of phase space isosurfaces to directly parameterize the space of conserved quantities without explicit models.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion maps reduce the high-dimensional space of isosurfaces to a low-dimensional embedding that parameterizes conserved quantities.

conceptDetails.name: wasserstein geometry
conceptDetails.description: Novel use of the 2-Wasserstein metric as a shape distance between trajectory distributions to capture geometric variations in conserved quantities.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Hamiltonian systems (pendulum, gravitational dynamics) serve as primary test cases where the method identifies energy and angular momentum as conserved quantities.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion maps, a spectral embedding technique, are central to extracting the low-dimensional manifold of conserved quantities.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Discovering conservation laws from trajectory data without an explicit model is framed as an inverse problem in the scientific discovery context.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The non-parametric geometric approach provides interpretable results compared to black-box deep learning alternatives.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Diffusion maps rely on a Markov chain construction over the trajectory data to define the geometric embedding.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The method enables building stable predictive models for complex nonlinear dynamics by encoding conservation constraints.

title: Inferring subhalo effective density slopes from strong lensing observations with neural likelihood-ratio estimation
abstract: Strong gravitational lensing has emerged as a promising approach for probing dark matter models on sub-galactic scales. Recent work has proposed the subhalo effective density slope as a more reliable observable than the commonly used subhalo mass function. The subhalo effective density slope is a measurement independent of assumptions about the underlying density profile and can be inferred for individual subhalos through traditional sampling methods. To go beyond individual subhalo measurements, we leverage recent advances in machine learning and introduce a neural likelihood-ratio estimator to infer an effective density slope for populations of subhalos. We demonstrate that our method is capable of harnessing the statistical power of multiple subhalos (within and across multiple images) to distinguish between characteristics of different subhalo populations. The computational efficiency warranted by the neural likelihood-ratio estimator over traditional sampling enables statistical studies of dark matter perturbers and is particularly useful as we expect an influx of strong lensing systems from upcoming surveys.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper probes dark matter models on sub-galactic scales by inferring density slope properties of dark matter subhalos through strong lensing observations.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: A neural likelihood-ratio estimator is the core machine learning contribution, replacing traditional sampling to infer population-level subhalo density slopes.

conceptDetails.name: subhalo effective density slope
conceptDetails.description: A profile-independent observable measuring the slope of a subhalo's density at its most lensing-effective scale, introduced as a robust dark matter discriminator.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The method uses simulation-based inference to implicitly marginalize over the high-dimensional latent space of individual subhalo parameters without explicit likelihood computation.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational lensing
conceptDetails.description: Strong gravitational lensing systems where both source and lens are galaxies provide the observational data used to constrain dark matter substructure properties.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The neural network estimates posterior distributions over the effective density slope parameter for populations of subhalos given observed lensing images.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring latent subhalo density structure from projected gravitational lensing images is a canonical inverse problem addressed with ML tools.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian inference provides the theoretical framework for combining subhalo population likelihoods across multiple lensing systems to constrain population-level parameters.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional neural networks process 100×100 pixel lensing images to extract features relevant to subhalo density slope estimation.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The neural network implicitly performs density estimation by learning the likelihood ratio between different subhalo population hypotheses from simulated data.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Traditional MCMC sampling is used as a baseline for individual subhalo inference, against which the computational efficiency of the neural estimator is compared.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Simulated strong lensing images generated with lenstronomy serve as training and validation data for the neural likelihood-ratio estimator.

title: Uncovering dark matter density profiles in dwarf galaxies with graph neural networks
abstract: Dwarf galaxies are small, dark matter-dominated galaxies, some of which are embedded within the Milky Way. Their lack of baryonic matter (e.g., stars and gas) makes them perfect test beds for probing the properties of dark matter -- understanding the spatial dark matter distribution in these systems can be used to constrain microphysical dark matter interactions that influence the formation and evolution of structures in our Universe. We introduce a new method that leverages simulation-based inference and graph-based machine learning in order to infer the dark matter density profiles of dwarf galaxies from observable kinematics of stars gravitationally bound to these systems. Our approach aims to address some of the limitations of established methods based on dynamical Jeans modeling. We show that this novel method can place stronger constraints on dark matter profiles and, consequently, has the potential to weigh in on some of the ongoing puzzles associated with the small-scale structure of dark matter halos, such as the core-cusp discrepancy.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Used as the core feature extractor to process stellar kinematic data represented as graphs, replacing summary statistics required by traditional Jeans modeling.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The central inferential framework: forward-simulated dwarf galaxy realizations train the model to infer dark matter density profile parameters without an explicit likelihood.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The primary scientific target; the paper infers spatial dark matter density profiles in dwarf galaxies to constrain microphysical dark matter properties and probe small-scale structure.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method produces posterior distributions over dark matter halo parameters (density normalization, scale radius, central slope) from observed stellar kinematics.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Neural density estimators are used within the SBI pipeline to approximate the posterior over dark matter and stellar profile parameters.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring latent dark matter density profiles from indirect observables (stellar line-of-sight velocities and positions) is the fundamental inverse problem addressed.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Used as the density estimator in the neural posterior estimation step to learn the mapping from compressed GNN features to posterior distributions over profile parameters.

conceptDetails.name: core-cusp problem
conceptDetails.description: The key astrophysical puzzle motivating this work — whether inner dark matter halo density profiles are cuspy (NFW) or cored — that the method is designed to help resolve.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The GNN learns to extract informative, permutation-invariant summary statistics from variable-size stellar kinematic datasets for downstream inference.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The full Bayesian posterior over dark matter profile parameters is estimated, enabling principled uncertainty quantification and comparison with Jeans modeling posteriors.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Forward-simulated dwarf galaxy stellar kinematics using the StarSampler code serve as the training dataset, encoding ΛCDM structure formation assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Importance sampling is used in the StarSampler forward model to generate phase-space realizations of stellar kinematics from a given dark matter potential.

title: Stable Object Reorientation using Contact Plane Registration
abstract: We present a system for accurately predicting stable orientations for diverse rigid objects. We propose to overcome the critical issue of modelling multimodality in the space of rotations by using a conditional generative model to accurately classify contact surfaces. Our system is capable of operating from noisy and partially-observed pointcloud observations captured by real world depth cameras. Our method substantially outperforms the current state-of-the-art systems on a simulated stacking task requiring highly accurate rotations, and demonstrates strong sim2real zero-shot transfer results across a variety of unseen objects on a real world reorientation task. Project website: \url{https://richardrl.github.io/stable-reorientation/}

conceptDetails.name: contact plane registration
conceptDetails.description: The core novel method that reformulates rotation prediction as contact surface classification followed by RANSAC-based plane fitting to determine stable object orientations.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A CVAE (conditional variational autoencoder) is used to handle multimodality in contact mask prediction, biasing predictions toward a single contact region rather than blurry averages.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Conditional generative modeling is the proposed solution to the multimodality problem in SO(3) rotation prediction for object reorientation.

conceptDetails.name: rotation multimodality
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly addresses three sources of multimodality in rotation prediction: representational discontinuity, object symmetry, and equivalent optimal rewards.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The method operates over point clouds in 3D space and predicts rotations in SO(3), requiring geometry-aware neural network design.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Per-point binary contact classification is the central prediction task, determining which surface points will be in contact with the table in a stable pose.

conceptDetails.name: sim2real transfer
conceptDetails.description: The system demonstrates zero-shot transfer of models trained in simulation to real-world unseen objects using commodity depth cameras.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Zero-shot generalization to novel unseen object shapes at inference time is a key evaluation criterion, enabled by the local-geometry-based contact formulation.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: RANSAC (random sample and consensus) is used for robust plane fitting to the predicted contact points, tolerating classifier noise.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The system is designed to be robust to noisy and partially-observed point cloud inputs from real-world depth sensors.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A weighted binary cross-entropy loss with a tunable precision-recall trade-off parameter r is designed to handle the imbalance between contact and non-contact points.

title: Neural Embedding: Learning the Embedding of the Manifold of Physics Data
abstract: In this paper, we present a method of embedding physics data manifolds with metric structure into lower dimensional spaces with simpler metrics, such as Euclidean and Hyperbolic spaces. We then demonstrate that it can be a powerful step in the data analysis pipeline for many applications. Using progressively more realistic simulated collisions at the Large Hadron Collider, we show that this embedding approach learns the underlying latent structure. With the notion of volume in Euclidean spaces, we provide for the first time a viable solution to quantifying the true search capability of model agnostic search algorithms in collider physics (i.e. anomaly detection). Finally, we discuss how the ideas presented in this paper can be employed to solve many practical challenges that require the extraction of physically meaningful representations from information in complex high dimensional datasets.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is learning neural embedding functions that map high-dimensional physics data manifolds into low-dimensional Euclidean or hyperbolic spaces while preserving metric structure.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames physics datasets as lying on low-dimensional manifolds within high-dimensional ambient spaces and learns these manifold structures explicitly.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The method compresses high-dimensional collider event data (e.g., R^150) down to as few as two dimensions while retaining physically meaningful structure.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: A primary application is quantifying model-agnostic anomaly detection algorithms at the LHC using the volume of the embedded space as a search capability metric.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: Energy Mover's Distance and Hellinger distance, both grounded in optimal transport, are used as the target metrics that the neural embedding approximates.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The method is demonstrated on hadronic jet datasets including toy jets and realistic LHC simulations with up to 4-prong resonance decays.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets the LHC experimental setting, addressing challenges of high-dimensional event representation, trigger systems, and new physics searches.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The embedding framework enables model-agnostic searches by quantifying the search space coverage of various anomaly detection algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The neural embedding learns physically meaningful representations that self-organize events into distinct kinematic categories.

conceptDetails.name: volume-adjusted roc
conceptDetails.description: A novel evaluation metric proposed in the paper that uses the embedded-space volume to quantify the breadth of the search space covered by an anomaly detection algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: hyperbolic embedding
conceptDetails.description: The paper explores embedding jets into hyperbolic space to better capture hierarchical and tree-like structure in QCD jet substructure.

conceptDetails.name: metric distortion
conceptDetails.description: Empirical distortion of the learned embedding relative to the original optimal transport metric is measured to validate embedding fidelity.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNN architectures are employed as one of the neural network backends for learning the embedding function from jet image inputs.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Transformer architectures are used as an alternative network backbone for embedding particle-level jet representations.

title: Confinement in non-Abelian lattice gauge theory via persistent homology
abstract: We investigate the structure of confining and deconfining phases in SU(2) lattice gauge theory via persistent homology, which gives us access to the topology of a hierarchy of combinatorial objects constructed from given data. Specifically, we use filtrations by traced Polyakov loops, topological densities, holonomy Lie algebra fields, as well as electric and magnetic fields. This allows for a comprehensive picture of confinement. In particular, topological densities form spatial lumps which show signatures of the classical probability distribution of instanton-dyons. Signatures of well-separated dyons located at random positions are encoded in holonomy Lie algebra fields, following the semi-classical temperature dependence of the instanton appearance probability. Debye screening discriminating between electric and magnetic fields is visible in persistent homology and pronounced at large gauge coupling. All employed constructions are gauge-invariant without a priori assumptions on the configurations under study. This work showcases the versatility of persistent homology for statistical and quantum physics studies, barely explored to date.

conceptDetails.name: persistent homology
conceptDetails.description: The central computational method used to extract topological features from gauge field configurations across filtration hierarchies, enabling gauge-invariant analysis of confinement phases.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The physical framework — SU(2) pure gauge theory on a 4D Euclidean lattice — within which all confinement and deconfinement analysis is performed.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The confinement-deconfinement phase transition is the primary physical phenomenon under investigation, probed through topological observables.

conceptDetails.name: instanton-dyons
conceptDetails.description: Semi-classical topological objects (instanton constituents at finite temperature) whose spatial distribution and probability are directly detected via persistent homology filtrations.

conceptDetails.name: topology-based observables
conceptDetails.description: Cubical complex filtrations of gauge-invariant fields (Polyakov loops, topological densities, holonomy algebra norms) serve as novel topological order parameters for phase structure.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The Hybrid/Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) algorithm is used to generate gauge field configurations distributed according to the Wilson action.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The broader theoretical context, encompassing non-perturbative QFT phenomena such as topological tunneling, instantons, and confinement mechanisms in QCD-like theories.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Center symmetry breaking is a key diagnostic of the deconfinement transition, and the paper probes how persistent homology captures its signatures across Polyakov loop observables.

conceptDetails.name: debye screening
conceptDetails.description: The discrimination between electric and magnetic field spatial structures due to Debye screening is identified as a visible signature in persistent homology at large gauge coupling.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Statistical mechanics and thermal field theory underpin the finite-temperature analysis, including probabilistic predictions for instanton appearance rates.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Lattice renormalization group arguments are used to relate the inverse coupling β to physical temperature near the critical point.

title: Sampling QCD field configurations with gauge-equivariant flow models
abstract: Machine learning methods based on normalizing flows have been shown to address important challenges, such as critical slowing-down and topological freezing, in the sampling of gauge field configurations in simple lattice field theories. A critical question is whether this success will translate to studies of QCD. This Proceedings presents a status update on advances in this area. In particular, it is illustrated how recently developed algorithmic components may be combined to construct flow-based sampling algorithms for QCD in four dimensions. The prospects and challenges for future use of this approach in at-scale applications are summarized.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core generative modeling technique used to learn diffeomorphic transformations that map a simple prior distribution to the target QCD gauge field distribution.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary physical theory studied, with flow models applied to sample gauge field configurations for QCD with dynamical fermions in four dimensions.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The broader theoretical framework within which the flow-based sampling algorithms are constructed, covering SU(3) gauge fields on a discrete spacetime lattice.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Gauge-equivariant architectures are central to the paper's construction, ensuring the flow transformations respect the SU(3) gauge symmetry of QCD.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Preserving gauge symmetry in the flow architecture is identified as critical for practical success in sampling QCD configurations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The independence Metropolis algorithm is used to convert approximate flow samples into exact samples from the target distribution.

conceptDetails.name: topological freezing
conceptDetails.description: A key sampling pathology in lattice QCD that flow-based methods are designed to overcome by generating statistically independent configurations.

conceptDetails.name: critical slowing-down
conceptDetails.description: The computational bottleneck near phase transitions in standard MCMC methods that normalizing flows can mitigate by learning the target distribution directly.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The flow model learns to approximate the Boltzmann distribution defined by the QCD Euclidean action, enabling efficient sampling.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: SU(3) group structure is fundamental to the gauge-equivariant coupling layers and the construction of the flow over link variables.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Spatially separated 4D convolutional neural networks parametrize the spline coupling layers within the gauge-equivariant flow architecture.

conceptDetails.name: pseudofermion sampling
conceptDetails.description: A novel conditional flow submodel is used to jointly sample pseudofermion and gauge degrees of freedom, enabling the inclusion of dynamical fermions in QCD.

title: Robust Clustering of the Local Milky Way Stellar Kinematic Substructures with Gaia eDR3
abstract: We apply the clustering algorithm HDBSCAN on the Gaia early third data release astrometry combined with the Gaia second data release radial velocity measurements of almost 5.5 million stars to identify the local stellar kinematic substructures in the solar neighborhood. Understanding these structures helps build a more complete picture of the formation of the Milky Way, as well as an empirical phase space distribution of dark matter that would inform detection experiments. The main goal of this study is to provide a list of the most stable clusters, by taking into account the measurement uncertainties and studying the stability of the clustering results. We apply the clustering algorithm in two spaces, in velocity space in order to study recently accreted structures, and in action-angle space to find phase-mixed structures. We find 23 (6) robust clusters in velocity space (action-angle space) that are consistently not associated with noise. They are attributed to the known structures: the Gaia Sausage-Enceladus, the Helmi Stream, and globular cluster NGC 3201 are found in both spaces, while NGC 104 and the thick disk (Sequoia) are identified in velocity space (action-angle space). We discuss the kinematic properties of these structures and study whether many of the small clusters belong to a similar larger cluster based on their chemical abundances. Although we do not identify any new structures, we find that the HDBSCAN member selection of already known structures is unstable to input kinematics of the stars when resampled within their uncertainties. We therefore present the most stable subset of local kinematic structures, which are consistently identified by the clustering algorithm, and emphasize the need to take into account error propagation during both the manual and automated identification of stellar structures, both for existing ones as well as future discoveries. (abridged)

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: HDBSCAN clustering algorithm is the core computational method applied to identify kinematic substructures in velocity and action-angle spaces.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Assessing the stability of cluster membership under resampling within measurement uncertainties is the paper's central methodological contribution.

conceptDetails.name: stellar streams
conceptDetails.description: Spatially or kinematically coherent debris from tidally disrupted accreted galaxies (e.g., Gaia Sausage-Enceladus, Helmi Stream) are the primary astrophysical targets.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically evaluates robustness of cluster assignments to input kinematic uncertainties, presenting only the most stable subset of structures.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Identifying accreted stellar structures provides an empirical phase-space distribution of dark matter that informs direct detection experiments.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Stars are resampled within their astrometric uncertainties using Monte Carlo techniques to test the stability of HDBSCAN cluster assignments.

conceptDetails.name: action-angle coordinates
conceptDetails.description: Action-angle space is used as an alternative clustering domain to identify phase-mixed structures that are dispersed in velocity space.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Phase-space overdensity identification underlies the detection of accreted stellar populations and their use as dark matter tracers.

conceptDetails.name: galactic archaeology
conceptDetails.description: The broader scientific goal of reconstructing Milky Way merger history through chemical and kinematic signatures of surviving accreted stellar populations.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Identifying kinematic substructures against the dominant in-situ thin-disk background is framed as detecting overdensities relative to a noise distribution.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Six-dimensional phase space is projected into velocity space and action-angle space to isolate recently accreted versus phase-mixed structures.

title: Characterizing the Expected Behavior of Non-Poissonian Template Fitting
abstract: We have performed a systematic study of the statistical behavior of non-Poissonian template fitting (NPTF), a method designed to analyze and characterize unresolved point sources in general counts datasets. In this paper, we focus on the properties and characteristics of the Fermi-LAT gamma-ray data set. In particular, we have simulated and analyzed gamma-ray sky maps under varying conditions of exposure, angular resolution, pixel size, energy window, event selection, and source brightness. We describe how these conditions affect the sensitivity of NPTF to the presence of point sources, for inner-galaxy studies of point sources within the Galactic Center excess, and for the simplified case of isotropic emission. We do not find opportunities for major gains in sensitivity from varying these choices, within the range available with current Fermi-LAT data. We provide an analytic estimate of the NPTF sensitivity to point sources for the case of isotropic emission and perfect angular resolution, and find good agreement with our numerical results for that case.

conceptDetails.name: non-poissonian statistics
conceptDetails.description: NPTF exploits non-Poissonian fluctuations in photon pixel counts to identify unresolved point source populations.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: NPTF is fundamentally a signal detection method for identifying unresolved point source populations in gamma-ray data.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The test statistic used to quantify NPTF sensitivity to faint point sources is based on the likelihood ratio.

conceptDetails.name: source count function
conceptDetails.description: The source count function parameterizes the brightness distribution of unresolved point sources in NPTF analyses.

conceptDetails.name: point spread function
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically varies the PSF (angular resolution) as a key parameter affecting NPTF sensitivity.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper investigates whether the Galactic Center Excess originates from dark matter annihilation, motivating the NPTF sensitivity studies.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Simulated gamma-ray sky maps are generated via Monte Carlo to systematically study NPTF performance under varying conditions.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: NPTF uses Bayesian posterior estimation to characterize point source populations and their source count functions.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames NPTF sensitivity as a hypothesis testing problem, distinguishing point source from diffuse emission models.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: NPTFit-Sim is used to simulate unresolved point source populations for systematic inference studies.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring the properties of unresolved point source populations from aggregate photon count statistics is an inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: NPTF methods are noted to have been applied to high-energy neutrino datasets, extending beyond gamma-ray astrophysics.

title: Reconstructing Cosmological Initial Conditions from Late-Time Structure with Convolutional Neural Networks
abstract: We present a method to reconstruct the initial linear-regime matter density field from the late-time non-linearly evolved density field in which we channel the output of standard first-order reconstruction to a convolutional neural network (CNN). Our method shows dramatic improvement over the reconstruction of either component alone. We show why CNNs are not well-suited for reconstructing the initial density directly from the late-time density: CNNs are local models, but the relationship between initial and late-time density is not local. Our method leverages standard reconstruction as a preprocessing step, which inverts bulk gravitational flows sourced over very large scales, transforming the residual reconstruction problem from long-range to local and making it ideally suited for a CNN. We develop additional techniques to account for redshift distortions, which warp the density fields measured by galaxy surveys. Our method improves the range of scales of high-fidelity reconstruction by a factor of 2 in wavenumber above standard reconstruction, corresponding to a factor of 8 increase in the number of well-reconstructed modes. In addition, our method almost completely eliminates the anisotropy caused by redshift distortions. As galaxy surveys continue to map the Universe in increasingly greater detail, our results demonstrate the opportunity offered by CNNs to untangle the non-linear clustering at intermediate scales more accurately than ever before.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNNs are the core ML architecture used to refine the residual reconstruction problem after standard first-order reconstruction preprocessing, exploiting their locality for the transformed local residuals.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the initial linear-regime matter density field from the late-time non-linearly evolved density field is the central inverse problem addressed in this work.

conceptDetails.name: density field reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The compound pipeline of standard first-order reconstruction followed by CNN refinement constitutes a novel two-stage density field reconstruction scheme that outperforms either stage alone.

conceptDetails.name: baryon acoustic oscillations
conceptDetails.description: The BAO feature in the matter correlation function is the primary cosmological signal motivating higher-fidelity reconstruction to improve standard-ruler distance measurements.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: N-body simulations provide paired initial and late-time density fields used to train and evaluate the CNN reconstruction method.

conceptDetails.name: redshift-space distortions
conceptDetails.description: Systematic warping of the observed density field due to galaxy peculiar velocities, which the method nearly eliminates through dedicated correction techniques applied within the CNN pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The CNN performs a voxel-level regression task, mapping the post-standard-reconstruction density field to the residual corrections needed to recover the initial linear density.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: BAO reconstruction improvements directly benefit dark energy surveys by sharpening the standard-ruler measurement used to map the Universe's expansion history.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The method recovers high-fidelity initial density modes at a factor of 2 higher wavenumber than standard reconstruction, effectively extending usable resolution in the reconstructed field.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The matter density field being reconstructed is dominated by dark matter, with galaxies serving only as biased tracers of the underlying dark matter distribution.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: Cited as a related reconstruction approach that casts the inverse gravity problem as an optimal transport problem, providing algorithmic context for the method developed here.

title: Bounding generalization error with input compression: An empirical study with infinite-width networks
abstract: Estimating the Generalization Error (GE) of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) is an important task that often relies on availability of held-out data. The ability to better predict GE based on a single training set may yield overarching DNN design principles to reduce a reliance on trial-and-error, along with other performance assessment advantages. In search of a quantity relevant to GE, we investigate the Mutual Information (MI) between the input and final layer representations, using the infinite-width DNN limit to bound MI. An existing input compression-based GE bound is used to link MI and GE. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first empirical study of this bound. In our attempt to empirically falsify the theoretical bound, we find that it is often tight for best-performing models. Furthermore, it detects randomization of training labels in many cases, reflects test-time perturbation robustness, and works well given only few training samples. These results are promising given that input compression is broadly applicable where MI can be estimated with confidence.

conceptDetails.name: input compression bound
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution: an information-theoretic bound linking mutual information between input and final-layer representations to generalization error, empirically studied for the first time.

conceptDetails.name: mutual information estimation
conceptDetails.description: InfoNCE variational upper and lower bounds on mutual information between input and network representations, computed without variational parameters via the Gaussian structure of infinite-width networks.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) and Neural Network Gaussian Process (NNGP) kernels underpin the infinite-width network framework used to compute tractable mutual information bounds.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Bounding generalization error from the training set alone serves as a principled uncertainty estimate for model performance without held-out data.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies input-to-final-layer representations and their information content as a proxy for how well a model generalizes.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Infinite ensembles of infinite-width networks provide a tractable, well-defined model class with analytic Gaussian predictive distributions used throughout the empirical study.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: ICB is shown to be predictive of test-time perturbation robustness, connecting information compression to adversarial and distributional robustness without requiring a differentiable model.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The NNGP kernel yields a Gaussian predictive distribution, giving the infinite-width network a Bayesian interpretation that enables closed-form MI computation.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Training-label randomization tests and perturbation robustness checks are used to validate when the ICB reliably predicts generalization without access to a test set.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classification tasks on multiple datasets serve as the experimental testbed for evaluating whether the ICB is non-vacuous and correlates with empirical generalization error.

title: Gauge-equivariant flow models for sampling in lattice field theories with pseudofermions
abstract: This work presents gauge-equivariant architectures for flow-based sampling in fermionic lattice field theories using pseudofermions as stochastic estimators for the fermionic determinant. This is the default approach in state-of-the-art lattice field theory calculations, making this development critical to the practical application of flow models to theories such as QCD. Methods by which flow-based sampling approaches can be improved via standard techniques such as even/odd preconditioning and the Hasenbusch factorization are also outlined. Numerical demonstrations in two-dimensional U(1) and SU(3) gauge theories with $N_f=2$ flavors of fermions are provided.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core methodology: gauge-equivariant flow models are trained to sample lattice field configurations by learning a bijective transformation from a simple base distribution to the target lattice distribution.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The physical framework studied, including 2D U(1) and SU(3) gauge theories with fermions, for which the flow-based sampling architectures are developed.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Gauge-equivariant architectures are the central technical contribution, ensuring that flow models respect the gauge symmetry of the underlying physical theory.

conceptDetails.name: pseudofermion sampling
conceptDetails.description: Novel application of auxiliary bosonic fields as stochastic determinant estimators within flow-based sampling architectures, enabling scalable treatment of fermionic theories.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary target application motivating the work; the pseudofermion approach developed here is essential for making flow-based sampling feasible in full QCD.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge symmetry is explicitly preserved throughout the flow architecture design, including in the new parallel transporter convolutional network for pseudofermionic degrees of freedom.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Flow-based generative models are used to directly sample from the target lattice distribution, replacing or augmenting traditional Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods.

conceptDetails.name: parallel transporter convolution
conceptDetails.description: A newly introduced gauge-covariant convolutional architecture that transports fermionic fields along gauge links, enabling equivariant modeling of pseudofermion degrees of freedom.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) serves as the comparison baseline, and the flow models are designed to address known HMC pathologies such as critical slowing-down and topological freezing.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Flow models must estimate the target probability density (including the fermionic determinant contribution) to compute the acceptance probability in the Metropolis-Hastings correction step.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The gauge groups U(1) and SU(3) govern the structure of the lattice variables and constrain the allowable neural network architectures via equivariance requirements.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a parallel transporter convolutional network as the key architectural building block for modeling pseudofermionic degrees of freedom on the lattice.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Pseudofermions function as stochastic estimators for the fermionic determinant, replacing an exact but computationally intractable quantity with a tractable stochastic approximation.

conceptDetails.name: hasenbusch factorization
conceptDetails.description: A standard lattice QCD preconditioning technique integrated into the flow-based sampling framework to improve model quality and sampling efficiency.

title: Modeling early-universe energy injection with Dense Neural Networks
abstract: We show that Dense Neural Networks can be used to accurately model the cooling of high-energy particles in the early universe, in the context of the public code package DarkHistory. DarkHistory self-consistently computes the temperature and ionization history of the early universe in the presence of exotic energy injections, such as might arise from the annihilation or decay of dark matter. The original version of DarkHistory uses large pre-computed transfer function tables to evolve photon and electron spectra in redshift steps, which require a significant amount of memory and storage space. We present a light version of DarkHistory that makes use of simple Dense Neural Networks to store and interpolate the transfer functions, which performs well on small computers without heavy memory or storage usage. This method anticipates future expansion with additional parametric dependence in the transfer functions without requiring exponentially larger data tables.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Dense Neural Networks are used as surrogate models to replace large pre-computed transfer function tables, enabling lightweight emulation of particle cascade physics.

conceptDetails.name: transfer function interpolation
conceptDetails.description: DNNs automatically interpolate transfer function matrices across arbitrary physical parameter values, replacing grid-based table interpolation.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper models exotic energy injections from dark matter annihilation or decay as the primary astrophysical motivation for the DarkHistory framework.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: The DNN-based transfer functions emulate the behavior of the original tabular transfer functions with comparable accuracy at a fraction of the memory cost.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central motivation for replacing tables with DNNs is improved scalability when additional physical parameters are added, avoiding exponential growth in data size.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The DNNs perform regression to predict transfer function values as a function of energy and physical parameters such as redshift and ionization fractions.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: DarkHistory simulates the temperature and ionization history of the early universe, which is the cosmological context in which the DNNs are deployed.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: CMB anisotropies are a key observable that constrains the exotic energy injections modeled by DarkHistory.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Custom loss functions and training strategies are employed to achieve accurate DNN fits to the transfer functions across multiple energy and parameter regimes.

conceptDetails.name: neural operators
conceptDetails.description: The DNN transfer functions act as learned operators mapping input particle spectra to output spectra across redshift steps, analogous to neural operator frameworks.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The particle cascade physics modeled by DarkHistory operates within the framework of Standard Model effective interactions in the early universe.

title: Deep Learning and Symbolic Regression for Discovering Parametric Equations
abstract: Symbolic regression is a machine learning technique that can learn the governing formulas of data and thus has the potential to transform scientific discovery. However, symbolic regression is still limited in the complexity and dimensionality of the systems that it can analyze. Deep learning on the other hand has transformed machine learning in its ability to analyze extremely complex and high-dimensional datasets. We propose a neural network architecture to extend symbolic regression to parametric systems where some coefficient may vary but the structure of the underlying governing equation remains constant. We demonstrate our method on various analytic expressions, ODEs, and PDEs with varying coefficients and show that it extrapolates well outside of the training domain. The neural network-based architecture can also integrate with other deep learning architectures so that it can analyze high-dimensional data while being trained end-to-end. To this end we integrate our architecture with convolutional neural networks to analyze 1D images of varying spring systems.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: The core technique of the paper — learning interpretable mathematical equations directly from data using a neural network architecture that replaces activation functions with symbolic primitives.

conceptDetails.name: parametric equation learning
conceptDetails.description: The novel contribution of this paper: extending symbolic regression to systems where equation structure is fixed but coefficients vary, via the SEQL and HEQL architectures.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A primary motivation for symbolic regression — producing closed-form, human-readable mathematical expressions rather than black-box models.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: L1 regularization is applied to the EQL network weights to encourage sparsity and recover compact, interpretable governing equations.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames symbolic regression as a tool to automate scientific discovery of governing equations across physical systems.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Discovering governing equations from observations of system behavior is an inverse problem central to the paper's methodology.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional encoder is integrated end-to-end with the EQL network to perform symbolic regression on high-dimensional 1D image data of spring systems.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression and coefficient recovery from data are the fundamental learning tasks addressed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: hypernetwork
conceptDetails.description: The HEQL architecture uses a hypernetwork that takes the varying parameter as input and outputs the weights of the inner EQL network to model parametric equations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A key design goal is scaling symbolic regression to high-dimensional datasets and complex parametric systems via end-to-end deep learning integration.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The method is applied to discover PDEs with spatially varying coefficients (e.g., Schrödinger, Maxwell equations), situating it within the physics-informed ML landscape.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The proposed architecture is positioned as a general tool for scientific equation discovery workflows, demonstrated on ODEs, PDEs, and image-based physical systems.

title: Strong Lensing Source Reconstruction Using Continuous Neural Fields
abstract: From the nature of dark matter to the rate of expansion of our Universe, observations of distant galaxies distorted through strong gravitational lensing have the potential to answer some of the major open questions in astrophysics. Modeling galaxy-galaxy strong lensing observations presents a number of challenges as the exact configuration of both the background source and foreground lens galaxy is unknown. A timely call, prompted by a number of upcoming surveys anticipating high-resolution lensing images, demands methods that can efficiently model lenses at their full complexity. In this work, we introduce a method that uses continuous neural fields to non-parametrically reconstruct the complex morphology of a source galaxy while simultaneously inferring a distribution over foreground lens galaxy configurations. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method through experiments on simulated data targeting high-resolution lensing images similar to those anticipated in near-future astrophysical surveys.

conceptDetails.name: continuous neural fields
conceptDetails.description: A coordinate-based neural network serves as the core non-parametric representation of the source-light distribution, mapping source-plane coordinates to intensity and variance.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational lensing
conceptDetails.description: Galaxy-galaxy strong gravitational lensing is the physical phenomenon being modeled, where a foreground lens galaxy distorts images of a background source galaxy.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the source galaxy morphology and lens mass distribution from the observed lensed image is the central inverse problem addressed.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method jointly infers posterior distributions over both the lens model parameters and the source light profile using variational inference.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: A probabilistic Bayesian framework underpins the joint recovery of source and lens configurations with calibrated uncertainty.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: A differentiable lensing forward simulator implemented in JAX is used as the generative model within the inference pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable rendering
conceptDetails.description: A differentiable lensing renderer enables end-to-end gradient-based optimization of the neural source representation and variational lens posterior simultaneously.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Full posterior distributions over lens parameters and per-pixel source uncertainty are recovered, enabling principled uncertainty propagation to downstream analyses.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The singular isothermal ellipsoid lensing equations and the lens equation are embedded directly in the differentiable forward model constraining the neural source field.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Constraining the abundance and properties of dark matter substructures is a primary science driver motivating improved strong lensing source reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The continuous neural field representation enables recovery of high-resolution source galaxy images from lensed observations at the pixel level.

title: The Dark Energy Camera Plane Survey 2 (DECaPS2): More Sky, Less Bias, and Better Uncertainties
abstract: Deep optical and near-infrared imaging of the entire Galactic plane is essential for understanding our Galaxy's stars, gas, and dust. The second data release of the DECam Plane Survey (DECaPS2) extends the five-band optical and near-infrared survey of the southern Galactic plane to cover $6.5\%$ of the sky, |b| < 10° and 6° > l > -124°, complementary to coverage by Pan-STARRS1. Typical single-exposure effective depths, including crowding effects and other complications, are 23.5, 22.6, 22.1, 21.6, and 20.8 mag in $g$, $r$, $i$, $z$, and $Y$ bands, respectively, with around 1 arcsecond seeing. The survey comprises 3.32 billion objects built from 34 billion detections in 21.4 thousand exposures, totaling 260 hours open shutter time on the Dark Energy Camera (DECam) at Cerro Tololo. The data reduction pipeline features several improvements, including the addition of synthetic source injection tests to validate photometric solutions across the entire survey footprint. A convenient functional form for the detection bias in the faint limit was derived and leveraged to characterize the photometric pipeline performance. A new post-processing technique was applied to every detection to de-bias and improve uncertainty estimates of the flux in the presence of structured backgrounds, specifically targeting nebulosity. The images and source catalogs are publicly available at http://decaps.skymaps.info/.

conceptDetails.name: photometric crowded-field deblending
conceptDetails.description: The crowdsource algorithm simultaneously models overlapping stellar PSFs in dense Galactic plane images to accurately recover individual source fluxes and positions.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops a new post-processing technique (CloudCovErr.jl) to de-bias and improve uncertainty estimates of stellar flux in the presence of structured nebulous backgrounds.

conceptDetails.name: structured background estimation
conceptDetails.description: CloudCovErr.jl estimates spatially correlated noise from nebulosity and structured backgrounds to correct flux biases and uncertainty underestimates for individual sources.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Photometric calibration across the survey footprint is validated using synthetic source injection tests and cross-matching with external catalogs.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: Synthetic source injection tests are used throughout to validate photometric pipeline performance, characterize detection biases, and assess blending effects.

conceptDetails.name: detection bias modeling
conceptDetails.description: A functional form for the faint-limit detection bias is derived and used to characterize completeness and flux bias across the survey's photometric depth.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The end-to-end DECaPS2 data reduction pipeline integrates community pipelines, source detection, photometric calibration, and catalog construction into a scalable automated workflow.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Source deblending and flux extraction from crowded, overlapping stellar images represent a classic inverse problem of recovering source parameters from observed pixel data.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The survey enables study of stellar populations across the Galactic plane, with color-magnitude diagrams used to validate photometric quality in diverse stellar environments.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The pipeline characterizes and flags sources affected by nebulosity, crowding, and other structured background contamination that bias photometric measurements.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The crowdsource pipeline resolves individual stellar sources in highly crowded Galactic plane fields, effectively achieving sub-arcsecond source separation.

title: A Stimulating Explanation of the Extragalactic Radio Background
abstract: Despite an intense theoretical and experimental effort over the past decade, observations of the extragalactic radio background at multiple frequencies below 10 GHz are not understood in terms of known radio sources, and may represent a sign of new physics. In this Letter we identify a new class of dark sector models with feebly interacting particles, where dark photons oscillate into ordinary photons that contribute to the radio background. Our scenario can explain both the magnitude and the spectral index of the radio background, while being consistent with other cosmological and astrophysical constraints. These models predict new relativistic degrees of freedom and spectral distortions of the cosmic microwave background, which could be detected in the next generation of experiments.

conceptDetails.name: dark photon oscillation
conceptDetails.description: The central mechanism of the paper: dark photons resonantly oscillate into ordinary photons in the presence of a plasma, generating the observed radio excess.

conceptDetails.name: stimulated decay
conceptDetails.description: Dark matter decay into dark photons is enhanced by stimulated emission from an existing dark photon blackbody bath, producing the required spectral index.

conceptDetails.name: dark sector model
conceptDetails.description: A new class of feebly interacting dark sector models is introduced where multiple dark sector components interact to produce the extragalactic radio background.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Dark matter particles serve as the decaying source that produces dark photons, which then oscillate into radio photons contributing to the extragalactic radio background.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes a new-physics explanation for the anomalous extragalactic radio background excess that cannot be accounted for by known astrophysical sources.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: The CMB provides both the thermal bath context for this model and serves as a target for predicted spectral distortions that future experiments could detect.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral analysis is used to match the power-law index and magnitude of the radio background brightness temperature across multiple frequencies.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs effective field theory frameworks to describe dark sector interactions and feebly interacting particles mediating dark photon production.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Cosmological constraints and redshift-dependent resonant oscillation conditions are evaluated against observational data to validate the proposed model.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Parameter fitting of the dark sector model to radio background data uses statistical inference methods to constrain model parameters.

title: Overcoming the Spectral Bias of Neural Value Approximation
abstract: Value approximation using deep neural networks is at the heart of off-policy deep reinforcement learning, and is often the primary module that provides learning signals to the rest of the algorithm. While multi-layer perceptron networks are universal function approximators, recent works in neural kernel regression suggest the presence of a spectral bias, where fitting high-frequency components of the value function requires exponentially more gradient update steps than the low-frequency ones. In this work, we re-examine off-policy reinforcement learning through the lens of kernel regression and propose to overcome such bias via a composite neural tangent kernel. With just a single line-change, our approach, the Fourier feature networks (FFN) produce state-of-the-art performance on challenging continuous control domains with only a fraction of the compute. Faster convergence and better off-policy stability also make it possible to remove the target network without suffering catastrophic divergences, which further reduces TD}(0)'s estimation bias on a few tasks.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses off-policy deep reinforcement learning, specifically value approximation in TD-learning algorithms like Q-learning.

conceptDetails.name: fourier feature networks
conceptDetails.description: A novel method introduced in the paper: networks using random Fourier feature input transformations to construct a composite NTK that overcomes spectral bias in value approximation.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes neural networks through the lens of kernel regression, using the neural tangent kernel framework to understand and improve value approximation.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Spectral bias — the tendency of neural networks to learn low-frequency components faster than high-frequency ones — is the central problem addressed by the paper.

conceptDetails.name: neural tangent kernel
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to the paper: the NTK framework is used to analyze gradient descent dynamics and motivate the composite Fourier feature kernel construction.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Random Fourier features are used to lift inputs into a higher-dimensional space, enabling the network to capture high-frequency components of the value function.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Fourier feature networks transform input representations to overcome spectral bias, effectively learning better input embeddings for value approximation.

conceptDetails.name: td learning stability
conceptDetails.description: A novel concern addressed: improved off-policy stability from localized generalization properties of the composite kernel, enabling removal of the target network.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets improved policy optimization in continuous control domains by improving the quality and stability of value function estimates.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: FFN achieves state-of-the-art performance with a fraction of the compute, demonstrating improved computational efficiency over standard MLP approaches.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes modifying the neural tangent kernel via a composite kernel construction, which implicitly changes the effective loss landscape for value function optimization.

title: Simplifying Polylogarithms with Machine Learning
abstract: Polylogrithmic functions, such as the logarithm or dilogarithm, satisfy a number of algebraic identities. For the logarithm, all the identities follow from the product rule. For the dilogarithm and higher-weight classical polylogarithms, the identities can involve five functions or more. In many calculations relevant to particle physics, complicated combinations of polylogarithms often arise from Feynman integrals. Although the initial expressions resulting from the integration usually simplify, it is often difficult to know which identities to apply and in what order. To address this bottleneck, we explore to what extent machine learning methods can help. We consider both a reinforcement learning approach, where the identities are analogous to moves in a game, and a transformer network approach, where the problem is viewed analogously to a language-translation task.
