While both methods are effective, the transformer network appears more powerful and holds promise for practical use in symbolic manipulation tasks in mathematical physics.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: A transformer network is used to frame polylogarithm simplification as a sequence-to-sequence translation task, and proves more powerful than the RL approach.

conceptDetails.name: polylogarithm identities
conceptDetails.description: Classical polylogarithm identities (reflection, duplication, etc.) form the action space for both the RL and transformer approaches to expression simplification.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: An RL approach is explored where polylogarithm identities are treated as moves in a game, allowing the agent to learn which identity sequences lead to simplification.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic computation
conceptDetails.description: The core task is automated symbolic manipulation — applying algebraic identities to reduce polylogarithmic expressions to simpler canonical forms.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Polylogarithms arising from Feynman integrals in scattering amplitude calculations are the primary motivating application for the simplification methods developed.

conceptDetails.name: symbol calculus
conceptDetails.description: The symbol map, which extracts the maximal transcendental-weight part of iterated integrals, is used both as a simplification tool and as an intermediate representation for ML.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper is grounded in QFT calculations where polylogarithmic expressions appear in loop integrals and perturbative expansions.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The symbol-to-polylogarithm reconstruction is framed as an inverse problem, motivating the use of ML methods that excel at such tasks.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper explores automated discovery of simplification sequences for mathematical expressions without human-guided heuristics.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Attention mechanisms within the transformer architecture enable the model to learn dependencies between terms in polylogarithmic expressions.

title: Revealing the Milky Way's Most Recent Major Merger with a Gaia EDR3 Catalog of Machine-Learned Line-of-Sight Velocities
abstract: Machine learning can play a powerful role in inferring missing line-of-sight velocities from astrometry in surveys such as Gaia. In this paper, we apply a neural network to Gaia Early Data Release 3 (EDR3) and obtain line-of-sight velocities and associated uncertainties for ~92 million stars. The network, which takes as input a star's parallax, angular coordinates, and proper motions, is trained and validated on ~6.4 million stars in Gaia with complete phase-space information. The network's uncertainty on its velocity prediction is a key aspect of its design; by properly convolving these uncertainties with the inferred velocities, we obtain accurate stellar kinematic distributions. As a first science application, we use the new network-completed catalog to identify candidate stars that belong to the Milky Way's most recent major merger, Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus (GSE). We present the kinematic, energy, angular momentum, and spatial distributions of the ~450,000 GSE candidates in this sample, and also study the chemical abundances of those with cross matches to GALAH and APOGEE. The network's predictive power will only continue to improve with future Gaia data releases as the training set of stars with complete phase-space information grows. This work provides a first demonstration of how to use machine learning to exploit high-dimensional correlations on data to infer line-of-sight velocities, and offers a template for how to train, validate and apply such a neural network when complete observational data is not available.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The network outputs per-prediction velocity uncertainties that are convolved with inferred velocities to produce accurate kinematic distributions.

conceptDetails.name: stellar phase-space inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel method of using machine learning to reconstruct full 6D stellar phase-space coordinates from incomplete 5D astrometry at catalog scale.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The neural network performs regression to predict continuous line-of-sight velocities from 5D astrometric inputs.

conceptDetails.name: galactic archaeology
conceptDetails.description: The paper applies the inferred velocity catalog to study the chemical and kinematic signatures of the Milky Way's past merger history.

conceptDetails.name: high-dimensional correlation learning
conceptDetails.description: The neural network exploits high-dimensional correlations between astrometric observables to infer the missing velocity component.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The network is trained on the small subset of stars with complete 6D phase-space data to predict missing velocities for the much larger 5D dataset.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The network's predictions are validated against held-out Gaia stars with measured line-of-sight velocities to assess predictive accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: GSE merger candidate stars are identified as a kinematically distinct subpopulation within the broader stellar catalog.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The network trained on stars with known velocities is applied to infer velocities for stars lacking spectroscopic measurements.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: GSE debris stars are relevant to understanding the dark matter halo structure and local dark matter distribution near the Sun.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Simulations of hierarchical structure formation provide the theoretical framework for interpreting the GSE merger event.

title: Degeneracy Engineering for Classical and Quantum Annealing: A Case Study of Sparse Linear Regression in Collider Physics
abstract: Classical and quantum annealing are computing paradigms that have been proposed to solve a wide range of optimization problems. In this paper, we aim to enhance the performance of annealing algorithms by introducing the technique of degeneracy engineering, through which the relative degeneracy of the ground state is increased by modifying a subset of terms in the objective Hamiltonian. We illustrate this novel approach by applying it to the example of $\ell_0$-norm regularization for sparse linear regression, which is in general an NP-hard optimization problem. Specifically, we show how to cast $\ell_0$-norm regularization as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem, suitable for implementation on annealing platforms. As a case study, we apply this QUBO formulation to energy flow polynomials in high-energy collider physics, finding that degeneracy engineering substantially improves the annealing performance. Our results motivate the application of degeneracy engineering to a variety of regularized optimization problems.

conceptDetails.name: degeneracy engineering
conceptDetails.description: Novel technique introduced in this paper that increases the relative degeneracy of the ground state in the objective Hamiltonian to improve annealing performance.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse linear regression with ℓ0-norm regularization is the central optimization problem studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: qubo encoding
conceptDetails.description: The sparse regression problem is cast as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization problem using redundant binary encodings of fit coefficients.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: Quantum annealing is the primary computing paradigm explored for solving the sparse regression optimization problem.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Linear regression with various norm regularizers (ℓ0, ℓ1, ℓ2) is the machine learning task applied to collider physics observables.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: High-energy collider physics provides the case study domain, specifically sparse regression over energy flow polynomials.

conceptDetails.name: physics-motivated optimization
conceptDetails.description: The optimization landscape is shaped by modifying the objective Hamiltonian, directly drawing on physics concepts of ground-state degeneracy.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Path integral Monte Carlo is used as a classical proxy for quantum annealing and compared against simulated annealing.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives a QUBO formulation of the ℓ0-norm penalty, translating the regularized loss into a quadratic binary objective.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Energy flow polynomials are collider observables built from jet constituents, forming the linear basis used in the regression tasks.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Sparse regression over EFPs aims to identify a minimal set of physically interpretable collider observables sufficient for classification tasks.

title: Towards Understanding Grokking: An Effective Theory of Representation Learning
abstract: We aim to understand grokking, a phenomenon where models generalize long after overfitting their training set. We present both a microscopic analysis anchored by an effective theory and a macroscopic analysis of phase diagrams describing learning performance across hyperparameters. We find that generalization originates from structured representations whose training dynamics and dependence on training set size can be predicted by our effective theory in a toy setting. We observe empirically the presence of four learning phases: comprehension, grokking, memorization, and confusion. We find representation learning to occur only in a "Goldilocks zone" (including comprehension and grokking) between memorization and confusion. We find on transformers the grokking phase stays closer to the memorization phase (compared to the comprehension phase), leading to delayed generalization. The Goldilocks phase is reminiscent of "intelligence from starvation" in Darwinian evolution, where resource limitations drive discovery of more efficient solutions. This study not only provides intuitive explanations of the origin of grokking, but also highlights the usefulness of physics-inspired tools, e.g., effective theories and phase diagrams, for understanding deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The central thesis of the paper: generalization in grokking originates from the emergence of structured representations in embedding space, not from memorization.

conceptDetails.name: grokking
conceptDetails.description: A novel phenomenon of delayed generalization where models suddenly generalize long after overfitting the training set, which this paper provides a mechanistic explanation for.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: A physics-inspired analytical framework borrowed directly from theoretical physics to model the dynamics of representation learning using a simplified effective loss function.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies four discrete learning phases (comprehension, grokking, memorization, confusion) separated by transition boundaries, analyzed via phase diagrams over hyperparameter space.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Learnable input embedding vectors whose geometric structure (parallelograms, circles) is directly linked to generalization performance and is the primary object of analysis.

conceptDetails.name: learning phase diagrams
conceptDetails.description: Macroscopic phase diagrams mapping learning outcomes (comprehension, grokking, memorization, confusion) across hyperparameter grids, introduced as a diagnostic tool for understanding generalization.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper aims to explain why and how neural networks generalize, using PCA visualizations and structured representation analysis to illuminate the internal mechanics of grokking.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Used as the primary neural architecture on which grokking is studied empirically, particularly for modular addition tasks.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Modular addition over abelian groups serves as the canonical task for studying grokking, and the algebraic structure of the group constrains the form of learned representations.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives an effective loss function that depends only on representations rather than the full decoder, enabling tractable theoretical analysis of training dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The dependence of grokking on training set size and the divergence of generalization time near a critical dataset size are analyzed as scaling phenomena.

title: Power Counting Energy Flow Polynomials
abstract: Power counting is a systematic strategy for organizing collider observables and their associated theoretical calculations. In this paper, we use power counting to characterize a class of jet substructure observables called energy flow polynomials (EFPs). EFPs provide an overcomplete linear basis for infrared-and-collinear safe jet observables, but it is known that in practice, a small subset of EFPs is often sufficient for specific jet analysis tasks. By applying power counting arguments, we obtain linear relationships between EFPs that hold for quark and gluon jets to a specific order in the power counting. We test these relations in the parton shower generator Pythia, finding excellent agreement. Power counting allows us to truncate the basis of EFPs without affecting performance, which we corroborate through a study of quark-gluon tagging and regression.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on jet substructure observables, specifically energy flow polynomials, and their systematic organization via power counting.

conceptDetails.name: energy flow polynomials
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: EFPs are an infrared-and-collinear safe overcomplete linear basis for jet substructure observables, central to the entire paper.

conceptDetails.name: power counting
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the systematic expansion strategy used to derive linear redundancy relations among EFPs for quark and gluon jets.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The work is motivated by LHC jet analyses and uses Pythia parton shower simulations to validate theoretical predictions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Power counting, the core technique, is drawn from soft-collinear effective theory (SCET) and applied to organize jet observables at leading and subleading logarithmic orders.

conceptDetails.name: infrared-collinear safety
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: the theoretical constraint ensuring jet observables are insensitive to soft and collinear splittings, which defines the EFP basis and the validity of power counting relations.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Quark-gluon jet tagging via logistic regression is used to benchmark the reduced EFP bases against the full basis.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Linear and logistic regression studies validate that truncated EFP bases retain full predictive performance for jet substructure tasks.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Power counting identifies a small, non-redundant subset of EFPs sufficient for jet analysis, effectively producing a sparse basis.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: EFPs serve as a structured feature basis for jets, and power counting guides which features are informationally distinct.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: EFPs are indexed by multigraphs, and their computational complexity is analyzed via graph tree-width, connecting to graph-based ML representations of jets.

title: Bias and Priors in Machine Learning Calibrations for High Energy Physics
abstract: Machine learning offers an exciting opportunity to improve the calibration of nearly all reconstructed objects in high-energy physics detectors. However, machine learning approaches often depend on the spectra of examples used during training, an issue known as prior dependence. This is an undesirable property of a calibration, which needs to be applicable in a variety of environments. The purpose of this paper is to explicitly highlight the prior dependence of some machine learning-based calibration strategies. We demonstrate how some recent proposals for both simulation-based and data-based calibrations inherit properties of the sample used for training, which can result in biases for downstream analyses. In the case of simulation-based calibration, we argue that our recently proposed Gaussian Ansatz approach can avoid some of the pitfalls of prior dependence, whereas prior-independent data-based calibration remains an open problem.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The central topic: removing bias from ML-based inference of detector observables, covering both simulation-based and data-based calibration strategies.

conceptDetails.name: prior dependence
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept introduced as the core problem: ML calibrations inherit properties of the training sample spectrum, causing bias when deployed on different data distributions.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Primary case study application: jet energy response calibration at the LHC using ATLAS/CMS detector data and simulation.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: Experimental context: calibrations at the Large Hadron Collider for ATLAS and CMS experiments, covering jets, muons, electrons, and tau leptons.

conceptDetails.name: gaussian ansatz
conceptDetails.description: Novel prior-independent simulation-based calibration method proposed by the authors that avoids the bias pitfalls of standard ML calibration approaches.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: Prior independence is framed as a universality/robustness requirement: a valid calibration must remain unbiased when deployed outside its training distribution.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Section III analyzes resolution and uncertainty in calibrations, distinguishing statistical uncertainty from calibration bias.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Simulation-based calibration uses (xD, zT) pairs from Monte Carlo simulation to learn detector response, a form of simulation-based inference.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Calibration functions are trained via MSE minimization, framing the problem as a conditional mean regression over detector-level features.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Calibrations apply to all reconstructed objects (jets, muons, electrons, photons, tau leptons) produced by HEP detector reconstruction pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Data-based calibration involves matching simulation and data distributions, requiring estimation of conditional or marginal densities.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper examines how choice of loss function (MSE, mode-based, quantile) determines which statistic the calibration targets and affects prior dependence.

title: Disentangling Quarks and Gluons with CMS Open Data
abstract: We study quark and gluon jets separately using public collider data from the CMS experiment. Our analysis is based on 2.3/fb of proton-proton collisions at 7 TeV, collected at the Large Hadron Collider in 2011. We define two non-overlapping samples via a pseudorapidity cut -- central jets with |eta| < 0.65 and forward jets with |eta| > 0.65 -- and employ jet topic modeling to extract individual distributions for the maximally separable categories. Under certain assumptions, such as sample independence and mutual irreducibility, these categories correspond to "quark" and "gluon" jets, as given by a recently proposed operational definition. We consider a number of different methods for extracting reducibility factors from the central and forward datasets, from which the fractions of quark jets in each sample can be determined. The greatest stability and robustness to statistical uncertainties is achieved by a novel method based on parametrizing the endpoints of a receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. To mitigate detector effects, which would otherwise induce unphysical differences between central and forward jets, we use the OmniFold method to perform central value unfolding. As a demonstration of the power of this method, we extract the intrinsic dimensionality of the quark and gluon jet samples, which exhibit Casimir scaling, as expected from the strongly-ordered limit. To our knowledge, this work is the first application of full phase space unfolding to real collider data, and one of the first applications of topic modeling to extract separate quark and gluon distributions at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper centers on analyzing quark and gluon jets from CMS collider data, studying their substructure distributions and tagging performance.

conceptDetails.name: jet topic modeling
conceptDetails.description: A novel application of statistical topic modeling separates quark and gluon jet distributions from mixed pseudorapidity-selected samples.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The analysis uses 2.3/fb of proton-proton collision data at 7 TeV from the LHC CMS experiment.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: OmniFold is applied for full phase-space central value unfolding to mitigate detector effects between central and forward jet samples.

conceptDetails.name: roc curve fit
conceptDetails.description: A novel method parametrizing the endpoints of a receiver operating characteristic curve provides the most robust extraction of reducibility factors.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Machine-learned classifiers are used to distinguish quark and gluon jets and to extract reducibility factors via ROC curve analysis.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: A log-likelihood ratio fit method is introduced as a more stable approach to extracting reducibility factors from mixed jet samples.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The intrinsic (correlation) dimensionality of quark and gluon jet samples is extracted, revealing Casimir scaling behavior.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Pythia 6.4.25 Monte Carlo results are used as cross-checks against the CMS Open Data measurements.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper is grounded in QCD, with Casimir scaling providing a theoretical expectation for quark/gluon jet dimensionality differences.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Topic modeling identifies maximally separable categories in mixed jet samples, analogous to unsupervised signal/background separation.

title: Learning Uncertainties the Frequentist Way: Calibration and Correlation in High Energy Physics
abstract: Calibration is a common experimental physics problem, whose goal is to infer the value and uncertainty of an unobservable quantity Z given a measured quantity X. Additionally, one would like to quantify the extent to which X and Z are correlated. In this paper, we present a machine learning framework for performing frequentist maximum likelihood inference with Gaussian uncertainty estimation, which also quantifies the mutual information between the unobservable and measured quantities. This framework uses the Donsker-Varadhan representation of the Kullback-Leibler divergence -- parametrized with a novel Gaussian Ansatz -- to enable a simultaneous extraction of the maximum likelihood values, uncertainties, and mutual information in a single training. We demonstrate our framework by extracting jet energy corrections and resolution factors from a simulation of the CMS detector at the Large Hadron Collider. By leveraging the high-dimensional feature space inside jets, we improve upon the nominal CMS jet resolution by upwards of 15%.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The central task of this paper: inferring unobservable quantities from measured data with proper uncertainty quantification in a prior-independent frequentist framework.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a Gaussian Ansatz to simultaneously extract maximum likelihood estimates and resolution (uncertainty) in a single training pass.

conceptDetails.name: gaussian ansatz
conceptDetails.description: A novel parametrization of the MINE network that encodes the log-likelihood ratio as a Gaussian function, enabling direct extraction of calibration values and resolutions.

conceptDetails.name: mutual information
conceptDetails.description: Used via the Donsker-Varadhan representation of KL divergence to quantify correlation between unobservable and measured quantities as part of the unified training objective.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The MINE network is shown to learn the log-likelihood ratio, enabling prior-independent frequentist inference of calibration values and resolutions.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The framework is demonstrated on jet energy corrections and resolution factors from CMS detector simulations at the LHC, improving jet energy resolution by over 15%.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The application domain is high energy particle collider physics, specifically CMS detector data at the Large Hadron Collider.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses reconstruction of particle kinematic properties from high-dimensional detector signatures, a core task in collider event analysis.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The Donsker-Varadhan representation is used to estimate the likelihood density ratio, underpinning the calibration and mutual information estimation.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A novel Gaussian Ansatz parametrization of the MINE network architecture is introduced to make simultaneous extraction of calibration values, uncertainties, and mutual information tractable.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper contrasts its prior-independent frequentist approach against standard regression methods that produce prior-dependent point estimates.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: High-dimensional jet substructure features are leveraged to improve upon nominal CMS jet energy resolution.

title: Rapid Locomotion via Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Agile maneuvers such as sprinting and high-speed turning in the wild are challenging for legged robots. We present an end-to-end learned controller that achieves record agility for the MIT Mini Cheetah, sustaining speeds up to 3.9 m/s. This system runs and turns fast on natural terrains like grass, ice, and gravel and responds robustly to disturbances. Our controller is a neural network trained in simulation via reinforcement learning and transferred to the real world. The two key components are (i) an adaptive curriculum on velocity commands and (ii) an online system identification strategy for sim-to-real transfer leveraged from prior work. Videos of the robot's behaviors are available at: https://agility.csail.mit.edu/

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The core training paradigm used to learn the locomotion controller in simulation, automatically discovering policies that maximize reward across diverse velocity commands and terrains.

conceptDetails.name: adaptive curriculum
conceptDetails.description: A novel automatic curriculum strategy that progressively expands the set of velocity command tasks while respecting physical locomotion constraints, enabling successful high-speed training that naive uniform sampling fails to achieve.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The policy trained entirely in simulation is deployed zero-shot in the real world, relying on sim-to-real transfer without fine-tuning on physical hardware.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The locomotion problem is framed as multi-task RL where each combination of linear and angular velocity constitutes a separate task, requiring the policy to generalize across a broad command space.

conceptDetails.name: domain randomization
conceptDetails.description: Simulation parameters such as ground friction, payload mass, and motor strength are randomized during training to promote robust sim-to-real transfer without real-world fine-tuning.

conceptDetails.name: online system identification
conceptDetails.description: An online strategy borrowed from prior work that estimates real-world system parameters during deployment to bridge the sim-to-real gap for the learned locomotion policy.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: A carefully designed multi-term reward function balances velocity tracking with stability, smoothness, and safety penalties to guide policy learning toward agile yet robust behaviors.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The learned controller demonstrates robust performance across diverse natural terrains (grass, ice, gravel) and recovers from disturbances including motor failure and tripping.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The RL framework is presented as a scalable alternative to model-based control, avoiding the need for human-engineered reduced-order models as task complexity grows.

title: Infinite Variance in Monte Carlo Sampling of Lattice Field Theories
abstract: In Monte Carlo calculations of expectation values in lattice quantum field theories, the stochastic variance of the sampling procedure that is used defines the precision of the calculation for a fixed number of samples. If the variance of an estimator of a particular quantity is formally infinite, or in practice very large compared to the square of the mean, then that quantity can not be reliably estimated using the given sampling procedure. There are multiple scenarios in which this occurs, including in Lattice Quantum Chromodynamics, and a particularly simple example is given by the Gross-Neveu model where Monte Carlo calculations involve the introduction of auxiliary bosonic variables through a Hubbard-Stratonovich (HS) transformation. Here, it is shown that the variances of HS estimators for classes of operators involving fermion fields are divergent in this model and an even simpler zero-dimensional analogue. To correctly estimate these observables, two alternative sampling methods are proposed and numerically investigated.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The core computational framework analyzed in this paper, specifically addressing how infinite variance undermines reliable expectation value estimation in lattice field theories.

conceptDetails.name: infinite variance sampling
conceptDetails.description: The central problem studied: formal divergence of estimator variance for fermionic observables under standard sampling, rendering CLT-based confidence intervals invalid.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework within which the sampling variance problems are studied, including QCD and the Gross-Neveu model as representative examples.

conceptDetails.name: hubbard-stratonovich transformation
conceptDetails.description: The auxiliary field technique whose continuous version produces infinite-variance estimators; the paper introduces a discrete variant to cure this pathology.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary phenomenologically-relevant theory motivating this work, where zero modes of the Dirac operator cause infinite variance in standard Monte Carlo estimators.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The general discretized spacetime framework used to study strongly-coupled quantum field theories numerically via importance sampling.

conceptDetails.name: sequential reweighting
conceptDetails.description: A novel method proposed in this paper that decomposes a non-negative infinite-variance observable into a product of finite-variance random variables to enable reliable estimation.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses the breakdown of variance-based confidence intervals when estimator variance diverges, motivating alternative sampling schemes.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The statistical properties of random variables under importance sampling, particularly the conditions under which the Central Limit Theorem fails due to infinite variance.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Used to analyze zero modes of the lattice Dirac operator, which are the source of divergent variance in fermionic observables.

title: Going Beyond the Galaxy Power Spectrum: an Analysis of BOSS Data with Wavelet Scattering Transforms
abstract: We perform the first application of the wavelet scattering transform (WST) to actual galaxy observations, through a WST analysis of the BOSS DR12 CMASS dataset. We included the effects of redshift-space anisotropy, non-trivial survey geometry, systematic weights, and the Alcock-Paczynski distortion effect, following the commonly adopted steps for the power spectrum analysis. In order to capture the cosmological dependence of the WST, we use galaxy mocks obtained from the state-of-the-art ABACUSSUMMIT simulations, tuned to match the anisotropic correlation function of the BOSS CMASS sample in the redshift range $0.46<z<0.60$. Using our model for the WST coefficients, as well as for the first 2 multipoles of the galaxy power spectrum, that we use as reference, we perform a likelihood analysis of the CMASS data. We obtain the posterior probability distributions of 4 cosmological parameters, $\{ω_b,ω_c,n_s,σ_8\}$, as well as the Hubble constant, derived from a fixed value of the angular size of the sound horizon at last scattering measured by the Planck satellite, all of which are marginalized over the 7 nuisance parameters of the Halo Occupation Distribution model. The WST is found to deliver a substantial improvement in the values of the predicted $1σ$ errors compared to the regular power spectrum, which are tighter by a factor of $3-5$ in the case of flat and uninformative priors and by a factor of $3-8$, when a Big Bang Nucleosynthesis prior is applied on the value of $ω_b$. Our results are investigative and subject to certain approximations, which we discuss in the text.

conceptDetails.name: wavelet scattering transform
conceptDetails.description: The core estimator applied to BOSS galaxy data, performing successive wavelet convolutions and modulus operations to extract non-Gaussian clustering information beyond the power spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: ABACUSSUMMIT galaxy mocks are used to model the cosmological dependence of WST coefficients, enabling likelihood-based parameter inference without analytic likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Posterior probability distributions over {ωb, ωc, ns, σ8} and the Hubble constant are inferred from the BOSS CMASS dataset using the WST and power spectrum models.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: A likelihood analysis framework is constructed to compare WST and power spectrum constraints on cosmological parameters, marginalized over HOD nuisance parameters.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The ABACUSSUMMIT N-body simulation suite provides state-of-the-art galaxy mocks tuned to match the anisotropic clustering of the BOSS CMASS sample.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: Surrogate models for WST coefficients are built from simulation outputs to efficiently cover the cosmological parameter space during likelihood evaluation.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The WST compresses galaxy clustering information into a compact set of coefficients that capture non-Gaussian structure without raising the density field to high powers.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The galaxy power spectrum multipoles serve as the reference statistic against which WST cosmological constraints are benchmarked.

conceptDetails.name: redshift-space distortions
conceptDetails.description: Galaxy peculiar velocities introduce anisotropy in the observed clustering pattern, which the WST analysis explicitly models through power spectrum multipoles and anisotropic statistics.

conceptDetails.name: halo occupation distribution
conceptDetails.description: A 7-parameter HOD model accounts for the stochastic galaxy-halo connection as nuisance parameters marginalized over in the cosmological inference.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: The nature of the late-time accelerated expansion of the universe is a primary motivation for extracting improved cosmological constraints from large-scale structure.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The distribution of dark matter shapes the large-scale structure probed by the galaxy survey, with σ8 and ωc directly constraining its abundance and clustering.

title: DiffCSE: Difference-based Contrastive Learning for Sentence Embeddings
abstract: We propose DiffCSE, an unsupervised contrastive learning framework for learning sentence embeddings. DiffCSE learns sentence embeddings that are sensitive to the difference between the original sentence and an edited sentence, where the edited sentence is obtained by stochastically masking out the original sentence and then sampling from a masked language model. We show that DiffSCE is an instance of equivariant contrastive learning (Dangovski et al., 2021), which generalizes contrastive learning and learns representations that are insensitive to certain types of augmentations and sensitive to other "harmful" types of augmentations. Our experiments show that DiffCSE achieves state-of-the-art results among unsupervised sentence representation learning methods, outperforming unsupervised SimCSE by 2.3 absolute points on semantic textual similarity tasks.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The core training framework in DiffCSE, using positive pairs (dropout augmentations) and negative pairs to learn sentence representations.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The primary goal of DiffCSE is to learn high-quality universal sentence representations that transfer across NLP tasks.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: DiffCSE produces sentence embeddings evaluated on semantic textual similarity and transfer tasks.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: DiffCSE is fully unsupervised, requiring no labeled data and relying on self-generated augmented views for training signal.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: DiffCSE is framed as an instance of equivariant contrastive learning, training encoders to be sensitive to certain harmful transformations rather than invariant to all.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Two complementary augmentation strategies are used: dropout-based (insensitive) and MLM-based word replacement (sensitive), which together define the equivariant training objective.

conceptDetails.name: difference prediction
conceptDetails.description: A novel conditional ELECTRA-style discriminator predicts which tokens in an MLM-edited sentence differ from the original, operationalizing equivariance to linguistic perturbations.

conceptDetails.name: mlm augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Masked language model sampling is used as a sensitive transformation to generate edited sentences that meaningfully alter sentence semantics.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: BERT-based transformer models serve as the sentence encoder backbone, with ELECTRA-style discriminator heads added for difference prediction.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: Pretrained language models are fine-tuned with the DiffCSE objective to adapt them for sentence-level semantic tasks.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: DiffCSE embeddings are evaluated on 7 SentEval transfer tasks to measure the universality of the learned representations.

title: Photometrically-Classified Superluminous Supernovae from the Pan-STARRS1 Medium Deep Survey: A Case Study for Science with Machine Learning-Based Classification
abstract: With the upcoming Vera C.~Rubin Observatory Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST), it is expected that only $\sim 0.1\%$ of all transients will be classified spectroscopically. To conduct studies of rare transients, such as Type I superluminous supernovae (SLSNe), we must instead rely on photometric classification. In this vein, here we carry out a pilot study of SLSNe from the Pan-STARRS1 Medium-Deep Survey (PS1-MDS) classified photometrically with our SuperRAENN and Superphot algorithms. We first construct a sub-sample of the photometric sample using a list of simple selection metrics designed to minimize contamination and ensure sufficient data quality for modeling. We then fit the multi-band light curves with a magnetar spin-down model using the Modular Open-Source Fitter for Transients (MOSFiT). Comparing the magnetar engine and ejecta parameter distributions of the photometric sample to those of the PS1-MDS spectroscopic sample and a larger literature spectroscopic sample, we find that these samples are overall consistent, but that the photometric sample extends to slower spins and lower ejecta masses, which correspond to lower luminosity events, as expected for photometric selection. While our PS1-MDS photometric sample is still smaller than the overall SLSN spectroscopic sample, our methodology paves the way to an orders-of-magnitude increase in the SLSN sample in the LSST era through photometric selection and study.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central task is photometric classification of superluminous supernovae using machine learning algorithms SuperRAENN and Superphot.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Random forest and semi-supervised classification methods are used to identify SLSNe from photometric survey data.

conceptDetails.name: photometric light curve fitting
conceptDetails.description: Multi-band light curves are fit with analytic and physical models to extract supernova parameters without spectroscopic confirmation.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: SuperRAENN employs a recurrent autoencoder neural network (RAENN) for unsupervised feature learning from light curve data.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar central engine
conceptDetails.description: The magnetar spin-down model is used as the physical mechanism to explain SLSN luminosity, with parameters inferred from light curve fitting.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: The SuperRAENN algorithm uses a recurrent neural network architecture to process time-series light curve data.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: MOSFiT uses Bayesian inference to fit magnetar spin-down models to multi-band light curves and derive posterior parameter distributions.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: SuperRAENN combines unsupervised representation learning with a supervised random forest classifier in a semi-supervised framework.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper derives and compares posterior distributions of magnetar engine and ejecta parameters across photometric and spectroscopic samples.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Random forest classifiers are employed in both SuperRAENN and Superphot as core ensemble-based classification components.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: MOSFiT serves as a physical surrogate model fitting multi-band light curves with a magnetar engine model for parameter inference.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Selection criteria are applied post-classification to reduce contamination and isolate genuine SLSNe from the photometric sample.

title: Luminous Supernovae: Unveiling a Population Between Superluminous and Normal Core-collapse Supernovae
abstract: Stripped-envelope core-collapse supernovae can be divided into two broad classes: the common Type Ib/c supernovae (SNe Ib/c), powered by the radioactive decay of $^{56}$Ni, and the rare superluminous supernovae (SLSNe), most likely powered by the spin-down of a magnetar central engine. Up to now, the intermediate regime between these two populations has remained mostly unexplored. Here, we present a comprehensive study of 40 \textit{luminous supernovae} (LSNe), SNe with peak magnitudes of $M_r = -19$ to $-20$ mag, bound by SLSNe on the bright end and by SNe Ib/c on the dim end. Spectroscopically, LSNe appear to form a continuum between Type Ic SNe and SLSNe. Given their intermediate nature, we model the light curves of all LSNe using a combined magnetar plus radioactive decay model and find that they are indeed intermediate, not only in terms of their peak luminosity and spectra, but also in their rise times, power sources, and physical parameters. We sub-classify LSNe into distinct groups that are either as fast-evolving as SNe Ib/c or as slow-evolving as SLSNe, and appear to be either radioactively or magnetar powered, respectively. Our findings indicate that LSNe are powered by either an over-abundant production of $^{56}$Ni or by weak magnetar engines, and may serve as the missing link between the two populations.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies and characterizes a new intermediate class of stripped-envelope core-collapse supernovae (luminous supernovae) between Type Ib/c and superluminous supernovae.

conceptDetails.name: light curve modeling
conceptDetails.description: Combined magnetar plus radioactive decay light curve models are fit to all 40 LSNe to extract physical parameters and classify their power sources.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar central engine
conceptDetails.description: Spin-down energy from a millisecond magnetar is modeled as the primary power source for the slow-evolving, bright subset of luminous supernovae.

conceptDetails.name: radioactive decay power
conceptDetails.description: Overabundant 56Ni radioactive decay is modeled as the power source for the fast-evolving subset of luminous supernovae.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian parameter estimation is used to fit magnetar plus radioactive decay light curve models to the supernova sample.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: The study examines progenitor masses and stripped-envelope evolution of massive stars leading to different supernova types.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC sampling is employed to explore the posterior distributions of physical parameters in the light curve models.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: LSNe are sub-classified into distinct groups based on their photometric and spectroscopic properties.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Light curve fitting via combined magnetar and radioactive decay models constitutes a regression task to extract physical parameters.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper identifies LSNe as outliers in the existing SN classification scheme, bridging two previously distinct populations.

title: Quantification of high dimensional non-Gaussianities and its implication to Fisher analysis in cosmology
abstract: It is well known that the power spectrum is not able to fully characterize the statistical properties of non-Gaussian density fields. Recently, many different statistics have been proposed to extract information from non-Gaussian cosmological fields that perform better than the power spectrum. The Fisher matrix formalism is commonly used to quantify the accuracy with which a given statistic can constrain the value of the cosmological parameters. However, these calculations typically rely on the assumption that the likelihood of the considered statistic follows a multivariate Gaussian distribution. In this work we follow Sellentin & Heavens (2017) and use two different statistical tests to identify non-Gaussianities in different statistics such as the power spectrum, bispectrum, marked power spectrum, and wavelet scatering transform (WST). We remove the non-Gaussian components of the different statistics and perform Fisher matrix calculations with the \textit{Gaussianized} statistics using Quijote simulations. We show that constraints on the parameters can change by a factor of $\sim 2$ in some cases. We show with simple examples how statistics that do not follow a multivariate Gaussian distribution can achieve artificially tight bounds on the cosmological parameters when using the Fisher matrix formalism. We think that the non-Gaussian tests used in this work represent a powerful tool to quantify the robustness of Fisher matrix calculations and their underlying assumptions. We release the code used to compute the power spectra, bispectra, and WST that can be run on both CPUs and GPUs.

conceptDetails.name: fisher information matrix
conceptDetails.description: The Fisher matrix formalism is the central object of study; the paper demonstrates that its Gaussianity assumptions can produce artificially tight or biased parameter bounds.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Two statistical tests from Sellentin & Heavens (2017) are used to identify non-Gaussianities in cosmological statistics, forming the methodological core of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The central assumption that a statistic's likelihood follows a multivariate Gaussian distribution is systematically tested and its violation is shown to bias Fisher forecasts.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The Fisher matrix formalism is used to quantify the accuracy of cosmological parameter constraints from statistics like the power spectrum, bispectrum, and WST.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Quijote suite of 44,100 N-body simulations provides the density fields used to compute statistics and perform Fisher matrix calculations.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The power spectrum and bispectrum are among the four statistics analyzed for non-Gaussianity and used in Fisher forecasts of cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: wavelet scattering transform
conceptDetails.description: The WST is one of four statistics whose non-Gaussianity is tested, representing a promising non-linear wavelet-based summary statistic for large-scale structure.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The Fisher information matrix provides a Cramér-Rao lower bound on parameter estimation uncertainty within the Bayesian/frequentist inference framework.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Non-Gaussian dimensions are identified and removed from high-dimensional summary statistics to Gaussianize the data vector prior to Fisher analysis.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The Quijote N-body simulations are Monte Carlo realizations of cosmological density fields used to estimate covariance matrices for the Fisher formalism.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Dark energy equation-of-state parameter w is one of the cosmological parameters whose Fisher constraints are shown to change by a factor of ~2 after Gaussianization.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The matter density parameter Ωm and related cosmological parameters tracing dark matter distribution are constrained in the Fisher analysis.

title: Towards Designing and Exploiting Generative Networks for Neutrino Physics Experiments using Liquid Argon Time Projection Chambers
abstract: In this paper, we show that a hybrid approach to generative modeling via combining the decoder from an autoencoder together with an explicit generative model for the latent space is a promising method for producing images of particle trajectories in a liquid argon time projection chamber (LArTPC). LArTPCs are a type of particle physics detector used by several current and future experiments focused on studies of the neutrino. We implement a Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) and PixelCNN which produces images with LArTPC-like features and introduce a method to evaluate the quality of the images using a semantic segmentation that identifies important physics-based features.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is a hybrid generative modeling approach combining VQ-VAE and PixelCNN to synthesize realistic LArTPC detector images.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The VQ-VAE (Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoder) forms the implicit decoder component of the hybrid generative model.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: The generative model is proposed as a faster alternative to traditional physics-based detector simulation for LArTPC experiments.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: LArTPCs are neutrino detectors, and the paper's application domain is producing realistic images of neutrino interaction events.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: PixelCNN, a convolutional autoregressive network, is used to model the prior distribution over the VQ-VAE's discrete latent codes.

conceptDetails.name: particle tracking
conceptDetails.description: LArTPC images capture charged particle trajectories, and semantic segmentation is used to evaluate generated images against physics-based tracking features.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The VQ-VAE learns a discrete latent code representation of LArTPC detector images capturing physics-relevant features.

conceptDetails.name: semantic segmentation
conceptDetails.description: A semantic segmentation network is introduced as a novel evaluation metric to assess whether generated images contain physically meaningful particle trajectory features.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The PixelCNN provides an explicit likelihood model over the discrete latent codes, enabling tractable density estimation of the latent space.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses using generative models for event reconstruction by solving inverse problems to infer particle momenta from detector images.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The encoder maps high-dimensional detector images to a compressed discrete latent space as part of the VQ-VAE architecture.

title: Pareto-optimal clustering with the primal deterministic information bottleneck
abstract: At the heart of both lossy compression and clustering is a trade-off between the fidelity and size of the learned representation. Our goal is to map out and study the Pareto frontier that quantifies this trade-off. We focus on the optimization of the Deterministic Information Bottleneck (DIB) objective over the space of hard clusterings. To this end, we introduce the primal DIB problem, which we show results in a much richer frontier than its previously studied Lagrangian relaxation when optimized over discrete search spaces. We present an algorithm for mapping out the Pareto frontier of the primal DIB trade-off that is also applicable to other two-objective clustering problems. We study general properties of the Pareto frontier, and we give both analytic and numerical evidence for logarithmic sparsity of the frontier in general. We provide evidence that our algorithm has polynomial scaling despite the super-exponential search space, and additionally, we propose a modification to the algorithm that can be used where sampling noise is expected to be significant. Finally, we use our algorithm to map the DIB frontier of three different tasks: compressing the English alphabet, extracting informative color classes from natural images, and compressing a group theory-inspired dataset, revealing interesting features of frontier, and demonstrating how the structure of the frontier can be used for model selection with a focus on points previously hidden by the cloak of the convex hull.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: The paper optimizes over the space of hard clusterings (deterministic encodings) as the core computational problem, mapping Pareto-optimal clusterings via an agglomerative search algorithm.

conceptDetails.name: information bottleneck
conceptDetails.description: The Deterministic Information Bottleneck (DIB) objective — maximizing mutual information I(Z;Y) subject to an entropy constraint H(Z) — is the primary optimization framework studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: pareto frontier mapping
conceptDetails.description: The central algorithmic contribution is the Pareto Mapper algorithm, an epsilon-greedy agglomerative search that maps out the full Pareto frontier of two-objective clustering problems.

conceptDetails.name: lossy compression
conceptDetails.description: The paper frames clustering as a lossy compression problem, seeking representations that balance fidelity (mutual information with target Y) against parsimony (entropy of the compressed variable Z).

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies how to learn compact discrete representations Z of input X that preserve relevant information about a target variable Y, a core representation learning problem.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The DIB framework reduces a discrete input domain to a smaller cluster space, and the Pareto frontier characterizes the full spectrum of achievable compressions.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper contrasts the primal constrained DIB formulation against its Lagrangian relaxation, analyzing how the choice of objective parameterization affects accessible solutions.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The paper proves and numerically validates logarithmic sparsity of the Pareto frontier, showing that the number of Pareto-optimal points grows only as O(log n) in the number of inputs.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper argues that the structure of the Pareto frontier — particularly prominent corners — enables principled model selection, providing insight into natural scales and clustering complexity.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: One of the three experimental tasks uses a group theory-inspired dataset to test the DIB frontier algorithm, demonstrating its applicability to structured mathematical domains.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides evidence that the Pareto Mapper algorithm achieves polynomial scaling despite the super-exponential size of the clustering search space.

title: AI Poincaré 2.0: Machine Learning Conservation Laws from Differential Equations
abstract: We present a machine learning algorithm that discovers conservation laws from differential equations, both numerically (parametrized as neural networks) and symbolically, ensuring their functional independence (a non-linear generalization of linear independence). Our independence module can be viewed as a nonlinear generalization of singular value decomposition. Our method can readily handle inductive biases for conservation laws. We validate it with examples including the 3-body problem, the KdV equation and nonlinear Schrödinger equation.

conceptDetails.name: conservation laws
conceptDetails.description: The primary target of discovery — the algorithm finds all functionally independent conserved quantities from differential equations numerically and symbolically.

conceptDetails.name: functional independence
conceptDetails.description: A novel nonlinear generalization of linear independence that ensures the discovered conservation laws are truly distinct and not redundant combinations of each other.

conceptDetails.name: differential rank
conceptDetails.description: A novel method introduced to count independent conservation laws for PDEs by computing the rank of the Jacobian of conserved quantities, generalizing SVD.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: A special class of dynamical systems (including Kepler, harmonic oscillator, three-body problem) used as validation benchmarks for the discovered conservation laws.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: A key component of the pipeline that determines the number of independent conservation laws by estimating the rank of the learned function set.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The overarching goal is a general-purpose algorithm that auto-discovers all conservation laws without human-crafted strategies per problem.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper's symbolic front searches for closed-form formulas for conservation laws using fast rejection criteria.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks are parameterized to satisfy the conservation condition ∇H·f=0, embedding physical constraints directly into the loss function.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The independence module generalizes singular value decomposition (a linear eigenvalue method) to detect nonlinear functional dependence among learned conservation laws.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A combined conservation loss and independence loss with a penalty coefficient guides simultaneous learning of multiple independent conserved quantities.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The symbolic discovery front produces human-interpretable formulas for conservation laws, going beyond numerical black-box representations.

title: Theoretical tools for neutrino scattering: interplay between lattice QCD, EFTs, nuclear physics, phenomenology, and neutrino event generators
abstract: Maximizing the discovery potential of increasingly precise neutrino experiments will require an improved theoretical understanding of neutrino-nucleus cross sections over a wide range of energies. Low-energy interactions are needed to reconstruct the energies of astrophysical neutrinos from supernovae bursts and search for new physics using increasingly precise measurement of coherent elastic neutrino scattering. Higher-energy interactions involve a variety of reaction mechanisms including quasi-elastic scattering, resonance production, and deep inelastic scattering that must all be included to reliably predict cross sections for energies relevant to DUNE and other accelerator neutrino experiments. This white paper discusses the theoretical status, challenges, required resources, and path forward for achieving precise predictions of neutrino-nucleus scattering and emphasizes the need for a coordinated theoretical effort involved lattice QCD, nuclear effective theories, phenomenological models of the transition region, and event generators.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino-nucleus cross sections
conceptDetails.description: The central topic of the paper: achieving precise theoretical predictions for cross sections over a wide energy range spanning MeV supernova neutrinos to GeV accelerator neutrinos.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: Used to provide first-principles calculations of nucleon form factors, two-body currents, and hadronic inputs needed for precise neutrino-nucleus cross section predictions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Nuclear effective field theories (chiral EFT) provide the systematic framework for connecting QCD to nuclear structure and reaction mechanisms relevant to neutrino scattering.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear many-body theory
conceptDetails.description: Ab initio nuclear many-body methods (coupled cluster, no-core shell model, GFMC) are applied to compute neutrino response functions and inclusive cross sections for light and medium nuclei.

conceptDetails.name: coherent elastic neutrino scattering
conceptDetails.description: CEνNS is analyzed as a precision probe of nuclear structure and new physics, requiring accurate nuclear charge radii and weak form factors from lattice QCD and nuclear theory.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Quantum Monte Carlo and other stochastic many-body techniques are used to solve nuclear structure problems and evaluate neutrino-nucleus scattering observables.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: Precise theoretical cross sections are essential for interpreting measurements from current and future neutrino detectors including DUNE and coherent scattering experiments.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The paper examines Standard Model contributions to coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering and neutrino interaction cross sections across all energy regimes.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Precise theoretical predictions enable searches for beyond-Standard-Model physics including neutrino magnetic moments, CP violation, and non-standard interactions.

conceptDetails.name: scattering amplitudes
conceptDetails.description: Hadronic tensor and nucleon form factor calculations underpin the scattering amplitudes for quasi-elastic, resonance, and deep inelastic neutrino-nucleus processes.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Neutrino event generators implement theory calculations as simulation frameworks used directly by experiments to reconstruct neutrino energies and constrain oscillation parameters.

title: Unsupervised Semantic Segmentation by Distilling Feature Correspondences
abstract: Unsupervised semantic segmentation aims to discover and localize semantically meaningful categories within image corpora without any form of annotation. To solve this task, algorithms must produce features for every pixel that are both semantically meaningful and compact enough to form distinct clusters. Unlike previous works which achieve this with a single end-to-end framework, we propose to separate feature learning from cluster compactification. Empirically, we show that current unsupervised feature learning frameworks already generate dense features whose correlations are semantically consistent. This observation motivates us to design STEGO ($\textbf{S}$elf-supervised $\textbf{T}$ransformer with $\textbf{E}$nergy-based $\textbf{G}$raph $\textbf{O}$ptimization), a novel framework that distills unsupervised features into high-quality discrete semantic labels. At the core of STEGO is a novel contrastive loss function that encourages features to form compact clusters while preserving their relationships across the corpora. STEGO yields a significant improvement over the prior state of the art, on both the CocoStuff ($\textbf{+14 mIoU}$) and Cityscapes ($\textbf{+9 mIoU}$) semantic segmentation challenges.

conceptDetails.name: unsupervised semantic segmentation
conceptDetails.description: The central task of the paper — discovering and localizing semantic categories in images without any human annotation, substantially advanced by STEGO.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: STEGO builds on pretrained self-supervised visual features (e.g., DINO) rather than learning from scratch, making self-supervised learning the foundational input to the framework.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: STEGO introduces a novel contrastive loss that encourages pixel features to form compact clusters while preserving semantic relationships across the image corpus.

conceptDetails.name: feature distillation
conceptDetails.description: STEGO's key mechanism of distilling rich but continuous unsupervised features into high-quality discrete semantic labels via correspondence-preserving optimization.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: The paper separates feature learning from cluster compactification, using clustering to convert continuous dense features into discrete semantic labels.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: STEGO uses Vision Transformers (ViT) as the backbone architecture, leveraging their self-attention mechanisms to generate spatially dense feature maps.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies the semantic consistency of unsupervised dense representations and distills them into structured semantic clusters for segmentation.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Dense per-pixel feature extraction from pretrained DINO models is central to STEGO's pipeline, providing semantically consistent correlations that are then refined.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A core contribution is the novel contrastive loss function that balances cluster compactness with preservation of cross-image feature relationships.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: STEGO operates on dense feature embeddings and distills them into compact, discrete embeddings that align with semantic categories.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Self-attention in the ViT backbone captures long-range pixel dependencies, enabling semantically consistent feature correspondences across images.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: STEGO transfers pretrained unsupervised features from DINO to the segmentation task, demonstrating that pretrained representations generalize without labeled data.

title: Creating Simple, Interpretable Anomaly Detectors for New Physics in Jet Substructure
abstract: Anomaly detection with convolutional autoencoders is a popular method to search for new physics in a model-agnostic manner. These techniques are powerful, but they are still a "black box," since we do not know what high-level physical observables determine how anomalous an event is. To address this, we adapt a recently proposed technique by Faucett et al., which maps out the physical observables learned by a neural network classifier, to the case of anomaly detection. We propose two different strategies that use a small number of high-level observables to mimic the decisions made by the autoencoder on background events, one designed to directly learn the output of the autoencoder, and the other designed to learn the difference between the autoencoder's outputs on a pair of events. Despite the underlying differences in their approach, we find that both strategies have similar ordering performance as the autoencoder and independently use the same six high-level observables. From there, we compare the performance of these networks as anomaly detectors. We find that both strategies perform similarly to the autoencoder across a variety of signals, giving a nontrivial demonstration that learning to order background events transfers to ordering a variety of signal events.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central method of the paper, using unsupervised neural networks to identify anomalous jet events as potential signals of new physics without relying on a specific signal model.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional autoencoder trained on background jet images serves as the 'black box' target anomaly detector whose decisions the interpretable mimicker networks are designed to replicate.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The primary contribution of the paper is creating interpretable anomaly detectors that use a small set of human-understandable high-level observables to mimic complex autoencoder decisions.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The application domain is boosted jet substructure at the LHC, where anomaly detection is applied to identify jets inconsistent with QCD background.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The physics motivation is model-agnostic searches for beyond-Standard-Model signals in LHC collision data without requiring a specific signal hypothesis.

conceptDetails.name: knowledge distillation
conceptDetails.description: The mimicking framework distills the implicit decision logic of a complex convolutional autoencoder into simpler, interpretable networks trained on high-level observables.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional neural networks form the architecture of both the autoencoder target and the mimicker networks operating on jet images.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Energy Flow Polynomials serve as the pool of high-level observables from which an iterative procedure selects the most informative features to explain the autoencoder's decisions.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The anomaly detection framework relies on characterizing background events so that signal events appear out-of-distribution and are flagged as anomalous.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The study uses Monte Carlo simulated LHC dijet events and various BSM signal samples (W, top, Higgs jets) as the experimental context.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The Paired Neural Network mimicker frames anomaly ordering as a pairwise classification task, learning which of two events is less anomalous according to the autoencoder.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: A key finding is that networks trained to order background events successfully transfer their ordering decisions to diverse signal events without signal supervision.

title: Flow-based density of states for complex actions
abstract: Emerging sampling algorithms based on normalizing flows have the potential to solve ergodicity problems in lattice calculations. Furthermore, it has been noted that flows can be used to compute thermodynamic quantities which are difficult to access with traditional methods. This suggests that they are also applicable to the density-of-states approach to complex action problems. In particular, flow-based sampling may be used to compute the density directly, in contradistinction to the conventional strategy of reconstructing it via measuring and integrating the derivative of its logarithm. By circumventing this procedure, the accumulation of errors from the numerical integration is avoided completely and the overall normalization factor can be determined explicitly. In this proof-of-principle study, we demonstrate our method in the context of two-component scalar field theory where the $O(2)$ symmetry is explicitly broken by an imaginary external field. First, we concentrate on the zero-dimensional case which can be solved exactly. We show that with our method, the Lee-Yang zeroes of the associated partition function can be successfully located. Subsequently, we confirm that the flow-based approach correctly reproduces the density computed with conventional methods in one- and two-dimensional models.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core generative model used to directly sample and evaluate the density of states for lattice field theories with complex actions.

conceptDetails.name: density of states
conceptDetails.description: The core physical quantity being computed: the distribution of field configurations over hypersurfaces of constant imaginary-action-generating observable, enabling one-dimensional reweighted integration.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: using flow-based models to compute the density of states directly rather than reconstructing it via numerical integration of its logarithmic derivative.

conceptDetails.name: sign problem
conceptDetails.description: The fundamental obstacle in lattice field theory with complex actions—where standard importance sampling fails—that the density-of-states approach is designed to circumvent.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The conventional MCMC-based approach that the flow-based method is compared against and shown to improve upon by avoiding error accumulation from numerical integration.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
