conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework in which the density-of-states approach is formulated, specifically applied to scalar φ⁴ field theory on the lattice.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics motivation: the sign problem in lattice QCD at finite chemical potential is the archetypal complex action problem this method targets.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses explicit breaking of the O(2) internal symmetry via an imaginary external field as the test case for demonstrating the flow-based density-of-states method.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Lee-Yang zeros of the partition function, which signal phase transitions, are successfully located using the proposed flow-based approach.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows provide explicit, tractable probability densities via the change-of-variables formula, enabling direct density evaluation without numerical reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: lee-yang zeros
conceptDetails.description: Complex zeros of the partition function whose location in the complex external-field plane signals phase transitions; demonstrated to be correctly reproducible with the proposed method.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Stochastic gradient descent and MCMC dynamics form the computational backbone for both training the flows and benchmarking against conventional methods.

title: Fault-Tolerant Neural Networks from Biological Error Correction Codes
abstract: It has been an open question in deep learning if fault-tolerant computation is possible: can arbitrarily reliable computation be achieved using only unreliable neurons? In the grid cells of the mammalian cortex, analog error correction codes have been observed to protect states against neural spiking noise, but their role in information processing is unclear. Here, we use these biological error correction codes to develop a universal fault-tolerant neural network that achieves reliable computation if the faultiness of each neuron lies below a sharp threshold; remarkably, we find that noisy biological neurons fall below this threshold. The discovery of a phase transition from faulty to fault-tolerant neural computation suggests a mechanism for reliable computation in the cortex and opens a path towards understanding noisy analog systems relevant to artificial intelligence and neuromorphic computing.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is proving that neural networks can achieve arbitrarily reliable computation under noise below a sharp threshold, a formal definition of fault-tolerance analogous to classical and quantum results.

conceptDetails.name: fault-tolerant neural computation
conceptDetails.description: Novel formal framework extending classical Boolean fault-tolerance to analog neural networks with continuous noise, proved constructively via grid-code-based logical neurons.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: A sharp phase transition is discovered separating the faulty from fault-tolerant regimes of neural computation, with biological neurons shown to lie within the fault-tolerant phase.

conceptDetails.name: grid code error correction
conceptDetails.description: Biologically-observed analog error correction codes from mammalian grid cells are adapted to encode and protect continuous neural activations from Gaussian noise during computation.

conceptDetails.name: analog fault tolerance
conceptDetails.description: A generalization of digital fault-tolerance to continuous-valued analog computation, requiring novel error correction techniques beyond majority voting for handling additive noise.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The fault-tolerant neural network construction is explicitly inspired by and analogous to quantum fault-tolerance frameworks, including concatenated error correction and threshold theorems.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Synaptic failure (discrete dropout) and additive Gaussian noise are the two biologically-motivated noise models central to the fault-tolerance analysis.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Noise thresholds p₀ and σ₀ are rigorously derived, below which reliable computation is provably achievable regardless of circuit depth.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Majority voting and repetition codes are used as the primary mechanism for constructing logical neurons from multiple unreliable physical neurons.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The fault-tolerant construction achieves O(N polylog(N/ε)) overhead, demonstrating that reliable computation scales efficiently with circuit size.

title: Flow-based sampling in the lattice Schwinger model at criticality
abstract: Recent results suggest that flow-based algorithms may provide efficient sampling of field distributions for lattice field theory applications, such as studies of quantum chromodynamics and the Schwinger model. In this work, we provide a numerical demonstration of robust flow-based sampling in the Schwinger model at the critical value of the fermion mass. In contrast, at the same parameters, conventional methods fail to sample all parts of configuration space, leading to severely underestimated uncertainties.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core algorithmic contribution: trainable diffeomorphic transformations that map a base distribution to approximate the target lattice field distribution, enabling efficient independent sampling.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The physical framework under study; the Schwinger model is discretized on a Euclidean spacetime lattice as a U(1) gauge theory, motivating the entire computational approach.

conceptDetails.name: topological freezing
conceptDetails.description: The critical failure mode of HMC where large potential barriers between topological sectors prevent ergodic sampling at criticality, which flow-based sampling is shown to circumvent.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Both the baseline Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) and the independence Metropolis correction applied to flow samples are central to the paper's comparison and correctness guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Gauge-equivariant coupling layers are used to construct flows that respect the gauge symmetry of the target distribution by design, using Wilson loops as gauge-invariant inputs.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper specifically demonstrates flow-based sampling at the critical fermion mass—a phase transition point—where conventional methods fail due to diverging autocorrelations.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Gauge invariance is enforced architecturally in the flow model rather than learned, ensuring the model density respects the physical symmetries of the Schwinger model.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The path-integral formulation of QFT provides the theoretical foundation; the paper addresses ab-initio nonperturbative calculations in the QFT language.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The normalizing flow is trained to estimate the target Boltzmann distribution of lattice gauge configurations by minimizing KL divergence from the model density to the physical distribution.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Spontaneous chiral symmetry breaking and the chiral condensate are the key physical observables used to validate sampling accuracy and demonstrate HMC bias at criticality.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary long-term motivation: flow-based methods demonstrated here for the Schwinger model are intended to eventually address topological freezing in full QCD calculations.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Autocorrelations and ergodicity violations in Markov Chain Monte Carlo are analyzed quantitatively, connecting the physical criticality problem to the theory of stochastic sampling.

title: Identifying equivalent Calabi--Yau topologies: A discrete challenge from math and physics for machine learning
abstract: We review briefly the characteristic topological data of Calabi--Yau threefolds and focus on the question of when two threefolds are equivalent through related topological data. This provides an interesting test case for machine learning methodology in discrete mathematics problems motivated by physics.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau topology
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau threefolds are the central mathematical objects, characterized by Hodge numbers, triple intersection numbers, and Pontryagin class as discrete topological invariants.

conceptDetails.name: topological equivalence
conceptDetails.description: The core open problem is determining when two sets of discrete CY data describe the same manifold up to a linear change of cohomology basis.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau threefolds are studied as compactification geometries for superstring theory, providing the physical motivation for classifying their topological types.

conceptDetails.name: triple intersection numbers
conceptDetails.description: The 3-index symmetric integer tensor C_{ijk} encoding intersection data of divisors is the primary discrete input on which equivalence detection is performed.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The central ML task is classifying whether two sets of discrete topological data (triple intersection numbers) describe equivalent Calabi-Yau geometries.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Equivalence of Calabi-Yau topologies is defined via integer basis changes in SL(N,Z), making group-theoretic invariants the key discriminating tool.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: ML methods are applied to discrete geometric structures (symmetric 3-index tensors) to detect topological equivalence without known analytic algorithms.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A key ambition is for ML to reveal new analytic invariants of triple intersection numbers, making the learned representations scientifically interpretable.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper seeks learned representations of intersection-number tensors that are invariant under SL(N,Z) basis changes, analogous to Specht-theorem invariants for matrices.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Kernel-based ML approaches are among the methods considered for detecting equivalence of discrete tensor data.

title: Topogivity: A Machine-Learned Chemical Rule for Discovering Topological Materials
abstract: Topological materials present unconventional electronic properties that make them attractive for both basic science and next-generation technological applications. The majority of currently known topological materials have been discovered using methods that involve symmetry-based analysis of the quantum wavefunction. Here we use machine learning to develop a simple-to-use heuristic chemical rule that diagnoses with a high accuracy whether a material is topological using only its chemical formula. This heuristic rule is based on a notion that we term topogivity, a machine-learned numerical value for each element that loosely captures its tendency to form topological materials. We next implement a high-throughput procedure for discovering topological materials based on the heuristic topogivity-rule prediction followed by ab initio validation. This way, we discover new topological materials that are not diagnosable using symmetry indicators, including several that may be promising for experimental observation.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is a high-throughput ML-driven pipeline for discovering new topological materials via topogivity screening followed by ab initio validation.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The topogivity rule frames topological material identification as a binary classification problem (topological vs. trivial) solved via interpretable ML.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A central goal is developing an interpretable, human-readable chemical heuristic rather than a black-box ML model.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The high-throughput screening pipeline automates large-scale candidate generation and validation for topological materials.

conceptDetails.name: topological invariants
conceptDetails.description: Machine-learned topogivity values serve as a proxy for wavefunction-based topological invariants that are expensive to compute directly.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: Crystal symmetry and chemical formula composition are the input features used to train and apply the topogivity rule.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Topogivity values are learned per-element features that encode each element's propensity to form topological materials.

conceptDetails.name: topological semimetals
conceptDetails.description: A primary target material class in the paper, including Weyl semimetals not diagnosable by standard symmetry indicators.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The topogivity rule acts as a fast surrogate for DFT-based topological diagnosis, enabling large-scale screening before expensive ab initio validation.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The topogivity heuristic is a sparse, low-parameter model (one scalar per element) intentionally chosen over complex black-box alternatives.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper contrasts its method with symmetry-indicator approaches and specifically targets non-symmetry-diagnosable topological materials.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Topological phase distinctions (insulator vs. semimetal vs. trivial) underlie the classification labels used in training.

title: Finite-Volume Pionless Effective Field Theory for Few-Nucleon Systems with Differentiable Programming
abstract: Finite-volume pionless effective field theory provides an efficient framework for the extrapolation of nuclear spectra and matrix elements calculated at finite volume in lattice QCD to infinite volume, and to nuclei with larger atomic number. In this work, it is demonstrated how this framework may be implemented via a set of correlated Gaussian wavefunctions optimised using differentiable programming and via solution of a generalised eigenvalue problem. This approach is shown to be significantly more efficient than a stochastic implementation of the variational method based on the same form of correlated Gaussian wavefunctions, yielding comparably accurate representations of the ground-state wavefunctions with an order of magnitude fewer terms. The efficiency of representation allows such calculations to be extended to larger systems than in previous work. The method is demonstrated through calculations of the binding energies of nuclei with atomic number $A\in\{2,3,4\}$ in finite volume, matched to lattice QCD calculations at quark masses corresponding to $m_π=806$ MeV, and infinite-volume effective field theory calculations of $A\in\{2,3,4,5,6\}$ systems based on this matching.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Pionless EFT provides the theoretical framework for describing low-energy nucleon interactions and bridging finite-volume lattice QCD calculations to infinite-volume nuclear physics.

conceptDetails.name: variational wavefunction optimization
conceptDetails.description: Correlated Gaussian wavefunctions are optimized via differentiable programming to represent nuclear ground states far more efficiently than stochastic variational methods.

conceptDetails.name: finite-volume eft
conceptDetails.description: Novel framework combining pionless EFT with periodic finite-volume regulators to match lattice QCD spectra and extrapolate to infinite volume and larger nuclei.

conceptDetails.name: differentiable programming
conceptDetails.description: Gradient-based optimization of correlated Gaussian wavefunction parameters replaces stochastic search, yielding order-of-magnitude fewer terms for equivalent accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: Finite-volume LQCD calculations at unphysical quark masses (m_π=806 MeV) provide the input data that the EFT framework is matched to and extrapolated from.

conceptDetails.name: correlated gaussian wavefunctions
conceptDetails.description: Trial wavefunctions composed of shifted correlated Gaussian functions serve as the variational ansatz for representing few-nucleon ground states.

conceptDetails.name: eigenvalue decomposition
conceptDetails.description: A generalized eigenvalue problem (GEVP) is solved to optimally combine multiple sets of trial wavefunctions and improve energy bound estimates.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Differentiable programming is used to optimize wavefunction parameters through gradient-based methods analogous to neural network training, enabling efficient nuclear structure calculations.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The underlying QCD framework and its non-perturbative regime motivate the need for EFT approaches to nuclear structure calculations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The stochastic variational method (SVM) serves as the baseline comparison, using stochastic selection of wavefunction terms that the differentiable programming approach supersedes.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The optimized EFT wavefunctions serve as efficient surrogate representations enabling predictions for A≤6 nuclear systems beyond direct LQCD reach.

title: Photometry on Structured Backgrounds: Local Pixelwise Infilling by Regression
abstract: Photometric pipelines struggle to estimate both the flux and flux uncertainty for stars in the presence of structured backgrounds such as filaments or clouds. However, it is exactly stars in these complex regions that are critical to understanding star formation and the structure of the interstellar medium. We develop a method, similar to Gaussian process regression, which we term local pixelwise infilling (LPI). Using a local covariance estimate, we predict the background behind each star and the uncertainty on that prediction in order to improve estimates of flux and flux uncertainty. We show the validity of our model on synthetic data and real dust fields. We further demonstrate that the method is stable even in the crowded field limit. While we focus on optical-IR photometry, this method is not restricted to those wavelengths. We apply this technique to the 34 billion detections in the second data release of the Dark Energy Camera Plane Survey (DECaPS2). In addition to removing many $>3σ$ outliers and improving uncertainty estimates by a factor of $\sim 2-3$ on nebulous fields, we also show that our method is well-behaved on uncrowded fields. The entirely post-processing nature of our implementation of LPI photometry allows it to easily improve the flux and flux uncertainty estimates of past as well as future surveys.

conceptDetails.name: photometric infilling
conceptDetails.description: Novel technique introduced in this paper: predicting the background pixel values beneath a stellar source using local spatial statistics to decouple stellar flux from structured backgrounds.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core technique (LPI) is a pixelwise regression that predicts background flux behind each star from neighboring pixel values using a local covariance model.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A central contribution is providing principled uncertainty estimates on the predicted background, which propagates into improved stellar flux uncertainty estimates by a factor of 2-3.

conceptDetails.name: structured background subtraction
conceptDetails.description: The domain problem addressed: removing the contribution of filamentary dust and gas structures from stellar photometry near the Galactic plane.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: LPI uses a nonparametric local covariance (kernel) estimate rather than a parametric kernel, distinguishing it from standard Gaussian process regression while sharing its mathematical foundations.

conceptDetails.name: local covariance estimation
conceptDetails.description: Novel nonparametric approach introduced here that estimates covariance from nearby pixels rather than fitting a global parametric kernel, enabling flexible background modeling.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: The paper extensively validates that the reported flux uncertainties are well-calibrated on both nebulous and uncrowded fields, removing >3σ outliers introduced by structured backgrounds.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The LPI method frames background prediction as Bayesian conditional inference over pixel values, computing a posterior mean and variance for the hidden background pixels.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian process regression, the mathematical foundation for LPI, is a stochastic process model used to characterize spatial covariance of the astronomical background.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: The authors validate LPI on synthetic dust fields and real DECaPS2 data, testing stability in the crowded-field limit and against known photometric outlier rates.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the true stellar flux from images where background nebulosity is entangled with the point-source signal is an inverse problem that LPI addresses through background interpolation.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The method improves detection and flux measurement of stars embedded in complex nebular environments, directly affecting the completeness and reliability of astronomical catalogs.

title: Cracking the Quantum Scaling Limit with Machine Learned Electron Densities
abstract: A long-standing goal of science is to accurately solve the Schrödinger equation for large molecular systems. The poor scaling of current quantum chemistry algorithms on classical computers imposes an effective limit of about a few dozen atoms for which we can calculate molecular electronic structure. We present a machine learning (ML) method to break through this scaling limit and make quantum chemistry calculations of very large systems possible. We show that Euclidean Neural Networks can be trained to predict the electron density with high fidelity from limited data. Learning the electron density allows us to train a machine learning model on small systems and make accurate predictions on large ones. We show that this ML electron density model can break through the quantum scaling limit and calculate the electron density of systems of thousands of atoms with quantum accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses Euclidean Neural Networks (e3nn) that encode rotational and translational equivariance, which is shown to be essential for accurately predicting electron density coefficients expressed as geometric tensors.

conceptDetails.name: electron density prediction
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept central to this paper: using ML to predict the quantum mechanical electron density represented in an atom-centered Gaussian basis set as the primary output quantity.

conceptDetails.name: quantum scaling limit
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept framing the paper's central challenge: the effective upper bound on tractable quantum chemistry calculations imposed by the steep polynomial scaling of algorithms like coupled cluster.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The e3nn framework leverages geometric deep learning principles, encoding 3D Euclidean symmetries including equivariance to rotations, which reduces training data requirements by orders of magnitude.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central contribution is breaking the polynomial scaling bottleneck of quantum chemistry, achieving near-linear scaling for electron density predictions on thousands of atoms.

conceptDetails.name: nearsightedness of electronic matter
conceptDetails.description: A novel concept exploited by the model: the physical principle that electron correlation has finite range, enabling a model trained on small clusters to generalize to arbitrarily large systems.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates that preserving rotational equivariance (rather than mere invariance) is critical for correctly predicting electron density basis function coefficients that transform as geometric tensors.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The ML model is trained on small molecular clusters and transfers accurately to systems thousands of atoms in size, exploiting the locality of electron correlation.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The ML electron density model serves as a surrogate for expensive quantum chemistry calculations, enabling predictions on systems far beyond the reach of conventional methods.

conceptDetails.name: molecular dynamics
conceptDetails.description: The electron density predictions enable quantum-accurate force calculations for large molecular systems relevant to condensed matter and biological simulations.

title: Constraining the Time of Gravitational Wave Emission from Core-Collapse Supernovae
abstract: The advent of sensitive gravitational wave (GW) detectors, coupled with wide-field, high cadence optical time-domain surveys, raises the possibility of the first joint GW-electromagnetic (EM) detections of core-collapse supernovae (CCSNe). For targeted searches of GWs from CCSNe optical observations can be used to increase the sensitivity of the search by restricting the relevant time interval, defined here as the GW search window (GSW). The extent of the GSW is a critical factor in determining the achievable false alarm probability (FAP) for a triggered CCSN search. The ability to constrain the GSW from optical observations depends on how early a CCSN is detected, as well as the ability to model the early optical emission. Here we present several approaches to constrain the GSW, ranging in complexity from model-independent analytical fits of the early light curve, model-dependent fits of the rising or entire light curve, and a new data-driven approach using existing well-sampled CCSN light curves from {\it Kepler} and the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS). We use these approaches to determine the time of core-collapse and its associated uncertainty (i.e., the GSW). We apply our methods to two Type II SNe that occurred during LIGO/Virgo Observing Run 3: SN\,2019fcn and SN\,2019ejj (both in the same galaxy at $d=15.7$ Mpc). Our approach shortens the duration of the GSW and improves the robustness of the GSW compared to techniques used in past GW CCSN searches.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central goal is constraining the search window for detecting gravitational wave emission from core-collapse supernovae using optical observations.

conceptDetails.name: light curve fitting
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: fitting early-time optical light curves using model-independent, model-dependent, and data-driven methods to precisely time the explosion.

conceptDetails.name: shock breakout timing
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: estimating the moment of shock breakout from the progenitor envelope to anchor the gravitational wave search window.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Quantifying the uncertainty on the time of core-collapse is the key output, directly determining the width of the GW search window.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper focuses specifically on Type II core-collapse supernovae (SN 2019fcn and SN 2019ejj) and their optical properties for GW search targeting.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The false alarm probability framework for triggered GW searches is central to evaluating how well the constrained search window improves detection confidence.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian parameter estimation methods are used to fit light curve models and derive posterior distributions on the time of shock breakout.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Analytical and model-dependent curve fitting (regression) of early supernova light curves is used to estimate shock breakout time.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: A data-driven approach using Kepler and TESS light curve templates serves as an empirical surrogate for analytical shock breakout models.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Theoretical core-collapse simulations are used to model the delay between core-collapse and shock breakout onset.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Progenitor star properties and pre-explosion stellar structure models inform the expected time delay from core-collapse to shock breakout.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: Neutrino emission is discussed alongside gravitational waves as a complementary probe of the core-collapse process.

title: Invariance Through Latent Alignment
abstract: A robot's deployment environment often involves perceptual changes that differ from what it has experienced during training. Standard practices such as data augmentation attempt to bridge this gap by augmenting source images in an effort to extend the support of the training distribution to better cover what the agent might experience at test time. In many cases, however, it is impossible to know test-time distribution-shift a priori, making these schemes infeasible. In this paper, we introduce a general approach, called Invariance Through Latent Alignment (ILA), that improves the test-time performance of a visuomotor control policy in deployment environments with unknown perceptual variations. ILA performs unsupervised adaptation at deployment-time by matching the distribution of latent features on the target domain to the agent's prior experience, without relying on paired data. Although simple, we show that this idea leads to surprising improvements on a variety of challenging adaptation scenarios, including changes in lighting conditions, the content in the scene, and camera poses. We present results on calibrated control benchmarks in simulation -- the distractor control suite -- and a physical robot under a sim-to-real setup.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: ILA operates by aligning latent feature distributions between source and target domains, making the learned representations invariant to unknown perceptual shifts.

conceptDetails.name: latent distribution matching
conceptDetails.description: The novel core contribution: matching the distribution of encoder features on the target domain to the known source-domain prior without paired supervision.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The core goal of ILA is to produce policies robust to unknown perceptual variations such as lighting changes, scene content, and camera pose shifts.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets visuomotor control policies trained via RL and improves their deployment-time performance without access to reward signals.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: ILA addresses sim-to-real and cross-domain transfer by adapting a pre-trained policy encoder to the target domain at deployment time.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The latent embedding space of the policy encoder is the medium through which domain alignment is performed, making embedding geometry central to the method.

conceptDetails.name: sim-to-real transfer
conceptDetails.description: A primary application domain—policies trained in simulation are deployed on physical robots with ILA bridging the perceptual sim-to-real gap.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: ILA uses an adversarial distribution-matching objective to align target-domain latent features to the source-domain prior without paired data.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Deployment-time adaptation is performed without any labels or reward supervision, relying solely on the structure of the latent feature distribution.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: ILA targets the out-of-distribution generalization problem where the target domain falls outside the training distribution and cannot be anticipated.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: ILA fine-tunes only the encoder at test time to align latent distributions while keeping the task-specific policy head frozen.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Standard data augmentation is analyzed as a baseline and shown to be insufficient when test-time distribution shift is unknown a priori.

title: Impact of Massive Binary Star and Cosmic Evolution on Gravitational Wave Observations II: Double Compact Object Rates and Properties
abstract: Making the most of the rapidly increasing population of gravitational-wave detections of black hole (BH) and neutron star (NS) mergers requires comparing observations with population synthesis predictions. In this work we investigate the combined impact from the key uncertainties in population synthesis modelling of the isolated binary evolution channel: the physical processes in massive binary-star evolution and the star formation history as a function of metallicity, $Z$, and redshift $z, \mathcal{S}(Z,z)$. Considering these uncertainties we create 560 different publicly available model realizations and calculate the rate and distribution characteristics of detectable BHBH, BHNS, and NSNS mergers. We find that our stellar evolution and $\mathcal{S}(Z,z)$ variations can impact the predicted intrinsic and detectable merger rates by factors $10^2$-$10^4$. We find that BHBH rates are dominantly impacted by $\mathcal{S}(Z,z)$ variations, NSNS rates by stellar evolution variations and BHNS rates by both. We then consider the combined impact from all uncertainties considered in this work on the detectable mass distribution shapes (chirp mass, individual masses and mass ratio). We find that the BHNS mass distributions are predominantly impacted by massive binary-star evolution changes. For BHBH and NSNS we find that both uncertainties are important. We also find that the shape of the delay time and birth metallicity distributions are typically dominated by the choice of $\mathcal{S}(Z,z)$ for BHBH, BHNS and NSNS. We identify several examples of robust features in the mass distributions predicted by all 560 models, such that we expect more than 95% of BHBH detections to contain a BH $\gtrsim 8\,\rm{M}_{\odot}$ and have mass ratios $\lesssim 4$. Our work demonstrates that it is essential to consider a wide range of allowed models to study double compact object merger rates and properties.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes detectable gravitational-wave signals from merging double compact objects (BHBH, BHNS, NSNS) as the primary observational output being modeled.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Massive binary star evolution processes (wind mass loss, mass transfer, common envelope episodes, supernovae) are the core physical uncertainties being varied across 560 model realizations.

conceptDetails.name: binary population synthesis
conceptDetails.description: The core computational methodology: rapid binary stellar evolution synthesis simulations coupled with cosmic star formation prescriptions to predict observable DCO merger populations.

conceptDetails.name: compact object mergers
conceptDetails.description: BHBH, BHNS, and NSNS merger rates and mass distributions are the primary predicted observables studied across all model variations.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Central to the paper's contribution: systematically quantifying how uncertainties in binary stellar evolution and cosmic star formation history propagate to predicted merger rates and mass distributions.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The metallicity-dependent star formation rate density S(Z,z) as a function of redshift represents the cosmic evolution component whose uncertainty is varied across models.

conceptDetails.name: chirp mass distribution
conceptDetails.description: The detectable chirp mass, individual mass, and mass ratio distributions are analyzed as diagnostic observables sensitive to different uncertainty sources.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Population synthesis simulations use Monte Carlo sampling to generate large numbers of binary star system realizations and propagate uncertainties.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: 560 distinct model realizations are combined to characterize the full uncertainty envelope in predicted DCO rates and mass distributions.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The methodology of comparing population synthesis model outputs directly against observational data to infer constraints on physical processes.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework for comparing predicted DCO merger populations against observed gravitational-wave detections to constrain stellar evolution models.

title: SymmetryGAN: Symmetry Discovery with Deep Learning
abstract: What are the symmetries of a dataset? Whereas the symmetries of an individual data element can be characterized by its invariance under various transformations, the symmetries of an ensemble of data elements are ambiguous due to Jacobian factors introduced while changing coordinates. In this paper, we provide a rigorous statistical definition of the symmetries of a dataset, which involves inertial reference densities, in analogy to inertial frames in classical mechanics. We then propose SymmetryGAN as a novel and powerful approach to automatically discover symmetries using a deep learning method based on generative adversarial networks (GANs). When applied to Gaussian examples, SymmetryGAN shows excellent empirical performance, in agreement with expectations from the analytic loss landscape. SymmetryGAN is then applied to simulated dijet events from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) to demonstrate the potential utility of this method in high energy collider physics applications. Going beyond symmetry discovery, we consider procedures to infer the underlying symmetry group from empirical data.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: The core architecture of SymmetryGAN, where a generator learns symmetry transformations and a discriminator distinguishes the transformed dataset from the original.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry discovery
conceptDetails.description: The central novel task of this paper: automatically identifying the symmetries of a dataset as a statistical distribution, distinct from symmetries of individual data elements.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical framework underlying symmetry characterization, including SO(2), SO(4), and discrete subgroups that SymmetryGAN is designed to discover and infer.

conceptDetails.name: inertial reference density
conceptDetails.description: A novel statistical concept introduced to resolve ambiguity in dataset symmetries due to Jacobian factors, analogous to inertial frames in classical mechanics.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: SymmetryGAN automates the discovery of unknown or emergent symmetries in data without requiring prior knowledge of the symmetry group.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Used in the statistical framework to define and detect when a transformation preserves the probability density of a dataset.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: SymmetryGAN is applied to simulated dijet events from the LHC to demonstrate practical utility in high energy physics.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The GAN discriminator implicitly estimates likelihood ratios between the transformed and reference datasets, forming the training signal for symmetry detection.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: A key downstream motivation for symmetry discovery: once a symmetry is found, it can be used to augment datasets for machine learning tasks.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Discussed as related work where neural architectures are constructed to respect known symmetries; SymmetryGAN targets the inverse problem of discovering those symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The statistical approach connects to simulation-based inference in that the inertial reference density plays the role of a known reference distribution for comparison.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: A related methodology mentioned where classifiers unable to distinguish a dataset from its transformed counterpart provide evidence for symmetry.

title: Neural Descriptor Fields: SE(3)-Equivariant Object Representations for Manipulation
abstract: We present Neural Descriptor Fields (NDFs), an object representation that encodes both points and relative poses between an object and a target (such as a robot gripper or a rack used for hanging) via category-level descriptors. We employ this representation for object manipulation, where given a task demonstration, we want to repeat the same task on a new object instance from the same category. We propose to achieve this objective by searching (via optimization) for the pose whose descriptor matches that observed in the demonstration. NDFs are conveniently trained in a self-supervised fashion via a 3D auto-encoding task that does not rely on expert-labeled keypoints. Further, NDFs are SE(3)-equivariant, guaranteeing performance that generalizes across all possible 3D object translations and rotations. We demonstrate learning of manipulation tasks from few (5-10) demonstrations both in simulation and on a real robot. Our performance generalizes across both object instances and 6-DoF object poses, and significantly outperforms a recent baseline that relies on 2D descriptors. Project website: https://yilundu.github.io/ndf/.

conceptDetails.name: neural descriptor fields
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution: continuous functions mapping 3D spatial coordinates to SE(3)-equivariant spatial descriptors that encode category-level object structure for manipulation.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: NDFs are designed to be SE(3)-equivariant, guaranteeing that object representations transform consistently under all 3D translations and rotations, enabling generalization to unseen object configurations.

conceptDetails.name: geometric deep learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly leverages recent progress in geometric deep learning to construct SE(3)-equivariant pose descriptor fields for 3D object manipulation.

conceptDetails.name: pose descriptor fields
conceptDetails.description: A novel extension of point descriptors to SE(3) poses via rigid query-point sets, enabling optimization-based pose estimation from few demonstrations.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: NDFs learn category-level spatial descriptors that encode both object geometry and relative poses between objects and manipulation targets.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: NDFs are trained self-supervised via a 3D auto-encoding reconstruction task, requiring no expert-labeled keypoints or task-specific annotations.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The SE(3)-equivariant architecture mathematically guarantees performance that is invariant to all possible 3D object translations and rotations.

conceptDetails.name: few-shot manipulation
conceptDetails.description: The system learns to generalize pick-and-place tasks across object instances and 6-DoF configurations from only 5-10 task demonstrations.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Spatial descriptors serve as embeddings mapping 3D coordinates to a feature space that is consistent across different object instances within a category.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A 3D auto-encoding surrogate task is used to train the NDF representations in a self-supervised manner without requiring task-specific labels.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The SE(3) special Euclidean group formalizes the 3D rigid body transformations (rotations and translations) that NDFs are designed to be equivariant with respect to.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Learned NDF representations transfer manipulation skills from a small set of demonstrations to novel object instances and unseen 6-DoF poses.

title: Building Quantum Field Theories Out of Neurons
abstract: An approach to field theory is studied in which fields are comprised of $N$ constituent random neurons. Gaussian theories arise in the infinite-$N$ limit when neurons are independently distributed, via the Central Limit Theorem, while interactions arise due to finite-$N$ effects or non-independently distributed neurons. Euclidean-invariant ensembles of neurons are engineered, with tunable two-point function, yielding families of Euclidean-invariant field theories. Some Gaussian, Euclidean invariant theories are reflection positive, which allows for analytic continuation to a Lorentz-invariant quantum field theory. Examples are presented that yield dual theories at infinite-$N$, but have different symmetries at finite-$N$. Landscapes of classical field configurations are determined by local maxima of parameter distributions. Predictions arise from mixed field-neuron correlators. Near-Gaussianity is exhibited at large-$N$, potentially explaining a feature of field theories in Nature.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper constructs Lorentz-invariant quantum field theories directly from ensembles of random neurons, establishing a non-Lagrangian route to QFT via neural network architectures satisfying the Osterwalder-Schrader axioms.

conceptDetails.name: neural network qft
conceptDetails.description: A novel framework (NN-QFT) in which quantum field theories are defined by choosing neuron architectures whose correlation functions satisfy the Osterwalder-Schrader axioms, replacing the role of an action.

conceptDetails.name: reflection positivity
conceptDetails.description: A key Osterwalder-Schrader axiom requiring the two-point function's power spectrum to be non-negative, which the paper uses to certify that Gaussian NN-QFTs admit a valid analytic continuation to Lorentzian signature.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Fields are built from random neurons whose parameter distributions define the stochastic ensemble, with correlation functions computed via parameter-space integration rather than path integrals.

conceptDetails.name: large-n duality
conceptDetails.description: Certain neuron architectures yield distinct theories at finite-N that become equivalent (dual) in the infinite-N Gaussian limit, constituting a new mechanism for large-N duality in field theory.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Dual theories that are equivalent at infinite-N develop different symmetries at finite-N, illustrating how finite-width effects break symmetries present in the Gaussian limit.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Euclidean and Lorentz symmetry groups are central to the construction; neuron architectures are engineered so that parameter densities and measures are invariant under the relevant transformation groups.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The Neural Network Gaussian Process (NNGP) correspondence — relating infinite-width networks to Gaussian processes and kernel methods — provides the theoretical foundation that this paper extends into QFT territory.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: The large-N expansion organizes interactions as 1/N corrections to the Gaussian (free) theory, mirroring the role of renormalization group flow and loop expansions in conventional QFT.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Near-Gaussianity at large-N is argued to explain why Nature's field theories appear nearly Gaussian, providing an effective-field-theory interpretation of the neural network construction.

conceptDetails.name: conformal field theory
conceptDetails.description: Scale-invariant neuron architectures with power-law two-point functions produce Euclidean-invariant theories that share structural features with CFTs, explored as examples in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Correlation functions are computed as integrals over parameter distributions rather than functional integrals, conceptually analogous to Monte Carlo sampling in parameter space.

title: PQ Axiverse
abstract: We show that the strong CP problem is solved in a large class of compactifications of string theory. The Peccei-Quinn mechanism solves the strong CP problem if the CP-breaking effects of the ultraviolet completion of gravity and of QCD are small compared to the CP-preserving axion potential generated by low-energy QCD instantons. We characterize both classes of effects. To understand quantum gravitational effects, we consider an ensemble of flux compactifications of type IIB string theory on orientifolds of Calabi-Yau hypersurfaces in the geometric regime, taking a simple model of QCD on D7-branes. We show that the D-brane instanton contribution to the neutron electric dipole moment falls exponentially in $N^4$, with $N$ the number of axions. In particular, this contribution is negligible in all models in our ensemble with $N>17$. We interpret this result as a consequence of large $N$ effects in the geometry that create hierarchies in instanton actions and also suppress the ultraviolet cutoff. We also compute the CP breaking due to high-energy instantons in QCD. In the absence of vectorlike pairs, we find contributions to the neutron electric dipole moment that are not excluded, but that could be accessible to future experiments if the scale of supersymmetry breaking is sufficiently low. The existence of vectorlike pairs can lead to a larger dipole moment. Finally, we show that a significant fraction of models are allowed by standard cosmological and astrophysical constraints.

conceptDetails.name: peccei-quinn mechanism
conceptDetails.description: The central topic of the paper: assessing whether the PQ mechanism successfully solves the strong CP problem across a large class of string compactifications.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the strong CP problem within type IIB string theory compactifications on Calabi-Yau orientifolds, using string-theoretic tools to compute quantum gravity contributions to axion potentials.

conceptDetails.name: axion quality problem
conceptDetails.description: The paper quantifies whether quantum gravity and high-energy QCD effects break the axion shift symmetry enough to spoil the PQ solution to the strong CP problem.

conceptDetails.name: d-brane instantons
conceptDetails.description: Euclidean D3-branes wrapping four-cycles in the Calabi-Yau generate the hidden-sector potential for axions; the paper shows their contribution falls exponentially as exp(-cN^4).

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The Peccei-Quinn shift symmetry breaking by various instanton contributions is the central quantity computed to determine whether the strong CP problem is solved.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The Peccei-Quinn mechanism and axion physics are analyzed through effective field theory, characterizing how UV physics contributions compare to low-energy QCD instanton effects.

conceptDetails.name: string landscape
conceptDetails.description: The paper surveys an ensemble of type IIB flux compactifications as a representative sample of the string landscape to make statistical statements about the strong CP problem.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper computes CP-violating contributions to the neutron electric dipole moment from high-energy QCD instantons using quantum field theory techniques.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes cosmological and astrophysical constraints on axion dark matter relic abundances and axion-photon couplings across the string compactification ensemble.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: An ensemble of over 100,000 flux compactifications is generated and analyzed statistically to characterize the distribution of instanton contributions across the string landscape.

title: Ref-NeRF: Structured View-Dependent Appearance for Neural Radiance Fields
abstract: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) is a popular view synthesis technique that represents a scene as a continuous volumetric function, parameterized by multilayer perceptrons that provide the volume density and view-dependent emitted radiance at each location. While NeRF-based techniques excel at representing fine geometric structures with smoothly varying view-dependent appearance, they often fail to accurately capture and reproduce the appearance of glossy surfaces. We address this limitation by introducing Ref-NeRF, which replaces NeRF's parameterization of view-dependent outgoing radiance with a representation of reflected radiance and structures this function using a collection of spatially-varying scene properties. We show that together with a regularizer on normal vectors, our model significantly improves the realism and accuracy of specular reflections. Furthermore, we show that our model's internal representation of outgoing radiance is interpretable and useful for scene editing.

conceptDetails.name: neural radiance fields
conceptDetails.description: The core framework being extended — NeRF and mip-NeRF represent scenes as continuous volumetric functions parameterized by MLPs mapping 3D coordinates to density and view-dependent radiance.

conceptDetails.name: reflected radiance parameterization
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary contribution: replacing NeRF's view-direction input with the reflection of the viewing vector about the local surface normal, making the radiance function smoother and easier to interpolate.

conceptDetails.name: view-dependent appearance
conceptDetails.description: The central challenge addressed: modeling how surface radiance varies with camera viewpoint, especially for glossy and specular materials that exhibit sharp, direction-sensitive highlights.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: Outgoing radiance is decomposed into explicit, spatially-varying components — diffuse color, specular tint, roughness, and normal vectors — enabling interpretable and editable scene representations.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A novel regularizer on volumetric normal vectors is introduced to concentrate density at surfaces and align normals, enabling accurate reflection direction computation.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The Integrated Directional Encoding maps reflected directions to smooth feature embeddings that facilitate interpolation of specular appearance across varying materials.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The structured radiance decomposition yields interpretable internal representations (roughness, diffuse texture, specular tint, normals) that directly support scene editing applications.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: NeRF learns a continuous neural scene representation from multi-view images; Ref-NeRF structures this learned representation to better capture specular surface appearance.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Novel view synthesis reconstructs unobserved scene geometry and appearance from sparse 2D image observations, framed as an optimization over a neural volumetric representation.

title: Machine Learning in Nuclear Physics
abstract:
Advances in machine learning methods provide tools that have broad applicability in scientific research. These techniques are being applied across the diversity of nuclear physics research topics, leading to advances that will facilitate scientific discoveries and societal applications.
  This Review gives a snapshot of nuclear physics research which has been transformed by machine learning techniques.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Used extensively across nuclear theory sections to extract physical parameters, quantify model uncertainties, and perform global analyses of nuclear data and parton distributions.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A central theme across all sections, addressing the need to rigorously quantify theoretical and experimental uncertainties in nuclear physics predictions and ML model outputs.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: ML-based surrogate and emulator models replace expensive first-principles calculations in accelerator operations, nuclear data pipelines, and nuclear structure computations.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: A dedicated major section covers ML techniques for LQCD including ensemble generation, sign problem mitigation at non-zero density, and improved correlation function estimators.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: ML methods augment and accelerate Monte Carlo sampling in LQCD ensemble generation, nuclear reaction calculations, and uncertainty estimation.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Widely used in experimental methods for particle identification, event classification, signal-background separation, and detector anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: ML approaches are developed to solve ill-posed inversion problems in nuclear physics, including spectral function reconstruction and extraction of parton distributions.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: ML is applied to accelerate and improve A-body calculations within nuclear effective field theory frameworks, including emulation of EFT predictions.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Applied to predict nuclear properties such as masses, radii, and binding energies from nuclear structure models and experimental data.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Deployed in accelerator science for fault detection, beam diagnostics, and identifying unusual machine behavior in non-stationary operating conditions.

conceptDetails.name: particle tracking
conceptDetails.description: ML methods, including graph neural networks and deep learning, are reviewed for charged particle track reconstruction in nuclear physics detectors.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Likelihood-free inference methods are used to compare nuclear physics simulations to experimental data without tractable likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: ML-driven optimal experimental design is reviewed for improving detector systems and interfacing theory predictions with experimental planning.

title: Substructure Detection Reanalyzed: Dark Perturber shown to be a Line-of-Sight Halo
abstract: Observations of structure at sub-galactic scales are crucial for probing the properties of dark matter, which is the dominant source of gravity in the universe. It will become increasingly important for future surveys to distinguish between line-of-sight halos and subhalos to avoid wrong inferences on the nature of dark matter. We reanalyze a sub-galactic structure (in lens JVAS B1938+666) that has been previously found using the gravitational imaging technique in galaxy-galaxy lensing systems. This structure has been assumed to be a satellite in the halo of the main lens galaxy. We fit the redshift of the perturber of the system as a free parameter, using the multi-plane thin-lens approximation, and find that the redshift of the perturber is $z_\mathrm{int} = 1.42\substack{+0.10 \\ -0.15}$ (with a main lens redshift of $z=0.881$). Our analysis indicates that this structure is more massive than the previous result by an order of magnitude. This constitutes the first dark perturber shown to be a line-of-sight halo with a gravitational lensing method.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational lensing
conceptDetails.description: Strong gravitational lensing via the gravitational imaging technique is the central observational method used to detect and characterize the dark perturber.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper probes dark matter properties by analyzing sub-galactic structure detectable through gravitational lensing, with implications for distinguishing CDM from alternative dark matter models.

conceptDetails.name: multi-plane lensing
conceptDetails.description: The multi-plane thin-lens approximation is used to model line-of-sight halos at redshifts different from the main lens, enabling redshift inference.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Fitting the redshift of a gravitational perturber as a free parameter from imaging data is an inverse problem requiring inference of physical parameters from observed pixel intensities.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The authors use Bayesian posterior estimation with MCMC sampling to constrain the redshift and mass of the perturber system.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: Posterior distributions over perturber redshift, mass, and concentration are derived to quantify uncertainty in the line-of-sight vs. subhalo classification.

conceptDetails.name: nfw profile
conceptDetails.description: The Navarro-Frenk-White profile is used as a mass model for the perturber alongside SIS and pseudo-Jaffe profiles to fit its density structure.

conceptDetails.name: halo mass function
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses implications for constraining the halo and subhalo mass functions, which encode dark matter physics at sub-galactic scales.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The analysis relies on forward-modeling the lensing system to compare simulated images with observations, a form of simulation-based inference.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The paper references expectations from CDM simulations for halo and subhalo mass functions to contextualize the detected perturber.

title: Infinite Neural Network Quantum States: Entanglement and Training Dynamics
abstract: We study infinite limits of neural network quantum states ($\infty$-NNQS), which exhibit representation power through ensemble statistics, and also tractable gradient descent dynamics. Ensemble averages of Renyi entropies are expressed in terms of neural network correlators, and architectures that exhibit volume-law entanglement are presented. A general framework is developed for studying the gradient descent dynamics of neural network quantum states (NNQS), using a quantum state neural tangent kernel (QS-NTK). For $\infty$-NNQS the training dynamics is simplified, since the QS-NTK becomes deterministic and constant. An analytic solution is derived for quantum state supervised learning, which allows an $\infty$-NNQS to recover any target wavefunction. Numerical experiments on finite and infinite NNQS in the transverse field Ising model and Fermi Hubbard model demonstrate excellent agreement with theory. $\infty$-NNQS opens up new opportunities for studying entanglement and training dynamics in other physics applications, such as in finding ground states.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks parametrize quantum wavefunctions, with the paper studying infinite-width limits of these neural network quantum state representations.

conceptDetails.name: neural network quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The core object of study: parametric neural network representations of quantum wavefunctions extended to the infinite-width limit for analytical tractability.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Ensemble averages of Rényi entanglement entropies are computed analytically via neural network correlators in the Gaussian process limit, with volume-law scaling demonstrated.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The quantum state neural tangent kernel (QS-NTK) is introduced to govern gradient descent dynamics of NNQS, becoming deterministic and constant in the infinite-width limit.

conceptDetails.name: quantum state neural tangent kernel
conceptDetails.description: A novel extension of the neural tangent kernel to the quantum setting that governs NNQS gradient descent dynamics and yields an analytic solution for quantum state supervised learning.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The neural network Gaussian process (NNGP) limit is central to the analysis, enabling exact computation of ensemble statistics and tractable entanglement entropy bounds.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Ensemble statistics over randomly initialized neural network quantum states form the framework for deriving entanglement entropy bounds and training dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: volume-law entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Specific neural network architectures in the Gaussian process limit are analytically shown to exhibit volume-law entanglement scaling, approaching the Page value.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: Numerical experiments on the transverse field Ising model and Fermi Hubbard model validate the theoretical QS-NTK framework against finite-width NNQS.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: In the NNGP limit, infinite-width NNQS can be trained via Bayesian inference using the deterministic NNGP kernel as an alternative to gradient descent.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks serve as compact representations of exponentially large quantum Hilbert spaces, with the paper analyzing their representational power in infinite limits.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Variational Monte Carlo underlies the training of finite-width NNQS used in numerical experiments to validate theoretical predictions.

title: Robust and Provably Monotonic Networks
abstract: The Lipschitz constant of the map between the input and output space represented by a neural network is a natural metric for assessing the robustness of the model. We present a new method to constrain the Lipschitz constant of dense deep learning models that can also be generalized to other architectures. The method relies on a simple weight normalization scheme during training that ensures the Lipschitz constant of every layer is below an upper limit specified by the analyst. A simple monotonic residual connection can then be used to make the model monotonic in any subset of its inputs, which is useful in scenarios where domain knowledge dictates such dependence. Examples can be found in algorithmic fairness requirements or, as presented here, in the classification of the decays of subatomic particles produced at the CERN Large Hadron Collider. Our normalization is minimally constraining and allows the underlying architecture to maintain higher expressiveness compared to other techniques which aim to either control the Lipschitz constant of the model or ensure its monotonicity. We show how the algorithm was used to train a powerful, robust, and interpretable discriminator for heavy-flavor-quark decays, which has been adopted for use as the primary data-selection algorithm in the LHCb real-time data-processing system in the current LHC data-taking period known as Run 3. In addition, our algorithm has also achieved state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks in medicine, finance, and other applications.

conceptDetails.name: lipschitz-constrained networks
conceptDetails.description: A novel neural network architecture enforcing per-layer Lipschitz bounds via column-wise weight normalization, enabling provable robustness guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is constraining the Lipschitz constant of neural networks to ensure robustness to small changes in experimental conditions or training sample deficiencies.

conceptDetails.name: monotonic residual connection
conceptDetails.description: A novel architectural component that adds a linear term to a Lipschitz-bounded base model to provably enforce monotonicity in any subset of inputs.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics application is classifying decays of subatomic particles at the LHC, specifically heavy-flavor-quark decays for the LHCb trigger system.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The monotonic Lipschitz network was deployed as the primary data-selection algorithm in the LHCb real-time data-processing trigger system during LHC Run 3.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The method is validated and applied to proton-proton collision data from the CERN Large Hadron Collider, addressing particle physics classification challenges.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Monotonicity constraints encode domain knowledge (outliers are more interesting) directly into the architecture, making model decisions interpretable in the physics context.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The weight normalization scheme is applied during training to enforce Lipschitz constraints layer-wise, representing a novel approach to constrained optimization.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The monotonicity constraint encodes the physics prior that outlier events are more interesting, enabling robust detection of novel or rare particle decay signatures.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Constraining the Lipschitz constant provides formal guarantees on model sensitivity, offering a principled measure of robustness and uncertainty in predictions.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The discriminator targets heavy-flavor-quark jet identification, a core task in collider physics event selection and background rejection.

title: Quantum reservoir computing using arrays of Rydberg atoms
abstract: Quantum computing promises to provide machine learning with computational advantages. However, noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices pose engineering challenges to realizing quantum machine learning (QML) advantages. Recently, a series of QML computational models inspired by the noise-tolerant dynamics on the brain have emerged as a means to circumvent the hardware limitations of NISQ devices. In this article, we introduce a quantum version of a recurrent neural network (RNN), a well-known model for neural circuits in the brain. Our quantum RNN (qRNN) makes use of the natural Hamiltonian dynamics of an ensemble of interacting spin-1/2 particles as a means for computation. In the limit where the Hamiltonian is diagonal, the qRNN recovers the dynamics of the classical version. Beyond this limit, we observe that the quantum dynamics of the qRNN provide it quantum computational features that can aid it in computation. To this end, we study a qRNN based on arrays of Rydberg atoms, and show that the qRNN is indeed capable of replicating the learning of several cognitive tasks such as multitasking, decision making, and long-term memory by taking advantage of several key features of this platform such as interatomic species interactions, and quantum many-body scars.

conceptDetails.name: quantum reservoir computing
conceptDetails.description: The central paradigm of the paper: using fixed-geometry Rydberg atom arrays as quantum reservoirs whose unitary dynamics perform machine learning tasks without trainable internal weights.

conceptDetails.name: recurrent networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces a quantum RNN (qRNN) that generalizes classical recurrent neural networks by replacing sigmoid activations with Hamiltonian spin-1/2 dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: The natural Hamiltonian dynamics of interacting spin-1/2 ensembles serve as the computational substrate of the qRNN, replacing classical activation functions.

conceptDetails.name: rydberg atom arrays
conceptDetails.description: The specific experimental platform of optically trapped Rydberg atoms whose long coherence times, controllable geometry, and programmable interactions implement the proposed qRNN.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper situates qRNNs as a NISQ-compatible quantum machine learning approach that exploits quantum dynamics without requiring fault-tolerant hardware.

conceptDetails.name: many-body quantum scars
conceptDetails.description: Ergodicity-breaking quantum many-body scar states in Rydberg arrays provide the qRNN with long-term memory, a key cognitive task demonstrated in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: Rydberg atom arrays act as analog quantum simulators whose many-body evolution encodes the reservoir computation without digital circuit decomposition.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Quantum entanglement generated by Rydberg interactions constitutes a computational resource that distinguishes qRNN performance from its classical counterpart.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The qRNN is shown to offer a potential speedup over classical RNNs when simulating stochastic dynamics, analyzed via a Markov chain representation.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: Multitasking across cognitive tasks is demonstrated as a capability enabled by inhibitory/excitatory neuron encoding via inter-species Rydberg interactions.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Driving the Rydberg reservoir near a quantum critical point is highlighted as a mechanism that enhances computational capacity of the reservoir.

title: New limits on light dark matter - proton cross section from the cosmic large-scale structure
abstract: We set the strongest limits to-date on the velocity-independent dark matter (DM) - proton cross section $σ$ for DM masses $m = 10\,\mathrm{keV}$ to $100\,\mathrm{GeV}$, using large-scale structure traced by the Lyman-alpha forest: e.g., a 95% lower limit $σ< 6 \times 10^{-30}\,\mathrm{cm}^2$, for $m = 100\,\mathrm{keV}$. Our results complement direct detection, which has limited sensitivity to sub-GeV DM. We use an emulator of cosmological simulations, combined with data from the smallest cosmological scales used to-date, to model and search for the imprint of primordial DM-proton collisions. Cosmological bounds are improved by up to a factor of 25.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper sets strongest-to-date limits on the dark matter-proton cross section for DM masses 10 keV to 100 GeV using large-scale structure observations.

conceptDetails.name: lyman-alpha forest constraints
conceptDetails.description: High-redshift quasar absorption spectra tracing the intergalactic medium provide the small-scale structure data used to constrain dark matter interactions.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Hydrodynamical simulations using MP-Gadget are run to model the effect of dark matter-proton scattering on the intergalactic medium and Lyman-alpha forest.

conceptDetails.name: emulation
conceptDetails.description: A machine-learning emulator replaces expensive cosmological simulations to enable efficient statistical inference over the dark matter parameter space.

conceptDetails.name: matter power spectrum suppression
conceptDetails.description: DM-proton collisional damping in the early universe imprints a characteristic small-scale suppression in the linear matter power spectrum that is the primary observable signal.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian statistical inference is used to derive posterior constraints on the dark matter-proton cross section from Lyman-alpha forest flux power spectrum data.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The dark matter emulator serves as a computationally cheap surrogate for hydrodynamical simulations, enabling comparison with observational data at scale.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
