conceptDetails.description: The forward modeling pipeline connecting DM-proton interaction parameters to observable flux power spectra constitutes a simulation-based inference approach.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian optimization as an active learning technique is used to adaptively sample the simulation parameter space and robustly test theoretical modeling assumptions.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC sampling is used to explore the posterior distribution over cosmological and dark matter parameters.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: A model-independent, velocity-independent cross section parameterization maps to specific particle physics models such as dark matter with magnetic dipole moment.

conceptDetails.name: cosmic microwave background
conceptDetails.description: Planck 2018 CMB parameters are used as fixed baseline cosmological inputs for the simulations.

title: Equivariant Contrastive Learning
abstract: In state-of-the-art self-supervised learning (SSL) pre-training produces semantically good representations by encouraging them to be invariant under meaningful transformations prescribed from human knowledge. In fact, the property of invariance is a trivial instance of a broader class called equivariance, which can be intuitively understood as the property that representations transform according to the way the inputs transform. Here, we show that rather than using only invariance, pre-training that encourages non-trivial equivariance to some transformations, while maintaining invariance to other transformations, can be used to improve the semantic quality of representations. Specifically, we extend popular SSL methods to a more general framework which we name Equivariant Self-Supervised Learning (E-SSL). In E-SSL, a simple additional pre-training objective encourages equivariance by predicting the transformations applied to the input. We demonstrate E-SSL's effectiveness empirically on several popular computer vision benchmarks, e.g. improving SimCLR to 72.5% linear probe accuracy on ImageNet. Furthermore, we demonstrate usefulness of E-SSL for applications beyond computer vision; in particular, we show its utility on regression problems in photonics science. Our code, datasets and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/rdangovs/essl to aid further research in E-SSL.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core framework, extending SSL methods to incorporate equivariance alongside invariance for better representation learning.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The primary SSL paradigm extended in this work, with SimCLR as the main baseline improved by equivariant objectives.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The central goal of E-SSL is to learn semantically richer representations by balancing invariance and equivariance to transformations.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces E-SSL by formalizing equivariance in self-supervised pre-training, predicting how inputs transform to guide representation learning.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant pretext task
conceptDetails.description: A novel pre-training objective introduced in E-SSL that predicts applied transformations to encourage equivariant representations.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Transformation selection (crops, rotations, flips, jigsaws) is central to both I-SSL baselines and the E-SSL framework's equivariant objectives.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper grounds equivariance in group theory, formalizing how representations should transform consistently with input group transformations.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Transformation groups (e.g., four-fold rotations) provide the mathematical structure for defining equivariance in E-SSL.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: E-SSL pre-trained models are evaluated via linear probe and fine-tuning on downstream tasks, demonstrating transfer learning utility.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: E-SSL trains with simultaneous invariant and equivariant objectives, effectively a multi-task pre-training strategy.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: E-SSL is applied to regression problems in photonics science, demonstrating generalization beyond computer vision classification.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper demonstrates E-SSL's utility for scientific data (photonics), motivating its relevance to IAIFI's physics-AI research agenda.

title: Surrogate- and invariance-boosted contrastive learning for data-scarce applications in science
abstract: Deep learning techniques have been increasingly applied to the natural sciences, e.g., for property prediction and optimization or material discovery. A fundamental ingredient of such approaches is the vast quantity of labelled data needed to train the model; this poses severe challenges in data-scarce settings where obtaining labels requires substantial computational or labor resources. Here, we introduce surrogate- and invariance-boosted contrastive learning (SIB-CL), a deep learning framework which incorporates three ``inexpensive'' and easily obtainable auxiliary information sources to overcome data scarcity. Specifically, these are: 1)~abundant unlabeled data, 2)~prior knowledge of symmetries or invariances and 3)~surrogate data obtained at near-zero cost. We demonstrate SIB-CL's effectiveness and generality on various scientific problems, e.g., predicting the density-of-states of 2D photonic crystals and solving the 3D time-independent Schrodinger equation. SIB-CL consistently results in orders of magnitude reduction in the number of labels needed to achieve the same network accuracies.

conceptDetails.name: contrastive learning
conceptDetails.description: The core pre-training mechanism in SIB-CL, using SimCLR/BYOL to learn invariant embeddings from physics-informed augmentations of unlabeled data.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The pre-training paradigm that enables SIB-CL to leverage abundant unlabeled data without requiring expensive labels.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: SIB-CL's second auxiliary source: cheap approximate data (e.g., coarse-grained simulations) used for transfer pre-training to supplement scarce target labels.

conceptDetails.name: data-scarce learning
conceptDetails.description: Novel framework contribution: systematically exploiting three categories of inexpensive auxiliary information to achieve orders-of-magnitude label reduction in scientific ML.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: Used in the surrogate pre-training stage and fine-tuning stage to adapt representations learned from auxiliary data to the scarce target problem.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Physical symmetries and invariances of the input domain are encoded as contrastive augmentation groups, ensuring learned representations respect known physics.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: SIB-CL learns compact, physics-aware metric embeddings that generalize well to the target task even with very few labeled examples.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: Abundant unlabeled inputs are exploited during contrastive pre-training to reduce reliance on the small labeled target dataset.

conceptDetails.name: fine-tuning
conceptDetails.description: The final SIB-CL stage where the pre-trained encoder is adapted to the target task using the small available labeled dataset.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Physics-informed invariance transformations (geometric and non-geometric) serve as the augmentation strategy for contrastive pair generation.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: The encoder-projector network produces metric embeddings in which physically similar inputs cluster together, enabling label-efficient downstream regression.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Predicting material properties (density-of-states of photonic crystals) and solving quantum equations from structural inputs are the target inverse problems.

title: Challenges for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Particle Physics
abstract: Anomaly detection relies on designing a score to determine whether a particular event is uncharacteristic of a given background distribution. One way to define a score is to use autoencoders, which rely on the ability to reconstruct certain types of data (background) but not others (signals). In this paper, we study some challenges associated with variational autoencoders, such as the dependence on hyperparameters and the metric used, in the context of anomalous signal (top and $W$) jets in a QCD background. We find that the hyperparameter choices strongly affect the network performance and that the optimal parameters for one signal are non-optimal for another. In exploring the networks, we uncover a connection between the latent space of a variational autoencoder trained using mean-squared-error and the optimal transport distances within the dataset. We then show that optimal transport distances to representative events in the background dataset can be used directly for anomaly detection, with performance comparable to the autoencoders. Whether using autoencoders or optimal transport distances for anomaly detection, we find that the choices that best represent the background are not necessarily best for signal identification. These challenges with unsupervised anomaly detection bolster the case for additional exploration of semi-supervised or alternative approaches.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central focus of the paper, exploring unsupervised methods to identify anomalous signal jets (top and W) against a QCD background.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The primary machine learning architecture studied, with analysis of how hyperparameter choices and metrics affect anomaly detection performance.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: Wasserstein optimal transport distances are shown to correlate with VAE latent space distances and are used directly as an anomaly score with comparable performance.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The broader autoencoder paradigm is analyzed for its limitations in unsupervised anomaly detection, including reconstruction error as a discriminant.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The physical context for anomaly detection: top and W jets are studied as anomalous signals against a QCD jet background.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The broader motivation is to find model-agnostic approaches to searching for physics beyond the Standard Model at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper is grounded in LHC physics, addressing challenges of anomaly detection in high-energy collider data.

conceptDetails.name: hyperparameter sensitivity
conceptDetails.description: A novel finding that optimal hyperparameter choices for one signal type are non-optimal for another, undermining model-agnostic universality claims.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The core problem studied is distinguishing signal events as out-of-distribution relative to the background training sample.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: VAEs are studied as dimensionality reduction tools whose latent space representations encode background distributions for anomaly scoring.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The VAE latent space is analyzed as a learned representation of the background distribution, with connections to optimal transport geometry.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper concludes that the challenges of unsupervised anomaly detection bolster the case for semi-supervised alternatives.

title: A neural simulation-based inference approach for characterizing the Galactic Center $γ$-ray excess
abstract: The nature of the Fermi gamma-ray Galactic Center Excess (GCE) has remained a persistent mystery for over a decade. Although the excess is broadly compatible with emission expected due to dark matter annihilation, an explanation in terms of a population of unresolved astrophysical point sources e.g., millisecond pulsars, remains viable. The effort to uncover the origin of the GCE is hampered in particular by an incomplete understanding of diffuse emission of Galactic origin. This can lead to spurious features that make it difficult to robustly differentiate smooth emission, as expected for a dark matter origin, from more "clumpy" emission expected for a population of relatively bright, unresolved point sources. We use recent advancements in the field of simulation-based inference, in particular density estimation techniques using normalizing flows, in order to characterize the contribution of modeled components, including unresolved point source populations, to the GCE. Compared to traditional techniques based on the statistical distribution of photon counts, our machine learning-based method is able to utilize more of the information contained in a given model of the Galactic Center emission, and in particular can perform posterior parameter estimation while accounting for pixel-to-pixel spatial correlations in the gamma-ray map. This makes the method demonstrably more resilient to certain forms of model misspecification. On application to Fermi data, the method generically attributes a smaller fraction of the GCE flux to unresolved point sources when compared to traditional approaches. We nevertheless infer such a contribution to make up a non-negligible fraction of the GCE across all analysis variations considered, with at least $38^{+9}_{-19}\%$ of the excess attributed to unresolved point sources in our baseline analysis.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The core methodology of the paper, using neural networks to perform likelihood-free inference on the Galactic Center gamma-ray excess without tractable likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Used for conditional density estimation to characterize posterior distributions over flux fractions of modeled GCE components.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper employs neural density estimation to learn the posterior distribution of parameters from simulated gamma-ray maps.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The method performs full posterior parameter estimation over GCE component contributions, accounting for spatial correlations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework is Bayesian, inferring posterior distributions over model parameters characterizing dark matter and point source contributions.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: One of the two primary candidate explanations for the GCE whose contribution fraction is being inferred by the method.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: A graph-based spherical convolutional neural network is used to extract summary statistics from gamma-ray sky maps for downstream inference.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks learn optimized summary statistics from high-dimensional gamma-ray maps for parameter estimation.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The method quantifies uncertainties in GCE component fractions and demonstrates resilience to model misspecification.

conceptDetails.name: model misspecification robustness
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly studies and demonstrates the method's resilience to mismodeled diffuse foreground emission and systematic uncertainties.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses detecting and characterizing the signal origin of the GCE — dark matter annihilation versus unresolved point sources.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Forward simulations using Monte Carlo methods generate training data for the neural inference pipeline.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring the physical parameters (flux fractions) of GCE components from observed gamma-ray maps is fundamentally an inverse problem.

title: Mixture Model Auto-Encoders: Deep Clustering through Dictionary Learning
abstract: State-of-the-art approaches for clustering high-dimensional data utilize deep auto-encoder architectures. Many of these networks require a large number of parameters and suffer from a lack of interpretability, due to the black-box nature of the auto-encoders. We introduce Mixture Model Auto-Encoders (MixMate), a novel architecture that clusters data by performing inference on a generative model. Derived from the perspective of sparse dictionary learning and mixture models, MixMate comprises several auto-encoders, each tasked with reconstructing data in a distinct cluster, while enforcing sparsity in the latent space. Through experiments on various image datasets, we show that MixMate achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art deep clustering algorithms, while using orders of magnitude fewer parameters.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: MixMate comprises K parallel auto-encoders, each reconstructing data for a distinct cluster using cluster-specific dictionaries.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Deep clustering of high-dimensional image data is the central task, with cluster assignments inferred via a generative model posterior.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse dictionary learning underpins the generative model, with Laplace priors enforcing sparsity in latent codes via an ℓ1 penalty.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: MixMate derives its architecture from a sparse dictionary learning mixture model as an explicit generative process for the data.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Cluster assignments are inferred through the posterior p(z|y) of the generative model, with the EM algorithm used to estimate parameters and latent variables.

conceptDetails.name: mixture of experts
conceptDetails.description: MixMate employs K parallel auto-encoders partitioning the dataset, following the mixture-of-experts paradigm with an attention module for soft cluster assignment.

conceptDetails.name: algorithm unfolding
conceptDetails.description: The FISTA optimization algorithm is unrolled into a fixed-depth neural network encoder, bridging classical sparse coding with deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: An attention module computes soft cluster assignments by converting per-cluster energies into posterior probabilities via a softmax operation.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The encoder projects high-dimensional data into sparse latent codes that serve as interpretable representations for clustering.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: MixMate's model-based derivation yields an interpretable architecture contrasting with black-box deep clustering methods.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The training loss is derived directly from the EM objective, connecting the neural network training procedure to principled probabilistic inference.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Each FISTA encoder maps high-dimensional inputs to low-dimensional sparse codes in a subspace defined by a cluster-specific dictionary.

title: Towards Quantum Simulations in Particle Physics and Beyond on Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices
abstract: We review two algorithmic advances that bring us closer to reliable quantum simulations of model systems in high energy physics and beyond on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. The first method is the dimensional expressivity analysis of quantum circuits, which allows for constructing minimal but maximally expressive quantum circuits. The second method is an efficient mitigation of readout errors on quantum devices. Both methods can lead to significant improvements in quantum simulations, e.g., when variational quantum eigensolvers are used.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The central goal of the paper — simulating high-energy physics models (gauge theories, particle physics) on quantum hardware to circumvent the sign problem and enable real-time dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: NISQ devices are the hardware platform; the paper develops algorithms specifically designed for current noisy intermediate-scale quantum processors.

conceptDetails.name: variational quantum eigensolver
conceptDetails.description: The hybrid quantum-classical VQE algorithm is the primary application context for both algorithmic contributions — expressivity analysis and readout error mitigation.

conceptDetails.name: readout error mitigation
conceptDetails.description: One of the two main contributions: an efficient method to mitigate measurement (readout) errors on quantum devices, improving simulation reliability.

conceptDetails.name: dimensional expressivity analysis
conceptDetails.description: The other main contribution: a method to construct minimal but maximally expressive parametric quantum circuits by analyzing the accessible submanifold of quantum states.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Quantum simulations rely on the Hamiltonian formulation in Minkowskian space-time, which is central to the VQE approach and avoids Euclidean MCMC methods.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The primary target physics — quantum simulations of lattice gauge theories (including QCD-like models) in lower dimensions are used as benchmarks.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The expressivity analysis framework explicitly allows circuits to incorporate or remove symmetries, which is critical for physically meaningful quantum simulations.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The long-term physics motivation: quantum simulations aim to address SM phenomena like CP-violation and matter-antimatter asymmetry that are inaccessible to classical methods.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: Classical tensor network methods are used to validate quantum simulation results in regimes accessible to both classical and quantum approaches.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods are the classical baseline whose sign problem and Euclidean-time limitations motivate the quantum simulation approach.

title: Pruning a restricted Boltzmann machine for quantum state reconstruction
abstract: Restricted Boltzmann machines (RBMs) have proven to be a powerful tool for learning quantum wavefunction representations from qubit projective measurement data. Since the number of classical parameters needed to encode a quantum wavefunction scales rapidly with the number of qubits, the ability to learn efficient representations is of critical importance. In this paper we study magnitude-based pruning as a way to compress the wavefunction representation in an RBM, focusing on RBMs trained on data from the transverse-field Ising model in one dimension. We find that pruning can reduce the total number of RBM weights, but the threshold at which the reconstruction accuracy starts to degrade varies significantly depending on the phase of the model. In a gapped region of the phase diagram, the RBM admits pruning over half of the weights while still accurately reproducing relevant physical observables. At the quantum critical point however, even a small amount of pruning can lead to significant loss of accuracy in the physical properties of the reconstructed quantum state. Our results highlight the importance of tracking all relevant observables as their sensitivity varies strongly with pruning. Finally, we find that sparse RBMs are trainable and discuss how a successful sparsity pattern can be created without pruning.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: RBMs are trained to represent and reconstruct quantum many-body wavefunctions from projective measurement data.

conceptDetails.name: quantum state reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The specific inverse problem of recovering a quantum wavefunction from projective measurement data using a trained neural network.

conceptDetails.name: restricted boltzmann machine
conceptDetails.description: A bipartite generative neural network with visible and hidden units whose weight structure encodes the quantum wavefunction representation studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Magnitude-based pruning is applied to create sparse RBMs with fewer weights while preserving reconstruction accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: magnitude-based pruning
conceptDetails.description: An iterative post-training compression technique that eliminates lowest-magnitude weights and fine-tunes the remaining parameters at each step.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: RBMs are used as generative models trained via maximum likelihood to learn the probability distribution of qubit measurement outcomes.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The paper finds that pruning sensitivity varies dramatically across the TFIM phase diagram, with the quantum critical point being far more sensitive to weight removal than gapped phases.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The central goal is learning efficient compressed representations of quantum wavefunctions that scale favorably with qubit number.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Entanglement entropy and volume-law vs. sub-volume-law scaling motivates whether sparse RBM connectivity can efficiently capture quantum correlations.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: KL divergence serves as the primary training objective, measuring how well the RBM distribution matches the data distribution.

conceptDetails.name: tensor networks
conceptDetails.description: DMRG, a tensor network algorithm, is used to generate ground-state samples of the TFIM that serve as training data for the RBMs.

title: Observation of enhanced free-electron radiation from photonic flatband resonances
abstract: Flatbands emerge from a myriad of structures such as Landau levels, Lieb and Kagome lattices, linegraphs, and more recently moire superlattices. They enable unique properties including slow light in photonics, correlated phases in electronics, and supercollimation in both systems. Despite these intense parallel efforts, flatbands have never been shown to affect the core light-matter interaction between electrons and photons, which is limited by a dimensionality mismatch. Here, we reveal that a photonic flatband can overcome this mismatch between localized electrons and extended photons and thus remarkably boost their light-matter interaction. We design flatband resonances in a silicon-on-insulator photonic crystal slab to control and enhance the radiation emission from free electrons by tuning their trajectory and velocity. In particular, we record a 100-fold radiation enhancement from the conventional diffraction-enabled Smith-Purcell radiation, and show the potential of our approach to achieve $10^6$-fold enhancements and beyond. The enhancement also enables us to perform polarization shaping of free electron radiation from multiple flatbands and demonstrate an approach to measure photonic bands via angle-resolved electron-beam measurements. Our results suggest flatbands as ideal test beds for strong light-electron interaction in various systems, with particular relevance for efficient and compact free-electron light sources and accelerators.

conceptDetails.name: photonic flatband resonances
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces and exploits photonic flatband resonances in silicon-on-insulator photonic crystal slabs to overcome the dimensionality mismatch in free-electron light-matter interaction.

conceptDetails.name: free-electron radiation enhancement
conceptDetails.description: The core contribution is demonstrating 100-fold (and theoretically 10^6-fold) enhancement of Smith-Purcell radiation by coupling free electrons to photonic flatband modes.

conceptDetails.name: smith-purcell radiation
conceptDetails.description: Smith-Purcell radiation from free electrons near periodic structures is the baseline effect that is dramatically enhanced via photonic flatband coupling in this experiment.

conceptDetails.name: photonic crystal slab
conceptDetails.description: A silicon-on-insulator photonic crystal slab is the engineered structure that supports the flatband resonances enabling enhanced free-electron radiation.

conceptDetails.name: momentum mismatch
conceptDetails.description: The transverse momentum mismatch between localized electrons and extended photons is the fundamental limitation overcome by photonic flatbands in this work.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Phase matching conditions between electron surfaces and photonic flatband resonances are central to achieving enhanced light-matter interaction in this work.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detection and measurement of 100-fold enhanced Smith-Purcell radiation from photonic flatband resonances constitutes the primary experimental result.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Band structure measurements and angle-resolved electron-beam measurements are used to characterize photonic flatbands and validate the radiation enhancement mechanism.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: The photonic crystal slab geometry and its periodic structure determine the flatband properties and radiation enhancement characteristics.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Polarization shaping of free-electron radiation from multiple flatbands exploits symmetry properties of the photonic bands.

title: Classical Shadows for Quantum Process Tomography on Near-term Quantum Computers
abstract: Quantum process tomography is a powerful tool for understanding quantum channels and characterizing properties of quantum devices. Inspired by recent advances using classical shadows in quantum state tomography [H.-Y. Huang, R. Kueng, and J. Preskill, Nat. Phys. 16, 1050 (2020).], we have developed ShadowQPT, a classical shadow method for quantum process tomography. We introduce two related formulations with and without ancilla qubits. ShadowQPT stochastically reconstructs the Choi matrix of the device allowing for an a-posteri classical evaluation of the device on arbitrary inputs with respect to arbitrary outputs. Using shadows we then show how to compute overlaps, generate all $k$-weight reduced processes, and perform reconstruction via Hamiltonian learning. These latter two tasks are efficient for large systems as the number of quantum measurements needed scales only logarithmically with the number of qubits. A number of additional approximations and improvements are developed including the use of a pair-factorized Clifford shadow and a series of post-processing techniques which significantly enhance the accuracy for recovering the quantum channel. We have implemented ShadowQPT using both Pauli and Clifford measurements on the IonQ trapped ion quantum computer for quantum processes up to $n=4$ qubits and achieved good performance.

conceptDetails.name: quantum process tomography
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper — ShadowQPT is a new efficient algorithm for characterizing the full dynamics of a quantum device via the Choi matrix representation.

conceptDetails.name: classical shadow tomography
conceptDetails.description: The core technique adapted from Huang, Kueng, and Preskill's quantum state shadow tomography, extended here to quantum processes enabling prediction of many observables from few measurements.

conceptDetails.name: choi matrix reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: ShadowQPT stochastically reconstructs the Choi matrix representation of the quantum channel, enabling a posteriori classical evaluation on arbitrary inputs and outputs.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper implements ShadowQPT on the IonQ trapped ion quantum computer, demonstrating the method on near-term NISQ devices up to 4 qubits.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: Quantum state tomography via classical shadows forms the theoretical foundation, extended here to full quantum process tomography through the Jamiolkowski process-state isomorphism.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Hamiltonian learning is a key application of ShadowQPT, demonstrated by efficiently reconstructing a random 1D Ising model with logarithmic sample complexity scaling.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: ShadowQPT stochastically reconstructs the Choi matrix through randomized Pauli and Clifford measurements, relying on random sampling and averaging over shadow snapshots.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Process tomography is fundamentally an inverse problem — recovering the full quantum channel description from limited measurement outcomes.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Ancilla qubits and the Choi-Jamiolkowski isomorphism rely on entanglement between system and ancilla to encode process information into quantum states.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The logarithmic sample complexity of ShadowQPT for k-local reduced processes parallels compressed sensing and sparse recovery, allowing efficient reconstruction without full exponential measurements.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian methods are cited as prior art in quantum process tomography, contextualizing the statistical approach ShadowQPT takes to channel reconstruction.

title: Inferring dark matter substructure with astrometric lensing beyond the power spectrum
abstract: Astrometry -- the precise measurement of positions and motions of celestial objects -- has emerged as a promising avenue for characterizing the dark matter population in our Galaxy. By leveraging recent advances in simulation-based inference and neural network architectures, we introduce a novel method to search for global dark matter-induced gravitational lensing signatures in astrometric datasets. Our method based on neural likelihood-ratio estimation shows significantly enhanced sensitivity to a cold dark matter population and more favorable scaling with measurement noise compared to existing approaches based on two-point correlation statistics. We demonstrate the real-world viability of our method by showing it to be robust to non-trivial modeled as well as unmodeled noise features expected in astrometric measurements. This establishes machine learning as a powerful tool for characterizing dark matter using astrometric data.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary target: inferring properties of dark matter subhalo populations in the Milky Way using astrometric lensing signatures.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Core methodology enabling likelihood-free inference over intractable high-dimensional astrometric datasets by learning likelihood ratios from simulations.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses neural likelihood-ratio estimation as its primary statistical tool to compare hypotheses about dark matter subhalo fractions.

conceptDetails.name: astrometric lensing
conceptDetails.description: Novel application domain: gravitational deflection of stellar/quasar angular velocities by dark matter subhalos as the observed signal channel.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Spherical convolutional neural networks are used to process pixelated vector fields on the celestial sphere, exploiting the symmetry structure of the data domain.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The architecture exploits spherical symmetry of the data domain, making the network equivariant to rotations of the celestial sphere.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The method is designed to detect correlated gravitational lensing signals from dark matter substructure in astrometric velocity fields.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The method performs Bayesian posterior estimation over the subhalo fraction parameter using neural likelihood-ratio surrogates.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring dark matter subhalo population properties from observed astrometric distortions is an ill-posed inverse problem addressed by the neural inference framework.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: Forward simulations of astrometric datasets with dark matter subhalo populations are used to train the neural likelihood-ratio estimator.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper benchmarks against existing two-point correlation (power spectrum) approaches, demonstrating that the neural method goes beyond power spectrum statistics.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The method is validated for robustness to modeled and unmodeled noise features expected in real astrometric surveys like Gaia.

title: Physics-Augmented Learning: A New Paradigm Beyond Physics-Informed Learning
abstract: Integrating physical inductive biases into machine learning can improve model generalizability. We generalize the successful paradigm of physics-informed learning (PIL) into a more general framework that also includes what we term physics-augmented learning (PAL). PIL and PAL complement each other by handling discriminative and generative properties, respectively. In numerical experiments, we show that PAL performs well on examples where PIL is inapplicable or inefficient.

conceptDetails.name: physics-augmented learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: a new ML paradigm where physics priors are encoded via neural network architecture design rather than loss penalties, handling generative properties that PIL cannot.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The established PIL paradigm — integrating physics as soft loss penalties — serves as the baseline that PAL generalizes and complements for discriminative properties.

conceptDetails.name: generative physics properties
conceptDetails.description: Novel theoretical class: physical properties (e.g., Lagrangian structure, positive definiteness) for which an ideal generator exists but no efficient discriminator, motivating the PAL framework.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The Lagrangian property serves as the primary example of a generative-but-not-discriminative physical property, demonstrating where PIL fails and PAL succeeds.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: PIL relies on differentiable penalty terms to encode discriminative physics constraints; the paper contrasts this with PAL's architectural approach to non-discriminative properties.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Manifest and hidden symmetries are analyzed as case studies: manifest symmetry is both generative and discriminative (PIL-compatible), while hidden symmetry is generative only (PAL domain).

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Hidden Hamiltonicity is shown to be equivalent to Lagrangianness and thus a generative-only property, illustrating why PAL is needed for latent physical structure.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Cited as enabling ideal generators for manifest symmetry properties, making symmetry a case where both PIL and PAL are applicable.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: The generator/discriminator abstraction from GANs directly inspires the paper's formal definitions of generative and discriminative physical properties.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Lie group symmetries and their group generators are used to formalize manifest symmetry as a discriminative property expressible via PDEs.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: PIL's success in forward/inverse PDE problems is used to motivate why discriminative properties are a prerequisite for the penalty-based approach.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper motivates physics-integrated ML partly through improved model interpretability alongside generalization, framing PAL as a step toward more interpretable scientific models.

title: Presenting Unbinned Differential Cross Section Results
abstract: Machine learning tools have empowered a qualitatively new way to perform differential cross section measurements whereby the data are unbinned, possibly in many dimensions. Unbinned measurements can enable, improve, or at least simplify comparisons between experiments and with theoretical predictions. Furthermore, many-dimensional measurements can be used to define observables after the measurement instead of before. There is currently no community standard for publishing unbinned data. While there are also essentially no measurements of this type public, unbinned measurements are expected in the near future given recent methodological advances. The purpose of this paper is to propose a scheme for presenting and using unbinned results, which can hopefully form the basis for a community standard to allow for integration into analysis workflows. This is foreseen to be the start of an evolving community dialogue, in order to accommodate future developments in this field that is rapidly evolving.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: The central subject of the paper: correcting detector effects to produce unbinned, multidimensional differential cross section measurements that can be compared to theory.

conceptDetails.name: unbinned unfolding
conceptDetails.description: A novel paradigm enabled by ML where cross section results are stored as weighted event samples rather than histograms, allowing post-hoc observable and binning choices.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Density-based models are one of the two primary ML approaches described for performing unbinned unfolding, estimating the true particle-level distribution.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The broader framework within which unbinned measurements are consumed — enabling statistical inference directly from particle-level event weights without binning.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: A major section of the paper addresses how to compute, store, and propagate statistical and systematic uncertainties for unbinned spectra.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: A key ML architecture used in density-based unbinned unfolding methods to learn continuous particle-level distributions.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental context — the paper targets HEP collider experiments (e.g., LHC, EIC) where differential cross sections are measured.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MC event generators are the standard comparison baseline and the paper discusses how unbinned results integrate with MC tuning and reweighting workflows.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Classifier-based models constitute the second major class of unbinned unfolding methods, using trained discriminators as likelihood ratio estimators.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: Classifier-based unbinned methods estimate the likelihood ratio between data and simulation to reweight events to the particle level.

conceptDetails.name: inference-aware binning
conceptDetails.description: A proposed concept where optimal bin boundaries are chosen at analysis time from unbinned data rather than fixed a priori by the experimenter.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Unfolding is fundamentally an inverse problem — recovering the true particle-level distribution from smeared detector-level observations.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: EFT Wilson coefficient extraction is highlighted as a primary downstream application motivating the need for unbinned multidimensional measurements.

title: Deep Set Auto Encoders for Anomaly Detection in Particle Physics
abstract: There is an increased interest in model agnostic search strategies for physics beyond the standard model at the Large Hadron Collider. We introduce a Deep Set Variational Autoencoder and present results on the Dark Machines Anomaly Score Challenge. We find that the method attains the best anomaly detection ability when there is no decoding step for the network, and the anomaly score is based solely on the representation within the encoded latent space. This method was one of the top-performing models in the Dark Machines Challenge, both for the open data sets as well as the blinded data sets.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central task of the paper: flagging individual LHC collision events as anomalous (potentially BSM) based on deviation from standard model background distributions.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The core architecture introduced — a Deep Set β-VAE that encodes particle events into a structured Gaussian latent space, with β=1 found to give best anomaly detection performance.

conceptDetails.name: deep set networks
conceptDetails.description: Novel architecture combining Deep Set / particle flow networks (permutation-invariant Φ+F structure) with a variational autoencoder for unsupervised anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The physics motivation: model-agnostic detection of Beyond the Standard Model (BSM) signals including dark matter, Z', and SUSY models at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental domain: proton-proton collisions at √s=13 TeV from the Dark Machines challenge dataset with 1 billion simulated events across 26 SM processes.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: BSM signal events are treated as out-of-distribution relative to the SM background, with the latent-space anomaly score quantifying distributional distance.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The network learns a compressed latent representation of each particle event; the quality of this representation (not reconstruction fidelity) drives anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: point cloud representation
conceptDetails.description: Collision events are represented as unordered point clouds of four-vectors, enabling permutation-invariant processing without fixed-size tabular inputs.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The deep set architecture enforces permutation invariance over the unordered set of reconstructed particles by summing per-particle Φ embeddings before the event-level mapping.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Input data consists of reconstructed physics objects (jets, b-jets, leptons, photons) with four-momenta and missing transverse energy, treated as unordered sets.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Competing top methods in the Dark Machines challenge used density estimation; the paper's finding that 'fixed target' mapping to a Gaussian outperforms reconstruction motivates comparison.

conceptDetails.name: optimal transport
conceptDetails.description: The Chamfer loss used to compare input and reconstructed particle sets is a set-distance metric closely related to optimal transport between point clouds.

title: Machine-Learning media bias
abstract: We present an automated method for measuring media bias. Inferring which newspaper published a given article, based only on the frequencies with which it uses different phrases, leads to a conditional probability distribution whose analysis lets us automatically map newspapers and phrases into a bias space. By analyzing roughly a million articles from roughly a hundred newspapers for bias in dozens of news topics, our method maps newspapers into a two-dimensional bias landscape that agrees well with previous bias classifications based on human judgement. One dimension can be interpreted as traditional left-right bias, the other as establishment bias. This means that although news bias is inherently political, its measurement need not be.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Generalized SVD/PCA maps high-dimensional phrase-frequency matrices into a low-dimensional bias space representing newspapers and phrases.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: SVD-based decomposition of the phrase-count matrix is the algorithmic core of the bias-space construction.

conceptDetails.name: phrase bias
conceptDetails.description: A novel operationalization of media bias as the degree to which phrase-frequency statistics alone reveal a newspaper's political identity.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The method learns joint latent representations for both newspapers and phrases that encode political bias without human supervision.

conceptDetails.name: media bias landscape
conceptDetails.description: A data-driven two-dimensional political map (left-right × establishment) that emerges from cross-topic bias correlations.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Newspapers and phrases are embedded into a shared d-dimensional bias space whose axes carry interpretable political meaning.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The driving task is predicting which newspaper published a given article from phrase frequencies alone.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: A Poisson likelihood is maximized instead of mean-squared error to correctly model the discrete, heavy-tailed nature of phrase counts.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Automated phrase selection identifies the most bias-discriminating n-grams without manual curation.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The recovered bias axes are validated to correspond to human-interpretable political dimensions (left-right and establishment bias).

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Phrase filtering and selection reduce the vocabulary to a sparse, maximally informative subset for bias modeling.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The fully automated pipeline processes roughly one million articles from one hundred newspapers without human annotation.

title: What You Can Learn by Staring at a Blank Wall
abstract: We present a passive non-line-of-sight method that infers the number of people or activity of a person from the observation of a blank wall in an unknown room. Our technique analyzes complex imperceptible changes in indirect illumination in a video of the wall to reveal a signal that is correlated with motion in the hidden part of a scene. We use this signal to classify between zero, one, or two moving people, or the activity of a person in the hidden scene. We train two convolutional neural networks using data collected from 20 different scenes, and achieve an accuracy of $\approx94\%$ for both tasks in unseen test environments and real-time online settings. Unlike other passive non-line-of-sight methods, the technique does not rely on known occluders or controllable light sources, and generalizes to unknown rooms with no re-calibration. We analyze the generalization and robustness of our method with both real and synthetic data, and study the effect of the scene parameters on the signal quality.

conceptDetails.name: non-line-of-sight imaging
conceptDetails.description: The core problem domain: inferring information about a hidden scene by analyzing indirect illumination on a blank wall without direct observation.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Two CNNs are trained on a 2D projection of wall video data to perform person counting and activity classification with ~94% accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper performs two classification tasks: counting zero/one/two people and identifying activity type (walking, jumping, waving, crouching).

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The method detects extremely faint temporal illumination changes (−20 to −35 dB SNR) on blank walls caused by hidden person motion.

conceptDetails.name: indirect illumination model
conceptDetails.description: Novel signal model exploiting multi-bounce light transport physics to extract a motion-correlated signal from imperceptible wall brightness variations.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring hidden scene properties (occupancy, activity) from indirect observations of a wall, without knowledge of room geometry or lighting.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The method is evaluated for generalization to 5 unseen rooms without re-calibration, and robustness to scene parameter variation is studied theoretically and synthetically.

conceptDetails.name: passive scene inference
conceptDetails.description: Unlike active NLOS methods using lasers or WiFi, this approach uses only a standard camera with no controllable light sources or occluders.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: Videos of the wall are projected into a 2D spatiotemporal representation that summarizes essential motion signal for downstream classification.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: High-dimensional wall video is compressed into a compact 2D representation to isolate the low-SNR illumination signal from noise.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: A synthetic simulation of the light transport setup is developed to study the effect of scene parameters on signal quality and supplement real training data.

title: Hardware-accelerated Inference for Real-Time Gravitational-Wave Astronomy
abstract: The field of transient astronomy has seen a revolution with the first gravitational-wave detections and the arrival of multi-messenger observations they enabled. Transformed by the first detection of binary black hole and binary neutron star mergers, computational demands in gravitational-wave astronomy are expected to grow by at least a factor of two over the next five years as the global network of kilometer-scale interferometers are brought to design sensitivity. With the increase in detector sensitivity, real-time delivery of gravitational-wave alerts will become increasingly important as an enabler of multi-messenger followup. In this work, we report a novel implementation and deployment of deep learning inference for real-time gravitational-wave data denoising and astrophysical source identification. This is accomplished using a generic Inference-as-a-Service model that is capable of adapting to the future needs of gravitational-wave data analysis. Our implementation allows seamless incorporation of hardware accelerators and also enables the use of commercial or private (dedicated) as-a-service computing. Based on our results, we propose a paradigm shift in low-latency and offline computing in gravitational-wave astronomy. Such a shift can address key challenges in peak-usage, scalability and reliability, and provide a data analysis platform particularly optimized for deep learning applications. The achieved sub-millisecond scale latency will also be relevant for any machine learning-based real-time control systems that may be invoked in the operation of near-future and next generation ground-based laser interferometers, as well as the front-end collection, distribution and processing of data from such instruments.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The central astrophysical phenomenon; the paper targets real-time detection and denoising of gravitational-wave signals from binary mergers using deep learning.

conceptDetails.name: inference-as-a-service
conceptDetails.description: Novel IaaS paradigm where trained ML models are hosted centrally and exposed to networked clients via HTTP/gRPC, enabling scalable and hardware-agnostic inference for GW pipelines.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Both DeepClean and BBHnet are built on 1-D convolutional neural networks for processing time-series gravitational-wave strain data.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: BBHnet performs real-time detection of binary black hole coalescence signals embedded in detector noise.

conceptDetails.name: hardware acceleration
conceptDetails.description: GPUs and FPGAs are deployed as accelerators within the IaaS framework to achieve sub-millisecond inference latency for GW data analysis.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: DeepClean is a 1-D convolutional autoencoder that predicts and subtracts correlated instrumental noise from the gravitational-wave strain channel.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: BBHnet classifies time-series data segments as either binary black hole signals or detector noise artifacts.

conceptDetails.name: multi-messenger astronomy
conceptDetails.description: The overarching scientific motivation: real-time GW alerts must be delivered rapidly to coordinate electromagnetic and neutrino follow-up of transient events.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A primary motivation for the IaaS paradigm is addressing peak-usage and scalability challenges as the global GW detector network expands.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper proposes restructuring low-latency and offline GW computing workflows around the IaaS model for better resource management and deep learning integration.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Identifying non-Gaussian noise artifacts (glitches) and transient noise sources that contaminate GW searches is a key challenge addressed by the deployed models.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The system functions as a real-time trigger infrastructure, delivering low-latency GW alerts to enable multi-messenger follow-up observations.

title: Towards an Optimal Estimation of Cosmological Parameters with the Wavelet Scattering Transform
abstract: Optimal extraction of the non-Gaussian information encoded in the Large-Scale Structure (LSS) of the universe lies at the forefront of modern precision cosmology. We propose achieving this task through the use of the Wavelet Scattering Transform (WST), which subjects an input field to a layer of non-linear transformations that are sensitive to non-Gaussianity in spatial density distributions through a generated set of WST coefficients. In order to assess its applicability in the context of LSS surveys, we apply the WST on the 3D overdensity field obtained by the Quijote simulations, out of which we extract the Fisher information in 6 cosmological parameters. It is subsequently found to deliver a large improvement in the marginalized errors on all parameters, ranging between $1.2-4\times$ tighter than the corresponding ones obtained from the regular 3D cold dark matter + baryon power spectrum, as well as a $50 \%$ improvement over the neutrino mass constraint given by the marked power spectrum. Through this first application on 3D cosmological fields, we demonstrate the great promise held by this novel statistic and set the stage for its future application to actual galaxy observations.

conceptDetails.name: wavelet scattering transform
conceptDetails.description: The central methodological contribution: applying the 3D WST to cosmological density fields for the first time to capture non-Gaussian information in LSS surveys.

conceptDetails.name: non-gaussian statistics
conceptDetails.description: Extracting higher-order correlations beyond the power spectrum from the large-scale structure is the core scientific problem the WST addresses.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: The Quijote N-body simulations provide the 3D overdensity fields on which WST is applied to extract Fisher information on cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: WST extracts a compressed set of scattering coefficients from the 3D density field that encode non-Gaussian structural information beyond the power spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Fisher information matrix formalism is used to forecast marginalized posterior constraints on cosmological parameters from WST coefficients.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: The power spectrum (2-point function) serves as the baseline statistic against which WST improvements in non-Gaussian information extraction are benchmarked.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Parameter constraints are derived from N-body simulation ensembles using Fisher forecasting, approximating the likelihood of WST summary statistics.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: A key result is that WST improves neutrino mass constraints by 50% over the marked power spectrum, demonstrating sensitivity to this subtle cosmological signature.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes the cold dark matter + baryon power spectrum as a baseline and improves constraints on dark matter-related cosmological parameters.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: WST is framed as a CNN with fixed wavelet filters and fixed weights, enabling interpretable non-Gaussian feature extraction without learned parameters.

conceptDetails.name: dark energy
conceptDetails.description: Cosmological parameter estimation targets dark energy equation-of-state parameters among the 6 parameters constrained by WST.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: WST coefficients provide a compact summary statistic that compresses the high-dimensional 3D density field into a lower-dimensional observable vector.

title: Deep multi-task mining Calabi-Yau four-folds
abstract: We continue earlier efforts in computing the dimensions of tangent space cohomologies of Calabi-Yau manifolds using deep learning. In this paper, we consider the dataset of all Calabi-Yau four-folds constructed as complete intersections in products of projective spaces. Employing neural networks inspired by state-of-the-art computer vision architectures, we improve earlier benchmarks and demonstrate that all four non-trivial Hodge numbers can be learned at the same time using a multi-task architecture. With 30% (80%) training ratio, we reach an accuracy of 100% for $h^{(1,1)}$ and 97% for $h^{(2,1)}$ (100% for both), 81% (96%) for $h^{(3,1)}$, and 49% (83%) for $h^{(2,2)}$. Assuming that the Euler number is known, as it is easy to compute, and taking into account the linear constraint arising from index computations, we get 100% total accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is CICYMiner, a branched neural network with hard parameter sharing that learns all four Hodge numbers simultaneously rather than separately.

conceptDetails.name: calabi-yau manifolds
conceptDetails.description: The central mathematical objects of study: complex manifolds with trivial canonical bundle whose tangent space cohomologies (Hodge numbers) are predicted by the neural network.

conceptDetails.name: hodge number prediction
conceptDetails.description: The specific scientific task: predicting the four non-trivial Hodge numbers h^{1,1}, h^{2,1}, h^{3,1}, h^{2,2} that encode the topology of CICY four-folds.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi-Yau four-folds are studied as compactification spaces in F-theory and string theory, where Hodge numbers determine the number of massless fermion generations.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Inception module-based convolutional architectures from computer vision are adapted to process CICY configuration matrices as 2D images.

conceptDetails.name: cicy dataset
conceptDetails.description: Complete Intersection Calabi-Yau four-folds encoded as configuration matrices form the structured dataset used to train and benchmark all models.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Hodge number prediction is framed as a multi-class classification problem, with accuracy measured over discrete Hodge number values.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: CICYMiner is described as a multi-task regression model, treating Hodge numbers as continuous targets with class-imbalance weighting.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The network learns internal representations of CICY configuration matrices that generalize across all four Hodge numbers via shared lower layers.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: State-of-the-art computer vision architectures (Inception) are adapted and transferred to the string theory domain for Hodge number prediction.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper exploits the 921,497-sample CICY four-fold dataset (much larger than three-fold datasets) to achieve improved generalization and accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: Configuration matrix equivalences and permutation symmetries are used to augment training data and improve model robustness.

title: Nonperturbative renormalization for the neural network-QFT correspondence
abstract: In a recent work arXiv:2008.08601, Halverson, Maiti and Stoner proposed a description of neural networks in terms of a Wilsonian effective field theory. The infinite-width limit is mapped to a free field theory, while finite $N$ corrections are taken into account by interactions (non-Gaussian terms in the action). In this paper, we study two related aspects of this correspondence. First, we comment on the concepts of locality and power-counting in this context. Indeed, these usual space-time notions may not hold for neural networks (since inputs can be arbitrary), however, the renormalization group provides natural notions of locality and scaling. Moreover, we comment on several subtleties, for example, that data components may not have a permutation symmetry: in that case, we argue that random tensor field theories could provide a natural generalization. Second, we improve the perturbative Wilsonian renormalization from arXiv:2008.08601 by providing an analysis in terms of the nonperturbative renormalization group using the Wetterich-Morris equation. An important difference with usual nonperturbative RG analysis is that only the effective (IR) 2-point function is known, which requires setting the problem with care. Our aim is to provide a useful formalism to investigate neural networks behavior beyond the large-width limit (i.e.~far from Gaussian limit) in a nonperturbative fashion. A major result of our analysis is that changing the standard deviation of the neural network weight distribution can be interpreted as a renormalization flow in the space of networks. We focus on translations invariant kernels and provide preliminary numerical results.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Nonperturbative renormalization via the Wetterich-Morris equation is the central technical contribution, extending the Wilsonian perturbative analysis of NN-QFT to the full nonperturbative regime.

conceptDetails.name: nn-qft correspondence
conceptDetails.description: The central object of study: the mapping between neural network priors and Wilsonian quantum field theories, extended here to the nonperturbative regime.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops and extends the NN-QFT correspondence, mapping neural network behavior to a Wilsonian quantum field theory where the infinite-width limit is a free field theory.

conceptDetails.name: functional renormalization group
conceptDetails.description: The Wetterich-Morris exact RG equation is deployed to flow through the space of NN-QFT theories, providing the nonperturbative improvement over prior Wilsonian analysis.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Wilsonian effective field theory provides the framework for describing finite-width neural networks, with non-Gaussian interaction terms capturing finite-N corrections.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Translation-invariant kernels are the primary setting for explicit numerical analysis, connecting the neural network prior to the QFT 2-point function.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: The local potential approximation is analyzed in both symmetric and broken-symmetry phases, revealing distinct regimes of the NN-QFT theory space.

conceptDetails.name: tensor field theory
conceptDetails.description: Random tensor field theories are proposed as a natural generalization of the NN-QFT correspondence when data components lack permutation symmetry.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Beyond-symmetric-phase analysis is included to characterize the full structure of the effective potential under the renormalization group flow.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Neural network weight distributions are treated as stochastic processes whose standard deviation parametrizes a flow through network space.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper explicitly situates itself within explainable AI, arguing that RG-based QFT formalism can provide a transparent theoretical understanding of deep learning.

title: Discovering Sparse Interpretable Dynamics from Partial Observations
abstract: Identifying the governing equations of a nonlinear dynamical system is key to both understanding the physical features of the system and constructing an accurate model of the dynamics that generalizes well beyond the available data. We propose a machine learning framework for discovering these governing equations using only partial observations, combining an encoder for state reconstruction with a sparse symbolic model. Our tests show that this method can successfully reconstruct the full system state and identify the underlying dynamics for a variety of ODE and PDE systems.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: A sparsity prior is imposed on the symbolic governing equations to select the most parsimonious, interpretable representation of the nonlinear dynamics.

conceptDetails.name: symbolic regression
conceptDetails.description: The method discovers the symbolic mathematical form of governing ODEs and PDEs from data by fitting sparse linear combinations of predefined basis functions.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The core motivation is to produce symbolic governing equations that provide physical insight, in contrast to black-box deep learning models.

conceptDetails.name: system identification
conceptDetails.description: The primary task is identifying the governing equations of a nonlinear dynamical system directly from observed trajectory data.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: An encoder network reconstructs the hidden, unobserved state variables from sequences of visible states, forming half of the joint architecture.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The framework simultaneously solves two inverse problems: hidden state reconstruction and identification of the underlying governing equations from partial observations.

conceptDetails.name: hidden state reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The framework uniquely addresses the recovery of unobserved state variables simultaneously with equation discovery under partial observability.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The encoder learns a low-dimensional representation of the full system state from incomplete observations, capturing hidden dynamical variables.

conceptDetails.name: self-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The architecture is trained end-to-end using finite difference time derivatives computed directly from the observed data as self-supervised training targets.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A weighted multi-order derivative MSE loss is designed to propagate gradient information through the symbolic model and encoder jointly.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Known physical structure such as symmetries and projection functions are encoded into the architecture to constrain and improve learning.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The learned symbolic model serves as an interpretable surrogate for the true dynamics that generalizes beyond the training distribution.

title: Neural Conditional Reweighting
abstract: There is a growing use of neural network classifiers as unbinned, high-dimensional (and variable-dimensional) reweighting functions. To date, the focus has been on marginal reweighting, where a subset of features are used for reweighting while all other features are integrated over. There are some situations, though, where it is preferable to condition on auxiliary features instead of marginalizing over them. In this paper, we introduce neural conditional reweighting, which extends neural marginal reweighting to the conditional case. This approach is particularly relevant in high-energy physics experiments for reweighting detector effects conditioned on particle-level truth information. We leverage a custom loss function that not only allows us to achieve neural conditional reweighting through a single training procedure, but also yields sensible interpolation even in the presence of phase space holes. As a specific example, we apply neural conditional reweighting to the energy response of high-energy jets, which could be used to improve the modeling of physics objects in parametrized fast simulation packages.

conceptDetails.name: conditional reweighting
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution: a method to estimate conditional probability ratios q(x|x')/p(x|x') using neural network classifiers, extending marginal reweighting to condition on auxiliary features.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The fundamental quantity estimated by neural classifiers; the reweighting function w(x) approximates the likelihood ratio q(x)/p(x) or its conditional analog q(x|x')/p(x|x').

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The broader framework within which neural reweighting operates, using classifiers to extract statistical information from implicit simulator distributions without analytic likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain: reweighting fast detector simulations (e.g. Delphes) to match full detector simulations (e.g. Geant4) conditioned on particle-level truth information.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A custom loss function is developed that enables conditional reweighting through a single training procedure and provides sensible interpolation across phase space holes.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Neural network classifiers are trained to distinguish between event samples P and Q, with classifier outputs interpreted as reweighting functions.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The concrete collider physics application: reweighting the energy response of high-energy jets to improve modeling in fast simulation packages.

conceptDetails.name: phase space interpolation
conceptDetails.description: A key technical challenge addressed by the custom loss function: producing sensible reweighting estimates in regions of phase space sparsely populated or absent in training data.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental setting motivating the method, including generation and simulation pipelines for particle collider experiments at the LHC.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Neural conditional reweighting is proposed as a tool for improving parametrized fast simulation packages by correcting detector response modeling.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo event generators (Pythia, Herwig, Powheg-Box) and simulators (Geant4, Delphes) form the datasets on which reweighting is applied and validated.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
