conceptDetails.description: Underlying statistical problem of estimating probability density ratios p(x)/q(x) in high-dimensional, unbinned phase space without analytic forms.

title: Flow-based sampling for multimodal and extended-mode distributions in lattice field theory
abstract: Recent results have demonstrated that samplers constructed with flow-based generative models are a promising new approach for configuration generation in lattice field theory. In this paper, we present a set of training- and architecture-based methods to construct flow models for targets with multiple separated modes (i.e.~vacua) as well as targets with extended/continuous modes. We demonstrate the application of these methods to modeling two-dimensional real and complex scalar field theories in their symmetry-broken phases. In this context we investigate different flow-based sampling algorithms, including a composite sampling algorithm where flow-based proposals are occasionally augmented by applying updates using traditional algorithms like HMC.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core generative architecture used to construct variational proposal distributions that approximate target lattice field theory configurations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Both traditional MCMC and flow-based MCMC (with Metropolis-Hastings correction) are central sampling algorithms analyzed and combined throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary application domain, with scalar field theories on Euclidean spacetime lattices serving as testbeds for the proposed flow-based sampling methods.

conceptDetails.name: mode collapse
conceptDetails.description: A central failure mode of flow-based self-training where models learn to sample only a subset of the target's vacua; the paper proposes multiple methods to prevent it.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework motivating the need for non-perturbative path integral sampling, including Standard Model QCD and beyond-Standard-Model theories.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: The paper specifically studies scalar field theories in their symmetry-broken phases, where multimodal and extended-mode distribution challenges arise.

conceptDetails.name: composite mcmc
conceptDetails.description: A novel combined sampling algorithm interleaving flow-based proposals with traditional HMC updates to exploit complementary strengths of each method.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Real and complex scalar field theories near phase transitions into symmetry-broken phases define the multimodal target distributions studied.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Symmetry-equivariant flow architectures are introduced as an approach to handle the discrete and continuous symmetries of multimodal target distributions.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Flow models are trained to estimate the target lattice field theory probability density, with quality assessed via effective sample size and related metrics.

conceptDetails.name: mixture flow models
conceptDetails.description: Architectural approach using statistical mixtures of multiple flow models or single-model adaptive mixtures to represent multiple separated vacuum modes.

conceptDetails.name: flow matching
conceptDetails.description: Flow-based sampling algorithms including composite and augmented MCMC schemes are developed, extending the flow matching paradigm to multimodal targets.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Architectural symmetrization and canonicalization techniques are used to enforce target symmetries in flow models for lattice field theories.

title: Learning Task Informed Abstractions
abstract: Current model-based reinforcement learning methods struggle when operating from complex visual scenes due to their inability to prioritize task-relevant features. To mitigate this problem, we propose learning Task Informed Abstractions (TIA) that explicitly separates reward-correlated visual features from distractors. For learning TIA, we introduce the formalism of Task Informed MDP (TiMDP) that is realized by training two models that learn visual features via cooperative reconstruction, but one model is adversarially dissociated from the reward signal. Empirical evaluation shows that TIA leads to significant performance gains over state-of-the-art methods on many visual control tasks where natural and unconstrained visual distractions pose a formidable challenge.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: The core framework in which TIA operates, specifically model-based RL from visual observations where distractors degrade policy learning.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: TIA learns structured visual representations that explicitly separate task-relevant features from irrelevant distractors.

conceptDetails.name: task-informed mdp
conceptDetails.description: A novel factored MDP formalism (TiMDP) introduced in this paper that formally separates task-relevant and distractor state components.

conceptDetails.name: distractor separation
conceptDetails.description: The key contribution of TIA: a cooperative two-model game that explicitly explains away task-irrelevant visual features during training.

conceptDetails.name: disentangled representations
conceptDetails.description: TIA factorizes the observation into disentangled task and distractor components to isolate reward-correlated features.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: Reward prediction is used as the supervisory signal to steer the task model toward learning only task-relevant features.

conceptDetails.name: world models
conceptDetails.description: TIA extends forward dynamics world models (Dreamer) to operate over factored latent spaces that exclude irrelevant visual content.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A cooperative reconstruction loss combined with adversarial reward dissociation is central to TIA's training objective.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: An adversarial training objective is used to dissociate the distractor model from the reward signal, adapted from GAN-style adversarial dissociation.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: TIA builds on VAE-based world models (Dreamer) for cooperative reconstruction of observations from factored latent states.

title: Single electrons on solid neon as a solid-state qubit platform
abstract: Progress toward the realization of quantum computers requires persistent advances in their constituent building blocks - qubits. Novel qubit platforms that simultaneously embody long coherence, fast operation, and large scalability offer compelling advantages in the construction of quantum computers and many other quantum information systems. Electrons, ubiquitous elementary particles of nonzero charge, spin, and mass, have commonly been perceived as paradigmatic local quantum information carriers. Despite superior controllability and configurability, their practical performance as qubits via either motional or spin states depends critically on their material environment. Here we report our experimental realization of a new qubit platform based upon isolated single electrons trapped on an ultraclean solid neon surface in vacuum. By integrating an electron trap in a circuit quantum electrodynamics architecture, we achieve strong coupling between the motional states of a single electron and a single microwave photon in an on-chip superconducting resonator. Qubit gate operations and dispersive readout are implemented to measure the energy relaxation time $T_1$ of $15~μ$s and phase coherence time $T_2$ over $200~$ns. These results indicate that the electron-on-solid-neon qubit already performs near the state of the art as a charge qubit.

conceptDetails.name: quantum computing
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly advances quantum computing by demonstrating a new solid-state qubit platform achieving competitive T1=15μs and T2>200ns coherence times.

conceptDetails.name: circuit quantum electrodynamics
conceptDetails.description: The hybrid cQED architecture coupling a single electron's motional states to a superconducting microwave resonator is the central experimental framework enabling strong coupling and dispersive readout.

conceptDetails.name: qubit coherence
conceptDetails.description: Measurement of energy relaxation time T1 and phase coherence time T2 is the primary figure of merit characterizing the new platform's performance.

conceptDetails.name: quantum states
conceptDetails.description: The motional states of a single electron trapped on solid neon serve as the qubit computational basis states manipulated via gate operations.

conceptDetails.name: charge qubit
conceptDetails.description: The electron-on-solid-neon system is demonstrated and benchmarked as a charge qubit, achieving state-of-the-art coherence for this qubit modality.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents a carefully engineered experimental apparatus integrating electron traps, DC gate electrodes, on-chip LC filters, and a superconducting stripline resonator.

conceptDetails.name: crystal structure
conceptDetails.description: The face-centered-cubic crystal structure of solid neon provides the ultraclean, spinless, low-noise substrate essential for trapping electrons with long coherence.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Large scalability is cited alongside fast operation and long coherence as a key advantage distinguishing the electron-on-solid-neon platform.

conceptDetails.name: entanglement
conceptDetails.description: Remote entanglement between electron qubits via microwave photons is identified as a key prospective capability enabled by the cQED architecture.

title: The Principles of Deep Learning Theory
abstract: This book develops an effective theory approach to understanding deep neural networks of practical relevance. Beginning from a first-principles component-level picture of networks, we explain how to determine an accurate description of the output of trained networks by solving layer-to-layer iteration equations and nonlinear learning dynamics. A main result is that the predictions of networks are described by nearly-Gaussian distributions, with the depth-to-width aspect ratio of the network controlling the deviations from the infinite-width Gaussian description. We explain how these effectively-deep networks learn nontrivial representations from training and more broadly analyze the mechanism of representation learning for nonlinear models. From a nearly-kernel-methods perspective, we find that the dependence of such models' predictions on the underlying learning algorithm can be expressed in a simple and universal way. To obtain these results, we develop the notion of representation group flow (RG flow) to characterize the propagation of signals through the network. By tuning networks to criticality, we give a practical solution to the exploding and vanishing gradient problem. We further explain how RG flow leads to near-universal behavior and lets us categorize networks built from different activation functions into universality classes. Altogether, we show that the depth-to-width ratio governs the effective model complexity of the ensemble of trained networks. By using information-theoretic techniques, we estimate the optimal aspect ratio at which we expect the network to be practically most useful and show how residual connections can be used to push this scale to arbitrary depths. With these tools, we can learn in detail about the inductive bias of architectures, hyperparameters, and optimizers.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core methodology: borrowing EFT techniques from physics to build a tractable, accurate description of deep networks by integrating out microscopic details layer by layer.

conceptDetails.name: depth-to-width ratio
conceptDetails.description: Novel central finding: the aspect ratio of depth to width governs the effective model complexity and controls deviations from the infinite-width Gaussian limit.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: The 'representation group flow' (RG flow) framework directly parallels the renormalization group, tracking how statistical properties of preactivations propagate and accumulate through network depth.

conceptDetails.name: neural tangent kernel
conceptDetails.description: A novel analytical tool (not in taxonomy) tracking how gradient-based learning updates the network's effective kernel; RG flow of the NTK is developed across multiple chapters.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: A central result: finite-width networks learn nontrivial representations (captured by the dNTK), in contrast to infinite-width networks where representations remain fixed.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The neural tangent kernel (NTK) and 'nearly-kernel methods' framework form the backbone of the paper's analysis of training dynamics and generalization.

conceptDetails.name: network criticality
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept: tuning weight-initialization hyperparameters to a critical point eliminates the exploding/vanishing gradient problem and yields near-universal behavior.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Chapter 6 develops a full Bayesian treatment of neural network predictions, connecting finite-width corrections to Hebbian learning and representation learning.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Criticality analysis identifies phase-transition-like fixed points that separate stable from unstable initialization regimes, organizing activation functions into universality classes.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Network predictions are shown to follow nearly-Gaussian distributions whose non-Gaussian corrections are systematically controlled by the depth-to-width ratio.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework treats the ensemble of trained networks as a statistical object, deriving its distribution from first principles.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: A stated goal of the book is to make neural network behavior analytically understandable, explaining inductive biases of architectures, hyperparameters, and optimizers.

title: Toward Automatic Interpretation of 3D Plots
abstract: This paper explores the challenge of teaching a machine how to reverse-engineer the grid-marked surfaces used to represent data in 3D surface plots of two-variable functions. These are common in scientific and economic publications; and humans can often interpret them with ease, quickly gleaning general shape and curvature information from the simple collection of curves. While machines have no such visual intuition, they do have the potential to accurately extract the more detailed quantitative data that guided the surface's construction. We approach this problem by synthesizing a new dataset of 3D grid-marked surfaces (SurfaceGrid) and training a deep neural net to estimate their shape. Our algorithm successfully recovers shape information from synthetic 3D surface plots that have had axes and shading information removed, been rendered with a variety of grid types, and viewed from a range of viewpoints.

conceptDetails.name: 3d surface reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The central novel contribution: recovering full 3D shape and depth from a single rendered grid-marked surface plot image without axes or shading.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A deep convolutional neural network is trained to estimate 3D surface shape from grid-marked surface images, forming the core of the paper's approach.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the underlying data function z(x,y) from a rendered 3D surface plot is framed as an inverse problem — reversing the rendering process.

conceptDetails.name: shape-from-contour
conceptDetails.description: The method builds on the classical shape-from-X paradigm, specifically recovering surface shape from grid line contour cues in rendered plots.

conceptDetails.name: synthetic dataset generation
conceptDetails.description: SurfaceGrid, a 98.6k-image dataset of 3D surface plots paired with ground-truth depth maps, is synthesized programmatically to train the model.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The neural network extracts geometric and structural features from grid-marked surface images to infer depth and shape information.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The model performs regression over pixel-wise depth map values to reconstruct the surface shape quantitatively.

conceptDetails.name: data augmentation
conceptDetails.description: The SurfaceGrid dataset is synthesized with varied grid types, viewpoints, and rendering conditions to improve model generalization.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses automating interpretation of scientific figures to support literature mining and data extraction from published papers.

conceptDetails.name: superresolution
conceptDetails.description: The task of extracting quantitative detail beyond what is directly visible in a rendered image parallels superresolution-style reconstruction from degraded observations.

title: Flow-based sampling for fermionic lattice field theories
abstract: Algorithms based on normalizing flows are emerging as promising machine learning approaches to sampling complicated probability distributions in a way that can be made asymptotically exact. In the context of lattice field theory, proof-of-principle studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of this approach for scalar theories, gauge theories, and statistical systems. This work develops approaches that enable flow-based sampling of theories with dynamical fermions, which is necessary for the technique to be applied to lattice field theory studies of the Standard Model of particle physics and many condensed matter systems. As a practical demonstration, these methods are applied to the sampling of field configurations for a two-dimensional theory of massless staggered fermions coupled to a scalar field via a Yukawa interaction.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The core machine learning method used to construct expressive generative samplers that learn the target Boltzmann distribution over lattice field configurations.

conceptDetails.name: pseudofermion method
conceptDetails.description: A novel application of the pseudofermion technique to represent the fermion determinant stochastically, enabling flow-based sampling without explicit determinant evaluation.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Four distinct generative sampling schemes are constructed to capture different decompositions of the joint distribution over boson and pseudofermion fields.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The primary target application: the paper develops fermionic flow-based sampling as a prerequisite for applying these methods to QCD on the lattice.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical framework within which the paper operates, developing flow-based sampling for path integrals in lattice-regularized quantum field theories.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The existing MCMC/HMC sampling paradigm that flow-based approaches aim to replace or augment, with asymptotic exactness guaranteed via Metropolis accept/reject steps.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Flow architectures are explicitly constructed to respect translational symmetry with antiperiodic temporal boundary conditions of the pseudofermion action.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Flow models are optimized to approximate the target Boltzmann distribution, framing the sampling problem as density estimation over field configurations.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops equivariant flow architectures that embed the discrete symmetries of the lattice action into the generative model design.

conceptDetails.name: staggered fermions
conceptDetails.description: The specific lattice fermion discretization scheme used in the two-dimensional Yukawa model benchmark to validate the fermionic flow-based sampling framework.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Critical slowing down near phase transitions is the key motivation for replacing MCMC, and the flow-based approach is benchmarked in regimes approaching criticality.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Markov chain dynamics underlie both the baseline MCMC algorithms and the Metropolis-corrected flow-based samplers developed in this work.

title: Light Field Networks: Neural Scene Representations with Single-Evaluation Rendering
abstract: Inferring representations of 3D scenes from 2D observations is a fundamental problem of computer graphics, computer vision, and artificial intelligence. Emerging 3D-structured neural scene representations are a promising approach to 3D scene understanding. In this work, we propose a novel neural scene representation, Light Field Networks or LFNs, which represent both geometry and appearance of the underlying 3D scene in a 360-degree, four-dimensional light field parameterized via a neural implicit representation. Rendering a ray from an LFN requires only a single network evaluation, as opposed to hundreds of evaluations per ray for ray-marching or volumetric based renderers in 3D-structured neural scene representations. In the setting of simple scenes, we leverage meta-learning to learn a prior over LFNs that enables multi-view consistent light field reconstruction from as little as a single image observation. This results in dramatic reductions in time and memory complexity, and enables real-time rendering. The cost of storing a 360-degree light field via an LFN is two orders of magnitude lower than conventional methods such as the Lumigraph. Utilizing the analytical differentiability of neural implicit representations and a novel parameterization of light space, we further demonstrate the extraction of sparse depth maps from LFNs.

conceptDetails.name: light field networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary contribution: a novel neural scene representation that directly encodes the 4D plenoptic light field of a scene, enabling single-evaluation per-ray rendering.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: LFNs learn a compact neural representation of 3D scenes by mapping oriented rays in 4D light space to radiance values, encoding both geometry and appearance.

conceptDetails.name: neural operators
conceptDetails.description: LFNs are coordinate-based neural networks (neural fields) that act as neural operators mapping 4D ray coordinates to scalar radiance, enabling continuous scene representation.

conceptDetails.name: meta-learning prior
conceptDetails.description: A gradient-based or hypernetwork meta-learning framework is used to learn a shared prior over neural light fields, enabling single-image multi-view consistent reconstruction.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses the ill-posed inverse problem of reconstructing full 3D scene representations from sparse 2D image observations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: LFNs achieve three orders of magnitude speedup over volumetric renderers and two orders of magnitude reduction in storage by eliminating per-ray iterative sampling.

conceptDetails.name: plücker ray parameterization
conceptDetails.description: A novel use of 6D Plücker coordinates to parameterize the 4D space of oriented rays, enabling continuous 360-degree light fields without a bounding reference plane.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: A prior distribution over LFNs is learned via meta-learning, enabling the model to generate plausible 3D-consistent scene representations from a single image.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Scene-specific latent codes z condition the LFN weights, allowing a shared network to represent a distribution of distinct 3D scenes.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: LFNs serve as differentiable neural surrogates for classical light field rendering, replacing expensive ray-marching with a single network forward pass.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: LFNs are trained solely from 2D image supervision without 3D ground truth, using differentiable rendering as a self-consistent training signal.

title: Symmetry-via-Duality: Invariant Neural Network Densities from Parameter-Space Correlators
abstract: Parameter-space and function-space provide two different duality frames in which to study neural networks. We demonstrate that symmetries of network densities may be determined via dual computations of network correlation functions, even when the density is unknown and the network is not equivariant. Symmetry-via-duality relies on invariance properties of the correlation functions, which stem from the choice of network parameter distributions. Input and output symmetries of neural network densities are determined, which recover known Gaussian process results in the infinite width limit. The mechanism may also be utilized to determine symmetries during training, when parameters are correlated, as well as symmetries of the Neural Tangent Kernel. We demonstrate that the amount of symmetry in the initialization density affects the accuracy of networks trained on Fashion-MNIST, and that symmetry breaking helps only when it is in the direction of ground truth.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a mechanism to determine and characterize input/output symmetries of neural network densities without requiring equivariant architectures.

conceptDetails.name: parameter-function duality
conceptDetails.description: Novel duality framing: parameter-space and function-space are two equivalent descriptions of a neural network, and computing correlators in one frame reveals symmetry properties of the other.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: QFT concepts — partition functions, path integrals, n-point correlation functions, and classical vs. quantum symmetries — form the mathematical backbone of the symmetry-via-duality framework.

conceptDetails.name: network correlation functions
conceptDetails.description: n-point correlators of network outputs, computed in parameter space, are the key technical tool used to infer symmetries of the unknown function-space density.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: Group transformations on network inputs and outputs are the precise language used to define, test, and prove invariance of network densities.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Neural Network Gaussian Processes (NNGPs) serve as the infinite-width limit baseline whose known symmetries are recovered and extended by the duality framework.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: The NNGP kernel and Neural Tangent Kernel are central objects whose symmetries are analyzed and determined via the parameter-space correlator approach.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Experiments on Fashion-MNIST demonstrate that breaking initialization-density symmetry improves accuracy only when the broken symmetry aligns with the ground truth structure of the data.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies the induced density over function space arising from a neural network architecture and parameter distribution, even when this density has no closed form.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Equivariant networks are the standard approach to symmetry in deep learning; this paper explicitly contrasts its method by achieving invariant densities without requiring equivariance.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian inference over network parameters is one of the training regimes in which the symmetry-via-duality mechanism is shown to apply.

title: The Dark Machines Anomaly Score Challenge: Benchmark Data and Model Independent Event Classification for the Large Hadron Collider
abstract: We describe the outcome of a data challenge conducted as part of the Dark Machines Initiative and the Les Houches 2019 workshop on Physics at TeV colliders. The challenged aims at detecting signals of new physics at the LHC using unsupervised machine learning algorithms. First, we propose how an anomaly score could be implemented to define model-independent signal regions in LHC searches. We define and describe a large benchmark dataset, consisting of >1 Billion simulated LHC events corresponding to $10~\rm{fb}^{-1}$ of proton-proton collisions at a center-of-mass energy of 13 TeV. We then review a wide range of anomaly detection and density estimation algorithms, developed in the context of the data challenge, and we measure their performance in a set of realistic analysis environments. We draw a number of useful conclusions that will aid the development of unsupervised new physics searches during the third run of the LHC, and provide our benchmark dataset for future studies at https://www.phenoMLdata.org. Code to reproduce the analysis is provided at https://github.com/bostdiek/DarkMachines-UnsupervisedChallenge.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central method of the paper: unsupervised algorithms assign anomaly scores to LHC events to flag potential new physics without a specific signal hypothesis.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics goal—detecting beyond-Standard-Model signals at the LHC in a model-independent way using anomaly scores to define signal regions.

conceptDetails.name: model-agnostic search
conceptDetails.description: A novel search paradigm where no specific BSM signal hypothesis is assumed; anomaly scores define signal regions applicable to any new physics topology.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper benchmarks algorithms on over 1 billion simulated proton-proton collision events at 13 TeV, directly addressing LHC data analysis challenges.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Multiple VAE variants (deep set, convolutional, beta-VAE, VAE+normalizing flows) form the core set of evaluated anomaly detection architectures.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Simple autoencoders and the DAGMM are evaluated as baseline anomaly detectors using reconstruction error as the anomaly score.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Planar, Sylvester, inverse autoregressive, convolutional, and spline autoregressive flows are evaluated both standalone and combined with VAEs for density-based anomaly scoring.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Kernel density estimation and flow-based density estimators are used to model the background distribution and identify low-density anomalous events.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The anomaly score framework operationalizes out-of-distribution detection—events inconsistent with the Standard Model background are flagged as potential new physics.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: A benchmark dataset of >1 billion Delphes-simulated LHC events across multiple final states is constructed and released for future algorithm development.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Clustering algorithms are evaluated as one of the four main approaches to anomaly detection in the benchmark challenge.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Dimensionality reduction methods are assessed as a strategy for identifying structure in high-dimensional LHC event data indicative of anomalies.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: An adversarial anomaly detection model is included among the evaluated methods, using a GAN-style discriminator as the anomaly score.

title: Covariance-Free Sparse Bayesian Learning
abstract: Sparse Bayesian learning (SBL) is a powerful framework for tackling the sparse coding problem while also providing uncertainty quantification. The most popular inference algorithms for SBL exhibit prohibitively large computational costs for high-dimensional problems due to the need to maintain a large covariance matrix. To resolve this issue, we introduce a new method for accelerating SBL inference -- named covariance-free expectation maximization (CoFEM) -- that avoids explicit computation of the covariance matrix. CoFEM solves multiple linear systems to obtain unbiased estimates of the posterior statistics needed by SBL. This is accomplished by exploiting innovations from numerical linear algebra such as preconditioned conjugate gradient and a little-known diagonal estimation rule. For a large class of compressed sensing matrices, we provide theoretical justifications for why our method scales well in high-dimensional settings. Through simulations, we show that CoFEM can be up to thousands of times faster than existing baselines without sacrificing coding accuracy. Through applications to calcium imaging deconvolution and multi-contrast MRI reconstruction, we show that CoFEM enables SBL to tractably tackle high-dimensional sparse coding problems of practical interest.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper builds on sparse Bayesian learning as its core framework, performing type II maximum likelihood estimation to recover posterior distributions over sparse codes.

conceptDetails.name: covariance-free em
conceptDetails.description: The novel CoFEM algorithm eliminates explicit covariance matrix computation in expectation-maximization by solving multiple linear systems with conjugate gradient.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse coding — recovering a sparse latent vector from compressed measurements — is the central problem CoFEM is designed to solve.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: SBL's key advantage over non-Bayesian sparse methods is providing full posterior distributions and credible intervals over sparse codes rather than point estimates.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: CoFEM obtains unbiased estimates of the posterior mean and covariance diagonal without explicitly computing the full covariance matrix.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The primary contribution of CoFEM is reducing EM's per-iteration complexity from O(D³) to O(UKτD), enabling SBL to scale to high-dimensional problems.

conceptDetails.name: preconditioned conjugate gradient
conceptDetails.description: CoFEM uses preconditioned conjugate gradient as the iterative linear solver to obtain posterior statistics without forming the covariance matrix.

conceptDetails.name: diagonal estimation
conceptDetails.description: A little-known stochastic diagonal estimation rule is exploited to obtain unbiased estimates of the posterior covariance diagonal, a key step in the M-step of EM.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Both application domains — calcium imaging deconvolution and MRI reconstruction — are inverse problems where CoFEM-equipped SBL recovers signals from indirect measurements.

conceptDetails.name: multi-task learning
conceptDetails.description: CoFEM is extended to multi-task SBL for the multi-contrast MRI reconstruction application, jointly solving related sparse coding problems.

title: Preserving New Physics while Simultaneously Unfolding All Observables
abstract: Direct searches for new particles at colliders have traditionally been factorized into model proposals by theorists and model testing by experimentalists. With the recent advent of machine learning methods that allow for the simultaneous unfolding of all observables in a given phase space region, there is a new opportunity to blur these traditional boundaries by performing searches on unfolded data. This could facilitate a research program where data are explored in their natural high dimensionality with as little model bias as possible. We study how the information about physics beyond the Standard Model is preserved by full phase space unfolding using an important physics target at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC): exotic Higgs boson decays involving hadronic final states. We find that if the signal cross section is high enough, information about the new physics is visible in the unfolded data. We will show that in some cases, quantifiably all of the information about the new physics is encoded in the unfolded data. Finally, we show that there are still many cases when the unfolding does not work fully or precisely, such as when the signal cross section is small. This study will serve as an important benchmark for enhancing unfolding methods for the LHC and beyond.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: The central method studied — OmniFold performs full phase space unfolding to remove detector distortions and recover particle-level distributions from collider data.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary physics goal: determining whether beyond-Standard-Model signals (exotic Higgs decays) remain detectable in unfolded data.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The study is set at the LHC, analyzing Z+jets and exotic Higgs decay processes to benchmark unfolding methods in a realistic collider context.

conceptDetails.name: bsm unfolding fidelity
conceptDetails.description: Novel benchmark concept introduced by this paper: the degree to which full phase space unfolding faithfully preserves new physics information as a function of signal cross section.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Neural network classifiers are the core computational tool in OmniFold, used to iteratively reweight simulated events to match observed data.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: OmniFold operates by comparing experimental data to Monte Carlo simulation, using the ratio to infer the underlying particle-level distribution.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The reweighting procedure in OmniFold is mathematically grounded in likelihood ratios estimated by classifiers between data and simulation.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper quantifies how much BSM signal information survives unfolding as a function of signal cross section, framing the problem as signal detectability.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Model-agnostic and unsupervised anomaly detection approaches at detector-level are contrasted with the unfolded-data search strategy proposed here.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: SM production of Z bosons and jets serves as the dominant background, and SM simulations form the prior for the unfolding procedure.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Detector unfolding is fundamentally an inverse problem — recovering the true particle-level distribution from distorted measurements — studied here in the full phase space regime.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: Detector-level reconstruction of particle events (calorimeter deposits, tracks) defines the 'Simulation' input that OmniFold corrects back to truth-level.

title: A reconfigurable neural network ASIC for detector front-end data compression at the HL-LHC
abstract: Despite advances in the programmable logic capabilities of modern trigger systems, a significant bottleneck remains in the amount of data to be transported from the detector to off-detector logic where trigger decisions are made. We demonstrate that a neural network autoencoder model can be implemented in a radiation tolerant ASIC to perform lossy data compression alleviating the data transmission problem while preserving critical information of the detector energy profile. For our application, we consider the high-granularity calorimeter from the CMS experiment at the CERN Large Hadron Collider. The advantage of the machine learning approach is in the flexibility and configurability of the algorithm. By changing the neural network weights, a unique data compression algorithm can be deployed for each sensor in different detector regions, and changing detector or collider conditions. To meet area, performance, and power constraints, we perform a quantization-aware training to create an optimized neural network hardware implementation. The design is achieved through the use of high-level synthesis tools and the hls4ml framework, and was processed through synthesis and physical layout flows based on a LP CMOS 65 nm technology node. The flow anticipates 200 Mrad of ionizing radiation to select gates, and reports a total area of 3.6 mm^2 and consumes 95 mW of power. The simulated energy consumption per inference is 2.4 nJ. This is the first radiation tolerant on-detector ASIC implementation of a neural network that has been designed for particle physics applications.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: A neural network autoencoder is the core ML architecture implemented in the ASIC to perform lossy data compression of calorimeter trigger data before transmission.

conceptDetails.name: on-detector inference
conceptDetails.description: A novel deployment paradigm placing ML inference physically on radiation-exposed detector front-end ASICs rather than off-detector FPGAs.

conceptDetails.name: calorimetry
conceptDetails.description: The CMS high-granularity endcap calorimeter (HGCAL) with over 6 million readout channels is the primary application driving the data compression challenge.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The CMS real-time trigger system creates the bandwidth bottleneck that motivates on-detector ML compression before data reaches off-detector FPGAs.

conceptDetails.name: quantization-aware training
conceptDetails.description: Training procedure that models fixed-point numerical precision constraints during optimization to produce a hardware-efficient NN with minimal accuracy loss.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The autoencoder compresses high-dimensional calorimeter sensor data to a lower-dimensional latent representation for efficient transmission.

conceptDetails.name: high-level synthesis for ml
conceptDetails.description: The hls4ml framework automatically translates trained neural network models into optimized RTL hardware descriptions for ASIC and FPGA implementation.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets the HL-LHC upgrade at CERN, where extreme collision rates create the data rate challenges addressed by the ASIC design.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The encoder portion of the autoencoder learns to extract the most information-preserving features of the detector energy profile.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Area (3.6 mm²), power (95 mW), and latency constraints drive architectural choices including quantization and hardware-optimized layer sizing.

title: Deep Learning for Bayesian Optimization of Scientific Problems with High-Dimensional Structure
abstract: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a popular paradigm for global optimization of expensive black-box functions, but there are many domains where the function is not completely a black-box. The data may have some known structure (e.g. symmetries) and/or the data generation process may be a composite process that yields useful intermediate or auxiliary information in addition to the value of the optimization objective. However, surrogate models traditionally employed in BO, such as Gaussian Processes (GPs), scale poorly with dataset size and do not easily accommodate known structure. Instead, we use Bayesian neural networks, a class of scalable and flexible surrogate models with inductive biases, to extend BO to complex, structured problems with high dimensionality. We demonstrate BO on a number of realistic problems in physics and chemistry, including topology optimization of photonic crystal materials using convolutional neural networks, and chemical property optimization of molecules using graph neural networks. On these complex tasks, we show that neural networks often outperform GPs as surrogate models for BO in terms of both sampling efficiency and computational cost.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian optimization
conceptDetails.description: The central framework of the paper: sequential global optimization of expensive black-box functions using a probabilistic surrogate model and acquisition function.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian neural networks serve as the core surrogate models replacing Gaussian processes to model expensive black-box objective functions during optimization.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian neural networks provide posterior distributions over function predictions, enabling uncertainty-aware optimization decisions.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: Predictive uncertainty from BNNs drives the acquisition function to balance exploration and exploitation in the optimization loop.

conceptDetails.name: active learning
conceptDetails.description: Bayesian optimization iteratively selects the most informative next data point to query, following an active learning paradigm to minimize costly function evaluations.

conceptDetails.name: experimental design
conceptDetails.description: The BO framework is framed as sequential experimental design for scientific problems in physics and chemistry.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: GNNs are used as BNN surrogate architectures for molecular property optimization, naturally encoding molecular graph structure and symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: CNNs serve as BNN surrogates for topology optimization of photonic crystal materials, exploiting spatial structure and translational symmetries in image-like inputs.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central motivation is that BNNs scale better than GPs (which scale cubically with data) to high-dimensional and large-dataset scientific problems.

conceptDetails.name: auxiliary signal modeling
conceptDetails.description: The paper exploits intermediate or auxiliary outputs from composite data-generation processes (e.g., full scattering spectra) as additional supervision to improve surrogate model quality.

conceptDetails.name: materials discovery
conceptDetails.description: Photonic crystal topology optimization is a primary application domain, demonstrating BO-guided materials design.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian process kernels are the primary baseline surrogate model, with the paper arguing their limitations in high-dimensional structured spaces.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Topology optimization of photonic structures is framed as an inverse design problem where BO navigates a high-dimensional input space.

title: A Compound Poisson Generator approach to Point-Source Inference in Astrophysics
abstract: The identification and description of point sources is one of the oldest problems in astronomy; yet, even today the correct statistical treatment for point sources remains one of the field's hardest problems. For dim or crowded sources, likelihood based inference methods are required to estimate the uncertainty on the characteristics of the source population. In this work, a new parametric likelihood is constructed for this problem using Compound Poisson Generator (CPG) functionals which incorporate instrumental effects from first principles. We demonstrate that the CPG approach exhibits a number of advantages over Non-Poissonian Template Fitting (NPTF) - an existing method - in a series of test scenarios in the context of X-ray astronomy. These demonstrations show that the effect of the point-spread function, effective area, and choice of point-source spatial distribution cannot, generally, be factorised as they are in NPTF, while the new CPG construction is validated in these scenarios. Separately, an examination of the diffuse-flux emission limit is used to show that most simple choices of priors on the standard parameterisation of the population model can result in unexpected biases: when a model comprising both a point-source population and diffuse component is applied to this limit, nearly all observed flux will be assigned to either the population or to the diffuse component. A new parametrisation is presented for these priors which properly estimates the uncertainties in this limit. In this choice of priors, CPG correctly identifies that the fraction of flux assigned to the population model cannot be constrained by the data.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood estimation
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution is constructing a new parametric likelihood—the Compound Poisson Generator (CPG) likelihood—that incorporates instrumental effects from first principles for point-source inference.

conceptDetails.name: compound poisson likelihood
conceptDetails.description: The novel CPG likelihood functional that generalizes NPTF by correctly factoring in point-spread function, effective area, and spatial distribution without factorisation approximations.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The paper uses Bayesian analyses with carefully chosen priors on population model parameters to recover posteriors for source counts and flux fractions.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: A central goal is recovering unbiased posteriors for the number of point sources N in a population, demonstrating that CPG avoids the biases seen in NPTF.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The paper addresses how standard prior parameterizations lead to unexpected biases in uncertainty estimates, and proposes a new coordinate system that correctly quantifies uncertainty in the diffuse-flux emission limit.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The CPG likelihood is derived from Compound Poisson Generator functionals, a class of stochastic processes that model the statistics of photon counts from discrete source populations.

conceptDetails.name: point spread function modeling
conceptDetails.description: Accurate treatment of the instrumental PSF is shown to be non-factorisable from other effects in NPTF, and is incorporated rigorously in the CPG construction.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets the problem of detecting and characterizing dim or crowded astrophysical point sources against background fluctuations in X-ray observations.

conceptDetails.name: source count distribution
conceptDetails.description: The differential source-count function dN/dS parameterizes the population of point sources; the paper demonstrates how prior choices on this function cause biases and proposes a corrected parameterization.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The differential source-count function (dN/dS) serves as a population-level flux density model whose parameterization and prior choices are analyzed in depth.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Monte Carlo simulations are used to generate test scenarios (non-uniform PSF, spatially varying effective area, non-isotropic source distributions) for validating CPG against NPTF.

conceptDetails.name: neutrino detection
conceptDetails.description: NPTF, the method CPG improves upon, is widely applied in gamma-ray and neutrino astronomy, providing direct motivation for the new likelihood's development.

title: Why is AI hard and Physics simple?
abstract: We discuss why AI is hard and why physics is simple. We discuss how physical intuition and the approach of theoretical physics can be brought to bear on the field of artificial intelligence and specifically machine learning. We suggest that the underlying project of machine learning and the underlying project of physics are strongly coupled through the principle of sparsity, and we call upon theoretical physicists to work on AI as physicists. As a first step in that direction, we discuss an upcoming book on the principles of deep learning theory that attempts to realize this approach.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central thesis is that sparsity is the unifying principle linking machine learning and physics — both fields succeed precisely because the problems they solve admit sparse structure in their solution space.

conceptDetails.name: no-free-lunch theorem
conceptDetails.description: The no-free-lunch theorem is analyzed at length as the formal reason AI is hard, establishing that without structure (sparsity), no learning algorithm can outperform random guessing on average.

conceptDetails.name: generalization
conceptDetails.description: Generalization — the ability of a trained model to perform well on unseen data — is identified as the core challenge that the sparsity principle must explain and enable.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper advocates using physics intuition and methodology to interpret and explain why deep learning works, treating ML models as objects of principled theoretical study.

conceptDetails.name: deep learning theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper previews a forthcoming book developing principled theoretical foundations for deep learning, framed as the concrete realization of physics-style reasoning applied to ML.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Effective field theory is invoked as a physics framework whose coarse-graining logic may apply to understanding deep networks at different scales of abstraction.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Renormalization group methods are suggested as a physics tool that could inform how neural networks learn hierarchical representations across scales.

conceptDetails.name: phase transitions
conceptDetails.description: Statistical mechanics concepts including phase transitions are referenced as part of the physics toolkit that could illuminate emergent behavior in machine learning systems.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The empirical success of deep learning at large scale is presented as a central puzzle that a physics-inspired theory must account for.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Image classification is used as the primary worked example to concretely illustrate the no-free-lunch theorem and the exponential difficulty of learning without structure.

title: Machine Learning the 6th Dimension: Stellar Radial Velocities from 5D Phase-Space Correlations
abstract: The Gaia satellite will observe the positions and velocities of over a billion Milky Way stars. In the early data releases, the majority of observed stars do not have complete 6D phase-space information. In this Letter, we demonstrate the ability to infer the missing line-of-sight velocities until more spectroscopic observations become available. We utilize a novel neural network architecture that, after being trained on a subset of data with complete phase-space information, takes in a star's 5D astrometry (angular coordinates, proper motions, and parallax) and outputs a predicted line-of-sight velocity with an associated uncertainty. Working with a mock Gaia catalog, we show that the network can successfully recover the distributions and correlations of each velocity component for stars that fall within ~5 kpc of the Sun. We also demonstrate that the network can accurately reconstruct the velocity distribution of a kinematic substructure in the stellar halo that is spatially uniform, even when it comprises a small fraction of the total star count.

conceptDetails.name: stellar phase-space inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel application of neural networks to complete stellar 6D phase-space information from partial 5D astrometric observations from Gaia.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The neural network performs regression to predict continuous line-of-sight velocity values for stars from 5D astrometric inputs.

conceptDetails.name: uncertainty quantification
conceptDetails.description: The network outputs an associated uncertainty on each velocity prediction using a learned heteroscedastic error model.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Inferring the missing 6th phase-space dimension from 5D observations is fundamentally an ill-posed inverse problem addressed with neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: The network recovers the underlying velocity distribution of stellar populations, including kinematic substructure, rather than just individual predictions.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: The method leverages a labeled subset with full phase-space information to infer missing velocities for the unlabeled majority of stars.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A custom loss function combining predicted velocity and learned uncertainty is used to train the network to produce calibrated probabilistic outputs.

conceptDetails.name: cosmological simulation
conceptDetails.description: A mock Gaia catalog generated via the Galaxia Besançon Galactic model serves as the training and test data.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The approach is applied to recover kinematic substructure (Gaia Enceladus-like halo streams) as a minority component within the bulk stellar population.

conceptDetails.name: dark matter
conceptDetails.description: Recovering stellar kinematics enables mapping of dark matter substructures deposited by disrupted satellite galaxies.

conceptDetails.name: transfer learning
conceptDetails.description: The network trained on bright stars with complete 6D information is applied to generalize predictions to a broader test population with only 5D data.

title: Modern Machine Learning and Particle Physics
abstract: Over the past five years, modern machine learning has been quietly revolutionizing particle physics. Old methodology is being outdated and entirely new ways of thinking about data are becoming commonplace. This article will review some aspects of the natural synergy between modern machine learning and particle physics, focusing on applications at the Large Hadron Collider. A sampling of examples is given, from signal/background discrimination tasks using supervised learning to direct data-driven approaches. Some comments on persistent challenges and possible future directions for the field are included at the end.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper is fundamentally about applying machine learning to LHC proton-proton collision data for particle discovery and measurement.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Signal/background discrimination via supervised classification is one of the two primary ML applications reviewed in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jet substructure and jet tagging are central examples throughout the paper, including quark/gluon discrimination and boosted object tagging.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Model-agnostic, data-driven new physics searches using anomaly detection are highlighted as a major emerging direction.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The optimal Bayes classifier is shown to approximate the likelihood ratio, connecting ML classifiers to first-principles statistical inference.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Finding evidence for beyond-Standard-Model particles at the LHC motivates much of the ML methodology reviewed.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Using LHC simulation tools to generate synthetic training data for ML models is a defining feature of particle physics ML methodology.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Architectures that encode Lorentz symmetry and permutation invariance of particle sets are discussed as physics-motivated improvements.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Estimating probability distributions over high-dimensional phase space underpins both optimal classification and data-driven background estimation.

conceptDetails.name: unfolding
conceptDetails.description: Correcting detector-level measurements back to particle-level distributions is presented as an important ML application for precision physics.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The tension between high-performance black-box models and physically interpretable features is a recurring theme and open challenge.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Generative approaches are discussed for fast detector simulation and as components of anomaly detection pipelines.

title: Deep learning: a statistical viewpoint
abstract: The remarkable practical success of deep learning has revealed some major surprises from a theoretical perspective. In particular, simple gradient methods easily find near-optimal solutions to non-convex optimization problems, and despite giving a near-perfect fit to training data without any explicit effort to control model complexity, these methods exhibit excellent predictive accuracy. We conjecture that specific principles underlie these phenomena: that overparametrization allows gradient methods to find interpolating solutions, that these methods implicitly impose regularization, and that overparametrization leads to benign overfitting. We survey recent theoretical progress that provides examples illustrating these principles in simpler settings. We first review classical uniform convergence results and why they fall short of explaining aspects of the behavior of deep learning methods. We give examples of implicit regularization in simple settings, where gradient methods lead to minimal norm functions that perfectly fit the training data. Then we review prediction methods that exhibit benign overfitting, focusing on regression problems with quadratic loss. For these methods, we can decompose the prediction rule into a simple component that is useful for prediction and a spiky component that is useful for overfitting but, in a favorable setting, does not harm prediction accuracy. We focus specifically on the linear regime for neural networks, where the network can be approximated by a linear model. In this regime, we demonstrate the success of gradient flow, and we consider benign overfitting with two-layer networks, giving an exact asymptotic analysis that precisely demonstrates the impact of overparametrization. We conclude by highlighting the key challenges that arise in extending these insights to realistic deep learning settings.

conceptDetails.name: benign overfitting
conceptDetails.description: The central phenomenon studied: overparametrized models that perfectly interpolate noisy training data while achieving excellent predictive accuracy.

conceptDetails.name: overparametrization
conceptDetails.description: Having far more parameters than training samples is identified as the key structural property enabling both efficient optimization and benign overfitting.

conceptDetails.name: implicit regularization
conceptDetails.description: Gradient methods are shown to implicitly bias solutions toward minimum-norm interpolants without any explicit regularization term.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces and the neural tangent kernel provide the theoretical framework for analyzing generalization in the linear regime of neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Linear regression in the interpolating regime serves as the primary analytical setting for studying benign overfitting with quadratic loss.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Eigenvalue decomposition of kernel and covariance matrices underpins the asymptotic analysis of bias-variance decomposition in overparametrized models.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: Proportional and polynomial scaling regimes (parameters vs. samples) are central to understanding how overparametrization affects generalization.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Minimum-norm interpolating solutions are analyzed as an instance of implicit sparsity imposed by gradient descent without explicit regularization.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Empirical risk minimization with quadratic loss is the optimization objective whose landscape properties are analyzed throughout the paper.

title: hls4ml: An Open-Source Codesign Workflow to Empower Scientific Low-Power Machine Learning Devices
abstract: Accessible machine learning algorithms, software, and diagnostic tools for energy-efficient devices and systems are extremely valuable across a broad range of application domains. In scientific domains, real-time near-sensor processing can drastically improve experimental design and accelerate scientific discoveries. To support domain scientists, we have developed hls4ml, an open-source software-hardware codesign workflow to interpret and translate machine learning algorithms for implementation with both FPGA and ASIC technologies. We expand on previous hls4ml work by extending capabilities and techniques towards low-power implementations and increased usability: new Python APIs, quantization-aware pruning, end-to-end FPGA workflows, long pipeline kernels for low power, and new device backends include an ASIC workflow. Taken together, these and continued efforts in hls4ml will arm a new generation of domain scientists with accessible, efficient, and powerful tools for machine-learning-accelerated discovery.

conceptDetails.name: scientific workflows
conceptDetails.description: hls4ml provides an end-to-end codesign workflow enabling domain scientists to deploy ML models on hardware without deep hardware expertise.

conceptDetails.name: fpga firmware synthesis
conceptDetails.description: hls4ml translates trained ML models into FPGA firmware via high-level synthesis, its primary technical contribution.

conceptDetails.name: quantization-aware training
conceptDetails.description: QAT is a core network optimization technique in hls4ml enabling low-precision inference for energy-efficient hardware deployment.

conceptDetails.name: edge inference
conceptDetails.description: Near-sensor, low-power ML inference at the edge is the central application domain motivating hls4ml's design.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: hls4ml targets real-time trigger systems in high energy physics experiments like the LHC that require ultra-low latency ML inference.

conceptDetails.name: asic design workflow
conceptDetails.description: A new ASIC backend enables hls4ml to target application-specific integrated circuits for even lower power consumption.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Pruning and sparse operations are used in hls4ml to reduce model complexity and enable low-power hardware implementations.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: hls4ml is designed to scale across FPGA and ASIC backends and accommodate varying resource constraints for different scientific applications.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Convolutional neural networks are a key supported architecture in hls4ml for hardware implementation on FPGAs and ASICs.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Graph neural networks are among the supported ML architectures that hls4ml can translate into FPGA/ASIC firmware.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: Hardware-deployed ML models in hls4ml act as fast surrogates for real-time inference in physics experiments.

title: The Luminous and Double-Peaked Type Ic Supernova 2019stc: Evidence for Multiple Energy Sources
abstract: We present optical photometry and spectroscopy of SN\,2019stc (=ZTF19acbonaa), an unusual Type Ic supernova (SN Ic) at a redshift of $z=0.117$. SN\,2019stc exhibits a broad double-peaked light curve, with the first peak having an absolute magnitude of $M_r=-20.0$ mag, and the second peak, about 80 rest-frame days later, $M_r=-19.2$ mag. The total radiated energy is large, $E_{\rm rad}\approx 2.5\times 10^{50}$ erg. Despite its large luminosity, approaching those of Type I superluminous supernovae (SLSNe), SN\,2019stc exhibits a typical SN Ic spectrum, bridging the gap between SLSNe and SNe Ic. The spectra indicate the presence of Fe-peak elements, but modeling of the first light curve peak with radioactive heating alone leads to an unusually high nickel mass fraction of $f_{\rm Ni}\approx 31\%$ ($M_{\rm Ni}\approx 3.2$ M$_\odot$). Instead, if we model the first peak with a combined magnetar spin-down and radioactive heating model we find a better match with $M_{\rm ej}\approx 4$ M$_\odot$, a magnetar spin period of $P_{\rm spin}\approx 7.2$ ms and magnetic field of $B\approx 10^{14}$ G, and $f_{\rm Ni}\lesssim 0.2$ (consistent with SNe Ic). The prominent second peak cannot be naturally accommodated with radioactive heating or magnetar spin-down, but instead can be explained as circumstellar interaction with $\approx 0.7$ $M_\odot$ of hydrogen-free material located $\approx 400$ AU from the progenitor. Including the remnant mass leads to a CO core mass prior to explosion of $\approx 6.5$ M$_\odot$. The host galaxy has a metallicity of $\approx 0.26$ Z$_\odot$, low for SNe Ic but consistent with SLSNe. Overall, we find that SN\,2019stc is a transition object between normal SNe Ic and SLSNe.

conceptDetails.name: supernova classification
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is classifying SN 2019stc as a transition object between normal Type Ic supernovae and superluminous supernovae based on combined photometric and spectroscopic evidence.

conceptDetails.name: magnetar spin-down
conceptDetails.description: Rotational energy dissipation from a newly formed millisecond magnetar (P≈7.2 ms, B≈10^14 G) is identified as the dominant power source for the first and brighter light curve peak.

conceptDetails.name: multi-peak light curves
conceptDetails.description: The double-peaked photometric evolution—with peaks separated by ~80 rest-frame days—is the primary observational signature driving the multi-energy-source interpretation and the paper's core novelty.

conceptDetails.name: circumstellar interaction
conceptDetails.description: Collision of supernova ejecta with ~0.7 M⊙ of hydrogen-free circumstellar material located ~400 AU from the progenitor provides the energy for the prominent second light curve peak that cannot be explained by radioactive decay or magnetar spin-down alone.

conceptDetails.name: stellar evolution
conceptDetails.description: Progenitor properties—including inferred CO core mass of ~6.5 M⊙ and stripped hydrogen/helium envelopes—are reconstructed to place the event in the context of massive star evolution ending in core collapse.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Physical parameters such as ejecta mass, magnetar spin period, magnetic field strength, and CSM shell mass are inferred by fitting multiple analytical energy-source models to the observed double-peaked light curve.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Posterior estimation of light curve model parameters under combined magnetar spin-down and radioactive heating models is used to constrain the physical properties of SN 2019stc.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: SN 2019stc is flagged as an unusual transient that defies standard Type Ic or SLSN classification, motivating it as a rare intermediate case warranting detailed multiband follow-up.

title: Real-time lattice gauge theory actions: unitarity, convergence, and path integral contour deformations
abstract: The Wilson action for Euclidean lattice gauge theory defines a positive-definite transfer matrix that corresponds to a unitary lattice gauge theory time-evolution operator if analytically continued to real time. Hoshina, Fujii, and Kikukawa (HFK) recently pointed out that applying the Wilson action discretization to continuum real-time gauge theory does not lead to this, or any other, unitary theory and proposed an alternate real-time lattice gauge theory action that does result in a unitary real-time transfer matrix. The character expansion defining the HFK action is divergent, and in this work we apply a path integral contour deformation to obtain a convergent representation for U(1) HFK path integrals suitable for numerical Monte Carlo calculations. We also introduce a class of real-time lattice gauge theory actions based on analytic continuation of the Euclidean heat-kernel action. Similar divergent sums are involved in defining these actions, but for one action in this class this divergence takes a particularly simple form, allowing construction of a path integral contour deformation that provides absolutely convergent representations for U(1) and SU(N) real-time lattice gauge theory path integrals. We perform proof-of-principle Monte Carlo calculations of real-time U(1) and SU(3) lattice gauge theory and verify that exact results for unitary time evolution of static quark-antiquark pairs in (1 + 1)D are reproduced.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops and analyzes real-time lattice gauge theory actions for U(1) and SU(N) gauge theories, addressing unitarity and convergence of discretized path integrals.

conceptDetails.name: path integral contour deformation
conceptDetails.description: The central technical contribution is the introduction of contour deformations in complexified field space that convert divergent real-time path integrals into absolutely convergent representations suitable for Monte Carlo.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Proof-of-principle Monte Carlo calculations are performed for real-time U(1) and SU(3) lattice gauge theory using the convergent path integral representations derived in this work.

conceptDetails.name: sign problem
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses the sign problem inherent in real-time lattice path integrals, where pure phase weights prevent standard importance sampling.

conceptDetails.name: real-time transfer matrix
conceptDetails.description: The paper systematically analyzes which discretized lattice actions yield unitary real-time transfer matrices and constructs convergent representations for computing their associated path integrals.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Unitarity of the real-time transfer matrix is the central property being preserved or restored across different lattice action formulations studied in this paper.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper operates within the framework of Minkowski and Euclidean quantum field theory, addressing the real-time formulation of strongly coupled gauge theories.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The structure of U(1) and SU(N) gauge groups governs the character expansions, heat-kernel actions, and convergence properties analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: The sign problem arises because real-time path integral weights are pure phase factors that cannot be used as probability distributions for importance sampling.

conceptDetails.name: quantum simulation
conceptDetails.description: The work enables numerical simulation of real-time unitary gauge theory dynamics, verified against exact results for quark-antiquark pair evolution in (1+1)D.

title: On the Minimal Error of Empirical Risk Minimization
abstract: We study the minimal error of the Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) procedure in the task of regression, both in the random and the fixed design settings. Our sharp lower bounds shed light on the possibility (or impossibility) of adapting to simplicity of the model generating the data. In the fixed design setting, we show that the error is governed by the global complexity of the entire class. In contrast, in random design, ERM may only adapt to simpler models if the local neighborhoods around the regression function are nearly as complex as the class itself, a somewhat counter-intuitive conclusion. We provide sharp lower bounds for performance of ERM for both Donsker and non-Donsker classes. We also discuss our results through the lens of recent studies on interpolation in overparameterized models.

conceptDetails.name: empirical risk minimization
conceptDetails.description: The central method studied: ERM with square loss over a convex function class, whose minimal error and adaptivity properties are the subject of sharp lower bound analysis.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies ERM in the nonparametric regression setting under both random and fixed design, characterizing when the estimator can adapt to simpler true models.
