conceptDetails.name: local complexity
conceptDetails.description: A novel key concept: the complexity of local neighborhoods around the true regression function, shown to govern ERM adaptivity in the random design setting.

conceptDetails.name: minimax lower bounds
conceptDetails.description: Sharp lower bounds on ERM error for any regression function (not just worst-case) constitute the primary technical contribution of the paper.

conceptDetails.name: overparameterized models
conceptDetails.description: The paper discusses its lower bound results through the lens of interpolation in overparameterized networks, explaining when benign overfitting is possible.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic processes
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian averages (Gaussian complexity) serve as the fundamental complexity measure for function classes, and Donsker/non-Donsker class distinctions drive the main results.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse vector regression and Lasso are used as motivating examples of ERM over a large class F that may or may not adapt to a simpler sub-class H.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: The adaptivity question—whether ERM over a large class F remains robust to the true model lying in a simpler sub-class H—frames the paper's central inquiry.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The square loss is the specific loss function defining the ERM procedure, and its properties under fixed versus random design are analyzed throughout.

conceptDetails.name: kernel methods
conceptDetails.description: Gaussian kernel complexity and metric entropy of function classes (covering numbers) are used throughout to characterize statistical rates.

title: Topological Obstructions to Autoencoding
abstract: Autoencoders have been proposed as a powerful tool for model-independent anomaly detection in high-energy physics. The operating principle is that events which do not belong to the space of training data will be reconstructed poorly, thus flagging them as anomalies. We point out that in a variety of examples of interest, the connection between large reconstruction error and anomalies is not so clear. In particular, for data sets with nontrivial topology, there will always be points that erroneously seem anomalous due to global issues. Conversely, neural networks typically have an inductive bias or prior to locally interpolate such that undersampled or rare events may be reconstructed with small error, despite actually being the desired anomalies. Taken together, these facts are in tension with the simple picture of the autoencoder as an anomaly detector. Using a series of illustrative low-dimensional examples, we show explicitly how the intrinsic and extrinsic topology of the dataset affects the behavior of an autoencoder and how this topology is manifested in the latent space representation during training. We ground this analysis in the discussion of a mock "bump hunt" in which the autoencoder fails to identify an anomalous "signal" for reasons tied to the intrinsic topology of $n$-particle phase space.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The primary subject of analysis — the paper systematically examines how autoencoder architectures fail as anomaly detectors due to topological properties of the data manifold.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The target application: model-independent detection of new physics signals in high-energy physics data, which the paper argues is fundamentally limited by data topology.

conceptDetails.name: topological obstructions
conceptDetails.description: The central novel contribution: proving that nontrivial topology of data manifolds (e.g., phase space as S^{3n-4}) makes it impossible for continuous autoencoder maps to achieve zero reconstruction error uniformly.

conceptDetails.name: manifold learning
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes intrinsic and extrinsic topology of data manifolds (circles, spheres, tori, phase space) and how autoencoders attempt to learn compressed representations of these manifolds.

conceptDetails.name: phase space topology
conceptDetails.description: A novel finding: n-particle Lorentz-invariant phase space has the topology of S^{3n-4}, a sphere, which creates unavoidable topological obstructions for any continuous latent-space map.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Autoencoders are analyzed as dimensionality reduction tools that compress data to a latent layer; the paper shows why matching latent dimension to intrinsic data dimension is insufficient when topology is nontrivial.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper grounds its analysis in a mock bump hunt scenario where an autoencoder fails to flag a signal resonance due to the intrinsic topology of 3-body phase space.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The latent space representation learned during training is studied explicitly, showing how topological features of the data are distorted or misrepresented in the encoder output.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper critiques the assumption that high reconstruction error reliably flags out-of-distribution events, showing both false positives (topological artifacts) and false negatives (interpolation bias).

conceptDetails.name: inductive bias interpolation
conceptDetails.description: A novel failure mode identified: neural networks' inductive bias toward local interpolation causes rare or undersampled anomalous events to be reconstructed with low error, defeating anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The motivating application domain — data from high-energy particle colliders is analyzed, including 4-vectors, jet substructure, and the Higgs golden channel as concrete examples.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The paper directly addresses the challenge of detecting localized signals on a background manifold (e.g., a resonance on 3-body phase space) and explains why autoencoders fail at this task.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jet substructure observables such as n-subjettiness are cited as a primary motivation for autoencoder-based anomaly detection where phase space topology plays a key role.

title: On the convergence of group-sparse autoencoders
abstract: Recent approaches in the theoretical analysis of model-based deep learning architectures have studied the convergence of gradient descent in shallow ReLU networks that arise from generative models whose hidden layers are sparse. Motivated by the success of architectures that impose structured forms of sparsity, we introduce and study a group-sparse autoencoder that accounts for a variety of generative models, and utilizes a group-sparse ReLU activation function to force the non-zero units at a given layer to occur in blocks. For clustering models, inputs that result in the same group of active units belong to the same cluster. We proceed to analyze the gradient dynamics of a shallow instance of the proposed autoencoder, trained with data adhering to a group-sparse generative model. In this setting, we theoretically prove the convergence of the network parameters to a neighborhood of the generating matrix. We validate our model through numerical analysis and highlight the superior performance of networks with a group-sparse ReLU compared to networks that utilize traditional ReLUs, both in sparse coding and in parameter recovery tasks. We also provide real data experiments to corroborate the simulated results, and emphasize the clustering capabilities of structured sparsity models.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The paper introduces and analyzes a group-sparse autoencoder architecture as its primary contribution.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Group sparsity, a structured extension of traditional sparsity, is the core constraint imposed on the autoencoder's hidden representations.

conceptDetails.name: group-sparse relu
conceptDetails.description: A novel activation function that forces non-zero units to occur in contiguous blocks, enabling structured sparsity in neural networks.

conceptDetails.name: convergence analysis
conceptDetails.description: The paper provides theoretical proofs that gradient descent on the group-sparse autoencoder converges to a neighborhood of the true generating matrix.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The autoencoder learns to recover a generating dictionary matrix and structured sparse representations from unlabeled data.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Group-sparse activations are shown to encode cluster membership, with inputs sharing the same active group belonging to the same cluster.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: The paper assumes data adheres to a group-sparse generative model and studies whether the autoencoder can recover the generating matrix.

conceptDetails.name: dictionary learning
conceptDetails.description: The autoencoder is analyzed in the context of learning an overcomplete dictionary from group-sparse data, recovering the columns of the generating matrix.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: Model-based deep learning is motivated by the need for explainable architectures, and the group-sparse structure enables interpretable dictionary learning.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: Gradient dynamics of the loss function are analyzed theoretically to prove convergence of network parameters.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The parameter recovery task—reconstructing the generating matrix from data—is a core inverse problem studied in the paper.

title: Few-nucleon matrix elements in pionless effective field theory in a finite volume
abstract: Pionless effective field theory in a finite volume (FVEFT$_{π\!/}$) is investigated as a framework for the analysis of multi-nucleon spectra and matrix elements calculated in lattice QCD (LQCD). By combining FVEFT$_{π\!/}$ with the stochastic variational method, the spectra of nuclei with atomic number $A\in\{2,3\}$ are matched to existing finite-volume LQCD calculations at heavier-than-physical quark masses corresponding to a pion mass $m_π=806$ MeV, thereby enabling infinite-volume binding energies to be determined using infinite-volume variational calculations. Based on the variational wavefunctions that are constructed in this approach, the finite-volume matrix elements of various local operators are computed in FVEFT$_{π\!/}$ and matched to LQCD calculations of the corresponding QCD operators in the same volume, thereby determining the relevant one and two-body EFT counterterms and enabling an extrapolation of the LQCD matrix elements to infinite volume. As examples, the scalar, tensor, and axial matrix elements are considered, as well as the magnetic moments and the isovector longitudinal momentum fraction.

conceptDetails.name: finite-volume pionless eft
conceptDetails.description: The novel FVEFT_π/ framework combining pionless EFT with finite cubic volumes is the paper's primary methodological contribution.

conceptDetails.name: effective field theory
conceptDetails.description: Pionless effective field theory (EFT_π/) is the central framework used to analyze few-nucleon systems at momenta below the pion mass.

conceptDetails.name: nuclear matrix elements
conceptDetails.description: Scalar, tensor, axial, magnetic moment, and momentum fraction matrix elements of A=2,3 nuclei are computed and extrapolated to infinite volume.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: LQCD calculations of multi-nucleon spectra and matrix elements provide the finite-volume data that FVEFT_π/ is matched to and extrapolated from.

conceptDetails.name: finite-volume extrapolation
conceptDetails.description: A core goal is extrapolating finite-volume LQCD results for binding energies and matrix elements to infinite volume using EFT_π/.

conceptDetails.name: stochastic variational method
conceptDetails.description: SVM is used to construct variational wavefunctions for few-nucleon systems in finite volumes, enabling spectrum and matrix element calculations.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: The stochastic variational method (SVM), a Monte Carlo-based approach, is used to solve the few-body Schrödinger equation in finite volumes.

conceptDetails.name: renormalization
conceptDetails.description: Low-energy constants (LECs) and counterterms are determined through renormalization matching between LQCD and EFT_π/ in finite volumes.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper is grounded in QCD and nuclear QFT, deriving EFT Lagrangians for nucleon interactions from first principles.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Extracting infinite-volume matrix elements from finite-volume LQCD data via EFT matching constitutes an inverse problem.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: Counterterm fitting involves statistical inference to match EFT parameters to LQCD data across multiple finite volumes.

title: Path integral contour deformations for observables in $SU(N)$ gauge theory
abstract: Path integral contour deformations have been shown to mitigate sign and signal-to-noise problems associated with phase fluctuations in lattice field theories. We define a family of contour deformations applicable to $SU(N)$ lattice gauge theory that can reduce sign and signal-to-noise problems associated with complex actions and complex observables. For observables, these contours can be used to define deformed observables with identical expectation value but different variance. As a proof-of-principle, we apply machine learning techniques to optimize the deformed observables associated with Wilson loops in two dimensional $SU(2)$ and $SU(3)$ gauge theory. We study loops consisting of up to 64 plaquettes and achieve variance reduction of up to 4 orders of magnitude.

conceptDetails.name: path integral contour deformations
conceptDetails.description: The central novel method of the paper: deforming the integration manifold of SU(N) path integrals into complexified field space to reduce sign and signal-to-noise problems.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: The primary theoretical framework of the paper, defining SU(N) lattice gauge theory as the setting for path integral contour deformations.

conceptDetails.name: sign and signal-to-noise problems
conceptDetails.description: The core computational challenge addressed: exponential deterioration of Monte Carlo estimator quality due to phase fluctuations in complex actions and observables.

conceptDetails.name: variance reduction
conceptDetails.description: A novel application of contour deformations to define modified observables with identical expectation values but dramatically lower variance, directly targeting the signal-to-noise problem.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: Used to generate gauge field ensembles and compute expectation values of Wilson loop observables in SU(2) and SU(3) gauge theory.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: The SU(N) group structure is mathematically central, governing the parameterization of gauge fields and the allowed contour deformations.

conceptDetails.name: wilson loops
conceptDetails.description: The benchmark observable used to validate the method; variance of Wilson loops in 2D SU(2) and SU(3) gauge theory is reduced by up to 10^4 using deformed observables.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The ultimate target application: the paper's contour deformation methods are motivated by and aimed at sign/StN problems in QCD with non-zero quark chemical potential.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The overarching theoretical framework; holomorphy and Cauchy's integral theorem from complex analysis underpin the validity of the contour deformation approach.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Machine learning techniques are applied to optimize the family of contour deformations for Wilson loop observables, achieving variance reductions of up to 4 orders of magnitude.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The constructed contour deformations are required to preserve the SU(N) gauge symmetry structure of the path integral.

title: The LHC Olympics 2020: A Community Challenge for Anomaly Detection in High Energy Physics
abstract: A new paradigm for data-driven, model-agnostic new physics searches at colliders is emerging, and aims to leverage recent breakthroughs in anomaly detection and machine learning. In order to develop and benchmark new anomaly detection methods within this framework, it is essential to have standard datasets. To this end, we have created the LHC Olympics 2020, a community challenge accompanied by a set of simulated collider events. Participants in these Olympics have developed their methods using an R&D dataset and then tested them on black boxes: datasets with an unknown anomaly (or not). This paper will review the LHC Olympics 2020 challenge, including an overview of the competition, a description of methods deployed in the competition, lessons learned from the experience, and implications for data analyses with future datasets as well as future colliders.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The central focus of the paper: developing and benchmarking model-agnostic methods to identify anomalous collider events that may signal new physics beyond the Standard Model.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics motivation: searching for beyond-Standard-Model signatures at the LHC without assuming a specific signal model.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental setting: proton-proton collision data from the LHC is used as the benchmark dataset for all methods in the challenge.

conceptDetails.name: model-agnostic search
conceptDetails.description: The overarching new paradigm explored by the challenge: discovering new physics without committing to a specific BSM signal model during event selection.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The primary data objects analyzed are hadronic jets; methods detect anomalous jet pairs consistent with dijet resonances.

conceptDetails.name: autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: Multiple teams used autoencoder architectures (including variational and graph-based variants) to learn background representations and flag poorly-reconstructed signal events.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: VAE-based methods (including recurrent VAEs) are used to model the latent distribution of background jets and identify anomalies as outliers in latent space.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Several approaches estimate the probability density of background events to flag low-likelihood events as potential new physics signals.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are used by multiple teams for background density estimation and to transform data into Gaussian space for anomaly scoring.

conceptDetails.name: bump hunting
conceptDetails.description: A classical technique adapted throughout the challenge to search for localized excesses (resonances) in the dijet invariant mass distribution after anomaly-based event selection.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: A category of methods in the challenge that use partial signal knowledge (e.g., quasi-anomalous knowledge or topic modeling) to improve detection sensitivity.

conceptDetails.name: clustering
conceptDetails.description: Unsupervised clustering methods (e.g., UCluster) are applied to group collider events and identify clusters inconsistent with Standard Model background.

conceptDetails.name: graph neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Particle Graph Autoencoders represent jets as graphs over particles and use graph neural network encoders to learn compressed latent representations.

conceptDetails.name: generative adversarial networks
conceptDetails.description: GAN-AE methods combine GANs with autoencoders for background modeling and anomaly scoring in the dijet invariant mass spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: The SALAD method uses reweighted simulations and likelihood-free inference to calibrate background models and improve anomaly detection sensitivity.

title: Introduction to Normalizing Flows for Lattice Field Theory
abstract: This notebook tutorial demonstrates a method for sampling Boltzmann distributions of lattice field theories using a class of machine learning models known as normalizing flows. The ideas and approaches proposed in arXiv:1904.12072, arXiv:2002.02428, and arXiv:2003.06413 are reviewed and a concrete implementation of the framework is presented. We apply this framework to a lattice scalar field theory and to U(1) gauge theory, explicitly encoding gauge symmetries in the flow-based approach to the latter. This presentation is intended to be interactive and working with the attached Jupyter notebook is recommended.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: The central method of the paper, using invertible neural network transformations to sample Boltzmann distributions of lattice field theories.

conceptDetails.name: lattice gauge theory
conceptDetails.description: One of the two primary physics applications, specifically U(1) gauge theory with explicit gauge symmetry encoding in the flow architecture.

conceptDetails.name: equivariant neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Flow models are constrained to be equivariant under lattice translational symmetries and local gauge invariance to reduce model complexity.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MCMC methods are used to correct approximate flow samples, providing provably correct statistics for lattice field theory observables.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: Physical symmetries including translational symmetry and U(1) local gauge invariance are explicitly encoded into the flow architecture.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The theoretical physics setting for both scalar field theory and U(1) gauge theory applications treated in the tutorial.

conceptDetails.name: coupling layers
conceptDetails.description: Building blocks of the normalizing flow with triangular Jacobians enabling efficient density computation in high-dimensional lattice field spaces.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows enable exact likelihood evaluation of the model density, essential for importance weighting and MCMC corrections.

conceptDetails.name: generative models
conceptDetails.description: Normalizing flows are used as generative models that can directly produce samples from approximate target distributions.

conceptDetails.name: lattice qcd
conceptDetails.description: The broader context motivating efficient field configuration sampling algorithms addressed by the normalizing flow approach.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The framework targets Boltzmann distributions (posterior distributions over field configurations) using variational optimization of the flow model.

title: E Pluribus Unum Ex Machina: Learning from Many Collider Events at Once
abstract: There have been a number of recent proposals to enhance the performance of machine learning strategies for collider physics by combining many distinct events into a single ensemble feature. To evaluate the efficacy of these proposals, we study the connection between single-event classifiers and multi-event classifiers under the assumption that collider events are independent and identically distributed (IID). We show how one can build optimal multi-event classifiers from single-event classifiers, and we also show how to construct multi-event classifiers such that they produce optimal single-event classifiers. This is illustrated for a Gaussian example as well as for classification tasks relevant for searches and measurements at the Large Hadron Collider. We extend our discussion to regression tasks by showing how they can be phrased in terms of parametrized classifiers. Empirically, we find that training a single-event (per-instance) classifier is more effective than training a multi-event (per-ensemble) classifier, as least for the cases we studied, and we relate this fact to properties of the loss function gradient in the two cases. While we did not identify a clear benefit from using multi-event classifiers in the collider context, we speculate on the potential value of these methods in cases involving only approximate independence, as relevant for jet substructure studies.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper's primary domain, studying ML strategies for LHC collision event analysis including searches and measurements.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classification of collider events (per-instance vs per-ensemble) is the central ML task analyzed throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: The Neyman-Pearson lemma and per-instance likelihood ratio are the theoretical foundation showing informational equivalence of single- and multi-event classifiers.

conceptDetails.name: per-ensemble learning
conceptDetails.description: Novel paradigm studied in this paper where a machine-learned function acts on multiple collision events simultaneously rather than one at a time.

conceptDetails.name: ensemble methods
conceptDetails.description: Per-ensemble (multi-event) classifiers that process multiple collision events simultaneously are the primary subject of comparison and analysis.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends its analysis to regression tasks (e.g., top quark mass measurement), showing they can be rephrased as parametrized classification problems.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes properties of loss function gradients to explain empirically observed differences in training efficiency between per-instance and per-ensemble approaches.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: The Neyman-Pearson framework for optimal hypothesis testing underpins the theoretical equivalence proofs between single- and multi-event classifiers.

conceptDetails.name: parametrized classifiers
conceptDetails.description: The paper shows regression tasks can be recast as parametrized binary classification, providing a unified framework for both task types.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: Dijet resonance searches at the LHC serve as a key empirical benchmark for comparing per-instance and per-ensemble classifier performance.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Jet substructure studies are discussed as a case where approximate (non-IID) independence might give multi-event classifiers a practical advantage.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: Top quark mass measurement is used as a Standard Model precision measurement benchmark for comparing regression strategies.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: New physics searches in the collider context relate to anomaly detection, and the paper's findings on classifier optimality are relevant to these tasks.

title: Fast convolutional neural networks on FPGAs with hls4ml
abstract: We introduce an automated tool for deploying ultra low-latency, low-power deep neural networks with convolutional layers on FPGAs. By extending the hls4ml library, we demonstrate an inference latency of $5\,μ$s using convolutional architectures, targeting microsecond latency applications like those at the CERN Large Hadron Collider. Considering benchmark models trained on the Street View House Numbers Dataset, we demonstrate various methods for model compression in order to fit the computational constraints of a typical FPGA device used in trigger and data acquisition systems of particle detectors. In particular, we discuss pruning and quantization-aware training, and demonstrate how resource utilization can be significantly reduced with little to no loss in model accuracy. We show that the FPGA critical resource consumption can be reduced by 97% with zero loss in model accuracy, and by 99% when tolerating a 6% accuracy degradation.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The paper extends hls4ml to support CNN deployment on FPGAs via streaming-based convolutional and pooling layer implementations achieving 5 µs inference latency.

conceptDetails.name: fpga inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel automated workflow using high-level synthesis to transpile neural network models into FPGA firmware for fully on-chip, microsecond-latency inference without off-chip memory.

conceptDetails.name: model compression
conceptDetails.description: Combined pruning and quantization pipeline is evaluated to fit CNN models within the computational constraints of particle-detector FPGA trigger and DAQ systems.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: The primary application target is the Level-1 trigger system at the LHC, which requires fixed microsecond-scale latency for real-time event selection at 40 MHz.

conceptDetails.name: quantization-aware training
conceptDetails.description: QAT via QKeras interface allows training with reduced-precision weights and activations so the resulting fixed-point model meets FPGA resource and latency budgets.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Network pruning is used to zero out weights and exploit sparse computation during HLS firmware generation, reducing FPGA critical resource consumption by up to 99%.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The LHC proton-collision environment motivates the ultra-low-latency, low-power FPGA deployment requirements that drive the entire hls4ml design philosophy.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: A central result is demonstrating that FPGA resource utilization can be scaled down by 97–99% through combined pruning and quantization with minimal accuracy loss.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: A digit classifier trained on the Street View House Numbers dataset serves as the benchmark task for evaluating model compression and FPGA resource trade-offs.

title: Detection and Parameter Estimation of Gravitational Waves from Binary Neutron-Star Mergers in Real LIGO Data using Deep Learning
abstract: One of the key challenges of real-time detection and parameter estimation of gravitational waves from compact binary mergers is the computational cost of conventional matched-filtering and Bayesian inference approaches. In particular, the application of these methods to the full signal parameter space available to the gravitational-wave detectors, and/or real-time parameter estimation is computationally prohibitive. On the other hand, rapid detection and inference are critical for prompt follow-up of the electromagnetic and astro-particle counterparts accompanying important transients, such as binary neutron-star and black-hole neutron-star mergers. Training deep neural networks to identify specific signals and learn a computationally efficient representation of the mapping between gravitational-wave signals and their parameters allows both detection and inference to be done quickly and reliably, with high sensitivity and accuracy. In this work we apply a deep-learning approach to rapidly identify and characterize transient gravitational-wave signals from binary neutron-star mergers in real LIGO data. We show for the first time that artificial neural networks can promptly detect and characterize binary neutron star gravitational-wave signals in real LIGO data, and distinguish them from noise and signals from coalescing black-hole binaries. We illustrate this key result by demonstrating that our deep-learning framework classifies correctly all gravitational-wave events from the Gravitational-Wave Transient Catalog, GWTC-1 [Phys. Rev. X 9 (2019), 031040]. These results emphasize the importance of using realistic gravitational-wave detector data in machine learning approaches, and represent a step towards achieving real-time detection and inference of gravitational waves.

conceptDetails.name: gravitational waves
conceptDetails.description: The primary detection target of this work, specifically inspiral signals from binary neutron-star mergers embedded in real LIGO interferometer data.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: The core deep learning architecture (CNN) used to classify gravitational-wave signals from BNS mergers and estimate their source parameters in real time.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The central task of identifying transient gravitational-wave events in highly non-stationary, non-Gaussian LIGO noise, demonstrated on all GWTC-1 events.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: The framework distinguishes three classes—BNS mergers, BBH mergers, and noise glitches—enabling source-type identification in real detector data.

conceptDetails.name: real-time gravitational-wave inference
conceptDetails.description: Novel end-to-end pipeline achieving sub-second detection and parameter estimation latency, enabling prompt electromagnetic follow-up of multi-messenger transients.

conceptDetails.name: posterior estimation
conceptDetails.description: The CNN is trained to estimate posterior distributions over source parameters such as chirp mass and mass ratio directly from gravitational-wave strain data.

conceptDetails.name: bayesian inference
conceptDetails.description: The conventional parameter estimation baseline against which the deep learning approach is compared, highlighted as computationally prohibitive for real-time use.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Parameter estimation of compact binary source properties from observed gravitational-wave strain is framed as an inverse problem that the CNN solves efficiently.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Training data is generated from simulated BNS waveforms injected into real LIGO noise segments, enabling the network to learn signal representations without analytic likelihoods.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The CNN learns a compact representation of gravitational-wave morphology directly from raw time-series strain, bypassing hand-crafted matched-filter templates.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Distinguishing genuine astrophysical signals from non-Gaussian noise artifacts (glitches) in real LIGO data is a key robustness requirement of the framework.

title: Field of Junctions: Extracting Boundary Structure at Low SNR
abstract: We introduce a bottom-up model for simultaneously finding many boundary elements in an image, including contours, corners and junctions. The model explains boundary shape in each small patch using a 'generalized M-junction' comprising M angles and a freely-moving vertex. Images are analyzed using non-convex optimization to cooperatively find M+2 junction values at every location, with spatial consistency being enforced by a novel regularizer that reduces curvature while preserving corners and junctions. The resulting 'field of junctions' is simultaneously a contour detector, corner/junction detector, and boundary-aware smoothing of regional appearance. Notably, its unified analysis of contours, corners, junctions and uniform regions allows it to succeed at high noise levels, where other methods for segmentation and boundary detection fail.

conceptDetails.name: boundary detection
conceptDetails.description: The primary task of the paper: simultaneously detecting contours, corners, and junctions at every image location under high noise conditions.

conceptDetails.name: junction modeling
conceptDetails.description: The generalized M-junction—a wedge-based patch model with M angles and a freely-moving vertex—is the novel core representation introduced in this work.

conceptDetails.name: robustness
conceptDetails.description: A central contribution is achieving unprecedented robustness to high noise (low SNR), where all prior segmentation and boundary detection methods fail.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: A novel spatial regularizer is designed that reduces boundary curvature while preserving sharp corners and junctions, unlike prior length- or elastica-based penalties.

conceptDetails.name: curvature regularization
conceptDetails.description: A novel regularizer enforces spatial consistency across overlapping patches by penalizing boundary curvature while preserving corners and junctions, enabling reliable low-SNR performance.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The paper extracts dense boundary features—contours, corners, and junctions—from every image location using a patch-based parametric model with M+2 parameters.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering complete boundary structure from noisy observations is formulated as a non-convex optimization problem solved cooperatively across all patch locations.

conceptDetails.name: representation learning
conceptDetails.description: The 'field of junctions' is a structured intermediate image representation that jointly encodes contours, corners, junctions, and regional appearance.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Each patch is represented by a compact M+2 parameter junction model, encoding boundary structure as a low-dimensional structured decomposition.

conceptDetails.name: scalability
conceptDetails.description: The paper improves upon prior low-SNR contour detection methods in both accuracy and scalability through its greedy initialization and patch-wise formulation.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: Deep encoder-decoder CNNs are evaluated as baselines, and their limitations in precise boundary localization and low-SNR generalization are discussed.

title: Quasi Anomalous Knowledge: Searching for new physics with embedded knowledge
abstract: Discoveries of new phenomena often involve a dedicated search for a hypothetical physics signature. Recently, novel deep learning techniques have emerged for anomaly detection in the absence of a signal prior. However, by ignoring signal priors, the sensitivity of these approaches is significantly reduced. We present a new strategy dubbed Quasi Anomalous Knowledge (QUAK), whereby we introduce alternative signal priors that capture some of the salient features of new physics signatures, allowing for the recovery of sensitivity even when the alternative signal is incorrect. This approach can be applied to a broad range of physics models and neural network architectures. In this paper, we apply QUAK to anomaly detection of new physics events at the CERN Large Hadron Collider utilizing variational autoencoders with normalizing flow.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: The core task of QUAK — identifying new physics events at the LHC by detecting anomalous signatures that deviate from known background processes.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The primary physics application domain — QUAK is designed to enhance sensitivity for discovering beyond-Standard-Model phenomena at the CERN LHC.

conceptDetails.name: signal priors
conceptDetails.description: Novel concept central to QUAK — approximate or proxy signal hypotheses that encode salient features of new physics signatures without requiring exact signal knowledge.

conceptDetails.name: variational autoencoders
conceptDetails.description: The primary neural network architecture used in QUAK to learn latent representations of physics events and compute reconstruction-based anomaly scores.

conceptDetails.name: normalizing flows
conceptDetails.description: Combined with variational autoencoders to improve density estimation in the latent space, enabling more precise likelihood-based anomaly scoring.

conceptDetails.name: semi-supervised learning
conceptDetails.description: QUAK operates as a semi-supervised approach, interpolating between fully signal-prior-driven supervised searches and fully unsupervised model-independent anomaly detection.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The experimental context in which QUAK is applied, specifically to LHC collision event data including the LHC Olympics benchmark dataset.

conceptDetails.name: likelihood ratio
conceptDetails.description: QUAK constructs a multi-dimensional score space from likelihood ratios between signal and background models to discriminate anomalous events.

conceptDetails.name: density estimation
conceptDetails.description: QUAK uses learned density estimates to construct anomaly scores that measure how unlikely an event is under known physics or proxy signal models.

conceptDetails.name: out-of-distribution detection
conceptDetails.description: QUAK's anomaly detection strategy identifies events that fall outside the distribution of known Standard Model background processes.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The LHC physics objects (jets) form the primary input features for the anomaly detection task, with dijet events used as the benchmark search channel.

conceptDetails.name: simulation-based inference
conceptDetails.description: Background modeling in QUAK relies on simulated Standard Model events to train the autoencoder prior, reflecting simulation-based inference methodology.

title: Learning to Unknot
abstract: We introduce natural language processing into the study of knot theory, as made natural by the braid word representation of knots. We study the UNKNOT problem of determining whether or not a given knot is the unknot. After describing an algorithm to randomly generate $N$-crossing braids and their knot closures and discussing the induced prior on the distribution of knots, we apply binary classification to the UNKNOT decision problem. We find that the Reformer and shared-QK Transformer network architectures outperform fully-connected networks, though all perform well. Perhaps surprisingly, we find that accuracy increases with the length of the braid word, and that the networks learn a direct correlation between the confidence of their predictions and the degree of the Jones polynomial. Finally, we utilize reinforcement learning (RL) to find sequences of Markov moves and braid relations that simplify knots and can identify unknots by explicitly giving the sequence of unknotting actions. Trust region policy optimization (TRPO) performs consistently well for a wide range of crossing numbers and thoroughly outperformed other RL algorithms and random walkers. Studying these actions, we find that braid relations are more useful in simplifying to the unknot than one of the Markov moves.

conceptDetails.name: knot theory
conceptDetails.description: The mathematical study of knots — including braid representations, Reidemeister moves, and knot invariants — is the physical domain of the entire paper.

conceptDetails.name: braid word representation
conceptDetails.description: Knots are encoded as sequences of braid generators, enabling the direct application of NLP sequence models to topological problems.

conceptDetails.name: reinforcement learning
conceptDetails.description: TRPO and other RL algorithms are used to find sequences of Markov moves and braid relations that simplify knots to the unknot.

conceptDetails.name: transformers
conceptDetails.description: Reformer and shared-QK Transformer architectures are applied to binary classification of the UNKNOT decision problem, outperforming fully-connected networks.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classification is the core ML task: determining whether a given braid word closure represents the unknot or a non-trivial knot.

conceptDetails.name: jones polynomial
conceptDetails.description: A key topological knot invariant whose degree is found to correlate directly with the confidence of the trained classifier, revealing a learned geometric structure.

conceptDetails.name: embeddings
conceptDetails.description: Word embedding layers encode braid generators into vector representations, enabling NLP-style processing of knot braid words.

conceptDetails.name: attention mechanisms
conceptDetails.description: Self-attention and locality-sensitive hashing attention (Reformer) are central to the transformer architectures applied to knot classification.

conceptDetails.name: reward optimization
conceptDetails.description: The RL environment defines a reward signal based on braid crossing reduction, guiding the agent toward unknotting sequences.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: RL agents autonomously discover valid Reidemeister/Markov move sequences that provably reduce knots to the unknot.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper analyzes which actions (braid relations vs. Markov moves) the RL agent prefers, yielding insight into the structure of the unknotting problem.

title: Mapping Machine-Learned Physics into a Human-Readable Space
abstract: We present a technique for translating a black-box machine-learned classifier operating on a high-dimensional input space into a small set of human-interpretable observables that can be combined to make the same classification decisions. We iteratively select these observables from a large space of high-level discriminants by finding those with the highest decision similarity relative to the black box, quantified via a metric we introduce that evaluates the relative ordering of pairs of inputs. Successive iterations focus only on the subset of input pairs that are misordered by the current set of observables. This method enables simplification of the machine-learning strategy, interpretation of the results in terms of well-understood physical concepts, validation of the physical model, and the potential for new insights into the nature of the problem itself. As a demonstration, we apply our approach to the benchmark task of jet classification in collider physics, where a convolutional neural network acting on calorimeter jet images outperforms a set of six well-known jet substructure observables. Our method maps the convolutional neural network into a set of observables called energy flow polynomials, and it closes the performance gap by identifying a class of observables with an interesting physical interpretation that has been previously overlooked in the jet substructure literature.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is a method to translate black-box ML classifiers into human-readable sets of observables, directly addressing ML interpretability.

conceptDetails.name: decision ordering metric
conceptDetails.description: Novel metric (Average Decision Ordering) based on Kendall's rank correlation that quantifies how similarly two classifiers order pairs of inputs.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: The demonstration case study applies the method to jet classification in collider physics, specifically discriminating boosted W bosons from quark/gluon jets.

conceptDetails.name: convolutional networks
conceptDetails.description: A convolutional neural network acting on calorimeter jet images serves as the black-box model being mapped into human-readable observables.

conceptDetails.name: energy flow polynomials
conceptDetails.description: Novel basis of IRC-safe collider observables used as the candidate high-level observable space into which the CNN is mapped.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classification of jet types is the primary task used to demonstrate the mapping technique.

conceptDetails.name: feature extraction
conceptDetails.description: The method iteratively selects high-level observables from a large candidate space to extract the most decision-relevant features from the black-box model.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper is set in the context of high-energy collider physics, using LHC-style jet data as the benchmark application domain.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: The technique compresses a high-dimensional low-level input space into a small set of human-interpretable high-level observables.

conceptDetails.name: surrogate modeling
conceptDetails.description: The mapped set of high-level observables acts as an interpretable surrogate that reproduces the black-box model's classification decisions.

conceptDetails.name: model validation
conceptDetails.description: A key motivation is using the interpretable surrogate to validate that the ML model has learned physical rather than artifactual features.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: The iterative selection procedure produces a sparse, minimal set of observables that approximates the full neural network's behavior.

title: Enhancing searches for resonances with machine learning and moment decomposition
abstract: A key challenge in searches for resonant new physics is that classifiers trained to enhance potential signals must not induce localized structures. Such structures could result in a false signal when the background is estimated from data using sideband methods. A variety of techniques have been developed to construct classifiers which are independent from the resonant feature (often a mass). Such strategies are sufficient to avoid localized structures, but are not necessary. We develop a new set of tools using a novel moment loss function (Moment Decomposition or MoDe) which relax the assumption of independence without creating structures in the background. By allowing classifiers to be more flexible, we enhance the sensitivity to new physics without compromising the fidelity of the background estimation.

conceptDetails.name: moment decomposition
conceptDetails.description: Novel method introduced in this paper that decomposes classifier dependence on a resonant feature into orthogonal moment components, enabling controlled non-independence.

conceptDetails.name: loss function design
conceptDetails.description: The paper's central contribution is MoDe, a novel moment loss function that allows controlled dependence on a resonant feature rather than enforcing strict independence.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper targets resonant new physics searches at collider experiments, improving sensitivity without compromising background estimation.

conceptDetails.name: background sculpting
conceptDetails.description: The problem of classifiers inducing spurious localized structures in the background mass spectrum, creating false signal candidates.

conceptDetails.name: classification
conceptDetails.description: Binary classification of signal vs. background events is the core task, with the challenge that classifiers must not sculpt the background mass spectrum.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: Detecting localized excesses (bumps) in otherwise smooth mass spectra is the primary physics goal the method is designed to enhance.

conceptDetails.name: decorrelation
conceptDetails.description: Existing technique of enforcing classifier independence from a resonant feature; this paper generalizes beyond it while retaining its guarantees.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The methods are developed and validated in the context of high-energy collider experiments, including ATLAS and CMS W-jet tagging searches.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Boosted hadronic W tagging is the primary physically motivated benchmark for demonstrating MoDe's performance improvements.

conceptDetails.name: anomaly detection
conceptDetails.description: Bump hunting — finding anomalous localized excesses in mass spectra — is the search paradigm this work strengthens.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Sideband methods for background estimation and statistical significance of potential signals underlie the evaluation framework.

conceptDetails.name: calibration
conceptDetails.description: Ensuring fidelity of background estimation (no sculpting) is a key constraint the method must satisfy alongside improving classification.

title: Twisted Quasimaps and Symplectic Duality for Hypertoric Spaces
abstract: We study moduli spaces of twisted quasimaps to a hypertoric variety $X$, arising as the Higgs branch of an abelian supersymmetric gauge theory in three dimensions. These parametrise general quiver representations whose building blocks are maps between rank one sheaves on $\mathbb{P}^1$, subject to a stability condition, associated to the quiver, involving both the sheaves and the maps. We show that the singular cohomology of these moduli spaces is naturally identified with the Ext group of a pair of holonomic modules over the `quantized loop space' of $X$, which we view as a Higgs branch for a related theory with infinitely many matter fields. We construct the coulomb branch of this theory, and find that it is a periodic analogue of the coulomb branch associated to $X$. Using the formalism of symplectic duality, we derive an expression for the generating function of twisted quasimap invariants in terms of the character of a certain tilting module on the periodic coulomb branch. We give a closed formula for this generating function when $X$ arises as the abelianisation of the $N$-step flag quiver.

conceptDetails.name: symplectic duality
conceptDetails.description: Symplectic duality between pairs of symplectic resolutions (X and X!) is the primary theoretical framework used to derive generating functions for twisted quasimap invariants.

conceptDetails.name: hypertoric varieties
conceptDetails.description: Hypertoric varieties serve as the primary geometric objects whose twisted quasimap moduli spaces and symplectic duals are studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: quiver representations
conceptDetails.description: Twisted quasimaps parameterize quiver representations whose building blocks are maps between rank one sheaves on P^1, forming the central geometric objects of study.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The paper studies moduli spaces arising from abelian supersymmetric gauge theories in three dimensions, with Higgs and Coulomb branches central to the mathematical framework.

conceptDetails.name: tilting modules
conceptDetails.description: The generating function for twisted DT invariants is expressed as a graded trace of an indecomposable tilting module over the quantized periodic Coulomb branch.

conceptDetails.name: koszul duality
conceptDetails.description: Koszul duality between category O for the loop space LX and the periodic space PX! is the key mechanism used to deduce the main theorem relating quasimap invariants to representation theory.

conceptDetails.name: quantization
conceptDetails.description: The paper develops quantizations of hypertoric varieties and their loop/periodic analogues to connect enumerative geometry with representation theory.

conceptDetails.name: enumerative geometry
conceptDetails.description: The paper computes refined Donaldson-Thomas/quasimap invariants as Betti numbers of moduli spaces, assembling them into a generating function.

conceptDetails.name: category o
conceptDetails.description: An analogue of the BGG category O for symplectic resolutions is central to the symplectic duality framework and the Koszul duality results proved in the paper.

conceptDetails.name: flag quivers
conceptDetails.description: The N-step flag quiver provides the explicit example for which a closed formula for the generating function of twisted quasimap invariants is derived.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry breaking
conceptDetails.description: Higgs and Coulomb branch symmetry breaking in 3D supersymmetric gauge theories motivates the geometric structures studied throughout the paper.

conceptDetails.name: spectral methods
conceptDetails.description: Cohomological and Ext group computations over quantized algebras are used to identify moduli space cohomology with representation-theoretic structures.

title: Search for low mass vector resonances decaying into quark-antiquark pairs in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s} =$ 13 TeV
abstract: A search for low mass narrow vector resonances decaying into quark-antiquark pairs is presented. The analysis is based on data collected in 2017 with the CMS detector at the LHC in proton-proton collisions at a center-of-mass energy of 13 TeV, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 41.1 fb$^{-1}$. The results of this analysis are combined with those of an earlier analysis based on data collected at the same collision energy in 2016, corresponding to 35.9 fb$^{-1}$. Signal candidates will be recoiling against initial state radiation and are identified as energetic, large-radius jets with two pronged substructure. The invariant jet mass spectrum is probed for a potential narrow peaking signal over a smoothly falling background. No evidence for such resonances is observed within the mass range of 50-450 GeV. Upper limits at the 95% confidence level are set on the coupling of narrow resonances to quarks, as a function of the resonance mass. For masses between 50 and 300 GeV these are the most sensitive limits to date. This analysis extends the earlier search to a mass range of 300-450 GeV, which is probed for the first time with jet substructure techniques.

conceptDetails.name: collider physics
conceptDetails.description: The paper presents a proton-proton collision analysis at 13 TeV at the LHC using the CMS detector to search for new resonances.

conceptDetails.name: jet physics
conceptDetails.description: Large-radius jets with two-pronged substructure are the primary objects used to identify boosted resonance candidates.

conceptDetails.name: jet substructure
conceptDetails.description: Two-pronged jet substructure variables (N-subjettiness, soft drop mass) are the primary discriminant separating boosted dijet resonances from QCD multijet background.

conceptDetails.name: new physics searches
conceptDetails.description: The paper searches for leptophobic Z' vector resonances predicted by extensions of the Standard Model with extra dimensions or new gauge symmetries.

conceptDetails.name: signal detection
conceptDetails.description: The invariant jet mass spectrum is probed for a narrow peaking signal over a smoothly falling QCD background across 50–450 GeV.

conceptDetails.name: boosted topology
conceptDetails.description: Resonances recoiling against initial-state radiation are reconstructed as single large-radius jets, enabling sensitivity to low-mass resonances below the dijet trigger threshold.

conceptDetails.name: trigger systems
conceptDetails.description: A key challenge is that low-mass dijet resonances saturate trigger bandwidth; initial-state radiation is exploited to satisfy trigger requirements at low resonance masses.

conceptDetails.name: hypothesis testing
conceptDetails.description: Upper limits at the 95% confidence level on the coupling of narrow resonances to quarks are set as a function of resonance mass.

conceptDetails.name: event reconstruction
conceptDetails.description: The particle-flow algorithm is used to reconstruct and identify individual particles, which are then clustered into anti-kT and Cambridge–Aachen jets.

conceptDetails.name: monte carlo methods
conceptDetails.description: MADGRAPH5, POWHEG, and PYTHIA are used to simulate signal and background processes, with GEANT4 modeling the detector response.

conceptDetails.name: detector simulation
conceptDetails.description: Full GEANT4 simulation of the CMS detector is applied to all Monte Carlo samples for accurate signal and background modeling.

conceptDetails.name: standard model
conceptDetails.description: The search is motivated by BSM extensions of the Standard Model predicting leptophobic vector mediators coupling to SM quarks.

title: Strictification and gluing of Lagrangian distributions on derived schemes with shifted symplectic forms
abstract: A strictification result is proved for isotropic distributions on derived schemes equipped with negatively shifted homotopically closed $2$-forms. It is shown that any derived scheme over $\mathbb{C}$ equipped with a $-2$-shifted symplectic structure, and having a Hausdorff space of classical points, admits a globally defined Lagrangian distribution as a dg $\mathbb{C}^{\infty}$-manifold.

conceptDetails.name: shifted symplectic structures
conceptDetails.description: The central mathematical object: homotopy-algebraic generalizations of symplectic forms with shifted degree, whose strictification and global Lagrangian distributions the paper constructs.

conceptDetails.name: derived algebraic geometry
conceptDetails.description: The overarching framework—derived schemes, dg algebras, dg C∞-manifolds, and homotopy-coherent structures—within which all definitions, propositions, and theorems are formulated.

conceptDetails.name: lagrangian methods
conceptDetails.description: The paper's core contribution proves existence and global gluing results for Lagrangian distributions (purely derived foliations satisfying an isotropic condition) on derived schemes with −2-shifted symplectic forms.

conceptDetails.name: moduli spaces of sheaves
conceptDetails.description: The primary application: moduli spaces of perfect sheaves on Calabi–Yau 4-folds carry natural −2-shifted symplectic structures, and the paper's results enable construction of their virtual fundamental classes via global Lagrangian distributions.

conceptDetails.name: group theory
conceptDetails.description: SU(4) and Spin(7) group structures govern the two families of special connections—DT4 and Spin(7)-instantons—whose set-theoretic isomorphism and algebraic-geometric realization are the paper's motivating application.

conceptDetails.name: string theory
conceptDetails.description: Calabi–Yau 4-folds, the geometric objects central to the paper's applications, are foundational to string theory compactifications; the Donaldson–Thomas and Spin(7) enumerative theories studied here are of direct relevance to string-theoretic dualities.

conceptDetails.name: quantum field theory
conceptDetails.description: The moduli spaces of instantons and the virtual fundamental classes constructed via Lagrangian distributions are algebraic-geometric analogs of path-integral constructions in gauge-theoretic QFT.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The isotropic and Lagrangian conditions encode the preservation of the shifted symplectic pairing under the distribution embedding, mirroring classical symplectic symmetry constraints.

conceptDetails.name: hamiltonian systems
conceptDetails.description: Shifted symplectic geometry is the homotopy-algebraic generalization of classical symplectic/Hamiltonian geometry; the local Darboux theorem (analogue of canonical coordinates) is a key tool in the proofs.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: The strictification problem—recovering a strict representative from a homotopy-coherent isotropic distribution—is structurally an inverse problem of resolving homotopy data into strict algebraic structure.

title: AI Feynman: a Physics-Inspired Method for Symbolic Regression
abstract: A core challenge for both physics and artificial intellicence (AI) is symbolic regression: finding a symbolic expression that matches data from an unknown function. Although this problem is likely to be NP-hard in principle, functions of practical interest often exhibit symmetries, separability, compositionality and other simplifying properties. In this spirit, we develop a recursive multidimensional symbolic regression algorithm that combines neural network fitting with a suite of physics-inspired techniques. We apply it to 100 equations from the Feynman Lectures on Physics, and it discovers all of them, while previous publicly available software cracks only 71; for a more difficult test set, we improve the state of the art success rate from 15% to 90%.

conceptDetails.name: regression
conceptDetails.description: Symbolic regression is the core problem addressed: discovering closed-form mathematical expressions that fit data from unknown functions.

conceptDetails.name: symmetry preservation
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm uses neural networks to detect translational, rotational, and scaling symmetries in data, reducing problem dimensionality.

conceptDetails.name: separability detection
conceptDetails.description: A novel technique using neural network gradients to identify additive or multiplicative separability in multivariate functions, enabling problem decomposition.

conceptDetails.name: physics-informed neural networks
conceptDetails.description: Neural networks serve as function approximators to probe physical properties like symmetry and separability that guide the symbolic regression process.

conceptDetails.name: automated discovery
conceptDetails.description: The method automates scientific discovery by finding symbolic equations from data, analogous to how Kepler discovered orbital laws.

conceptDetails.name: recursive decomposition
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm recursively breaks hard symbolic regression problems into simpler sub-problems with fewer variables until solvable.

conceptDetails.name: interpretability
conceptDetails.description: The goal of symbolic regression is inherently interpretability — producing human-readable equations rather than black-box models.

conceptDetails.name: compositionality
conceptDetails.description: The algorithm exploits compositional structure of physical functions, representing them as parse trees and recursively solving sub-expressions.

conceptDetails.name: inverse problems
conceptDetails.description: Recovering the underlying symbolic law from observational data is a form of inverse problem central to the paper's contribution.

conceptDetails.name: dimensionality reduction
conceptDetails.description: Dimensional analysis and symmetry detection reduce the number of independent variables, simplifying the regression search space.

conceptDetails.name: sparse models
conceptDetails.description: Sparse regression is one of the baseline methods compared against, and polynomial fitting (a sparse linear approach) is used within the algorithm.
